Makurdi — The Senator representing Benue North East Senatorial District, Mr Daniel Iorkegh Saror, has raised serious doubts over the genuine identities of the 19 soldiers allegedly killed by Tiv militia, saying "local accounts seem to contradict the claim that the captured persons were indeed Nigerian soldiers."
Saror who met on Saturday, with Ukum people at Sankera, one of the theatres of the massacre by Federal troops on 22 and 23 October this year, said nothing in the least is convincing about the much publicised claims by both the army authorities and the Federal Government that genuine soldiers on legal peace keeping operations were slain by Tiv people.
Although the Benue State Governor, Mr George Akume had offered an earlier apology to Nigerians on the death of the allegedly slain soldiers, the former Vice Chancellor, Ahmadu Bello University (ABU) Zaria, insisted that the "validity of the claim that 19 Nigerian soldiers were abducted at Vaase and brutally murdered and mutilated in Zaki-Biam" is still hanging in the balance.
Among the issues raised against the authenticity of government and army's claim is the "soldiers" were in civilian vans driven by civilians. Their uniform did not conform to the dress of Nigerian soldiers. They had no helmets on; they had no army boots but wore assorted shoes including flip-flops not characteristic of the nation's soldiers on official assignment."
Saror also questioned why "there was not a single military vehicle with them" even though the army high command claimed that these Nigerian "soldiers" were deployed for peace keeping assignment at the Benue/Taraba border from Bauchi.
According to Saror, besides the disturbing fact that the alleged soldiers had assorted guns including "regular hunting rifles, quite uncharacteristic of the Nigerian Army" it was worrying that although a total of 23 persons were actually killed at the said incidence the army is only claiming 19 without an explanation on the identities of the remaining four.
Even the 19 that are being alleged to be soldiers, Saror, questioned the validity of their said identity cards and numbers. "Some of these numbers were laminated while others were said to be on pieces of paper? Adding that "four of the 23 persons could not give any identification of any kind even though they were in Nigeria military dress."
Insisting that it is these and other reasons that have informed the insistence on the setting up of a judicial commission of inquiry for Nigerians to publicly know the truth, Saror hinted too that "Jukun militia that had been attacking Tiv villages on almost all previous occasions were almost always dressed in army uniform and usually accompanied by genuine soldiers from Takum."
The senator, therefore, averred that the said soldiers "were part of a Jukun militia team of over 50 persons who were operating on the border "insisting that "a few days earlier, the same team who were in white Toyota Hilux vans with inscription "for official use only" had visited Abako village in the guise of being peae keepers. They later set Abako on fire."
Saror particularly lamented that, not only has the soldiers' invasion of Sankera federal constituency completely destroyed the economic base of Ukum local government area, the administrative structures have been completely destroyed.
He said it was still difficult to ascertain the head count of all the Ukum people who were killed by the soldies or later died as a result of bullet wounds. He called on community leaders in the area "to compile an acurate inventory of the losses suffered by members of the community." The compilation he advised should include details of individuals killed, those missing and the extent of property destroyed.
Another area which the senator raised concerns is the heightening refugee problems which has continued to rise daily as silent killings against Tiv people allegedly continue in parts of Taraba State unchecked.
Besides, Senator Saror feared that hunger is imminent within the area as the people "lost their stored food items and most now rely on their yam farms and guinea corn which is not ready for harvest yet."

Original_Search_Title: Official Clears 'Gift' to Black  Star
Headline: Nigeria/Ghana: Official Clears 'Gift' to Black  Star
Body_Text:
GOVERNOR Peter Odili's monetary gift to members of the Black Stars
after a World Cup game against the Super Eagles should not be made an
issue, a top NFA official has said.
Rather, the gift, the official said portrays the hospitality inherent
in African culture.
According to him, "it is a common thing in Africa to give gifts to
visitors when they are about to go.
In the olden days, our forefathers gave yam tubers, goats, cowries
and so on.
So, if the Governor of Rivers State in his magnanimity decided to
give a visiting team some money after a game, he is keeping to the
culture of his people.
It will be act of mischief to read any other meaning to the gesture",
said the F.A official who pleaded for anonymity.
And in Ghana, the Executive Council of the Ghana Football Association
over the weekend cleared Mr. Ben Koufie of any wrong doing over the
affair.
An emergency meeting convened in the wake of widespread attack on the
GFA boss which roped in journalists accompanying the team, stressed
that the bribery allegations against them were "unfounded and without
merit".
The matter has gained international media attention and was discussed
widely even on the world wide web as well.
The following comment was on one such US based site: I was not in
Port Harcourt to witness the euphoria following the match, but from
the reports that I have seen, "the only real issue here is that the
Governor is apparently misspending taxpayers money.
Unless of course it is his own personal money (I understand that he
was a wealthy US-based doctor), in which case he is suffering from a
paucity of judgment (or just too giddy from too much champagne)"

Original_Search_Title: Song: Little Haven in Adamawa State
Headline: Nigeria: Song: Little Haven in Adamawa State
Body_Text:
Song local government in Adamawa State is the home town of Professor
Jubril Aminu the renowned surgeon; former vice chancellor; former
secretary of the National Universities Commission, former education
Minister, former Petroleum-Resource Minister, and present Ambassador
to the United States of America! On this merit alone; this is a place
to dig its name and fame.
Professor Jubril Aminu is a big name.
According to Abdurahman Maina yaji, Mutawwadi Michika, and
information officer of the local government who exchanged views with
newsmen recently in Yola, apart from this, Song, which is about 80
kilometres from Yola the Adamawa State Capital, is noted in tourist
circles for the three-sisters rock, a natural phenomenal whose
curvature is a beauty to behold.
On the trunk A road, to Maiduguri, Song is known for its peaceful
countryside. The green lush grass, the rich savannah grassland, the
potentials for investment, the rice, beans, legumes, tomato, spinach
guava, lemon, oranges, banana, groundnut, maize, coco yam, and all
the agricultural products you can think of, flourish there.
With the undulating land, the serene environment is a symbol of peace
that eases the mind. The quietness and calmness of the area alone is
a blessing, and is very good for studies, reflection and up date on
former events.
Additionally it is one local government that has sizable number of
cattle, sheep, and goats. Besides, the rich humus soil across the
local government, is an indication of the economic buoyancy of the
area.
This is why, Abdurahman Maina Yaji, the information officer
emphasises that Ruma River can be used for purposes similar to the
Argungu Fishing Festival in Kebbi State.
Five Kilometres wide, and seven Kilometres long, the Ruma River has
millions of fish. With research experts who inspected the river from
the Chad Basin Development Authority, and a visit from officials in
Abuja-Ruma river is 15 kilometres from Song, the headquarters of the
same council.
This is food for throught for National Tourism Board.
On top of the green scenery, research indicates that each farming
season earns more than a billion Naira annually from the crops that
are grown there.
Therefore; not only is Song considered a tourist haven from this
perspective, but efforts have been put in place by Alhaji Umaru
Hamajam, the executive chairman of Song Local Government area in
Adamawa State to give investors and tourists a delightful conducive
atmosphere and environment when there.
A shopping complex has been built which is at this moment utilised by
business men. There are 36 shops operational and quarters for rent,
plus 8 chalets for guests and an administraion block.
Information gathered reveal that all roads linking the villages and
towns including Gulatabal Salasa, Zumo, Paikin, Loko, Garin -Madugu,
Dumne are graded to make users, visitors, Investors and guests
accessible with tractors worth more than seven million Naira acquired
by the chairman. The roads to Dumne to Dirma, winding to Salasa cost
the local government endlessly N3 Million.
Alhaji Umaru Hammajam is the Chief Exeuctive and Chairman Song Local
Govenrment Area in Adamawa State.
He is one who would not dip his hands into the treasury of the
council.
Therefore this chairman was able to stablise workers salaries
consistently too. He has been able to work on the welfare of staff
carefully with due attention.
He has re-furbished the local government Secretariat and bought
chairs, tables, curtains, air conditioners and has settled leave
grants and typewriters have been bought for clerks.
To this end, he has been given various awards for industry, hard
work, commitment to duty and to tolerance.
His relationship with the National Union of Local Government
Employees is said to be harmonious and cordial.
The Song Local government area in Adamawa State dug 3 wells in each
of the eleven wards and consitutencies and rehabilitated 2 each in
the same places, which makes a total of 5 per 11 wards.
Forty three motorcycles were bought and distributed to unemployed
Secondary School leavers on Loan to lessen hardship. Some have proved
worthy ambassadors, and have been able to pay back the loans, and
have purchased more on their own to generate lump sum revenue and
independent self -sustenance.
These are apart from the buses that have been bought to ply Mubi
Gombi, Biu, Maiduguri, Kano, Jos, Bauchi, Gombe, Jalingo, Yola, and
surrounding market towns opening up business channels and swelling
government purse in terms of revenue.
As the hometown of Professor Jubril Aminu, the medical Surgeon, this
local government scored one of the best marks in the primary health
care drive.
It has conducted the National Programme on Immunisation (NPI) the
National Immunisation Days (NID), kick Out Poliomyelitis, Roll Back
Malaria have been successful here.
Portraits of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, Atiku Abubakar Vice-President,
and Governor Boni Haruna adorn bill boards and residential buildings
calling on parents to immunize their children. These are translated
from English language, into Hausa and Fulfulde so that the messages
are passed across.
Song Local Government Area in Adamawa State has made a distinction
going by current eftorts to construct drainages to alleviate the
flood menace that engages the headquarter in a battle.
A little fact finding on the Universal Primary Education in the area
is instructive. Since the teachers are provided necessary logistics
and the improvement in rehabilitating of schools, a lot of water has
passed under the bridge.
In the morning on week days it is nnpressive to watch pupils going to
school, in green, white, yellow, brown uniforms in large groups with
their bags and books. The Umaru Hammajam administration in Song LGA
of Adamawa State has resuscitated the fallen glory of education; at
most or at least in Song as a council and area.
Still, the National Poverty Education Programme (NAPEP) has taken off
here.
At the time of writing this report; graduates professionals, school
certificate holders and Diploma holders are busy, requesting for the
signature of the chairman; Alhaji Umaru Hammajam and endorsement by
various councilors in the eleven wards for onward delivery to Yola.
Infact this is one aspect, the Obasanjo administration has to be
commended for. This programme has lessened theft, idleness of the
youth, and has gingered and stimulated acquisition of skills. In
Song, the joy is palpable as the forms are available without passing
through hurdles. The talk is PDP and NAPEP only, especially and
particularly, the large troops of the employed, one saw at the
secretarial that day.
But one thing that is interesting in Song local government area of
Adamawa State is the innovation, creativity , inclination)
inventiveness) and resourcefulness of Alhaji Umaru Hammajam, the
chairman.
He has built a viewing centre in each of the eleven wards of the
local government. He provided a television set each in the viewing
centres. He provided a video set and an antennae too. Then, he
provided a generator, a guardsman who is the operator to link the
Adamawa Television in Yola and NT A Yola when they start
transmission.
As from four O'clock every day; the neighbourhood gathers to watch
programmes. This has enlightened them fairly on activities of the
government and they are abreast of developments in Nigeria to the
extent that they are no longer suspicious of visitors or journalists
in particular. They do volunteer information and talk with confidence
unlike during military rule.
Alhaji Umam Hammajam, the executive chairman, who is a learned
Islamic scholar uses this to advantage by producing quality
documentary programmes on his tremendous and remarkable achievements,
clinics, roads, primary schools, unveiling plaque, delivering
speeches and what have you. These are normally beamed to them on
other weeks or when an occasion occurs.
I saw reason in bringing information dissemination closer and nearer
to the people in this strategy which takes note of government
activities.
Finally, he requested the newspaper distributor in Yola to establish
an area office in Song headquarters, for proper accessibility to
dailies, newspapers, magazines and publications.
In all, Song local government area in Adamawa State is a delightful
place to visit The people are innocent and very ready to give you a
helping hand with your broken down car or when in need of pure water,
after an exhaustible energy journey from Salasa to Dirrna, Satellite
towns of the area. There is no doubt an individual who thirsts for
fresh air, a quiet countryside to watch nature in its true form and
beauty will have a blissfully rich visit.
Emmanuel Kwache writes from 30 Gambia crescent, Bekaji estate,
Jimeta-Yola, Adamawa State.

Original_Search_Title: Encourage Use of Shea Butter - Gov't Told
Headline: Ghana: Encourage Use of Shea Butter - Gov't Told
Body_Text:
Koforidua — A Koforidua industrialist and herbal medicine manufacturer, Alhaji
Ahmed Idris, has appealed to the government to assist entrepreneurs
to expand the use of shea butter with the view to improving the
incomes of the millions whose livelihood depends on the wild tree
product in the north.
Idris, also known as Alhaji Awareso, who has for decades been buying
and selling shea butter routed the appeal through our Eastern Region
Bureau at Koforidua.
"After years of trading in the shea butter, I have come to observe
that our poor mothers and brothers who pick the pods and sell them
are earning too little to support them in this era when the cost of
living is getting tougher and tougher".
He, therefore, called for the diversification of the use to which the
butter is put so as to increase its demand and improve its producer
price.
He was optimistic that if researchers and manufacturers co-operate
and are backed with adequate funding, the objective would be realised
in no time.
At the moment, apart from the butter being used as pomade by village
folks and as cooking oil in the northern regions, little use is made
of the Saharan product.
But "we can train our women to extract the edible oil from it in such
a way that it will be palatable, and attractive to the sophisticated
urban dweller as well" Iddris argued, adding that the products even
have export potentials.
Other uses the extracted fat can be put to is hair Vaseline, perfumed
body cream and a variety of lotions that can cure skin decease, he
said.
"There is also no doubt that research can lead to the discovery of
other products that can be derived from the raw material," Alhaji was
optimistic.
The extraction of the butter from the pod remains a job done through
crude and inefficient methods.
According to Iddris, the storage of the butter is equally poorly
done, as lack of modern technology and the adulteration of the stuff
with yam "fufu" and the like expose the butter to fast deterioration.
Already manufacturing talcum and dealing in shea butter, Alhaji
Awareso said he knew many entrepreneurs can take up the challenge if
given the push.

Original_Search_Title: Pete Edochie: Honour For an Artiste
Headline: Nigeria: Pete Edochie: Honour For an Artiste
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ohazulike, at a colourful ceremony he and his council organised to
mark this year's New Yam Festival, honoured a few Igbo sons with
chieftaincy titles.
Recipients of such deserved honours included a renowned home video
artiste, Pete Edochie (a.k.a.
Okonkwo). FIDELIS NJOKU was there and files in this report
In Igboland, it is uncommon to see a person honoured strictly for his
or her moral service to the community or society without any
reasonable material contributions attached to it. This may explain
the reason why honours, traditional or otherwise, are most often
bestowed on money bags in that part of the world regardless of the
source of such material wealth and the characters of the persons
honoured.
Thank God, things change with time. In this generation of the Igbo
race, Ezeigbo, Lagos State, H.R.H. Igwe Hyacinth Ohazulike, tends to
reverse the negative trend. The Igwe Omereoha I of Ndigbo feels like
a few others in Igboland that money is not everything.
Service to one's fatherland, he believes, cannot be measured in terms
of material contributions only.
Therefore, as deserving wealthy citizens are honoured for touching
lives and making economic impact on the society, those who have used
their God-given skills or talents to positively affect the society
also deserve honour.
The Ezeigbo demonstrated just this recently when he honoured at a New
Yam Festival held in his palace at Ifako three Igbo sons who have
made enviable marks in the art industry through home video
productions. The most popular and outstanding among them is Pete
Edochie, now Chief Pete Edochie, the Ebubedike I of Ndigbo.
Until he acted Okonkwo in the drama version of Chinua Achebe's most
popular novel, "Things Fall Apart" Chief Pete Edochie was not quite
visible in the art industry. But with his marvellous debut in the
"Things Fall Apart" drama, Edochie rose suddenly from grace to grace.
His central role as Okonkwo, the protagonist of the book, gave him
out as an artiste par excellence. Those who have read and understood
the character and role of the real Okonkwo as vividly painted by
Prof. Achebe in the novel testify that Edochie did not only play the
vantage part excellently but that his physique also resembles that of
the real Okonkwo being referred to in the book.
It is perhaps on this note that ever since then, Pete Edochie is
called Okonkwo or "Ebubedike" which is also a title name ascribed to
Okonkwo in the novel.
While conferring the title on Edochie and other artistes recently,
Igwe Ohazulike said Igbo artistes like Pete Edochie have done much to
revive Igbo cultural and traditional values. He stated that Edochie
and others in the art industry are doing good work by revisiting
Igbos cherished traditional values and cultural heritage through
their creative art works. The Igwe opined that the value of the art
works of Pete Edochie and others goes beyond mere entertainment. As
far as he is concerned, they go a long way to remind the Igbos of
their root. For their immense service, Pete Edochie and others
deserved to be honoured and encouraged, Igwe Ohazulike stated.
Soon after being conferred with the chieftaincy title of "Ebubedike
of Ndigbo", Arts Parade had a chat with Chief Pete Edochie. Excerpts:
Q: Chief, how do you feel about the chieftaincy titles conferred on
you and others?
A: I feel highly elated and encouraged by the honour done me and my
colleagues today. I think it is a monumental recognition of our
humble moral service to humanity and fatherland. I thank Ezeigbo,
Lagos State for this singular recognition extended to us.
Q: Is acting your part time or main job presently?
A: I am presently a full time professional artiste.
Q: How long have you been in the business?
A: I can't remember exactly how long, but I have been in the business
for quite a long time. You should know that I had been in the
business working underground long before I made breakthroughs and
became noticed in the profession a few years ago.
Q: Which of your works gave you notice?
A: From what I could gather from public opinion, my role as Okonkwo
of "Things Fall Apart" gave me a big mark and attracted unprecedented
public attention to me as an artiste.
Q: Beside your role in the drama version of "Things Fall Apart" which
other work have you played a leading role?
A: A lot! Without sounding immodest, I play major part in any art
work I am involved. So, virtually all the parts I have played so far
in films and other art works can be described as central and pivotal.
Q: Which one are you working on now?
A: I am not just working on one. I and my colleagues are right now
very busy with a few films. One or two of them may be ready in no
distant time from now. Of course, I can't tell you their titles now.
Q: What are they like?
A: Oh! They are fantastic. It is better you see for yourself . As I
said earlier, it will not be long from now by the grace of God.
Q: Chief, what impact do you think your artworks have made on the
society?
A: A lot of impact. We have used art to remind our people of their
root, where they are coming from. We have created and sustained high
tempo awareness of our traditional and cultural heritage among the
city populace most of who know little or nothing about their
tradition and culture. We have this intention of letting the Western
world know that Africa has a rich cultural heritage which deserves
international attention and I think we are already achieving this
goal. Moreso, you can agree with me that African artistes have fairly
succeeded in diverting and refocusing the attention of film watchers
from Western films to African films. By this singular feat, we have
given our people a cultural identity and a sense of belonging in the
continent.
The moral education being imparted on the young and the old through
African-oriented films cannot be over-stressed. Also, the economic
and entertainment value of African art works is unquantifiable.
Frankly, African artistes, be they Igbo, Yoruba or any other
ethnic-based artistes across Africa, including my humble self have
made appreciable impact on the society.
The event of today is an eloquent testimony to what I am saying and
we thank God for granting us the opportunity to play our own small
part to make our fatherland and Africa as a whole great.
Q: Chief, what does it take to be a successful artiste?
A: Talent, hardwork, commitment and perseverance. A good measure of
Western education is also necessary.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers to Use Tricycles
Headline: Ghana: Farmers to Use Tricycles
Body_Text:
Techiman — Five prototype tricycles to facilitate intermediate transport of yams
from farms to marketing centres have been launched at Forikrom in the
Techiman district. Estimated to cost millions of cedis, the
tricycles, the first of its kind in the country, were developed by
Product Development Support of The Netherlands (KIEM) and Ecumenical
Association for Sustainable Agriculture and Rural
Development(ECASARD) with support from the Konrad Adenaur Foundation
(KAF)
The tricycles, which will be on trial for about one year, would be
mass produced for farmers and export, if they prove successful.
Launching it, Mr. Isaac Osei-Antwi, DCE for Techiman, commended
ECASARD and KIEM of The Netherlands for their initiative in
introducing the tricycles to the Ghanaian farmers and Techiman in
particular.
Mr. Osei-Antwi intimated that with the introduction of the tricycles,
the burden of farmers, especially women, who have to carry large
loads of farm produce on their heads over long distances, would be
reduced.
He assured farmers that under the Poverty Alleviation Programme, more
funds would be allocated to those of them who form viable
co-operative societies to enable them expand their farms.
The national co-ordinator of ECASARD, Dr. King David Amoah, disclosed
that ECASARD is a network of some 98 non-governmental organisations
(NGOs) and community based organisations established in 1991.
Amoah noted that the network was established in response to a felt
need by NGOs that are committed to promoting sustainable agriculture
and to share resources, information and expertise so as to empower
rural farmers.
Mr. Bernard Y. Guri, programme officer of KAF, said the feedback of
the test of the tricycles will enable the designers and engineers in
Holland come out with final products.
Guri added that ECASARD and KIEM of Holland with support from KAF are
looking up to the possibility of setting up a company, Rural
Enterprise for Agro-Logistics, to locally manufacture tricycles at
relatively cheap cost instead of importing.
Mr. Kay Kizito, Brong Ahafo Regional trade officer of the Ministry of
Trade and Industries, stressed the need for linkages between
ministries of Agriculture and Trade and Industry to help solve
problems of farmers in the areas of agro-technology and marketing.
He called on farmers, especially yam producers, to add value to their
products to enable them get good prices to boost Ghana's foreign
trade.
In a welcome address, Nana Kwaw Adams, the Brong Ahafo Regional
co-ordinator of ECASARD, said the Techiman district was chosen for
the test of the tricycles since it is a leading producing and
marketing centre of yams and other food crops in the country.
Nana Akomea Sakye, Akwamuhene of Forikrom, who presided, gave the
assurance that the chiefs and people of Forikrom would make land
readily available if they choose to establish the tricycle factory in
the district.

Original_Search_Title: Full Stomach, Empty Stomach
Headline: Nigeria: Full Stomach, Empty Stomach
Body_Text:
Bad news, United Nations Children's Fund, UNICEF says Nigerians are not eating well; quantity yes, quality, no
Nigerians are among the most malnourished in the world.   Nigeria occupies 15th position among nations with the highest mortality rate. According to the United Nations Children's Fund, UNICEF, statistics 187 out of every 1,000 Nigerian children die before the age of five.
But the Nigerian government is trying to change all that. The federal government in collaboration with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture, IITA; UNICEF and United States Agency for International Development, USAID, is working on a project to increase nutritional value of some of the nations' staple foods. Based on a proposal submitted by the IITA, the government is working with donor agencies on how to improve nutrients in maize, cassava and yam, staple foods in   Nigeria  .
To start with, the project is focusing on maize. Government officials are working with employees of IITA, as well as their counterparts in UNICEF and USAID, to conduct a food consumption and nutrition survey across the country. The survey is covering 12 states namely Borno, Taraba, Nasarawa,   Kaduna  ,   Kano  , Kebbi, Osun,  Edo , Kwara, Bayelsa, Akwa Ibom and   Imo  States  . In all the states, 72 local governments, covering 216 enumeration areas, are being randomly selected for the programme. Newswatch learnt that the surveyors would visit 6,480 households randomly selected to represent the whole nation and study their feeding habits.
The study, it is believed, would help the government to formulate food and nutrition policies; help to provide education programmes on food and nutrition; set agenda on food security; promote health and welfare of the people.
Lukas Brader, director-general of IITA for 11 years, who is retiring from the services of the institute at the end of November, told Newswatch that it was one of the programmes introduced by the institute to help sub-Saharan nations eat healthy food and maximise their nutrients intake from local food stuffs. "It is a way of helping the governments to realise their set objectives to provide quality and nutrients food for the public," Brader said.
He said in the last four years, nutrition had been made part and parcel of the IITA research. According to him, the main plan was to reduce malnutrition and poverty through income generation.
He said the fact that   Nigeria   has no good food policy, made the programme necessary. Brader warned that   Nigeria   would require more than twice as much food items as it produces now. The nation would also need a greater commitment to agricultural produce before it could have food security.
Busie Maziya-Dixon, who heads the IITA nutrition department, initiated the project, the first of its kind in  Africa . She told Newswatch that the survey would include taking the blood sample of respondents in order to see the level of the micronutrient in their blood. Ninety-six interviewers, 12 state supervisors, four zonal coordinators, four top IITA staff and four scientists are involved in the project.
"In each village, we randomly select 30 households. We are focusing on women of childbearing age and children. They are the most vulnerable age. Our target is to improve the nutrient value of maize to 30 percent," Maziya-Dixton told Newswatch. She said the choice of maize was based on presence of vitamin A in the yellow maize and because maize was commonly used as the weaning food in   Nigeria  .
"The focus of the project is to improve the nutritional status of the people through development of maize varieties with high level micronutrient. The micronutrient we are interested in is pro-vitamin A, which is contained in yellow maize. The second part is to work with processors, i.e. millers in small and small-scale level, to see if it would be visible for them to practise fortification of maize flours for this micronutrient," Maziya-Dixon said.
Maziya-Dixon told Newswatch that a scientist would be sent to the   United States   training for one year, to help to see if the micronutrient would be available once the maize varieties had been developed. The survey, she said, would tell investigators how much nutrients the people get from the food they eat. The programme is going to last for three years.
UNICEF report indicates that one third of people in the sub-Saharan  Africa  are malnourished, thereby affecting their motivation, attention, curiosity and cognitive abilities. "Malnutrition and repeated infections that accompany it produce a lethal lethargy that can prevent a young child from enjoying the care he or she deserves," the UNICEF report says. UNICEF believes that malnutrition is responsible for nearly half of all child deaths. It is also said to be responsible for "low birth-weight, recurrent illnesses, a lack of psychosocial stimulation, poor nutrition and the stresses of poverty (that) can lead to poor health and a general lowering of intellectual, behavioural and social abilities."

Original_Search_Title: The Poet Returns
Headline: Nigeria: The Poet Returns
Body_Text:
Lagos — Professor Niyi Osundare is a lecturer at the Department of English
Studies, University of Ibadan. A writer, columnist and a poet of
international acclaim. Osundare has won many awards, including the prestigious Overall
Joint-Winner of Commonwealth Poetry Prize, 1986; Noma Award USA, 1991
and the Association of Nigerian Authors Award, 1986. He returned to
Nigeria a couple of weeks ago after about four years sojourn abroad.
In this interview with Kunle Owolabi, he spoke extensively on the
underlying philosophy of his literary output and his activities while
in the United States of America. Excerpts:
Q: Looking at the concept of your literary works, one notices that
they are mainly poetry at the expense of other genres. What accounts
for this?
A: Oh! Well, I don't know whether you've really looked at the entire
corpus. Poetry is my first passion and self engagement quite right.
But I certainly have tried my hands on other genres. Remember, since
1986, I have been writing for Newswatch, writing columns, doing
interviews, writing essays and writing travelogues. All these are
important genres of literature. I mean look at the 18th century, when
we talked about the poetry of Alexander Pope and John Dryden. We also
talked about the journalism of Addison and Steel, even the journalism
of the most intellectual giant of that period, Samuel Johnson. And in
an university today, both in Europe and America, the journalism of
Adison and Steel as published in Spectator or the Rambler, I think
that was the one Samuel Johnson ran or the Tatler. The content of
these media are being studied with seriousness and they revealed so
much about the England of that time, especially literarily,
culturally, and politically at that time. So, the journalistic aspect
of my output, I consider as important as the so-called purely
artistic output. Many people dismiss journalism as literature that
rarely couldn't realise the status of defined letters. I think there
is a lot about that that is wrong. Journalism is a steady genre on
its own, solid and steady and I have never seen a country where
serious letters do not draw heavily on the so-called journalistic
letters, between the two is a symbiotic relationship.
So, I am fair when I write my poetry, I also write poems that are
journalistic. Which was why for five years, I held a poetry column in
some Nigerian Newspapers. And I still do it anyway. I rested it for a
while. In fact, right now, I am working on some, it depends on how
ideas come to me. So, it is hard concentrating on poetry, but if you
look at the poetry very well, you will know that it is a multifaceted
kind of poetry that I am doing. Take the Eye of the Earth for example
and compare it with Songs of the Field or take Moon songs and compare
it with Songs of the Market Place. I have also done some works on
drama. The irony of it all is that I started out with drama. In 1979,
I wrote a play "The Man Who Walked Away", which was commissioned by
the NTA then as one of the major events marking 20 years of
Television in Africa. I had just returned from Canada and I was
staying at the University Guest House when a friend, Moyo Ogundipe, a
fine artist and an old classmate, came to me and told me about the
project he had and I wrote that play, which went on Television and
was quite successful. About two years later, in 1981, I wrote The
State Visit, but I just put it away, it didn't see the stage until
1997. It was one of the activities marking my 50th anniversary. Since
then, I have written two others- The wedding Car and The Man Who
Refused To Say Yes.
Q: Are the plays published?
A: Three of them are in the press. The State Visit is coming out on
the paper back of Craft Books and two of the others plays - The
Wedding Car and The Man Who Walked Away are due out soon by African
University Press Plc, Ibadan. The other one, I am still hanging on
to.
So, I may not be as diversified as it would have been, but I have
been trying my hands certainly on other things.
Q: When one looks at your works particularly in the Eye of the Earth
where nature and orature are celebrated. Does this inspiration come
from observation of nature or do you worship nature like the Romantic
poets of the 18th century?
A: I am too much of a radical humanist to worship nature. I don't
worship nature but I respect nature deeply. I cherish nature; I
glorify in it because that is the beginning, the Alpha and Omega of
humanity. I am happy the way you've linked my collections with
nature, my concentration on nature with my exponents of Orature. It
is not just a morphological game that both words end in "ture."
Nature, Orature. These two are very important to me. Let's begin with
nature. I believe that nature is a perfect artist and everything we
are trying to do as human beings is an attempt to approximate the
majestic prowess of nature. I was born in Ikere Ekiti, one of the
most rocky places in the world. Everywhere you stand in Ikere, you
will see a rock. Whether it is Olosunta or Orole or Ugele or at my
father's farm, you have rocks all over the place.
You have to scrap up together to make moulds to cultivate yams and on
our way to that farm is River Osun, the huge river, which swells in
July and August and threatens our houses and our farms and withdraws
very quietly into its bank in other months, looking at us, supplying
all the water we need either for drinking or for the irrigation of
our farm. In August, Osun is brown, copper brown, the current is
powerful. It is angry, but when the heavy rains are over, what you
see is a very quiet, almost quietescent river, friendly, extremely
friendly. I have grown to appreciate water, as the river, but I have
grown to marvel at the ambiguity of water. Osun is not just a river,
it is not just a goddess, Osun is all those things and it is also the
spirit of justice. I grew up in an oral culture. I grew up seeing my
father composing songs and my mother forming songs. My father
couldn't work on his farm without singing. In fact, the singing
helped his farming. My mother would sit at the loom, the vertical
loom, the one you fixed against the wall. And while she passes the
shuttle and run in the thread she will be singing. I grew up seeing
women rocking babies in their hands singing all kinds of songs. And I
saw their babies cooing and responding to these songs. When their
babies became infants, I saw them singing childhood songs. And why
they became adults, I saw them taking part in festivals. Our lives,
were completely surrounded by songs, by drums, by a kind of artistic
execution of the word. The Yoruba talk about "Ewa Ete" i.e the beauty
of the lips. That is the beauty and integrity of the word as it comes
out of the human mouth.
Literacy is extremely important, but my policy is that we shouldn't
allow literacy to take all of these away from it. The more successful
you are as a writer, the more you are likely to depend upon the
spoken words. I mean, look at the work of the Asian writer, or the
Latin American writer. When you read Garcia McCay - the Colombian
novelist for example- I mean you can hear the cadence of the oral
sentence or Carols Suentes ( the Mexican novelist), when I read his
prose what comes to my mind or to my ears immediately, is the poetry
of Octavio Pas, the compatriot of McCay.
Good prose is good poetry. You can rarely execute good prose without
a lot of poetic integrity in it.
So, I will come to Nigeria here. How do you typify or typologise Wole
Soyinka, a poet, a dramatist, a prose writer. I mean, basically a
poet and he's basically a talking poet. What makes Death and the
King's Horseman so irresitable is the presence of the spoken words in
all the lines. When I teach Death and the King's Horseman in the U.S,
I tell my students to sing the lines. It is like an opera; like a
Yoruba Opera composed in English. You know, or the work of Ola Rotimi
- "You turn here Kurunmi, You turn there Kurunmi, Kurunmi, Kurunmi,
Kurunmi, Haba!." I mean you can see or the tortoise is going on a
journey, we say, tortoise, when will you be back and tortoise will
say "not until I have been disgraced, disgraced, disgraced, not until
I have been disgraced." I mean, what do you call that. It is written
in prose because it is in verse. It is so poetic, but it is the
source of music.
Q: May be, we can as well say that those are the poets who motivate
you.
A: Yes, my first motivators were oral artists, my father was one of
them, his colleagues - some composers and singers and those who put
festivals in Ikere together - Egungun festival, Ogunoye festival,
Olosunta festival, Egbereode festival, Oliki festival, Osun festival.
A typical Yoruba festival is not complete without songs and there can
be no song without poetry and music. This is really it.
Q: Most often than not, one discovers that you solidarise with the
oppressed, the disposed, the down-trodden and believe in these
people's revolutionary power. All these are traits peculiar to
socialist ideology. But do we seem to meet Osundare the politician or
Osundare that is totally committed to the arts and arts only?
A: You can't differentiate between the two. If you are asked to
divide me into two, you would find it difficult to know where to put
knife. It is Osundare the socialist humanist who is Osundare the
artist, Osundare the father, Osundare the husband, Osundare the son,
Osundare the farmer's son, Osundare the little boy born in Ikere
Ekiti also Osundare who is a child of the world. Osundare the
preacher (if you like); the totality of what I would say is Osundare
the human being.
Q: Before you conclude. I observe something from Don Buness article.
He said the theme of hope is important in Osundare's poetry. In
reality, do you still dream of a hope and a glorious dawn for
Nigeria?
A: Oh! Yes. Remember in Noma Award acceptance speech in Harare in
1991, I did say that a human being could live for a couple of days
without food, water, but no human being can live for a second without
hope. Hope eternal, hope is extremely important; it is what nourishes
us. It is what makes it possible for us to say: after all this
darkness, the sun shall shine again. After this tunnel, we shall see
the light.
Q: Some time ago, you reacted to the issue of brain drain. When your
colleagues were going abroad you remained at home. But all of a
sudden, you also left the shores the country. Do it mean the economic
condition then was unbearable no longer?
A: Oh! Well, I remember my departure in 1997 suddenly became
front-page news in a couple of newspapers and remember I also
reacted. I didn't leave because of economic or political situation;
if I wanted to leave, 1997 was not the time I should have left. I
didn't leave in Babangida's time; I didn't leave when he annulled the
election; I didn't leave when Abacha just came in; you know when we
were being pushed from pillar to post; I didn't leave because of the
ASUU strike, the clampdown on the so-called radicals in these
universities on lecturers. I didn't say because I didn't see any
reasons I should shy; the future of Nigerian universities, away. No,
I left for personal reasons. I said this, time and time again. We
have a daughter who is deaf and so gifted. She is an extremely gifted
girl and a lovely human being. We went everywhere, she couldn't go
beyond primary three in Nigeria. Nothing for her because there was
nobody to take care of her and she was wasting away, she became very
uncooperative. I had to take permission from the university and go
away. When we got to the U.S; you should have seen the way experts
rallied round us. Took this girl in a high school- this is a girl who
never completed primary education anyway. So, there was a lot of
distortion but she's a girl of immense intelligence and energy and
courage. She saw this through and when she sees this through,
educationally and medically she is being taken care of. So, I left
her not out of my own volition, but because I wanted to save somebody
that could serve Nigeria in the future. Nigeria was going to destroy
that girl. I wouldn't like to see her staying by the roadside with a
bowl in her hands and selling her disability to people so that they
could drop a few penny in her bowl.

Original_Search_Title: Massacre At Gbeji Village
Headline: Nigeria: Massacre At Gbeji Village
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Dennis Gbeji, a pensioner, lost fifteen members of his family in the
invasion of Gbeji village on October 22 by soldiers.
The emoluments of Mr. Dennis Gbeji, a pensioner who retired from the
services of the Benue state government can hardly take him and his
family beyond a week. He relies on his family back at Gbeji village
in Ukum local government area for food stuff to augment his little
retirement benefits that are hardly paid on time.
His father, Zwakor Gbeji never hesitates to come to his son's help.
As a successful village farmer, the senior Gbeji considered such
gesture to his first son as a thing of pride. To the Gbeji family,
whose patriarch founded Gbeji village several decades ago, this
robust relationship between Dennis and his family has been his only
source of hope since he retired from his unrewarding civil service
job some years back.
This hope was shattered when on October 22, soldiers invaded Gbeji
village, killing everybody in sight including 14 members of Zwakor
Gbeji's family. Dennis and his son, Teragu Gbeji, are now the only
surviving members of the Gbeji family.
Dennis told TheNEWS that the soldiers first made an appearance in
Gbeji village on Saturday 20 October in a convoy of four armoured
cars and nine personnel carriers. "They told the villagers that they
were on a peace mission at the instance of Governor George Akume."
Having explained their mission to elders, the villagers immediately
took the responsibility of hosting their guests whom they considered
as their protectors from the menacing Jukun militia. He narrated that
they prepared food and bought drinks for them and even gave them
tubers of yams.
"After the Saturday merriments, the major who led the team appealed
to the Gbeji people to cooperate with his men who will be assigned to
the village for peace keeping operation. Before he left for
Zaki-Ibiam, the local government headquarters he demanded to know the
village's market day which fell on Monday, 22 October.
He promised to be back on the market day for another meeting with the
elders of Gbeji and Tse Gube, a neighbouring village. He told them
that a market day will be ideal as most of the villagers would be
present when he will be addressing them, Dennis narrated.
Although the people of Gbeji have of recent been scared of soldiers,
because some Jukun militia are said to be hiding under military
uniforms to attack them, the friendly disposition of this contingent
said to have been sent by their Governor, Mr. George Akume did not
provide any room for suspicion.
Using military psychology, the major and his men were able to get
sufficient confidence of the villagers to blow the vision of any
soothsayer from seeing the danger ahead. The village head, elders,
women, men, youth and children were all looking up to the military
men as a Salvation Army and waited anxiously for the meeting.
When the soldiers came on the appointed day, nothing had changed in
their expression except that instead of four armoured tanks and nine
personnel carriers they came with three armoured tanks and seven
personnel carriers. Even the reduction in the number of vehicles did
not attract suspicion from any of the villagers who gathered at the
market square for the all-important address of the troop commander.
Unknown to the villagers, the missing amoured tank and two personnel
vehicles had been strategically stationed around the village to
prevent anybody from escaping from an attack. The tank was stationed
along Afia Road, an exit route from Gbeji. Unsuspecting villagers
continued to work according to the script of the well-rehearsed plan
for the trap set for them by the soldiers to the last minute.
At the square, the major asked all women and children to leave the
venue of the meeting as the discussion was for adult males alone.
Seeing that a good number of the men in the village where present at
the so-called briefing, Dennis narrated, "the major told the
villagers that he was going to call the officer that will address
them." On getting to the end of the village where the two personnel
carriers were parked, he was said to have ordered that all the
commercial motorcycle operators (Okada) at the junction be killed.
The strategy was to prevent them from escaping to nearby villages to
alert their kinsmen of what was happening in Gbeji. This was at about
3p.m on 22 October the same time the soldiers allegedly killed by Tiv
militia were being buried in Abuja.
The sound of the persistent gunshots alerted the villagers that they
were face-to-face with death. But it was too late as their
friend-turned-foes ordered them to lie face down. "They poured petrol
on them and set them ablaze," Dennis said amidst sobs.
While a group of soldiers watched menacingly over those being
consumed by fire, others moved their armoured tanks around the
village shooting women and children who were fleeing into the bush.
The operation which Dennis said lasted for one hour fifty minutes
left over 140 people dead.
Gbeji presents the highest casualty figure of all the places in the
three local governments of Ukuru, Katsina-Ala and Logo attacked by
soldiers. And the family of Zwakor Gbeji had the misfortune of having
the highest number of deaths amongst the families in the military
vengeance mission on the Tiv people.
Fifteen of them were killed.
According to Dennis, members of Gbeji family massacred by soldiers on
Monday 22 October included his father Zwakor Gbeji, Uwuhe Gebji,
Pinega Atakera Gbeji, Terhemen Gbeji, Tyohemba Gbeji, Shiaondo Gbeji,
and Msugh Gbeji. Others were; Terkimbi Abuul Gbeji, Terzungwe Tiga,
Msugh Iwambe, Mwase Sonkpen, Mrs.
Ternenge Mtseakaa, Kpaju Mbaveluior, Mzagh Iwambe and Terzugwe Akiga.
After the massacre in Gbeji, the soldiers moved into other Tiv
villages leading to the destruction of the house of Lt-General Victor
Malu the immediate past Chief of Army Staff at Tse-Adoor village. For
Dennis Zwakor, the invasion of the soldiers has not only destroyed
his village, his local government and his family it has also made him
hopeless.

Original_Search_Title: Tiv, Jukun; A Catalogue of Crises
Headline: Nigeria: Tiv, Jukun; A Catalogue of Crises
Body_Text:
The perennial crisis threathening the peaceful co-existence of Nigeria recently erupted again between Jukun and Tiv across the Benue/Taraba axis.
In the crisis, not less than 19 solders of the Nigerian Army were killed.
The present crisis is not an isolated incident. Conflict and rivalry between the Tiv and the Jukun predates the foundation of the modern Nigerian state. Even during the time of Lord Lugard, the first Governor-General of Nigeria, the colonial regime had difficulty subduing the Tiv but with the Jukun, they (the British) encountered very little problem. The British fought series oif battles with the Tiv before they (the Tiv) could be subdued.
Also it is important to note that the Tiv had allied with the Jukun to fight the Hausa/Fulani in the past but over time an interface of circumstances engendered enmity and distrust between them, that is the Tiv and the Jukun by Dr Tesemchi Makar, a Tiv historian, in his book, A Short History of the Tiv People gave an account of the circumstances that brought about the antagonism between the two groups.
He notes that on January 1, 1900 all Tiv in Benue, including those in the defunct Muri Province were placed under one administration and in August 1900 Tiv districts north and south of Benue River, westward from Katsina-Ala were administered from Akwanaja in Nassarawa District, Lower Benue Province.
Also, In 1901 another administrative change saw some Tiv districts in Lower Benue Province merged with the newly created Muri Province. Accoirding to Makar, this was the first time Wukari, Takum and Donga Districts of the defunct Ibi Division were being administered together with the Tiv. The remaining part of Lower Benue Valley, including Tiv groups were renamed Nassarawa Province, with headquarters at Nassarawa, capital of the present Nassarawa State.
In 1904, another change took place. This time the southern Tiv, along with Idoma (in Benue State) and Igala (now in Kogi State) districts were administered from Obudu in the defunct Ogoja Province, now a part of Cross River State and this lasted till 1914. (It should be noted that from 1901, all Tiv were no longer being administered under one administration. They have now been split up and annexed variously with non-Tiv.
In 1914 following the amalgamation of the northern and southern protectrates, the southern Tiv became part of the Northern Nigerian Government. That means they now rejoined their kith and kin who had been in Muri Province since 1901.
In 1918 Munshi area, originally a Division administered from Abinsi, the headquarters, and Katsina Ala, were transferred from Muri Province and absorbed into Bassa Munshi Province. During this exercise, Ibi Division comprising Wukari, Takum and Donga Districts, among others, remained in Muri Province.
In 1926, following another major administrative change, Benue Province was created and the newly constituted independent Tiv districts were transferred to Ibi Division and placed under the jurisdiction of the Aku Uka, the paramount ruler of the Jukun people in Wukari. The Tiv resented this action and ever since Wukari Division has not known peace. Wukari is said to be the traditional home of the Jukun people.
Disturbances among the Tiv can be classified into two categories. The first category used to be disputes between lineage groups over farmlands, markets etc. It was generally limited by lineage system. Today, this kind of dispute can, and do extend to non-Tiv, especially those who share these facilities with them. This explains why there are disputes involving Tiv and Jukun, Chamba and Fulani (Taraba); Alago, Eggon and Koro (Nassarawa); Ankwe (Goemei)and Kwalla (Piateau); Udam and Ogoja (Cross River); and Idoma, Egede, Jukun and Etulu (Benue). These disputes are mostly over farmiand.
The second category belongs to the uprising which essentially is a violent response directed against constituted authority, and those who, in the eyes of Tiv, are deemed to have abused power vested in them. Given a conducive atmosphere this type of uprising can spread like bush fire over large area.
The early period of British occupation of Tiv land up to the late forties, witnessed a number of protest rnovements such as: 1912 (yov), 1929 (Haakaa), Ivase (Undated), |1939 (Nyamboa), 1945 (Kumendur) and 1948 (Hoyoj. All these are protest movements directed against the British and their agents like clan or kindred heads, tax collectors, judges, policemen, scribes, messengers and particularly where there have been abuses of power.
The protest movements were not restricted to Tiv land, but they extended to Jukun land as well.
The Haakaa and Nyambua riots which took place in 1929 and 1939 respectively led to violent uprising, coincided with the periods of acute famine in Tiv land. As a result, many Tiv were said to have poured into Jukun land.
There was the Uva Va'ase episode of 1954 in which the Tiv in Wukari Division, were said to have been incited by their brothers from Tiv Division. They organised an uprising against the constituted authority demanding for a separate Native Authority, during which many lives and property were destroyed. They further demanded to usurp the position of the Aku Uka. Led by Uva Va'ase, the Tiv marched to the palace of Aku Uka and demanded that Manu Va'ase be installed as Aku Uka.
The 1 960s were eventful in further straining relations between the two groups, with series of unrests in Tiv Division which later spilled to the land of their neighbours, the Jukun.
For example, when political parties were formed to usher in the First Republic, the Hausa/Fulani, whom the Tiv love to hate belonged to the Northern Peoples Congress (NPC), the party that eventually formed the government of Northern Region in 1960, with Ahmadu Bello, the Sardauna of Sokoto as Premier. The Tiv, on the other hand, belonged to the United Middle Belt Congress (UMBC) which was founded by Joseph Sarwuan Tarka, one of their own. The UMBC became a dominant opposition party in the North. Rivalry between the NPC and UMBC was so tense that their supporters within Tiv Division were always engaging themselves in violent upheavals, arson and murder. The sum total of this act was code-named atem tiyo (head breaking). It got to a point where even non-Tiv supporters of NPC outside Tiv Division were not spared by Tiv UMBC supporters. The Jukun were largely sympathetic to NPC.
The year 1964 was to witness another round of Tiv violence. Again, the Jukun found themselves at the receiving end, with heavy losses to life and property. Some people believe that this riot precipitated the 1966 military coup in Nigeria.
In 1992, there was another bloody crisis between the two ethnic groups.
Though the Tiv are predominantly in Benue State with Gboko, where the Tor Tiv, the traditional ruler of the Tiv kingdom, resides as their traditional capital, they are found in large numbers in Nassarawa State apart from Wukari, which is a major town in Taraba State.
What then is the cause of these conflicts? The distrust between the two ethnic groups is deep rooted and this has even pitched the Benue State government with governments of other states where the Tiv are indigenes. According to THISDAY investigations, there are so many reasons why the conflicts keep reoccuring. One is that the Benue State Government at various times, tends to take undue interest in any dispute Tiv residing in Taraba State and other non-Tiv by supporting the Tiv outrightly, despite that such issue is exclusively the internal affairs of Taraba State.
In the same vein, the Tor Tiv and other prominent Tiv sons in Benue State in showing filial solidarity to Tiv indigenes of other states, aggravate whatever crisis there is.
The Jukun are afraid of being dominated by the Tiv. Investigations revealed that the people of Nassarawa, Keffi, Lafia, Wukari, Idoma Division in former Benue Province, had a bitter experience when seven parliamentary seats meant for the Province were all snatched by Tiv Division, on the basis of population. A similar scenario also happened in the defunct Wukari Division in 1959 when, with the active connivance from Tiv Division, many voters came to swell the population of the Tiv and voted one of their own to the Federal legislature.
There is the issue of renaming villages. The Tiv are being accused of changing names of villages in Jukun areas as soon they (the Tiv) constitute a sizeable population to reflect their own identity. An example frequently cited is Tsokundi, a Jukun town, which was changed to Ayu, a name of an early Tiv immigrant to the area. The other is Dan Anacha, formerly known as Kwarrarafa, now named after a spicie of Tiv yam being widely cultivated in the area.
The frequent mounting of road blocks any time there is dispute between the two ethnic groups is equally seen as one thing that escalates the conflict. It is said that any moment a dispute occurs between Tiv and non-Tiv in Wukari, road block is mounted along the common boundary with Taraba State where they stop vehicles are stopped, searched and innocent people are killed. Such an action attracts immediate retaliation, leading to wanton killing and destruction of property. In the case of the present crisis the burning down of a border town, Chonku in Taraba State brought about a prompt retaliatory action from the Jukun, who in turn burnt Jootar, a border village in Benue State.
Former Lagos State Commissioner of Police, Alhaji Abubakar Tsav, himself a Tiv, had cause to raise alarm recently when he wrote to the Benue State Commissioner of Police and the State Security Service in Makurdi. He maintained: "In 19964 the Tiv people engaged themselves in a senseless violent conflict in what was generally regarded as politically motivated riots. Many people lost their lives while property of unestimated value were destroyed.
The Tiv culture went away with these disturbances. Many believe that the Tiv riot of 1964 was one of the reasons for the military takeover of government in nigeria. Since then, the Tiv people have at various times either engaged themselves or their neighbours in violent conflicts over land resulting in the wanton loss of precoius lives and property."
Then Alahji Tsav raised other issues which have not been given much attention, the role of ex-servicemen in these conflicts and the 2003 factor.
He noted; "it is reliably understood that most ppoliticians from different camps in the state have started arming themselves with offensive and deadly weapoms for the 2003 general election campaigns. Our people need employment food, shelter and medical care and not sponsored thugery".
The question is, for how long will the Tiv and the Jukun continue to tear themselves apart?
Something has to be done urgently.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo: Commander in-Chief of Aso Rock?
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo: Commander in-Chief of Aso Rock?
Body_Text:
"I am happy that the armed forces have not played into the hands of
the perpetrators and remain calm and at the same time (are) accepting
the situation as occupational hazard ... No amount of provocation
will make us abandon this desirable, needed and noble cause" -
President Olusegun Obasanjo, October 22, 2001.
WHEN President Obasanjo made this pronouncement during the burial
ceremony for the 19 soldiers killed in Benue State allegedly by Tiv
youths, the Commander-in-Chief almost sent me to the hospital; I
almost choked on the Gulder, (for me its Gulder or Guinness, to keep
my sanity in Nigeria), in my mouth at the time. Obasanjo has acquired
a reputation as a funny fellow who could have made a distinguished
career as a comedian of high rank if the army had not got him first.
But praising the Nigerian army for self_restraint when tempers were
still high was deadly comedy revealing how far removed the C-in-C was
from the forces he is presumed to command. So, with certainty, I told
a colleague after recovering from fits of coughing that the President
would soon eat his words. The Nigerian army the rest of us have known
since Obasanjo's friend Major Chukwuma Nzeogwu led the first coup can
be accused of many things (usually evil) but restraint is not one of
them. I knew that Tivs would soon pay dearly for the 19 lives the
army lost. The army, predictably struck back as soon as the bodies of
their fallen colleagues were lowered into the grave, giving the
C-in-C a slap in the face and went in to slaughter 200 or more in
reprisals. They even gave former Chief of Army Staff a taste of their
deadly medicine
The reasons why the massacre occurred despite the C-in-C's thinly
veiled plea to soldiers in the statement above are not hard to
discover: The Nigerian army which General Obasanjo and his Chief of
Army Staff, Lt. Gen.
Theophilus Danjuma (rtd) left behind in 1979, which by itself was a
lawless bunch from the topmost to the last recruit had, gradually
disintegrated to the point where a former Chief of Army Staff, Lt.
Gen. Salisu in departing, described it as an "army where nothing
goes". The horror show at the Oputa Panel has revealed the lower
depths to which the Nigerian Army had descended under IBB, Abacha and
Abubakar.
Thus, the army Obasanjo re-inherited in 1999 was nothing but the same
bunch of undisciplined "gangsters" (apologies to Lt. Gen. Alani
Akinrinade (rtd) his successors had groomed to terrorise civilians
and to commit genocide, if necessary, in order to ensure their hold
on power.
Granted that the Nigerian Army had not always been cohesive and
honourable according to a book written by Maj. Gen. James Oluleye
(rtd), now being serialized by Nigerian Tribune. In fact it would
appear that military discipline departed from the Nigeria armed
forces the minute the last British soldier departed and Colonel
Ironsi was made the Chief of Army Staff instead of Colonel
Ademulegun. The coup that toppled Ironsi was perhaps the first
demonstration of how degenerate the army had become in a few years.
The detachment of soldiers led by Maj.Theophilus Danjuma, the current
Minister of Defence, already had Ironsi (then Commander in Chief)
cornered with his host Colonel Adekunle Fajuyi; they could have asked
the man to surrender and put them in detention the way Babangida did
with Buhari; they could even have sent them on exile. Instead,
General Ironsi and Colonel Fajuyi were thoroughly beaten to the point
of death by junior officers before Danjuma reportedly shot Ironsi.
Army discipline has not improved since that fateful day. Quite the
contrary, it has gone from bad to worse.
Today there is no discipline. An officer and a gentleman is an
oxymoron in Nigeria, there is no gentleman soldier. That was why,
Obasanjo's grave side address and the implied plea for restraint
which if made by the President of any great country, instead of a
Banana Republic, would have been accepted as an order. But not the
Nigerian Army.
The last four or five Chiefs of Army Staff rose through the ranks of
an army where promotion was not determined by competence but by the
ability to successfully gang up against superior officers and push
them out of office.
Murderers, rapists, safe crackers have reached high ranks in the
Nigerian Army whose armed chair Generals and Brigadiers cannot
withstand an Israeli captain in combat. So, the army ignored
Obasanjo's plea for restraint and went on a rampage as if the C-in-C
had spoken only for himself because in fact he did. And when the
C-in-C ordered them to leave Benue, one saucy officer proclaimed that
they would not withdraw until they fish out the culprits. Apparently
after killing 200 people in retaliation, our blood thirsty soldiers
still want more blood.
Meanwhile, the President who once prematurely praised them for
self-restraint has found some virtue in retaliation. According to him
people who shoot soldiers are asking for trouble. Well, we knew that
all along and we don't need a President to remind us. In fact we can
tell the President that people need not kill a soldier for the armed
gang he leads as C-in-C to commit murder. Ikorodu town in Lagos State
recently experienced army brutality when soldiers went on rampage on
account of a matter that should have been handled by the police but
which was not because it involved soldiers.
Till today, if a hit-and-run driver kills a soldier near an Army
Barracks, everybody in the vicinity should run for cover, even
innocent passers by are not spared. Obasanjo, like his predecessors
might be C-in-C of Aso Rock but his influence beyond the gates of the
Rock over the armed forces is doubtful. And he has the embodiment of
military indiscipline in 1966 as his Defence Minister.
N200 million Media Tour
"First things last" apparently is the motto of some of our honourable
ministers. The Ministry of Information finally released the cost of
the media tour which according to one source was N1.7 billion and
according to another N200 million. Even if the lower figure is
accepted, in the spirit of transparency and accountability, the
honourable minister should at least circulate to all the media houses
the break down of the expenditure. It will be interesting to discover
who got what during the loot sharing. I can bet a Naira that
journalists got the short end of the stick, ministry officials and
food contractors got more.
Still N200 million to undertake in earnest a task which should have
been left to the media itself is not the best way to spend N200
million. FUTO like all Nigerians universities needs a micro film unit
to enable the university store in its library more reading materials
and preserve them from pilferage and damage and to conserve space;
one unit (tokunbo) goes for about N500,000. All Nigerian universities
and polytechnics could use one and N200 million would get 400 units.
To me that's a better way to spend the money than through a media
jamboree designed to lure the tourists to sleep. How many boreholes
nationwide? How about filling potholes on the Onitsha Owerri Road,
Oga minister?
Now a minister in a government where officials have been "invited to
come and chop"might be forgiven for not knowing that you don't have
to blow the budget to sedate journalists. Why? for under N2 million,
I can send all Nigerian journalists to bed on Gulder and pounded yam
at the Kirikiri Canal. If the Gulder does not get them, the lumpy
pounded yam will finish them.
Last word on Governor Adefarati's accident
We received a rejoinder from Mr. Pady Dare, Chief Press Secretary to
Governor Adefarati of Ondo State disputing my article of October 21,
2001.
The rejoinder was erroneously sent to Ambassador Sagay because he
writes on Page 9.
As the real author of the report, I want to observe that Mr. Pady
Dare added no new information to what the governor himself had
stated. I was on the scene of the accident and I interviewed those
who witnessed the actual impact. I repeat without fear that the dead
young Kogi pharmacist was the victim not the cause of the ghastly
accident in which he died.
What motive would I have for maligning Chief Adefarati who is a
Yoruba leader and I am from the same tribe whereas the dead young man
is unknown to me? Truth must be told because above all, I know we
shall all die one day. The late Kogien youth is not around to defend
himself. It will be a shame though if some of us allow our conscience
to die before our bodies go six feet under out of deference to
transient holders of power.

Original_Search_Title: Media Tour: The Undeserved Vituperation
Headline: Nigeria: Media Tour: The Undeserved Vituperation
Body_Text:
I HAVE always viewed the column, Frankly Speaking, as one of the few
remaining oases of professional excellence in today's desert of
cynical journalism in Nigeria.
However, the piece on the national media tour, Sunday Vanguard,
October 14, was a clear indication that the raging fire of cynicism
is already consuming the semblance of elderly responsibility and
decorum left in the profession.
In the piece in question, the writer, Dele Sobowale, chose to ignore
the national media tour's myriads of positive effects on our people's
socio economic and political awareness since its commencement in June
this year.
He also deliberately refused to acknowledge the programme's
tremendous facilitation of the right of the media to know how the
nation's new democratic governance is faring, at federal, state, and
local council levels, towards improving the lot of Nigerians.
Instead, he unleashed his usual tirade on Gana, invoking the memory
of 'Gana in MAMSER", "Gana in SDP," and so on, in an obvious rehash
of his familiar demonstration of an absurd distaste for Professor
Jerry Gana's continous loyal service to his fatherland. He may not
believe it, but God was a witness when I recently objected to some
journalists' angry suggestion in my office, after reading the piece
in question, that he nowadays cut the picture of a tired "veteran",
casting his covetous old eyes on the information minister's job,
perhaps as a parting gift for so many years of journalistic
pretensions.
It is not that he is not free to dream, or to attempt to get there by
hiding under Mr. Amuka Pemu's roof to take pot shots at all manners
of Nigerians who are doing their honest jobs. My objection against my
colleagues' angry conclusion was actually, based on personal
conviction that Sobowale is a "veteran" writer and a respectable
elder, who happens to find himself under the suffocating pressure of
today's print media in Nigeria, which thrives, almost solely, on the
cynical side of life.
I calmed my friends' frayed nerves by drawing their attention to the
implication of the writer's need to survive and remain relevant,
against such a background. It is thus clear that I am, personally,
neither surprised nor bothered by his increasing employment of
invectives and epithets of opprobrium in his column.
Therefore, this rejoinder was not prompted by his vitriolic attack on
the national media tour programme per se, as Professor Jerry Gana
himself has strongly advised us to always concentrate on the noble
objectives of development oriented information dissemination and
management and never to succumb to the distracting temptations of
direct engagement with cynics and rabble rousers in the print media
whose antics are usually tailored to meet their obvious need for
relevance and survival in today's cynical journalism.
Rather, this brief response is aimed at protesting against Sobowale's
uncharitable and vicious assault on the integrity of all journalists
in the national media tour team. His description of those diligent
and senior professional journalists, including Vanguard
representative, as 'praise and worship team" who "see no evil, hear
no evil, and report no evil" was very unfortunate. The insinuation
that those respectable ladies and gentlemen of the Nigerian media
received a brain dulling daily sedation of "pounded yam plus egusi
soup and assorted meat" and so on, was a clear indication that the
writer's effort to outdo his junior colleagues in the competitive
world of cynical journalism has indeed transformed his once vibrant
and frank column into a yellow page of diabolical humour.
Rabble rousers, snipping at the national media tour programme, have
raised a wide variety of false and baseless allegations to which
Sobowale's own "pounded yam and egusi soup" slur was only an
egregious addition. Some of his colleagues in the cynical trade had
built up various imaginary expenditures of unreasonable magnitude as
having been involved, while others insinuated that it was a
politically motivated jamboree.
I must reiterate the fact that the media tour is, and will remain, a
very important national programme, involving senior and
professionally oriented journalists from the nation's leading media
houses, and that it transcends all ethnic, regional, religious, or
partisan considerations.
Far from being a jamboree or a wasteful venture, the programme is
aimed at widening, deepening, strengthening and stabilizing the
nation's nascent democracy, by facilitating the right of the media to
know what the governments, at federal, state, and local council
levels, are doing to better the lot of our people.
It is not true that journalists were sedated with "pounded yam and
egusi soup" by the state governors in order to draw them into a
conspiracy of silence. Indeed, a number of state governors and local
council chairmen who were found to lag behind in one sector of
performance or the other are not likely to forget in a hurry the
razor sharp frankness with which their lapses were openly pointed out
by the media team, which was led by NUJ, the nation's apex body of
journalists.
It is also not true, as recently insinuated by some dodging governors
in a few states, that the programme had lost its focus at a stage.
Indeed, nobody was fooled by their non causa pro causa arguments and
excuses for banning the media team from inspecting their projects,
particularly after the journalists' unfettered tour of all the nooks
and crannies of those states, primarily to inspect federal projects,
but with their eyes trained on everything visible, and with their
ears to the ground, feeling the pulse of the largely hapless people.
Our media team was a microcosm of Nigeria itself, as it included
journalists from different media, different states, and different
socio-cultural, political, and religious backgrounds, making the
governors' whispered talk and fear of a PDP federal government's
organised media tour the ruse of the century.
By facilitating the Nigerian media's collective inspection and open
assessment of the concrete achievements on the ground, at federal,
state, and local council levels, the Federal Government has taken a
giant step towards entrenching transparency and good governance in
the new democratic Nigeria. However, good governance cannot and ought
not be seen as the exclusive business of the Federal Government
alone.
Rather, it demands the active support and participation of every
state government, every citizen, and every public and private
institution, with the media leading the way, by playing its watchdog
role, constructively and positively, without the encumbrance of deep
seated cynicism of the Sobowale type.
Lawal, is the chief press secretary to the Minister of Information
and National Orientation

Original_Search_Title: Police DCO Flogged By Soldier - CP
Headline: Nigeria: Police DCO Flogged By Soldier - CP
Body_Text:
The Divisional Crime Officer of Zaki-Biam divisional headquarters of the Nigeria Police in Benue, was flogged mercilessly by soldiers who invaded some villages in the state two weeks ago, to avenge the death of their colleagues, according to the state Police Commissioner, Mr. Cyrian Onephrojire.
Onephrojire made the disclosure while briefing Vice-President Atiku Abubakar at Zaki-Biam, in Ukum local government area of the state, during his one-day assessment visit to the area.
He said that apart from flogging the D.C.O., the Police Divisional Headquarters at Zaki-Biam was also burnt down by the soldiers, and all the detainees were released.
The commissioner said that only 28 persons were killed in Zaki-Biam during the soldiers' siege.
NAN reports that most of the affected houses were still burning at the time of the Vice-President's visit, while three corpses still littered the yam market, where most of the killings took place.
The Ukum local government headquarters and the home of former Chief of Army Staff, retired Maj-Gen. Victor Malu, were also burnt by the soldiers.
The News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) reports that most of the residents of the affected area, who are non-indigenes were seen leaving the town.

Original_Search_Title: Soldiers On Vengeance
Headline: Nigeria: Soldiers On Vengeance
Body_Text:
Soldiers sent to maintain peace between Tiv and Jukuns kill hundreds
of Tiv people and burn down the house of former Army Chief of Staff,
General Victor Malu, In December 2000, Paul Unongo, a politician and
Tiv leader, told journalists at a press conference in Makurdi, that
soldiers stationed in Takum, Taraba State, were being used by some
prominent people in Wukari to kill Tivs in their communal clash with
the Jukuns. Unongo had earlier sent a letter in similar vein to
President Olusegun Obasanjo.
The renewed hostilities between the Tivs and the Jukuns this year,
brought Unongo's call to the fore, when 20 soldiers were killed by
Tiv youths, who alleged that the soldiers were Jukuns in military
uniform.
When last week the Federal Government decided to send troops to the
troubled area, many observers knew that the soldiers, in sympathy
with their dead colleagues, would turn the peace mission into a
vengeance. The fear was that the soldiers might turn their guns
against the Tiv people, to re-enact the Odi episode of Bayelsa State
in November 1999.
By Monday, 22 October, less than one week after their official
deployment, the soldiers from Taraba State sought vengeance in three
local governments in Tiv land. In anger, the soldiers who appeared to
be on specific instructions, crushed all obstacles on their way from
Ukum to Tse Adoor in Katsina Ala Local Government. On Tuesday, they
burnt down the house of former Chief of Army Staff, General Victor
Malu, and killed four members of his family including the head, Pa
Pev Adoor. Others killed by the rampaging soldiers include Mrs.
Grace Pev and Miraan Tyobo.
The soldiers had earlier overrun Zarkin-Ibiam and Sankera, the Ukum
Local Government headquarters, where they burnt down the Local
