The irony, however, is how very unconcerned in manner the girls sat, casually chatting among themselves and enjoying every bit of this adventure, oblivious of the imminent danger.
We spent only a few minutes on the island. Somehow, everyone found a spot on the rocks, and just sat and observed the vastness of the sea, or was it the glory of God? It was exhilarating. The journey back was not as frightful, getting back to a 'heroes' welcome from the others.
After a few dips in the sea, surfing along the waves, I joined the rest of the group as we checked out of the hotel. I felt completely relaxed after only one night here. It suddenly dawned on me that it was Monday the day after! Traffic fumes, rising dust, sound of hooting motorcar horns, the constant ringing of my mobile phone, and bad English grammar on radio, all staring me in the face. Why couldn't I just stay here?

Original_Search_Title: Not Guilty
Headline: Nigeria: Not Guilty
Body_Text:
Ex-council boss caught in corruption and misapplication of public fund controversy fights to clear his name but the odds are stacked against him
Udo Edukere, deposed chairman of Esit Eket lo-cal government, Akwa Ibom State, who was accused of corruption and misappropriation of N15 million, is now fighting on many fronts to regain the post. Edukere has taken his case to the law court to clear his name and petitioned the anti-corruption panel, President Olusegun Obasanjo and Victor Attah, governor of Akwa Ibom State, to intervene in the matter.
A high court at Eket is already looking at Edukere's complaints.
Edukere was in combat mood when Newswatch visited him at Eket July 23. "If I am found guilty at the end of the day, so be it. I am ready to go to jail. I am doing these because of my good name and because I don't want to leave a bad name for my children," he said. That looks like a long shot. But for now, Edukere has to overcome some forces against him.
"The auditor-general has recommended that he should refund the sum of N15 million and we are moving in very soon to recover the council's money he misappropriated," Nkereuwem Akwaowo, the new chairman who succeeded Edukere in office, told Newswatch. He disclosed that the state government has set a machinery in motion to recover the N15 million.
That is not the only problem facing the former chairman. The councillors in Esit Eket local government are all against him. In fact, they were the architects of his fall. On March 26, seven of the councillors tabled a catalogue of allegations titled: Notice of guilt of gross misconduct, which they used to remove him. The councillors accused Edukere of lack of probity and accountability; gross and wanton breaches of financial regulations and procedures; misappropriation of council funds through spurious contracts and execution of non-existing projects as well as violation of the local government (administration) law 2000, among others.
Specifically, the councillors charged that the budget for the year 2000 for rural electrification was N11,500,000 but Edukere allegedly increased it to N19,267,142 without approval.
The councillors also alleged that under the cost/other charges sub-head, they budgeted N29,750,000.00, but Edukere unilaterally increased it to N49,846,467.77. He was equally accused of inflating the personnel cost, salary and allowances to N10 million.
They said the former chairman inflated the contract for the construction of the council hall by more than N5 million without approval. In the same charge, they said the council voted N1.5 million for the purchase of a pick-up van, but Edukere changed it to N2.6 million and the N5.85 million he spent on the purchase of other vehicles was not authorised.
They said though the council had no budget for consultancy services, the former chairman still went ahead to spend N1.3 million for that. The councillors also charged that Edukere was not rendering monthly statement of account as required by law. They also accused him of violating the local government law.
The councillors also accused Edukere of spending N2,598,000.00 of the council's money on the burial of his father without approval. They claimed that Edukere unlawfully enriched himself through inflation of contracts and inflation of budget votes and proceeds of which he used to complete his personal house at Eket, which was abandoned for several years. They also said he was now constructing a storey building at SDP Road, Eket.
Taking all the allegations together, the councillors said it was obvious that Edukere was not competent to remain in his post as council chairman and therefore, ordered his suspension. The matter was then sent to a panel of inquiry set up by the state chief judge, which also found him guilty of gross misconduct.
Edukere denied all the allegations. He told Newswatch he was not corrupt and that he did not embezzle government money to warrant any refund. He charged that contrary to allegations, he had run an open government "that is accountable to God and to the people of Esit Eket directly through the elected councillors." He said it was untrue that he was not calling regular general council meetings and showed Newswatch reporter some of the minutes of the council meetings.
He challenged the head of personnel to produce all general council minutes from June 1999 to 2001 whether those decisions were not reflected there. He told Newswatch that he normally circulated statement of income and expenditure to council members as approved by general council meetings. He claimed that he had never done any project without the permission of the council or increased contract sums without approvals. He also showed Newswatch some of the monthly returns he sent to the council for vetting and debate. Edukere insisted that he had never inflated any contract or used his position to embezzle public funds.
Edukere said the allegation that he bought vehicles without due authorisation was false. He said soon after inauguration, it was discovered that the council lacked functional vehicles and the general meeting of June 27, 2000, resolved to buy the vehicles.
On the electrification contracts he said that the finance and general purpose committee meeting, September 28, 2000, approved them and the tender for the award was published in the Pioneer newspaper of August 14, 2000. He quoted variously from the minutes and resolutions of the council to buttress his point.
On the personnel cost, he said the head of personnel and the treasurer who handle such matters are the people in a position to explain the reasons for the N10 million increase in personnel cost.
Edukere said contrary to the allegation that he unilaterally inflated the contract for the construction of the council hall by more than N5 million, he made reference to the finance and general purpose meeting of May 26, 2000, which awarded the contract to Qua Ventures Nigeria Limited for N15.9 million.
He said the contract was reviewed upward when the contractor asked for variation. He said he did not approve the variation, even when Theophilus Umoyo, leader of the council, led a delegation to his office pleading for the variation. "I insisted that all the 10 councillors must sign to authorise me to refer the variation to the consultants," said Edukere. He showed Newswatch the document signed by the councillors to authorise the variation as well as the recommendation of the consultants, who approved tentative payment of N4 million to the contractor. But Edukere approved N3 million.
Edukere similarly denied allegation of corrupt enrichment. He said that the personal building referred to by the councillors took him seven years to build. He claimed to have built it with his personal savings, loan and cash gift from friends. The other plot of land that he has, he said, was bought from the state government in 1994 at the cost of N20,000.00. And that the storey building on the land, still under construction, was actually a block of stores. "I am constructing this building with my legitimate earnings and a loan from Citizens International Bank, Eket, and with a money gift from my brother-in-law residing in the United States of America," Edukere said.
On the money spent on the burial of his father, Edukere said with regrets: "I wish I did not accept the unsolicited Greek gift of Esit Eket local government." He argued that though it was a standard practice at any government level to assist bereaved government officials, he did not apply for any assistance. For instance, he alleged that the council gave Bassey Essien, speaker of Akwa Ibom State house of assembly, financial assistance of N1,313,500.00 during the burial of his mother.
Similarly, he said that the council gave same assistance to the tune of N1,377,500.00 to Nkereuwem Akwaowo towards the burial of his father. He argued: "All I did was to give effect to council's resolution. Indeed, I did so reluctantly after pressure from the councillors. Other than the cash gift to me, I did not, repeat did not, handle the funds or control how they were spent. The vice-chairman was fully in charge." Besides, he told Newswatch that normally, because his late father was a certificated village chief, it was incumbent on the council to give him a cow towards his burial.
Edukere said it was Akwaowo, the vice-chairman, who chaired the council meeting, which deliberated and resolved to give him financial support towards the burial of his father. "I never asked them to give me money to bury my father," he said. He told Newswatch that he received N500,000.00 cash.
He said that notwithstanding, he was not part of decision to spend N2.5 million on the burial. Edukere said it was Akwaowo, the vice-chairman, who chaired the council meeting, which decided that.
That in itself, he said, was not unusual. He told Newswatch that Essien and Akwaowo were equally given cash. He said both the speaker and former vice-chairman were given N250,000.00 each and that the balance was used to purchase such items like cows, goats, yams and other items towards the burial as well as for repairing the roads leading to their various villages.
Edukere told Newswatch that the council decided to repair the road leading to Essien's village because "if we waited on the state, they might not take cognisance of the need to repair the road. And since this is my local government area, the council met and said, what assistance can we give to him so that he may not be humiliated. Supposing people came and there was no road to his house, the first person they would accuse would be the local government, ok. They would also talk about the state and the state would turn around and blame the local government. So, we moved in .  We went and we graded the road. We brought sand to fill the road to make the road passable, because it was during the raining season. And then we gave cash. All we spent was N1.3 million. We gave him N250,000.00; we also gave him a cow, a goat and some yams, to enable him entertain the avalanche of visitors that came into the local government."
Edukere said he knew that trouble was afoot when the councillors started making some demands from him. "Severally, the vice-chairman and the councillors had sent proposals asking me to share council funds, which I have stoutly resisted.
"For instance, on September 6, 2000, the vice-chairman sent a proposal to me demanding for N5.5 million to be shared among himself and the councillors but this was turned down," Edukere alleged.
Three months after, he alleged that another proposal came from Umoyo demanding N5.4 million to be shared. The same leader, he said, later demanded N2.0 million as take-off grant, but he approved N500,000.00 to be disbursed to the councillors after much pressure. Edukere told Newswatch that he turned down another request of N2.6 million, which was meant to be Christmas bonus for the legislators.
He believes that he invoked the wrath of Essien, the assembly speaker, because he had campaigned against him in the 1998 election. He equally believes that the speaker has not been feeling comfortable to have an AD chairman in his local council. "The speaker,  in his elevated position, of course there is no way he could have felt good in that he is number three in the state and his local government is under an AD chairman. We are talking about politics if you are a leader in his position, there is no way you can talk authoritatively if your local government is in the back of the party you are representing."
He believes Attah, Essien, Akwaowo and his councillors were part of the conspiracy. First, Edukere noted that only five of the ten voted for his removal at the meeting of March 26. Four of the councillors, he maintained, were not served the notice of the meeting hence three of them did not attend. He said the councillors required two-thirds majority to remove him from office.
He faulted the legality of the meeting, saying the mace, the symbol of authority of the legislature, was still in the custody of the police at their headquarters in Uyo at the time the councillors met. Besides, he argued that the law required that he should be served notice of gross misconduct at least seven days before the meeting and he has the liberty to reply to the allegations within 14 days. He claimed that he only received the notice after he was suspended from office. On the day of his suspension, Edukere alleged that 22 armed policemen were deployed to the council headquarters to stampede people out of the place.
Edukere also flawed the panel's decision in the case. He admitted to Newswatch that he did not appear before the panel on grounds that there was a case in court challenging the legality of his suspension. "It was wrong for the panel to sit when a superior court had ruled that it has jurisdiction to hear the cases brought by councillors against and for me," he said.
Essien told Newswatch in Uyo, July 24, that Edukere was only looking for a scapegoat. He said he was not responsible for his removal or played a part in it. "As the speaker of the state assembly, of what importance or what consequence would the removal or non removal of chairman of the local council be to me?" He asked. Instead, he said he had mediated thrice between him and the councillors to ensure that peace and cordial relationship existed in the council.
He denied ever collecting any money from the council towards the burial of his mother. He also said the council did not repair the road to his village at the time. He told Newswatch that the only repairs done on the road was carried out by a friend of his who was working at Strabag at that time. He said the friend, one Chief Beke, filled the road with stones to make it passable. A Newswatch reporter, who visited Essien's village noticed that the road was stone-filled, untarred and hardly motorable. But the reporter did not meet Beke when he called at his office in Uyo.
He also defended the governor and the chief judge, saying they were only doing their constitutional duties. "It is very wrong to blame their failure or their non performance on innocent bye-standers," Essien said.
Akwaowo, who was Edukere's vice, also told Newswatch that his former boss was a liar. "Edukere is the greatest liar in Esit Eket local government," he said. The new chairman claimed that his predecessor had acted like a sole administrator and that in fact, he told someone that he was a sole administrator of the council. He charged that the former chairman appropriated money and spent money the way he liked.
Akwaowo denied the allegation that he tabled councillors' request for money before Edukere. He also said he did not approve N2,598,000.00 for the burial of his father. He did not say how much was actually spent. Instead, he promised to give Newswatch reporter a document on the issue, but in the end, he said he could not lay his hands on it. As for his father's burial, he claimed that he was given only N250,000.00 by the council.
"He approved N250,000.00 for the burial of my father. The record would show that was what was brought to me. If any money was given to anybody, the council does not know it because he was running the council like his personal estate, without consultation, without discussion with anybody. If he gave out money most of us are not in the know."
S. J. Isonguyo, the clerk of the local government, told Newswatch that he could not speak about the matter, and that no councillor would talk to me on it because they had appointed Theophilus Umoyo, the leader, to speak on their behalf. Udoyiu E. Udoyiu, personal assistant to the leader, said his boss was ill and was out of town.
Edukere was suspended from office March 26, following a council debate on allegation of gross misconduct pronounced him guilty. The matter was then forwarded to a judicial panel set up by the state chief judge, which found him guilty as charged. Sylvanus Albert, a lawyer, who headed the seven-man panel that looked into the case, said the panel was satisfied that the plaintiffs proved all the allegations against the former chairman. But the panel said that the allegation that Edukere corruptly enriched himself was not established. "It is therefore presumed that the plaintiffs had abandoned the allegation," said the panel report.
As soon as the panel submitted its report, the councillors started an impeachment procedure and removed him from office that Thursday, June 5.

Original_Search_Title: Centre Developing Vitamin a From Vegetables
Headline: Ghana: Centre Developing Vitamin a From Vegetables
Body_Text:
The Plant Genetic Resources Centre at Bunso in the Eastern Region is conducting research into 32 indigenous leafy vegetables to identify those high  in Vitamin A and other nutrients to enrich Ghanaian diets.
Dr Samuel Odei Bennett-Lartey, Director of the centre said the identified plants would be used in nutrition programmes for children and nursing mothers to meet their Vitamin A requirements.
He told Professor Dominic Fobih, Minister of Environment, Science and Technology who was on a familiarisation tour that the centre was doing a lot to popularise the scientific use of plants.
The centre, a subsidiary of the Centre for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) was established to collect and help conserve the nation's plant genetic resources especially plants which are becoming extinct.
Dr Bennett-Lartey said the centre was nursing the seeds of six of the vegetables - Alefu, Ayoyo, Borkor borko, Cat whiskers, Lagos Spinach and Gboma - for distribution to interested farmers.
The centre was also conserving seven yam species and 300 cassava accessions, and undertaking the collection, characterisation and evaluation of Frafra Potato to be produced on a large scale for the hotel industry as a substitute for Irish Potato.
Cultivation of non-traditional export crops like black pepper, sweet berries and nutmeg was also being popularised.
Dr Bennett-Lartey said in collaboration with the Ghana Tourist Board and the Ghana Wildlife Society, the centre would establish an arboretum which would contain a herb garden. The arboretum would be used to promote eco-tourism.
Professor Fobih said the government's policy of positive change enjoined all workers to re-examine their attitude to work and make amends where they fell below expectation.
"We as a nation can achieve the change we desire if we put in our best to ensure that our work places achieve their goals and objectives."
Prof. Fobih urged the centre to explore avenues of revenue generation to supplement government's subvention.
GNA

Original_Search_Title: FG to Supply Free Nets to Pregnant Women, Children
Headline: Nigeria: FG to Supply Free Nets to Pregnant Women, Children
Body_Text:
Abuja — Determined to stem the rate of materials and child mortality in the
country, the Federal Government is to commence the supply of Insecticide Treated
Nets (ITN) free of charge to pregnant women and under five years-old
children in the country by July 2002.
A statement signed by the Assistant Public Relations Officer of the
National Health Primary Healthcare Development Agency, Sa'adu Salahu
stated that they were acing on President Olusegun Obasanjo's
directive that the Federal Ministry of Health should start free
supply of the materials to both pregnant women and under-five years
old children.
Mr. Salahu stated that the chief consultant in the Federal Ministry
of health, Dr. Dayo Mosanya disclosed this while delivering a lecture
at a three-day Training of Tainers (TOT) workshop for zonal technical
officers of the National Primary Healthcare Development Agency in
Kaduna State, he said.
Dr. Mosanya disclosed that the Federal Government had already removed
tariff on insecticides treated nets and reduced customs duty on
importation of yam from 15 % to 5% in its efforts to ensure the
success of the Rold Bak Malaria Campaign.
She described malaria as the commonest cause of hospital admissions
at the grassroot level and an all year round disease in the country.

Original_Search_Title: First Agriculture Information Centre Opens
Headline: Ghana: First Agriculture Information Centre Opens
Body_Text:
An agricultural information centre, the first of its kind in the country was on Tuesday inaugurated at Atebubu in the Brong Ahafo Region.
The centre, located inside the Atebubu yam market, is a joint initiative of the Directorate of Agricultural Extension Services (DAES) of the Ministry of Food and Agriculture and the German Technical Co-operation Agency (GTZ).
It is intended, among others things, to provide a forum for the exchange of information among stakeholders in agriculture.
The centre will also provide services to other people like input suppliers and wholesalers, traders, transporters, banks,  non-governmental organisations
and district assembliesAlhaji Amadu Ali, Member of Parliament for Atebubu South said the centre would enhance efficiency in agricultural production and processing and add value to agricultural products to maximise profit.
He said post-harvest losses were a major disincentive in food and general agricultural production.
Alhaji Ali, therefore, lauded the DAES and GTZ for their initiative,
saying, "The objective is holistic and will make a positive impact on the lives of the people who are predominantly farmers".
He said access to credit facilities was a problem for farmers because of their inability to provide collateral security as individuals.
The MP advised farmers in the district to form co-operatives to make it easier for them to secure loans from the banks.
Mr. Franklin Donkor, Director of DAES, advised the district assembly to team up with relevant agencies to open more of such centres in the district.
Mr. Philip Kwame Titriku, Regional Director of Agriculture said due to inadequate resources and personnel, agriculture information dissemination had been slow and inefficient.
He said statistics showed that the agent to farmer ration was about 1:1,800 and that made it virtually impossible to reach the majority of farmers with information.
Mr. Titriku said the establishment of the information centre to explore alternative ways of disseminating agricultural information would bring the extension agents and farmers closer.
Mr. Heimo Posamentier, a GTZ official said the centre would become more beneficial to the farmers when they voluntarily went there for information and solution to their problems.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Swells With New Yam
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Swells With New Yam
Body_Text:
Lagos — Harvest time is here and the markets are awash with fresh food items
that had hitherto been scarce. One of the food items in large supply to
markets is yam, one of Nigeria's staple foods. At the Lagos food depot, the
popular Mile 12 market in Kosofe, full lorry loads of yams, especially from
the North keep streaming in on a daily basis.  The development, according to
Metro Business findings, has forced down the price of yam which went
sky-high during the planting seasons.
Just before this season, a medium sized tuber of yam at Mile 12 market
and other markets in Lagos was sold for as high as N200, while a heap of
five tubers attracted N1,000 A market survey conducted by Metro Business
shows that the prices of the new yams which are now available in almost all
markets in Lagos are gradually dipping. However, the new price, according to
market women, is not low as expected. A sizeable tuber of yam goes for
N100. A market woman, Madam Susan, who spoke to Metro Business at Mile 12
said yams were more cheaper last year.
According to her, a tuber of yam which now sells for N100 was sold for
between N40 and N60 last year. She, however, attributed the development to
the general downturn in the economy, citing the price of garri as a major
determinant of fixing prices for food items by dealers. A yam dealer at the
market, Mr. Gabriel, explained that new yams are still coming in trickles
and that by the time proper harvest of yams is done (by next month), prices
might be forced to come down drastically as there will be enough supply in
the market. He explained that because of poor agricultural policy in the
country, most crops are only harvested once in a year.

Original_Search_Title: KIA Records Growth in Non-Traditional Exports
Headline: Ghana: KIA Records Growth in Non-Traditional Exports
Body_Text:
Government's effort at encouraging export of Non-traditional commodities seems to be yielding positive results as exports through the cargo village at the Kotoka International Airport (KIA) have been recording steady growth since 1995.
Imports, through the same route, however, have slowed down considerably over the past three years.
Pineapples account for about 60 per cent of exports through KIA, while yams, vegetables and handicrafts make up 35 and five percent respectively.
Statistics available to the Ghana News Agency show that 25,301 tonnes of the items were exported in 1998; 29,880 in 1999 and 32,379 tonnes last year.
Commodities exported from January 1998 to July 2001 stood at 64,071 tonnes.
Mr Maxwell Arthur, Director, Corporate Planning, of the Ghana Civil Aviation Authority said there was a drop in exports in the first half of this year, as against the same period last year. This he said was due to a slow down of economic activity in the country and the temporary ban on yam exports.
He also mentioned the rather stiff competition from the export of similar  products on the world market from the Caribbean, saying, "this really hit our drive."
Mr Arthur was quick to add that business is likely to pick up by September.
An official of African Ground Operations (AFGO), said the demand for pineapples is now on a daily basis, unlike previously when such exports were seasonal.
He said the survival of the aviation industry depends largely on the economic well being of the country, saying, " there is a correlation between Gross Domestic Product and exports especially through KIA.
Mr Arthur said spare parts; machinery freighted through KIA has been declining.
In 1999 imports recorded 16,877 tonnes while in 2000 it was 14,447 tonnes.
He said imports are expected to increase by December, when traders and importers bring in goods for the Yuletide.
Estimated revenue accruing from  exports and imports through AFGO in 2000 was 8.3 million dollars, while that of the first half of this year stands at 3.7 million dollars.

Original_Search_Title: FAO, UNDP to Assist Oncho Endemic Areas
Headline: Ghana: FAO, UNDP to Assist Oncho Endemic Areas
Body_Text:
Tamale — Four previously oncho endemic areas in the Volta basin of the
Northern, Upper East and West regions are to benefit from the
socio-economic investment programmes of the Food and Agricultural
Organisation (FAO) and the United Nations Development Programme
(UNDP).
The areas are South Eastern Wa District in the Upper West region,
Southern Builsa District in the Upper East region, West Mamprusi and
West Gonga districts in the Northern region.
To this end, a workshop has been held in Tamale on the sustainable
socio-economic development of these oncho-free areas to broaden
stakeholder support to the project proposals and to sensitise donor
communities.
In a speech read on his behalf, the Minister of Finance, Mr. Osafo
Maafo, recounted the history of the then oncho endemic areas, saying
they were bedevilled with the river blindness or onchocerciasis
disease, which was not only a health hazard but an impediment to
socio-economic development. Mr. Maafo said now that the disease has
been eradicated, the several million hectors of fertile land in the
areas have to be used for settlement and development purposes.
However, a study conducted in these areas revealed some constraints.
"These include lack of accessibility and other social, economic and
administrative infrastructure; others are flooding, poor access to
market, agricultural inputs, credit and extension services."
Consequently, the four selected areas in the three regions were
identified for a pilot project on sustainable agricultural and rural
development, under the Support Strategy for Pilot Programme
(SSPP),assisted by the FAO and the UNDP. According to the minister,
the pilot project proposes a five-year development programme of four
components.
It comprises agricultural development, rural infrastructure
development and project management, monitoring and evaluation. US$5
million will be needed to implement the programme. He said: "When
these are accomplished, we would have increased our food crop
production and have improvements in our rural development and on the
way moving upwards from extreme poverty, that engulfed our
oncho-freed areas.
The minister added that within the Ghana Poverty Reduction Strategy
(GPRS), "accelerated agric-business zones" would be established in
the oncho-freed areas in the Afram Plains. The aim is to
substantially increase the production of rice (50%), maize (300%),
millet (200%), cassava (100%), yam (40%), and seed cotton (15%).
He commended the Oncho Control Programme for eradicating the disease
from the areas and said "We in Ghana are indeed proud of the
remarkable achievement of the Oncho Control Programme in disease
control and highly commend all those involved from its inception.
Mr. Moise Sonou, a Senior Development Officer, who represented the
Director-General of FAO, said funding for the pilot investment
programmes came from its Technical Co-operation Programme.
He said so far, nine countries have been covered with Ghana being the
tenth one. Financial assistance for the Ghana programme was granted
under the UNDP's Support for Policy and Programmes Development
(SPPD).
Mr. Sonou added that while the FAO emphasizes the need to ensure
national ownership throughout the formulation and implementation
stages, it cannot ignore transnational issues that may affect the
sustainable management of national resources and the resettlement in
the oncho-freed zones.
Consequently, he said, the FAO is preparing a regional umbrella
programme to back up the national programmes.
He asked the stakeholders to ensure that the hard-won development
potential of the oncho-freed areas is not hampered by isolation, lack
of socio-economic infrastructure and non-sustainable farming
practices.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices in Bolgatanga Still High
Headline: Ghana: Food Prices in Bolgatanga Still High
Body_Text:
Food prices at the Bolgatanga central market have doubled as compared to the situation last year, a Ghana News Agency survey has shown.
The survey conducted over the weekend covered the staples - the root crops, cereals, grains, legumes and plantain.
A bag of late millet, which sold between 100,000 cedis and 120,000 cedis during the same period last year, now sells at between 200,000 and 250,000 cedis.  A bowl of millet sells between 6,000 and 7,000 cedis compared to 3,000 cedis last year.
The price of a maxi-bag of maize ranges between 160,000 cedis and 200,000 cedis, whiles a bag of groundnuts is between 400,000 cedis and 450,000 cedis depending on the source of supply.
An average size of yam sells between 8,000 cedis and 10,000 cedis and one finger of plantain sells at 1,000 cedis or 1,500 cedis.
The traders attributed the high prices to the unfavourable rainfall pattern at the beginning of the current farming season, which has delayed the harvesting of the early millet and other food crops.
They said the high price of plantain, cassava and yam was largely due to the high cost of transportation.
GNA

Original_Search_Title: 'I Wonder As I Wander' - Sister Hawa's headaches
Headline: Ghana: 'I Wonder As I Wander' - Sister Hawa's headaches
Body_Text:
Hon. Nana Akomea, Deputy Minister for Tourism, says, according to GTV news a few days ago, that tourism must be added to the school curriculum. In the actuality accompanying the story, however, I didn't hear the minister propose one more subject for the BECE or the SSCE.
Well, somebody surely sorely needs some schooling in tourism. But that somebody is certainly not our children, absolutely not our toddlers and infants. It is big people who badly need education in tourism.
Take the Ghana Airways. It has so many stories for getting people stranded that it is not worth getting angry at Mawuko's PR department
anymore. It is very tempting to conclude that somebody is determined to run down the company as a mission. If you have any appointment anywhere in the world, the national airline will help you miss it.
The US is fast becoming our biggest tourist market. A national airline is a logical first attraction for people coming here for the first time.
Getting stranded in Baltimore, meanwhile, seems to be the specialty of the Ghana Airways. It confuses its passengers when it runs on time.
Indeed, if we did not have a man like Mr. Sam Jonah there now, it would be appropriate to advocate that 'bookmen' of the GPRTU might run the company more efficiently.
The hotel industry has been growing since the late 1980s. But there is a lot of very basic things they are yet to learn to improve. Food is a big challenge to most small hotels in the country, in Accra or elsewhere.
You cannot take for granted that there will be breakfast for you in most hotels. That is why in Ghana hotels don't charge rooms with breakfast included.
This gets to be more challenging outside of Accra. But it has also to do with the thinking that hotels must not serve Ghanaian foods. Breakfast, for our hotels, cannot consist of koko and koose, or mpampa, or even tea and maasa. You get into a hotel in some corner of the country. You ask for a menu. You are shown some piece of paper, and the pride of the cook is "fly" rice. And this is in yam country during yam season.
I've been in a hotel in Cape Coast where I couldn't be served kenkey and fish. Our catering schools specialize in "fly" rice.
It's like most other things in our lives. We must run away from ourselves all the time. It is unthinkable to get into a hotel in Nigeria and not get to be served Nigerian foods. No. But the Ghanaian's pride, which we want tourists to pay money to visit, is how far away we want to be from ourselves.
Perhaps tourism ends in Accra. Or should. But if it must go into the country, a lot has to be done that is not the job of the ministry. What can the ministry do about torn bedcovers and sheets, torn discoloured towels, stinking bathrooms with hand folded pieces of toilet paper and half tablets of soap, or dirty mattresses in dusty rooms with cobwebs?
What about carpets and rugs that are not vacuum-cleaned and so leave hotel rooms dank and stuffy?
Tourists coming to Ghana are not likely to be your luxury types who patronize 5-star hotels. Indeed we must encourage more the kind of tourist who would patronize the kinds of hotels we have in Ghana. Even then, compared to what is available to you in places like Kenya, Uganda, Burkina Faso, Senegal, or even South Africa, hotel rates are too high in Ghana.
We don't have the pyramids. And no matter what investments we put out, the Boti falls will never be like the Victoria Falls. Bosumtwi can never draw to itself tourists because of its size. The biggest tourist attraction we can offer is not a natural landscape or a human-made monument. We must not underrate 'Ghanaian hospitality'. That is what we have to sell. It will be more valuable if we package that well by improving the services of our airline and our hotels.
Perhaps Nana Akomea and Hawa could start from a simple, modest budget survey of tourists. That should give you basis for diagnosis and for improving things.

Original_Search_Title: Taking the War to the Enemy
Headline: Kenya: Taking the War to the Enemy
Body_Text:
To Kanu, civic education is a life and death matter Okonkwo knew. He knew that if he wanted to escape the curse of his father - the debts, the poverty, the contempt of his peers - he had to tend his yams and keep his family well-fed and satisfied.
He knew what needed to be done, how it needed to be done, and when. It took hard work and sense to rise above the miasma of his father's failures.
Unlike Okonkwo, the opposition in this country knows what it wants - power - and it may have a vague notion of what needs to be done, but will not lift a finger to get it done. You have to hand it to Kanu, that party has not kept an iron grip on power by sitting on its backside and moaning. It has done so by snatching the initiative, taking the war to its enemies, playing dirty, but playing hard.
And that is why the opposition remains putty in Kanu's hands. It is not solely about Kanu having more money and access to state machinery. That is a part of it. But it also has to do with being focused, resolute and cunning.
Let's get to the point. The Constitution of Kenya Review Commission has been visiting the provinces to gather views on how Kenyans want the review to be conducted. Of great importance is the question of civic education. To Kanu, civic education is of political life and death. Being in essence a dictatorial party - by tradition and increasingly by force of necessity - Kanu believes in the control of information.
It fears that the free flow of unembellished education might enlighten the masses upon whose ignorance it has profited for 38 years. It therefore wants a civic education curriculum that does not include anything that might challenge its political well-being.
Kanu leadership understands
But it did not wait for the commission to collect the views and then make noise at the twelfth hour. The Kanu leadership understands two things: First, the Ghai commission issupposed to figure out what the majority want by listening to views.
Secondly, the Kanu leadership understands the process of the formation of public opinion and of defining the public agenda. It knows that in actual fact, there is no such thing as public opinion.
Opinions are not formed in committees and barazas because there is no such thing as collective thinking. All opinions originate from individuals, they become public opinions when their authors manage to convince others.
Which brings us to the pinnacle of the argument. Kanu wants the Church and NGOs off civic education. It also wants to have control over what ends up in the curriculum. It knew making noise in barazas is not going to influence the commission.
So it brought out the big guns - ministers Nicholas Biwott, Henry Kosgey and Julius Sunkuli - and sent them hunting. As a result, not only are its views firmly in the commissions books, but it has also set the agenda and quite possibly won a few hearts.
On the flip side, the opposition snores, deep in slumber. It has views different from those of Kanu but would not recognise an opportunity even if it bunched itself into a fist and struck it on the eye. What, pray, does the opposition think its job in the review is?
Doesn't it see that a presentation to the commission is also an opportunity to market opinions where it matters - among the people?
What a disappointing absence of focus. Okonkwo would have been furious.
In last week's column, it was said that Houston was the state capital of Texas. The political and administrative capital of Texas, where the state legislature meets once every two years, is actually Austin. Houston, President Bush's hometown, is the state's commercial, financial and pollution capital.
The writer wishes to apologise to readers for the error and thank them for their numerous corrections. He also wishes to assure them, especially those from North America, that it is not his desire to dismiss out of hand Mr Bush as a man of lower than average abilities.
mutuma@nation.co.ke

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis Blamed On Government
Headline: Nigeria: Food Crisis Blamed On Government
Body_Text:
The blame for the astronomical increase in the prices of food items
in the country has been put at the doorstep of government.
Nigerians in one voice conduced in a Guardian Opinion Poll (GOP) also
believed that the solution lies with the government. The poll asked
the question: "Who do you hold responsible for the sharp increase in
the prices of food in the country?" Overall 675 respondents
representing 57.30 per cent out of a total of 1,1,78 sampled opinion
put the blame on government.
However, 197 or 167.72 per cent believe that Nigerians generally
should be held responsible, 196 (16.64 per cent) picked middlemen, 62
(5.26) say farmers are culpable, 10 respondents representing 0.85 per
cent blamed the consumers, 18 (1.73) blamed other variables not
listed while 19 (1.61 per cent) offer no response at all.
The zonal breakdown of the 675 respondents who blamed the government
showed that North-Central topped the list with 138 respondents
followed by North-East with 129, North West 112, South South 107,
South-West 107 and South-East 82 respondents.
On a zonal analysis, the South East geo-political zone topped the
list of those who blamed the middle-men for the rising prices of food
with 84 respondents followed by South-South with 32, North-West 28,
North-East 22, South-West 16 and North-Central 14 out of a total of
196 respondents belonging to this category.
Food is easily the most essential need for man. However, the current
food crisis in the country and its implication are a sad pointer to
the fact that the government is yet to fully appreciate the
importance of food to the citizenry.
Since January this year, prices of food items such as garri, rice,
beans, yam, meat, fish, fruits tomatoes and others have soared
astronomically and complaints by people though loud and persistent,
have not changed the situation. Whatever hopes Nigerians had
entertained that the sudden predicament would be normalised soon, may
have disappeared by now as government has consistently shown by its
comments and actions that it does not have a solution to the crisis
at hand.

Original_Search_Title: Burgess And Hassim Win Sanlam Prize
Headline: South Africa: Burgess And Hassim Win Sanlam Prize
Body_Text:
Yvonne Burgess has won the 2001 Sanlam Literary Award for Fiction for  her novel ALarger Silence, published by Penguin. In the unpublished  category,
Aziz Hassim won for his novel The Lotus People. Burgess's novel was  preceded by Anna and the Colonel: "And the Colonel just would not die," she  explains.
So she brought him back in A Larger Silence -- and "simply took  dictation from the Colonel". Burgess lives in a retirement village in Port  Elizabeth.
Adjudicators Arthur Maimane, Tim Huisamen and Stephen Gray compiled a shortlist of Elleke Boehmer's Bloodlines, Zakes Mda's The Heart of  Redness,
Phaswane Mpe's Welcome to Our Hillbrow and Sheila Roberts's Purple  Yams.
Huisamen said Hassim's "marvellous, engrossing work" held him from  chapter to chapter, fuelling his interests in meta-fiction and the role of the writer as historian. Spanning more than a century, The Lotus People  served as "a form of catharsis" for the 65-year-old Hassim. He calls the  cleansing process his "personal Truth and Reconciliation Commission".
Gray hailed the novel as "unputdownable ... one of those one-off, unpredictable things ... a masterwork".
This year the Sanlam prize received some 100 entries.
She was fÃªted like royalty and performed for royalty when the queen  of
Malaysia attended her album launch

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices to Drop By the End of September
Headline: Ghana: Food Prices to Drop By the End of September
Body_Text:
Ghanaians should expect an improvement in the availability of food with a corresponding reduction in prices by the end of September, Mr Emmanuel Aggrey-Fynn, Director of Statistics, Research and Information of the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MOFA), said on Monday.
Mr Aggrey-Fynn told the GNA in an interview in Accra that the food situation should be normal by that time, while prices of most produce would reduce to at least half of their current levels.
"A field assessment indicates that, indeed, prices of some produce have already gone down," he said. "A bag of maize in the bush, for instance, now sells at 90,000 cedis from 150,000 cedis."
A report of the food and agriculture situation for the second quarter says rainfall was generally adequate in all regions, while the availability of inputs was satisfactory.
However, some regions recorded weeks of drought in May, which led to the scorching of crops, especially maize, planted early.  Mr Aggrey-Fynn said current levels of food prices, which were recently the subject of debate in Parliament, are extremely high. Ghanaians spend about 51 per cent of their income on food.
"It should be noted that by this time of the year, known as the lean season (April to June), food prices tend to rise and reach a peak around June. However, this year's food prices in the season have been exceptionally high," he said.The price increases for cassava and plantain from the same period last year  have been 321.4 per cent and 266.7 per centrespectively. Other commodities also recorded varying price increases ranging between 42.5 per cent and 154.5 percent.A food analysis report from MOFA indicates that a 100-kilogram bag of maize, which  sold at 101,251 cedis in last year's lean season, cost 181,657 cedis this season, recording an increase of 79.4 per cent.
The price of local rice went up by 53.7 per cent, while that of yam and tomatoes went up by 154.5 per cent and 122.4 per cent respectively.
Similarly, the prices of agricultural inputs escalated, with that of a hoe going up by 71.5 per cent and cutlass by 133.8 per cent.
Mr Aggrey-Fynn blamed the situation on high inflation, frequent fuel price increases and the excessive exports of produce to neighbouring countries.
"The cedi depreciated by about 100 per cent against the dollar between the last lean season and this year's," he said. Mr Aggrey-Fynn said the exchange rate of the cedi significantly impacts on food prices as many agricultural inputs are imported. "Besides, inflation compels farmers to increase their profit margins."
At the end of April last year, the average buying and selling rates for a dollar, according to the Ghana Association of Bankers, were 4,341.18 cedis and 4,504.55 cedis respectively.
For the same period this year, the rates were 7,037.45 and 7,242.00 respectively.
The weather conditions in the West Africa sub-region were unfavourable for exports to neighbouring countries last year.
This caused those in the arid zone to depend on those in the forest areas for some commodities.
However, he said, exports of produce should not be encouraged during the lean season since they deepen shortfalls on the local front."We must adopt some non-tariff measures to discourage the practice until we achieve food security." Mr Aggrey-Fynn said to improve on the food security situation the nation needs a buffer stock, adding that the government had tasked a committee with that responsibility.
In addition, he said, post-harvest losses must be reduced by improving the road network in major food growing areas, encouraging on-farm preservation of produce and processing.
About 20 per cent of Ghana's 20 million metric tonnes of food produced are lost through poor preservation practices, distribution and marketing practices. "In the Afram Plains area, where about one million metric tonnes of food is produced, the road network is extremely bad. Therefore, about 40 per cent of the food is lost through spoilage."
Ghana annually imports about 350 million dollars of food, mainly rice, sugar, wheat, fish and meat.

Original_Search_Title: 'Revolution Imminent If ...' Wayas, Ex-Senate President Warns
Headline: Nigeria: 'Revolution Imminent If ...' Wayas, Ex-Senate President Warns
Body_Text:
The current rise in prices of staple food in Nigeria may lead to a
revolution by the people if the Federal Government does not take
urgent steps to check the upward movement of prices, Senate President
in the Second Republic, Dr. Joseph Wayas has said.
Wayas was of the view that governments in some parts of the country
have been brought down by high food prices, insisting that Nigerians
possess the ability and resource to feed themselves.
Speaking at a state banquet in his honour at 60 years by the Cross
River State Government, he said the government should not allow the
peoples' anger to degenerate to violence because of hunger, which has
been occasioned by the unaffordable prices of foodstuff.
In the banquet in Calabar, weekend, which was attended by
Vice-President Atiku Abubakar, Wayas stated that "the situation
becomes worrisome when people can no longer afford gari, rice and yam
which are locally cultivated, bearing in mind that in majority of the
778 local governments in Nigeria, about 600 of them are farmers."
"We may wake up one morning and find people stoning cars on the
streets. They will not be selective in their violence, they simply
will stone any car in sight, believing that these are the people who
have made food unavailable," Wayas said.
On the cause of the present socio-political problem in the country,
he said, every blame must be placed at the door of military people
who strayed into politics, "creating so much problems both
economically, politically and otherwise."
Wayas linkened Nigeria's poor showing in democracy to a child just
preparing to walk, pointing out that if such a child is always lifted
from the ground by the parents rather than allowed to learn the
process of walking," it will take quite some time for such a child to
know how to walk".
"Consequently, Nigeria must be allowed to walk (learn the art of
democracy) and if mistakes are made, we should be given the
opportunity to learn from these mistakes," he said adding that
"constant interference in politics by the military is killing
democracy and Nigerians will never learn."
He described himself as an ardent federalist who will "fight and die
for the unity of Nigeria", stating that if anyone insists on
dismembering Nigeria, "such a person has another thinking coming
because Nigeria will never be divided by anyone or group of people
for their selfish intentions".
Also speaking at the banquet attended by former Inspector General of
Police, Alhaji M.D. Yusuf; Major-General Olu Bajowa; former Senate
President Chief Evans Enwerem as well as Senator Ita-Giwa, the
special guest, Vice-President Abubakar praised Wayas for piloting the
Senate at the time in a dignified manner.
Abubakar, who praised Governor Donald Duke for honouring "an
important Nigerian and elder statesman such as Wayas", was of the
view that the erstwhile Senate President may be the first President
of Nigeria from the South South region, acknowledging also that the
former Senate President was a detribalised Nigerian who has always
been fighting for the good of Nigerians.
Duke, on his part, observed that Wayas would be remembered for his
dogged fight for the creation of South Eastern State which
metamorphosed into Cross River State, describing Wayas as an enigma.
"There is more to Joseph Wayas than most of us can see," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Nutrition in Relation to Pregnancy
Headline: Nigeria: Nutrition in Relation to Pregnancy
Body_Text:
ONE of the noble roles of a woman in this world of matter is
child-bearing. As we already know, this state of pregnancy is not an
illness. It is a stage in the life cycles of a woman, and she must be
well prepared for it from deep within her. Indeed, her being had been
so structured. The attraction of the right baby (child) must follow
the law of attraction of similar species. But she has to pray
fervently for this right baby to come through her. Her immediate
environment must be permeated with lobe so as to attract this right
spirit. The type of friends she keeps and others which she has direct
dealings with may influence the incarnation of her baby.
Social activities must be cut down until mid-way through pregnancy
when the spirit takes possession of the body.
So far on the spiritual preparation or in ward preparedness for the
reception of a baby.
Now to dietary consideration. This is of vital importance in the
formation and until the birth of the baby. The following are
programmes or schedules of suggested diets for an expectant mother.
Also other activities which relate to healthier out-look are also
included:
(A) First thing in the morning, take a glass of lukewarm water and
orange juice. This juice must be freshly made.
Or cut a lemon into four pieces and add the juice of a quarter to a
glass of water instead of the orange juice
Or take a cup of any of the following herbal teas, pepper mint,
Rosehip, Red sorrel (sobo), lemon grass tea.
Or freshly juiced of any sweet fruit in season, e.g. tangerine,
pawpaw, etc. Dilute juice half glass with half glass water.
After this drink, take a walk for 15 minutes in a quiet path of your
area. Avoid crowded area and if possible go in company of your
husband. The time of the walk is good for reflection and imagination
of what your baby will look like. It is also a time for gaining
strength and inhaling lots of oxygen and throwing off static energy.
If due to weather or other reasons you are unable to go for a walk,
follow simple exercises of lying down with your back flat on the
floor and stretching your whole body and relaxing the joints,
especially the highs etc. When you go for walks, always remember that
you can rest if tired.
After the fore-going, the next thing to do is known as dry brush
massage. The inside of the outer cover of coconut fruit is natural
enough to use as brush for a dry brush massage or old used local
sponge. Rub this all over the body but carefully with the abdominal
area not to do any rigorous brushing, especially in the last few
weeks before delivery. Brush the whole of the body as if bathing and
bath or mild water of temperature too cold to bear.
Breakfast must include some sweet fruits in season, e.g. bananas
apples, grapefruits, or any other including pawpaw. Or one cup of
yogurt, goat's milk or fresh cow's milk. A handful of sun flower
seeds, Beniseeds (Ridi-Hausa) are also good.
Another choice is a bowl of fresh fruits salad or cottage cheese; Or
wheat - meal prepared as cereal (whole grain cereal means cereal
ground with the whole skin).
One or two fertile eggs soft-boiled can also be preferred. Separate
yolk form other part.
Mid-morning snack
You can eat an apple, banana or other fruit or one cup of yogurt or a
slice of cheese or an avocado pear one hour before lunch take your
supplements e.g. brewers yeast powder (B-Group Vit.) bone meal in
powder form and others.
Lunch
1.) Bean cake (moin moin) with vegetable. Dish can be taken or beans
with vegetable or salads
Or
2.) A bowl of any grain prepared as tuwo to be eaten with any soup
with green (Ewedu) etc. or bean soup (Gbegiri).
3.) Potatoes, yams, green beans, (local beans),
Or
Two slices wholemeal bread with two slices of cheese vary according
to taste or desire. Afternoon siesta so important whenever possible.
Dinner to be vegetable salads or bowl of fresh fruits with bread .
Bed time snack
One glass of milk, one cup of yogurt, one apple eaten slowly and
masticated well.
One cup of herbal tea sweetened with honey. Pepper mint or rosehip
tea can be taken
Stretch Marks or Stress Marks?
This area of stretch marks is always of concern to women. Women
generally do not desire these stretch marks.
It may be taken as an avoidable side effect of pregnancy but it needs
not occur if proper nutrition has been taken before the pregnancy and
during pregnancy.
Stretch marks are results of the body's weakened activities of
cellular and collagen integrity. It is an inability to cope or meet
the demands of stress. It means that the skin is not elastic enough
to shrink back to the original size without leaving marks.
Deficiencies of some minerals and vitamins are involved and these are
vitamins E and C - Complex of zinc, silicon and pathothenic acid.

Original_Search_Title: Focus on Communal Farming Project
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Focus on Communal Farming Project
Body_Text:
Bodokro — While heavy rains have pounded
southern Cote d'Ivoire in recent weeks, wells in the northern area of
Bodokro are virtually dry. A canal built recently in the locality may have
to wait a few weeks before it collects its first rainwater.
The dry spell has ruined this year's peanut harvest and threatens to have a
similar effect on the soy bean crop in Bodokro, some 60 km northwest of Cote
d'Ivoire's second largest city, Bouake. The greatest hope for farmers there
is to have a constant source of water, the accountant of the men's farming
cooperative in Bodokro, N'Guessan Kouakou Augustin, told IRIN in Bouake, 350
km north of Abidjan.
Insufficient rainfall is one of two major obstacles farmers face in northern
Cote d'Ivoire. The second is a lack of modern equipment that would allow
them to work more efficiently. Members of Bodokro's men and women's farming
cooperatives have received some hand-held tools from the World Food
Programme (WFP) but they would like to work with machines so as to increase
their production.
Bodokro is one of 37 sites selected for the Projet Bas-Fond (PBF) - an
agricultural project supported by WFP and the Japanese and Ivorian
governments. The PBF, whose main aim is to ensure food security for rural
communities and promote rural development, is being implemented in a zone
north of the capital, Yamoussoukro, that the World Bank classified in 1998
as the poorest area in Cote d'Ivoire.
The project, now in its pilot phase, had been delayed by the tense political
situation in the country in 2000 following a military coup and various other
upheavals.
Nineteen of the sites are located around the town of Bondoukou in the
northeast, while 18 are near Bouake. A total of 2,942 participants have been
identified, 1,491 of them women. Rice and vegetables - including peanuts,
corn, cassava, yams, tomatoes and sesame - are the two main products.
To begin the project, 758 ha of ricefields and 48.8 ha of vegetables are
being cultivated. By the end of the pilot phase, the project's backers plan
to increase the rice acreage to 1,000 ha and the vegetable crop area to 55
ha. They also plan to devote about 50 ha of land to plantain production.
Sites are selected after assessments by teams from various institutions
including the Agence nationale d'appui au developpement rurale (ANADER),
WFP's principal operating partner, with which the UN agency has signed a
technical support agreement for US $273,000.
Other participating institutions include the Association for International
Cooperation in Agriculture and Forestry (AICAF), a Japanese NGO that is
providing technical support and Cote d'Ivoire's Ministry of Family, Women
and Children which is monitoring the social impact of the project. The West
Africa Rice Development Association (WARDA) is providing rice-related
technical support. WFP says it hopes to obtain the participation of other
institutions in the provision of education and training, and the compilation
of socioeconomic data.
The US $8-million project is expected to ensure a minimum of food security
for people in the impoverished target areas, promote rural development, and
provide financial stability for the beneficiaries through the sale of
surplus food.
WFP has decided to link this lowland project with a school-feeding programme
(*) which it has been implementing since 1989: a portion of the PBF output
is to be used to provide lunches for the children. The ultimate goal is for
communities to provide all the food needed for the school canteens in about
four years.
The National Directorate for School Canteens and WFP "agreed to build a
strong synergy between the PBF project and the school-feeding programme,
recognising that the objectives of both projects were complementary", WFP
said in its 2000 project report. "Accordingly, the existence of a school
canteen in the village is now considered to be one of the main criteria for
the selection of a new site."
Soil type, topography, hydrology, poverty, motivation, interest for the
project and capacity to work in groups are some of the other main selection
criteria. Once a site is identified, rural extension officers explain to
farmers the project's framework, strategies, objectives and the type of
technical assistance that will be provided.
The main obstacle the project faces is locals' unwillingness to buy into it,
ANADER Director-General Yao Kouassi, said. The key to success is to ensure
that they fully participate in the project so that they can experience its
benefits, he said.
Farmers are encouraged to participate in the project through the
"Food-for-Work" strategy under which they receive rations of rice donated by
Japan in exchange for work performed in the fields, including land clearing,
digging, and helping to survey the land.
The three kilogrammes of rice participants receive for each day's work is
given to the women to ensure that the food goes to the entire family.
Food-for-Work aims to help households cope during the lean agricultural
season and reduce their food bills so that savings are used to meet other
needs.
It is also a way of involving women, who constitute the project's main
target group. It aims to give them more decision-making power within the
community, enables them to have greater control of resources such as food,
land and money, and boosts their self-confidence, project sponsors said.
Women account for 51 percent of participants and WFP hopes this figure will
soon reach 60 percent, in line with its goal of committing 60 percent of
project resources and interventions to helping women in countries where they
lag behind.
The project's national coordinator, Germain Akoubia, said he was pleased to
see women engaged in traditionally male activities but he noted that strong
female participation could be a sign of food scarcity since men's labour in
the fields was not enough to feed their families.
One of the major effects the project has had on women is to reduce their
desire to head south to look for work in major towns, from where they
usually came back with no earnings and "dirty diseases" (STDs), the
president of the Bodokro women's cooperative, Kouakou Eugenie, said. Women
now have an activity in which they can participate fully and they are
motivated to do so, she added.
WFP and its partners hope that, by the end of the year, the number of rice
and vegetable plots will have increased. They also hope to provide, by then,
another 10 boreholes, 25 warehouses and other facilities as needed.
WFP said its immediate concern was to obtain positive results that would
convince the Ivorian and Japanese authorities, and other possible partners,
to finance and expand the project to other impoverished areas.
The idea, Kouakou said, is "to get us out of poverty".
[(*) For more on the schoolfeeding programme see
http://www.reliefweb.int/IRIN/wa/countrystories/cotedivoire/20010615.phtml ]

Original_Search_Title: Industrialising Edo State
Headline: Nigeria: Industrialising Edo State
Body_Text:
As Edo State government takes giant stride in industrialisation,
residents should be patient for the gains to materialise writes Tom
Anuge.
It is on record and there is no gain saying that agriculture was the
main stay of Nigerian's economy before the discovery of oil in 1959
in commercial qualities. We had cocoa, cotton, groundnut, rubber and
staple foods like yam, cassava and millet in large quantities. The
country earned a lot of foreign exchange from the exportation of
these products and our per capita income was high.
But with the discovery of the money-spinning product - oil, the need
to continue to cultivate agricultural products was thrown into the
sea. So much money was realised from the sales of oil that it became
a problem knowing what to do with the available cash. We then decided
to engage on a spending spree importing all that took our fancy. The
result was that agriculture was completely neglected; and therefore,
the base for a sound industrial take-off was lost.
States were contended to sit akimbo and wait for monthly allocation
from the Federation Account in order to meet their needs. It is now
that the cry for resource control is on that we have realised that
there is need to diversify. Even though, oil brought some degree of
prosperity to this country, it has also helped to keep us lazy: with
the result that other sectors of the economy have been grossly and
sadly neglected.
It is a known fact that oil is not a renewable resource. As a result,
our oil reserves would one day be exhausted. Even before such a
period, it is also possible that advancement in technology would make
the use of oil less attractive as alternative and cheaper source of
power would have been discovered. When this happens, the country
would be in trouble as it would be difficult to meet the needs of the
inhabitants. The only way generations yet unborn would be saved from
such planlessness is to begin to industrialise now.
It is on the basis of this that the executive governor of Edo State,
Chief Lucky Nosakhare Igbinedion, has taken a giant stride to
industrialise Edo State in the true sense of the word. The commitment
of the governor in this regard can be seen in the establishment of
small and medium scale industries in the state.
There are the fertilizer blending plant at Auchi, the granite
polishing plant at Ikpeshi, Cassavita processing factory at Uromi,
fruit juice at Ehor and a host of others. This is a welcome
development, as activities in this sector of the Nigerian economy
have been sadly below expectations.
In the last decade or so, aggregate output measured by the Gross
Domestic Product (GDP), has been growing at the stunted rate of
between 2.2 per cent and 3.8per cent respectively.
The Edo State government is set to reverse this trend. It has been
observed that the achievement of the real sector has been hampered
largely by low level investments, weak infrastructural facilities,
and dismal performance of equipment and inappropriate technology in
the agricultural sector.
These and other problems of economic growth, this government has set
out to tackle by establishing these industries. A visit to some of
these industries sites reveal that the factories have been built
applying the latest technology available. There is no doubt
therefore, that this laudable initiative of the government would
eventually take Edo State to a permanent plateau of industrialisation
and prosperity.
It is the hope of the state government that these efforts begin to
yield fruits. Edo State would no longer be described as a "civil
service state", but rather, an industrial giant in the comity of
states. We have the human resources to harness the mineral and
agricultural products which abound in the state. And the government
is not hesitating to provide the enabling environment.
While Dr. Sam Ogbemudia is today being remembered and acknowledged
for the development of sports and social infrastructure in Edo State,
the late Professor Ambrose Alli is remembered for the development of
education in the state. The only area left is the industrialisation
of the state which has attracted the attention and goodwill of the
present government. Chief Lucky Igbinedon, like his predecessor,
would also be remembered for it.
Inspite of all these, skeptics wonder why new industries are being
set up when the ones on ground are moribund. They need to be told
that even though Nigeria and other developing countries have shown
interest in industrialisation through small and medium scale
industries (SMEs), Edo State (formerly Midwest and Bendel States),
had lagged behind in no small measure in embracing the concept and
putting the necessary infrastructure in place. The time to reverse
the trend is now.
Government wants to development the state and SMEs are relevant in
the achievement of growth and full employment; development of local
technology and stimulation of indigenous entrepreneurship. Other
factors which are stimulating the establishment of these industries
are the potential to foster linkages within and between the industry
vis-a-vis other sectors of the economy. All of which will accelerate
rural development and promote the utilisation of domestic resources.
An evaluation of the performance of small and medium scale industries
in areas where they have been established and with respect to
employment generation, contribution to the growth of industrial
output, degree of local sourcing of raw materials, shows that they
have made a positive impact on the life of the people. And, this is
what government has envisaged for the people of Edo State.
However, these contributions notwithstanding, small scale industries
in Nigeria face many problems such as lack of functional
infrastructural facilities. This has compelled most of them to resort
to provision of water, electricity, roads etc., by themselves. All
these lead to extra cost and often hinder production.
We are happy in Edo State that these problems will not inhibit our
desire to industrialise the state. Already, government has negotiated
and signed an agreement with a gas turbine firm to supply
uninterrupted power to all nooks and crannies of the state. The issue
of water is also being vigorously tackled. We are aware of the
discussion with a South African firm for this purpose, and also, of
the numerous boreholes being sunk for the purpose and interest of all
and sundry. Rural communities are also being linked by massive road
construction projects in all corners of the state.
In recognition of the potential role of SME's, various measures and
programmes have been designed and are being executed by the state
government to encourage their development. One of such measure is the
state's Micro Credit Scheme, which is open to any eligible Edo person
who wants to set up a small or medium sized industry.
Even though new companies have been set up and others in the
pipeline, it is not the intention of government to actively take part
in the management of these companies. Privatisation is the concept in
vogue, and the state government has already embraced it. This is the
reason that the old and ailing companies, as well as the new ones
would all be privatised. Government is only willing to maintain a low
equity participation in them. This is the trend the world over. Since
individuals do not have the required resources to set up these
companies, government has to set them up and provide the enabling
environment for their smooth operations. Through privatisation,
government gets back its initial investment which can now be
channeled to other areas. This practice allows individuals who would
not have been able to muster the resources to establish companies, to
now become part-owners (shareholders), in blue chip companies.
There is no doubt that the resources available to the state
government would not be able to cope with the rate at which the
governor would want to industrialise the state. It is, therefore,
necessary that government take advantage of the new funding window
about to be opened for small-scale industries in Nigeria. The
arrangement which is between the Nigeria Industrial Development Bank
(NIDB), and the Industrial Development Corporation (IDC), of South
Africa would finance companies in the mining, agro-processing,
energy, textiles, information technology, communication, and tourism.
We know Edo State would take the lead to explore this beautiful
opportunity. Some of these projects definitely have long gestation
periods and as a result, the gain would not be immediate. We,
therefore, implore Edo residents to be patient as these laudable
programmes come to fruition.
Mr. Anuge writes from Benin city.

Original_Search_Title: Statistics Reports Cautioned Inflationary Pressure
Headline: Nigeria: Statistics Reports Cautioned Inflationary Pressure
Body_Text:
Reports of the federal office of statistics (FOS) has indicated a
continued inflationary pressure in the economy as the composite
consumer price Index (CCPI) which level of retail measures changes in
the average prices paid by consumers in both urban and rural areas,
rose by 2.9 per cent in the month of May, 2001, against April, 2001
record.
The index which stood at 4330.2 compared with 4207.1, according to
FOS, rose as a result of increases in the prices of food items and
drinks, though prices of kerosine and transport fares fell in many
centres of the economy.
FOS reported that the average consumer price level moved up by 23.0
percent between May 2000 and May 2001 a slight decline in the 2.3 per
cent observed in the preceding month of the same period. The price
indices in the urban and rural areas surged by 22.5 and 23.0 percent
respectively.
The changes notices in the percentage of an average CCPI for the
twelve month period under review which terminates in May 2001 as
against the previous twelve month was 15.9 per cent..
This infact means that a market price of consumer goods and services
was bought with N100.00 during the twelve-month period ending May
2000 and sold for N115.7 for the period ending May 2-001.
The composite food index took an upward movement by 5.2 percent in
May 2001 this was necessitated by increase in price of staples e.g
Gari, yam, tuber, plantain, beans and rice. The prices in processed
foods and edible oil rose in many centres.
Consequently, between May 2000 and May 2001 advanced by 30.2 per
cent, the average annual rate of rise of the composite food index in
the twelve month period ending May 2001 recorded an 11.3 percent
increase.
The composite consumer indexes less farm produce in exclusion of the
prices of agricultural produce dropped by 0.3 per cent in May 2001,
the index rose by 11.3 percent, the average annual rate rise of the
index was 20.9 per cent for the twelve month period ending May 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis May Lead to Revolution
Headline: Nigeria: Food Crisis May Lead to Revolution
Body_Text:
Calabar — The rising cost of staple food in Nigeria may lead to a revolution if
the Federal Government does not take urgent steps to check the trend,
Second Republic Senate President, Dr. Joseph Wayas has warned.
Wayas was of the view that, governments in some parts of the world
have been brought down by rising cost of staple food prices,
insisting that Nigerians possess the ability and resources to feed
themselves.
Speaking at a party organised in his honour by the Cross River State
Government for his 60th birthday, Senator Wayas said the government
should not allow the peoples' anger to degenerate to violence because
of hunger occasioned by the unaffordable prices of foodstuff.
At the banquet in Calabar, weekend, attended by Vice-President Atiku
Abubakar, Wayas said: "the situation becomes worrisome when people
can no longer afford gari, rice and yam which are locally cultivated
bearing in mind that in majority of the 778 local governments in
Nigeria about 600 of them are farmers."
"We may wake up one morning and we find people stoning cars on the
streets. They will not be selective in their violence, they simply
will stone any car in sight believing that these are the people who
have made food unavailable" Wayas said.
On the cause of the present socio-political problem in the country,
he said, every blame must be placed at the door of military people
who stray into politics, "creating so much problems both
economically, politically and otherwise."
Wayas likened Nigeria's poor showing in democracy to a child which is
just preparing to walk, pointing out that if such a child is always
lifted from the ground by the parents rather than allowed to learn
the process of walking "it will take quite some time for such a child
to know how to walk."
Consequently, "Nigeria must be allowed to walk (learn the art of
democracy) and if mistakes are made, we should be given the
opportunity to learn from these mistakes," adding that, "the constant
interference in politics by the Nigerian military is killing
democracy and Nigerians will never learn."
He described himself as an ardent federalist who will "fight and die
for the unity of Nigeria," stating that, if anyone insists on
dismembering Nigeria, "such a person has another thing coming because
Nigeria will never be divided by anyone or group of people for their
selfish intentions."
Also at the forum attended by former Inspector-General of Police,
Alhaji M. D. Yusuf, Major General Olu Bajowa, former senate president
Chief Evan Enwerem as well as Senator Ita-Giwa, the special guest,
Vice President Abubakar praised Wayas for piloting the senate at the
time in a dignified manner.

Original_Search_Title: Cooperatives Urged to Be Open
Headline: Ghana: Cooperatives Urged to Be Open
Body_Text:
Mr. Okyere Darko, Nkoranza District Co-operative officer on Monday called on co-operative societies and groups to exhibit transparency, accountability and probity in their activities.
Addressing the fifth annual general meeting of the Techiman District branch of the Co-operative Yam Producers and Marketing Society Limited, he advised the members to unite to promote their welfare.
Mr. James Okyere, Deputy District Co-operative Officer said the society made a net surplus of more than 24 million cedis in the 1999/2000 financial year as against 22.7 million cedis in the previous year.
Mr. Okyere said the society's total income of 54,561,036 cedis was derived from the sale of yams, bank interests and entrance fees.The society spent 30,277,748 cedis on committees' allowances, wages and salaries, transport, stationery, donations and funerals.
Mr Okyere said since the society was established no member had contributed to the share capital, adding that 2,512,934 cedis being dividend on shares accruing from the net surplus has been turned into shares for the members.

Original_Search_Title: Author's Latest Novel, Purple Yams, Released
Headline: South Africa: A South African Abroad
Body_Text:
Shortlisted for this year's Sanlam Literary Award, Sheila Roberts's
new novel, Purple Yams, is just out from Penguin. Stephen Gray interviewed
her on her recent return to Johannesburg.
Since 1975 when she made her debut with the prize-winning collection,
Outside Life's Feast, Sheila Roberts has sustained an impressive career in
publishing.
Two later collections have followed, and she has three previous novels
to her credit -- the first, He's My Brother (1977), was banned in a
notorious case for the way it portrayed foul-spoken South African men.
That year she went into voluntary exile in the United States, where she
teaches creative writing at the University of Wisconsin at Milwaukee.  One
of her recent stories was included in The Picador Book of African
Stories.
Q: Quite unexpectedly your new novel pulls your bipolar world
into a triangle.  It's set, not in the US, but in Japan.
A: I got the chance of a visiting professorship there and being single
by then I was in an ideal situation to tackle it. So I found myself a lone
professional woman, for more than two years. Halfway through the loneliness
