a long way to go in the search for all round development.
Nonetheless, investment in projects with multiplier effects, and more
importantly, inroad into the rural areas are distinctively the
highpoints of the administration's efforts since assumption of office
in May 1999. In four strategic areas - rural water, roads and
electricity supply; education; health and industry - the
administration has made the acclaimed testimonial impact. The result
of this are praises from many who freely comment on the
administration's efforts so far; and prayers to Almighty God by the
silent downtrodden majority in the rural areas for the architects of
the new dawn.
Yet, this extension of democracy dividends to the hinterland has
become the taunted albatross of the administration. Though most
people acknowledge that the feat is unprecedented, and indeed
commendable, some others voice a preference for the development of
the urban areas, particularly Lafia, the state capital, even if
sacrificially at the expense of other projects in other areas. The
champions of this agenda posit that every state must have a rallying
point for its people, a city they would be proud of. Lafia, they
contend, is yet to attain such a status, nearly five years after it
became a capital.
But the geography and spread of the population of the state appear to
give impetus to the style adopted by the administration. Nasarawa is
agrarian with majority of its citizens living in the rural areas
where they engage in farming. Every council in the state is an
agricultural haven, hence the state is described by people as the
food basket of the country, especially in yam and cattle. It is also
the home of solid minerals which yearn to be tapped. Given this
setting, what is known of the state in terms of economic and social
life takes place in the rural areas.
Besides, the area that makes up the state, which was carved out from
the former Plateau State on October 1, 1996, was essentially the
lower Plateau that suffered neglect, deprivation and
underdevelopment, the relative academic advantage of its people
notwithstanding.
Adamu, 56, lawyer and businessman, did analyse the situation before
he took a stand on the policy thrust of his administration. He said
so in an interview with The Guardian. He is in a position to
critically review events in the state, having played politics in the
area for over 22 years during which he held the positions of chairman
of the defunct National Party of Nigeria (NPN) in the former Plateau
State, governorship candidate of the United Nigeria Congress Party
(UNCP), Nasarawa State and Minister of State for Works and Housing,
among others, before becoming the governor of the state.
He said of the situation: "From the word go, when I set out to seek
political office as the chief executive of the state, I never had any
dreams as to the tenure going to be a retreat. I never expected to
have time for pleasure and things like that, or to come here for
whatever goodies or pleasure one would get. Therefore, I was not
surprised to see the enormity of the challenges that confronted me as
soon as I was sworn in as governor".
The challenge? As a young state, Nasarawa is bound to experience some
teething developmental problems. Its rural nature and years of
neglect have been accentuated by the poor revenue base occasioned,
quite significantly, by its placement among the states that receive
the least statutory allocation. Denied of take-off grant on creation
as was the pattern earlier, the state could not make the initial
expected quantum leap in infrastructure development, particularly in
the capital.
Absence of self-help efforts in community development is also
pronounced. On another front, Governor Adamu characterised the
situation he met on ground: "Going through Nasarawa State, you don't
have a single private enterprise that is employing as many as 25
people. There is no company that is known to us that is actually into
production that is rolling anything worth N5 million as turnover in a
year".
The result of these are dearth of basic infrastructure, unemployment,
poverty and general underdevelopment. But the democratic dispensation
of the past two years has brought in a flurry of activities in the
state - political, economic, social and cultural activities - which
have improved the lives of the people and presented a ray of hope to
them.
Foremost in this direction is the administration's investment in
education. The sector has recorded a lot of improvement, including
the establishment of more institutions of learning - primary, post
primary and tertiary, improving the learning environment and welfare
of teachers.
The greatest investment in the sector appears to be in post primary
education. The last two years have marked the building of six new
science schools - two in each of the two senatorial zones; a boarding
school in each of the 13 councils; and general rehabilitation of all
schools in the state: a project that involved the building of new
classrooms and domitaries in schools that were hard pressed for
accommodation.
Some of the schools benefited from the provision of water and
electricity programme of the state government. Supply of free text
and exercise books as well as almost free boarding schools are also
part of the investment. The same applied at the primary school level.
The state was among the first to launch the Universal Basic Education
(UBE) scheme in the country.
A major aspect of the efforts put into tertiary education is the
establishment of a state polytechnic in Lafia, which admitted the
first batch of students in 2000/2001 session. A proposed university
will tentatively to take off this year.
A country-wide teachers recruitment exercise saw the engagement of
over 1000 new teachers into the secondary school system. Some
moral-boosting allowances were introduced for the teachers.
The administration's determination to go this far in the education
sector is described by Governor Adamu as a conscious effort he is
making to ensure that youths in the state are sufficiently equipped
for the future. And the result of the huge investment in education is
the degree of seriousness noticeable among pupils and teachers in the
school system.
The State Commissioner for Education, Dr. Emmanuel Okpede, also gave
an insight into the matter: "When we came into office in May 1999, we
were not left in doubt about the degree of neglect which education
had suffered. Nobody was left in doubt about the state of decay in
which the infrastructure had undergone. Nobody was also left in
oblivion as to the poor performance of our students in examinations",
he said, adding that the summary of the situation was that "in all
sectors of the economy, Nasarawa State had problem of manpower. In
all areas that required specialisation, we had problem there". He was
glad to note that "the situation has changed completely". Yes, it has
changed. For instance, candidates from the school system performed
brilliantly in the last Senior School Certificate and NECO
examinations.
The thrust of the rural electricity and water supply programme of the
administration is to, within the shortest possible time, reach as
many communities as possible, which have long expected a touch of
government. To this end, the electricity programme involved a
three-pronged approach, namely: the connection of some communities to
the national grid, use of generators and solar-powered equipment to
supply electricity to some others, particularly in the rural areas.
For a start, the administration was said to have earmarked such
projects for each of the 147 electoral wards in the state. Already
commissioned are solar-powered electricity projects at Kwara in
Wamba, Ungwan-Zaria in Akwanga, Mada Station in Nasarawa Egon, and
some others in Mararaba Gongon, Rifi Wamba, etc.
The water supply programme involved repair works on the Lafia Water
Scheme and rehabilitation of the gigantic Akwanga Water Scheme, which
serve many communities in the three senatorial zones of the state.
Solar-powered schemes planned for all the electoral wards have been
realised in over 20 areas so far. These include Wowen, Wulko, Mada
Station, Endehu, Masaka and Gora. Others are Marasaba, Agwada, GSS
Agwada, Wamba, Assakio, Agyragu, Marmara, Marasaba Udege and Giza.
Some others are awaiting commissioning.
However, some people have criticised the administration for embarking
on small-scale water and electricity schemes that serve few
communities, and have the higher rate of system breakdown. To these
people, major projects that cut across several communities, even
councils, would have served the state better.
But as Adamu put it, a government desirous to reach as many
communities - which are not ordinarily close-knit - as possible, and
which has lean resource, cannot afford to embark on white elephant
projects. In his words, "it is a deliberate policy to spread out and
maximise the reach".
In the health sector, the setting up of a referal centre by the
Federal Government and the policy of establishing a general hospital
in each of the councils and upgrading of clinics, have helped to
improve healthcare delivery in the state. The administration also
built two training institutions for health workers - a school of
nursing in Lafia and school of health technology in Keffi.
In Nasarawa, government is still the sole employer of labour as there
are no industries and businesses that attract a sizeable labour
force. The state government's efforts in the area of setting up of
industries are yet to mature as all the five industries the
administration has planned are at the foundation laying stage. These
include the packaging factory at Akwanga, whose foundation-laying
ceremony was conducted by the Minister of Industries, Chief Kola
Jamodu, and the ultra-modern beef processing factory at Masaka near
Abuja. Others are the fertilizer factory in Lafia, precious minerals
lapidary plant, Keffi, ropes production factory, Akwanga and
information technology centre, Lafia. The Karu International Market,
financed jointly by the government and Fortune Bank, is now letting.
Ignatius Aboki, senator in the Second Republic and former chairman of
the Bank of the North, commended the administration's efforts. His
words: "My impression is that the governor is quite energetic, quite
ambitious. He has laid down structures that will lift up the state".
The administration has also invested in other areas such as
agriculture, housing, roads, tourism, solid minerals, information and
sports. Of note is the introduction of a people's forum where, in
each senatorial zone, government officials and the people assemble to
exchange ideas on the administration of the state. Questions are
asked and answers given on government's policies and programmes.
Comments are also free.
According to the Information Commissioner, Labaran Maku, the people's
forum concept is akin to the community hall system whereby the people
and their leaders exchange ideas on the administration of their area.
The last of the forum was held in Keffi on April 10.
But Lafia, the state capital, is still to record significant
face-lift. Several new buildings, government buildings, housing
estates, secretariat, government house, etc., are springing up; but
the road network and environmental sanitation still leave much to be
desired. The administration has commenced the building of some
township roads, but the magnitude of the problem is such that
requires much more investment and a quality-driven construction
company.
The strides recorded in the past two years have been attributed to
the harmonious relationship existing among all the tiers of
government in the state. The administration's acceptance to pay the
minimum wage soon after it was proposed in May last year also worked
to ensure industrial peace in the state. This has also helped to
realise these projects some of which were executed by direct labour.
The crisis in Toto, between the Egburas and Bassas, has remained a
sore-point in the state till date. Elections have not been held in
the area. Few weeks ago, the administration set up a committee to
administer the council and ensure return of peace. It is hoped that
peace will eventually return there.
Expectations are high on what the government can do for the people.
Every community comes with thanks for what it has so far received or
promised, but with a bag full of requests for other needs that should
be addressed, the challenge of governing Nasarawa State is indeed
enormous.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Score Card: Hunger, Poverty Grips Country
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Score Card: Hunger, Poverty Grips Country
Body_Text:
Lagos — Midway into the first term of the democratic experiment and the
tenure of the PDP-led Federal Government of President Olusegun
Obasanjo, Nigerians sink deeper into hunger and hardship. PHILIPSON
ABAH writes
Exactly two years ago, Abuja and all the thirty six state capitals
were aglow with ceremonies of handing over by military to a
democratically elected government. Parades were mounted by members of
the armed forces and cultural performances were staged on the
historic day.
To mark the day, long speeches sugar-coated in the most alluring
prose were read to Nigerians who applauded thunderously at all venues
of the ceremony or from the comfort of their homes where they watched
the live proceedings of the function at Abuja and their various
states on television.
That day, Nigerians were not clapping with anxiety because of the
melodious voices of the people reading the speeches. The rounds of
applause were induced by the hope of a better, hunger-free and
comfortable Nigeria painted in the inaugural speeches.
Now, the same Nigerians who were so excited as a resullt of those
promises have discovered that it was just another antic of
politicians. During his inaugural speech, President Olusegun Obasanjo
promised to change the face of the country in one year. Specifically,
he emphasised without any display of cynicism, that his government
would, among many other things, provide for the suffering masses of
this country; food security through sound agricultural policies,
create and enabling environment for investments create jobs and also
introduce policies that would stimulate macro economic growth. This
same promises were replicated in the states by the various state
governors.
The hope of Nigerians were therefore brightened by these promises.
For a start, it appeared as if all was going to be well. With some
pronouncements on the operations of government parastatals like NEPA,
and NITEL, the introduction of the Poverty Alleviation Programme
(PAP), and some moves that looked as if targetted at provoking a
horde of investors into the country, Nigerians started to count their
eggs before they were hatched.
The hope for better living was further heightened when, in May last
year, President Obasanjo announced a minimum wage of N7500 to
government workers. Civil servants who had for years been pauperised
by insulting wages lauded the move.
That the government of President Obasanjo has lost focus, began to
dawn on Nigerians when firstly, the Poverty Alleviation Programme was
hijacked by some PDP stalwarts who milked the nation dry through it,
thereby diminishing its essence. Thousands of jobless youths who were
kept waiting for the PAP manna became more frustrated, having waited
endlessly.
Instead of taking adequate care of other variables before considering
the issue of fuel pricing, Obasanjo's government surprised Nigerians
with a directive to the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation
(NNPC) to commence the sale of Premium Motor Spirit (PMS) at N30 from
its former price of N20. Though the Nigerian Labour Congress
ferociously battled with the government which finally slashed the
price. The move precipitated a chain of reaction in the market place.
Apart from the occasional fuel scarcity which was initially thought
to have gone with the military regime, transport fares all over the
country went up. The spiralling effect of this became conspicuous on
the prices of foodstuff which began an upward rise in price.
Yet again, the government began moves to endorse the deregulation of
the down stream sub-sector of the oil industry, not minding what
effect it may have on the domestic economy.
Garri, a food item considered the most affordable for the Nigerian
masses began to soar in price. Today, a bag of garri which used to
cost about N1500 now goes for as high as N4,600. A bag of imported
rice which was selling for N2,400 is now N3,500, signalling an
impending food crisis.
In the face of all these, the value of the naira continues to dip
with the government watching in complete helplessness.
Nigerians, who before the emergence of the civilian government were
groaning under poverty are today worse off as the grip of misery
continues to tighten around their necks. More than ever before,
joblessness is on the increase as the governments at various levels
have sacked more persons than they have employed since they came to
power.
NEPA's endemic problem and the crisis in the oil industry, including
other problems associated with the dearth of infrastructure, have
made it increasingly difficult for companies to survive, thus forcing
them to embark on retrenchment in the guise of restructuring.
The man at the helm of affairs does not feel ruffled by this
developments. Instead, all he does to keep himself busy is to shuttle
the entire globe. If P.M.News checks are correct, President Obasanjo
has made 45 trips since his assumption of office. Obasanjo's claim
that his trips are to solicit for foreign investments, amongst other
reasons, have already lost meaning as investigations have shown that
apart from few construction and service rendering firms, the
country's economic climate remains far from being attractive to
foreign investors who are said to be scared of the rising wave of
crime in the country and the high cost of investment following NEPA
and telecommunication inefficiency coupled also with the bad roads
that litter every part of the country. Another problem which
discourages foreign investors, it was gathered, is the absence of a
good transportation network.
Sadly, the president appears not to be admitting that these are part
of the problems hindering economic prosperity of the country. During
this year's May Day celebration, President Obasanjo told a
hungry-looking gathering of Nigerians that the option out of the
economic doldrums is for people to de-emphasise foreign products and
patronise locally made ones. This statement portrays him as a leader
who does not know that in his country, a common barbing salon can not
operate effectively without a generator; the least of which cost over
N50,000.
Recently, government officials, lauding Obasanjo's achievements in
the last two years pointed to the increased foreign reserve of the
country which they claimed has moved from $4b to about $7b. This, to
the majority of Nigerians who are wallowing in penury, does not mean
anything as it has not helped to put any meal on their table at a
cheaper cost.
With the depressing economic situation, not a few people affirm that
the Obasanjo government has not met the aspirations of Nigerians.
This opinion is shared by some objective privileged Nigerians as well
as the most indigent obscure ones who are now living as endangered
species.
According to Air Comdr. Dan Suleiman, a retired top military officer,
who spoke on the two years anniversary of President Obasanjo's
government, "talking about the fruits of democracy, which in essence
means the economic and general well being of the people, I think
there is a serious shortfall here. A short fall in the sense that the
infrastructure that ought to be in place are yet to be firmly in
place."
A house wife, Mrs. Gloria Onyemachi, painted the picture of the agony
of the woman at home in this austere period. "With the current
situation, one can not buy anything from the market. The last time I
bought garri, it was N50, when I got to the market yesterday, it was
N250. One can not buy other things like yam or plantain. We are
talking of food which is a basic requirement of life. I pity the
woman who is under pressure from her husband and the children. For
me, I have not seen or felt what they call democratic dividend. I
also do not think that Obasanjo has any plans for us," she said.
However, despite the outcry against what is now considered as
Obasanjo's failure, his supporters are pleading for time. Two years
have discretely slipped by and the hope that was visible in the
horizon is gradually getting lost in the cloud.
Will Obasanjo, after taking stock of his mid-term report, hasten to
extend to Nigerians the well deserved democratic dividends.
Obasanjo himself does not seem to be honest in appraising himself as
he has immodestly awarded a pass mark to his government, giving very
frivolous explanations to the rising cost of foodstuffs and indeed
every item in the market.
However, the truth is that: "he who knows it, feels it." And so, the
verdict of the people is that Obasanjo has failed at least in his
first two years.

Original_Search_Title: A Town of Four Kings
Headline: Nigeria: A Town of Four Kings
Body_Text:
Oke-agbe in Ondo State gives a lie to the Yoruba proverb that two
heads cannot rule a town at the same time, writes Olusegun Ajiboye.
Oke-agbe does not hide its rural ways. In the headquarters of Akoko
North-West Local Government Area, men, women and children file back
from the stream with water laden clay pots. From the goats that walk
on the tarmac to the people there is an ordered and unhurried pace to
life.
Oke-agbe, literally, "the hills shall be our boundaries" is a
conubation with a difference. Unlike in towns growing into
shoulder-lock with each other or swallowing up adjoining villages,
Oke-agbe came about its own way-everything planned and executed.
Its origin is not shrouded in the dim past but mercifully in the
early 1900s. As Pa Claudius Olanipekun Akorede, an 86-year-old native
told TEMPOLife, the story of Oke-agbe began in 1920. Nine different
communities had seen the wisdom in size and had begun talking on how
to abandon their old settlements and form a mega-settlement that
would mitigate the calamities small communities suffer in times of
war.
But only four communities followed through-Afa, Aje, Ido and Oge. The
hills they thought would be their boundaries soon saw homes on them.
Today, Oke-agbe is one seamless community with no demarcation-
natural or man made.
Yet, demarcations exist. Each of the communities is styled a kingdom.
So, Oke-agbe is made up of four kingdoms, each independent of the
other and none any less glamorous. Oke-agbe is a peculiar place. None
of the four kings reside in the town. The king nearest home lives in
Lagos. As Chief Aremo of Afa told TEMPOLife, "the kings are young and
educated men who are in their prime, hence the decisions to appoint
regents for them to help in the day-to-day activities of the town in
their absence." He added that these stay-away monarchs visit every
fortnight and are required by custom to be present at every festival.
There have been attempts at uniting these kingdoms by at least
getting one paramount ruler. But all such attempts have foundered
because the communities guard their independence jealously. "We don't
see ourselves throwing away our independence in the future," Aremo
asserted.
Of the four kingdoms that make up Oke-agbe, Oge is the only one with
a crown from Ile-Ife. On the origin of Oge, Pa Akorede has this to
say, "my fathers came from Ile-Ife. They left Ife at the same time
with other Yoruba towns such as Ajero, Oyo and so many other notable
Yoruba towns."
On the origin of Aje town, the Ewi of Aje, Chief Michael Oloinyo says
it can be traced to Ado-Ekiti. A pointer to this is the title of the
king which is the same as that of Ado-Ekiti. They both use the Ewi
title. This suggests that there is a link between the people of Aje
and Ado-Ekiti. Equally, their festivals are quite similar to those of
Ado-Ekiti.
The story of Afa as told by Chief Aremo, a retired teacher of repute
who has travelled far and near is a very rich one. The Afa comprises
several quarters with three principal ones, namely; Ogosi, Ogunna and
Ogotun with each claiming to have come from different places.
Chief Aremo stated categorically that the Ogotun quarters can be
linked to a town in Akoko North Local Government Area. The story of
the Ogunna people is said to be complex. Tradition has it that the
people had problems with their siblings over who should possess the
crown, a situation which led "our fathers to leave Owo." Chief Aremo
went further to say that the cognomen of the people is similar to
that of the Owo people. To give credence to this story he said, "you
see, the second in rank to the Olowo bears the same title with our
Oba, that is Ajana."
The Ido people of Oke-agbe have their own rich history too. According
to Prince E.O. Ojomu of the present ruling house in Ido, Oke-agbe,
"prior to 1924, the four towns lived in different parts, and because
of the inter-tribal wars which plagued the area in that period, the
people decided to come together to form a common front." He believes
his people came from the Igashi axis of Akoko. In the present day
Ondo State, according to him, the peaceful co-existence which has
prevailed since the inception of the town is based on trust among the
four settlements which make up Oke-agbe.
Cultural festivals are celebrated separately at different period of
the year by the four settlements. For example, the egungun festival
is celebrated by each settlement at different period of the year. The
only festival which enjoy universal celebration in the town are those
of the Christian and Muslim faiths.
The oldest church in Oke-agbe is the Saint George's Anglican Church,
built in 1929. The church is, however, enmeshed in controversy. The
Ido people on whose part of the settlement the church is located
claim ownership of the church. They also base their argument on the
claim that their son, the late Reverend Jacob Jegede together with
Reverend L.A. Lenon, a Jamaican brought the church to Oke-agbe.
The other settlement do not agree with Ido on the ownership of the
church. The regent of Aje, Chief Oloniyo told TEMPOLife that the
church does not belong to any particular quarter in Oke-agbe.
According to her, "if anybody should lay claim to Saint George's
Anglican Church, then that person should be Reverend Lenon."
Majority of the people of Oke-agbe are subsistence farmers. The men
do most of the farming, ably assisted by their wives and children.
They produce mostly food crops like yam, beans and cocoyam. Richer
farmers produce cash crops like cocoa and coffee.
The Aje women are professional clay pot makers. Regent of Aje, Chief
Michael Oloniyo avers that "our women are gifted in pot making, as
long as she is born of Aje parent, she does not have to learn it or
undergo any training."
Apart from some individual efforts to develop the town, there are
numerous clubs and societies that come together from time to time to
sponsor developmental projects. One of such projects is the
beautification of the town's roundabout. Tell tale signs of the
defunct Petroleum Trust Fund (PTF), is noticeable in Oke-agbe, as it
is in all of the Akoko region. The one man whose name always rings
bell in Oke-agbe is the town's most illustrious son, Chief Rufus
Giwa, the former chairman of Lever Brothers Nigeria Plc. As the head
of PTF in the zone, he used his influence to make sure that the only
road that leads to Akoko from Akure via Owo was tarred, and the tiny
colonial bridge, which had actually become a death trap was abandoned
and a new one constructed.
It is in realisation of this and other developmental projects carried
out by Chief Rufus Giwa that the four kingdoms came together to make
him the Aro. He hails from the Afa quarters of the town.
In this respect, Chief Rufus Giwa, is the only indigene of Oke-agbe
whose title transcends quarters. He is not just an Afa chief, but his
title cuts across the entire town.

Original_Search_Title: Our Wives the Magicians
Headline: Nigeria: Our Wives the Magicians
Body_Text:
I never really knew the depths into which we have sunk until two weeks back, when on my way back from the village, we made the usual stop over at Mosogar and Oghara to buy home made garri, a must eat component of my daily diet. Well, let me rephrase that. I knew prices of goods, especially, foodstuff were hitting the roof-tops, but I never suspected they had already burst through the roofs and are already aiming at the heavenly firmaments.
Since in bargaining, I am what the Yoruba traders would gladly call an alaroro, an astute bargainer, Christie, the best wife in the whole world, tactically stayed behind for a moment. And it never crossed my mind that that was her own way of letting me into the burden, she and I believe most Nigerian wives now bear on a daily basis - how to juggle the food budget to chase away kwashiorkor from their homes.
I pointed at an average sized bag, about the size we used to buy for N400. The sharp, confident reply from the lady incharge was N4000.
"Four thousand what?" I screamed. I drove off, but the next stop was not better. The lady simply said:
Oga, na N4000 last, with a large dose of mishief and sarcasm.
"You be thief, how you go say this small garri na N4000 last?" I replied, raising my voice involuntarily.
Unperturbed, she turned her back and headed back to the wooden stool, from which she had, a moment before, come to negotiate with me. But not before she had thrown in a deft uppercut: "Oga, you no dey dis Nigeria? E don tay wey garri price don go up. In fact, dis size na four-five (N4,500) we dey sellam; na bicos you speak our language naim even make I say make you pay N4000"
"Darling" my wife suddenly materialised beside me, pouring iced water on the anger threatening to burst over, "that is about what a bag of garri of this size now sells for; garri is very expensive now."
Haggle as we would, compromise was still 1000 per cent above what we paid six months back. As we say in Warri, no do, no do, we ended up paying N3200 for the tiny quantity which would probably last for one month.
I emerged from that encounter looking like the whole world had crumbled on my back; it made me look so foolish. Indeed, I felt like Alhaji Bashir Tofa who did not know how much a litre of petrol sold for during his presidential debate with Chief M.K.O. Abiola in 1993; or maybe like President Olusegun Obasanjo who did not know that the Naira now exchanges for about N140 to an American dollar; nor at that, what the combined refining capacity of our refineries is.
As I was to be later educated both by my wife and traders at the various places we had stopped to pick one item or the other, yam, plantain, fish, meat and even kolanuts have all joined the luxury flight destined for the homes of only Senators, House of Assembly members, Ministers, Special Advisers and all those whose lives are cushioned by one government office or the other.
It is perhaps only this set of people who do not feel the crunch, who do not know that there is a serious food crisis in the country. It is this level of what Professor Sylvanus Ekwelie of the University of Nigera would describe as voluminous ignorance, it is possibly this loss of touch with the realities of the common man in Nigeria that prompted one of President Olusegun Obasanjo's men to insult the intelligence of Nigerians when he said that the prices of garri, a common staple in the South, was soaring because it was then the dry season, that farmers were finding it hard to harvest cassava tubers because of the hard soil.
It is perhaps based on an advise from an ignoramus like this that President Obasanjo recently decided to recast himself as Emperor Nero's wife, who when she heard that the people were about dying of hunger because there was no bread to eat, promptly replied: "if there is no bread, let them eat cake!"
As it turned out, President Obasanjo, until we shouted him down, wanted us to eat Burkina Faso beans if we could no longer afford our regular staple foods.
The positive side of my encounter with the garri sellers of Delta State is that I have developed more respect for my wife and all Nigerian women. It also enabled me to know the true meaning of ghost workers. It dawned on me, suddenly, that we have a ghost ministry in the current government. You know, something like the non-existent committee which the Senate President, Anyim Pius Anyim, authorised some couple of hundred millions for the other time.
I am talking of what should properly be known as the Ghost Federal Ministry of Agriculture, which has a ghost minister, ghost parastatals, ghost permanent secretaries and officers down the line. It is only in a ghost ministry like this that billions of Naira could have disappeared so effectively in the last two years, impacting on the country the worst food crisis since the civil war.
It would not be a bad idea, assuming the so called anti-corruption tribunal set up by President Obasanjo is not a ghost tribunal, to start with successive ministers who have manned this ghost ministry over the years. At least, let them come and tell us what ghost projects they spent the more than N100 billion they received in the last five years on.
As we continued our journey, I looked often at my wife, who has had to take the brunt of my anger in recent times when she, in hindsight now, was forced to request for more funds to run the home. I had usually criticised her management of funds, not minding her protest that prices of foodstuffs have gone through the roof. The first inkling I had was when I was told that a can of 450gms Bournvita now sells for N420. And that a loaf of bread now goes for N100.
At a stage, I looked at her again and blurted out, my voice full of apology: "how have you been managing?" All she could do was give me a sad, yet understanding smile and a shrug of her shoulders.
Further discussions was to reveal clearly to me that, as a nation, we do not need to look too far now to see the best financial managers in Nigeria. Our wives are. They are simply magicians.

Original_Search_Title: Why Is Gari Expensive?
Headline: Nigeria: Why Is Gari Expensive?
Body_Text:
Why gari is scarce and expensive-Adams Idufueko, Chairman, Agricultural and Non-oil Trade Group, LCCI.
We presently seem to be faced with a food crisis arising from the high cost of gari, the supposedly common man's staple and other food items. What do you think is responsible for the problem?
To start with, we can look at the problem as it exists at present. There are two things that account for the high price of basic food stuffs like gari, yam, plantain, etc. In 1999, there was bumper harvest of cereals - maize and guinea corn and so on in the North. And so the price collapsed and food stuff became very cheap in the market.
So, last year, most of the Northern growers of maize, guinea corn, the basic grains refused to plant or planted very little. Consequently, supply became erratic and at the same time, because the planting wasn't high enough, it was obvious that there will be crisis; crisis because the prices were rising even during the harvest so that by January, February this year, the prices have gone high.
Now the only food stuff that is readily available happens to be gari. So, gari is going to the North at a rate nobody expected to fill part of the gap caused by the shortfall in the basic maize, guinea corn diet they're used to. That is one immediate cause. Then the second point is also similar. In the South in 1999, because of the huge surplus in maize, guinea corn and very little "export" (though not quite the appropriate word), Northerners weren't buying too much. So the only outlet for gari was overseas. The number of people here exploited selling gari abroad, to neighbouring West African states and so on. And that has taken off and at a very good pace. So today, gari is being exported at a rate that nobody could have envisaged in the past.
In addition to that, tapioca which is a cassava by-product that is exported from leading countries like Thailand in the far east, is also beginning to gain prominence in Europe. There is also cassava chips. You just cut cassava into pieces dry these in the sun and bag. These items are being sold in dollars. So local demand is up in the Northern part of the country and at the same time, there is increasing export. And the total acreage was depressed in the previous year because the price of gari was cheap. And now higher demand, higher export have all posted the price of gari in particular so that today, rice is cheaper than gari for the first time in years.
One observes that the sharp rise in the price of gari does not seem to correspond with a scarcity of the product in the markets. What I'm saying is that if you go to the markets today, you still find enough of the product to buy. The only problem is that it has become rather expensive. How do you explain this?
Actually, the total volume of gari is less than it used to be. And why should they sell gari cheaply when the people who are buying for export are always coming round to pick up the product. So, you can see total volume of gari might be the same or slightly less compared with the year 2000. But the increased demand overseas and the neighbouring countries and also in the North, all these have contributed to the reason why gari is expensive.
In a way, if this increase could reach the farmer, one will be happy. But unfortunately, most of it is going to the middlemen. Let me also add that, generally in this country the period from December through end of March or middle April is always the time most farmers don't want to harvest cassava because that is the period of dry season in the South. That is because the soil is usually very hard at this time. And don't forget that there has been prolonged dry season this year. Because of that most farmers are not harvesting. Now that the rains have started, don't be surprised in two, three weeks time as we get towards the end of May, gari will come out in larger quantity. The farmers are smart, they will want to take advantage of the present high prices.
Are you saying the present high price of gari is a temporary thing and that sooner or later we will get over it?
Yes, for cassava and gari in particular. And now again the harvest is coming at the planting season for cassava. Now that the price is very good I can bet most people will plant cassava in the cassava growing areas this year. But it will not affect the price in the market until basic food stuffs like maize and other alternatives start coming out in June, July. So, at that time, the demand for gari will come down and the price will also fall. You know, price is determined by supply and demand, that is low volume against high demand.
But if the trend of the export you talked about continues unabated, do you still think that the price will fall even in the face of sufficient alternatives?
The exact position is this: Nigeria potentially can produce 10 times what she is producing today. The estimate is around 10-15 million tonnes of cassava products produced annually. And if you consider the total land area in which you can plant cassava, we are not utilising up to 5 per cent. If everything was to go for cassava, it can produce 10 to 20 times the total quantity being produced.
So, that's why I'm hoping that the gains of the price increase will reach the farmers. The middlemen are the people who make all the money. And it may surprise you for the simple reason that agriculture has been neglected in the past. There is no direct access to capital by the farmers, so they borrow money from shylocks.And some of them sell a whole farm in advance, not minding what harvest they could get in order to meet other basic needs like clothes, books and children school fees, etc. They sell cheaply to meet these basic necessities of life. And the person who makes all the money is the middleman who buys the farm and does the harvest. So, if the farmer didn't know the price will be good and sold earlier, then of course, this problem will still be repeated because he can only farm that land for which he has the requisite capital. On the other hand, if farmers have direct access to capital and mechanisation is inclusive, I mean, the sky is the limit. At that time, the export need to the neighbouring countries and different parts of the country and also overseas, will be met.
Thailand produces over 20 million tonnes every year of cassava and cassava by-products. And Thailand doesn't have a better geographical climate than Nigeria. In the same sense, Nigeria is by far closer to Europe which is the big market. Let me also tell you that right now, there is a quarrel between EU (European Union) and the United States of America. United States normally supply maize, 20, 30 million tonnes a year to Europe which is used to feed livestock-poultry, pigs, cattle. What has happened is that the United States has gone ahead with genetically modified corn, the yield is about 50 per cent higher than it used to be. So, they have surplus maize. But Europe is by far behind in that. Europe in order to protect its own production which genetic engineering is not yet as advanced as the US, is resisting input. And of course there is a gap. Can you believe it, India has just been given an order for 10 million tonnes of maize; India that used to be a beggar economy in terms of food supply 20 years ago. But the late Mrs. Indira Ghandi with foresight put in so much effort that today India is a net exporter of maize. India is expected to sell 10 million tonnes (of maize) to Europe this year to replace part of what they will not take from the Americans.
Nigeria is here with not only the land that is so beautiful, so much virgin land that is not being used. Take a ride from Lagos to Benin to Asaba or go towards the North, immediately you leave the slums every acre, every hectare you can grow maize, and the Lord is so merciful that He blessed us with rain fed agriculture. I think what has happened is that because of cheap money from crude oil and so on, agriculture was neglected. The leadership had money in their pockets but we the poor people don't have money. And that is why Nigeria is rated today as the 13th poorest country in the world. Whereas our mates in the '70s and early '80s like Brazil, South Korea and so on, have GDP and per capita income, of over $20,000, Nigeria is still stagnating at less than $500 because the source of wealth which everyone expected was stolen. It is estimated that between 200 and 300 billion dollars have been made by Nigeria from crude petroleum. The total amount that anybody can quantify is less than 100 billion, what happened to the difference? In private pockets. So, that is what has ruined agriculture. But now one can thank General(Olusegun) Obasanjo, the president for paying sufficient attention to agriculture, at least in words. We're waiting for him to match words with action.
Recently, it was reported that government was making plans to import beans from Burkina Faso to make up for the shortfall in local supply and arrest galloping prices of foodstuffs. Do you subscribe to this as a necessary measure to address the problem?
Well, Burkina Faso is such a poor country compared with Nigeria. But if they say they have (beans) and the price is right, I think government can go ahead, that is as long as it is temporary. Because whatever you're bringing from there is like we're paying for it and supporting growth and labour, we're exporting labour. Whereas the same amount of money we use to import can be used to provide employment here. There's poverty in the land in spite of government's poverty alienation and eradication programmes. Any amount of capital you export, reduces what is available for local production. Burkina Faso is not a better place to provide beans than Nigeria.

Original_Search_Title: Matters Arising With The Food Price
Headline: Nigeria: Matters Arising With The Food Price
Body_Text:
It started gradually last year but few people noticed. Nigeria, the world's largest producer of yams and cassava was experiencing a down turn in basic food productivity.
The causes of the current crisis include: removal of fertiliser subsidy; misplaced priorities on anti-poverty programmes and, oddly enough, democracy dividends which people had expected.
The removal of fertiliser subsidy by the Federal Ministry of Agriculture has had the immediate impact of reducing fertiliser usage, decreasing yield of some basic food items like yams, rice, cassava, maize, beans, sorghum and acha which constitute the bulk of Nigerian food intake.
Prices of these items were already on the rise when the rural areas started feeling the impact of anti-poverty programmes which in many states and local governments were corruption-ridden, give-away programmes discouraging productivity.
Since last year, farmers have been experiencing difficulties attracting farm labourers who now receive over 400 per cent more income from doing next-to-nothing under the federal government's ill-conceived Poverty Alleviation Programmes (PAP).
With farm labourers lost to PAP, yields are declining everywhere.
Cassava is probably the hardest hit of all farm produce. Here the problem is as much about low productivity and lack of manpower to harvest the cassava. So tubers rot in the ground.
Furthermore, last year's record harvest resulted in farmers and their creditors losing money. This year neither creditors nor farmers want to risk their money. Scarcity brings higher prices and favours farmers. And right now farmers are happy even if urban dwellers are howling with anguish.
In advanced countries, the excess production would have been purchased by government acting as a buyer-of-last resort and stored in strategic reserve silos which exist in various parts of the country. This was not done. President Obasanjo's order for release of grains from the strategic reserve could not be carried out because there was little or no strategic reserve to release.
Furthermore, democracy dividends, if by that we mean more money in circulation, is actually being received and for the short-term it is creating problems. Under military rule there were no councillors or the large number of political office holders now in existence. Each office holder is surrounded by a host of people.
In rural areas, many of those now drawing political funds were low-paid farm hands. Now they receive what amounts to unbelievable funds for participating in political activities. They now eat more and work less. With so much more money pursuing few products food prices must rise.
What government needs to do is to tie more of the antipoverty expenditure to food production; insist on yields and reactivate the strategic grains reserve silos to accommodate excess output.

Original_Search_Title: AD Carpets Federal Government Over Food Crisis
Headline: Nigeria: AD Carpets Federal Government Over Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Alliance for Democracy (AD) yesterday warned that the country
could be at the threshold of massive discontent and revolt if the
federal government fails to urgently address the current food crisis
in the country.
Holding the PDP-led government of Olusegun Obasanjo responsible for
the present shortfall in the national food supply, the AD further
alleged that the ruling party had failed to recognise that the
availability of food was central to national security.
In a statement signed by Barrister Opeyemi Bamidele, the party's
National Director of Publicity, the AD noted that the prices of
foodstuff had gone up by as much as 400 per cent in the last few
months and that the Federal Government could be walking a minefield
if it did not take urgent steps to bring down the skyrocketing
prices.
"If the trend continues, we are afraid it could set off a massive
discontentment of unimaginable magnitude", the AD warned.
Noting that the party's secretariat was daily inundated with requests
for the AD to intervention, Bamidele accused the PDP government of
adopting a "fire- brigade" approach to the problem.
He said that the resolve to massive importation of items would only
provide temporary succour and that what the situation demands is a
well articulated agricultural and food policy.
The AD also faulted the release of grains to the states from the
national strategic reserve, asking what would become of the supply of
such staples as gari, yam, palm oil, vegetable oil, melon and so on
which are not known to be on the national food reserve.
Describing the recent release of subsidised fertiliser to farmers as
belated, Bamidele said that President Obasanjo's assurance of a
bountiful harvest next season was unrealistic.
He said it was senseless to promise a bountiful harvest "because the
government neither knows the extent of cultivation nor the machinery
of nation- wide food distribution".
Bamidele asked the federal government to immediately evolve a
masterplan that would transform the country into "a food paradise
before the expiration of its mandate in 3003".
He said the way this can be achieved is to gainfully engage the army
of unemployed youths in agriculture and food production with a
back-to-land policy that would also give dignity to the profession.
It said: "As a long term panacea to national food crisis, the AD
calls for a programme that will make farming a highly rewarding and
dignifying profession.

Original_Search_Title: Yendi: the German Connection
Headline: Ghana: Yendi: the German Connection
Body_Text:
Accra — The Tamale to Yendi road is a manifestation of the failure of
politicians to live up to the expectations of their people. The many
villages which dot this important road have got little to show by way
of an improvement in the lives of their people.
But for the efforts of some NGOs life for some of these villagers
would not have been any different from what it was when the Union
Jack gave way to the Red, Yellow and Green flag of the new republic.
The only difference between this road and a farm path is that the
former is broader and has a place in major topographical maps of this
part of the country. It is riddled with potholes and pools of water
at this time of the year when the rains have just started. It is not
comfortable traveling on this road as the more than the occasional
jolting one experiences when the vehicle runs into a pothole
reactivates the pains of old sprains and fractures. For most part of
the two-hour journey in a military Land Cruiser our driver relied on
the bottom gears.
When the uncomfortable part of the journey terminated at Sambu some
six or so kilometres away from Yendi I could not help but ask whether
the politicians were here to campaign during the last elections.
Perhaps they came in helicopters and so could therefore not see the
horrible nature of the roads on which most of the 'puna' yams we eat
in Accra pass through. River Dakkar which is the source of drinking
water for the town welcomes all visitors to the town for after
passing through Sambu the next major spot is the bridge which spans
the river. A few vehicles are parked on its banks as drivers' mates
busy themselves washing off red soil from the trucks which perhaps
had visited many village market days far and near. On the right hand
side is a pointer to the town's waterworks.
If the road, which leads to this ancient capital of Dagbon, has
little to show for modernity, Yendi itself presents a classic example
of a neglected town. Many tourist attractions abound in this town yet
the roads in it are worn out and in most cases laid bare without the
trace of coal tar which marked them in the early sixties. Those were
the days of the Osagyefo when even though there was no electricity in
the town things did not look as bad.
The town though has grown beyond its early boundaries with the
arrival of the noveau riche, most of whom are women. New brick houses
now compete with the old thatched roofed round buildings for
attention. The contrast itself is attractive. The presence of
electricity in the town is evident everywhere as second-hand fridges
have found another safe haven to do more damage to the environment.
Spent iced water sachets are beginning to riddle the town.
After visiting the Ya Na's palace where I spent a few minutes paying
homage I decided to have a look at the old European graves in the
town the next day. The graves are not too far from the palace and
when I called at the location the next day it was an eye-opener. They
spoke about the relationship between this ancient town with the
European powers many years after the partition of West Africa.
As early as the latter days of the 1800s the Germans whose territory
Yendi was had planted kapok and mahogany looking-like trees along the
principal streets of the town. But for the felling of the kapok trees
to make way for the tarring of a few kilometers of the town's roads,
the other hardwoods still stand as a reminder of German occupation of
Dagbon. They exhibit scars here and there, a reminding one that some
herbalists have been there to take some barks for medicinal purposes,
leaving the trees to heal their wounds.
Yendi is steeped in history. Some years after the cessation of
hostilities between the Ashantis and the British the Dagombas too
fought the Germans at a town called Adiboo. Today members of this
large ethnic group recognize this important date as Adiboo Dali or
The day of Adiboo. The mention of Germany evokes uncomfortable
memories to those who have benefited from the oral rendition of this
black day in the history of Dagbon.
Although I do not understand German, I could tell from the
inscriptions on one of the tombstones at the spot not too far from
the Yendi Prison that it was in that language. It was not the only
grave there. There were others but they all belong to white people.
The first one, which attracted me read: Alois Hondobier,
Stationsasistens, geb.5.V.1869, gest.3.IV. 1902.
The second grave belonged to an American, Guy R. Hickock, Born
Parishville, New York May 29, 1903 Died Tamale Jan. 16, 1933. Yet
another grave belonged to a female American who it seems had been on
very good terms with the people of this town. She even had a Dagomba
name, which appeared, on her tombstone and a church educational
center in the town was named after her. The following inscriptions
appear on her tombstone: Miss Beaulah E. Buch Walter (Miriama Zee)
Born USA Dec.14, 1909 Died: Tamale Nov.15, 1942. Miriam Zee means
Miriam the fair complexioned one.
One of the graves had an old black chain around it and a legend in
the town has it that it belonged to a German who had not finished
serving out his sentence before he died and this is why his grave has
that peculiar feature. What I made out of the inscriptions on the
grave runs thus: SEI GETREU BIA IN DEN TOD, SO WILL ICH DIE KRONE DES
LEBENS GEBEN. A portion of the tombstone had fallen off and lay close
with these writings on it GEB. 10 MAI 1873 IN FREINDWALDER40 GEST. 23
JANR. 1910 ZU WAPULI. The legend adds that the chain should have been
removed when the prison sentence period elapsed. Perhaps the
inscription explains better why there is a black around this grave.
Yendi has a new feature. Women both old and young use bicycles as a
means of intra-town movement. One day I saw a woman with her baby
strapped on her back and another son or so sitting on the back of the
bicycle.
The market trucks which ferry the market women to the various market
days of the neighbouring villages operate in such a manner that one
wonders whether the Police MTTU exists in the town. Passengers sit
dangerously on top of mammy trucks as if death to them means nothing.
Although the town lacks many of the amenities of urban settings it
now shares with such locations the high prices for many food items
like guinea fowls, eggs and others. Yams are relatively cheaper than
in places like Accra not so however for livestock.
The Greenwich Meridian also passes through this town although the
exact location is a subject of contention. While some hold that the
spot just opposite the town's hospital is part of the imaginary line
others who claim to have used a more scientifically reliable method
the spot point at a different spot in the town.
The Department of Game and Wildlife will need to do a lot of work
here as the people do not seem to know that some species of wildlife
are endangered. A hired articulated truck with a lot of youth on
board was on its way out of the town. It was going to drop them off
at a point for a hunting expedition in which all animals were
potential targets.
Yendi has produced many important personalities for the nation - the
current Vice President, Alhaji Aliu Mahama, the Minister of the
Interior, Alhaji Malik Yakubu and to some extent the National
Security Advisor whose hometown of Sunsong is just a few kilometers
away from here.
Yendi suffers from less of the pollutants of the cities and so in
terms of breathing fresher air it outdoes Accra. As for the people of
this ancient town they are waiting to see whether something would be
done about the roads especially the one which links them to Tamale.
Yendi is unlike other traditional capitals in the country which
double as both administrative and traditional capitals - Kumasi,
Accra, Ho, Wa, Bolga and others. In the case of Yendi it ceded its
administrative capital status to Tamale where the major facelifting
developments take place. The tourism potentials in this ancient
traditional capital of Dagbon are begging for attention.

Original_Search_Title: Food Prices Hit Roof Top
Headline: Nigeria: Food Prices Hit Roof Top
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of basic staple foods like garri, rice, beans and bread have
risen to unprecedented heights in Lagos in the past few weeks,
forcing consumers to adopt austerity measures.
Other commodities whose prices have gone up in the market include:
Yam, Egg, Fish, Beef, Palm Oil Groundnut Oil, Salt and Plantain.
And in response to these high prices, the Masses Movement of Nigeria,
led by the estranged wife of President Olusegun Obasanjo, Moji, has
given the government a 21- day ultimatum to find solution to these
high prices or its members would take to the streets.
P.M. News investigations revealed that the prices of these major
foodstuffs have increased by as much as 200 percent. Worst hit is
Garri, commonest staple food found in most poor homes which price has
risen by as much as 500 percent.
Most consumers now stretch their meagre incomes to buy bits of these
foodstuffs for their families. An average worker earning N5500 a
month can hardly afford most of these foodstuffs at their current
prices.
A market survey carried out by P.M. News at major markets in Lagos,
including Mushin, Tejuosho, Ketu, Mile 12, Oshodi, and Ikeja (Alade)
markets shows that prices of the commodities have risen beyond the
reach of the average consumer.
At Tejuosho Model market, Yaba, a bag of garri now costs N2400.
It was sold for N900 last December. The 4-litre paint jar of the
commodity, which formerly sold for N70 at the market, is now sold at
N180 and N240, depending on the grade. A milk cup of garri is now
N10, instead of N5 for which it previously sold.
A bag of beans, the commonest source of protein for the low income
earners, is now sold at N6500, from the previous N5500. A Derica cup,
which previously sold for N40 is now N50, while the milk cup,
formerly N10 is now N15.
Also, a bottle of palm oil at the market is now sold for N80, instead
of N60.
At Mushin Model Market, a bag of garri is now N4000, from the former
N900. The 4-litre paint jar which previously sold for N50 is now
N250, while a milk cup now goes for N15, as against the former price
of N5. A bag of rice in the market now goes for N2,800 from the
former N2500. A derica cup is now sold for N50 instead of N40, the
former price. Survey conducted at Oshodi shows that a bag of garri is
now sold for N3700 and N4200 depending on the size of the bags.
The 4-litre paint jar is sold for N180, while a kongo (measure) now
goes for N70. A bag of beans at Oshodi now costs N2800, while a milk
cup is N10.
A trader at the market attributed the increase to rising cost of
transportation occasioned by fuel scarcity.
At Alade Market, Ikeja a bag of rice now costs between N1,000 and
N3000 depending on the quality. A medium tuber of yam costs between
N80 and N100 in the market.
Meanwhile the Senate has rejected moves by the Federal Government to
import beans from Burkina Faso to make up for the shortfall in the
production of the commodity in Nigeria. The senate argued that the
decision had the tendency of discouraging Nigerian farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Malu Returns to Vandeikya
Headline: Nigeria: Malu Returns to Vandeikya
Body_Text:
Victor Malu loses out in a contest. But he goes with others, reports
Ebelo Goodluck.
Those who are familiar with the fast evolving power relations in the
military and the civilianised lords of Aso Rock are rather surprised
that Victor Malu lasted 23 clear months. Malu, the insider in the
years of the General Sani Abacha military dictatorship, had hung on
when other military top brass was pulled out at the beginning of the
democratic dispensation.
Though he got the strategic job of Chief of Army Staff (COAS) and was
supposed to be the evangelist of the new regime's professionalism
doctrine, he never fitted the role. Strangely, Malu came across as a
throw back fighting a new doctrine he was supposed to be promoting.
Malu had spent 33 years in the army and was looking forward to his
retirement in two years time He told TEMPO in an interview in March
just that. "I'm going to leave the service either when I'm 65 years
or when I would have done 35 years of service. Either way, I am
supposed to be leaving in two years' time. Unless I commit an offence
and you know I will never do that. I think I've jailed too many
people to know the law". The jailor who knew the law by heart from
passing death sentences on fellow officers on trumped up coup charges
was too legalistic in his approach to know that what kept one in high
office was more than intimate knowledge of the law. That the
circumstances of an appointment, the goals it was supposed to serve,
were as important, if not more important than the regulations of
office. Malu, the less political of abundantly politicised military
didn't know how well to draw the order of battle in a democratic
government with a clear agenda of re-orienting the military that he
came out fighting on all fronts and lost without attaining his 65
years or 35 years service mark.
The presidency has tried hard to put a cushion to it in the public's
eye by saying that the three service chiefs, Lt. Gen. Victor Malu,
Vice Admiral Victor Ombu and Air Marshal Isaac Alfa had retired on
their own. The service chiefs were no where to know that they were to
be kicked out. In fact, as late as Monday evening, the soon to be
sacked Alfa had called up a Lagos editor to reschedule an interview
appointment he had agreed to.
Victor Malu who survived many purges in the army rose to head
Africa's number one political army after years of military misrule
had wrecked the country and ruined the army had his ideas on how the
army should be managed. What may have precipitated Malu's fall on the
one hand was a temperamental similarity with his Commander-in-Chief,
Gen. Olusegun Obasanjo.
Obasanjo sees officers of the armed forces, retired and serving as
his boys. This is actually a hangover from his military days as
Commander-in-Chief. The President is fond of asking "what was your
rank when I was in the army." This attitude came to the fore when the
President was visiting Bayelsa State. The governor brokered a meeting
between the president and leaders of the Ijaw National Congress led
by one of its vice-presidents, Wing Commander Biakpare, a retired Air
Force officer and special adviser to the Delta State Governor James
Ibori. Obasanjo was at his angry best. He got angry and left the
meeting due to some little disagreement over a reference to the
country as a tripod. He left the meeting in a huff and when he was
persuaded by the governor he shot at Biakpara "you, Wing-Commander,
what was your rank when I was in the army"? The meeting came to an
abrupt end.
Last year, when Lagos was convulsing in the Oodua People's Congress
(OPC)- Hausa killing orgy, an emergency security meeting was under
way in Aso Rock, Abuja. In attendance was the President, the service
chiefs, security chiefs and some presidential aides. Malu was
furious. He wanted direct military intervention to stop the mayhem.
Obasanjo was very reluctant, knowing that unleashing soldiers would
quadruple casualties and very mindful of what the political
implications would be in his home regions of the South-West. Though
Malu was not alone as an advocate of military intervention, he was
the most vociferous. He told the President his reluctance was
prompted by his ethnic affiliation to the Yoruba who were thought to
have the upper hand in the killing. Obasanjo was livid. Here was an
officer who was barely a major when he Obasanjo reigned as military
ruler and had become Chief of Army Staff at his pleasure confronting
him so openly in a security meeting. Obasanjo kept a cool exerior.
Not to be seen as condoning a Yoruba slaughter of Hausas, he gave the
order to throw soldiers into the rioting. Obasanjo was not as much
bothered by the suffering that decision would have on his Yoruba kin
but affronted by his Chief of Army Staff's obduracy. He simply bidded
his time.
If Malu worsted his boss on the Lagos riots. He also was prepared to
contest the wisdom that informed Obasanjo dual pet projects for the
military. Military Professional Resources Initiative (MPRI) and
Operations Focus Relief. As Malu himself explained, the former was to
"assist in the reprofessionalisation programme of the armed forces"
and the latter "involves training and equipping units that are being
selected to go for operation in Sierra-Leone. These two programmes
were at the heart of military reforms Obasanjo had agreed to with his
American backers even before he assumed power. But Malu didn't share
the goals of the C-in-C and said so loudly. "Part of the
misunderstanding we had with the Americans was that they wanted to
train us even in the rudimentary art of soldering". As though that
insult was not enough, the Americans were seen to be pursuing goals
that would have undermined the sovereignty of Nigeria and its ability
to put up a fight to safeguard it. "Mistakes have been made... when
they drew their programme, the implementation plan, there were some
aspects of it that we thought should not be handled by them because
of the security implications. So we've been objecting."
Beyond barring the Americans from visiting sensitive military
installations unaccompanied, Malu hit the government roundly for
under funding. And Obasanjo who had promised to keep funding the
military generously had reneged on his pledge confronted by the
mammoth scale of spending required as well in other areas and by an
IMF, World Bank team that had been insistent on tighter budget
control and a rein on capital spending unable to meet Malu's
expectation of a bonanza for the army. According to Malu, out of
proposals for funding to the tune of N4.3 billion the Army submitted
for equipment acquisition only a paltry N400 million was approved.
And out of a budget of N900 million for the 2000 fiscal year only
N109 million his been released as at September.
He was not alone. Other sacked service chiefs joined in the whining
before the Senate Defence Committee. Victor Ombu the former Chief of
Naval Staff also said that the Navy had got approximately 25 per cent
and since he saw no wisdom in disbursing the money it was left at the
CBN as "it is not enough". Not to be outdone, Chief of Air Staff,
Isaac Alfa told of the monumental devastation the Air Force had
suffered over the years.
As irritating as the service chiefs' bleating about poor funding was,
Malu put up a performance at the Justice Chukwudifu Oputa panel that
clearly riled his boss and punctured all the air of a soldier's
soldier he had managed to cobble around himself. On the Abacha badge
he wore through the trial of Oladipo Diya and his co-coup plotters,
Malu composed a paean to Abacha. "Yes, I wore the badge...I am proud
about it. I believed in it and in loyalty to the then
commander-in-chief, Abacha... So, I feel proud wearing the badge of
Abacha. I will wear it again, if I am given any." Coming from the
COAS in a regime that has taken Abacha's destruction of the military
as given, his testimony scandalised the President but he couldn't
move against Malu then because he would be readily accused of
personalising his well-known visceral hatred of Abacha whom Malu was
deifying.
While Obasanjo continued to insist on a doddering Danjuma being his
Minister of Defence, he was clearly becoming offended by Malu's
presence in the military. In July last year, Malu announced plans by
the military authority to disengage 5,000 officers so as to have a
"more compact, mobile and effective army." He told the press that the
army would not need more than 76,000 officers and men. Though the
plan did not differ significantly from Obasanjo's own goals for the
army, he was appalled by Malu's levity. Obasanjo realised that a
badly managed demobilisation in the 1970s had resulted in Col. Buka
Dimka's bloody coup that cut down his boss, Murtala Mohammed.
But with such a long list of sins as it were, it was surprising that
a man of Obasanjo's temperamental state would stand Malu for so long.
But his longevity lay in the hard ball politics that saw him becoming
the COAS at the start of the regime. Though Malu could say he was the
most senior officer around and should naturally have been made COAS
since his peers were stained by the single yardstick of having held
political office before but he had it more to his origins.
Obasanjo's second coming was expected to solved the single abiding
paradox of the Nigerian Army. An institution where the Middle-Belt
with its history of warriors dominated the officers corps of the
armed forces but finds itself pushed to the margins by officers of
Hausa-Fulani descent. It wasn't just that many of these officers were
not Moslems but their forebears were never conquered by the
Jihadists. What's more, their ancestors had formed the rump of the
local army the British had used in pacifying its Nigerian colony.
Though this pedigree had naturally attracted them to the military,
they were short changed by the British which saw the Hausa-Fulani as
more suitable to the realisation of its post-independence policies in
Nigeria.
Arising from this was the fact that it soon suffered high fatality
rate in the armed forces given the proclivity for coups and
counter-coups in the armed forces. So the Obasanjo, Danjuma mission
was at heart to restore power back to its owners. And when the region
got the position of the head of the army, the air force and the
defence establishment, it saw in full glare what it has always lost
to military politics. Cries of marginalisation by the far North and
the disavowal of people who had hitherto been seen as Northerners
drove the point home. This was the scenario under which Malu emerged,
it was too dangerous to play hire and fire with such a fragile
system. But no matter what historical forces were at play, the
personality of the two individuals was bound to escape to the
surface. And when they collided sparks flew until they finally
consumed Malu's commission.
Samuel Victor Leo Malu, the son of a Benue yam farmer was born on 15
January 1947. After a stint at the Government College, Sokoto for his
Higher School Certificate, he went to the Nigerian Defence Academy
when the guns of war had begun to boom. He held different command
positions. But nothing stood out the young officer until Col. Dimka
visited him at Zaria where he was military instructor days before
Dimka was to pump a volley of bullets that silenced the ebullient
Murtala Muhammed forever. Malu was picked up but was let go. From
there, nothing much was heard of him until he was made ECOMOG field
commander. Malu has not been directly accused of enriching himself
from the wars in Liberia and Sierra - Leone but he is not the Spartan
soldier in the mould of General Ishola Williams . He is by all means
a rich man. What Malu was remembered for was that his time as ECOMOG
commander saw the rebels get some real hiding and restored order to a
peace keeping effort gone awry.
He came back to head the 2nd Divsion of the Army. Malu's place in the
public perception took a fresh pot of paint when Abacha appointed him
to head the tribunal that tried Oladipo Diya and others for coup
plotting in 1998. Malu's role is yet to be properly evaluated. While
some hail him as a perfect example of a soldier others decry his role
as a ready tool in the hands of a mad man.
Though the Oputa panel has sought of cemented the view in the public
mind that there was actually a coup, Malu's own testimony at the same
commission gave him the dubious distinction of an Abacha zealot let
loose on Diya and company. May be it was that part of him that seeps
to the surface to speak his mind but Malu was badly tainted when he
refused to recognise what ruin those years brought to the military
when indeed at a different forum when he was taking over from Ishaya
Bamaiyi, he had said it became "difficult to distinguish at times
between ourselves (soldiers) and the business men except that we are
wearing the green uniform." Malu commanded the very political Lagos
Garrison Command and was appointed into the office of the COAS from
there.
So why was an officer who summoned "the skills of a politician , a
diplomat and an army officer" to succeed in Liberia unable to muster
those same skills to survive the politically mined office of the
Chief of Army Staff? Malu will live to tell his story. But it was
clear Wednesday that the soldiers and officers will not miss him
much. He had promised a massive renovation of barracks across the
country but that never was. Malu blames it on a kobo pinching
administration of Obasanjo but his men think he may not have been as
very transparent with what he got.
Those who had worked with the man before he became COAS have always
doubted his prioritization. Malu as commander of the Lagos Garrison
had sought approval for the construction of a cemetery at the Ojo
Cantonment, Lagos . The project was massively funded but it came at a
period when the living conditions of soldiers and officers had
plummeted drastically and whatever was available should have gone to
welfare.
As COAS, he had revived the cemetery project even posting a protege,
Gen. Ziddon, to the Lagos Garrison Command to watch his flanks Add to
this, is the nepotists attitude of the former COAS to a sibling,
Major John Malu by influencing choice posting in the army. The young
officer was said to have returned a Peugeot gift to collect a jeep
from big brother.
The discipline Malu was supposed to inculcate in the troops was not
happening. Most embarrassing to the regime was Major-General Vijay
Jetley's report indicting Malu's boys of fuelling the war in
Sierra-Leone by enjoying the trade in diamonds. The officers are
Major Gen. Gabriel Kpamber, Brig. Generals Mohammed Garba, Oluyemi
Adeniyi and late Maj.-Gen. Maxwell Khobe. Jetley's charge was that
those men collaborated with Foday Sankoh's rebel RUF to illegally
mine diamonds. In fact, he referred to Khobe as the ten million' man
a reference to how much he enriched himself from the illicit diamond
trade.
A flustered Obasanjo had implored the United Nations to investigate
thoroughly. But was clearly miffed by Malu's inability to maintain
discipline in the army.
When the axe finally fell, it was a testament to the strength of Malu
that other careers had to be chopped off too to give the thing a
policy veneer.

Original_Search_Title: Combat Ready! Addo Kufuor's Marriage With the Military
Headline: Ghana: Combat Ready! Addo Kufuor's Marriage With the Military
Body_Text:
Accra — If there is one thing that governments of the developing countries
fear most, it is their military. The military in Africa became laws
unto themselves when they tasted the sweet fruits of coup d'etats in
the mid-sixties of the last century. Ghana suffered her first coup in
1966 when the country was only nine years old after independence. In
the 44 years of independence, Ghana has witnessed four successful
coups d'etat, one palace coup and several coup attempts.
The military therefore presents special headaches for governments.
The NPP government is aware of this Damocles Sword and though
enjoying a popular mandate from Election 2000, coupled with a
national coup fatigue, is still not taking the threat lightly and has
therefore made the issue of a contended and professional military a
priority in its administration.
To underscore this fact, the President chose his own junior brother,
Dr. Addo Kufuor to be Defence Minister in the opening stages of his
administration. The minister has taken to his job like a duck to
water visiting military installations, interacting with officers and
men, and two weeks ago reviewing his first military manoeuvres. The
manoeuvres were international in scope and involved soldiers from
Ghana and her surrounding Francophone neighbours. The operational
headquarters was Yendi, the ancient capital of the kingdom of Dagbon
and birthplace of Vice President Alhaji Aliu Mahama.
The Military High Command gave The Accra Mail the honour and
privilege of participating in the manoeuvres. The "war correspondent"
was A.R. Alhassan who spent one week with the soldiers. The first
part of his report is presented below:
At the Yendi aerodrome, the sound of a helicopter heralded the
arrival of the Minister of Defence, Dr. Addo Kufuor to town. He was
accompanied on this trip by the acting Army Commander, Brigadier
Yaache, the General Officer Commanding, Northern Command, Brigadier
Aryiku, the Chief Director, Ministry of Defence and other senior
officers.
When the engines were cut off and the visitors stepped out I could
not help asking whether the Defence Minister had stepped out already.
My question was important because with my digital camera I did not
want to miss a memorable picture of the minister coming into the
ancient capital of Dagbon to visit the multi-national force engaged
in a peacekeeping exercise.
A soldier standing close to me told me that the dark complexioned man
in the military OG and flanked by the two brigadiers was the Minister
of Defence. I was still not sure. The aerodrome had been properly
secured earlier by a detachment of troops before the minister's
arrival.
The top military men covering the Minister on the visit looked
professional and purposeful and I could not also help noticing the
new confidence the country's defence forces were exhibiting towards
Ghana's new democracy. This is not surprising because many of the
country's troops have gone on various peacekeeping duties outside
Ghana, an exposure, which has opened their eyes to the deleterious
effects of authoritarian regimes.
They would therefore not wish for their motherland the self-inflicted
calamities, which befell those countries.
When the Minister led his entourage to the Ya Na's (King of Dagbon)
palace for a courtesy call, I noticed a little telling detail. The
Army Commander offered his minister his walking stick and I could
tell that the army chief wanted to ensure that his boss cut an
appropriate military bearing in the presence of the Dagomba King!
