At the field Officer's Mess of the West Battalion Headquarters, the
esprit de corps was visible as the minister who sat at the head of
the table ate lunch and chatted with the senior military officers who
all did their best to make him feel at home. It was as if the medical
doctor had been a friend of the military for over a decade. Besides
the Army Commander and the Northern Command GOC, there were the
Airborne Force CO and an array of officers who all played their part
to make the minister happy.
The CO's briefing to the minister and his entourage was most
outstanding. Here was a CO of a battalion engaged in a military
exercise telling his minister in the presence of his Commander about
the problems of his unit. Ordinarily the CO would have swept these
under the carpet and made things look good when the real situation
was different. This transparency so pleased the troops that many of
them were heard saying that now things have changed.
If Lt. Col. George Biah the CO, was so transparent, the Minister, Dr.
Addo Kufuor was even more assuring and trustworthy when in responding
he announced a personal donation of one million cedis for the troops
welfare. That was not all. He went on to add the two rams and fifty
tubers of yam given him by the Ya Na to make things easy for the
soldiers and the thunderous ovation which followed spoke volumes
about the confidence the military now have in the democratic system
of government.
Lt. Col. George Biah at a durbar with his officers and men had told
them that democracy is the preferred method of governance the world
over and so such exercises as Exercise Cohesion Kozar are designed to
protect the elected governments from threats of instability by rebels
and other recalcitrant elements.
The seeds of instability are sown when there is no trust between
those who are enjoined by the constitution to protect it and those
elected by the people to govern. The unsavoury situation can also
rear its head when there is no openness on the part of those charged
with the responsibility of governing by the people.
One Major whispered to me, "I am really amazed at the Minister's
grasp of the geo-politics of the sub region. The impression I had
about the man has indeed changed." This was after the officer had
listened to the Minister explain the importance his government
attached to such exercises.
His assurance to the top military hierarchy to inform his Ministry
about the exercises they intend undertaking in the year so these can
be included in budgetary estimates showed again the way the Minister
wants to deal with the military. There is no denying the fact the
country's military will find in the new administration compatible
bedfellows which augurs well for democracy.
The PNDC/NDC governments had so politicised the military that
soldiers became playthings only to be used in provocative political
route marches and parades where they were usually incited either
against the civil society or the opposition, which by the fortunes of
democracy is in power today.
The Kufuor government will not want history to repeat itself. In 1972
when President Kufuor was the Deputy Minister of Foreign Affairs in
the government of the 2nd Republic, the 2nd coup d'etat in Ghana's
history was perpetrated by the late General I.K. Acheampong. His
political tradition has been out of power since then, until January 7
2001 when President Kufuor was sworn in as the nation's Chief
Executive with a majority in parliament. It is therefore
understandable that he would want the military to be his partners in
nurturing democracy.

Original_Search_Title: Reverend and Son Docked for Assault
Headline: Nigeria: Reverend and Son Docked for Assault
Body_Text:
Lagos — Seventy-year old Reverend Emmanuel Shodipe and his son, Ademola
Shodipe, have been charged before an Ikeja Chief Magistrate Court
with assault.
In suit number MIK/A/295/2001 filed against the duo by prosecuting
police officer, Inspector Okonkwo Okorie, they were alleged to have
committed the offence in March 2001 at 3 Olaniyi Street, Idi-Oro,
Mushin. They allegedly assaulted Isaiah Ezeogu, contrary to the
provision of Section 355 of the Criminal Code.
They were also accused of maliciously damaging the windscreen of a
Mercedez Benz valued at N21,000, property of their victim and thereby
committed an offence punishable under Section 451 of the Criminal
Code.
They pleaded not guilty to the charges and Chief Magistrate J.
Osinowo released them on bail in the sum of N100,000 while the case
was adjourned to 21 May 2001 for further hearing.
In another development, three men were also brought before the same
court for allegedly conspiring to steal properties belonging to a
family at No. 19 Cappa Estate, Mobolaji Bank Anthony Way, Ikeja in
December, 2000. In charge number MIK/A/295/2001, the trio were
alleged to have broken into the dwelling house of one Mrs. P. E.
Odigbo and stole several properties worth over N1.9m, contrary to the
provisions of sections 516, 411(C) 412 and 390 (9) of 1993 Criminal
Code.
The charge against them reads: "That you Nyiam Ekairo, Martina
Ogwakong (F) Ezekiel Ejim and others at large stole gold jewelry
valued at N1.4m, one National yam pounder valued at N10,000, rug
valued at N2,500, two security lights valued at N15,000, two painted
pictures valued at N14,000 some children stockings valued at N500,
two pairs of men suits and the sum of N10,000 property of Mrs. P. E.
Odigbo..."
They pleaded not guilty to the charges while the presiding magistrate
adjourned the matter to 9 May 2001 for further hearing.

Original_Search_Title: Defence Minister Visits Troops
Headline: Ghana: Defence Minister Visits Troops
Body_Text:
Accra — Dr. Addo-Kufuor, the Minister of Defence last Thursday visited troops
undertaking exercises in and around the Dagbon capital of Yendi.
Dressed in military fatigues, the minister landed at the Yendi
aerodrome in the company of the Acting Army Commander, Major General
Yaachie and the General Officer Commanding the Northern Command,
Brigadier Ayiku.
The minister and his entourage were met on arrival by Lt. Col. Biah
who briefed him about the exercise. He told the minister that due to
the paucity of funds available for the exercise, certain vital field
items were in short supply.
In reply, the minister said the government attached great importance
to the exercise and asked the military top brass to include the cost
of such exercises in the MOD's budget so that adequate resources
would be voted to obviate the kind of problems those undertaking the
exercise were facing.
He made a personal donation of one million cedis to the troops.
The minister later paid a visit to the Yaa Naa who presented him with
a ram and 50 tubers of yam. He told the Yaa Naa that he had called on
him to explain the rationale of the exercise, which in addition to
the military's own programme of field exercises was also to foster
cordial civil-military relations.
The exercise, code-named "Cohesion Khozar" is a joint exercise
between Ghana, La Cote'Ivoire, Togo and Burkina Faso.
The minister has since returned home and the exercise ends tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: Prices of Foodstuffs Soar
Headline: Nigeria: Prices of Foodstuffs Soar
Body_Text:
Lagos, Nigeria — Prices of foodstuffs have been on the increase in the past few months
across Nigeria, according to figures released by the Federal Office of
Statistics (FOS).
The FOS said in its latest report that the Consumer Price Index (CPI)
increased from 8.6 percent in January to 10.3 percent in February.
"With an increase of 10.3 percent in the CPI, this means that a fixed
market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with 100 naira during
the 12 months period ending this month now sells for 110 naira," the report
said.
It said the increase in CPI was largely caused by the appreciable
increase in the prices of foodstuffs like gari, a local stable processed
from cassava, as well as guinea corn and yams.
"Prices of fish, processed foods and edible oil also rose in many price
collection centres between February 2000 and February 2001," the report
added.
Experts attribute the increase to the high cost of transportation,
resulting from the scarcity of petroleum products experienced in the country
for the past several months.
The falling value of the national currency, the naira, has also affected
the prices of foodstuffs, even when most of them are processed locally.
The naira has fallen from about 88 naira to the dollar in May 1999, when
President Olusegun Obasanjo took office, to about 115 naira to the dollar at
present.

Original_Search_Title: Multi-national Peace Keeping Exercise Kicks Off In Ghana
Headline: West Africa: Multi-national Peace Keeping Exercise Kicks Off In Ghana
Body_Text:
Accra, Ghana — A multi-national peacekeeping exercise involving the Arm Forces of 10 West African countries including Ghana took off in the Upper East Region of Ghana and Togo on Wednesday 18th April.
The exercise code named "EXERCISE COHESION KOZA" will last for a week. Other participating countries are: Nigeria, Senegal, Cote'd Ivoire, Togo, Benin, Niger, Mali, Burkina Faso. It is under the sponsorship of the participating countries and the French Government.
Briefing the Press at Yendi on Tuesday, the Commanding Officer of the Ghanaian troops, Lt. Col. George A. Biah explained that it is the aim of the exercise, among others,  enhance the capacity of the forces to be able to cope with any emergency crisis in the West African region.
He said it would also improve upon the inter-operation ability of the forces in the sub-region by harmonizing operation between French and English speaking forces in the sub-region.
Citing the scenario of the exercise, the Commander said an imaginary country called "Republic of Fibaloland" has been thrown into confusion and anarchy following dissatisfaction by one of the three major ethnic groups about the result of recent elections, which it claimed, had been rigged by the ruling government.
He said that at the request of the ruling government, the United Nations Security Council decided to deploy a multinational force, "United Nations Mission in fibaloland (MINUFI), to contain the situation.
The forces are therefore to calm tension and facilitate the return of peace, create favourable conditions for the provision of humanitarian services, supervise the cease-fire and ensure the return of displaced persons. Lt. Col. Biah said that was the purpose for a peacekeeping mission for which they are exercising.
Earlier, Lt. Col. Biah and his officers paid a courtesy call on the Overlord of Dagbo, Ya-Na Yakubu Andani II, during which he briefed the Overlord on the exercise.
Ya-Na Andani II donated a bull and hundred tubers of Yam to the soldiers and wished them well in the exercise.

Original_Search_Title: Between Sharia And Resource Control
Headline: Nigeria: Between Sharia And Resource Control
Body_Text:
The loud call by southern governors for the control of resources from their domain may be a fall-out of the re- launch of the Sharia legal system in some states in the north writes Dele Ajaja.
If the word "boomerang" has an alternative meaning, it can as well
mean "the use of resource control by southern Nigeria to retaliate against northern Nigeria's earlier use of Sharia as a political weapon to rubish power-shift to the south."
Perhaps, many are yet to realize the agreement between natural and human laws. What experience has revealed time and again is the fact that natural and human laws are inseparable and closely knitted. While the natural law says that "action and reaction are equal and opposite," the human law says that "one reaps whatever he sows." Merging the twin laws above, one finds out that for every action taken by anyone, either positive or negative, there is always a reaction waiting. This is not far from the two hottest political issues in modern Nigeria. The north re-launched Sharia into the nation's volatile political terrain, as a weapon to fight a southern president. Sharia had been part of the Nigerian legal system from the very beginning. It came therefore as a big surprise when northern states pushed aside the burning issues of poverty, diseases, and illiteracy among the people, to devote so much time to the re-launch Sharia.
Having been frustrated by the northern states' recalcitrant political stand on Sharia, southern states re-enacted the spirit of natural and human laws to fight back. Thus, generating a "reaction" to the north's earlier "action" of employing Sharia as a political weapon to rubbish the power shift to the South. It is logical for anyone to believe that Sharia was a political weapon against the South. This is so because Sharia was never re-launched during the tenure of northern Muslims. Realizing how volatile the issue of Sharia could be, not even General Sani Abacha, Nigeria's worst dictator re-introduced Sharia. The question then is "why bring it up now when a southern Christian is in power?" Let everyone answers the question as a Nigerian and not as a northerner, southerner, Muslim, or Christian.
The issue of using religious and economic issues as weapons for fighting political war might never end in Nigeria as long as segments of Nigeria describe themselves first as members of these segments before they see themselves as Nigerians. If some states could place some laws above the nation's constitution, which emerged through the mutual agreement of all the sections, then, a reaction is likely from the "offended" sections.
Regrettably, some Nigerians would not see the demand for resource control as an issue hastened by the north's earlier re-launch of Sharia. There is no gain- saying that for the issue of resource control is almost as old as the nation, but never in the history of Nigeria has it been echoed than after the north started creating a separate legal zone with the re launch of the Sharia legal system. So much has been said about the call for resource control by the South. One of such calls came from Alhaji Wada Nas, former adviser to former dictator, late General Sani Abacha. He reportedly said he "can imagine northern states for example taking effective advantage of their agricultural resources. Evidently, with revenue no longer coming from oil, they may form a cartel to impose heavy taxes on agricultural produce leaving their areas to the south." Without malice to Alhaji Nas, this writer is of the view that Alhaji Nas has continued to tread on the terrain he knows how to tread best - "benefiting from disunity."
Or how else can one explain off this statement. Alhaji Wada Nas would have addressed the issue properly by identifying why southern governors had to take their present stand, and suggest positive ways of resolving the strain in federal and state governments relationship. Perhaps, Alhaji Nas forgot that the South could cultivate their own yams and tomatoes or even import from elsewhere if they cannot afford the produce from the north, or simply choose to eat what their lands can produce.
If Alhaji Nas supported the re launch of Sharia in the North, with its weighty political implications on the unity of our country, why must he go against the call for resource control by the South? Besides, if he thinks that the north would raise the prices of farm produce, what gives him the impression that the South lacks further "reactions" to the north's action?
Governor Joshua Dariye of Plateau State was not myopic in his own reaction to the southern call for resource control. He spoke as a statesman seeking a solution. He said "it is unconstitutional for a group of people in the country to call for resource control." Although, he did not see things from the southern governors' point of view, but he phrased his statement in a better way.
Well, a southerner might re-phrased the governor's speech in this manner: "It is unconstitutional for a group of people in the country to call for Sharia" in flagrance disregard for the nation's constitution.
It is this writer's opinion that we own all the nation's resources collectively and jointly. It is expected that we should fairly share whatever we have as a nation and as a people. This writer equally hopes that the north will realize that the issue of resource control is a reaction to their initial action of the re- launch of the Sharia legal system. The northern states can as well retrace their steps, with regards to the use of religious weapon to fight political war, so the south can do a rethink about countering the north with an economic weapon.
- Mr. Ajaja sent this via e-mail from New York.

Original_Search_Title: No Democracy Here Yet - Enahoro
Headline: Nigeria: We Don't Have A Democracy
Body_Text:
Chief Anthony Enahoro, a leader of the National Democratic Coalition
and now head of Movement for National Reformation, speaks to Ademola
Adegbamigbe on various national issues.
Q: How do you feel as an old politician, compared to your early days
when there was still much energy in you?
A: Are you referring to physical or mental energy? As far as mental
energy is concerned, by the grace of God there is no difference
between the past and the present. Physically, the genes of my
forefathers and my years of devotion to golf and before that to
tennis, have helped to keep me fit.
Q: As a traditional chief here, give us a peep into your activities
back home?
A: Since I am not ordinarily at home in Uromi all the time, I
received special dispensation about certain activities at home. In
normal times, I went home from time to time as and when required to
do so, especially for the New Year and New Yam Festivals and
important meetings of chiefs.
Q: Let us discuss your days as an exile. What were the troubles you
had with NADECO members abroad? What were the points of disagreement
and area of convergence?
A: We did not have serious threats to the organisation. Occasional
points of disagreement were resolved by consensus, which was a
tradition I introduced and applied both at home and abroad.
A: When you returned from exile, you delivered a speech, revealing
that five pieces of advice were offered you by your admirers: that
you should abstain from politics; join an existing political party;
form a new one; vie for the presidency or that you should nurture
MNR. Which did you take to?
A: You must have read in the newspapers to which you have referred
the concluding portion in which I said that I would continue to
develop the MNR.
Q: Given your antecedents as a NADECO chief, people expected that
your natural habitat would be AD. Why did you not join it?
A: In that same publication, I explained why I did not think that I
should join any of the existing parties at the time. I have as yet
nothing to add to that.
Q: People expected that NADECO would form a formidable political
party. But that did not happen, why?
A: NADECO did not form a political party, formidable or otherwise,
because its members drifted in different directions. If you recall, I
was abroad when the parties were formed.
Q: Is NADECO still relevant, now that democracy is here?
A: If it is your opinion that democracy is here, I am afraid I don't
share your opinion. The constitution we have was imposed on the
country by the retreating military. Is that democracy?
Q: You toured some states when you returned. Was it for alliance
purposes?
A: I had been away for four years and I thought it rational to find
out the opinions held and positions taken by different parts of the
country before committing myself to any course of action.
Q: Was it the kind of alliance you had with the NPN?
A: I have not decided what relationship, if any, I will have with any
political group or association outside of the MNR.
Q: You visited the North and the East, but your critics claim that
you did not visit the West. Why?
A: If there are such critics, they do not have the facts. I was
received in Lagos by the Governor and at the State Assembly. I have
had meetings with the Western governors, I have visited Ondo State
where I was received by the Governor and the Executive Council, and
the State Assembly received me with a special session, which is an
honour that legislatures do not lightly accord to people.
Q: There have been many overtures that you should vie for the
presidency. Have you agreed?
A: I have said repeatedly that this is a matter which the MNR
Conference will consider, if it wishes.
Q: What is your current position on the Sovereign National
Conference?
A: My position is to state as simply as possible what the situation
requires. What is required is that the members of the conference
should be chosen by the nationalities to present their views and
negotiate their positions at the Conference, and the decisions of the
Conference should not be subject to amendment or interference by any
person or agency other than the people of Nigeria by a popular
referendum.
I have said publicly often enough that I do not believe a National
Conference would in any way threaten the integrity of Nigeria. Many
people who argue against a National Conference are
"I'm-all-right-jacks", that is persons who benefit from the
inequities of today. Nit-picking about the sovereignty of the
Conference is merely diversionary. We have never suggested that such
a Conference should discharge and replace the current incumbents of
public office before the expiration of their terms. In simple terms,
the argument is that the decisions of the Conference should not be
subject to tampering by any other person, clique or authority; they
should be subject only to approval or amendment by a popular,
nationwide referendum.
As to the modalities for such a Conference, with particular reference
to its composition and proceedings, Nigerians are entitled to take
offence, and should take offence, at any suggestion that the task of
conception and organisation is beyond our capability. Of course,
responsibility for it should not be entrusted to those who are daily
demonstrating their incompetence in many areas.
Q: Would delegates be elected or chosen?
A: What do you mean? To be elected is to be chosen, but to be chosen
is not necessarily to be elected.
Q: The National Assembly said it could handle any imbalance which the
SNC is clamouring for. What is your view here?
A: There issue here is twofold. One is the supremacy of the people's
will. It is the people who should determine their own constitution.
The second is that the National Assembly was imposed on the people by
the military and the Assembly is tainted by that indisputable fact.
We advocate a constitution produced at an assemblage of
representatives specifically delegated and mandated for that task and
representing the nationalities and not the states, considering that
the states were themselves created irrationally by the military
without reference to the people.
Q: Still on SNC, many countries that went through it, as critics
argue, ended in disaster. The Balkans, Soviet Union, etc.
A: What of the countries which went through the process successfully
and whose number is far greater than those you are quoting? The
Soviet Union was an empire created by Russia and it broke up just as
empires before it disintegrated.
Q: Resource control is an aspect of SNC. What is your position on it,
especially the new face of Chief Bola?
A: The MNR has for many years been advocating fiscal federalism. This
is not a new subject, although some people are only just becoming
aware of it.
Q: Dr. Sylvester Ugoh, a vice presidential candidate, once criticised
you on SNC, because, as he claimed, it negates your position on
Biafra which you described as "an anathema" in 1966. What is your
reaction?
A: If Dr. Sylvester Ugoh said that I described Biafra as "anathema"
in 1966, I am surprised that up till now, he has not been able to
produce any evidence to support his allegation or any reference to
the occasion on which I used that expression. The MNR position is
that each ethnic group -Igbos, Ijaws, Ogonis, Edo, etc -should enjoy
domestic self-determination within the Nigerian family.
Q:What is your assessment of Nigeria's political parties -PDP, AD,
APP?
A: I have nothing to add to the reference which I made to them in my
statement last December, of which you are obviously aware.
Freedom of association, the free formation, existence and operations
of political parties and freedom of candidature for elective public
office are essential ingredients of democracy. Where these freedoms
do not exist, democracy is absent.
Nobody should have the power to deprive Nigerians of their
fundamental human right to aggregate and freely form political
parties and nobody should rob law-abiding Nigerian citizens of their
right to contest elections on any platform they prefer, including as
independents. Orchestrated protest against any attempt by any
authority or individual to assume or exercise such power, would in my
view be a cogent expression of the people's desire for the growth of
democracy in Nigeria.
My understanding of the situation is that the ball is at the moment
in the National Assembly's Court.
Q: Appraise your colleagues in the AD, the party and its Governors.
Did you intervene in its crisis? Give an assessment of the Olusegun
Obasanjo government. How would you rate Governor Lucky Igbinedion?
A: Some of my colleagues, past or present, are in the three parties.
It would be premature to make an assessment of them or their parties
or governors, or of the President himself, before they've even
finished half of their terms.
But less than half way through the term of office of the present
incumbents in public office, there is already a scramble to displace
or replace many of them. I wish there was comparable intense interest
in continuous examination of their successes or failures.
Some of today's incumbents may have planted seeds which need time to
grow but some others are already manifestly a disappointment to the
electorate and a disgrace to their office.
I hope the media will give us well informed half-term reports on them
all. The reports will be interesting, perhaps even dramatic in some
cases.
Q: Obasanjo once criticised Babangida's economic policy as lacking a
human face. What is your description of Obasanjo's economic face?
A: The answer I have just given you applies equally to this question.
Q: Comment on the potentials of Association of South-South
Progression (ASSPRO).
A: I understand that I am to be briefed soon by the sponsors of the
front. When this has been done, I will in turn brief my colleagues in
the Movement for National Reformation (MNR).
I will not be in a position to make a further statement until these
processes have taken place. There are a number of associations in the
South-South just as a number are emerging in the other parts of the
country. I am watching them all with interest.
Q: You have been trying to energise MNR. For what purpose?
A: Let me give you a copy of the MNR brief. You will find therein the
purposes of the movement.
Q: Why did you break away from Awolowo's camp in the Second Republic?
A: I am afraid you'll have to wait to read my memoirs. I hope you'll
buy a copy when the time comes.
Q: Were you and Joseph Takar actually promised the presidency then?
A: My answer is the same. Wait and buy a copy of my memoirs.
Q: Is deregulation desirable as some people claim?
The centrality of the cost of fuel, be it petrol or kerosene or
diesel or gas, to the standard of living of the average person, is so
impacting that it is sheer nonsense to argue against fuel subsidy.
Transportation of agricultural products, market women's prices, the
impact of urban transportation on wages, children's schooling, family
feeding costs, rents, etc.-all are affected by fuel prices.
I think it is absurd for an oil-rich country which has no social
security, no unemployment benefits, no full employment, no public
transportation system, no healthcare service, and no comprehensive
free education, to quibble over subsidised fuel prices, especially
when all the country's oil resources are appropriated, inequitable
apportioned and manipulated as they are by a central authority.

Original_Search_Title: We Don't Have a Democracy
Headline: Nigeria: We Don't Have a Democracy
Body_Text:
Chief Anthony Enahoro, a leader of the National Democratic Coalition
and now head of Movement for National Reformation, speaks to Ademola
Adegbamigbe on various national issues.
Q: How do you feel as an old politician, compared to your early days
when there was still much energy in you?
A: Are you referring to physical or mental energy? As far as mental
energy is concerned, by the grace of God there is no difference
between the past and the present. Physically, the genes of my
forefathers and my years of devotion to golf and before that to
tennis, have helped to keep me fit.
Q: As a traditional chief here, give us a peep into your activities
back home?
A: Since I am not ordinarily at home in Uromi all the time, I
received special dispensation about certain activities at home. In
normal times, I went home from time to time as and when required to
do so, especially for the New Year and New Yam Festivals and
important meetings of chiefs.
Q: Let us discuss your days as an exile. What were the troubles you
had with NADECO members abroad? What were the points of disagreement
and area of convergence?
A: We did not have serious threats to the organisation. Occasional
points of disagreement were resolved by consensus, which was a
tradition I introduced and applied both at home and abroad.
A: When you returned from exile, you delivered a speech, revealing
that five pieces of advice were offered you by your admirers: that
you should abstain from politics; join an existing political party;
form a new one; vie for the presidency or that you should nurture
MNR. Which did you take to?
A: You must have read in the newspapers to which you have referred
the concluding portion in which I said that I would continue to
develop the MNR.
Q: Given your antecedents as a NADECO chief, people expected that
your natural habitat would be AD. Why did you not join it?
A: In that same publication, I explained why I did not think that I
should join any of the existing parties at the time. I have as yet
nothing to add to that.
Q: People expected that NADECO would form a formidable political
party. But that did not happen, why?
A: NADECO did not form a political party, formidable or otherwise,
because its members drifted in different directions. If you recall, I
was abroad when the parties were formed.
Q: Is NADECO still relevant, now that democracy is here?
A: If it is your opinion that democracy is here, I am afraid I don't
share your opinion. The constitution we have was imposed on the
country by the retreating military. Is that democracy?
Q: You toured some states when you returned. Was it for alliance
purposes?
A: I had been away for four years and I thought it rational to find
out the opinions held and positions taken by different parts of the
country before committing myself to any course of action.
Q: Was it the kind of alliance you had with the NPN?
A: I have not decided what relationship, if any, I will have with any
political group or association outside of the MNR.
Q: You visited the North and the East, but your critics claim that
you did not visit the West. Why?
A: If there are such critics, they do not have the facts. I was
received in Lagos by the Governor and at the State Assembly. I have
had meetings with the Western governors, I have visited Ondo State
where I was received by the Governor and the Executive Council, and
the State Assembly received me with a special session, which is an
honour that legislatures do not lightly accord to people.
Q: There have been many overtures that you should vie for the
presidency. Have you agreed?
A: I have said repeatedly that this is a matter which the MNR
Conference will consider, if it wishes.
Q: What is your current position on the Sovereign National
Conference?
A: My position is to state as simply as possible what the situation
requires. What is required is that the members of the conference
should be chosen by the nationalities to present their views and
negotiate their positions at the Conference, and the decisions of the
Conference should not be subject to amendment or interference by any
person or agency other than the people of Nigeria by a popular
referendum. I have said publicly often enough that I do not believe a
National Conference would in any way threaten the integrity of
Nigeria. Many people who argue against a National Conference are
"I'm-all-right-jacks", that is persons who benefit from the
inequities of today. Nit-picking about the sovereignty of the
Conference is merely diversionary. We have never suggested that such
a Conference should discharge and replace the current incumbents of
public office before the expiration of their terms. In simple terms,
the argument is that the decisions of the Conference should not be
subject to tampering by any other person, clique or authority; they
should be subject only to approval or amendment by a popular,
nationwide referendum. As to the modalities for such a Conference,
with particular reference to its composition and proceedings,
Nigerians are entitled to take offence, and should take offence, at
any suggestion that the task of conception and organisation is beyond
our capability. Of course, responsibility for it should not be
entrusted to those who are daily demonstrating their incompetence in
many areas.
Q: Would delegates be elected or chosen?
A: What do you mean? To be elected is to be chosen, but to be chosen
is not necessarily to be elected.
Q: The National Assembly said it could handle any imbalance which the
SNC is clamouring for. What is your view here?
A: There issue here is twofold. One is the supremacy of the people's
will. It is the people who should determine their own constitution.
The second is that the National Assembly was imposed on the people by
the military and the Assembly is tainted by that indisputable fact.
We advocate a constitution produced at an assemblage of
representatives specifically delegated and mandated for that task and
representing the nationalities and not the states, considering that
the states were themselves created irrationally by the military
without reference to the people.
Q: Still on SNC, many countries that went through it, as critics
argue, ended in disaster. The Balkans, Soviet Union, etc.
A: What of the countries which went through the process successfully
and whose number is far greater than those you are quoting? The
Soviet Union was an empire created by Russia and it broke up just as
empires before it disintegrated.
Q: Resource control is an aspect of SNC. What is your position on it,
especially the new face of Chief Bola?
A: The MNR has for many years been advocating fiscal federalism. This
is not a new subject, although some people are only just becoming
aware of it.
Q: Dr. Sylvester Ugoh, a vice presidential candidate, once criticised
you on SNC, because, as he claimed, it negates your position on
Biafra which you described as "an anathema" in 1966. What is your
reaction?
A: If Dr. Sylvester Ugoh said that I described Biafra as "anathema"
in 1966, I am surprised that up till now, he has not been able to
produce any evidence to support his allegation or any reference to
the occasion on which I used that expression. The MNR position is
that each ethnic group -Igbos, Ijaws, Ogonis, Edo, etc -should enjoy
domestic self-determination within the Nigerian family.
Q:What is your assessment of Nigeria's political parties -PDP, AD,
APP?
A: I have nothing to add to the reference which I made to them in my
statement last December, of which you are obviously aware. Freedom of
association, the free formation, existence and operations of
political parties and freedom of candidature for elective public
office are essential ingredients of democracy. Where these freedoms
do not exist, democracy is absent. Nobody should have the power to
deprive Nigerians of their fundamental human right to aggregate and
freely form political parties and nobody should rob law-abiding
Nigerian citizens of their right to contest elections on any platform
they prefer, including as independents. Orchestrated protest against
any attempt by any authority or individual to assume or exercise such
power, would in my view be a cogent expression of the people's desire
for the growth of democracy in Nigeria. My understanding of the
situation is that the ball is at the moment in the National
Assembly's Court.
Q: Appraise your colleagues in the AD, the party and its Governors.
Did you intervene in its crisis? Give an assessment of the Olusegun
Obasanjo government. How would you rate Governor Lucky Igbinedion?
A: Some of my colleagues, past or present, are in the three parties.
It would be premature to make an assessment of them or their parties
or governors, or of the President himself, before they've even
finished half of their terms. But less than half way through the term
of office of the present incumbents in public office, there is
already a scramble to displace or replace many of them. I wish there
was comparable intense interest in continuous examination of their
successes or failures. Some of today's incumbents may have planted
seeds which need time to grow but some others are already manifestly
a disappointment to the electorate and a disgrace to their office. I
hope the media will give us well informed half-term reports on them
all. The reports will be interesting, perhaps even dramatic in some
cases.
Q: Obasanjo once criticised Babangida's economic policy as lacking a
human face. What is your description of Obasanjo's economic face?
A: The answer I have just given you applies equally to this question.
Q: Comment on the potentials of Association of South-South
Progression (ASSPRO).
A: I understand that I am to be briefed soon by the sponsors of the
front. When this has been done, I will in turn brief my colleagues in
the Movement for National Reformation (MNR). I will not be in a
position to make a further statement until these processes have taken
place. There are a number of associations in the South-South just as
a number are emerging in the other parts of the country. I am
watching them all with interest.
Q: You have been trying to energise MNR. For what purpose?
A: Let me give you a copy of the MNR brief. You will find therein the
purposes of the movement.
Q: Why did you break away from Awolowo's camp in the Second Republic?
A: I am afraid you'll have to wait to read my memoirs. I hope you'll
buy a copy when the time comes.
Q: Were you and Joseph Takar actually promised the presidency then?
A: My answer is the same. Wait and buy a copy of my memoirs.
Q: Is deregulation desirable as some people claim? The centrality of
the cost of fuel, be it petrol or kerosene or diesel or gas, to the
standard of living of the average person, is so impacting that it is
sheer nonsense to argue against fuel subsidy. Transportation of
agricultural products, market women's prices, the impact of urban
transportation on wages, children's schooling, family feeding costs,
rents, etc.-all are affected by fuel prices. I think it is absurd for
an oil-rich country which has no social security, no unemployment
benefits, no full employment, no public transportation system, no
healthcare service, and no comprehensive free education, to quibble
over subsidised fuel prices, especially when all the country's oil
resources are appropriated, inequitable apportioned and manipulated
as they are by a central authority.

Original_Search_Title: KMA to Pay ¢8.8m T&T Monthly
Headline: Ghana: KMA to Pay Â¢8.8m T&T Monthly
Body_Text:
Kumasi — The Chronicle has firm conviction that the Kumasi Metropolitan
Assembly (KMA) would pay Â¢100,000 monthly to each of the 88 members
of the House as travel and transport (T&T) allowance.
The Administrative authority has approved of an increase in sitting
allowances for members.
Members would now take Â¢35,000 per sitting as approved by the
authority since March 30, 2001.
Meanwhile, 28 persons have been confirmed and sworn in as government
appointees to the KMA.
They include Messrs I. K. Gyasi, Kwame Boafo, Dr. Baffoe Bonnie, Nana
Kwame Agyeman Atwereboanda. Nana Boakye Yam, Oheneba Kwame Kyeretwie,
the Apagyahene and Baffour Appiah Dankwah, the Anantahene of Kumasi
among others.
The appointed members were sworn in by Mr. Justice G. M. Quaye, a
Kumasi High Court judge.
Justice Quaye congratulated them for their appointment and reminded
them to work in line with the President's zero tolerance of
corruption.
They also swore the oath of allegiance to bear true faith to the
Republic of Ghana and preserve, protect and defend the Constitution.
The election of a Presiding Member was, however, postponed to today,
April 12, 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Urges Support for Scientific Researchers
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Minister Urges Support for Scientific Researchers
Body_Text:
Abidjan, Cote d'Ivoire — Stakeholders in the Ivorian
economic field have been urged to support scientific research by the
country's academics because end-products would eventually benefit
their ventures.
Speaking at the 2000 Scientific Research Award presentation
ceremeony, Cote d'Ivoire's higher education and research minister,
Sery Bailly said promotion of scientific research would significantly
contribute to the progress of science and technology, the economic,
social and cultural
development of the country.
Three groups of Ivorian researchers, winners of the fifth annual
national scientific research prize, were presented with 15 million CFA
Francs and diplomas at a function held at the University of Abidjan.
Research by the first group, led by Professor Tea Gokou, was on
anti-viral molecule related to the fight against AIDS.
The second team, which focused on food and the improvement of yam
production in forest and savannah zones, was piloted by Assa Ayemou
and Ettien Djedji.
Professor Ouattara Tionan was awarded for his study on the
Fohobele minority ethnic community in the Bandama valley, 434 km north
of Abidjan.
"They emerged above the best ones. They are real heroes for they
obtained high quality results despite the difficulties of all sorts
that our country has gone through", Bailey said, commending the
researchers' work.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo, Are We Better Off Today?
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo, Are We Better Off Today?
Body_Text:
If we were to do a national opinion poll today, and the question is asked ordinary Nigerians, "are you better off today than you were two years ago?" What would the answer and indeed the rating of Nigerians of the Obasanjo regime be? Would most Nigerians for an example declare that they are better off today? Would their answer be that there has been no significant improvement in their lives; or would they say they are worse off today than they were before the "saint of Aso Rock", Chief Olusegun Obasanjo became the infallible custodian of all wisdom?
As it is, we are nearly at the mid way mark in the tenure of the current administration, both at the federal level and the states. And it is getting to that time of the year when the media would soon be swamped by self congratulatory supplements and advertisements by government officials, hangers-on and contractors eulogising the president and governors. Soon imaginations will run lose and fairy tales will be coated in glamorous language aimed at deluding the public, prodding them to award undeserved applause to their principals, the governors and the president.
While some like James Ibori of Delta, Orji Kalu of Abia, and their counterparts in Bauchi, Kaduna and Lagos might indeed earn genuine applause, others will be like President Obasanjo who has been long on promises and very short in delivering what has come to be known as dividends of democracy.
I, however, on behalf of my family have done the scoring of the Obasanjo regime, I have taken stock, just as a number of people who I know have, by their daily experiences. The result has not been flattering at all. I will vote with those whose lives have been affected negatively by the Obasanjo regime.
Indeed, I have become a laughing stock in the office, where the general taunt, from Obasanjo's kinsmen, has been "you see what that man you people elected into power is doing to this country and to Nigerians? "
Even I, I am now weary of my standard answer: "given another chance, I would rather vote for Obasanjo than for Falae." Indeed, I have had cause to ask myself over and over again, if Falae's government would have been as insensitive, if it would have been as non-productive as this one.
How for example would Falae have handled the question of NEPA? Two years on, would we still be in the dark, nebulous throes of promises, even as we gradually get less and less power supply from NEPA?
Baba, first told us, by December 2000, NEPA's darkness would be behind us; this was later edited to December 2001. Eight months to eldorado, most homes are worse off with power supply today. It is a miracle for example to have a 30 minutes power supply in any given day in my Amuwo Odofin GRA area. The Naira has never had it so rough. From N85 to $1 when Obasanjo took over, it is now about N135 to a $1.
Food too has become a major problem. I will not be shocked if one of President Obasanjo's ministers, preferably that one who has never lied in his life, comes up one day to announce to the nation that garri is to be deregulated; that afterall, Nigerians abroad pay far more than we currently pay for this common man's food, than Nigerians at home do; that indeed, it makes sense to continually increase the price of that common diet of the poor so that our neighbouring Benin Republic, or Cameroun, or even Niger indigenes will not develop long throat for it. It will then not matter that the cost of garri has risen more than 400% in the last two years, just like all staple food such as yam, plantain, millet, etc. It will be in government's character of inflicting the greatest havoc on the least resilient part of the populace, the majority poor.
I really wonder, given that nothing concrete has been achieved by this government, what in his quiet moments, the president would sincerely say he has achieved in his close to two years reign at Aso Rock. Is it the factories that are closing down in droves; the massive retrenchment which currently sees more than 35% of adult Nigerians out of work and graduates unemployed?
What would he point at? The dilapidated infrastructure nationwide or the hunger sweeping the land? Are these what he is beating his chest about and Chief Tony Anenih and co say there is no vacancy at Aso Rock?
Well, we shall meet this president at the polls in 2003, then he will know that his newly acquired sartorial elegance and saintly pretensions will not jell with Nigerians. At least, not with my wife and I.

Original_Search_Title: GSM: Is the Competition Won And Lost Already?
Headline: Nigeria: GSM: Is the Competition Won And Lost Already?
Body_Text:
The only good news coming from NITEL since the GSM auction is the news last week that it could continue serious discussions with MSI as a core partner that could take up to about 49 per cent of its GSM stakes.
Mind you this piece of news has nothing good to say about the ignominious treatment meted to Mr. Emanuel Ojeba, the former NITEL boss who was retired just three months away from going into full retirement. After serving this country for about 35 years, the man deserved a better treatment from some politicians who hold the yam and the knife.
On this page we had said that Ojeba didn't have the steam to stand the competition that was coming but nobody suggested such a shameful exit. There is always a reason for a sack but we dare say here that the gentleman is not responsible for the eclipse enshrouding NITEL's GSM license which it got on a platter of gold.
Those people who suggested that NITEL had no business with GSM but should instead concentrate on offering is primary but backbone services are already being justified. And quite interestingly, the two compertitors already rearing up to go, don't even believe that they have a fight coming from NITEL. MTN in a massive radio campaign says it is the better service provider while Econet on the hand is providing its own information bulletin on the advent of the GSM technology, where the company comes in, and what it plans to do. Merge this with the print campaign already awash in the newspapers, it becomes an over-kill.
Somebody asked me, where is NITEL? Moaning and being disillusioned. The answer comes straight from within the organization. On being told that both ECONET and MTN were due to rollout by next month, a highly placed NITEL source screamed: That is three months before we start. But the point here is that while others are busy recruiting personnel, importing equipment, and building a campaign pending a massive rollout, with a boast by one of them that it will hit a million mark in the first one year, dear friends, NITEL is quiet, extremely quiet and there is every indication that it will now come in as an under-dog.
The good news is that there will be phones soon with or without NITEL if you trust MTN , with time to operate outside NITEL network as company officials have promised. The very sad thing is that NITEL would have narrowed down the competition to just a straight fight between ECONET and MTN. The implications are grave, two of them being loss of revenue and playing a second fiddle in a land where it has been a superman.
The easier thing would be to leave NITEL to the mysterious strength of market forces but the most patriotic and expedient thing to do would be for concerned Nigerians to save NITEL from itself and from the choking hands of politicians.

Original_Search_Title: Consumer Price Index Still On the Rise
Headline: Kenya: Consumer Price Index Still On the Rise
Body_Text:
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) maintained its upward trend in the second month of this year, a trend that started in the first quarter of last year.
The upward movement was largely attributed to the rise in the prices of kerosene, cost of transportation and food items.
According to a report released by the Federal Office of Statistics (FOS), the Composite Consumer Price Index (CCPI) rose to 3927.3 in February up from 3848.9 recorded in January which show a 2.0 per cent increase.
In the preceding 12 months to February 2001, the average consumer price level increased by 18.6 per cent which was higher than the 17.6 per cent recorded in the preceding month. During the same period also, the urban and rural indices showed increases of 20.9 and 18.1 per cent respectively.
The report released recently also indicated that the percentage change in the average composite (CPI) for the 12 months period ending February 2001 as against the average of the CPI for the previous 12 months period of 2000 was 10.3. This means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00 during the 12 month period ending February 2000 sold for N110.3 on the average for the corresponding period ending February 2001.
Just as the composite consumer price index keeps to the upward trend so also the composite food index which also rose by 3.1 per cent between January 2001 and February 2001. In contrast however, the increase in food index, according to the same report was caused largely by the upward movement in the prices of staples such as Gari, guinea corn and yam tubers.
The report also observed increases in the prices of fish, processing foods and edible, which rose in many centers. Within the said period of February 2000 and February 2001 also, the composite Food Index rose by 13.8 per cent and the average annual rate of rise for the 12 months ending February 2001 was 4.8 per cent.
In the same vein the All Item less farm produce index which excludes the agricultural produce moved up by 0.8 per cent in February 2001 while in the 12 months before February 2001, the index rose by 24.3 per cent. The average annual rate of the rise of the index was 17.7 per cent for the 12-month period ending February 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Only True Federalism Can Make Nigeria Great-Omeruah
Headline: Nigeria: Only True Federalism Can Make Nigeria Great-Omeruah
Body_Text:
Yenagoa, Bayelsa — Former Minister of Sports, Air Commodore Samson Emeka Omeruah (rtd) has declared that only the practice of true federalism where all the federating units control their resources can set Nigeria on the track of actualising its full potentials.
The former airforce chief who was reacting to President Olusegun Obasanjo's declaration that the clamour for resource control will lead to another civil war said the gruesome 30 months civil war which rocked the 60s was not the struggle for resource control but because of the genocide committed against the Igbos.
Commodore Omeruah who spoke to Vanguard at the Port Harcourt International Airport described the Nigerian system as imperialistic and autocratic in nature for the federating states to attain their full potentials.
His words: "What is going on now in this country is not true federalism, if we must energise the kinetic energy existing in the individual state we must practice true federalism.
"The system that is presently operating in the country is imperialistic and autocratic. So we have to put in place true federalism. That is the only time when you can now release the abundant energy that exist in all the facets of our polity. If you really want to live in a place where your land grows yam and corn, you will want to be part and parcel of the eating of that corn so I don't see anything wrong with it. In the American system which we are trying to copy, resource control is in the states. What the federal government does at the national level is to tax those states with resources."
Continuing, he said: "If government can realise that there is need for those who are in the environment where the resources are to benefit from it, it will do our country well because it is God endowed and if God has given you a talent you should exploit it. I don't see anything wrong with it.
"But the fact is that the federal government, in order to meet up with other exigencies and expenditure should tax the people appropriately, that is the way it is done. The people within the vicinity should have control over their resources.
On the civil war, he said "most struggle this way has been that of resource control but the Nigerian civil war was not really resource control fight. The war started because the Igbos were purged, they were being massacred and they didn't know where to go but only to run to their homeland. "There were some argument which were not met and because of disagreement in meeting those agreement there was a civil war. "So it was basically not resource control war. If we know there was so much oil in Igboland as it is now the war would have been highly competitive."
He called on the people to be steadfast in their legitimate aspiration to have control of their resources saying the president's statement was meant to frighten them into abandoning the struggle.
On the alleged marginalisation of Ndigbo he said "marginalisation, I think, is a thing of the mind. Luckily for the Igbo race they have the capacity and sagacity do away with marginalisation."
According to him "if the Igbo can do those things they know best which is their belief is communalism and their drive for commercial ventures they should be able to do away slightly with the talk of marginalisation."
Said he: "you discover that even those we think are better off in terms of infrastructures are also talking about marginalisation. So you don't know who is being marginalised."

Original_Search_Title: OPD Settles Mile 12 Market Rift
Headline: Nigeria: OPD Settles Mile 12 Market Rift
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Office of Public Defender (OPD) has settled the age-long rift
between warring factions at the popular Mile 12 market.
The rift, which resulted in Yoruba/Hausa ethnic clashes last year,
continued unabated until the OPD waded in recently.
Speaking at the peace meeting brokered by the OPD, the Vice President
of the Shukura Yam Sellers Association, Alhaji Ismaila Umaru Lado,
expressed gratitude to government representatives at the meeting.
He appealed to both sides to forgive and forget the past in order to
give room for peaceful cohabitation among Yorubas and Hausas.
Alhaji Lado explained that the cross-cultural trading existing in the
market has led to different problems and misunderstandings. He,
however, stated that what mattered most is the peaceful resolution of
the cases without rancour.
He also called on the traders to report their grievances to their
respective leaders in the market so that such issues can be resolved
amicably.
While appealing for calm and patience from the different ethnic
groups in the market, he advised that all conflicts in the market
should be channelled through the OPD.
Responding, the head of OPD, Mrs. Akinlade called on all aggrieved
parties in the market to embrace peace.

Original_Search_Title: Government is Insensitive
Headline: Nigeria: Government is Insensitive
Body_Text:
Comrade John Ejoha Odah is the Acting General Secretary, Nigeria
Labour Congress, (NLC) which is organising the anti-fuel price
increase throughout Nigeria. He speaks on this conflict with the
Federal Government, salary arrears for civil servant and the
anti-people disposition of many federal legislators in this interview
with ADEMOLA ADEGBAMIGBE
Q: Many Nigerians do not quite understand the issues involved in this
NLC's showdown with the government...
A: The first one is to protest against the harrowing scarcity which
government has subjected Nigerians in the last couple of months. We
see it as part of the blackmail strategy to deceive and cow Nigerians
to submission in terms of accepting the increment. This blackmail is
further reinforced by the statement made by President Olusegun
Obasanjo at the Nigerian Defence Academy to the effect that Nigerians
are already paying above the official prices, so, they can pay much
higher for fuel!
Q: The Federal Government said that deregulation does not necessarily
mean increase in price...
A: No. During his February media chat, the president was categorical.
He said deregulation means they want to sell according to
international price so that they can get N200 billion to spend on
other things. On Monday, 19 March 2001, when he was addressing
students of the Nigerian Defence Academy, the president said the fact
that Nigerians had been paying above the official prices, is an
indication that they could pay more for fuel. Anybody talking to the
contrary (Jerry Gana and others) are just trying to confuse
Nigerians. That was on the basis of concrete recommendations to the
government by the I.M.F and the World Bank to increase the prices of
petroleum products so that they can get enough money to pay foreign
debts.
Q: The NLC has also argued that there is no subsidy. Do you have
statistical proofs to the contrary?
A: Yes. Even from government official documents, there is no subsidy.
According to the NNPC figures, government sells crude to NNPC at the
rate of $9.5. The NNPC spends $4 to refine it. At that level,
government is making a profit of $5.5. When it is refined, the NNPC
spends N15 on refining, transportation and the margin for marketers.
Here, government makes another N7 to N9 on a litre of fuel in Nigeria
through taxation and others. The argument about subsidy, therefore,
is not logical. What government is saying is that because crude is
sold at the international market at $26, it (government) should be
able to sell it to the NNPC at that margin. It is like if a yam
farmer sets aside some tubers for his family consumption, he cannot
price it the same way as the ones he takes to the market. The farmer
has no additional costs (on transportation, margin for middle men,
etc.) on the ones his family would consume. It is, therefore, wrong
to compare Nigerian price with the international price, because we
produce 2.1 million barrels of oil a day. We set aside only 300,000
barrels for local consumption . The balance is for the international
market. Nigerians do not receive international wages. Therefore, it
cannot pay international price for crude. In the United States, the
minimum wage ranges from $5.5 to $8 per hour. It translates to about
N700 per hour. Whereas, the minimum wage for one month in Nigeria is
N5,500.
Q: Many federal ministry workers are not being paid regularly. What
is the NLC doing about it?
A: If you can flash back to our new year message, we made a point
that this year, we would take on government and other employers who
are in the habit of not paying workers as at when due. We will
enforce the logic of "no pay no work."
Q: Nigeria Prisons and Immigration officials have not been paid for
four months running. Since they cannot go on strike, is there
anything the NLC can do about this?
A: By the time this comes to our attention, we will make a point
there . Government has no point keeping people in employment and
refusing to pay them.

Original_Search_Title: Experts Canvass Agricultural Revolution To Boost Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Experts Canvass Agricultural Revolution To Boost Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — In a bid to hit the projected target of six per cent growth in
agricultural output in 2001 against four per cent achieved last year,
the federal government has been advised to embark on an agricultural
revolution whereby states could identify certain areas and improve on
them.
Chief executive officer, Inter-Trust Limited, Dr. Chilo Offiah who
gave this advise in an interview with Vanguard recently frowned at
the non-challant attitude of government to agriculture, saying that
the sector should be given more attention particularly when viewed
against the fact that President Obasanjo is an established farmer.
Offiah however, suggested that Nigeria should embark on agricultural
revolution programmes where states should be mandated to handle
certain agricultural areas and improve on them.
According to him, "eastern states should go back to palm oil
production with a production target of one million to ten million
bags a year. The western states should be asked to go back to cocoa
producing or plantation and they should have a target".
He added that "the north also should be given target in animal
husbandry, groundnut-pyramid, the tuber yam seedlings and so many
other things they can produce.
In the 2001 fiscal year, the economy is projected to grow by about 5
per cent compared with 3 per cent in year 2000, the projected growth
of 5 per cent in anticipation of improvement in agriculture,
manufacturing, and other productive ventures. Secondly, the policy
thrust of the year includes stimulating of agricultural and
industrial production.
However, Dr. Offiah who was also the former managing director of FBN
(Merchant Bankers) Limited said that for the nation to meet the
estimated target on the contribution of agriculture to economic
growth, every state should go into agricultural production and
specialise in whatever area it has comparative advantage.
He stated that this would enable the country to be agriculturally
sufficient in the next few years as well as being able to export food
to West African and Africa as a whole.
Offiah in his words, 'we cannot keep on depending on oil which ha
been causing us a lot of problem. We have not done much in
agriculture and it is a shame. At times I wonder what the Ministry of
Agriculture has been doing in the past 18 months."
"I want to see the ministry take initiative on his own because the
ministry is quite our focus."
On a similar note, the president-general of Senior Staff Association
of University Teaching Hospitals, Research Institutes and Associated
Institutions of Nigeria (SSAUTHRIAI), Comrade Ralph A. Adeniyi
recently advised the government to emulate the advanced countries on
how they are committed to developing agriculture in their countries.
Adeniyi cited example with the International Institute for Tropical
Agriculture (IITA) based in Ibadan, but sponsored by American
government.
According to him, the Institute gets more funds than all the eighteen
National Agricultural Research Institutes in Nigeria. He stated that
the United States of America and Japan spend over 2.4 per cent of
their GDP on research and development annually while Ghana spends
about 0.3 per cent on agricultural research alone.
He complained "most of our relevant research projects in Nigeria have
been abandoned midway, the mass exist of experts is on ht increase
and many prototype technologies cannot be fully developed and
commercialised because the links with industry are yet to be finally
established,' Adeniji said.

Original_Search_Title: Ebonyi: Rekindling Hope Through Agriculture
Headline: Nigeria: Ebonyi: Rekindling Hope Through Agriculture
Body_Text:
Lagos — With the country's fluctuating oil revenue and increasing demand for
infrastructure, Ebonyi state state has turned to agriculture for a
life-line, writes Joseph Ushigiale
Several decades ago, Nigeria was reputed to have derived her mainstay
and sustainability through her agricultural endowments which were
adequately exploited for maximum proceeds. During these years, as the
history of economics shows, Nigeria currency was quite strong and
stable. The gross domestic product, income per capital and standard
of living indices including growth maintained an enviable status.
It is also on record that the country's immeasurable agricultural
potentials attracted British economic interest and eventual
colonisation. Nigeria's huge agricultural potentials made up largely
of unprocessed raw materials were used by British factories
especially during the Industrial1 revolution and period of the great
recession.
But the glorious period of groundnut pyramids in the Northern part of
Nigeria, cocoa and timber in the Southwest was punctuated with the
discovery of oil in commercial quantity in 1956 at Oloibiri.
Gradually, emphasis and attention of both individuals and government
shifted from the sector to oil where very little effort was employed
to reap what has come to be regarded as black gold.
The energy crises of the early seventies which was triggered by the
Arab- Israeli war which eventually shot up oil prices to an
unprecedented high was the last straw that obliterated whatever hopes
of returning agriculture to its past glory. This is because the
government of the day instead of formulating and implementing
appropriate agricultura1 policies for that purpose relapsed into
widespread and unrestrained importation of finished products, which
at the long run had catastrophic consequences on our economy.
Although two unsuccessful attempts have been made, not only to
position the country as an agricultura1 products exporting country
but also to ensure that there is self-sufficiency in food production.
The first was the Obasanjo Operation Feed the Nation (OFN) and the
Shagari's green revolution programmes. Both programmes laudable as
they appeared on paper failed to reach the implementation stages.
Today, however, with dwindling oil revenue, the federal government is
now grappling with the problem of sourcing complementary revenue to
oil through agriculture. The federal government is not alone,
following in this stead are state governments which have found
themselves bogged down by increasing demands for infrastructure from
the electorate and an equally disappointing share from the statutory
allocation. These increasing worries have culminated in the present
quest for resource control by some 17 governors in the south.
Again, some of these states are now confronted with the alternative
of turning to agriculture for a life-line. If there is any state
government in Nigeria that is greatly desirous of charting its growth
through agriculture, irrespective of its financia1 1imitations, it is
Ebonyi state State. The state government's direction in agriculture
within the run of governor Sam Egwu administration clearly indicates
its readiness to re-enact and rekindle hope through this sector.
"Before the advent of petroleum, the agricultural sector of the
Nigerian economy provided the greatest proportion of the most needed
foreign exchange earnings and the base for the social and economic
development of Nigeria," Egwu told Obasanjo during his visit 1ast
December.
He said past governments in the geopolitical zone covering Ebonyi
state State today, had always paid lip service and least attention to
agriculture with consequent disappearance of the notable Abakaliki
rice, Onueke cattle, Abakalilki chicken, yam and others adding that
the present lack of attention and the non provision of the necessary
incentives and auxiliary services to farmers in the state by past
administrations have resulted in the decreasing productivity of
farmers and increased marginalisation of this segment of the state
population that constitute the majority.
According to him, on assumption of office, his administration had
re-assessed the state developmental priorities and concluded that
improvement in the productivity of agriculture that engage over 90%
of the total population could result in the improvement of the total
standard of living of the generality of the people and ensure
sustainable poverty alleviation.
The state commissioner for agriculture Dr Donatus Onu agreeing with
the assertion explained that the major thrust of the administration
has been to improve agricultural production in the state on the
understanding that the majority of farmers in the state are basical1y
small-scale farmers.
According to him, "we believe that if we extend to the people,
technology that can improve the individua1 farmer productivity, it
will lead to commensurate improvement in their standard of living".
He clarified that "since the rural population constitute the majority
of the population if the standard of living of this percentage is
improved, we can comfortably conclude that the state government has
achieved its goals in this direction. This explains why most of our
agricultural packages are highly subsidised by the state, it is not
aimed at maximising revenue for itself. The facilities being put in
place are to facilitate our production system".
At the moment, he explained that it is socio-economically impossible
for farmers here to acquire fairly large agro production technology
like installation of irrigation facilities.
As soon as the administration came on stream Egwu's first step
towards realising his agricultural objectives was to lead trade
delegation to Israel for the purpose of attracting Israel technica1
assistance in agricultural development and food production; another
visit was equally undertaken to South Africa for the same purpose,
the fruits of these visits are now manifest in the joint venture
agreement between the state government and an Israeli company
Dizengoff W.A. which is currently engaged in the bui1ding of an
integrated modern Poultry farm at Nkaliki valued at about Sl3m.
Ebonyi state State possesses huge agricultura1 potential, which are
especially manifest in its endowment with good climatic conditions.
Onu affirmed that all the agro-ecological conditions of the state
favour the production of all types of crops and livestock confirming
that the soil type and other relevant climatic condition stand the
state out as a potential investment haven for agro based
entrepreneur.
Although the state government thrust has not begun to tap these
potentials in a manner that will chart an alternative revenue base
for the state in the light of its least position on the statutory
revenue allocation table, it has implanted specific technology which
will in the long run ensure that revenue accrues to the state in
future.
One of these steps is the establishment of an ultra modern oil palm
nursery in Ezzamgbo, About 40,200 palm seedlings are sold monthly to
farmers at subsidised prices. For this initiative the state
government spent N80m for the construction of standard net house oil
palm nursery. The nursery which is located at the veterinary
investigation centre with a facility of producing 2m palm seedlings
per annum and is equipped with modern irrigation facilities.
The state government's direction, according to Egwu, is to assist and
raise small holders in the sub-sector with inputs such as hybrid
seedling species developed in the National Institute for oil Palm
Research (NIFOPR) Benin which are currently being made available to
include farmers and agricultural agencies for pilot schemes.
As an agrarian state, where farmers have consistently relied on old
and archaic traditional methods of farming, the state government in
its drive to create awareness and improve yields for its farmers, has
introduced modern technique of farming especially in the use of
fertilisers and tractors. Millions of naira have been committed by
the Egwu administration towards the procurement and distribution of
different grades of fertiliser at 50 percent subsidy.
To underscore the level of importance it attached to the project, a
company, Solidarity International, an affiliate of Yagus
Internationa1 of Illinois, renowned manufacturers of blending plants
across the world, Onu said, was contracted and mobilised to the tune
of N191m to supply and insta1l the plant.
Another area that has gained significant attention of the state
government in the sector is in poultry production. Soon after Egwu
assumed office, he led a trade delegation to Israel the outcome of
that visit today is a contract with an Israeli firm, Dizengaff W.A
for the modernisation of the Nkaliki hatchery When completed, it is
projected that the poultry project considered about the largest in
the west coast will at its full capacity produce 16,800 day old
chicks and 8000 broilers weekly, 4.16m broilers per year and 8.74m
eggs yearly.
The poultry will not only be a source of revenue and foreign exchange
earning but also a major source of employment as well as meeting the
challenge of protein production for the entire South East zone and
Nigeria.
On the philosophy behind the poultry investment Onu stressed that
"the philosophy lies in the history that before now, the only
livestock that attracted attention and practice of indigenous farmers
in Ebonyi state is poultry production.
In the process, these farmers encountered problems of procuring
day-old chicks, the production of this strategic poultry in put
require high capital and machinery that are unavailable to them. He
further said that their search for day-old chicks sometimes took the
farmers to Otta Farms in Abeokuta in Ogun state to procure them, a
course that often exposed them to risks that affect their
productivity.
He said that at the inception of the Egwu administration, the state
government evaluated the situation and evolved a plan to ensure that
day old chicks were produced to provide a lasting solution to
problems of the small- scale poultry farmers.
"We have achieved all these for now, we are selling eggs at N5 per
egg broilers at N350 per one. The poultry project is going to be
about the highest this side of Africa and there are p1ans to
privatise it for efficient equipment utilisation, materia1 and human
development". he enthused.
As an indication of its seriousness the state government recently
awarded contract for the construction of a 2.5km road, to provide
access to the hatchery.
Onu said government is ready to build houses for interested investors
and to assist them to mechanise their activities at highly reduced
rate. We are still looking at the possibilities of direct involvement
in production that will be shared by equity between the state
government and the private investors. But at the same time, we are
1ooking for potential investors who are ready to enter into agro
-based ventures with the state. We want an element of private
initiative in collaboration with the state" For the grassroot
farmers, the state government has been able to assist them through
the state Agricultural Development Programme strengthen an extend
improved agricultura1 to services, which are capable of not only
revolutionising their production capacity alone but also to
remarkably ensure a clear cut departure from old traditiona1 ways to
modern day service.
Onu noted that funding for the agricultural programme was done
through tripartite arrangement with the World Bank, federal
government and the benefiting state after which the state takes over
after the programme has run its course; the state is still operating
its programme under the arrangement.
To ensure that the state's rural farmers are not incapacitated by
lack of funds, the government recently procured a N50m loan from the
Nigerian Agricultural and Co-operative Bank (NACB) to fund its
micro-credit facilities for which each farmer is to get about N75,000
loan to strengthen the financial base of their farms. In all, Ebonyi
state expects these efforts to result in food sufficiency for her, as
well the country.
What's more? Observers note that Ebonyi's novel 'green' efforts
should be a worthy model to be cloned by other state's who share her
socio-economic constraints.

Original_Search_Title: Battling the Weeds of Destruction
Headline: Nigeria: Battling the Weeds of Destruction
Body_Text:
Lagos — When water hyacinth made its surprise appearance on Nigeria's water
around 1979 on a massive scale, it caught the authorities at the
different tiers of government unawares. As a result, they were at a
loss as to how to control the destructive weed.
Various suggestions were made ranging from the sensible to the
bizarre. Some experts even suggested the importation of a particular
kind of weevil that would eat up the seed that germinate the water
hyacinth. Efforts made then at controlling the menace were believed
to be half-hearted, hence the weeds returned even stronger some years
later.
Since then, it has been off and on and economic life in the riverine
areas that centre mainly on fishing has been the worse for it. But it
is not only the fishermen that have been suffering. Speedboat
operators who use the waterways on a daily basis also have bitter
tales to tell. Perhaps, the story is better told by a first time
visitor to Aiyetoro, the popular riverine community near Igbokoda in
Ondo State.
Sometime in 1985, this reporter had cause to go to Aiyetoro on an
assignment to see how the people of this Island run their lives.
Socialism, one had been told, was their way of life and in a backward
capitalist economy like Nigeria's socialism beckons to the average
Nigerian who could barely afford to make ends meet.
On arrival at Igbokoda, the sprawling water of the lagoon became an
attraction and a source of fear having never travelled on water
before. But the boisterous manner the speedboat operators were
conducting their business and the movement back and forth of the
passengers, particularly smoked fish sellers, exchanging their wares
for other agricultural produce, like yam, not grown in their
waterlogged islands reassured me all was well and safe to travel by
the boats afterall.
After some haggling over the fare, the speed boat with about 20
passengers on board, sitting on five rows of seat made of planks
departed, heading for Aiyetoro and other nearby islands. The
estimated one and a half hours journey began well for me but as I was
beginning to enjoy the voyage, tragedy nearly struck. The boat ran
into a large expanse of water hyacinth, the kind I had never seen
before. The entire water was covered by the weeds. As the captain, or
skipper, of the boat was trying to navigate through, the engine
suddenly stopped, the speedboat lost its speed, stalled in the weeds
