violence. Dan's father too was not too enthusiastic about his first
child's desire to go to the 'jungle of Africa' to work. The prevalent
mentality in the United States is of Africa as a continent of highly
backward people. Dan's other siblings, a brother and two sisters,
would also probably have stopped Dan from making the trip had he not
been the oldest. Dan recalls that when his younger brother who is a
medical doctor called him at the station recently and was put on hold
with the music of Britney Spears playing on the station, he could not
believe a Nigerian station could be playing Britney Spears' latest
music! Dan's younger sisters, a nurse and a lawyer, have largely come
to understand he's doing a job he is enjoying. One year on, he's not
had cause to regret his decision to come down here. He describes the
time he has had here so far as fun. He's fully settled and thanks to
the free hand he's given to innovate and create on his five-hour
morning programme. Dan is having a ball. Dan is not married yet but
this is a situation he might remedy in a very short while. Courtesy
of the love he's found right here in Nigeria. Dan's life has
certainly taken on a new kind of fun. He won't allow her name in the
papers yet because he doesn't want to pre-empt or pre-spoil anything.
He has found love and she's a Nigerian.

Original_Search_Title: Home-made Bomb Goes Off In Gayaza
Headline: Uganda: Home-made Bomb Goes Off In Gayaza
Body_Text:
Kampala — A "highly lethal" homemade bomb was yesterday detonated by police in
Kasangati-Kyankima village, off Kampala-Gayaza road.
Terrified residents scampered for cover when the bomb, which had been
concealed in an aluminium Britannia biscuit container, was detonated
by the Police Bomb Squad at about 8.00am.
Two police dogs were used to sniff and confirm that there were
explosives.
The police at Kasangati described the bomb as "highly lethal". They
confirmed it was "planted" in the home of a Kampala businessman,
Emanuel Kavuma.
Kavuma's house, on Nakigoye Road, is surrounded by a short mistle-toe
hedge without a gate.
A visibly-shaken Kavuma, a trader in the city's Nabugabo business
area, said he returned home with his wife at about 10.00pm and packed
their car beside their bedroom window.
"I woke up at 5.00am to check on the car which was packed just
outside our bedroom window. I saw a tin near the car. The tin was
covered with part of the hedge, yams and black plastic bags. At first
I thought a mad person or a nightdancer had visited us," Kavuma said.
"I rang the Nangabo LC3 chairman, Kizza Seninde, my brother David and
the police. There were wires leading to the car. My wife said it
might be a bomb.
The police brought dogs. I suspect it was meant to explode if I
turned on the car's ignition," he added.
On the motive of planting the bomb at his home, Kavuma said, "The
chairman (Seninde) and I are some of President Museveni's supporters.
This bomb might be a result of political differences, may be here or
at Nabugabo where I work.
But we leave it to the police to investigate and tell us."
Meanwhile, the anti-terrorism unit detonated another home-made bomb
at Kampala Casino last night, report Davis Weddi and A. G Musamali.
The bomb was discovered by the special-hire drivers who park in front
of the casino on Kimathi Avenue. The bomb was hidden in a tree on the
pavement.
The drivers informed the security at the casino, whose managing
director, Bob Kabonero, called in the anti-terrorism unit based at
the Chieftaincy of Military Intelligence. The unit came with a robot
to detonate the bomb that went off in a big explosion at about
8:00pm.
The police said the bomb had a timing device and they suspected it
had been set to go off later that night during the busier hours.

Original_Search_Title: Traditional Medicine Men Invade Street Pavements
Headline: Zimbabwe: Traditional Medicine Men Invade Street Pavements
Body_Text:
Harare, Zimbabwe — Zimbabwe's traditional medicine
men are seldom far from controversy.
They are at it again, this time claiming they have
discovered an effective AIDS drug, the African potato.
As if the claim is not controversial enough, the medicine
men have invaded major cities in the country, vending the drug on
street pavements.
Street corners and shop fronts in the cities, particularly
the capital Harare, have been turned into open air pharmacies,
much to the annoyance of medical authorities who think the drug
is fake and perhaps harmful.
The African potato, a yam-like tuber whose botanical name is
hypoxis, first hit town in January this year, and has since
spread throughout the country.
The medicine men claim it cures many ailments, such as
cancer and AIDS, which conventional medicine is not yet able to.
With a quarter of Zimbabwe's 12.5 million people estimated
to be suffering from AIDS, the drug has found a ready market.
Officially, 5,000 people are thought to be dying from the
incurable disease weekly in the country, a figure many say is a
gross under-estimate.
"During the first few days, we used to sell about five
pieces (tubers) but now we are selling much more," said Kurauone
Phineas, an African potato vendor.
"I am not disclosing how much because there are many people
who are fighting us," he said, evidently excited about the
booming business.
Cost is another major attraction of the drug to sufferers.
It costs between Z$30 and Z$50 per tuber, depending on size. The
vendors say this is enough for one person for a month.
Conventional retro-viral drugs, by comparison, cost hundreds
of times much more, and are beyond the reach of most Zimbabweans.
But, as in similar cases in the past, the traditional
medicine men are not having it easy with medical authorities with
their latest discovery.
Conventional doctors, including Minister of Health Timothy
Stamps, are warning people against using the African potato which
they say could be harmful as it has not yet been subjected to
laboratory tests.
"Purchasing the African potato from the street is very
dangerous. These herbs are toxic and can kill people on the spot
or damage their kidneys permanently," said David Mabaya, a
director of government pharmacies.
Stamps agreed: "There are eight different types of African
potato classified as poisonous and medicinal herbs, implying that
whoever prescribes it must know what he is doing as it can be
poisonous."
"The herb has a strong action in the human body. An overdose
can lead to poisoning. In fact, it does not really cure or
reverse any illness. What it does is that it makes someone feel
different," he added.
But the traditional medicine men say this is yet another
ploy by modern medical practitioners to under-play their
discovery, in order to dominate the medical industry in the
country.
"Some of them are buying (the African potato) from us and
then reselling to their clients at very high prices. They are
charging patients in excess of Z$3,000 for a stomach ailment but
we charge Z$100 or less for the same concoction," said Phineas.
Patients, for now, seem to support the traditional medicine
men who have frequently locked horns with the country's medical
authorities for dismissing most of their medicines as fake.
"I was in a serious condition when I came to this clinic (of
a traditional medicine man) a month ago. But Mr Ncube prescribed
for me among other things, the African potato. I am now feeling
much better and I have re-gained my weight," said an AIDS
sufferer.
In spite of the medical authorities' skepticism of the
African potato's medicinal value, AIDS sufferers in Zimbabwer are
flocking to the traditional medicine men's pavement pharmacies,
including tourists.
"There are many whites flocking into this market looking for
the African potato. Some are tourists while some are locals,"
said Lizwe Baloyi, who operates a thriving pharmacy mainly
specializing in the drug.

Original_Search_Title: Prof. Nkrumah Recalls Accra Of The '50s
Headline: Ghana: Prof. Nkrumah Recalls Accra Of The '50s
Body_Text:
Accra —  Pof. Joe Nkrumah of the Ghana National Museums and Monuments Board
had part of his childhood days in the city of Accra in the then Gold
Coast. He recalls those good old days with nostalgia in a chat with
the Accra Mail in his office.
SOCIAL LIFE IN The CITY
Accra in the 50s was an interesting and hectic place especially in
the central part, the Orgle Street area or Faase as the Gas called
it. Faase means behind the stream. This is an implication that there
was at a certain period of time a body of water or a stream at the
place. There were various cultural activities in Accra because of the
different ethnic groups existing in the city. The half mile square
radius area around the present day Bank of Ghana and the Parliament
House were scenes of many social activities at weekends and even
during week days. The present day Ghana Commercial Bank was the site
of the famous Rodgers Club, a social centre built by the one-time
colonial governor of the Gold Coast, Governor Rodgers. This spot was
open to all races unlike the European Club, which was an exclusive
all-whites club. Across the road in front of the old Parliament
Building was the Accra Town Hall built by the then Anglican Bishop of
Accra during the tenure of Governor Hodgson. The old Polo Ground
where the Kwame Nkrumah Mausoleum stands today used to be the Accra
Community Centre. There was also the Baden-Powell Memorial Hall,
which also served as a social centre. When the aforementioned centres
were occupied the Bishop's Girls' School could also be used for
social functions.
CINEMA HOUSES
The elite of society had their own cinemas and these were the four Rs
cinemas - Rex, Royal, Roxy and Regal, which were owned by Captan. The
Temani Brothers also built the Cinema Palace, which was the first to
show daytime programmes at 12.15 not forgetting the 8.15pm shows.
Then the Opera Cinema came on board to compete with Cinema Palace. It
cost 6pence to watch a show. As kids in those days we raised money to
enable us go for these shows by selling newspapers like Graphic,
Evening News, Daily Echo and Morning Post. This venture could fetch
us some 5shillings a day.
GROWING UP IN THE CITY
As kids we slept little waking up at 3am because we had to engage in
a number of ventures like selling newspapers and helping the
fisherfolks to earn some money and at the same time go to school.
This was a time when discipline was at its highest peak. When fish
was in season we went to the beach through a stairway near the Ussher
Fort to help the fishermen cart their catch home. We created a sort
of balloon with a cloth and this we used to hold some of the fish we
carried for the fishermen. We carried with us a basket each. We added
the few fish we were able to get to the ones the fishermen gave us
and sold them. This done we went to collect the morning newspapers
which we sold before going to school.
SCHOOLS
As a kid I lived at Osu RE initially and it was at this place that I
started schooling. While there I sold cut yams for my mother. The
pieces of yam were arranged in a pan and to keep them fresh I carried
water in the pan for watering.
In 1951 the Gold Coast District was situated at Nima junction and it
was responsible for primary education. Giffard Camp was for Middle
School education and this was where I had my middle school education.
The late General Kotoka was one of my teachers at Giffard Camp. At
that time all the teachers in the garrison schools for kids of
soldiers like myself had teachers from the military's Education
Corps. My father was a soldier in the Gold Coast Regiment fighting in
both India and Burma. At the end of the war he re-enlisted as a
teacher.
I was not strictly a barracks boy because my father was part of the
administrative and professional class in the military who lived
outside the barracks. I can not remember the feeling I had when my
father was leaving for the war because I was too small.
CHILDHOOD DAYS IN AKWAPIM MAMPONG
I had part of my childhood days in Akwapim Mampong and it was
interesting watching soldiers engage in military exercises during
which they used live ammunition. After the exercises we took the
unused bullets and the empty cases of the spent rounds with which we
played with. We opened the brass shell and emptied the powder and
made it to generate a fine smell. Another thing we did was create
holes on a plank and place the live rounds in them. We then supported
this between two stones and employed a certain trick to do target
practice.
BARRACKS LIFE
The only time that I experienced life was in Kumasi. In Accra in the
40s administrative and professional staff lived outside the barracks.
It was only the infantry men and bands men who lived in the barracks.
SUPERMARKETS
The Kingsway stores was next door to the Ussher Fort in those days
and the building still stands today. It occupied two floors. The
shops were later moved to the new Kingsway when the place was
finished. Other shops were the CFAO, A.G. LEVENTIS, SCOA, UTC, SAT,
G.B. OLLIVANT, John Holt. The Unilever House was built by UAC.
ACCRA IN THE '50s
Places like Abossey Okai, Mamprobi and others were seen as being
outside Accra. From Abossey Okai one had to pass through Fadama,
which was a reclaimed land and the Owusu Memorial Park where the yam
market is. The city had two cemeteries but churches like the
Methodist Church and others had their own churchyards.

Original_Search_Title: PJ Dines At The Ghanaian Village
Headline: Ghana: PJ Dines At The Ghanaian Village
Body_Text:
Accra — La-Palm Royal Beach Hotel has recently opened another restaurant.
Two, as a matter of fact, but it's The Ghanaian Village, which serve
local dishes that thrilled me on my visit there for lunch the other
day. I am welcomed to its very airy sheds with an optional calabash
of sweet palm wine, whose first taste on my tongue is quite
refreshing.
The main meal on the menu for today is Banku and Tilapia, and Chicken
khebab with an assortment of accompaniment, including yam balls,
fried potatoes and Jollof rice in small portions. I opt for the
khebab dish. I am not about to leave La-Palm this afternoon with an
unsavoury smell on my hands. I, like many Ghanaians I know, devour
tilapia, and this is neither the place nor the time to engage in a
plate of the delicate fish, best eaten with the hands, like many
local dishes! A chicken dish is always a civilised one. I could use
my cutlery and behave civilly.
The restaurant is set in the gardens of the hotel, quite near the
coastline. A gangway of some sort leads to its entrance with
different colourful plants lining its side. The ocean roars nearby as
you eat, and there is certain serenity about this place, which makes
you relax without even trying. The decor adds immensely to all this.
It's all thatch for the roof, calabash for lampshades, trunk beams
for pillars, bamboo here, and cane there, with a ceramic and earth
colour scheme. It's simply beautiful, the way it's done.
The waiters are eager to please, and seem to be at the 'beck and
call', ready to provide any item one asks for. I want hot pepper, I
tell one, and in an instant, it is provided.
The seats are cane, wide and with comfortable cushions. When the food
arrives, two tall khebabs on one plate, nicely garnished and
accompaniment in another on the side, I mumble something in my head
about the portions. They seem small, but fill me up somewhat, I
realise by the time I'm through. The presentation is most impressive.
The starter was coconut salad, which was a mix of lettuce and onions
with slices of real coconut fare. Very tropical! There is creme
caramel for dessert, which I pass.
Eating here is an experience. There's quite an ambience, and the
prices are high. But you get to be fussed over. Most credit cards are
accepted here.
The Ghanaian Village is inside La-Palm Royal Beach Hotel at La. Call
711700 to make reservations.

Original_Search_Title: Issues: the $5.8 Billion External Debt
Headline: Ghana: Issues: the $5.8 Billion External Debt
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's external debt makes a mouthful of zeros behind the main
figures. Dressed, we simply call it Â¢31.7 Trillion just wondering how
many zeros fill our imagination. The loan itself is not as seriously
high as the length of the actual figure expressed in cedis if it
comes to be written. Why do Ghanaians want to ridicule their nation
this way?
The only comments available on the internet concerning the Ghanaian
currency is about the story of a foreigner who was going to receive
money transferred to him through one of our banks. He said he saw
people in the queue going away with filled black plastic bags. It was
when his thousand dollars were packed into a sizeable BLACK PLASTIC
BAG - a good seven million cedis of fourteen bundles that he started
trembling. He had nowhere to hide the trash!
POCKET MONEY
One tends to carry his pocket money in his wallet and not on his head
to the glare of all thieves and armed robbers. And exactly that is
how heavy our small loan of $5.8 Billion has become! Indeed to some
of us it only goes to show that we are not credit worthy enough as a
nation.
To think of it this same amount would have been around seven trillion
cedis just three years ago. Our cedi was then around Â¢2,300 to one
dollar. We have chosen the path of making the same amount of $5.8
Billion cedis sound 31.7 Trillion. This is more than four times what
the Ghanaian tax payer is being asked to pay for that same amount of
dollars!
Meanwhile, for those three years the best many employers have been
able to do has been to increase the yearly salary by a maximum of 25%
per annum. This has in only a few cases ended up in a 100% increase.
Petrol prices got about 25% increase each year to at most 100%. Beer,
soft drinks and other taxable products have not had their prices
increased much. Yet these tax payers are being asked to pay three
times the cedis for the same amount of dollars. This does not make
any economic sense.
If that foreigner were to carry that bag of seven million cedis
exchanged for his one thousand dollars back to New York NO BANK WOULD
EXCHANGE The LOAD TO GIVE HIM EVEN ONE DOLLAR FOR HIS BUS JOURNEY
HOME!
Which means that the cedi is globally worthless no matter how low we
sink it in relation to the Dollar.
On the other hand if that stranger took twenty thousand of that load
of cedis and bought two tubers of yam, he could sell it to some
Ghanaian couple he or she knows in the same New York for some $ 5 or
even $10 to get a good meal. This goes to show how we are just
burdening ourselves to try to cook a price for our cedi. It is
worthless outside.
If we do not wise-up about this fact we shall be putting a useless
yoke on the tax payer for nothing. It is only the products we put on
the international market that can help us to pay the external debt.
And many of these debts have only one digit interest rate and many
years of moratoriums!
The worth of the cedi should therefore not be allowed to stifle the
very process of production! Anyone who borrowed dollars for any means
of production three years ago can simply not pay for it.! Many may
have to abandon their dream some have gone back to Europe or America.
The wonder story of the ex-ministers' cars are all a part of this
problem. Â¢38 million price of the car was around $16,000 three years
ago. One quarter of that amount is now $4,000 after 3 years. This is
today Â¢28 million.
Why do those who operated that kind of cedi depreciation of the
economy not understand the simple mathematics of it and pay Â¢28
million? Ghana listens to any foreigner who comes along as an
economic expert to talk the book knowledge we seen too much to
understand and adore with no ability to re-shape before applying. Yet
we claim to be better educated than most African countries.
The reader should just open page 33 of the latest West Africa
magazine.
On this page is listed the worth of each African country's currency
to the dollar. Even Somalia without a government manages around 2,680
of her currency to one dollar. We are only next to Mozambique whose
floods have shattered her economy.
There is a lot of carelessness in our micro and macro fiscal
management within the various zeros behind our currency figures.
Normally the small child would have asked for a unit figure, one
cedi, to buy herself something. We have trained and are still
training our children to ask for one thousand cedis, five hundred
cedis or the least two hundred! It is the same training we are giving
to our economic managers. It is one point or three point something.
Our cedi is worth nothing! We can as at now never get an
international worth for it! The Minister of Finance would himself
recognise how many pebbles would clear of his eyes and the eyes of
all Ghanaians if we were to say today that our new currency is now
called so so and so,.That it is worth 70 to the dollar and worth
Â¢100. This means the cedis become like our pesewas. This would not
change our external debt much.
BURDEN
Even more courageously the minister can give a new worth to our own
cedis. Seven hundred cedis to one dollar shall never change the
dollar. It shall change the necessary artificial burden we have given
ourselves. It shall make us able to produce more without the tension
of fear!
Surely our internal debt burden can be reduced through that
corruption promoting good for nothing so called market forces induced
high interest rate. There are more than enough computer experts in
the transparent market of the USA. Yet only a few days ago when the
interest rate was going to be changed BY The NEW ADMINISTRATION no
one knew whether it would go up or come down. No one could guess by
how much.
Republicans lower interest rates. Democrats increase it. Both do so
for particular purposes. It was increased by half of one per cent.
This was never done through market forces!
Which market forces in Ghana then are supposed to determine the
interest rate? The sale of cocoyam leaves and snails in the
hinterland? We should take a radical care of the worth of the cedi to
the dollar. If we are able to add a radical cut even a surgical
amputation to the level of the interest rate, this country would see
wonders! People are prepared to borrow, produce and market!
Those who want to remain at their desk in the office should watch us
produce! Artificial manipulation of interest rate and the worth of
the cedis through supposed market forces is no capitalism. It is just
a lazy manipulation.

Original_Search_Title: Bekwai Youth Bare Teeth
Headline: Ghana: Bekwai Youth Bare Teeth
Body_Text:
Accra — A meeting of the Bekwai Traditional Council was last Monday disrupted
when a delegation of the Abusuapanin, Nana Kwame Osei Tutu decided to
meet the council on issues bordering on the proposed destoolment of
the Omanhene of Bekwai, Nana Appau Karikari.
A group of concerned citizens had earlier called on the Omanhene to
abdicate the Ayeboafo stool within two weeks.
Hardly had the delegation led by the spokesman, Opanin Akwasi Poku
briefed the council on their mission that the Queenmother, Nana Adwoa
Pramang III, prompted the council to close the meeting to avoid the
delegation.
The delegation which had been prompted into action following concerns
raised by the concerned citizens wanted to know the reaction of the
council to the destoolment charges against the chief.
Nana Appau Karikari is said to be ineligible because he is not a
member of the Oyoko royal stock.
He is also reported to have impregnated an SSS1 graduate of the local
SDA Secondary School causing her to drop out of school. The said
student (name withheld) is presently married to the chief.
Present at the said meeting were the Krontihene, Nana Okofo Asiedu,
Akwamuhene Nana Ntiamoah Amankuo III, Twafohene Nana Kwabena Gu,
Gyasehene Nana Kwabena Asante III and Akyeamehene, Nana Asante.
In an interview with the Chronicle, Twafohene Nana Kwabena Gu said
the Abusuapanin's delegation came at the time the meeting had closed
having exhausted the agenda.
Nana Gu admitted that the Omanhene had duly been served with a letter
calling for his abdication and that neither he nor the council could
react to the said letter because the Omanhene had travelled outside
the country.
Abusuapanin Nana Osei Tutu and some kingmakers have already initiated
moves to prefer destoolment charges against the Omanhene before the
Asantehene.
Manhyia sources have confided in the Chronicle that the Asantehene's
senior linguist, Nana Boakye Yam has summoned the Abusuapanin of
Bekwai, Nana Kwame Osei Tutu and Opanin Akwasi Poku to a meeting to
this effect.

Original_Search_Title: We Must Talk - Emeka Ojukwu
Headline: Nigeria: We Must Talk - Emeka Ojukwu
Body_Text:
Q: Let us start off with your title which has been expanded from Eze
Ndi Igbo to Eze Ndi Igbo Gburugburu. What is its significance, given
the "Republican" nature of the Igbo nation and that leadership among
the Igbos is considered non- monolithic?
A: Let me seize this opportunity to thank you for according me the
honour of addressing the vast readership of your magazine. Your
magazine, TheNEWS, has been involved in the long struggle for
democracy, which we appear to be winning. I remember that TheNEWS was
pushed aside initially, but today I am confident that people who are
interested in Nigerian politics read TheNEWS. I want to wish your
readers a prosperous year. I want to assure them also that, like all
of them, I look very much forward to peace and prosperity. I will
like to add 'equity' in Nigeria also.
Q: Thank you very much for the compliments. Now your expanded
title...
A: You asked me an interesting question actually, particularly when
you added the 'Republican' nature of Igbo society. First, my title
Eze Igbo is different from Eze Ndi Igbo. I am Eze Igbo by virtue of
the general will of our people. There are people who don't understand
the significance of Eze Igbo. There are many people who, sometimes,
betray ignorance of the language even when they try to make some form
of translation. Eze in Igbo is not just a king as you would have Oba
or Emir. Eze is a champion. If you are a good boxer, you become (in
boxing) an Eze. If you are a yam farmer, the same thing-an Eze. If
you take some prestigious titles, you become an Eze of something. I
take pains to explain to people that my own is far more on the
conscience of Ndigbo.
Q: How?
A: If you are really looking for a comparison, you talk about the
College of Cardinals in the Roman Catholic Church. They are the
princes of the church. But they are not really princes, except that
where they are gives them a quasi princely presence. So, you find
that an Eze is without territory or territorial pretensions. I don't
tax anybody. I am just somebody who is just at the pinnacle of the
Igbo conscience. When you get a Bishop and you say "My lord," he is
not a lord, he just happens to be addressed in that way. So, I am Eze
Igbo because the people have been going around in different areas,
saying it. Even when I arrived from exile, I was given the title
Ikemba, I was again given Eze Igbo dike di oramma (the leader of the
Igbo who has found favour with his people). Later then, the Eze Igbo
became more prominent.
Q: That title has been extended with an additional word,
gburugburu....
A: Actually, there is no official extension. People, again of their
own volition, have started saying gburugburu to differentiate my
position in the heart of Ndigbo from the position of the local Eze
Ndigbos that you find in various areas. Gburugburu only indicates the
totality of the Igbo nation. Why that? I am flattered, of course,
when people say so. But when you say Alayeluwa (owner of the world in
Yoruba) to an Oba, he does not own aye (the world). So generally,
these titles that you add is to create an impression. When they say
uku akpolokpolo in Benin and all that, it is to create impression.
The point is that our people have chosen to separate my position by
making sure that this Eze Igbo is gburugburu. That is, 'to
everybody'.
Q: How do we relate that to the Republican situation here?
A: The Igbos are not Republican! They have never been. You don't take
pride in the break-down of society! The essence of society is that
the group has, within it, a hierarchy. People are quite mischievous
when they say we are 'Republican,' to the extent that they deny us
any form of political organisation. When you say the Igbos are
Republican, it is another word for saying that the Igbos are without
order. No society can thrive without order, you know it. Because we
have been forced into reading and speaking in English, our
explanations and positions are usually, at best, approximated.
'Republicanism'? No. Otherwise we would not have in our language, a
word for the Eze phenomenon. It is our language and right through the
ages, it has remained a word for some form of excellence. If you tell
me that our society has a lot of freedom in it, no problem. Tell me
that we will not dobale (prostrate) in our society, yes we don't
dobale. But we do bow to the Eze and to certain people. That,
however, is not evidence of 'Republicanism,' There is a title in Igbo
land which says Okwoto Ekene Eze (he who stands up to greet a king).
You can imagine what that signifies that this person is such a
personality that in a community, he alone could greet a king standing
up. What do the others do then? They bow to the king. So you see, the
concept of 'kingship' is there. All societies produce kings, leaders,
presidents... It is wrong to think that because we are 'Republican,'
that we lack order. No, we have order and we have heirarchy in our
society. I hope I have touched the three major parts of your
question.
Q: There was a renewed crack-down on the Movement for the
Actualisation of the Sovereign State of Biafra (MASSOB) and Ralph
Uwazuruike, its leader. What, in your view, could be responsible for
that?
A: I don't know what was responsible for that. What I can tell you is
that people are getting panicky over nothing. I deplore the
crackdown. I deplore the attack. I abhor the killings attendant upon
the attempt. Why? This is an organisation of young, non-violent
people. The young people have remained non-violent in everything they
have done up till today. The police at no point said that these
people shot at them. For some unknown reasons, they decided to
attack. Why did they attack? Have they invited them to the police
station for questioning and they refused? I think we have to curb the
excesses of everybody in Nigeria that we, the tax payers, pay for and
give arms to. I abhor the rampant situation in Nigeria where, anytime
you get the police on duty, they tell you it is 'accidental
discharge.' Damn it! The time has come to make sure that there are no
accidents, because those people killed are Nigerian citizens that
(the Police) are supposed to protect. The Police would say 'we shot
five, we shot 10.' No. Shooting, even one, is wrong, very wrong. I
will, therefore, pray that the Federal Government reins in its
coercive agents and, certainly, check the level of command that can
unleash death on the citizenry of Nigeria.
Q: What do you want the government to do about the incident?
A: I will like to see an inquiry into the excess at Okigwe. I will
like to see a situation where, if the police were found to be wrong,
compensation is paid. I don't mean money will pay for the deaths, no
amount of money would do that. But I want it done, symbolically, to
tell the whole of Nigeria, one, that the life of every Nigerian is
worth something, two, that we don't pay with our taxes, assassins in
Nigeria.
Q: The police explained that 'MASSOB Police' shot at their men. That
brings up the issue of insecurity and the advent of ethnic militias .
What, in your opinion, could be done to bring about security in the
land?
A: A lot of things in Nigeria have degenerated. The Police, (I am not
saying anything new), have, on various occasions, failed Nigerians.
The police, at various times, have been highhanded, inefficient. But
there is no doubt in my mind that the security of our people is such
an important matter that cannot be abandoned to individual self-help.
Therefore, if police have to ensure our security, it is then
necessary we look again into their recruitment, training, deployment
and service conditions. There is no doubt in my mind that at each
stage of what I have enunciated, our government has failed. I have an
orderly here whose uniform remains at half mast! That is here (he
touches his shin, that front part below the knee). Why should that
be? The police should never look ridiculous. I know, for a fact, that
the police would go for three or four months without salaries. That
is very wrong, because if you don't pay the police, you are inviting
danger. This area should be looked into. It is very clear to most of
us that in cases of accidental discharges, there is something wrong
with the training. This is because properly trained police personnel
should not discharge accidentally. I am talking with the experience
of a senior army officer. If the police were properly trained, but
you have accidental discharge, then the weapon is bad. So, the
question of equipment comes in. Not only that the society of
yesterday is not the society of today, a country like Nigeria that
has gone through a war is not the pre-war Nigeria. There will be a
lot of arms more than before in our society. I look and I see the
police, over the years, they are being forced to a retreat. Very soon
the police may not ever go out of its barracks because it is
dangerous out there and the government hasn't made it less dangerous
for them. Bullet proof vests and things like that should be normal
part of their equipment. I think the communications system between
their policemen on the street is so basic it doesn't exist. There are
riot situations that you know that guns are being freely used, there
is no point sending the police in soft-skinned vehicles, because
again, they would be killed. Above all, let me ask: Does their salary
reflect the danger they are expected to operate within? These are
things we have to do.
Q: What should the commission of inquiries do about the MASSOB
incident?
A: One, to know really whether MASSOB actually shot at the police. It
is so easy to say 'you shot at us, we shot back!' It is also
significant to tell you that up till today, nobody has told you how
many policemen were injured. It is also significant to know that
nobody has told you how much damage their shooting caused. Don't
forget, it is not every time that you hear pam that you start firing.
There are many stages before you actually shoot to kill. Sometimes
police shoot...But we need to know more about what happened. It is
deplorable that a non-violent organisation would be treated with such
violence. The police are meant to take care of us the citizens. I
thank you for asking me this question and I take this opportunity to
point that the most effective police anywhere in the world is the
police that invest themselves in the community they are defending. I
am not accusing anybody. But when you send a Sokoto policeman to
maintain order in Nnewi, you are asking for trouble. There are other
things he can do in Nnewi, but he is not going to invest his life in
Nnewi. And if you are part of the community, you would know before
anything where danger is likely to come from. You would be more
efficient. The policeman in Nnewi, at least, should be able to speak
Igbo to be fully useful. When you get an Igbo policeman operating in
Okitipupa for example, well, he doesn't know what is going on around
him. Even when people are gathering and people are talking about it
in Okitipupa market, he doesn't know because he doesn't understand
the language. The only thing he can do actually is, 'show me your
particulars' and at the very first sound of danger, he runs. 'Save
your life and not save the polity.'
Q: What should the government do?
A: I will urge the government not to dismiss off- handedly, the
various suggestions to create local police force. What government
should look into is that possibility of regulating it and presenting
it in such a way that it does not threaten the existence of the
Federation. Let us look back. The Scotland Yard is actually the
police for the whole of Great Britain. Every other person you see in
uniform is local police. There are other examples. I know there must
be fears in other areas. You can do a lot of studies to find that
point where you draw the line. And we should know that the most
effective police is that police that can mount a patrol, either on
foot, or bicycle, but within contact with the people of the area he
is taking care of.
Q: What is your reaction to what critics have dubbed the 'rabble
rousing' nature of Governor Orji Uzor Kalu of Abia State?
A: We have a tendency in Nigeria to call people names. Who would dare
say that Governor Orji Uzor Kalu is rabble-rousing? Who are the
rabble that he has been rousing? Governor Kalu is the most effective
governor that we have in the East today. Certainly when he speaks on
Igbo matters, Gov. Kalu speaks the heart of the people he is
governing. And you cannot dismiss him by just giving him a name,
'rabble rousing.' Now we are moving further into democratic practice.
Among so many other things, we must be tolerant of people who have
different opinions from what we have. It is not democratic to dismiss
everybody else's opinion. There are aspects of governance that Gov.
Kalu keeps pointing at. In a certain way, he doesn't agree with the
president who, incidentally, is my very good friend. We trained
together, we served together and I see him regularly. But he has not
become a God yet! If he does not play the game properly, the duty of
his best friend is to criticize him. So, to the question (I am sorry
that I sort of pointed at a person) as an Igboman, I don't see
Governor Kalu as a rabble-rouser. In fact, I applaud so many things
that he has been saying. Newspapers and magazines, (like yours) would
be better advised to take each point he raised and carry out
research. That is the way to categorise things. At the end of the
day, you may find out that he is the most truthful governor, not
rabble-rousing. And finally, what 'rabble' has he roused? I hope you
are not saying that the Igbo public are rabbles? You can't say that
in front of me and go free!
Q: I asked that question because of what people have been saying.
This is because journalists act as the devil's advocates on many
occasions...
A: Yes, yes, I understand ....
Q: With what Governor Kalu is saying, that Igbo leaders like you,
Chief Ojukwu....
A: I don't like being called 'Chief,' I am 'dim' (d,i,m), because
chief rhymes so much with 'thief' that I don't want anybody mistaking
me for a 'thief.'
Q: I am sorry....
A: That's all right.
Q: The question is, with Governor Kalu's outspokenness of late,
observers reason that Igbo leaders like you, Dr. Alex Ekwueme, Prof.
Benjamin Nwabueze and others might have ordained him (Kalu) as Igbo
leader...
A: I don't know about that. What I know is that he stood election in
Abia state and won. So, Abia State 'ordained' him governor of Abia.
He is doing his work. At the end, we will see how far he has
succeeded and to what extent he has failed. The governor has not been
ordained anything beyond governor to my knowledge. Let me take this
question of Igbo leadership. Actually, why are people so anxious to
impose a leadership on our people? Who is the Yoruba leader? Who is
Hausa leader? Efik? Ibibio? Edo? Look at them, who are their leaders?
Why must only the Igbo produce, at this stage, a universally
acclaimed leader? Why, why, why? I think that is mischief making! It
is either you are running away from a leader or insisting on this
leadership so as to have a target! Igbo leader? Don't worry. At any
stage in Nigeria's interraction within itself, there will always be
an Igbo leader, that I can promise you. We can decide to hide our
leader. Why shouldn't we? What we have seen in Nigeria does not
encourage us to put forward our leader. I think the media should
think again. We are no different from the other ethnic groups in
Nigeria. And the day you find leaders and point them out for all the
others, and the Igbos are found wanting in that, then you have a
point to make about Igbo leadership. I repeat, who is the Yoruba
leader today?
Q: You once called some Igbo leaders 'hirelings and job seekers.' Do
you want to modify that statement given the protest it attracted? Who
are these hirelings?
A: I haven't seen any protest. First, everybody knows me well in
Igboland. I said that the so-called Igbo summit was something put
together by a clique. And it was that clique that I called 'job
seekers and hirelings.' I am quite prepared (should those individuals
raise their voices) to tell them when they were hired. I am prepared
to tell them what jobs they sought. I can go ahead to tell them what
jobs some of them got. The authenticity of what I am saying has not
been lost to any Igboman. I am prepared to tell them that as far as
the Igbo leadership is concerned, I will only be part of an
organisation for Ndi Igbo where the leadership (whoever is part of
the leadership) would be deemed to have resigned (if he is in the
executive or appointed by the Federal Government). Because really,
the struggle of trying to get the Ndi Igbo into the centre of Nigeria
is raging. You have to decide where you are to be able to lead the
struggle. I don't want to be misunderstood. That struggle is not to
tear Nigeria apart. No. In fact, it will make Nigeria very very
strong. That is why it is a cardinal point (and it should remain
such) of our struggle that somehow we mount sufficient pressure to
get the government of Nigeria to accept a national conference. To set
Nigeria right, we have to understand the things of Nigerianism. We
are adults. Let nobody spoonfeed us. There is nothing more arrogant
than anyone saying 'I know the problems, I can solve them.' Absolute
nonsense! You can't. Let us, around the table, discuss these problems
and let nobody say 'these are no-go areas.' Every citizen of Nigeria
is a sovereign of Nigeria. It is the citizenry that makes up the
sovereignty of Nigeria, not individuals. We are the ones to decide
our future. Nigerians have never yet written a constitution.
Q: How can we get a proper constitution because the one in use now is
heavily criticised.
A: I am one of the few, perhaps, that believe that until Nigerians
have a document fully accented to by the Nigerian peoples, that
begins with the wonderful sentence: 'We the people of Nigeria
agree....' Other countries have it. That is the basis of the
documents that bind those nations together. Not that the British
handed us an independent constitution. Not that the military wrote
this 'nineteen what ever it is.' No. Every creation of state is
illegal and remains illegal. This is because it was not accented to
by the sovereigns that agreed to the Nigeria union after the British.
Even that constitution they gave to us told us how to create states.
We created the Mid West (which was the only legal exercise). The
others have been puniture. We cannot do much in Nigeria if we build
on illegality. It is one of the reasons I always make a clean line,
urging us to consider a national conference and not a sovereign
national conference.
Q: Why don't you like a 'sovereign' national conference?
A: I go away from that because the disruption that it will bring will
be beyond control. There can be no two sovereigns in a state. If it
is sovereign national, it is that conference that assumes sovereighty
while it is on and it may result in anarchy (in fact I would prefer
certain oppression to anarchy and this is my personal opinion. Others
might have theirs). So, the sovereign national might frighten those
in various offices too. Many would say 'frighten them' is not
important, it is just practical, if you want to get on, you must
accept that they are there for the time being. I believe, however,
that we should sit round the table. All the nations of Nigeria must
sit round that table. I believe then that the nations should use
their own resources to negotiate their own positions within the
federation. It is for that, that I support fully people of the
South-South in their belief that they must have resource control.
What they are saying is that Nigeria does not exist today as such.
But for them to be part of Nigeria, they must control their
resources. There is nothing wrong with that, let us debate it. We
might decide also that the rest of us, in order to protect you, would
require a certain percentage of whatever it is you have. You can't
have it both ways. So, there is always give and take in negotiations.
We should start from somewhere. And you cannot say we need to control
this and at the same time be part of telling me to leave my resources
as national reserve. No. There are many things you can discuss round
the table.
Q: The South-South is very much concerned about resource control,
among other issues. What does the Igbo nation want?
A: Let me take this opportunity to enlighten some people that as long
as the pain of the war remains, we Ndigbo will continue to question
our participation in this organisation that causes us so much pain. I
would think that: One, the start point of wisdom for Nigeria is total
reconciliation. And you can tell me that it is 'costly and it doesn't
matter!' Wealth is made for people not people for wealth. We can sit
round and decide. This problem we keep having in Nigeria is too much.
We have wasted so many years trying to patch so many things. Let us
reconcile. And when I talk of total reconciliation, I am not just
saying Igbos and the rest of Nigeria. No, it should be for all of us.
The North claims that they are marginalised . Ok, there is nothing
wrong with the claim...
Q: Can that argument be sustained?
A: When we sit round the table, we will evaluate that claim. That is
the way things are done. What is everybody afraid of? People of the
West are my brothers and sisters and I believe that they look upon me
as their brother. We talk about how to get ourselves moving together.
It is significant that it is the big government that is at fault.
Okafor can Marry Binta and they will live very well in their homes.
So there is nothing really in itself bad about Okafor marrying a
Binta. Femi will marry Ngozi and they will live well. It is when you
are trying to be Lord Almighty over so much that the problems come.
Let us put away our pride. Pride of governing a people you have never
conquered anyway. It is ridiculous. It is bad enough to be conquered,
but to govern me without first conquering me, I think there is
something wrong with that.
Q: There was that slogan, 'no victor no vanquished....'
A: Let nobody point at the war as a conquest. Because for years, for
centuries and, perhaps millenniums, the debate will continue raging
who won and who lost. I went to war to save the Igbo nation. Whenever
I go to Lagos and Kano, I see the fruit of my victory, not the fruit
of my defeat. That is the truth. The other truth is that even at the
point of victory, the Nigerian policy was 'no victor no vanquished.'
My own position is let us stop lying to ourselves. If there were no
victor no vanquished, there should be no bar to total reconciliation.
Finally, I want to point out that very many people are still
suffering as a result of dismissals wrongly given as a result of the
war. So many people are suffering as a result of enforced penury at
the end of the war. So many people are suffering as a result of
wounds inflicted during the war. The catalogue is long and that is
what we should be discussing. I believe that with open minds, we
should be able to organise a Nigeria that can accommodate all of us.
Q: Apart from the pains of the war, what are other areas of Igbo
marginalisation?
A: There is no way you can reassure me about the safety of Nigeria if
you deliberately bar me from the security of Nigeria. It is as simple
as that. You can also extend this to certain areas of governance.
When I feel that for some unknown reasons, the likes of myself are
not ever allowed to take part, this is marginalisation.
Marginalisation comes up when you have a set of rules, either setting
out deliberately to favour somebody and disfavour me, or set up a
universal application and whenever it affects me, different reasons
are adduced. What I am trying to say about marginalisation is when
you bring me as a citizen under different rules because of where I
come from. There is marginalisation when you say other states should
adopt Sharia as the religion or system of law in that area where,
clearly because we are a nation, people from other areas must be
living. And in a situation where our constitution is quite clear that
you cannot adopt a religion as a state religion. There are so many
instances of marginalisation. And my own concept of justice and
fairness is also that whatever I produce as evidence of
marginalisation, I agree that other people would also have their own
catalogue of marginalisation. The only way to resolve it is to sit
round the table. What are we afraid of? Come with your baggage of
marginalisation . Put it on that conference table. It doesn't matter
if it is three years, four or five. We cannot continue running a
colonial left-over and call it Nigeria. Nigerians must not accept
this pattern for their future. Finally, I think I am in a better
position than most to say what I am saying. I have been the one who
talked about the handshake across the Niger. I have been the one
telling you that in Geography class at school, I learnt that the
opposite of north is south, that the opposite of the north has never
been east and west. I have so much personal experience of those
areas.
Q: Handshake across the Niger notwithstanding, there was a report
that you would be General Ibrahim Babangida's running mate. What is
your reaction?
A: I don't work under my junior. At school, educationally,
administratively ( I was an administrative officer of some repute),
militarily , experience- wise, I can even say (I don't know what
anybody might have outside), but money -wise too, you know where I
come from! So generally, when I say things, I would implore Nigerians
to look deeper and think again. I am not flattered by Nigerians'
coming 40 years after and saying 'Ah Ojukwu was right anyway.' That
is not it, if only people would think twice, leave the immediate and
think beyond. Realising that I was right 40 years ago would mean 40
years wasted. There are many things that I can put to Nigerians. For
some reasons, they thought I was a rebel some years back, they should
realise that I have been proved right. And, therefore, they should
listen.
Q: The Enugu Port-Harcourt expressway was awarded to a relatively
unknown contractor, instead of Julius Berger or Strabag. Is this
another form of not taking the South East seriously?
A: I have to confess that it is this type of question that I dread. I
would like Igbo contractors. I would like Nigerian contractors. I am
a bit reticent because I don't want to pronounce 'give me a white man
instead of black man.' I find it difficult. I know why it is so. All
we are saying is that we want an internationally accepted good road
from Enugu to Port Harcourt. It is necessary. Get us the best
contractors. There is the other side of course that you can say that
Julius Berger is Nigerian. I don't want anybody to say I am a
hypocrite on this matter. I, myself, went to the late General Sani
Abacha and said 'give us our share of Julius Berger as well.' I said
so. You understand where I am coming from. I will like as many
Nigerians in this, because this way we grow. But there are certain
things we cannot show capacity for just yet. I am of the opinion that
if Nigeria wanted to establish a nuclear energy plant, I will support
Nigeria using an expatriate firm, a firm from countries that have the
renown and experience for producing such. We have gone in circles. I
would, in this particular matter, like a competent firm ( I will
hesitate to pronounce for one company or the other, there are many
competent firms). Let us use the best we can in Nigeria.
Q: Another area of marginalisation, according to the Igbo, is
appointments, especially in the armed forces. How would you assess
President Olusegun Obasanjo's handling of this situation given what
critics said about the Igbos losing their 'military' opportunities
during Biafra and their refusal to join the armed forces?
A: There are certain lies that when they are told so often, they
become truth! And this is one of them. There has never yet been a
recruitment drive that the Igbos have been lacking in producing
candidates. None. That cannot justify what I can say is a deliberate
attempt not to accept Igbos. This is what I look at primarily when I
talk about total reconciliation. As a human being yes, anybody that
went through those years, 1966-1970, which way you look at it, there
must be certain fears. My point is that the fears have lasted too
long in this area and that if we must move together as an entity,
then the fears should be taken away and we should all look upon
ourselves as one. For every fear that you have for Oyen- Igbo, I can
count six reasons I, as Oyen-Igbo, should have fears about you, the
group that fought against us during the war, raped our women after
the war, condemned us to penury by making all our wealth, for which
we are known, Â£20 each! I should be more afraid for our houses that
are standing under different ownerships for which we were not
compensated. Oh, the catalogue is long! But in 2001, for something
that ended 1970, I am anxious to see a way to clear these things. Let
me ask Nigerians. What does it cost to pay for certain buildings?
Pay! Sit down, think about it. 'Oh, well we cannot be changing back,
forth, this that or whatever it is', many would say. But let's do
something equitable. It is part of our expenses for the greatest
present we can look for: peace in Nigeria. I keep telling people for
as long as these things stand ...
Q: What do you think should be done about the Â£20 issue you raised?
A: Everyone that got Â£20 knew what he had in the bank. What we should
now say, is 'look, it's over, bring your documents. Afterall this is
your contract with your bank, not with the Federal Government. Go and
claim your money from the bank.' What is wrong with that? What is
wrong with that other than the so-called 'victory'. No, if Nigeria
must march forward, these are things we must rectify.
Q: Are you ready to pursue the abandoned property and the Â£20 issue
further?
A: Oh yes and even abandoned posts. Can you ever say that the Igbos
were not threatened outside Igboland? When they are threatened and
everybody knows that they were threatened, should they sit down and
wait for their heads to be cut off? There are ways to do these
things. Have sympathy for your brother so that your brother can
remain your brother. That is all I am saying.
Q: There is the issue of federal industrial presence in the Igbo
nation. The Federal Coal Corporation was established in 1916. There
was an agreement between the National Council of Nigerian Citizens,
NCNC and Northern People 's Congress, NPC, about the establishment of
Kainji Dam in the North and a steel company in Onitsha (which was
never done).
A: My reaction is that we can solve all these problems on the table.
We can also, at that table, make appointments reflect more of
national needs. These are evidence for anybody to see for the need
for a national corporation.
Q: There seems to be an irony in the fact that the Igbos seem to be
down there, despite their political gains. Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe was
president; Nwafor Orizu, Senate president; Alex Ekwueme, Vice
President... Or is it that the Igbo leaders are actually guilty of
politics of expediency?
A: Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe was, at independence, cheated. There is no
doubt in my mind that the leader of Nigeria, before independence, was
Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe. With all due respect, there were Chief Obafemi
Awolowo, the Sardauna, (Sir Ahmadu Bello), but the uncontested leader
was Nnamdi Azikiwe. At independence, what happened actually was that
the whole English habit/solution to many problems was applied to Zik.
He was 'kicked upstairs'. He was given the presidency without powers.
His irrelevance to Nigeria's future was symbolised by his being first
appointed the representative of Her Majesty the Queen. Nwafor Orizu,
president of the senate, reflected the power configuration of the
time when we had three regions. Then Alex Akwueme... You are the ones
now cheating, not Nigeria! Forgive me for saying that, because you
know damn well that no vice president has power. You quietly moved
away from power and now first talking about symbolism, ceremony. This
is because one of the problems Alex Ekwueme has actually today is
that he does not feature in the power structure of Nigeria. Do you
know that the whole war, even though the Igbo fought the war, was
essentially for Yoruba benefit?
Q: How? Please explain.
A: The war which we fought was because of a coup that was designed to
give the premiership of Nigeria to Chief Awolowo. When it failed, I
found myself obliged to oppose Gowon and Murtala Mohammed on the
basis that without Aguiyi Ironsi, Ogundipe should be anointed. He
fled. And I changed and said that in the absence of Ogundipe, it
should be Adeyinka Adebayo. That was my break with Nigeria. It wasn't
for Ojukwu to go to Lagos. No. There are other things. I think a way
to answer you is to say that over the years Ndigbo have appeared to
be too honest for Nigeria.
Q: Still on Igbo marginalisation and Igbo leadership. There is this
criticism that part of the Igbo problem is that their leaders cannot
withstand sustained heat for their followers. Critics would readily
cite Uwazuruike who escaped on a motorcycle and that you travelled to
Cote d' Ivoire, leaving Biafrans behind....
A: Tell me what Yoruba leader has committed suicide. Tell me any!
When even a Yoruba leader would fly into exile at pure suspicion.
What are they talking about? Uwazuruike was able to deceive the
powers of oppression and got away on a motorcycle. What was the
essence of the fame of the scarlet pimpernel? How many times did he
go to the French to commit suicide? Today I am talking to you. And
you have seen (you have to accept ) that I have been vindicated. I
don't know what it would have been if I had been dead. You wouldn't
have this opportunity. Opposition of Nigeria to Ndigbo is that we
refuse to die! We have the skills. Has anybody in Nigeria shown who
(you know sometimes it is flattering when these things are said,
because you may think we are gods) you will compare. My good friend
Wole (Soyinka). Who threatened him into exile? He is still my friend
and I respect him a great deal. Who? Let's face it. We are not
talking about toy guns now, but real guns. We are not talking about
symbolic take-over of a radio station. No, we are talking about real
war. Now come of it! I even believe that one of the problems we had
during the war was the fact that Ikene was so directly under Gowon's
rule and that Ibadan was under his military. But man-to-man, I still
honour very much Pa Obafemi Awolowo, because I know what I discussed
with him...
Q: What did you discuss?
A: Suffice it to say that I knew that had things been different, he
would have gone differently.
Q: What is your view about the Federal Government's dragging of the
state governments to court over resource control?
A: Whose court by the way?
Q: Federal, I mean supreme court.
A: O tan (that's all).
Q: The question concerns the disappointment of Nigerians with Chief
Bola Ige who used to have a pro-true federalism persona. Now he has
dragged the constituent units to court over a fundamental federal
issue.
A: I don't like judging people. Because this is the type of question,
really, that you should ask Bola. Very often, men find themselves in
contradictory conditions. And this could be one of those occasions.
For one reason or the other, the Federal Government thinks that the
courts should decide on a matter of constitution. But I would have
thought that this should have come the other way round, that is the
state government seeking clarifications, because the government has
it within its right to do one thing or the other. Why do we choose
the long way? If we are around the table, it was there their own
percentage was lifted from one per cent to 13. One sitting! If we go
through a national conference, there is nothing stopping them. They
might end up with 50 or 60 per cent. The position of Chief Bola Ige,
I don't know. It is, perhaps, because of this that I am not in any
position, because I like freedom. I like to act totally as I feel
inside me.
Q: You are a member of the All People's Party, APP. People wonder why
you have not also joined the exodus to the People's Democratic Party,
PDP...?
A: The reason I am not joining the PDP is the reason I did not join
in the first place. What have they done? Because they've won, does
that make them a better party? No, it doesn't actually, particularly
in the Nigerian context. I am a member of the APP and, in the
Nigerian context, I am proud to remain a member. I am totally
satisfied with APP because I have been brought up in a school that
says that parties should distinguish only by ideology. Ideologically,
the APP, PDP and the Alliance for Democracy, AD, have nothing much
separating them. If that is so, why then the difference in the first
place? You would ask me. I will tell you. I prefer the people I am
working with in APP to the people I have to work with if I were in
PDP.
Q: There was this innuendo that you did not join the PDP because you
hate President Olusegun Obasanjo?
A: I call him Omoba(Prince). He is my good friend. I can work with
him anywhere. In freedom and captivity, I can work with him. Today,
if I pick up my phone, we will talk. I phone him always. There is no
time I go to Abuja that I don't have a meal with him. He is my
friend.
Q: What are the chances of APP in the 2003 presidential election?
A: You should ask the Secretary General that question. There are so
many things we don't know. First, I think you should not register
parties and therefore parties should spring up. I believe that
passionately, not because we happen to have slidden to the little gap
of APP, PDP that you should block others who want parties. Two, I
believe if we are really a democratic country, that independent
candidature should be allowed. This is because that is the only one
that blows the whistle on conspiracies. I will like to see minority
interest reflect more on the configuration of political parties. I
will like somebody to form a party of those who like eating chicken
on Sunday! It is part of our human life.
Q: You were once quoted as saying you would play a 'significant role'
in 2003. If APP wants you to play the 'significant' role of being its
presidential flagbearer, would you accept?
A: It is easy to answer it by just cliches. But I believe that it is
an important one. I remember watching the Cable News Network (CNN)
and listening to Ehud Barak, discountenancing the possibility of
cooperation and collaboration with Ariel Sharon. Today, we wake up
with a 'Hurray' that he is not only going to collaborate with Sharon
but that his group would hold defence and foreign affairs. So, don't
let us be childish. The facts are these: I am 67. That, to me, means
retirement, with more need to train younger ones than to maintain the
leadership position. Secondly, I feel that my name, having come up as
the Head of State of Biafra, I have to be very careful and be
absolutely certain that whatever post I go into should not look like
opportunism. One other thing that worries me is that I have a sense
of duty. Honestly, I have given my life fighting the cause of Ndigbo
here. Only because I feel they are oppressed. If they insist and wish
me to go, but particularly if the people of Nigeria want me to, God
knows, if the circumstances are right, one might consider. But I
haven't seen the possibility yet. But my preference, today is to help
bring up a breed of Nigerians who believe that politics is a service,
not recompense. A people who seek their success in the hearts of
their people not in the size of their own pocket. I will like to see
a new group which knows when to come in and knows when to get out. I
will like to see a people living in honesty with one another. That is
my choice.
Q: What is your view about the purported military pact between the
United States and Nigeria?
A: Well I don't know, because I have not seen the details. I don't
know if it is true. And in any case, in the context of how it applies
is what one worries about. There is no doubt in my mind that our
military, as it is today, is not certainly what we will wish for. The
propensity for coup making is too much. Everybody in the barracks
wakes up thinking 'how do I?' And the most famous sentence in the
Army today is 'fellow countrymen and women'. I think, therefore,
something drastic has to be done. The same way I have talked about
the police, I have to talk about the Army. They should be retrained.
If it is a training pact, there is nothing wrong in that. But if the
idea is to put an umbrella over exploiters of the people, oppressors
of the people, give them longer tenure of office, I would oppose it.
It would be very, very wrong. Nigerians should be given the
opportunity to choose their own leadership.
Q: There are agitations that President Obasanjo should go for a
second term. What is your view?
A: President Olusegun Obasanjo, in my mind, is a human being. He has
every right to want it. There is nothing wrong with it, he has every
right. We should learn not to oppose people like this. His work will
tell, if at the end of the first term, he is successful, why
shouldn't he? Constitutionally he can. We have refused to to put
rotation into our constitution and, therefore, have not designed a
country where a rotating presidency will be existing. Now, all the
arguments against running is based on rotation. This country has not
been designed for that. If you want to constitute a national
conference, I agree that it should be rotatory. Then you can then use
those arguments. Let Obasanjo do, as a citizen of Nigeria, what the
constitution permits him to do. Let him not hinder anybody else so
permitted to do the same thing.
Q: Chief Ojo Maduekwe, Minister of Transport, reportedly called those
calling for Igbo presidency 'idiots'. What is your reaction to that?
A: I called for Igbo presidency in 2003. If I am an idiot in his
view, he has every right to say so. I have nothing against him if he
said so. Am I an idiot? It sounds odd only because I haven't told you
what I think of him. Do you understand? We shouldn't bother about the
use of words. I don't really believe that he believes we are idiots
anyway. It was a manner of speech, a bit loose, sometimes excessive.
We have our own quirks too. Ojo that I know is not somebody who can
be written off easily. You might not agree with everything he does (I
don't agree with everything he does). But whenever we think of the
Igbos as a nation, I will want him around and will certainly want him
on my side. I cannot just judge him on one statement. If he said so,
I wouldn't say so. If he said so, he has every right.
Q: If the marginalisation of the Igbos continues, will there be a
recurrence of Biafra? In other words, would you carry arms?
A: If we woke up and there has been a landslide that wipes out the
whole of the north and a flood that wipes out the whole of the west,
will the Igbos remain? If, if, is what everybody is saying. It is
really so hypothetical that it is difficult to give a good answer.
But I put it slightly different for you. If your intention is to
continue marginalising the Ndigbo, you would be foolish to expect
Ndigbo to stay. That's all.

Original_Search_Title: Hop On The Jazz Train
Headline: South Africa: Hop On The Jazz Train
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — It's a weekend of celebration as local jazz musicians bring out four
new discs. In the past week, it has been difficult to talk to anyone
in the music industry without mentioning the name Moses Taiwa
Molelekwa. His tragic death has cast a haunting shadow on the scene.
This weekend four artists and two groups feature in two concerts that
will no doubt lift the gloom. The range of talent on offer is broad "
from the mesmerising, eclectic sound of Zimbabwean Oliver Mtukudzi to
the lilting timbre of Gloria Bosman. It'll be a fitting, albeit
unofficial, tribute to the late pianist.
Mtukudzi, the legendary native from our beleaguered neighbour, has
practically made South Africa his second home, or rather one of many
as his stature in world music grows with every album. He flies in
from the Perth International Arts Festival to present material from
his latest album, Bvuma (tolerance).
For this appearance he will be joined by a long-time collaborator, as
well as a more recent musical acquaintance. Steve Dyer was musical
director of the ensemble Mahube, of which Mtukudzi was head honcho,
and has been Mtukudzi's producer for five albums over the past four
years. He has found time to put together a solo album, Down South in
Africa, and will undoubtedly take pleasure in sharing the stage with
a man he has been friends with for a decade.
Around the middle of last year, Mtukudzi happened upon a recording of
Ringo Madlingozi's Into Yam from his latest offering Buyisa. The
track grabbed Mtukudzi so much he composed a Shona translation for
himself. The recording, now a firm favourite at Kaya Fm, is said to
be the first that combines Shona and a Nguni language, in this case
Xhosa. The reception has been mind-blowing.
On a recent tour of Zimbabwe by Mtukudzi and Madlingozi, crowds of
10?000 " in Harare and Bulawayo " formed mass choirs to sing along.
The people in the capital accompanied the lagging Shona section while
Madlingozi led in Xhosa.
In the Ndebele-dominated Bulawayo, Madlingozi found strong
accompaniment in Xhosa while Mtukudzi followed with his enchanting
tenor. This weekend, South Africans get their turn to savour the
magic for the first time live on stage.
In a second and equally thrilling concert the country's progressive
jazz label, Sheer Sound, unveils new offerings from an established
exponent and two new finds.
Gloria Bosman has practically had everything fall into place since
the release of her debut album, Tranquillity. She scooped the South
African Music Award for best new artist. She then crossed the great
divide to collaborate with kwaito artist S'bu in the latter's Abantu
Abazwani.
She's collaborated with serious music divas Sibongile Khumalo and
Sylvia Mdunyelwa and has been joined on stage by Bongo Maffin's
Thandswa Mazwai and the celebrated Busi Mhlongo.
Bosman's intended tour with Mhlongo " a phenomenal idea " is unlikely
to happen since it was the brainchild of musical director Molelekwa
and his late wife, Florence Mthoba.
For her new album, The Many Faces of Gloria Bosman, she was supposed
to combine covers with original material. "I decided against using
the tried and tested to support the originals because I felt the
songs could stand on their own," she says of the album, which she
produced, keeping her hand in all 10 tracks.
She describes the process of taking creative control of her work as
gratifying and alludes to being a control freak. "If it turns out to
be a mess, I want to have myself to blame," she says.
Her last album exudes remarkable longevity, with roughly six of the
nine tracks enjoying airplay. Her attempt at repeating her debut feat
begins on Saturday, in the company of the group Voices, who she knows
well, and Heavy Spirits, Sheer Sound's latest find.
Voices are the low-key version of the Sheer All Stars, only these
stars have not recorded solo projects. For their debut, Quintet
Legacy, they bring together experience spanning decades and a heavy
academic influence.
Acclaimed drummer Lulu Gontsana leads a quintet of respected session
musicians. University of Natal-educated pianist, Andile Yenana, notes
how Gontsana "holds us together. He is our link to what is happening
in the country jazz-wise."
Bass guitarist Herbie Tsoaeli's name has been in a number of Sheer's
projects, most notably with that of McCoy Mrubata. Together with
Yenana, they are the less-mentioned reasons for the success of
Mahube. On saxophone, the mean five " as Bosman calls them " have
Sydney Mnisi, a product of Pretoria Technikon who, since 1998, has
frequently performed in Holland. To round off, there is Marcus Wyatt
on trumpet and flugelhorn. Wyatt has extended his talents to arrange
for and produce Heavy Spirits, a new group that's also featured on
the bill.
Heavy Spirits hail from Atteridgeville in Pretoria and have been
together for just more than a year. They got their break at a
performance in Silverton. "We have a loose approach to our
composition and like to experiment" says tenor saxophonist Paul
Vranas. Their approach has yielded a fairly standard bass guitar and
saxophone blues set.
On their first major performance in Johannesburg, the group expects
"to put on a good show, but more importantly, to make people give the
album more than one listen".
The company on stage is likely to give them butterflies in the
stomach. But the irony of how, as they hit the big time, another
artist has just bowed out will not be lost to them.
Oliver Mtukudzi and others are performing on February 23 at Mega
Music Warehouse. For more info contact Sheer Sound on Tel: (011)
444?1818. Gloria Bosman is live in concert on February 24 at the
Civic Theatre from 7.30pm.
Tickets available at Ticketweb, Tel: 082?1400?500

Original_Search_Title: Join Fight Against Bush Burning - Christian Leaders Told
Headline: Ghana: Join Fight Against Bush Burning - Christian Leaders Told
Body_Text:
Accra — Men of God throughout the country have been admonished to preach
against the rampant menace of bush burning and its attendant havoc
instead of preparing the minds of their congregation towards greener
pastures somewhere beyond the blues.
About 95% of the Volta Region has been destroyed by bush fires in
recent months making it the first on the National Bush Fire Map. The
Public Relations Officer of the Regional Fire Service, Division
Officer Joy Agbleze reminded Christians and other church-goers of the
sinful nature of the destruction of the vegetation God has commanded
them to keep and to replenish.
At Tokokoe and Adaklu Kpodzi in the Ho District, six acres of cassava
farm and another six acres of oil palm plantation of 1,200 palm trees
were consumed by bush fire on Valentine Day while a farmer, Anas
Tsigbey of Tanyigbe lost 600 tubers of yam on the same day.
D.O. II Agbleze commended Togbe Noagbesenu of Awate in the Kpandu
District and the chiefs of Lolobi, Baiku, Ayoma and Lukpe in the
Hohoe and Jasikan districts for their strict observance of the
anti-bush fire laws.
He made a suggestion to the government to empower the fire service to
prosecute fire offenders at the law courts since the police appeared
not to attach any importance to fire cases.
Agbleze intends to translate series of poetry he has written on bush
fires into the various Ghanaian languages in order to whip up
awareness of the danger inherent in bush burning.

Original_Search_Title: Getting Out Of Hands
Headline: Nigeria: Getting Out Of Hands
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigerians struggle to survive as the prices of commodities rise
beyond the reach of the average man. The spiralling cost of luxury
and basic goods have remained up, even after two months into the new
year. The price increases started gradually in November last year
when a bag of 100 kg yellow garri which hitherto sold for N1,500 went
up to N2,000. Refilling a 12.5 kg gas cylinder also jumped from N600
to 1,000. At the time, the rising cost was attributed to the approach
of the yuletide season, with buyers hoping that the situation would
return to normal. But that is not to be. Instead, there has been a
consistent increase in the prices of almost every commodity.
The prices of basic food items like eggs, bread, garri, yam and
beans, among others, have also gone up. A survey of prominent markets
and supermarkets in the Lagos metropolis shows that the price
increase cuts across almost every item. "A big crate of 30 eggs which
was N240 last year, sells for N270 now while a crate of the
medium-sized eggs which used to cost N220 is now N250," an egg
seller, Mrs. C. Dada, said. Bread, a favourite for breakfasts is
gradually disappearing from the menu of most people because of its
high cost which bakers attributed to the hike in the prices of baking
ingredients. At UAC Foods, a chief baker explained that the basic
materials such as flour, butter, sugar and yeast have all attracted
higher prices. " A 50 kg bag of Golden Penny flour which previously
sold for N1,900 is now N2,300; 50kg sugar has gone up from N2,100 to
N2,600 and a tin of 10-litre butter of N1,700 now sells for N2,000"
he said. This has resulted in about 20 per cent increase in the price
of bread. A big loaf of bread of brands such as Splendid, UAC, UTC
and Leventis that previously sold for N80 now sells for between N100
and N120.
"A 100kg of red garri which went from N1,700 mid-last year to N2,000
in December is now N2,500" explained Tony Nwocha, a big time grain
trader. This translates to about N450 for a tin of red garri and N400
for the same quantity of white garri.
Garri Ijebu of the same quantity now sells for N2,800 instead of the
former N2,000 while 100kg of drum beans which was N4,000 now attracts
additional N4,600. Oloyin beans of the same quantity has risen from
N3,400 to N3,800.
At the Mile 12 market, Ketu, Lagos, popular for its reasonable
pricing of foodstuff, Consumer Watch noted that the smallest tuber of
yam sells for N60. The same size of yam was bought for N30 last year.
A survey of major supermarkets like Masco Super Market, Ikeja; The
Source Supermarket, Surulere, Leventis and UAC stores, revealed that
one of the areas most hit is food. The managing director of "The
Source Supermarket," Mr. Chike Durujo, noted that virtually all
canned baby foods have attracted price increases. For instance; 300g
Frisocream milk cereal is N300 as against N285; 450g Frisolac infant
powder milk is N320 as against the November price of N295, while SMA
Gold jumped from N700 to N765. The same fate befell Similac and other
baby foods.
Drugs and other pharmaceuticals are also not left out. Three months
ago, a sachet of Chemiron blood tonic capsule sold for N40, now it
sells for N60. Female toiletries and cosmetics did not escape the
hike. For instance, Clear Essence body lotion sells for N800 as
against N500.
At the Balogun market, Lagos and Tejuosho market Yaba, there was a
major price increase in the cost of fabrics and fashion accessories
from the Far East and U.S. Cod and Coupion laces that sold for N5,000
two months ago, now go for about N9,000. George wrappers, mostly
imported from India, and hair accessories, most of which come from
Taiwan and China also attracted additional prices. In all the
markets, buyers sighed and cursed as they tried in vain to buy at old
prices.
Ready-made dresses imported from the United Arab Emirate are almost
out of the reach of the average working Nigerian. The dresses which
previously cost N3,000 for trouser and skirt suits; N1,000 for
blouses and about N2,000 for gowns have almost doubled in prices.
With the emergence of these wears in Nigerian markets about five
years ago, a lot of ladies who wanted 'Ready Made' dresses but could
not afford the ones imported from US and European markets heaved a
sigh of relief. However, with this increase in prices, it is like
