going back to square one.
Right now, it costs an average of N2,220 to refill a 12.5kg gas
cylinder. Early last year, it went for N500 but gradually went up to
about N1,300 in December.
Newspaper houses and printing companies are also not left out as the
cost of newsprint and other printing materials have been affected. A
ton of newsprint which sold for N65,000 early last year, went up to
N140,000. Naturally, the price has translated to increases in the
cover prices of newspapers and magazines.
Diverse reasons have been adduced for the steady increase in prices.
While Garri sellers attribute it to the heavy rainfall of last year;
beans sellers said they have not harvested their produce. Yam
traders, however, said that the increase in the cost of transporting
their produce to the market is to blame.
Major importers, like the chief executive officer of Wilport Nigeria
Ltd, Mr. Sina Williams, said the price hike was as a result of
'Domino effects.' "With fuel scarcity, lack of infrastructures and
the dwindling value of the naira, how do you expect us to record any
meaningful progress in the economy." Continuing, he noted that "a
situation where you have NITEL and most other establishments relying
on NEPA to function shows that no industry or company will work to
its maximum capacity as NEPA is inefficient."
Lamenting the increase in the price of goods, an industrialist, Paul
Okeke, the CEO of Hollifield Industries, called on the Federal
Government to stop the devaluation of the naira. As he said "the
devaluation is to blame for the price increases. I spend almost
double what I used to spend on importation as the cost of foreign
exchange transactions has gone up tremendously."
He also noted that the poor performance of NEPA has caused
manufacturers to transfer the overhead of running generators to the
price of goods. "We all know how expensive diesel is," adding that
the traders who pay more on transportation will also pass the buck to
the consumer.

Original_Search_Title: Federal Government Secures $23 Million Loan For Root Crops Production
Headline: Nigeria: Federal Government Secures $23 Million Loan For Root Crops Production
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has secured a concessional loan of 23.05
million United States dollars from the International Fund for
Agricultural Development (IFAD) to finance root corps production in
the country.
Enugu State coordinator of the National Agricultural Co-ordinating
Committee (NACC), Chief Alfred Eneh, told newsmen in Enugu that those
who would benefit from the loan scheme were farmers in yam, cassava,
potatoes and coco-yam production.
He revealed that Cocoa Growers Association of Nigeria had been given
an initial release of N90 million for their members. Chief Eneh who
had just come back from Abuja after attending a meeting of joint
representatives of the Project Co-ordinating Unit (PCU) and leaders
of 47 commodity association, revealed that both Dr.
Perim Saint Arige and Dr. Jean Delion from the World Bank, had
confirmed that IFAD assisted cassava multiplication project would
also lay emphasis in processing, utilisation and marketing of crops.
He stated that a six-man committee had been inaugurated to study
farmers draft constitution recently submitted to NACC by Professor
Emeka Opara led constitution drafting committee. Eneh who also is the
National Chairman, Cashew Growers Association, said that the Farmers
Draft Constitution Review Committee led by Chief Sylvanus Ekeanyanwu,
would complete its job within the stipulated time to "create enabling
environment for the second all farmer's summit" due to hold this
month.

Original_Search_Title: Yam, NYC, Youth Groups Organize Cleansing Exercise
Headline: Gambia: Yam, NYC, Youth Groups Organize Cleansing Exercise
Body_Text:
Banjul — Last Wednesday volunteers from youth groups such as Lend A Hand
society and the Red Cross in collaboration with Yam, the National
Youth Council organised a cleaning exercise coined "set-settal" at
the Banjul Ferry terminal. The purpose of the exercise was to help
the country and part of Yam's initiative.
Mr. Gibba President of the NYC, said Yam and his council felt it was
a "fantastic idea" to work together in a cleansing exercise that
brought together many youth.
Yam consists of 21 students from Denver, Colorado in the U.S.A. Mr.
Gibba said volunteers cleaned the whole area and painted the
building.
Sam Periannan who is the leader based in The Gambia aid he was
travelling by the terminal when he saw that the whole place was
dirty, and felt that it needed some cleaning. He talked it over with
Raymond Gibba and Pastor Forbes and others who endorsed the idea ad
received support from various administrators. He said Yam is the
mother of five Mercyships and that they have 160 places and employ
14,000 people full time. They have five mercyships. He said they are
a Christian organisation made up of youths and adults. They travel
from country to country and clean places.
Ousman Njie the Maine Operations Managers said that the cleansing
exercise was a welcome idea and has been done at the right time. He
further went on to say that volunteers appreciate the job they have
done.

Original_Search_Title: Doctor Raises Alarm Over Food Poison
Headline: Nigeria: Doctor Raises Alarm Over Food Poison
Body_Text:
Lagos — A medical doctor and the pioneer/executive chairman of Market of
Convenience, Dr. Bayo Kumolu-Johnson has raised an alarm over the existence
of adulterated and poisonous foods in Nigerian market.  Speaking during a
courtesy call on P.M. News recently, Dr.  Kumolu-Johnson said about 21
varieties of essential foods are in circulation and are prone to being
poisoned.
Some of the food listed are rice (imported only), beans (variety from
Maiduguri called Olo), sugar (granulated, cube and variety in sachet), milk
(liquid and powder), egg (large, medium and small sizes), palm oil, soup
condiment, salt, vegetable oil, groundnut oil, gari, fresh fish, stock fish,
frozen chicken, frozen turkey, yam tubers, potatoes, semovita (small packs,
plastic containers, poly bags) and fruits in the season.
Dr. Kumolu - Johnson said the foods are being adultrated or poisoned due
to the market environment and the greed of most manufacturers to make money
at the expense of the consumers. He said "palm oil sellers store the product
for a longer period than necessary, worse still, pap and potash are being
added to it to make fake vegetable oil from used oil, bleach palm oil for
more money as well as sell groundnut mixed with sawdust as ground pepper
among others".
Continuing, he said most of the ailments in the country which are being
diagnosed like typhoid fever and some other general sicknesses are caused by
food poisoning. To avert this, he said his organisation has established an
alternative market called Oja Irorun (Market of Convenience) in the
neighbourhood which sells to people in schools, offices, banks, churches. He
revealed that plans are in the pipeline to establish markets in all the
local governments in Lagos State first before moving to other states.

Original_Search_Title: I've Never Been Bothered About My Health - Obiageli Molobe
Headline: Nigeria: I've Never Been Bothered About My Health - Obiageli Molobe
Body_Text:
Lagos — A Pioneer of the Nigerian home video industry, Obiageli Molobe has
never ceased to show her talents and mastery in the artical arts. Now
a grandmother, she is not in the habit of disclosing her age, but she
admits she's has "many" children and has been around for "a while".
Besides featuring in countless movie productions, Obiageli spends
time doing radio drama on Radio Nigeria. She also junkets several
other studios doing voice overs for radio commercial jingles all in
the struggle to keep body and soul together. She however admits that
all too often, the spirit may be willing but the body too weak to
comply. So how does she keep it all together healthwise? Hear her...
"I wake up in the morning at about 7:00 am on a typical day that I
don't have to move out very early. Even then, I don't usually get to
bed later than midnight.
If NEPA allows it, I'm inclined to watch the late night movies
otherwise I read or switch on my battery-operated radio for some
music. Just before retiring to bed, I check my schedule for the next
day. My schedule usually determines whether I'd have to wait a little
bit more before hopping into bed. But I must be in bed by 12:15 am at
the latest.
I have a body system that makes sleep almost impossible whenever I
stay up till about 1:00 am or thereabout. I always leave home at
about 9:00 am. However, I'm in the habit of doing something before
leaving home.
After listening to the morning news, I do my daily chores. I'm a
grandmother now and do not have to cope with suckling babies anymore.
As a result, my morning chore is quite different.
Soon after, I step into the bathroom for a cold shower water which I
have breakfast which must be balanced like tea, bread and eggs.
Sometimes it is ogi (pap) and akara.
I hate taking heavy meals in the morning because they make me feel
heavy and unable to do my theartherical and other duties comfortably.
Lunch comes in the form of rice or eba. This is my real meal. I eat
heavily at this period mostly because it is the time for real work
and one needs to be "solid". For supper, depending on how I feel. I
may skip it altogether. On the other hand, I could make do with bread
and tea, a cup of beverage or boiled yam. When I'm hungry, I eat as
the weight of my hunger determines. When my body begins to tell me
its time for rest, I do not hesitate to rest. As soon as I feel any
form of fatigue, I rest and try to avoid undue stress.
I've never really been bothered about my health system, really
because I do not have that kind of consciousness.
My kind of work is stressful enough. I'm on the move most of the time
shunting here and there. Sometimes it could be all night in case of
the over night productions. Like I said, once my body begins to show
signs of fatigue, I relax.
As a rule, Fridays and Sundays are for relaxation. I try to remain
indoors throughout during these periods. It is at such times that I'm
able to read all the novels within my reach. My favourite authors
include Danielle Steel, Barbara Tyler and Bradford. They thrill me
any day. I love mystery novels. Sydney Sheldon's Bloodline
particularly thrills me. Novels that have been made into movies
generally give me great joy. I love I also watch any home movie that
may be around at that time. I could take a trip to the beach to rest
with anyone of my children who wants to accompany me.
What you would find me doing on a typical rest day is window-
shopping. just go down to Cash & Carry and while away the time
comparing the prices of various items on display. Believe it or not,
this helps to relax my tensed nerves."

Original_Search_Title: A Panel's Thorny Path To Peace
Headline: Nigeria: A Panel's Thorny Path To Peace
Body_Text:
Lagos — Apparently worried and in a bid to find a lasting solution to several
re- occurrences of bloody ethnic clashes which have resulted into
wanton destruction of lives and property, the Lagos State government
on December 1, 2000 inaugurated the tribunal of inquiry into the
civil disturbances in the state between December 1999 and October
2000.
The tribunal is headed by a retired High Court judge, Justice Victor
Famakinwa with other members drawn from various professions.
There is no doubt that the ugly incidents in the state termed, "Lagos
Mayhem" or "The dark days" are still fresh in the minds of many
Lagosians who were caught unawares by the bloody violence in October
last year allegedly unleashed on the state by members of the Oodua
Peoples Congress (OPC) and the Arewa Forum representing Northern
interest.
For many Lagosians, the prayer was 'never again' to such incidents
which had led to a colossal loss of lives and property worth millions
of Naira. Utter confusion, and a feeling of insecurity amidst
wailings were the order of the day during the mayhem.
Although Lagos State is the smallest state in the federation, yet it
has the highest population due to its cosmopolitan nature. The state
which was formerly the country's capital is still regarded as the
commercial nerve centre of Nigeria. Its population is over five
percent of the national estimate comprising various ethnic groups.
According to the 1991 national census, the state has a population of
5,725,116 out of a national estimate of 88,992,220. However, based on
a United Nations (UN) study, the state is estimated to have about 8.5
percent of the total state population with a land mass of 37 percent.
In a recent UN study however, the city is expected to hit the 24
million population mark and thus be among the ten most populous
cities in the world by the year 2015.
No doubt, the population explosion coupled with the high rate of
unemployment of the youths in the state, has in no small measure
contributed to the hydra-headed re- occurrences of ethnic clashes.
In his speech at the inauguration, Lagos State governor, Senator Bola
Tinubu had stated thus: "I am sure that the chairman and members of
the tribunal will apply themselves diligently to this arduous and
challenging task which will assist the state in putting an end to the
gruesome and senseless killings of innocent citizens under the guise
of ethnicity and bring succour to the common man. I am certain that
you will bring to bear your well known reputation for integrity and
diligence."
Though the tribunal was set up mainly as a fact- finding mission into
the crises, the following are also its terms of reference:
* To inquire into the remote and immediate causes of the civil
disturbances which occurred.
* To ascertain the circumstances surrounding the incidents.
* To determine the number and identities of casualties in the course
of the disturbances.
* To identify persons or groups responsible for the civil
disturbances.
* To determine the extent of loss of lives and damage to properties
occasioned by the disturbances.
* To inquire into the other matters incidental to the above which may
assist the state government in coming to a fair and just conclusion
on the subject matter of the inquiry.
* To make recommendations on this and any other matters incidental to
the above which in the opinion of the Tribunal would assist
government in preventing future occurrence.
According to Justice Famakinwa, "a total of 64 witnesses have been
heard, while over 300 memoranda / petitions have also been received
with more entries pouring in daily." In pursuance of its objectives
and in an attempt to have a stronger view and under-standing of the
situation at the affected areas, the Panel, therefore, had on January
23, 2001 embarked on a tour to make an on the spot assessment of the
different local government areas which were mostly affected by the
mayhem.
Among the local government areas visited are Kosofe, Ifelodun, Ojo,
Agege, Ifako- Ijaiye and Mushin.
The visits, however, have been evoking mixed feelings among Lagosians
particularly from the Hausa communities in Lagos who have at
different occasions protested the rationale of the Tribunal visit,
accusing it of discrimination as most of their (Hausa) affected areas
were allegedly intentionally over looked during the visit. In fact, a
brawl almost resulted at Ajegunle when some Hausa leaders had minor
misunderstanding among themselves on who should be the spokesman.
However, Justice Famakinwa rose in stout defence of his action.
Debunking the allegation, he said, "it is not true that we
intentionally left out some of their affected areas as claimed. As
human beings, there is a limit to the areas we can cover per day. We
cannot be everywhere in a day. It is not possible. But I am assuring
them that all areas shall be visited. All they have to do is to send
in their memoranda and we shall act appropriately."
Justice Famakinwa added that "I don't want our people to see this
Panel as a means to victimise them or to apportion blames, but rather
as a fact- finding body seeking first hand information in order to
arrive at a logical conclusion in its recommendation to the state
government."
Throughout the visits to all the council areas, two questions kept
re-occurring . These bordered on the issue of the Oodua Peoples
Congress (OPC) and the clashes between the three major ethnic groups
in the country. Counter-accusations were the order of the day as each
group tried to absolve itself of blame on who started the mayhem.
Different versions of how and what started it were freely traded.
The burnt Akanimodo and Ajelogo markets at the popular Mile 12 in
Kosofe area were the first point of call. It was reportedly burnt
down by some miscreants believed to be members of the OPC employed by
two warring parties belonging to the Sukura Yam Sellers association.
Power tussle within this association was said to have led to the
bloody clash last October. According to one of the many eye
witnesses, Sukura Market is predominantly composed of Hausas who call
the shots there. One of the stories of how it started has it that the
Yorubas in the area apparently irked by the dominance of the Hausas
decided to call their bluff by attempting to take control of the
market. This subsequently led to a bloody clash between the two
ethnic groups with the OPC featuring prominently.
Another version said the power tussle among the incumbent executives
at the Sukura market was responsible for the problem. It was said
that the executive refused to leave the post after their normal
tenure. This Vanguard Midweek Features (VMF) gathered did not go down
well with the other party which was accused of using OPC members to
threaten the executive. And the exco largely made of Hausas decided
to eject the Yorubas from the market after earlier asking them to
leave the place as their continued stay in the market constituted
insecurity to their lives and property.
Also miscreants mainly from Ajelogo and Akanimodo were said to have
been used for the attack rather than the wide spread speculation that
OPC members carried out the attack.
The council boss who expressed deep concern over the illegal
acquisition of the market place by touts and hoodlums described the
place as the "robbers den" where no human being except criminals dare
go into.
Barrister Braimoh said, all efforts by both the local and state
governments to eject them proved abortive as they kept coming back.
The Ajelogo and Akanimodo market was said to have been initially
planned to accommodate Kosefe High School, Health Centre and Police
Station by the then Shomolu Local Government before it came to be
under Kosofe Local Government.
According to one Mrs Yetunde Ogunleye, the coordinator of the market
(Mile -12) "the land belongs to the defunct Marketing Board and later
bought over by Kosofe Local Government before being hijacked by these
hoodlums.
The land span then was over 77.5 hectares of land".
The panel later moved to Ajegunle in Ajeromi Ifelodun area from where
the crisis was said to have spread to other areas in the state. It
was led by the council boss Prince Rabiu Adio Oluwa, who took the
panel members first to Achakpo and Alayabiagba where the crisis
recorded its highest casualties.
The case was however not different from that of Mile-12. The clashes
recorded here were also purportedly executed by miscreants who seized
the opportunity to unleash their criminal intent.
The council boss, described Achakpo area as a predominant Hausa
settlement, bedevilled with all kinds of problematic and provocative
lay abouts. He described Ajegunle as a model of Nigeria where you can
find all shades of tribes that make up Nigeria. "We have the Yorubas,
Hausas, Igbos, Efik, Deltalites, Ijaw, Fulani and so on," he said.
Prince Oluwa partly blamed the Police for their slow response to the
crisis and therefore called for a more efficient Police Force, with
adequate men on ground to combat crime.
Other areas also visited were the Lagos State Abattoir and Lairage,
Ayokunnu Bakery and Kampala Way, all in Agege area.
Others included Ajangbadi, Alaba International and PPL Street all in
Ojo area.
The last places visited were Alasatu, Oluwatosin Street and the
notorious Alaka Street, all in Mushin area.
Testifying at one of the sittings of the tribunal, the Commissioner
of Police, Lagos State, Mr. Mike Okiro described the emergence of
militia groups as a child of circumstances brought about by
repressive military regimes and also by the ailing economy of the
country. He said many of these hoodlums are able-bodied men who find
themselves in crimes due to unemployment and lack of proper
upbringing on the part of parents and guardians. He added that "lack
of confidence in the police can be anchored on our degenerating legal
system; it is not because we are inefficient."
Remarkably, the tribunal chairman, local government chairmen and
other petitioners seem to hold similar opinions as to how to check
further crisis apart from identifying similar traits as the causes of
the crises.
According to many of the complaints from petitioners, community
leaders, local chiefs and opinion leaders are always in the centre of
the crises. They are said to be guilty of making inciting statements
that often lead to bloody clashes and also fuel the crises beyond
imagination.
One of such allegations was made against the Seriki Hausawa of
Ajegunle, one Alhaji Watago. He was alleged to have urged his kinsmen
to take up arms against the Yorubas in the area which sparked off the
gruesome killings in October last year.
A cross-section of Lagosians also had opportunity to express their
views on the crises and what should be done to stem the ugly trend.
Apart from calling for a restructuring of the Police, they also said
that the panel would only remain relevant if it focuses on its terms
of reference and not mortgage its integrity. They said the panel's
terms of reference should also include justice and adequate
compensation for victims of the mayhem.
It was also suggested that government must also ensure that the
panel's findings and recommendations must not be swept under the
carpet, as was the case with this similar investigation panels set-up
in the past. Government was also advised not to tamper with the
panel's report before it is made public. If government retrains from
doing this they argued, the public will be encouraged to accept the
panel's findings and recommendations.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Records Increase In Non-traditional Exports
Headline: Ghana Records Increase In Non-traditional Exports
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's exports of non-traditional
products garnered 214.47 million dollars in the first half of
last year, up 19.41 percent from the 179.6 million dollars earned
during the same period in 1999, the Ghana Export Promotion
Council (GEPC) said on Monday.
The performance analysis of the GEPC said the sub-sectors of
agriculture, manufacturing and handicraft dominated the export
drive.
Major export destinations are the United Kingdom, the
Netherlands, Italy, France, Germany, Spain, Belgium, Greece and
the United States. Ghana's traditional exports are gold, cocoa
and timber.
Figures for the second half are still being compiled.
"The volume of exports was made possible by 2,275 exporters
of 253 items compared to 1,740 exporters of 240 items within the
same period of 1999," it said.
Of the total earnings, the manufacturing sub-sector
contributed 167.495 million dollars (136.456 in 1999),
agriculture 43.464 million dollars (40.095 million in 1999) and
handicrafts 3.5 million dollars (3.06 in 1999).
Wood and wood products led the manufacturing sub-sector,
earning 60.96 million dollars.
Prepared foods and beverages brought in 36.8 million
dollars, followed by cocoa cake, liquor and butter with 29.4
million dollars, plywood 17.672 million dollars, sliced veneer
15.324 million dollars and builders woodwork 11.84 million
dollars.
The rest are aluminium products (5.486 million dollars),
palm oil (4.83 million dollars) while furniture and furniture
parts fetched 4.2 million dollars, and common salt 1.875 million
dollars.
Horticultural products brought in 14.369 million dollars,
from fresh pineapples, banana, papaw and fresh yams. Fish and
seafood earned 9.865 million dollars, sheanut 2.62 million
dollars, robusta coffee 2.742 million dollars and cocoa waste 1.1
million dollars.
The handicraft sub-sector contributed 3.516 million dollars
during the period made up of assorted handicrafts and basket-
ware, the report said.

Original_Search_Title: Action Now!
Headline: Ghana: Action Now!
Body_Text:
Accra — The need for some form of forum for truth and reconciliation keeps
beckoning. The pains of the past keep crying for healing.
The government of President Kufuor is being looked upon to midwife a
new era of healing which will hopefully help the nation put behind it
the pains of June 4 and December 31. Many people were willfully
damaged by the system, some with flimsy excuses that they had
children with other people's spouses.
One of the early victims of the Rawlings coup of December 31, 1981
was a mild mannered civil servant called Mr. Bentum-Eshun. His crime
was that he had fathered children out of wedlock and allegedly not
taking care of his wife.
He was vilified and hounded by the powers that be, with the
connivance of the editor of the Ghanaian Times, who wrote scathing
editorials against the man and commissioned articles from a
mysterious "Correspondent of the 31st Movement" to do the man "pasa
pasa."
The Ghanaian Times and the Daily Graphic were used to clobber any
individual or group that were perceived to be either personal enemies
of the powers that be, or enemies of the "revolution". The editor of
Ghanaian Times, who today sits on the National Media Commission,
thanks to Rawlings, surpassed himself and was considered by many
people as a terrorist of sorts. As a result, the circulation of the
Ghanaian Times under his editorship fell to its lowest point. Both
state-owned papers became vehicles for planted letters from the
Castle to destroy the integrity of people who had neither the chance
of answering back, nor seeking redress in the courts.
It is needless to add that after the terror of the 31st DWM and
Ghanaian Times, Bentum-Eshun eventually lost his job and suffered
terrible humiliation and penury until his death in 1999. He has gone
to his grave with no chance of defending himself. His final home call
was with the humiliation he suffered from the Rawlings "revolution".
It did not end there, the lady "guilty" of having a child with him,
was herself hounded and vilified by the 31st December Women's
hierarchy. She also lost her job and with it went her confidence to
face life. A promising civil servant and sports administrator
herself, she fell into tough times and has since been reduced to
selling fried yams to take care of herself and her child. The
following is her own story of how state power can be misused to flout
the human rights of others. Why was this poor woman treated the way
she was by the 31st December Women's Movement? An honest answer to
this simple question can begin the healing process of this poor woman
and her lonely child.
I fell in love with one Mr. Kofi Bentum-Eshun, a Principal Secretary
at Ministry of Trade and had a child with him.
There was a publication in the Ghanaian Times of 11th June 1984 about
Mr. Bentum-Eshun titled, 'The fall of a respectable man' and an
editorial, 'Pillars of society'. With that publication, Mr. Bentum-
Eshun was retired from the Civil Service and then they [the
leadership of 31st DWM] started on me.
I wrote to Christian Aggrey [Editor of Ghanaian Times] about the
newspaper article, wanting to know who the 31st December
Correspondent was so that I could seek redress. Mr. Aggrey told me
that the orders were that he shouldn't counteract anything about the
said publication. I felt I had been cheated by them using the
state-owned media to victimise Mr. Bentum-Eshun and me.
In 1988 I went on an ILO course in Harare and when I came back I was
arrested and sent to BNI (Bureau of National Investigations). At the
BNI, they made me sit down from 8 in the morning to 8 in the evening.
Anytime I got up to enquire why I was being kept there - because I
was nursing a baby at the time - they asked me to sit down. Later on,
one Mr. Annor Kumi and one other interviewed me and they said I was
employing people to become dissidents. I disagreed saying I didn't
know anything about what they were saying. After the interrogation, I
was allowed to go.
Later in the year, around November, I went to officiate a match at
Abuja. When I came back, I was again arrested.
They said I had collected some monies from people telling them I was
taking them to Israel.
That was a frame-up and I told them it was not true. I was sent to
James Fort for two months and then from there to the one of the
public tribunals which operated as courts.
Another Mr. Aggrey was the Tribunal Chairman and my lawyer was Lawyer
Arthur. Unfortunately for me, Lawyer Arthur had the Bitar Timber
case, so he transferred the case to Mr. Kofi Bosumpem. The case went
on for four years.
After that, I was at the Accra Sports Stadium officiating a match
when I was arrested again. They didn't even allow me to see my
lawyer. They said for the past four years they had been looking for
me and couldn't find me. That wasn't true because I hadn't travelled
outside Accra - even to my hometown within that period. It was a
Saturday; they took me to Korle Bu Police Station and on Monday
morning, I was taken to Nsawam for 18 months with no paper on me. The
Police told them they were just asked to bring me there, so later
they would bring the paper. The paper never came and I was there for
18 months only to be told that somebody had gone to pay Â¢3 million on
my behalf before I was released.
When I came out I tried to get in touch with people I had written
petitions to. There was a man called Akrasi Sarpong at the Castle and
I wrote a petition to him. He was supposed to take the case to P.V.
Obeng, but later I was told that the case wouldn't get anywhere
because it involved 31st December Women's Movement. All these years I
have been writing petitions and getting nowhere. The last petition I
wrote was to CHRAJ a copy of which I gave Mr. Bebaako Mensah who was
became Secretary to the Cabinet of the former government. I was
assured that they would go through the matter; but nothing happened
and it has been lying there ever since.
To take the matter up myself, I tried to get copies of the tribunal
proceedings and was directed to a court at the seaside. They told me
they couldn't find any documents because the tribunal had stopped
functioning. I was again directed to Ho where the clerk in charge of
the case, one Ofori, was, but I couldn't get anything from him
either. Unfortunately for me, my lawyer, Mr. Kofi Bosumpem, also
died. I tried to get in touch with the Chairman of that tribunal, but
I couldn't find him.
At the moment I don't have any job and it has become like a stigma
because I went to Nsawam [jail]. But there is no paper, nothing on
me. I feel I have been cheated and I have been treated poorly. With
my background and education I can't get a job because of that and I
am seeking redress. Because of the stigma, the victimisation, I am
even afraid to come out because anytime I wrote applications for
jobs, someone would go and say I had been sent to Nsawam before.
I rose through the ranks to become a Labour Officer and I am as good
as any other woman who holds public office. Even though I am
qualified to even be a Minister with my educational background, I now
fry yams for a living. The daughter I had with Mr. Bentum is now in
secondary form two and since Mr. Bentum is now dead, I'm finding it
very difficult to pay her school fees.
I have been victimised and need assistance. I am taking it upon
myself to bring this out in the papers so that the case can be
recalled. I think this was somebody's private affair. It wasn't the
first time a man had taken two wives, nor will it be the last time.
Now that there is a chance for me, I have to take my matter up again
to seek redress. CHRAJ couldn't help me much; I am therefore
appealing to the Minister for Women's Affairs and the Attorney
General to help me get a lawyer to take my matter up.

Original_Search_Title: Why Ghai Should Visit My Republic
Headline: Kenya: Why Ghai Should Visit My Republic
Body_Text:
Nairobi — If there is a Prof Yash Ghai out there without a job, I have one for
him.
I would like him to reconcile the various factions in the Whis
Republic, which I also call home.
The prime minister of the republic, her majesty Thatcher, leads one
faction. The girl who calls herself Pajey leads the second. Her real
name is the Investment alias the Pajero. She also goes by other names
including pre-woman, girl-child and Ka-Beijing.
At one time, she could not decide whether she was a young man or a
young woman but now she has decided that she is a pre-woman. That is,
she is neither a girl nor a woman, but is the source of many
troubles.
The third faction is led by the character called Whispers Junior
alias Ras Whispero, alias Man Whispero. He is the same character who
recently converted to Mungiki. That is why he is causing all the
trouble that is cooking in my house. I say trouble is cooking in my
house because it all ends up in the kitchen and not because Thatcher
is at times given to talking to pots and pans rather loudly when she
should be talking to me.
Trouble ends up in the kitchen because each faction wants to rule in
that place. Let's start with the Investment. She has this idea that
when women met in Beijing some years ago to decide that they, too,
had a right to roast and eat meat, they met to talk about her. So she
has this thing that she calls the "Beijing Declaration" on the
girl-child and pre-women, meaning girls of her age.
I don't know whether the declaration says anything about what fathers
should be called. All I know is that she no longer calls me dad. She
calls me Mum's slave-driver or the resident Kabila. By now, I hope
you know that Kabila was very famous for dispatching a few fellows to
the past whenever he had something bad for breakfast.
I won't commit suicide for being called all those things. What will
most likely lead me to an early death is that I am supposed to obey
the Beijing Declaration on the girl-child with my wallet. As a
result, my wallet is weeping every day. But there are other reasons
behind my weeping.
Take, for example, her claim that the Beijing Declaration gives the
pre- woman, meaning her kind, the right to express themselves. She
thinks that the right includes bursting her vocal chords and lungs in
song when I am trying to reconcile the various factions in my head
created by what I had been drinking the previous night.
I perhaps might not mind her singing but I get mad when she disturbs
my peace by barking like a dog in the name of singing. Here I am
trying to make the warring factions in my head understand that I need
peace when she suddenly starts singing something like, "Who let the
dogs out?" Then she goes, "Wo! Wo! Wo!" like a mad dog.
I hear that the barking is supposed to be a song by somebody who has
been rewarded for doing it. I can assure that daughter of mine that I
will reward her for her barking with a total ban from living in the
house for which I pay rent.
Let us now go to what she cites as another clause in that
declaration. She alleges that the clause says a woman should have
total control over her body. The Investment says this right means
that a pre-woman should have a right to ask from her father for
things that will make her body "look right". Looking right to her
means looking like that insect called a praying mantis.
If you have never seen the insect, it has a very thin waist. In fact
it looks as if its waist is being strangled permanently. It has a
rather long neck and indeed its entire body suggests that it feeds on
air. The investment wants to look like that insect. In other words,
she wants to be in what is called "shape".
She wants to look like one of those pre-women called Spice Girls.
Unlike the praying mantis, she does not want to feed on air. She
wants to feed on things that have never been heard of in my house Ã
or in my clan for that matter Ã and expects me to pay for them.
In an effort to turn herself into that relative of a mosquito, the
Investment is demanding that I buy items that have never appeared in
the dictionary of the Nyaituga clan to which she belongs. She is
demanding that I buy her things called "Portobello mushrooms". The
only mushrooms I knew of were those that looked like umbrellas. They
used to grow up on the Slopes where I was looking after goats as I
grew up. Something happened and they vanished. Even then, those
plants were not for eating. They were for kicking about like a
football.
They were soft on our toes that had been chewed up by jiggers and we
really enjoyed kicking them around. If you ask me, those "portobello"
mushrooms might as well be things that are given to European goats to
expel worms from their tummies. Why the Investment is demanding them
is beyond my thinking. She says they are supposed to provide the kind
of calories that will make her waist as slim as that of a mosquito.
She also demands an item called "Lemon meringue cake". She insists
the "meningitis" cake is the right stuff for a pre-woman who has
control over her body. That lemon "meningitis" cake is supposed to
strengthen her hair. If she does not eat it, her hair will suffer
stress. When that happens, she says, she too will suffer from stress
and might have to see a psychiatrist.
The Investment, alias Pajey, is not taking any chances with her
efforts to ensure that her waist resembles that of a praying mantis.
That is why she has forced me to buy what should be found only in a
butchery. She has forced me to buy a weighing scale so that she can
watch her weight.
The first thing she does in the morning is to step on that weighing
machine and look at its needle very suspiciously. She wants to ensure
that she has not gained any weight in the night. It is as if sleeping
can make her gain weight. Come 10 am, she is on that weighing machine
again.
She spends five minutes looking at its needle to make sure that the
strungi (strong tea) she took with lemon in the morning has not made
her gain weight. At 4 pm, she climbs onto it again, this time to
ensure that what she had for lunch has not changed her weight. All
she took for lunch is half a carrot and a suspicious looking leaf
whose name I cannot memorise.
All I know is that the leaf costs real money at a green grocer where
those who visit don't use coughing cars like mine. They go there in
cars that say that it is bad manners for a car to sneeze.
Let us leave that girl and her Beijing Declaration and turn to her
brother Whispers Junior alias Ras Whispero and recently converted to
Mungiki. Ever since his conversion, the fellow talks very little.
Instead his nose is very busy receiving snuff according to the rules
of his sect.
He keeps loading that stuff into his nose as if his brain, like a car
engine, receives fuel. Apart from banning himself from talking too
much, the fellow has also outlawed himself from using underwear. The
fellow says that his sect considers that item to be designed for
women and he is not one. Instead, he is now wearing very strange
items.
He cuts off his old trousers at the knees and uses them as inner
wear. He calls them "boxer shorts" and claims that is what real men
should wear. I don't mind a young man consuming the stuff that his
ancestors used to partake of, but the things that he is demanding for
his mouth are worrying me.
The fellow has declared that his sect does not allow what he calls
European food. He claims his sect demands that he only eat the food
of his ancestors. That is why he demands boiled arrow roots, sweet
potatoes, roast yams and bananas as well as boiled green vegetables
which can only be found in the valleys of Mount Kenya.
The last time I tried to make the character understand that he cannot
force us to eat the food of his ancestors, he clogged his nostrils
with tobacco and, after sneezing a number of times like an angry
bull, looked towards Mount Kenya. He said a prolonged prayer to the
God of Kirinyaga, the "Owner of the Ostrich". He asked the Owner of
the Ostrich to give his hands the strength of Dedan Kimathi, he who
put terror in the hearts of the white men and made them flee from
Kenya.
He prayed that he needed that strength so that he could fight the
enemies of the Owner of the Ostrich now resident in the same house
with him. He called the house Babylon and asked the Owner of the
Ostrich to help him get out of slavery where he is being forced to
eat the food of slaves instead of that of his ancestors. I did not
need to look very far to know whom he referred to as "the emperor of
Babylon".
The character was talking so negatively about me that I decided to
give him enough money to go and buy the food of his ancestors. Just
at that moment, Thatcher stretched her hand. She wanted money for her
own food. She, too, had her special menu. It happened that a few
weeks ago, her sect of Many Waters got a new bishop. The bishop came
with new rules. One of the rules is that no food that has been cooked
with fat shall pass the mouth of any of the sect of Many Waters
member unless she or he wants to burn in hell.
Thatcher does not want to suffer in hell where she says sinners are
fed with hot tar in the morning for breakfast. So she has been eating
boiled cabbages, boiled beans, boiled rice, boiled potatoes and such
stuff. From the look of things, she will soon ask for boiled air.
What all this means is that there are three cooking pots in the
house. There is one for the relative of the praying mantis, another
to make the food of the ancestors and the third to make what is
called holy food or the dish for the mouths of those who wish to go
to heaven.
It means is that when I get home in the evening, I find outstretched
hands asking for money to buy items for crazy menus. When I ask where
my food is, I get looks that suggest that I should cook for myself.
If Prof Ghai you're out there reading this third-rate column today, I
need your help please. Tell me how I can cook a solution to this
kitchen problem on wis@mitsuminet.com

Original_Search_Title: Heroes Of The 20th Century: Alhaji Audu Bako
Headline: Nigeria: Heroes Of The 20th Century: Alhaji Audu Bako
Body_Text:
Lagos — And he gave it for his opinion, that whoever can make two
ears of corn. . . grow upon a spot of ground where only one grew
before would ... do more essential service to his country, than the
whole race of politicians put together, Jonathan Swift 1667-1745.
In which part of Nigeria were the following grown most in 1966: round
pepper (rodo), tatase, yams, tomatoes, rice, cassava, okro,
vegetable? Where are they most grown in Nigeria today? Which state of
Nigeria is today most nearly self-sufficient in food production?
Virtually all the food products listed in the first question were
produced in southern states and the Middle Belt area; the core north
produced very little except millet, maize, sorghum and alligator
pepper as well as groundnuts. Surprisingly the answer to the second
and third questions is: Kano State. Kano State the state which made
Nigeria famous with its groundnut pyramids rivalling in height those
pyramids of Egyptian rulers from Cleopa (2650 - 2630 BC) the first
builder, has now become Nigeria's food basket and within one
generation has been transformed into the state which is most nearly
self-sufficient in food. Name it, cassava, yams, rice, yams, rice,
tomatoes, onions, okro, vegetables, millet, maize, sorghum, wheat,
potatoes (including sweet potatoes) cabbage, lettuce, carrots as well
as a wide variety of livestock (goats, cows, fish, poultry) are
available in this state far in excess of the quantity that the
state's nearly seven million population can produce.
If ever a state can be said to have benefitted from the wave of
global green revolution which has eluded most other parts of Nigeria,
then that state is Kano. And the father of Kano's green revolution
was Alhaji Audu Bako, the governor of Kano State from 1966 to 1975
during the Gowon administration and the only northern governor who
escaped indictment for corrupt self-enrichment when Gowon's
government was overthrown. In fact, Alhaji Audu Bako was perhaps the
only one of two persons who achieved the status of state governor in
Nigeria and still died a poor man relatively.
The other was Pa Adekunle Ajasin. And it was not because of
squandermania or on account of investments which went bad. He just
didn't have the money. He was so poor, when they were booted out of
office, he just moved into a modest rented house and later moved to a
building purchased for him through donations by friends and former
subordinates when he was governor.
In 1966, according to a close associate and a top official in
Government House Kano at the time, Audu Bako, for reasons still
unclear had decided that his administration would make one great
achievement and that is food self-sufficiency for the people of Kano
State.
Perhaps his resolve to ensure this might have been prompted by the
experience of food shortage in Kano during the civil war when
suppliers from the South, especially yams from Biafra became
irregular. Audu according to the source called a meeting and vowed
that his people would never again depend on others for food and he
set about working with the Federal Government, the World Bank and
other institutions to establish irrigation systems and to acquire
farming equipment to help farmers clear land and prepare them for
planting. He simplified land acquisition, established Kano State
Agricultural Company (KASCO) with branches in several parts of Kano
State providing seeds, fertilizers and pesticides at subsidized rates
to farmers. And he strengthened the Ministry of Agriculture by
employing people capable of providing farmers with training and other
forms of support.
By the time Audu Bako left office, the groundnut pyramids due to no
fault of his own were already disappearing from the Kano landscape as
imported vegetable oil induced by sharply increased crude oil revenue
has started to make local groundnut oil uncompetitive and groundnut
cultivation unprofitable. In fact, Kano State's agriculture might
have been totally devastated and the peasants in rural areas
depending on farming might have been in deep trouble if Audu Bako has
not diversified the scope of agricultural output making it possible
for farmers to switch from one product to another in response to
market demand.
Audu Bako had also targeted Lagos State and its huge population but
small landmass coupled with what he rightly perceived as the
Lagosians distaste for farming. If his people could grow abundant
food, they could "export" to Lagos on a large scale. At the time,
nobody ever imagined that the federal capital will ever move from
Lagos to Abuja.
Today, Audu's target market had multiplied; Nigeria has three
capitals all hungry for food from the north. Lagos is the commercial
capital; Abuja the political capital and Port Harcourt the
fossil-fuel capital of Nigeria.
If the man were alive now, he could not help feeling satisfied that
his dream had come true because other northern states soon emulated
Kano and now the south is dependent on t he north for food. In fact
if the south were to hang on to its crude oil and the north its food
in a war of mutual embrago, it is likely that the south will cave in
first despite ability to import. And all these had taken place within
a generation after Audu Bako placed food at the top of the northern
agenda. And if ever the history of the 20th century Nigeria is ever
written, then the name Audu Bako can never be excluded from the
standpoint of lasting achievement.
Prayer House needs paint
Massey Morning Daily Praying Band, the nation's oldest morning
multi-denominational prayer house 63 years old this year ;needs
repainting. Colours selected are: bluish-grey textured for exterior
and white gloss for interior. Those wishing to donate paint or assist
with money should contact me or send their gifts to 2, Massey Street,
Lagos Island.
The Lord blesses a cheerful giver.
In defence of OPC
"It ain't what people don't know that causes all the problems; it's
what they think they know that just ain't so."
I have on these pages once asked my brother Dr Faseun to disband the
OPC and to start all over again chiefly because the OPC like the
Frankenstein monster had grown out of control of its inventor. And
secondly because private militia dispensing jungle justice
constituting themselves into accuser; prosecutor, judge and
executioner eventually will constitute worse dangers to society than
the menace they set out to eradicate in the first place. Injustice,
in the end will be multiplied.
Those views remain strong even today. Paradoxically, however, I find
a lot of the attacks on OPC not only stemming from ignorance but also
often induced by meddlers tribalism. The 19 northern state governors
and a few w Igbo leaders as well as commentators give the impression
that OPC attacks and kills non-Yorubas indiscriminately.
Nothing could be further from the truth. In reality, the OPC has
eliminated more Yorubas suspected to be armed robbers than any other
tribal group Within a week of blind rage in Lagos Island last year
the OPC summarily executed and burnt nothing less than twenty armed
robbers; everyone was a Yoruba. Their war with armed gangs in the
Akala area of Mushin previously a notorious den of robbers when, the
police could not dislodge yielded over 40 dead in body count;
virtually all Yorubas. Nobody raised an eye-brow at the time.
It was when OPC moved to Ajegunle, heavily populated by non-Yorubas
that the first charges of tribalism were made; one suspects by
erudite robbers and their barons. The truth remains that the easiest
way to get anybody killed in Lagos by OPC or any mob is to shout
"Ole" (thief!) and the unfortunate victim can only escape cruel fate
by divine intervention. The uproar in Ajegunle which spread to other
parts of Lagos last year started with that dreaded word "Ole". The
selected victim just happened to have been a northerner and before
anybody knew it an inter-tribal war was underway. Had the victims
been a Yoruba, nobody would today be calling for the prosecution of
Faseun or the proscription of the OPC.
Furthermore 20 per cent of the houses burnt in the jungle city were
owned by Yorubas and other southerners. Clearly, the OPC, if it were
bent on attacking only northerners could not be rendering Yorubas
homeless.

Original_Search_Title: Robbers Den Uncovered at Kosofe
Headline: Nigeria: Robbers Den Uncovered at Kosofe
Body_Text:
Lagos — A den of robbers has been discovered at the popular Akanimodo market,
Mile 12 in Kosofe local governments area. Making this disclosure,
chairman of Kosofe local government, Barrister Tunde Braimoh said the
den is located inside the market.
He told the Justice Victor Famakinwa panel of inquiry investigating
civil disturbances in the state when the members visited the local
government area that the market covers over 77.5 hectares of land. He
said the market is supposed to be yielding millions of Naira to both
the state and local government but that it is being inhabited by
people of questionable characters.
The panel was told that some parts of the popular Mile 12 market
especially, some of the uncompleted stalls are being used as
residence by those criminals.
The panel also gathered that the occupants of the sheds neither pay
any form of levy or revenue to the local government nor the state as
they are being shielded by some market leaders.
Mr. Essien Emmanuel Umoren the District police officer of Kosofe
local government told P.M.News in an exclusive chat that the police
is having a tough time flushing out the criminals.
"You see that there are many exits. It is not easy raiding this area
during surveillance," the police boss disclosed. Barrister Buraimoh
prayed tribunal to include the area in its recommendation to the
state government as the place was inherited from the old Shomolu
local government. He said the market contained displaced traders from
Jankara market in central Lagos.
During the visit the panel visited Ajelogo market, Mile 12, Sulewa
yam market and other areas of the local government.

Original_Search_Title: Passage: Otse Agilla Goes Hunting
Headline: Nigeria: Passage: Otse Agilla Goes Hunting
Body_Text:
Lagos — The king is dead. Long live, the king. Joy and fanfare
characterise the burial of Philip Agbese Onazi, district head of
Agilla and father of Dan Agbese, Newswatch editor-in-Chief
Ordinarily, every death evokes sympathy and sorrow. But in Idomaland
of Benue State, that of a king elicits joy and happiness. When the
people of Agilla in Ado local government area of Benue State gathered
January 4, at Apa, a village in the town, it was with joy and funfare
as they bid farewell to Philip Agbese Onazi, the Otse Agilla and
their district head, who joined his ancestors December 22.
Of course, it is a popular belief in the town that a chief does not
die. He is said to have gone to the bush for treatment. "Yes," said
Lawrence Eza, a retired army sergeant and head chief of the Osiroko
clan, "a chief in our place doesn't die, we say he has gone to the
bush to take medicine. Today is declared a day that everybody knows
he has gone to the bush,"
"The King never dies. He goes to hunting until whenever he comes
back," intoned Michael Onazi, the king's first son and heir-apparent.
It was the thinking that their king was alive that the people went
into celebrative mood on the 14th day after his passage. Nobody
except the king makers saw his corpse. The visitors from far and near
had gathered at the expansive square outside the king's palace from
early in the morning. Drums started beating very early in the morning
with occasional cannon shots booming into the air. The traditional
chiefs who have been sleeping in the compound for seven days appeared
in their best flowing robes with equally flowing Idoma traditional
caps to add colour to it. It was a carnival type of occasion which
every Idoma male, female, old and young appeared in his or her
"Christmas" dresses full of anticipation for the events which were
billed to start in the afternoon.
Inside the compound, kegs of palm wine made rounds amongst the male
folks while the women and young ladies busied themselves with the
preparations of the various delicacies to be served later. "It is a
special day, it is not every time that a king travels. If an ordinary
person had died, we would have been wailing by now," said a woman who
was supervising other women preparing pounded yam.
In a small house painted all white was an object dressed in a
chieftaincy regalia and cap sitting on a royal chair. On a casual
look, passers-bye would think that the king is seated on his throne.
The villagers and visitors came to the palace to pay homage to the
king for a good life lived before he travelled. That included his
generosity, bravery, care for the entire subjects and his ability to
manage his large family up to the time of his death without crises or
squabbles. Onazi is survived by about nine wives and more than forty
children. An elderly woman in her seventies who came from Igumale, a
neighbouring town, just walked up to the house, looking at the
"sitting king" and praised him for his kindness to her in times of
trouble.
Speaking in Idoma, she gave testimonies of how the king came to her
rescue in times of financial crises, and his good judgment in the
cases he delivered. She dropped N40.00 into a tray in front of the
house to bid the king farewell. She was amongst hundreds of visitors
and natives who came to pay homage to the king and praise him for his
good lifestyle as well as bid him farewell. The farewell by the
various clan chiefs, men, women and youths alike were done with good
words accompanied by donations.
Masquerades and various dance groups were also present. They came
from the various neighbouring towns where the Agilla head was known.
Ekpe masquerade came all the way from Enahumu in Ebonyi State.
Unaloko, a king masquerade in Agilla also made an appearance. It went
straight to the little house, with its knees on the ground, bowed
down and paid its own homage to the king. It then went to the square
to entertain the already fascinated guests.
When, however, the Egwuaja, a masquerade, from Idah in Kogi State,
appeared on the stage from the surrounding bush, the crowd went wild
with jubilation amidst scampering of feet among women and children.
Patricia Oluma, a 46 year-old worker in the fisheries department of
the ministry of agriculture in Ado local government, who jumped up
when she saw the masquerade, told Newswatch it was the second time in
her life time that she was seeing the masquerade.
"It does not come out often. I saw it last when my father, who was
the Adochie of Agilla died many years back," she said. Probably
enjoying the reverence attached to it, the masquerade, with two bells
in each hand ran like a possessed man to the sitting king and
prostrated. It then headed to the square to the admiration of the
king makers who by mid-afternoon, had exhausted several gourds of
palmwine. The natives who were then animated, danced and sang praises
as each masquerade or dance group appeared.
The masquerades, which the women saw during the day was at best all
they could see. They were not allowed to see the masquerades that
performed in the night. Those ones were strictly for the men and the
"initiated." When the owu masquerade from Igumale made its entry late
the previous night, women ran for their dear lives. It is part of the
culture of the Agilla people that women should not see certain
masquerades. The women themselves have accepted it as part of them.
Said Regina Agbese, a daughter-in-law and director of the National
Youth Service Corps, NYSC, Plateau State: "We don't argue with our
men. They give us freedom to do whatever thing we want but when they
say we should not do any thing, we simply obey them."
Newswatch gathered that the joyous mood in the town was as a result
of the elaborate funeral rites which the family organised to bid
farewell to the deceased. Impressed with the funeral arrangements,
villagers were seen pumping hands with Michael, the king's first son
and showered him with praises. He later explained to Newswatch that
it was compliment for the ability of his family to organise the
burial successfully. "It is believed that the king lived in enjoyment
all through his life. So they are thanking me for sending my father
away with the whole rites," he said.
Michael also said the late Onazi Agbese was an "embodiment of every
thing to me and my brothers." Said he: "He never went to school but
he did his best to send us to school, knowing fully well that
education would pay him better than farming." Regina described the
late king as a very loving father, a fine gentleman who gave enough
time to his children. "He was a great farmer, hunter and warrior,"
she told Newswatch.
Onazi's fame in farming, hunting and family life earned him the
kingship of Agilla on December 16, 1984. Eza told Newswatch that he
(Onazi) was "a popular and brave man," who commanded the respect of
almost everybody in the town. "He is a very generous man," he added.
The late Onazi was the father of Dan Agbese, editor-in-chief of
Newswatch magazine.

Original_Search_Title: The Man From Yendi
Headline: Ghana: The Man From Yendi
Body_Text:
Accra — Alhaji Alhassan was named after his grandfather, a renowned
Islamic scholar in Yendi. In Dagomba tradition names of important
personalities in families are hallowed. They are not mentioned but
are instead replaced by expressions like Alfatogma, which means the
namesake of the teacher or Baba, which also means father.
He loves history very much so when the interview lasted he frequently
sent me back to the Gold Coast of the 1930s and sometimes things he
heard from some of his relations. I would take a break from
contemporary issues for a visit to the good old days of the Northern
Territories of the '30s which Alhaji Afatogma relishes recounting.
District Councils
Gaining employment in the then District Councils was a cherished
dream of many a young educated man in those days. Such councils were
responsible for the day to day provision of local government services
and educational matters. In those days clerks of councils were highly
revered. They were responsible for the provision of bursary
scholarships to students from that part of the country and could
determine whether an applicant actually hailed from the region and
thus qualified to enjoy the facility or otherwise.
In the early fifties one Abdulai Musa otherwise called Abdulai Cralwi
was in charge of the Yendi District Council and he was later replaced
by Abdulai Nantong. It was common in those days for people to adopt
the names of their towns of origin as their surnames, a trait shared
by the Hausa of Northern Nigeria. Nantong is the name of an important
town in Dagbon.
Mr. J.H. Allassani, one of Ghana's first crop of indigenous ministers
from Dagbon, showed interest in the affairs of the council in Yendi
where a lot of malfeasance was going on at the time.
He found in one Mr. Harruna-Atta the person to reverse the
unacceptable trend at the local council. Harruna-Atta was the father
of the Managing Editor of The Accra Mail.
His arrival in Yendi sent worrying signals to the status quo who saw
him as a man who had come to disturb their old ways of doing things.
They resisted his efforts, as he remained unwavering in his resolve
to stamp out institutional misdemeanor in the Yendi District Council.
A royal whose uncle is the Gushenaa, B.A. Yakubu, one-time IGP, the
late Harruna Atta was born and raised in Kumasi and so his Dagbani
was with an accent.
This led cynical Yendi indigenes to refer to him as the Fulani man
especially since he was tall and lanky. Adamant in his resolve he
soon streamlined the operations of the council.
He was known to have refused overt attempts at bribing him with gifts
of tubers of yam and guinea fowls to the satisfaction of J.H.
Allassani and others who were instrumental in bringing him to the
north.
So peeved were the indigenes especially beneficiaries of the
malfeasance that they used party political machinery to have him
banished to a place in Trans-Volta.
Southerners In Dagbon
There were quite a number of southerners in Yendi and indeed the
whole of Dagbon many of who were civil servants and traders. In the
case of the former they were given special allowance to entice them
to go and work in the NTs. For those who had never traveled to the
north before, there were many strange and false stories about that
part of the country especially the temperature. I can recall names
like Mr. Amoah, who was the chief clerk in the District Office who
was a Fanti from Saltpond.
There was also one Mr. Turkson who I remember lived in Yendi in about
1949. His brother later became the Commissioner of Income Tax. Mr.
Tamakloe whose descendants are still living in Tamale was also a
staff of the District Council and one of the houses of Tamale
Secondary School was named after him. The Veterinary Services
Department had personalities like Mr. Binka and Olympio.
The Police Service in Yendi at the time under review had an Inspector
Agyiri as the commander. He had under him one Sgt. Timbilla whose
prowess at checking rioting Konkombas was well known. Konkombas were
notorious land poll or 'lampoo' resisters and when they did it was
violent with the bow and arrow, their hallmark. It was Sgt. Timbilla
who was charged with the task of controlling them.
First Aircraft In Dagbon
In 1936, my uncle the late Alhaji Amadu Hussein took me to Tamale
where an announcement had gone round about the arrival of an aircraft
to the town. Chiefs and other persons from far and near converged on
the town to catch a glimpse of the aeroplane, a first time experience
for most people. I had to be lifted to see the 'yigrigu' which means
the flying object.
The Early  Drivers
One had to serve as a driver's mate for a long time before learning
how to drive. The service meant taking care of the 'master's' laundry
and other miscellaneous chores. After sometime the master introduces
the obedient mate to the rudiments of driving. Then comes the task of
facing the testing officer who could fail or pass a candidate. The
passing of the driving test in those days was special because it was
not automatic.
Most of the drivers in Dagbon in my youthful days were Gas and they
included personalities like Ataa Boi, Abiaduka, Tetteh Blankson.
Labadi produced some of the early commercial drivers of the Gold
Coast. The early Dagomba drivers were Mumuni Driver who was the
father of Alhaji Yakubu Ka1suli the Chief Executive Officer of the
Gbewaa Civil Engineers Ltd., Abudu and Bankparma among others.
Mail Delivery
It was difficult getting mail delivered to Tamale and vice versa.
Commercial drivers were therefore used for this purpose but this too
proved unreliable, as the drivers were reluctant in carrying out this
assignment. The authorities therefore devised a method of dealing
with the situation.
Drivers who came to Yendi to discharge goods and passengers were
obliged to deposit their licences with the Police. When they came to
recover these on their way out of town they were given the available
mail for delivery to Tamale. Licenses were so important in those days
that it was unimaginable for drivers to operate without them. Drivers
were obliged to hang numbered driving licenses around their necks
which were in form of metal plates.
The reliance on motorized transport for mail delivery had been
preceded by the use of bicycle for the over fifty mile trip to
Tamale. This task was ably performed by the late Mahaman Massenga a
Yendi indigene whose children are now holding important positions in
the country.
They include Mr. Amadu Mahama who was a one-time senior staff with
the Northern Electricity Department of the VRA, Dr. Ramatu Alhassan,
wife of the CPP chairman and a veterinary surgeon, Dr. Charles
Mahama.
Colonial District Copmmissioners
Most of the DCs as they were then known were military officers
working for the Colonial Service. I can recall names like
Silminkpibga and Mr. Cook. The former was so nicknamed because the
indigenes did not find in him the harshness associated with other DCs
like Captain Armstrong who was nicknamed Halilana or the
temperamental man. His wife too had a share of the nicknaming with
the tag Bugumdia, which means the fire, has gutted. The story about
Silminkpibga was narrated to me by my mother. Silminkpibga was
regarded by the indigenes as an orphan. He was fond of greeting the
people and returning their compliments. One day he sought to know the
meaning of the nickname they had given him. When one day when no less
a person than the Zohenaa, an important chief in Yendi called him by
that name he had him arrested and sent to the Police station. It took
the intercession of the then Yaa Naa Abudu to have the chief
released. The chief explained that he was not an orphan as his
parents were alive in England.
Silminkpibga was also given a name, Bugumdirkugle, which means stone
catches fire. This implied that even the DC's people could be
punished for wrongdoing. Some DC's servants because of their
closeness to the corridor of power used the unauthorized derived
authority to make illegal demands at the marketplaces and others.
Some cooks in particular were fond of asking for guineafowls and eggs
on behalf of their bosses. When this came to the notice of the DCs
they took swift action to stem the thievery.
Contemporary Issues
Asked why he campaigned for the NPP during the recent elections since
he was originally a CPP man who is now a PNC man he had this to say:
'When a lion chases you and you make it to the top of a tree where
you cannot afford to die from starvation you avail yourself of every
available opportunity to survive. In this case joining forces with
the NPP to oust the old order was just the right thing to do.'
On how he feels now that the NDC has been defeated he said, 'I can
now sleep soundly. I had relief only when I was reading the Holy
Quran at night.'
On the Save Dagbon Mission to the North he said 'it was not an easy
task because on one occasion my grand daughter whose husband is an
NDC man rebuked me for being engaged in a campaign for the NPP when
younger men could have done that. It was an uphill task attempting to
turn the scale at a place awash with people contaminated with NDC
goodies. Many of the people had been compelled to swear on oath that
they would vote for that party after receiving assortment of gifts
like torch lights, kettles and money. Eventually however they
responded to our pleas to change their position and the result was
what happened on December 28 for which we are grateful to God.'
On how he felt when the NDC boasted that it was going to win the
polls he said 'I felt that they were going to kill to achieve their
objective and I would have been one of the victims since I would not
have stopped pointing at the ills of that party.'
Alhaji Afatogma said he has met President Kufuor only twice and on
the first occasion his impression of the man was that he is a
God-fearing person. To NPP supporters he said they must be wary of
unguarded statements like 'Ya gye ye man' which does not help the
image of the party. The recent electoral victory he went is not for
one party but for all Ghanaians and he has this message for the
President: ' Beware of sycophants who are capable of derailing your
good intentions for the nation and be guided by the fear of God in
whatever you do.'
To his fellow citizens, he says, 'We should be tolerant of one
another and contribute towards the building of our battered country
irrespective of our party affiliations. For now, what the President
should concern himself with is the economy in which all of us should
assist. Issues like probes should be left for the time being.'
He has travelled widely outside Ghana and has been to countries like
Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Lebanon, Italy and Britain.

Original_Search_Title: Wrestling With Destiny
Headline: Nigeria: Wrestling With Destiny
Body_Text:
Lagos — After a celebrated year of firsts, few might think there
remains more to accomplish for Canada's first-ever world and Olympic
free style wrestling champion.
But there is one thing, something deep in the soul of Nigerian-born
Daniel Igali, that a maple leaf flag, an Olympic gold medal in
Sydney, Australia, and all the international recognition as Canada's
reigning athlete of the year just will not reconcile.
Since exiling himself from his central African roots following his
defection to Canada after attending the Commonwealth Games in
Victoria in 1994, Igali has embraced the Canadian way with ardour.
The picture of the 27-year-old Igali, a new Canadian and now Olympic
champion draped in the national flag of his adopted country is well
documented.
So too is his affection for a Burnaby woman, Maureen Matheny, who
welcomed him as a son into her family when he was new to the country.
He later dedicated his first world championship gold medal to her.
She died five days after that historic event.
The homecoming Igali returned to his homeland for a second time last
winter, after a six-year exile.
There, inside the crude mud and thatched roof huts of his village, he
saw his likeness in the wide, dark, staring eyes of the admiring
Nigerian children. He was welcomed as their hero. He was followed by
swarms of shirtless youngsters, up and down the sandy streets,
everywhere, everyday, through seas of tiny searching hands -
outstretched - all wanting closer, to feel and touch the skin of
their real-life idol.
"It was a great feeling to go and it's more what you see in the eyes
of the kids, everyone wants to be like Daniel Igali".
But while poor. shoeless children dream of breaking free of society's
tight hold and becoming like their hero, Igali dreams of returning
one day to the grasslands and the dirt fields of his youth where as a
youngster he first grappled with the sport that would ultimately
change his life.
While in Nigeria, Igali was constantly asked on local talk shows if
he could ever have achieved his status as the best in the world as an
African.
He bluntly answered, "No", to every suggestion, putting him on the
wrong side of civil authorities again. "Never, and that was an issue
with the authorities. In one sense it was not good but it was also
true".
He says his remarks raised resentment from outside his family and
sport. Some said Igali was likely Nigeria's only chance to ever
embrace a world champion, that he was a traitor to his people and
doing it just for the money. "I tried to make them understand, that I
am happy to be doing it for my adopted country Canada".
But there is still so much pride in being African that Igali can no
longer hide it under the huge maple leaf he so exuberantly draped
over his muscled torso after winning his first world gold medal in
Turkey.
It's in the blood He says returning home was how he expected it would
be - a paradox. A love unrequited and in many ways still unfulfilled.
An ongoing struggle between the political and the moral. His values
against the rule of authority.
"I kind of think they (Nigeria) don't deserve it", he said of their
recognition for his world title. "One thing in my mind says it would
be nice to have a little Nigerian flag, it would be for them, I

learned to wrestle there. If I come to the conclusion it is right,
for sure, I'll probably do it, but I won't do it to satisfy any
administration anywhere".
In the end, it was his adopted country of Canada that received his
outpouring of affection and the ultimate glory. For that, he has no
regrets whatever.
Igali says winning an Olympic medal in Sydney as a Nigerian national
would have been a trade off that an elevated standard of living in an
unsure environment could ever justify.
To give up the general level of respect and human rights that
Canadians enjoy would be too big a price to pay, he says.
But some unseen hand still tugs at Igali's singlet. A grip so tight
and so strong that even the best 69 kilogram wrestler in the world
cannot slip its gut- wrenching hold.
Igali is being called home, home to Eniwari town on the South-East
coast of Nigeria 600 kilometres from the nearest large city Port
Harcourt, where the mighty Niger River meets the Atlantic Ocean.
He desires to one day become a traditional wrestling champion of his
village.
"It's a cultural thing," he explains. "When I grew up all my heroes
were these people - champions of their villages. Unfortunately, I did
not have the chance to do it before I retire".
That chance has now come. Igali is leaving for his homeland Dec. 9,
starting a journey back to his tribal roots - with all its mystery
and tradition.
A high price to pay Both Igali's grandfathers were village champions.
His father's father took line of champions. In fact, Igali's
grandfather was so good he did not have to pay a dowry when he
married.
Igaly says village champions win only prestige, not chattels, and
many are the most wretched in the village by the time they reach old
age.
Villagers could care less if their wrestlers lose 10 matches on the
day to visitors as long as their best defeated their neighbour's
champion just once.
It's a question of community pride and everything is riding on the
success of your champion - including voodoo.
"It does happen", says Igali, "Voodoo is big time in village
wrestling. If I want to wrestle I would go to a voodoo man to protect
me".
But the price paid by village champions is a dear one adds Igali. He
describes the wrestlers as the poorest of the poor. Many are left
without children in their old age, and to a man their lives have been
shadowed by ill fortune in life and money matters every step of the
way.
He say these broken men live off the goodwill of villagers and
admirers like himself to give them money so they might eat and drink.
Igali makes a point of always visiting these men first when he
returns home.
"But those are the guys you want to pray for you when you get home
and then it's up to you to do something for them. It's very
powerful".
Talking drums Igali sees the semi-naked men in white ankle-face and
torsos, muscles burstling and oiled from sweating in the hot sun.
One drum, the seventh and largest - the talking drum - speaks louder
than all the rest. It calls his spirit and muscle to the test. The
wrestler answers, asking the hare skin stretched over the hollow
wooden cylinder to invoke the spirits of his wrestling ancestors to
give him the power.
"I can listen to a talking drum when I'm wrestling. It's like a coach
saying, 'get his leg, get his leg'. You can hear that with a talking
drum. 'your opponent is getting tired. He's getting tired, you see
it's time to attack now".
The gods have spoken Before a match is sanctioned, the two village
camps descend on an open field of coarse grasses, stiffened by the
dying sun when harmattan time sends a cool wind from the north
creating a drying pattern after the harvest of cassava, plantain,
bananas and yams.
Supporters size up protective opponents from among the neighbouring
wrestlers and when a suitable match is found, ask permission of the
referee from a neutral village, before engaging in combat.
Stripped to the waist and clad only in a pair of short pants, the two
combatants grapple, each trying to throw the other to the ground.
Much betting and drinking take place as the village atmosphere
explodes into song -tunes made up on the spur of the moment from old
rhythms - all through their man's prowess over that of their
neighbour.
In a traditional match, the handlers tell the wrestlers the right
time to fight.
It is essential the wrestler be in an aroused state, totally consumed
by the unseen spirits conjured up by the elders.
"It's sort of like saying the gods have spoken", says Igali.
"It is a different kind of emotion. It's like you're so pumped you're
at the highest point of power you can get unexploded power".
Igali describes the feeling as being the very opposite to the
outpouring of emotions he felt after realizing he had won the Olympic
gold medal in Sydney two months ago.
"It's in my head even when I have success now. It's always in my head
all the time.".
"It's in my youth, I remember how they celebrate. It's something you
just want to be".
A dream full circle The combatants fight barefoot on the baked grass
- shoeless- and under clear skies with the searing sun beating down
on their broad backs.
It can be very slippery too in Bayelsa State because the fields are
surrounded by swamps and you don't have to dig more than two feet
into the ground to find water there.
If you slip and fall to your knees, it doesn't matter, it is still a
strike against you.
Winning is not guaranteed in Eniwari, not even for a world and
Olympic free style champion.
There are no divisions in the village fields and Igali will give away
almost 40 kilograms in weight to his opponents.
One slip could cost a wrestler everything.
A fighter can go to one knee but no farther.
"It will be a conclusion of sorts", Igali says. "You don't know how
excited I am to get to wrestle in front of my village. Wrestling for
the glory of the village is the biggest thing I can do for them. My
village would value that more than a gold medal".
When the time comes for him to wrestle, Igali knows he will be ready.
