"The talking drum will call me out and ask me, 'Are you ready to take
prisoners here?' "The elders will call on my ancestors - my great,
great grandfather - to come at this time, on this day of war and back
up his son. 'Give me all your power to wrestle'.
"I will kneel down to the elders and pray, while alcohol poured onto
the dry earth is drunk in by the spirits of past champions".
Igali's name in the Ijaw language is Ibrikp-ikpi (pronounced Ebree
bee bee) 'Thundering Storm' and he has returned home again to bring
Eniwari honour.

Original_Search_Title: New Chapter In Ghana-Togo Relations
Headline: Ghana: New Chapter In Ghana-Togo Relations
Body_Text:
Accra — Ghana's new President, Mr. John Agyekum Kufuor's visit to the
Republic of Togo is seen by many political analysts as a shift of the
country's foreign policy that will create more business and economic
opportunities for the citizens of the two West African states.
In the past, relations between Ghana and Togo were very sour with
pulsating cross border clashes and dissident activities that
destabilised the two countries and destroyed the trading link and
good neighbourliness that existed in the early years of independence.
In those years, Togo's President Gnassingbe Eyadema had to contend
with dissident activities by Togolese exiles in Ghana. The Togolese
authorities reciprocated the unwelcome gesture by habouring Ghanaian
exiles, who also launched similar attacks on Ghanaian territory in a
bid to take over power.
The two countries have now changed their foreign policies from
antagonism to friendship and mutual economic cooperation. This
development effectively closes the dark pages of history and the bad
blood of antagonism. President Eyadema set the stage for good
neigbourliness by honouring an invitation to attend the inauguration
of President Kufuor.
President Kufuor's political opponents and the Togolese opposition
consider his visit last weekend to Togo as unacceptable in the face
of the perceived political nature of the call. However, some economic
analysts see the historic visit as a tactical political move that
will bring considerable economic gains and the dividends of peace to
Ghana and Togo in the spirit of Economic Community of West African
States (ECOWAS). Incidentally, President Eyadema is the Chairman of
OAU.
The inclusion of Alhaji Asuma Banda and Mr. B.A Mensah, both business
tycoons in President Kufuor's delegation stresses the willingness of
the new government to lead a crusade to open a channel for business
among the two neigbours.
Togo's Natural Resources
Togo is rich in mineral resources. The country offers a strong
mineral potential and has large reserves of phosphate, in addition to
limestone and marble quarries. Its reserves of iron ore, manganese,
limestone and pure dolomite are genuine assets for industrial and
economic development. The leading manufactures include cement, flour,
palm oil, cotton textiles, beverages, and soap.
The country is a leading world producer of phosphate, which is by far
its most significant mineral product. In the early 1990s, about 3.7
million metric tonnes of phosphate rock was mined annually.
Togo's phosphate is of a high grade that allows making fertilizers
highly concentrated in phosphoric compounds. This phosphate is very
much appreciated for its production of phosphoric acid a chemical
important for use in research laboratories and industry. Ghana's
match factory at Kade may be revived if phosphate, which is the raw
material for the production of matches, is imported from Togo.
The iron ore reserves of Togo are estimated at more than 500 million
tonnes. According to the latest research findings, there is a genuine
ferruginous basin as well as substantial mixed mineral reserves
including iron titanium.
Togo also possesses large clinker reserves that have been exploited
during the 1980s by Cements of West Africa jointly owned by Ghana,
Togo, and Ghana's western neigbour, Cote d'Ivoire. Despite the
large-scale exploitation of high-grade clinker for the manufacture of
cement, important reserves remain to be exploited in the near future.
The main manufacturer of cement in Togo is Company of Togo's Cements
(CIMTOGO), which is operating in partnership with Norwegian
interests. As Ghana and Togo enter partnership to broaden bilateral
trade between them, Ghana Cement (GHACEM) could take advantage of the
open doors and import cheap clinker for its manufacturing plant at
Tema.
Cross-border Trade
The country's food industry revolves around the processing of local
agricultural commodities. There is a sugar refinery named Anie Sugar
Factor that has an annual capacity of 60,000 tonnes of refined sugar
and 6,000 litres of medical alcohol per day. There is also a
flourishing industry for palm oil and groundnut oil extraction,
coffee roasting, and cassava processing.
Togolese and Ghanaians along the two countries' border stand to
benefit from increased cross-border trade in agricultural produce and
this would have a positive impact on the Anglophone and Francophone
economies who are foes- turned-friends.
The large numbers of Ghanaian traders who shuttle between Accra and
Lome hardly cross into Ghana without the usual cargo of wax prints
and fine jewellery. The textile industry in Togo is much alive to the
demand of customers beyond the borders of that country and this can
be measured with ease when you consider the high value that Ghanaians
place on such goods.
This recent industry is controlled by Togotex International, which
manages two privately owned factories at Kara and Dadja. As trade
between Ghana and Togo increases, the Ghanaian textile industry will
have to raise standards to be able to meet the challenges in the
context of ECOWAS globalisation.
Lome, Togo's chief port and the country's principal administrative,
transportation, and economic centre has an interesting political
history that dates back from the 18th century. Lome is the country's
major port; the port of Kpeme handles phosphate shipments. An
international airport is located near the capital.
Most of Togo's international trade passes through the Lome port,
which was enlarged and deepened in 1968 to accommodate ocean-going
vessels. Most of Togo's exports pass through the nearby port of
Kpeme. Railroads extend inland to Palime, Atakpame, and Sokode and
along the coast to Anecho. Lome is the seat of the University of
Benin (1965) and the National School of Administration (1958).
Lome grew after 1897 as the capital of German Togoland and as a port
exporting raw materials. It passed to Anglo-French control
(1914-1922) and to French control (1922-1960), before becoming the
capital of independent Togo in 1960.
Togo has 37 ethnic groups and a population of about 6 million. The
principal groups in the south are Ewe-speaking peoples; in the north,
the Kabre (or Kabye) and Gurma are most numerous.
Agriculture
Although fertile land is scarce in Togo, more than three-quarters of
the country's labour force work in agriculture. Most of these
agricultural workers are sharecroppers and subsistence farmers on
small plots of land. In many cases, groups of families own farmland
collectively and cultivate corn, millet, yams, sorghum, and cassava
for direct consumption.
About 78% of Togo's labour force is engaged in agriculture. These
farmers cultivate about 26% of the country's land. Togo is generally
self-sufficient in basic food production. The principal food crops
are cassava, yams, corn, millet, and sorghum.
The leading export crops are coffee (13,000 metric tonnes produced
annually in the early 1990s), cotton (41,000), and cocoa (7000).
Livestock raising is important on the northern plateau. In the early
1990s, the country had a total of about 2 million pigs, 1.5 million
sheep, 800,000 goats, and 320,000 cattle. The fishing industry had a
yearly catch in the 1990s of some 12,500 metric tonnes.
Foreign Trade
Togo's yearly foreign trade usually shows a deficit. In the early
1990s, annual exports totalled $512 million and imports totalled $583
million. The leading exports were phosphates (which accounted for
about half of the total), raw cotton, cocoa, coffee, cement, karite
nuts, and palm kernels.
The principal imports were food and food products, textiles,
machinery, electrical equipment, construction materials, and
transportation equipment. Togo's chief trading partners for exports
are Canada, France, Spain, and Italy; principal partners for imports
are France, the Netherlands, Germany, and the United States.
Togo has more than 7,800 km (about 4,850 miles) of roads, about
one-fifth of which are paved. A northern-southern road system links
the port of Lome with Burkina Faso. A 537-km (about 334-miles)
railroad network that includes three northern-southern lines serves
the country.
Political History
According to historians most of the ethnic groups inhabiting Togo are
descended from peoples driven into the area during the 18th and 19th
centuries, except for the Ewe, who left the Niger River area for Togo
some time between the 11th and 16th centuries. During the 18th and
19th centuries, the area was under pressure from the Akwamu
Confederacy and the Ashanti Kingdom to the west and from the Kingdom
of Dahomey to the east.
Togo was a part of the Slave Coast, a primary area of European
slaving activities. Small slave posts were established in that region
in the 17th century, but most of the slave trade was carried on in
Dahomey (now Benin) and the Gold Coast (now Ghana).
The German protectorate of Togoland was established in 1884, when the
rulers of the region signed a treaty granting sovereignty to the
Germans. From 1887 to 1889, Germany, Great Britain, and France fixed
the territorial limits of the protectorate.
The Germans created the port of Lome and developed the resources of
the region. During August 1914, the first month of World War I, the
Germans surrendered the region after an invasion by French and
British forces.
In 1920 the final division of the area between the two countries took
place, and Lome and the entire coastline were assigned to French Togo
in exchange for an enlarged British territory in the interior. In
1922, the League of Nations granted both nations mandates over their
respective territories.
UN Plebiscite
On December 13, 1946, the United Nations (UN) granted France and
Great Britain trusteeships over Togo to supersede the mandates
established by the League of Nations. Because of a plebiscite held in
1956, the British territory became part of the Gold Coast and was
later incorporated into Ghana.
In another UN-supervised plebiscite in 1958, a majority of the votes
in the French territory was gained by the National Union Party, which
favoured complete independence. Sylvanus Olympio, head of the party,
became the premier. In February 1960, Olympio rejected the suggestion
advanced by President Kwame Nkrumah of Ghana that the two countries
be united. Togo achieved independence on April 27, 1960, and was
admitted to the UN in September.
President Olympio was assassinated in January 1963, during a military
coup. The army selected Nicolas Grunitzky to form a provisional
government, and he subsequently assumed the office of president. A
new constitution was approved by a national referendum. In January
1967, the army staged another coup, installing Gnassingbe Eyadema,
then a Lieutenant Colonel and the Army Chief of Staff, as head of
government.
The constitution was abrogated and the legislative body dissolved. In
April, Eyadema assumed the offices of President and Defence Minister.
A new party, the Togolese People's Assembly, was founded in 1969. In
1970, a plot to overthrow President Eyadema was foiled. In early
1975, a treaty between 46 developing nations and the European Union
was signed in Lome.
The Togolese president promulgated a new constitution in December
1979, under which he was unanimously re-elected to office. In January
1980, the 13th anniversary of his coup, he proclaimed the Third
Togolese Republic. France sent troops to Togo in September 1986 to
help suppress a coup attempt.
He was re-elected to another seven-year term in December 1986.
President Eyadema apparently bowed to popular pressure in August 1991
and yielded power to a transitional government, for democratic
elections that he won for another term. In subsequent months,
however, troops loyal to Eyadema repeatedly tried to overthrow the
interim regime.
President Eyadema's position as former Chairman of ECOWAS made him a
major political player in the West African sub-region. He is among
one of the longest serving political leaders in Africa and the world
and many political analysts credit him with his ability to create
stability in his country. This probably explains the reason why he
was elected as the Chairman of OAU.

Original_Search_Title: Indians Open Free Hospital At Hastings
Headline: Sierra Leone: Indians Open Free Hospital At Hastings
Body_Text:
Freetown — As part of its ongoing humanitarian assistance to Sierra
Leone, the Indian contingent of UNAMSIL has opened a Medical Aid Camp
at the Police Training School at Hasting.
The camp was inaugurated yesterday by the Vice President, Albert Joe
Demby.
The opening of the camp comes as a big relief to the civilian
population especially thousands of displaced persons in the area.
Thousands of civilians from Kossoh Town,Yams Farm and Devil Hole also
stand to benefit from the camp.
The camp will provide a wide range of medical facilities including
surgical dressing, gynaecology, ophthalmology, paediatric, dental and
a dispensary for free medicine.
Vice President Dr. Albert Demby said the opening of the camp at
Hastings could not have come at a better time as all the surrounding
villages are now occupied by vulnerable displaced people and refugee
returnees who require urgent medical attention.
"It is indeed no surprise that such a great gesture should be
undertaken by Indian medical personnel whose exemplary performance in
the country like in Daru, Mile 91 etc have helped in no small measure
to give hope to our traumatized people" the VP said.
Station Commander, Indian Aviation Unit, Hasting, Group Captain B. S.
Siwach said the camp is a special gesture to provide medical
facilities to the people of Sierra Leone.
" The best of Indian Armed Doctors will be here for the next three
days from 9am to 4:30pm to offer medical consultation, medicines and
treatment" Siwach said. Senior members of the NIBATT contingent at
the ceremony were the Commanding Officer of the Choithram Hospital,
Lt. Col. Dayanand and Commanding Officer of NIBATT 2 Col Kushal
Thakur.
This program was facilitated by three other units, the Mariners
Company, the Artillery Unit and the mechanise Company.
The Force Commander is expected to visit the camp.
The first patients to be treated were police detectives who suffered
from cramping hands and malaria.

Original_Search_Title: Fiscal Policy: Textile Manufacturers Still In The Woods
Headline: Nigeria: Fiscal Policy: Textile Manufacturers Still In The Woods
Body_Text:
Lagos — Despite the slash of various import duties on textile raw
materials in the tariff amendment and fiscal policy measures for year
2001 released by the federal government recently, operators in the
textile sub-sector of the economy are not happy. Their grouse is that
the measures fell short of their expectations, particularly as they
pertain to dumping of woven fabrics and second hand materials.
Although the Federal Government had, in a bid to improve the
industrial climate and stimulate higher capacity utilisation, and
provide the level playing field to investors, reduced import duties
on major inputs for manufacturing sector, the textile operators said
they expected higher duties on finished products than the government
wanted.
Going by the provisions of the new guidelines, textile raw materials,
whose duties were slashed, are synthetic mono-filament from 30
percent to 15 percent; cotton, not corded or combed from 30 percent
to five percent; jute or other textile bast fibres from 10 percent to
five percent.
Other inputs whose import duties were reduced are textile flock and
dust and will neps (H.S.code 5402. 1000-6900) from35 percent to 15
percent, and synthetic filament yam from 30 percent to 10 percent.
On the other hand, the federal government increased the duty on woven
fabrics from 35 percent to 45 percent, operators say that the 45
percent increment is not enough deterrent to smugglers who are having
field day at the nations porous borders in the east, north and
western parts of the country.
For instance, Mr. Ibukun Adeogun, the Senior Manager, (Corporate
affairs) of Churchgate group, the largest conglomerate in the
country, said that what the government gave with the right hand,
through import duty reductions, it took away with the blanket opening
of the borders to all manners of goods.
Although government removal of import prohibition is in line with the
principles of the world trade organisation regarding trade
liberalisation except gypsum whose importation will attract a duty of
65 percent.
Adeogun argues that, Nigeria signed the WTO principles of
liberalisation in a hurry. Specifically he argued that if it were not
the obsequious tendencies of the Abacha administration, which signed
the agreement because it was looking for international friends at the
time when the international community, ostracised Nigeria, the
authorities would have signed it too early. At least, the Government
ought to have waited until the country's infrastructure improves
before signing the WTO agreement.
The implication of the agreement is that Nigeria's trade policy has
opened the borders to well established foreign companies to compete
with local industries. He argued that since the government did not
effectively tackle the myriads of problems that weigh down the local
industries the competition is now skewed against operators of textile
industries.
Specifically, he complained that the textile companies are still
weighed down by the effects of sticky wages and deteriorating
infrastructure, the overall performance of the textile sector is
nothing to write home about as they are still weighed down by unsold
stock.
Other constraints, which contributed to the anaemic growth of the
sector, include high cost of inputs due to weakening naira, weak
purchasing power, unfavourable taxes and levies which favour
importation coupled with the predilection of Nigerians to go for
imported goods rather than made in Nigeria fabrics.
As the organised private sector, put it in their pre-budget 2001 memo
to the federal government, the long-standing constraints to
manufacturing activities have not abated. The sector is still
characterised by the applying effects of globalisation, arising,
principally, from unfair competitive pressures, triggered off by the
problem of smuggling and dumping.
Apart from that, the capacity utilisation of the industry is
currently estimated at below 30 percent.
Indeed, it was for these reason that the OPS comprising the
Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), the Nigerian Association
of Chambers of Commerce, Industry, Mines and Agriculture (NACCIMA)
and the Nigerian Employers Consultative Association (NECA) called on
the federal government to impose 100 percent duty on imported textile
and strictly enforce the ban on second hand clothing.
The OPS also advocated that the 50 percent import duty on polyster
staple fibre and polyster filament yarn be increased to 75 percent.
However, the government met the expectations of the operators half
way, for political and diplomatic reasons more than economic
expediency.
According to Mr. Adeogun, all the African Prints Manufactured by the
Aba textile mills, the United Nigerian textile mills Kaduna, Aswani
textiles, Afprints, are not selling because of the influx of foreign
brands.
While, the Aba textile mills suffer from the activities of importers
east of the Niger, the companies located in Kaduna, Kano and other
states suffer from the crushing weight of dumped products from the
Niger Republic, Chad and Cameroun. Commenting on the stiff
competition between foreign and local brands, he said that while the
made in Nigeria wrappers also known as African Prints matches their
foreign counterparts in terms of quality, the foreign brands under
cutting the local brands.
While the African Prints sale for an average of N460, the foreign
brands sale for N380, thus making it extremely difficult for Nigerian
companies to break even. But if the government had imposed 100
percent import duty as advocated by the OPS, it would have tided the
industry over pending the improvement of the nation's infrastructure
.
Accordingly, despite the reduction of the price of inputs, the
textile industry is not totally out of the woods. Unless the
government revisits the tariff and tax amendment measures, textile
operators will have to contend with the problem of unsold stock.
As it appears, the government favours the stance of the world trade
organisation at the expense of the textiles industries.
But the reason is not far fetched. To continue to gain international
confidence, Nigeria's trade policy is expected to emphasise
consistency, transparency and predictability.
The textile industries are therefore left to either device new ways
of survival or die.
But the only escape route market watchers say is for the industries
to explore the African Growth Opportunities Act (AGOA), passed by the
US government.
According to Chief (Mrs) Priscilla Kuye, President of the
Nigerian-American Chamber of Commerce, the textile companies should
come together to explore the opportunities.
In her words, "I know about 20 textile companies folded up, not too
long ago. But what I believe the textile companies should do, is that
they should organise themselves into co-operatives, so that they can
be formidable.
If they organise themselves, I think they will be able to take
advantage of the provisions of AGOA. I don't think one company can do
it."
The president, Institute of Directors of Nigeria shared the same
view.
According to him, although the government did not grant all that the
OPS recommended, tariff amendment is an on-going process.
He argued that Nigerian companies should warm up to the competition.
Barring the long-standing problems of the sector, the textile
industry should have been able to absorb the shocks of unfair
competition.

Original_Search_Title: For The Love of Christ
Headline: Nigeria: For The Love of Christ
Body_Text:
Lagos — The aged, the poor, the widows and the orphans in St.
Timothy's Catholic parish, Ojodu, a Lagos suburb, had no reason to
worry about what they would eat or wear on last Christmas day. The
Justice Development and Peace Commission and St. Vincent De Paul
Society took care of their needs.
On December 21, the societies distributed new clothings, live
chickens, rice, yams and toiletries to the needy at the church
premises along Otunba Street after they were sumptuously fed at a
get-together party three days earlier. The party began with a
thanksgiving mass at 6:30 p.m. followed with Christmas carols sang by
various church groups which included the senior and junior choirs,
Catholic Women's Organisation, the singing ministry, the block
rosary, the parish brigade and the Catholic Youths of Nigeria.
The idea of the get-together party was first mooted by Ethelbert
Ifeanyi Iwu, the priest in charge of the parish, early in the year.
It could still have remained a dream but for the generosity of his
friend whom he simply referred to as Mr. V. T. Iwu told Newswatch: "I
have been thinking of how or what to do to help the poor in my parish
until one of my friends came around. He refused to allow me mention
his name but I prefer to call him Mr. V. T. He gave me a large sum of
money and told me to do something for the poor and the widows in my
parish. So it was like a dream come true when he gave me the money."
The Catholic priest said he decided to spend all the money on the
poor in order to give them a sense of belonging during the Christmas
celebration. "I am happy that many people were sponsored to celebrate
the festivities for the love of Christ and they were very happy," he
said.
The parish priest said there would be a better arrangement next year
for the get-together for the poor. Said he: "I didn't even campaign
for it this year until I got the money. By the grace of God, I will
start in time next year. I will go to my friends and some of the
companies that produce the materials that would be given to the poor.
I got the money and bought those things this year. I will get the
materials and meet more people. The poor will have more things to
enjoy from my friends next year."
Apart from the Christmas party, Iwu said the parish was also working
on a poverty alleviation programme. He said the man who gave him
money for the party had earlier donated money which he used to start
a parish clinic. He had, in addition asked him to come up with a
project idea which would help widows in the parish.
"I am still preparing the proposal. Before the middle of next year,
we shall set up something permanent that will benefit the widows. We
have more plans for the widows, the poor, the aged and orphans in the
parish," he assured.

Original_Search_Title: "They Are Killing Us"
Headline: Nigeria: "They Are Killing Us"
Body_Text:
Lagos — William Oketa, chairman of the committee set up by the
Erovie community in Ozoro, Isoko north local government, is now very
sick.
Oketa, once energetic and an outspoken community activist, now looks
gaunt and feeds on drips and injections to keep life going. Newswatch
was told that Oketa's doctors could not identify the cause of the
strange ailment which has defied medical treatment.
But the villagers and some toxic experts have linked Oketa's poor
health to the alleged dumping of suspected substances into well 3,
Isoko deep field in June last year by Shell Petroleum Development
Company Limited, SPDC. About 1,200 litres of the waste substances
were said to have been injected into the land through the well before
the community stopped the project in July last year. But it appeared
the havoc they feared had already been done.
Oketa had contact with the substances in July last year when he was
given three samples of the waste which he later took to the Benin
Owena River Basin/University of Benin Joint analytical research
laboratory. The substances were found to be toxic. Thirty days after
doing that, he fell sick and his health has deteriorated ever since.
The toxic waste is now a major threat to the 50,000 inhabitants of
Erovie community in Ozoro. Their farmlands have been ravaged by the
chemical components of the dumped toxic waste and people are
reporting strange ailments.
Odhegolor Lucky Abikelegba, a biochemist and the national leader of
Erovie community in Ozoro, told Newswatch that between April and June
this year, about 93 people, including pregnant women and school-age
children who must have had direct or indirect contact with the dumped
waste had died. "Strange infections in Ozoro which doctors have
confirmed their existence here include vomiting, lung and skin
diseases, heart attack, dysentery and typhoid fever," he said.
Some Erovie people who are sick were said to have had indirect or
direct contact with the toxic waste through farming, fishing or
eating fruits or root- crops harvested from the areas allegedly
polluted by the waste. "I know of two members of our committee who
are having problems with their legs because of the toxic waste. The
cells of their legs have been killed by the substance," said
Abikelegba. Frederick Ogbo, the traditional ruler of Erovie
community, told the story of a family of five, which died recently
after eating yam tubers harvested from the polluted farmland. Ogbo
also said that a farmer from the community almost lost his sight
after washing his face with water from a polluted pond adding;
"pregnant women also had pre-mature babies."
Farming is the main occupation of Erovie and, indeed, the Isoko
people. This has been put on hold for now because of the alleged
pollution of their farmland. Ogbo said: "We have no other job than
farming. SPDC has polluted our soil and driven every farmer away from
our land. Many of us had farm there. I have six farms on that land,
many of us left our crops unharvested last year. None to my knowledge
has gone back to farm there till today. Herdsmen have lost a great
deal of their animals; fishermen and farmers are relocating."
The department of geology, University of Nigeria, Nsukka, UNN, which
conducted the environmental assessment of the waste samples on behalf
of Isoko north local government early last year had advised the
farmers to "halt further development of the land in the vicinity of
the dump in order to locate, isolate and comprehensively characterise
the waste and co-ordinate its removal and proper disposal in
accordance with existing environmental guidelines."
UNN also advised government to "conduct intensive and detailed
hydro-chemical and soil studies to identify and isolate areas of
potential soil and groundwater contamination" because the
environmental assessment of the dump showed clearly that it is toxic.
All the three samples which Emmanuel Edevor, chairman, Isoko north
local government, took to UNN for analysis were confirmed to be
toxic. The university said the concentrations of chemical parameters
in the analysed samples suggested that "a good number are enriched up
to and above the limits recommended for use." UNN analysis showed
that "the samples are toxic to both human beings/animals and plants."
It showed that some of the parameters tested such as lead (pd), zinc
(zn) and mercury (Hg) "are poisonous even at concentrations far below
that observed in the samples."
UNN said if a material containing these samples was dumped on any
part of Isoko land without adequate preparation of the site to
control the movement and spread of the leachate arising from the
material, it would constitute an environmental hazard. As such, it
said, "the soil and groundwater systems would be endangered. The
shallow groundwater system which is the major source of water to the
rural inhabitants, could be damaged beyond use. Such a damage could
be difficult, even expensive, to rectify."
UNN experts also analysed the chemical contents of the dumped
substance. After analysing the waste's electric conductivity which is
a measure of the concentration of chemical materials in the soil or
groundwater, the university said the values it obtained range from
11,650 us/cm to about 19,650 us/cm. "The normal soil conductivity in
the Isoko area ranges from less than 1,000 us/cm for sandy loams to
less than 2,000 us/cm for clay/salty soils. These natural values are
much less than the values obtained in the samples. In fact, the three
samples have conductivity values more than 10 times the normal soil
values and in this regard, the samples are polluted," the report
said. (see table for details of UNN report)
The university took particular note of the concentration levels of
sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, mercury and chlorides in the
samples tested and declared that soils with any concentration of
chlorides are "saline and are useless for any crop production." It
said the mercury chemicals it found in the tested samples, though in
small concentrations, "are usually toxic and poisonous even at very
small concentrations."
UNN study also found grease in the tested samples. "Because of low
solubility, grease separates from water adhering to the interior of
soil grains, pipes and tank walls. They thus reduce the productivity
of soils and even the biological treatability of the waste and
produce greasy sludge that is difficult to process and degrade," the
university said.
On the environmental impact of the waste dumped, the report said the
groundwater of Isoko community is vulnerable to pollution "unless
artificially protected." The university said: "Relatively fast
groundwater movement is also suggested by the sandy nature of the top
soil which implies good permeability. Any land-based pollution
sources, is thus a serious threat to the quality of the groundwater
in Isoko area. Dug wells, especially those in individual homes used
for domestic supplies would be the first to be contaminated and
subsequently polluted."
The findings of UNN tally somewhat with the test results obtained by
Erovie community from the University of Benin, UNIBEN. The
university, in a joint report with the Benin-Owena River Basin, said
the waste contained heavy chemical metals which are inimical to human
beings, animals and plants, (see box).
The test result of UNN and UNIBEN confirmed the findings of the
committee constituted, August 4, last year, by James Ibori, Delta
State governor, to look into the toxic issue. The five-man committee
which was headed by L. T. Popo, acknowledged "the presence of heavy
metals at above acceptable limits and the unusually high
concentration of ions which make the substance to be toxic." The
committee therefore warned that "if these substances were to
infiltrate into the underground water or acquifer, it would have
serious environmental and health implications."
Up till today, SPDC has not come out to reveal how the toxic waste
got to Isoko land. But the Popo-led committee fingered Dowell
Schlumberger, a prominent agent of SPDC. The committee also said that
the various ailments presented by the people could be "directly
attributed to the effect of contact with the substances."
But the Federal Ministry of Environment said in its "full and final
report" published in The Guardian of October 30, that the people's
ailments were not associated with the waste. "A medical appraisal of
the site and detailed medical review of seven members of the
community who had close contact with the samples was carried out by
Scotchville Industrial Consortium, Warri. The medical report
submitted, indicated no conclusive evidence to link the suspected
harmful substances with any real or perceived negative health impact
on the Ozoro community or its environs," said Imeh Okopido, minister
of state for environment.
Those on which medical examinations were carried out following
complaints of strange ailments were A. Cheranu, M. Ebiri, G. Egelbe,
J. Atagbagba, Joseph Adakpo and William Oketa. Most of the people,
especially Adakpa and Oketa are at the throes of death. The ministry
insisted that the dumped wastes were not toxic. Okopido said that the
"substances had no obvious significant harmful impact on human and
the immediate environment," because they were injected "at a depth of
5,300-5,400 ft. while the fresh water depth in Delta is 1,560 ft
beyond which is brine."
"Considering the depth at which the substances were injected," says
Okopido, "it is most unlikely that the injected substances would have
any negative effect on the flora, fauna and acquirer" which is the
water level. He said the samples of the injected substances were
tested by 13 science-based firms. They include Jawura Environmental
Services Limited, FM Environmental Reference Laboratory, Lagos, Shell
Petroleum Development Company, Laladson Limited, Jebeco Envirotech
Limited, and Scotchville Industrial Consortium, Warri.
There is already a raging controversy on the membership of the joint
investigative panel set up by the federal government to probe the
alleged dumping of the toxic substances in Ozoro.
Names of Ozoro representatives on the panel, were given as Emmanuel
Edevor, chairman, Isoko north local government area; Christopher
Imoga, an Ozoro high chief, Abikelegba, national leader of Erovie
community in Ozoro and Fred Obe, president-general, Isoko National
Youth Movement. All the people were surprised to find their names on
the list of the panel.
Said Edevor: "There was no day I was invited to the meeting of the
panel. Look at the list of firms whose reports were considered by the
ministry in deciding this case, my report from UNN is not there. I
only saw my name in the publication. I did not participate in any way
in the panel." Said Abikelegba: "I knew nothing about the meetings of
the panel until they published their contractors' findings as the
full and final results." Imoga: "I was not party to the decision.
Shell may have bribed some people to kill us and our children." Obe:
"Nobody in his right senses could have said I was party to that
report. I think what the ministry was saying was that I was present
when it gave samples of the substances to interested members of the
public to test and report to it."
Angered by the seeming helplessness of Erovie people in the face of
the ministry's description of its report as "full and final," Felix
Morka, executive director of the Social and Economic Rights Action
Centre, SERAC, a non- governmental organisation, has taken up the
fight on behalf of the people. At a press conference in Lagos,
December 14, he disagreed with the conclusion of the panel that "no
evidence had yet been found of any immediate or discernible threat to
lives and property."
Said Morka: "This conclusion was reached without a full, and careful
review of all available evidence so far developed on the matter."
Morka cited a laboratory test carried out jointly by the Benin-Owena
River Basin and the University of Benin, saying the result showed
that the injected substances contained very high and dangerous levels
of toxicity. He said another scientific analysis of the substances
carried out by the department of geology, University of Nigeria,
Nsukka, similarly showed that the substances injected by Shell into
well 3, Ibo bush was highly toxic. "Both tests confirmed that the
substances contained dangerous high levels of heavy metals and that
most of the parameters were far in excess of DPR/FEPA limits."
The tests further confirmed that the substances contained unnatural
hydrocarbon and a very high degree of conductivity."
Morka alleged that tests carried out by the ministry were sponsored
by Shell to escape being indicted. "Curiously enough, little weight
was given by the panel and the federal ministry of environment to
aspects of another Shell- sponsored tests conducted by Peak
Engineering which concluded on page seven paragraph four of its
report that "the re-injection project at Ibo bush, Ozoro is likely to
remain environmentally friendly if spills of materials are completely
avoided," Morka observed.
He wondered why the panel conveniently ignored the "veiled warning
contained in that report of the inherent danger to human life and
environment." Morka then called on President Olusegun Obasanjo to
reject the panel's conclusion and to institute an independent
judicial inquiry into the alleged toxic waste dump by Shell.
The realities in Ozoro and the palpable fears and anxieties expressed
by the people over the alleged toxic waste dump in their community
clearly makes the need for an independent inquiry necessary. There is
the urgent need now to determine the environmental impact of the
substances injected into Ibo bush.
When Newswatch visited the oil well into which the substances were
allegedly injected last week, most economic trees around the well
appeared, affected by the substances. For instance, the mango tree
close to the well reportedly bore no fruit this year. Banana stalks
bore withering seeds and there appeared to be some obvious problems
with the ecology of the place.
The problems now being experienced by the people do not allow them to
trust the government's report that the suspected harmful substances
were injected very deep into the ground. "It's all a lie. They are
saying that to make us feel safe. At least some of us can feel that
way even if it meant that the future of our children is doomed.
Earthquake often brings up anything hidden underground. Shell knows
that," Imoga said.
Virtually all ailments in the community are being attributed to the
suspected harmful substances. An Ozoro-based medical doctor who only
spoke to Newswatch on condition of anonymity, blamed SPDC for what
the people are passing through now. "The people have sufficient
reasons to believe that SPDC is out to kill them. One, SPDC violated
known procedures for re-claiming an oil well they abandoned many
years ago," he said. He said SPDC usually obtains the consent of
immediate land-owners before entering their land for oil exploration.
"In this case, SPDC sidetracked Erovie people who owned the land they
were entering. The people are saying that if SPDC had come to Ozoro
to do clean business, they would have contacted the right people, the
Erovie community. Again, the fears of the people have been upheld by
the reports of UNN and UNIBEN. Even if the suspected substances have
no immediate human and environmental effects, the state of fears and
anxieties which the people now live in has thrown up psychosomatic
problems," the doctor said. He added: "people are reporting strange
ailments not totally because of the problems emitted by the
substances but because of the great injury which the breach of trust
by SPDC had inflicted on the people's minds. On the increase now in
the community are skin and heart diseases, typhoid fever and
dysentery. Yes, some people have died. The only psychotherapy which I
know of is immediate evacuation of the suspected substances and
offering of due apology to the people in form of compensation."
Problems started in June last year when members of Erovie community
in Ozoro found SPDC operating on their land without obtaining their
consent. Erovie, a community of 50,000 people is said to be a
separate entity in Ozoro clan. "We sought to know how they came in
there in defiance of section 47, subsection 2(m) of our constitution
and their own policy which we stoutly relied on. We also sought to
know what manner of work they were to do in a supposedly dry well. We
were, however, informed by Ozoro clan community organisation leaders
that SPDC met with them and sought permission to re-enter our Erovie
community," Joseph Adakpo, chairman, Erovie community told Newswatch.
Surprised by the breach of protocol by SPDC, Adakpo said the company
was asked to suspend operation "pending the determination of the true
nature of the chemicals being injected into the well. But within 24
hours, to our greatest surprise and consternation, the company went
to the site and dismantled their equipment-hastily and started
packing away hurriedly. On hearing this, we raised an alarm and the
vehicle (truck) with registration number AA 997 KLK that came to
remove the two giant tanks being their last trip was abandoned as
they fled." At the site when Newswatch visited last week were a truck
and the two tanks. Forty loads of such tanks were said to have been
injected into the area before the community heard about the
activities.
The community, through its leaders, quickly reported the development
to the presidency and the National Assembly. Based on the people's
outcry, the federal ministry of environment set up a joint
investigation panel, last year. The panel headed by Ikemefuna
Onyekwelu, head of the Lagos office of the ministry, released its
preliminary report in April this year. The panel said then that the
substances had no "any immediate or discernible threat to lives and
property."
Abikelegba said last week that federal government was being misled on
this matter by Onyekwelu who had come in April to say the substances
were non-toxic. "We believe that the honourable minister was misled
by information and manipulated results presented to him by the panel
headed by Onyekwelu who did not hear from us and did not collect any
analytical result from us before informing the honourable minister of
state of their findings," Abikelegba said.
SPDC said in Lagos last week that it stood by the report of
Onyekwelu-led panel on the matter. Bisi Ojediran, head, corporate
affairs, Shell Lagos told Newswatch on phone: "We stand by the
ministry's report. We believe it is credible." He said what was being
disposed of by SPDC in Ibo bush were drilling muds and not toxic
substances as alleged by the community.
On why a dried oil well in Isoko was considered for such exercise,
Ojediran said the well 3, Ibo bush, was nearest to where the mud was
generated. "Proximity," he said, "informed the choice of the well for
the exercise."
In any case, Isoko people do not believe any explanation given by
SPDC. Most of them are standing firm on the laboratory findings of
UNN and UNIBEN. "I disagree that it is not toxic substance. Federal
government is being fed by SPDC as their budgets depend on SPDC
revenue that is why they have supported the company to say the
substance is not toxic. We stand by UNN and UNIBEN decisions and we
are depending on the media and the court of law to get justice in
this case," said Patrick Egerueme Ogblioni, an Ozoro high chief.

Original_Search_Title: Ringo's Long Hard Road To Stardom
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ringo's Long Hard Road To Stardom
Body_Text:
Harare — On the stage, he is the embodiment of enchantment and off it
he is polite, soft-spoken and an unrepentant charmer.
Born Sindile Brian Madlingozi 36 years ago in Gugulethu township just
outside Cape Town, South Africa, he has travelled the hard road to
musical stardom.
He has traversed through the jungle of imitating such Reggae gurus as
the late Bob Marley and has also flirted with musical styles of the
R& clique.
After years in the musical wilderness, the artist now popularly known
as Ringo met Chris Black - well, a producer of Island Records and
Marley's music, who advised him to sing in his mother tongue, Xhosa.
"I had tried to impress Blackwell with my Reggae songs so when he
told me to sing in my language and stick to my culture I thought he
was playing a cruel joke on me," Madlingozi told the Financial
Gazette in Harare last week.
"Eventually I saw the light and took Blackwell's advice. In a very
big way he was right because as soon as I started singing in my own
language about issues I was more conversant with, my popularity
soared." The artist, who laughs and grins a lot throughout the
interview, adds: "On reflection now, I realise I could have sounded
quite funny singing about Harlem and Jamaica which I didn't even
know. I wasn't even sure the things I was singing about were the way
I assumed they were."
Madlingozi was in the capital to launch his latest album Buyisa and
also to perform at two end-of-year shows with local music maestro
Oliver Mtukudzi. The two artists have a duet on the new album: a
re-mix of Madlingozi's Into Yam.
He grins widely as he talks fondly of growing up in Cape Town,
kissing his mother goodbye each morning before leaving for school and
going about his life without a care.
"Cape Town that time was very quiet and even when I went to school at
times without anything in my lunch box, I never for once thought
things in the country were not stable. It was only after my Matric
and I began toying with the idea of going to university that my
political awareness was raised," he said.
He said although at that early stage his passion was music, he still
felt that he had to have a good education but eventually that was
overtaken by his love for music. He joined up with friends and came
up with a band called Peto, which is township lingua for friend.
By 1986 he was still trying to find his feet and he says his road to
fame was via participation in a road show that saw him gain
recognition. Soon after, he formed another band, Gecko Moon.
Madlingozi began to churn out sizzling hits starting off with the
album Vukani. From then on the road became smoother with his lyrical
prowess showing in the production of Sondelani, an album that was
made popular by the song Sondela.
And more recently he unleashed on the unsuspecting entertainment
world Into Yam, a gem of a song. This album has re-mixes of songs
like Khaya and Isipithi Pithi. Now with the album Buyisa he has
firmly planted his feet on the world of music giants.
He says his music, which focuses mostly on love, has done very well
in Africa and more especially in southern Africa.
"In five years' time I see my music on a higher level. I will also
continue to explore working with other musicians like I have just
done with Oliver (Mtukudzi). Collaboration with other artists is
enriching because it produces a unique product," says Madlingozi.
He says the idea of doing a duet with Mtukudzi was something that
just came up without any prior consideration. He said Mtukudzi was in
Johannesburg recently for just eight hours and within those few hours
they re-worked Into Yam.
"Oliver is amazing. With his direction we came up with a beautiful
piece of music and he also worked with my backing female vocalists
for the Shona version of the song," says the South African artist.
While he waxes lyrical about his music, mother, father, his
friendship with Mtukudzi and food, Madlingozi however wants to remain
an enigma when it comes to his personal life.
"There are things that must remain within me. There is something that
I call a personal cabinet where some things about me remain locked
up," he says with a boyish grin.
Using his wicked sense of humour, the artist evades any question that
seems to point towards his personal life and speaks of no special
woman in his life or anything outside show business.
As he prepares to jump onto the stage with Mtukudzi to perform the
famous duet, he looks around him and with his trademark grin says
that his vocation is to make people happy and to churn out the kind
of music that people can relate to.

Original_Search_Title: Troop to Receive Christmas Gifts
Headline: South Africa: Troop to Receive Christmas Gifts
Body_Text:
Even though they won't enjoy Christmas in the comfort of their homes with their families, the South African National Defence Force (SANDF) members deployed in Burundi and the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) will definitely welcome the special treat coming their way.
They will be presented with Christmas gifts collectively worth R31 500 on Dec. 27.
The SANDF contributed R27 500 towards the cost of the 900 gifts and Spar Supermarket sponsored the balance.
The gifts consist of two packs of biscuits, potato fries, fruit cocktail, two packs of sweets, biltong, a sports drink and two packs of peanuts.
Speaking in Thaba Tshwane near Pretoria today, SANDF Chief Siphiwe Nyanda said the aim of sending the gifts was to illustrate that 'the top structure' cared for them.
'We want to take this opportunity to hand over a small token of our appreciation to the deployed soldiers,' he said.
About 700 South African soldiers are deployed in Burundi's capital of Bujumbura to protect returning exiled Hutu leaders, while another 100 troops are in the DRC as part of the United Nation (UN) peacekeeping mission.
Last month defence minister Mosiuoa Lekota visited the troops in both countries and told them that their sacrifices would be recorded in South African history like those of freedom fighters such as former president Nelson Mandela.
'You are walking on the steps of great leaders of the country by making sacrifices that will benefit many men, women and children you do not even know,' he said.

Original_Search_Title: Troop to Receive Xmas Gifts
Headline: South Africa: Troop to Receive Xmas Gifts
Body_Text:
Pretoria — Even though they won't enjoy Christmas in the comfort of their homes
with their families, the South African National Defence Force (SANDF)
members deployed in Burundi and the Democratic Republic of Congo
(DRC) will definitely welcome the special treat coming their way.
They will be presented with Christmas gifts collectively worth R31
500 on 27 December.
The SANDF contributed R27 500 towards the cost of the 900 gifts and
Spar Supermarket sponsored the balance.
The gifts consist of two packs of biscuits, potato fries, fruit
cocktail, two packs of sweets, biltong, a sports drink and two packs
of peanuts.
Speaking in Thaba Tshwane near Pretoria today, SANDF Chief Siphiwe
Nyanda said the aim of sending the gifts was to illustrate that 'the
top structure' cared for them.
'We want to take this opportunity to hand over a small token of our
appreciation to the deployed soldiers,' he said.
About 700 South African soldiers are deployed in Burundi's capital of
Bujumbura to protect returning exiled Hutu leaders, while another 100
troops are in the DRC as part of the United Nation (UN) peacekeeping
mission.
Last month defence minister Mosiuoa Lekota visited the troops in both
countries and told them that their sacrifices would be recorded in
South African history like those of freedom fighters such as former
president Nelson Mandela.
'You are walking on the steps of great leaders of the country by
making sacrifices that will benefit many men, women and children you
do not even know,' he said.

Original_Search_Title: Gwazantini May Be Remanded Too Long
Headline: Malawi: Gwazantini May Be Remanded Too Long
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Chief State Advocate has not ruled out a possibility that Alice
Gwazantini, on allegations that she scalded her maid to death, will
remain in remand "too long" saying investigations take "too long"
sometimes.
Mc Lean Kamwambe said some remandees have had to wait for over nine
years to have their homicide cases heard. "She [Mrs Alice Gwazantini]
is only been in prison for a couple of months [Since August this
year]," he said.
But Civil Liberties Committee Executive Director Emmie Chanika
yesterday said Gwazantini is there "accidentally."
"It's not a thing she planned for and there is no need to say that
she can stay too long. They should speed up their investigations as
soon as possible to protect her rights" she said.
Investigations into the case in which the Namiwawa-based woman, who
allegedly doused hot water on 13-year-old Ntchayi Jakisoni after she
allegedly ate her boss's sweet potato, is progressing at a snail's
pace following the transfer of a police officer who was handling the
case.
The Chief State Advocate said that the case will only resume after
another police officer has been identified.
"We do not want to do our investigations in a hurry. We want this
case to be properly investigated," he said.
Kamwambe said the state had failed to immediately appoint another
person to handle the case.
Chanika, however, failed to make out why the policeman handling a
sensitive story could be transfered just like this.
Henry Phoya, lawyer for Gwazantini refused to comment arguing the
matter is in court.
Gwazantini, who was arrested mid-August this year was denied bail by
Justice Frank Kapanda on the grounds that her offence was not
bailable.

Original_Search_Title: Kyogero Herb Available As Soap
Headline: Uganda: Kyogero Herb Available As Soap
Body_Text:
Mothers who have never bathed their children with kyogero to remove rashes have been rescued
Many career women do not have time to go on a hunt for wild plants to give their children a herbal bath.
Those who even have a chance of using the herbs (known as kyogero in Luganda) get them from elderly relatives in the villages.
However, there are many women who do not even know what kyogero looks like, which is why many children have skin diseases.
The good news is that mothers may not have to worry about going into the wilderness in search of the kyogero, because a group of women from Rubaga North constituency have brought it to their doorstep.
Their innovation involves mixing ordinary soap with the herbs, so that your child can enjoy the healthy herbal bath. Do not expect anything with a sweet scent, because the herbal soap is not artificially scented.
At the Nkalubo exhibition, which took place at Mengo Senior Secondary School football ground recently, these women were among of the most fascinating exhibitors.
The exhibition organised by the Nkalubo programme was an initiative of Rubaga North LCV woman councillor, Florence Bagunywa Nkalubo. The programme has trained several groups of women and November 17, was the day to find out what different women groups had to offer.
Though the packaging of the herbal extractions was not appealing, the products drew the attention of those who visited the exhibition.
Did you know that sweet potato leaves are good for your hair? The extract from the leaves clears dandruff and can be used as a hair dye. The women mix the extract from the potato leaves and that from the leaves of the Rosemary tree to make shampoo.
They make other herbal products like cleansers, hand lotions and petroleum jelly for the body. The products are also said to get rid of bad body odours and pimples.
Harriet Kyambadde, a nurse, testifies of the quality of the herbal products.
"My baby uses their soap," she says. "I have no time to look for the kyogero. It is nowhere to be seen in Kampala. Whenever I realise that the soap is about to get finished, I ask for another piece," she adds with a smile.
"Unlike my other three children, this baby's skin has not been problematic. With the other children, most of the time, I would use diprosone ointment, because their skins would have irritations, but this time I haven't had any difficulties," Kyambadde assures other mothers.
To make the herbal soap, the women use the ordinary soap on the market. They boil a mixture of herbs, squeeze the liquid out of them and sieve the mixture. They soak the soap in this mixture, then sun dry it.
They also make petroleum jelly from honey and the cleanser is from mint grass.
Different women bring different ingredients of what is needed to make the item for the day. Upon completion, they sell the products to other women groups. Their items are as cheap as sh500.
The women took to herbal extraction after attending a one-day workshop conducted by Dr. Grace Nambatya Kyeune, who is undertaking research on herbal medicine.
Dr. Nambatya, a chemist and researcher with the Ministry of Health, says:
"Most of the herbs work as body cleansers. If people took them seriously, they wouldn't fall sick. It is because people lack knowledge about cleansers and they don't know the intricate value of herbs," she said.
"The main objective for the Nkalubo Programme is to enhance development for the women. I wanted to create awareness about what is happening in Rubaga North," said councillor Nkalubo, of the highly publicised event.
"What the Nkalubo Programme lacks today is substantial funding to be able to increase on the already lined up women and youth programmes. The programme has all the qualities of becoming an intermediary agency and should therefore be given that opportunity," she appealed.

Original_Search_Title: Witch Weed Devastates Cereal
Headline: Uganda: Witch Weed Devastates Cereal
Body_Text:
STRIGA (witch weed), one of the notorious weeds that affects cereals has affected virtually all sub-counties in Adjumani district raising fears of poor harvest and famine.
District officials say the weed has grown in almost every field of sorghum and maize affecting their growth and productivity. Efforts to control the weed, which mostly grows in arid and semi-arid regions have been hampered by lack of herbicides, officials added.
"The weed is proving to be a serious menace to us," said Ben Anyama, chairman of the district local council multi-purpose committee.
"It grows in every garden of sorghum and is very difficult to control. The farmers have no way of controlling it and we have received no assistance from the ministry of agriculture, despite several appeals," he added.
Anyama said the most affected sub-counties are Ciforo, Pakelle and Dzaipi, all in East Moyo constituency. Other areas affected include Mijalle village in Adropi sub-county and parts of Pakelle sub-county.
Adjumani district, famous for sorghum and sim sim production. The district also grows cassava, maize, sweet potatoes and vegetables.
But striga, locally known as ava, is affecting the production of cereals, particularly sorghum, one of the staple foods in the district.
"We are contemplating giving up sorghum growing unless government comes to our rescue. We may only concentrate on sim sim but this is also affected by other weeds. It is a big problem for us," said Joseph Waram, a refugee in Magburu settlement, Ciforo sub-county. The district speaker, Angela Bangi said the weed has caused fears of famine outbreak in the district.
"There might be a serious food shortage unless we receive assistance from the central government. Our budget is quite small," she explained.
Bangi said that a recent study commissioned by the district to study the impact of the weed on cereal production recommended that farmers diversify crop production.
