Original_Search_Title: Nation Has Capacity to Produce Surplus Grain But.....
Headline: Zimbabwe: Nation Has Capacity to Produce Surplus Grain But.....
Body_Text:
Bulawayo — Zimbabwe has the capacity to produce surplus grain in the current
season because the whole country is expected to have normal rains in
the first half of the season from October to December with some areas
in the northern districts of the country expected to have
above-normal rains, the Famine Early Warning System says.
There are, however, a number of factors that could affect this. One
of them is that farmers who normally buy inputs after selling their
harvest may not be able to afford the inputs as there was little or
no surplus to sell last season.
FEWS says input costs have increased from about $800 per bag of
fertiliser to $1 800 since last year. But the government has
announced new price controls which will see the price of fertiliser
reduced by 35 percent.
FEWS says some farmers were unable to repay their input credit from
last season to the Grain Marketing Board. They will therefore not
receive inputs this season. These farmers are not able to buy them
without credit. Worse still, financial institutions are charging
prohibitively high interest rates for agricultural inputs.
Another problem is that productive farmers who normally produce a
surplus are objecting to the government policy requiring them to sell
their maize to the GMB. Farmers say the GMB price is too low and
object to having to pay transport costs if they deliver the maize
through GMB collection points.
They also have to pay high transport costs for GMB inputs. This
arrangement is less profitable than selling maize through private
buyers, who not only provide cash at a farm gate price higher than
that offered by the GMB, but also help transport the inputs to the
farm. As a result of this policy, some farmers have indicated that
they will be reducing the hectarage under maize this season.
FEWS says in the maize-growing districts of Murehwa and Mutoko, for
example, farmers disgruntled by the maize marketing system have
indicated that they will be expanding into sweet potatoes and
vegetables while those in Guruve will switch to cotton. These crops
are expected to have higher returns than maize.
Farmers have also complained that the GMB input credit scheme could
exclude less well-off farmers because the screening criteria is based
on assets held by the farmer, such as ownership of draft animals, a
good house, and farm implements.
Production by commercial farmers is expected to drop by 40 percent
due to disruptions and violence on the farms. Some 1 183 farms have
been affected by work stoppages and 350 farms have shut down
completely.
FEWS says large-scale commercial farmers in Midlands, Mashonaland
Central and West Provinces have been prevented from establishing an
early irrigated maize crop that could have been harvested early,
artificially dried, and sold early in the season to mitigate
shortages.
According to the World Food Programme about 706 000 people require
food aid. This represents as much as 31 percent of the population in
some districts such as Mberengwa and Zvishavane in the Midlands;
Binga, Hwange and Tsholotsho in Matebeleland North; all the districts
of Matebeleland South; all the districts of Masvingo except Gutu;
Kariba in Mashonaland West; Lower Muzarabani, Lower Guruve and Mount
Darwin in Mashonaland Central; and Chipinge in Manicaland. While
donors have said they are ready to pour in food aid, the government
has ruled that it wil be the sole distributor of food aid arguing
that charity organisations have a political agenda and could use food
aid to campaign for the opposition.

Original_Search_Title: The Magic of the Great Nowhere
Headline: South Africa: The Magic of the Great Nowhere
Body_Text:
There's a baby grand piano laden with candelabra and kitsch in a  magical dining room in the middle of the great Karoo. Just when you thought you couldn't face another Ultra City, and there's no way you can turn back, because you've travelled 700km and there's still 700km to go before you  hit
Clifton, another drab dorp - but one with a difference - is  suddenly framed by the windscreen.
This time there's a seedy Caltex garage, and one or two abandoned shops  on the side of the road - but, hey, there's also a rather subtle sign  saying
"No 3, Darling Street", promising food and a bed. So you decide, what  the hell, and turn right, left, and right again, and in three minutes or so  you feel a bit like Alice when she fell through the hole and landed in
Wonderland.
Hanover is the tiniest of dorps in the heart of the country. It clings  for dear life to the N1 that roars past it in cruel oblivion. And yet,  right here, Mark Banks (he of laugh-a-minute fame), Derek Vaughan, a retired bassoonist, and one-time theatre design specialist Lawrence Huckstep  have pooled their considerable talents to create a unique restaurant and B&B establishment: "No. 3, Darling Street".
Vaughan discovered Hanover in 1991 when sheltering from a Karoo  dust-storm.
Love was instantaneous, and the desire to escape the rat race  irresistible.
So, with his current partners, he established a successful Karoo-style  guest house whose camp, kitsch outrageous touches soon became de rigueur with  a certain class of traveller for whom getting there is as much fun as the destination.
Travellers enter a kind of time-warp. The ochre-coloured restaurant,  its stoep bordering the pavement of a broad gravel road, beckons with  strains of
1950s smooch music (when last did you hear Nat King Cole sing Smoke  gets in your eyes?), gaudy fairy lights, and a bar full of Africana and  colonial memorabilia. Guests can quaff a beer or sip a cocktail under a  tinsel-framed
"Die Bou van 'n Nasie" and a photograph of the 1947 royal visit.  Someone in the house is, clearly, a compulsive junk-shop rummager. No Whistle Stop  or
Select highway cafÃ© this, not a hint of the bland, predictable  franchise culture that has colonised our national routes.
The dining room is extravagantly lit with an array of candles. Wooden surfaces gleam, and beyond the open French doors, a garden glows
("Disneyland in the Karoo," explains Huckstep). Lucas Zozi is a waiter  with a suave style and wide smile. There's a set menu. "People don't want to  make decisions when they get here; we take over. They can take a walk to the koppie or flop on to their beds until we serve them dinner."
Travellers can savour, as we did, the tangy delights of a pumpkin and  orange soup, 22 (yes!) snails in butter and lemon, fall-off-the-bone Karoo  lamb knuckles, creamy leeks that smell and taste like leeks used to  (remember?), crunchy beans and bacon - and the best potato croquettes with the  faintest whiff of nutmeg this side of Paris. And the Cape brandy tart was as  sweet as it should be, though not quite syrupy enough.
The food - in keeping with the dÃ©cor - is fabulous: a blend of  robust country fare and clever urban touches, organic to the point of being orgasmic. "I'm in charge of parks and recreation, and that includes the veggie garden. Even though I'd never before grown anything apart from  fat," says Banks, who goes on to explain, "I've always lived 'between' -  never quite been 'there', so I live here two weeks each month because there's nothing - and I can read all of Charles Dickens."
People come here for good food, good conversation, but also the  cleanest air in the country, and a million stars - thus experiencing something of  what
Olive Schreiner did in the seven years she lived here with husband  Cron. And when you hit the road the next day, together with the bottles of  home-made greengage and brandy jam and pickled onions, and the fruit-shaped  candles made by the tannie in the neighbouring dorp, you'll take with you  memories of stars like you've never seen before, and fairy lights, and magic in  the middle of the great nowhere that is the Karoo.

Original_Search_Title: The Village Concept
Headline: Zambia: The Village Concept
Body_Text:
Lusaka — THE Village Concept is emerging as the cornerstone of the campaign
platform of the Heritage Party. And if General Miyanda takes reins of
power the vision could radically transfigure the face of Zambia. It
promises to alter the balance of economic power from towns to rural
areas.
We have good reasons for our confidence. The village concept spells a
new economic paradigm, a vital new avenue towards national
prosperity.
Zambia lives there, about 60 per cent of us, in hundreds of thousands
of villages. The IMF and World Bank have for more than 20 years
stage-managed our economy. Their tight conditionalities and
benchmarks successfully converted us from a mildly surplus nation in
the 1970s to a Highly Indebted Poor Country today. True, corruption
added its sad woes.
In the light of our demise General Miyanda's innovative village
concept must be welcomed by us all. I see four primary strands to it.
First, the vision sees villages not merely as cultural units, but as
economic entities. Here individuals engage in production, but as
members of communities criss-crossed by family and clan ties. This
indigenous relational matrix must at long last be applied towards
economic ends. Don't we admire our Asian friends for their
unflinching solidarity? And see what it does for them? Heritage plans
to arouse villagers to start where they are, with what they have and
what they know to become shapers of their own welfare and destiny. A
Heritage government will not be 'the finger that feeds you,' but big
brother who coaches you how to fish.
My early childhood in the Luapula valley engraved two images on my
psyche that are germane to this. First in the 1950s every family in
our village owned ubutala (a grain stall). Here we stored amale
(millet), amasaka (sorghum) and sometimes kapesula (sun hardened
cassava). Each home also boasted ulwino.
This was a raised pole and bamboo platform often above the fireplace.
Here mother preserved fish, meat, vegetables, nuts and other types of
dried relish. In short households took charge of their food security
and nutritional needs. This is critical. The independence era shows
that entrusting food security to Namboard or a Food Reserve Agency is
very risky. Buying agents cheat on peasants with low prices. Or grain
rots from rain while big bwanas clamour for ncekelako (commissions)
on American imports. They export grain, while the nameless masses
languish in hunger.
The village concept by-passes both donor and corruption syndromes.
The Heritage Party stands for an empowering ethos. Ordinary Zambians
must once again take control of their nutritional status through
family centred food security.
Second, The Heritage Party is saying to us abena kalale (urban
dwellers), "it is time to retrace your roots and get excited about
it. Go and reclaim a slice of your motherland." This will not be the
heartless order to 'Go Back to the Land' or the coerced village
regrouping campaign. But the more enlightened among us already see
the chance to acquire land under our headmen and chiefs. It is part
of our heritage. Like the jubilee idea of the Old Testament, we may
return to our fathers' lands and so break free from years of debt and
deprivation.
Third, the village concept carries exciting spin-off effects. Among
these are reversing the rural-urban drift. The reason is simple. When
aba mukalale find new joy in their villages we shall gladly transfer
our skills, assets and technology.
An intercessor friend Margie, is a University of Zambia graduate. She
has carved a respectable living in her village not far from Chipata.
It can be done. It must be done. Such a reversal of the brain drain
will inevitably accelerate the productive capacity of rural areas.
The potential is huge.
Fourth, the village concept is dynamic and transferable. Villagers
may seem to be 'uneducated'. But ignorant? No! They display a natural
instinct for key biblical ideals. One such is the principle of
'seed.' Grandmother knew not to eat all the crop. She always selected
the best millet, groundnuts or pumpkin seed and set it aside for the
next season. She kept the future in view. 'Tabuca limo,' she would
say (or as the English put it 'save some for a rainy day').
Urbanites may translate this village concept into the idea of saving.
It means refusing to spend all the money as soon as one gets it.
Above all it entails refusing to be locked up into perpetual debt.
Put crudely the village concept undercuts the miners' pa sate (month
end) disease'. As Copperbelters know, when a miner walks out of a pay
line, lenders are at the gate. Any kwachas left over get drowned in
chibuku or mosi. Arriving at home, his irate wife ends the night out
on a hospital bed. My wife once worked in Konkola mine hospital. She
remarks, 'If you'd forgotten the day of the month, the line of
assault cases soon reminded you.' A stereotype?
Maybe. But the curse of rising debt, mixed priorities and pay packets
that refuse to stretch over the month afflict us all urbanites. It's
time for change!
How do we soar above all this? The village concept holds the clue. It
promises a new urban culture of financial thrift, prudence and
sufficiency.
Grandfather wisely saved seed and planted it, in endless cycles. We
too may save and plant our financial assets. The Heritage Government
will cultivate a culture of saving rather than one of borrowing. It
is not loans but savings that will build personal and national
assets. Basic to the village model also is the idea of individual
enterprise couched in community accountability. It is already being
tested. A Canadian friend of mine, Mike McLoughlin, set up Christian
Enterprise Trust of Zambia (CETZAM) five years ago. The British
government thankfully invested $3m dollars in it.
Individuals get loans but only as members of a community, a trust
bank.
They are empowered with business skills. Character too is reinforced.
The results are startling. For the first time in Zambia's financial
history, CETZAM sees 97 per cent loan recoveries.
The village concept means giving Zambians personalised economic
power.
Family food and nutritional security is the first battle line against
poverty. Thereafter surplus produce will cover school fees, clothing
and other services. The village concept removes the stigma against
rural areas.
Urbanites will embrace our home areas with due pride. We shall invest
our energies, capital and know how among relatives. The result?
Zambia's economic centre of gravity will shift from the line of rail
to rural areas.
A boom in the hinterland actually means thriving towns and cities.
That's the beauty of it all. Where I live in Ndola truckloads of
arm-sized sweet potatoes from Solwezi head, under my nose to markets
in Lusaka. And who does not know that North-Western rain forests feed
Lusaka with mukwa, and London with bees wax? It boasts huge potential
to revitalise Ndola's dead rubber industry too.
The Village Concept really means putting rural Zambia in fast track
to convert it into an engine of economic growth. My friends in
Vancouver used to say that to every port there is an hinterland.
Vancouver is a very wealthy port city on the Pacific coast of Canada.
But it wouldn't be so without the lumber, copper or nickel deposits
from snow-capped ranges of British Columbia or the wheat fields of
the prairies.
The village concept is a vision whose time has come. It promises an
ethos of thrift, prudence, and productivity in villages and towns to
put food on tables all year long. A city-centred economic paradigm
has failed lamentably. And the time for handouts, whether from the
donor community or from a self absorbed government must end. Sons and
daughters of the land, we must stir ourselves into action. It is time
to empower relatives in our villages to take command of their needs
for food, better education, clothing and water. And a boom in
villages means a thriving line of rail and decongestion of the towns.
Heritage has the vision. And the will of steel to get it done.
The author is the Chairman of the Zambia Intercessory Network and
Heritage Party sympathiser

Original_Search_Title: News In Brief
Headline: Africa: News In Brief
Body_Text:
UNITED NATIONS: The UN Security Council on Wednesday welcomed Secretary-General Kofi Annan's latest proposals on the next phase of the deployment of UN Mission in the DRC, known as MONUC. In a presidential statement, the council said it supports Annan's idea of starting to deploy the UN peacekeeping force in the eastern part of the country to enable it to go into areas controlled by rebel forces.
NAIROBI: Most Kenyans are unhappy with their government, according to the results of a survey conducted on behalf of the International Republican Institute (IRI). 63.2% of the (2,984 Kenyan) respondents think the country is headed in the wrong direction as opposed to 21.7%. 71.8% want a new constitution before the presidential and legislative polls, and 66 percent said Moi "has too much power." The survey also found that the main concerns of Kenyans were food security and corruption.
DAR ES SALAAM: Tanzanian police have said that a letter from Pakistan, which was sent to the University of Dar es Salaam (UDSM) last week, did not contain any
trace of anthrax, allaying fears that there is an outbreak of the disease in the country. The Regional Police Commander Alfred Tibaigana said here on Monday that laboratory test of the "powder" from the letter showed that there were no potential germs for the deadly disease.
BUJUMBURA: The World Food Programme (WFP) says it has completed in most of Burundi's seven provinces the distribution of 4,000 tones of food to some 375,000 people.
It carried out rapid assessment in the provinces of Kayanza, Makamba and Rutana and reported storm damage to maize, beans, sweet potatoes, cassava and bananas. However, in Ngozi province, if the current good rainfall continues, the area should experience improved harvests.
KINSHASA: The government of Belgium has contributed an additional $1.25 million to the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) to fight persistent food insecurity in urban and rural areas of the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The funds will be channelled through two FAO programmes. The aim is to support cassava cultivation in all provinces as part of a nation-wide effort to combat diseases and insects that have severely damaged cassava crops throughout the country. Cassava is the staple diet for 70 percent of Congolese. -Agencies

Original_Search_Title: State Distributes Agricultural Inputs
Headline: Zambia: State Distributes Agricultural Inputs
Body_Text:
Ndola — The distribution of fertiliser and seeds is in preparation for the
2001-2002 farming season.
Community Development and Social Welfare Minister, Jane Chikwata
flagged off the distribution programme, which was targeted at
vulnerable farmers in Lusaka yesterday.
Mrs Chikwata said the food security pact was a three-year programme
targeting 200,000 small-scale farmers annually, throughout Zambia.
The objective of the pact was to improve household food security
among vulnerable but viable families, which would benefit the female,
and child headed households.
"Other beneficiaries include households affected by floods, drought,
other natural calamities and farmers cultivating less than one
hectare, orphans and those keeping terminally ill patients."
The programme also aimed at helping to reduce the number of people
that are currently on the Public Welfare Assistance Scheme (PWAS).
The minister was optimistic that the early distribution of inputs
would lead to a good harvest in next year's agriculture produce in
the country and improve the household food security.
Government would ensure that it sourced funds to fight poverty and
that the distribution of the seeds and fertilisers was timely to
reach the farmers for good farming season.
She said the Ministry of Agriculture Food and Fisheries and Ministry
of Finance and Economic Development also fund the programme.
At the same function, Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM) acting
director, Helen Samatebele explained that the seed distribution would
be monitored up to the intended beneficiaries.
Mrs Samatebele said the inputs included 838 tonnes of maize, 166

"PAM will start distributing these inputs immediately but first with

She said that many areas in Northern Province would receive the
