Original_Search_Title: Cassava for the rescue
Headline: Ghana: Cassava for the rescue
Body_Text:
The crushing poverty that our people live means so many social things.
It means, first and foremost, a life without hope for tomorrow. It means nights without dreams, because a hungry body gets no sound sleep to produce dreams, sweet or otherwise. It means a life of envy, wishful thinking, temptations, insecurity, waning self-confidence, and self doubt. Grinding poverty, as our people live daily, means powerlessness and readiness top compromise on one's cherished values. It could mean a readiness to throw away principle for crumbs.
Poverty has reduced the self-worth of our people to nothingness. There are millions of men and women in our villages who cannot, on any one day for weeks or sometimes months on end, count in their hands 5,000 cedis as their own. What is the self worth of a young woman who cannot count one decent cloth in her wardrobe as her own? It is constant sadness, unbearable shame and mental confusion and a feeling of impotence for a young man who has to run away from the village because he cannot afford to feed the baby he has had with the young woman he must have loved. It is a loss of pride when a father and a mother cannot keep their child in school because they cannot bear the costs.
Anything, any project that promises to lift our people any level above this dehumanizing scale of poverty must be welcome. Anything that minimizes, by any scale whatsoever, this deplorable level of poverty must be supported. Any scheme that attempts to release our rural people from the consuming grips of such levels of poverty must be welcome. Of course, we are not talking about the many schemes of speculation, deceit and destruction that have been plotted to squeeze more fat from the bones of our people. We are not talking about bogus "poverty
alleviation" scams.
President Kufuor's Special Initiative, I think, promises to make some improvement in the lives of its beneficiaries. Take the cassava scheme, for example. Well organized, well administered, appreciating the views of the beneficiaries regarding terms of the assistance and related matters, it should go very far in minimizing the pains and pangs of poverty for the immediate beneficiaries.
For it to make lasting impact on fighting poverty, however, it is hoped the President's people would consider several issues some of which are raised below, and which we hope have been considered already.
We do know that basing our economy on just producing and selling raw materials has not resulted in sustained economic strength and development. In fact, cocoa, the principal cash crop, has not succeeded in sustaining the wealth of our farmers nor our national development interests. Perhaps in the short term the cassava project will bring very good returns to our peasants in some districts. It should reduce poverty there for some time to come.
How, for instance, will it affect food production for our own
consumption? Why wouldn't it attract most farmers to grow cassava and stop growing our staples? What can be done to stave off possible food shortages as a result?
What is going to be done with the parts of the cassava that will not be exported? First, are we simply going to ship off the raw starch? Are we, at some point, going to manufacture the industrial products here? What will happen to all the other things that can be extracted from the
cassava? Is there any scheme to get the peels and other rejects into feed for say sheep and cattle?
The plan seems to "and should" be directed at small farmers. What is there to ensure that big money will not take over or monopolize much of that business by investing in big cassava farms and edge out the
peasants? What advise will be given to the peasants to encourage them to grow other foods on the same plots of land to avoid the fear of food shortages that could result from a wholesale diversion toward cassava cultivation?
We've had schemes foisted on our peasants about how this or that crop will bring wealth and end poverty.
At one time it was the sunflower. At one or another time it was soya beans. Some other time it is cashew.
Indeed, some past years in places like Asante Mampong or so, it was potatoes. There never took any peasant anywhere.
The cassava scheme appears a bit different because of the industrial uses. But will it be Ghana alone producing it? Others will get into it.
There could be a glut on the market one day. The problem of other cash crops could come up to face us. What plans do we have to face the challenges of a starchy business?

Original_Search_Title: Kamba Elders to Discuss Ngilu
Headline: Kenya: Kamba Elders to Discuss Ngilu
Body_Text:
Kamba leaders led by the Machakos Kanu branch chairman Mr Mulu Mutisya yesterday said that the community will meet to discuss Kaluki Ngilu for abusing the community by wielding quiver full of arrows, a bows and arrow in presence of men.
Mr Mutisya accused those who conducted the ceremony of making her a Kamba elder a blunder of giving a woman bows and arrows instead of a lesso and basket full of either cassava or sweet potatoes the befitting a woman.
The leaders who included ministers, MPs, councillors and other elected leaders from the four Kamba districts said that the Kitui Central MP will be fined several bulls and goats to cleanse the Kamba community for breaking traditions by wielding a bows and arrows in public.
On politics, the leaders accused the legislator of taking the Kamba community for a ride for along time by shifting them from one party to another for her own selfish games.
Mr Mutisya told journalists that the MP's new National Party of Kenya is a non-starter in Ukambani and told her to officially defect from Social Democratic Party of Kenya to a newly formed political party.
Councillor Peter Mwanthi of SDP said that priorities of the Kamba community are provision of clean piped water, well equipped medical institutions, tarmac roads and other essential facilities and not formation of new political parties without agenda to tell wananchi.
He challenged Ngilu to immediately defect to her new party to pave way for a by-election in her Kitui Central constituency and told her to stop her threats to those leaders opposed to her leadership.

Original_Search_Title: State Distributes Agricultural Inputs
Headline: Zambia: State Distributes Agricultural Inputs
Body_Text:
Lusaka — GOVERNMENT has started the distribution of agricultural inputs to the
73 districts in the country valued at over K32 billion under the food
security pact programme.
The distribution of fertiliser and seeds is in preparation for the
2001-2002 farming season.
Community Development and Social Welfare Minister, Jane Chikwata
flagged off the distribution programme, which was targeted at
vulnerable farmers in Lusaka yesterday.
Mrs Chikwata said the food security pact was a three-year programme
targeting 200,000 small-scale farmers annually, throughout Zambia.
The objective of the pact was to improve household food security
among vulnerable but viable families, which would benefit the female,
and child headed households.
"Other beneficiaries include households affected by floods, drought,
other natural calamities and farmers cultivating less than one
hectare, orphans and those keeping terminally ill patients."
The programme also aimed at helping to reduce the number of people
that are currently on the Public Welfare Assistance Scheme (PWAS).
The minister was optimistic that the early distribution of inputs
would lead to a good harvest in next year's agriculture produce in
the country and improve the household food security.
Government would ensure that it sourced funds to fight poverty and
that the distribution of the seeds and fertilisers was timely to
reach the farmers for good farming season.
She said the Ministry of Agriculture Food and Fisheries and Ministry
of Finance and Economic Development also fund the programme.
At the same function, Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM) acting
director, Helen Samatebele explained that the seed distribution would
be monitored up to the intended beneficiaries.
Mrs Samatebele said the inputs included 838 tonnes of maize, 166
tonnes of sorghum, 38 tonnes of millet, 55 tonnes of rice, 1,500
tonnes of groundnuts, 750 tonnes of beans, 125 tonnes of cowpeas, 20
tonnes of soya beans and 10,000 tonnes of banana suckers.
Other inputs are 11.7 million tonnes of sweet potato vines, 13
million tonnes of cassava cuttings, 3,500 tonnes of D-compound, 3,500
Urea and 3,500 Agriculture lime.
"PAM will start distributing these inputs immediately but first with
districts that are cut off during the rain season like Kaputa,
Kalabo, Chama, Chavuma and Chiengi.
She said that many areas in Northern Province would receive the
inputs earlier than the Southern region because the on-set of rains
is early.
Mrs Samatebele said PAM is contracted by Government to distribute the
inputs to storage points where the non- governmental organisations
(NGOs) will be dispatching them to the local people.

Original_Search_Title: Claude's Worcester Source
Headline: South Africa: Claude's Worcester Source
Body_Text:
Dignitaries in the Boland town gathered to celebrate their first  cricket
'Springbok'
The number of buttons on a jacket can sometimes tell you where a man is from. When 150 burghers of Worcester gathered last week to celebrate  Claude
Henderson's selection to the national side, it was clear the guest  speaker in the hip, black four-button suit was not a local. Henderson's Western
Province captain HD Ackerman had made the trip "over the mountain".
Ackerman started well by reminding the audience that it was an old
Worcesterite who had decided on green jerseys for the national rugby  team.
The United Cricket Board is anxious to rebrand the cricket team as  something other than Proteas. In Worcester the flower hasn't caught on anyway:  for the locals, Henderson is their first cricket "Springbok".
The Lion's Club was the venue for the tribute dinner and with a cold  wind nipping in like a surprise arm-ball off the Du Toit mountains, it would  not have been surprising to see some empty seats. Henderson was expecting  to meet a few mates in the pub Ã± instead there were 18 tables, a full  house, flower arrangements, lashings of food and the best wines of the region.  With the nearby KWV Cellar being the biggest in the world, there was no  shortage of warming brandy either.
Parked closest to the hall were the diesel bakkies that delivered the  tender meats, salads, roast potatoes, sweet potatoes, rice and hot pudding  that made up the feast. In an impressive example of platteland organisation,  it took just three days to put the event together. Organiser Danie Nel  said every sponsor approached had come on board.
The idea for the dinner sprang from what must be the least successful betting group in the country. Nel and Claude's father, "Mossie"  Henderson, are founder members of the Rugby Raai Klub, started during the 1995  World
Cup. In six years no one has won the now massive pot but the club's  rules are peculiar and very particular: the score-line has to be exactly  correct and no one is allowed to bet against the Springboks!
There was lots of hilarity as the party was reminded of the unusual  domestic chore pattern in the Henderson household. Mossie chalked the lines to  make the garden look like Augusta for golf competitions and Lord's for the cricket season but it was mother Susan who cut the lawn to the correct specifications.
Although the evening was organised as a tribute to Henderson the effect  was to celebrate his parents, the town and its cricketers. Worcester was  joint host with Wellington of the first South African Country Week in 1954  and cricket on the platteland is still strong. There are at least 7Â000  country cricketers travelling vast distances on dusty roads all over South  Africa every week. The cricketers of Willowmore in the Eastern Cape were until recently making a 360km round trip to Graaff-Reinet to play home games.
Henderson's first chance in national colours came in 1989 when he was selected for the South African Country Districts (SACD) under-17 team  that toured England under the guidance of Anton Ferreira.
Lance Klusener averaged 85 for Natal Country Districts at the 1993 tournament and was part of the SACD team that beat host province  Western
Transvaal. Tertius Bosch, Fanie de Villiers and Makhaya Ntini are all examples of platteland talent.
Back in Worcester, cricket is largely Afrikaans Ã± like the town  itself. To
Mossie went the greatest tribute as the person who had taught his son  "die liefde vir die game (the love of the game)". This rubbed off on all the
Henderson boys. Oldest brother Kiffie played for the Boland Kavaliers  in his day and younger brother James is in Scotland as a pro after averaging  40 for
Boland in the Supersport Series last season. The active cricketers  share a house halfway between their rival provinces in Gordon's Bay.
Claude and James are not common names for Afrikaans-speaking boys but  then
Mossie's real name is Henry and he was an English-speaker before he  fell for
Susan and moved to Worcester. Henderson Snr did some research on his
Scottish roots last year when his sons flew him to Britain for a  holiday and now that he's retired he may get around to visiting the national  archives to find out more about the family lll history.
Ackerman's instinctslll about spin paid dividends last season: 400  overs from Henderson produced 36 wickets and Paul Adams took 18 wickets in  200 overs before he left for the West Indies. Only three (pace) bowlers  took more wickets. With coaches Vincent Barnes and Eric Simons encouraging  him to
"be brave", and with a supportive captain, Henderson's spin showed more  bite and bounce than ever before. Control he has always had.
Henderson's selection is proof that country boys can make it. He's had  to work very hard and at 29 he's earned his stripes as a professional.  This platteland cricketer has also played in the Birmingham, Derbyshire and
Lancashire leagues.
When Henderson steps on to the field for South Africa he will be aiming  to
"do what I've always done". In that, he is like the place that formed  him.
They don't take kindly to pretension in the Breede River Valley.
Even the wine estates keep it simple: at the dinner we drank Du Toit's
Kloof, Dassies Rood and Botha Kelders. Like the mature brandy of the  nearby
KWVCellar, Claude Henderson the cricketer is now ready for the  international market.
l Officially, Henderson is the second player from Worcester to make the national team. Eddie Fuller, who played in the 1950s, was born in  Worcester but went to school and played his club cricket in Cape Town.

Original_Search_Title: Research Project Prompts Exchange
Headline: South Africa: Research Project Prompts Exchange
Body_Text:
A joint research project between SA and Kenya in biotechnology
research on sweet potatoes has led SA and US scientists to plan the
setting up of a student exchange programme between SA and Iowa State
University for the first time.
The research programme will allow graduate students in biotechnology
in the US to visit the Agricultural Research Council in SA.
US companies involved in biotechnology claim that US farmers save
$450m a year in input costs through the use of the technology.
Yesterday, Iowa governor Tom Vilsack and a delegation from the
private sector and academic fraternity in Iowa visited the research
facilities outside Pretoria where the biotechnology is being
conducted.
Vilsack chairs the US governors' biotechnology partnership, a
committee consisting of 27 governors hoping to boost the technology
in Africa.
Vilsack, backed by Iowa State University science and technology dean,
Rollin Richmond, said funds would be found to start the exchange
programme for students on graduate level.
"The very existence of a legal framework in SA to regulate research
into biotechnology and introduce it into farming in this country is
extraordinary.
A new law to govern the application of the science was adopted by
Parliament last year. No such clear policy' existed in Iowa, Vilsack
said.
The research on sweet potato is aimed at improving disease control.

Original_Search_Title: Braai Aces Cook Up a Hot Contest in City
Headline: South Africa: Braai Aces Cook Up a Hot Contest in City
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Forget the Boks and Bafana Bafana - Cape Town is to host a sizzling
contest that will really separate the men from the boys - an
international braai competition.
The Amabraaie Braaie will pit their barbecue skills against the best
in the world at the third annual World Barbecue Cook-off, to be held
from November 2 to 4 at Century City in Cape Town.
Teams from as far afield as Moscow and Texas will compete.
According to the SA Barbecue Association, an affiliate of the World
Barbecue Association, there are 6.5 million barbecue enthusiasts
worldwide.
The association's motto is: "Making people happy by fire, food and
fun is a way of saying: Let's barbecue and make friends around the
world."
The organiser, Lannice Snyman, said she was delighted with the
response from international teams so far.
"In addition to top South African braaiers, we expect superb
representation from all over the world.
In all, 50 teams will be competing and entries have been received
from the UK, the US, Belgium, the Netherlands, Reunion, Switzerland,
Austria, Germany, Russia and Japan."
Other attractions at the event will include a barbecue and outdoor
living expo.
The first annual world championship was held in Switzerland in 1999
and the second in Nashville, Tennessee, where the event attracted 40
000 visitors a day.
All competitors were required to use their braaiing expertise in five
categories: chicken, fish, beef/ostrich, pork/lamb, and dessert.
Snyman said Tennessee saw the Amabraaier Braaiers create a string of
South African dishes which delighted the judges, such as smoked fish
with bacon-wrapped figs, which earned them fourth place in the fish
category, Cape Malay spice rubbed beef fillet with African peanut
samp and beans, and an African-inspired dessert: sweet potato
fritters with amarula cream.
Billy Martin, brand manager of Chateau Libertas, which is sponsoring
the Cape Town event, said braaiing in South Africa had been born of a
rich and varied past in the many diverse cultures that exist here.
"It has been practised by South Africans for more than a thousand
years," he said.
The SA Barbecue Association has invited all barbecue lovers who would
like to enter the competition to contact Tamsin Snyman or Lannice
Snyman for information on the championships on 790-3367 or fax
790-1055.
l Fire in the Soul, a documentary film exploring the deeper stories
of the food, people and locations of barbecue, is currently being
filmed by an American/Canadian crew for worldwide distribution.
Filming began at last year's event and will continue at the world
championships in South Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Appealed for Review of VAT On Inputs for Exports
Headline: Ghana: Farmers Appealed for Review of VAT On Inputs for Exports
Body_Text:
Farmers engaged in non-traditional exports have appealed to the government to review the upfront payment of the Value Added Tax (VAT) on packaging materials and seeds as well as the cumbersome procedures to obtaining refund.
The review should aim at releasing funds to enable exporters to meet their export orders adequately and promptly.
The appeal was made by Mr Henry Anim-Somuah, Managing Director of the Agromite Limited, in an interview with the Ghana News Agency at Kwahu-Amanfrom on Thursday, where he is engaged in vegetable production for exports through irrigation on the banks of the Afram River.
The company is to start exporting varieties of sweet pepper to the United Kingdom and France from August 22 from its 11.3 hectares of MI 2, Scotch Bonnet and Jalapeno pepper varieties and okra, he said.
It had also acquired 20.8 hectares for the cultivation of sweet potatoes for export to the UK within the next 10 months.
In collaboration with Technoserve and AMEX International, under the USAID-supported Trade and Investment Reform Programme, he said the company was benefiting from technical assistance in scientific production methods, post-harvest handling and out-grower management and local and export marketing.
Mr Anim-Somuah, an graduate  in irrigation technology, said 150 out-grower farmers, comprising 15 farming groups, were being formed to help the company to produce enough to meet its export orders, saying this would also help offer employment to the youth in the Kwahu South District.
He, therefore, urged the Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MOFA) to assist the company to acquire a cold-chain facility from the Japanese grant available to the government, to enable it to cope with the storage and handling of produce as a condition of meeting the requirements set by the European Union's EUREP-GAP policy for foreign food exporters.
Mr Anim-Somuah also appealed to the government to expedite action on the rehabilitation of the Begoro-Amate road and the extension of power to the area to facilitate the production and marketing of farm produce. GNA

Original_Search_Title: Thousands of Farmers Receive Farming Aid
Headline: Sierra Leone: Thousands of Farmers Receive Farming Aid
Body_Text:
Some 10,530 low income rural families in Sierra Leone have received farm
implements and
inputs that promise to end hunger as the country emerges slowly from 10
years of civil war, PANA reported.
The chief agricultural officer, Dennis Kamara, told PANA in Freetown on
Tuesday that the farmers had received tractors, power tillers, seeds, tools,
and other agricultural inputs, including 11,000 bags of sweet potato vines
and 8,000 bundles of cassava cuttings for the current planting season. The
donations were made by the Chinese and Swedish governments, as well as the
FAO and other international NGOs.
Despite its mineral wealth, Sierra Leone's economy is largely agricultural
with between 64 percent and 75 percent of the population earning a living
out of this activity. Up to 70 different crops have been cultivated but the
main export crops are coffee, cocoa, palm kernels and piassava (a fibre
crop), cola nuts and ginger. Cocoa accounts for about 45 percent of total
agricultural exports. Before the civil war in 1991, some three-quarters of
the farmers cultivated the staple, rice.

Original_Search_Title: Exporters Eye Chinese Market
Headline: Uganda: Exporters Eye Chinese Market
Body_Text:
The Uganda Export Promotion Board (UEPB) has drawn up a programme to
help local exporters penetrate the Chinese market, acting executive
director William Omongole has said.
The official said since the Chinese market is one of the biggest in
the world, a substantial share of it can turn around Uganda's
economy. "Now that we have seen a slowdown in Uganda's economic
growth in the recent past, its only the export market that can help
us escape a full blown up recession," Omongole told The Monitor,
August 2.
He said UEPB will later this month officially launch a comprehensive
programme involving embassies of the two countries, to encourage
joint ventures, exports and imports between them.
UEPB has singled out non-traditional commodities for promotion,
including beans, honey, bananas, handicrafts, sweet potatoes among
others.
Uganda's ambassador to China, Phillip Idro was in the country last
month, Meeting local businessmen and holding workshops on trade
opportunities in China.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Bank Makes Handsome Net Profit
Headline: Ghana: Rural Bank Makes Handsome Net Profit
Body_Text:
The Akuapem Rural bank has made a net profit of 632.4 million cedis in its operations last year as compared to 216.2 million cedis in 1999, representing 192.5 per cent.
Total income of the bank increased by 80 per cent from 621.8 million cedis to 1.1 billion cedis during the same period.
The Chairperson of the Board of Directors of the bank, Dr (Mrs) Gloria Nikoi, who made this known at the 20th  annual general meeting of the shareholders at Mamfe-Akuapem on Saturday said, total deposit increased from  two billion cedis to 3.6 billion cedis.
She said the bank granted loans and advances totalling one billion cedis last year, as against 852 million cedis the previous year.
Mrs. Nikoi  commended the board and staff for their dedication, discipline, commitment and diligence, which enabled the bank to achieve the feat.  Mr Joseph . B Clottey, Acting Head, Banking Supervision Department, Bank of Ghana (BOG), said, the bank is committed tosupporting the steady growth and development of an efficient rural banking system, capable of harnessing resources in the rural areas for rapid, sustainable socio-economic development.
He said the BOG would also continue to exercise its supervisory role strictly over rural banks and provide the required assistance for them to achieve improved performance.
Mr. Clottey urged rural banks to adopt measures to strengthen the credit management procedures and processes to achieve good loan portfolio quality while enhancing profitability.
According to him, it has been observed that appropriate appraisal of credits had been generally unsatisfactory, resulting in poor recovery  and ultimately losses.
He commended the bank for maintaining stable operations and  steady growth over the years.
Mr. Clottey noted that the general performance of rural banks in the country has recorded impressive developments of late despite the unfavourable micro-economic conditions. He said satisfactory rural banks, which numbered 53 at the end of March last year, increased to 64 at the end of March this year.
The Akuapem North District Chief Executive, Dr (Mrs) Eugenia Quist expressed appreciation to the bank for its Women-ln-Development programme aimed at assisting women to undertake income generating ventures.
She called on the people in the area to undertake viable projects such as sunflower, pawpaw, sweet potato and sweet pepper production to supplement their earnings.                            GNA

Original_Search_Title: The Healing Value of Potato
Headline: Nigeria: The Healing Value of Potato
Body_Text:
Nowadays, people are increasingly becoming aware of the need to take
personal responsibility in matters affecting their physical health
and well being which should not be left alone to the health care
practitioners.
At the same time, the knowledge of the art of living naturally is
made available to all who seek and it does not necessarily require
intellectual brilliance or higher education. It all lies in the
observation and in obeying the Universal Rules of Nature i.e. the
Laws of Nature which the Lord Almighty had made during creation.
Man as we are privileged to know presently, is a child of this
creation and the physical body he wears as a cloak is a product of
nature and as such must be cared for, strengthened and healed when it
falls sick with those natural agents that belongs to nature. These
are: vegetables of the garden, fruits of the trees, grasses of the
field, honey of the bees and herbs which the Creator causes to grow
for the service of man.
Unfortunately, the pathway that were originally meant to lead to good
health and happiness were abandoned for an artificial way of living.
POTATO is one of the healing agents that has its efficacy and healing
virtues well established and validated among the nutritional
scientists all over the world.
1) Consuming baked potato or slightly boiled with the skin helps in
relieving migraine headaches. This is highly efficacious when it is
taken in addition with the infusion (tea) made from Vervain, or
Passion Flower. This will also help in relieving waist pain or
1umbago.
2) A diet regimen of Potato
will help in maintaining the acid - alkali balance of the body,
will assist in overcoming constipation and piles. It is recommended
in cases of digestive disorders, kidney stones, water retention and
in reducing high blood levels of uric acid in the body. It is often
combined with other vegetables such as fluted pumpkins, and other
deep green vegetables, tomatoes etc.
3) A decoction of clean potato peels or tea made from the peels is
recommended in cases of malignancy, rheumatism or in getting rid of
excess uric acid from the body.
4) Raw potato juice. is often not very palatable to drink, because of
this, it is usually combined with carrot or lemon juice. It is
recommended in the following conditions.
i) Skin problems like eczema
ii) Arthritis, rheumatism, gout, lumbago, etc. The juice has anti-
inflammatory actions and reduces excessive deposition of uric acid in
the joints. For these cases, it could be taken with an infusion made
from herbs like, Sage, Nettle, Garlic, Agrimony Catnip, etc.
iii) Potato juice when taken alone or in combination with carrot
juice assists in healing intestinal or peptic ulcer and gastritis Â½
potato juice to 1 full glass could be taken 30 minutes before any
meal. The teas made from Sage, Nettle, Mistletoe, Fleabane, Chickweed
could also help. And a teaspoonful of ground Cayenne pepper could be
sprinkled on the meal each time before eating. The initial burning
sensation noticed will disappear with subsequent intakes of the
pepper.
iv) Potato starch or when the juice is mixed with carrot and pure
honey will disinfect the bowels in case of any food poisoning and
this also stops diarrhea just as quickly as lemon juice mixed with
blended raw garlic will do.
5) Potato juice intake will overcome indigestion and flatulence when
it is taken with the tea made from Garlic, Sage, Ginger, Fennel, etc.
B) Sweet Potato
The sweet potato have similar healing virtues as the white specie,
but the main difference between the two is that the sweet one
contains more carbohydrate with less potassium content and in
addition, it has rejuvenating tonic effects on the male reproductive
organs and the juice is recommended in case of male infertility.
External use of potato
As poultice or compresses, it is good in the following cases.
swellings of joints, synovitis, sprain, bruises, swollen eyelids,
under eye puffiness, skin infections, insect bites, warts, etc.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya Slips Further in Poverty List
Headline: Kenya Slips Further in Poverty List
Body_Text:
Kenya has suffered a major development setback, dropping to United Nations rankings worse than a decade ago.
All quality of life indicators show that it is among the poorest of nations at position 123 out of the 162 countries cited in the UNDP's Human Development Report 2001. Despite global advances in technology, Kenya is in the family of nations that have suffered "setbacks in the human development index". Its rating this year is lower than it was in 1990, according to the report.
But Kenya's position is a slight improvement over the 1998 ranking, when it was placed 137 out of 174. Still, this year's ranking is dismal compared with relatively highly-placed Lesotho, Ghana, Namibia, Gabon, Algeria, Zimbabwe, Equitorial Guinea and Swaziland - countries perceived to be underdeveloped. Kenya ranks a mere point ahead of the Comoros. It also does better than Cameroon, Nigeria, Madagascar, Djibouti, Sudan, Tanzania, Rwanda and Burundi. War-torn Sierra Leone is at the bottom of the list.
Among the factors behind Kenya's decline are the Aids epidemic, poverty, less foreign investment, failure to capitalise on the boom in information technology and a high level of brain drain.
In terms of technological advancement, Kenya, Sudan, Tanzania and Mozambique fall in the bracket of the "marginalised". The US and UK are at the top, Spain and Italy are "potential leaders" and Egypt, Algeria and Zimbabwe are "dynamic adopters".
Kenya has declined in adopting technology when other regions have registered improvements in the past 30 years. East Asia and the Pacific have made rapid progress in most areas - from expanding knowledge to improving survival and raising standards of living, says the report.
Internet technology is reportedly still "raw" in the country. There were only two internet hosts in Kenya for 10,000 people, according to the report, compared with Finland's ratio of two people for every host.
South Africa, the highest placed African country, has 17 hosts for 10,000 users. Only 11 out of 1,000 Kenyans have phones. In South Africa, the figure is 270 for 1,000.
Kenya reportedly lacks a policy on information and communication technologies. Although the Government has lately created a department in the Planning Ministry headed by Dr Juma Oketch, formal structures are yet to be established.
The message underpinning the report is that globalisation boosts development and that technology is a major factor in market competition.
For years, the UNDP stood aloof from the controversy surrounding genetically-motivated foods. But, in the current report, the agency recommends the use of such foods in fighting famine and malnutrition, which threatens millions in poor nations.
While acknowledging the environmental and health risks that need to be addressed, the report stresses the unique potential of GM techniques for creating virus-resistant, drought-tolerant and nutrient-enhanced crops.
However, the agency exhorts the developing world to establish a feedback mechanism to establish whether or not the benefits of technology are reaped. Says the report: "In Kenya, it took 18 months to approve the research use of transgenic sweet potatoes, despite the very low risk involved."
The report also says developing countries are losing billions of dollars as a result of emigration of professionals. India, for instance, loses as much as $2 billion a year owing to brain drain to the US.

Original_Search_Title: Brand New Weapons in Fight Against Malnutrition
Headline: Kenya: Brand New Weapons in Fight Against Malnutrition
Body_Text:
After years of studious silence, the United Nations Development Programme has finally recommended genetically modified foods to fight famine and malnutrition. The twin calamities threaten millions Third World lives.
In its report, Human Development Report 2001, the organisation says many developing countries could reap great benefits from GMOs, whose use has remained as controversial as their invention. The report stresses the GM techniques' "unique potential" for creating virus-resistant, drought-tolerant and nutrient-enhanced crops.
"Malnutrition affects more than 800 million people worldwide. GMOs would be valuable to poor farmers working in marginal lands in black Africa," the report says. While many scientists consider GMOs the panacea to the world's unstable food situation, environmental groups, led by Green Peace, see them as a threat to nature and human life.
In Kenya, the Ministry of Agriculture has thrown its weight behind their use to resolve the food deficit bedevilling the country.
Now the UNDP urges public investment in research and development to ensure biotechnology meets the agricultural needs of the poor. "We can't count on the private sector alone to do the job," says Sakiko Fukuda-Parr, the report's leading author.
Kenya has entered a biotechnology research plan with multinational companies such as the controverted Monsato, headquartered in the US. Recently, the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute said it had developed a superior-quality, disease-resistant sweet potato Monsanto's help.
What continues to worry environmentalists, however, is the question of biosafety. Kenya introduced tight biosafety legislation only in 1998.

Original_Search_Title: Springhare, Africa's Replica of Kangaroo
Headline: Africa: Springhare, Africa's Replica of Kangaroo
Body_Text:
THE Springhare is, at first glance, a semi-replica of a Kangaroo. It is
rabbit-sized with a short head, high snout , large eyes and ears.  Like the
Kangaroo, its front legs are greatly shortened with five digits ending with
very powerful digging claws. Its hind legs are lengthened, especially the
feet. Its body colouration varies between cinnamon (yellowish-brown) to
reddish-brown with a white yellow underside. Its tail is long and thickens
towards the end, the end half of the tail is brownish black or
black. Springhares weigh between 3-4kg, the tail length is between
35-39cm.
After a gestation period of two months, a single young is born, and
remains hidden in the burrow where it is suckled by the mother. They are
active only at dusk or by night. Being nocturnal they have large eyes and
ears.  Their big ears can be moved to pick up sounds of danger. Their long
whiskers help them detect and avoid obstructions in the dark. Before leaving
their holes, they take great care to check the surroundings just to be sure
that the coast is clear. Then having assured themselves that all is fine,
leap out and go about in search of food in unhurried locomotion
hops. However, when danger threatens, they move quickly, making long leaps
between 2-8 metres at any one stretch with their body in horizontal
position, front legs together and the tail fanning side ways of
balance.
The springhares have a host of enemies among them honey badger, senal,
carcal, African wild cat, jackal, large snakes, monitor lizard and large
owls, and of course, man who in some countries farms them for their
delicious flesh. When in danger springhares always flee to a particular
hole. Their burrows, however, have several entrances- which are sealed up by
sand when the springhare is at rest. It sleeps sitting with hind legs
stretched forward, with down-curved forepart of body between the hind legs,
its head to the ground to enable it to detect ground vibrations of
approaching enemy.
They inhabit sandy plains, grassy or thin bush or open woodland savannas
on sandy soil. This explains why in Zambia, springhares are mostly confined
to the western half of the country with a few extending to parts of Central
Province where sandy soils afford them habitat in which to dig their
burrows. They are herbivores, eating grass, grass seeds, leaves of low
bushes, berries of bushes and in cultivated areas become pests eating
groundnuts, sweet potatoes, wheat, maize and pumpkins.
As already mentioned, in Zambia they are found in the western half of
the country due to its sandy terrain. Elsewhere they are found in Kenya and
Tanzania, middle part of Angola onwards to South Africa. Their local names
are Sinkuyu, Mpila, Kakuyu, Shikuyu and Ndombolo. Usually solitary, the
rodent when captured can and will inflict nasty cuts with its sharp teeth
and claws. They bite to the bone on fingers, wrists, toes and feet.
They can be seen in parks like the Kafue National Park in the headlights
leaping about. Those who camp at Chunga Safari camp will definitely see
them, during the day their burrows can be easily seen filled up with sand as
the springhare sleeps below.
Kangaroos, are of course native to Australia, but there are rodents that
resemble them slightly in body structure like our own springhares do. Other
groups of rodents that do look like miniature Kangaroos are the Jerboas,
which are small nocturnal leaping rodents which inhabit the plains and
deserts of North Africa, Asia and Eastern Europe. A Kangaroo, however, is
not a rodent but a marsupial (pouched) mammal.
Apart from the Kafue National Park, those travelling through Western
Province should keep a look out for them along many of the roads that
crisscross the province particularly in large undisturbed areas like the
Likwa plain and Sioma Ngwezi national parks. Springhares are part and parcel
of Zambia's rich diversity of small mammals, which we will be looking at in
upcoming features. They may be small but their presence in the wilderness is
of vital importance.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Production Not Greatly Affected By Insecurity
Headline: Burundi: Crop Production Not Greatly Affected By Insecurity
Body_Text:
Despite insecurity in several parts of Burundi, the situation did not
hamper crop production too much during the 2001 B season, the Food and
Agriculture Organisation (FAO) said following a recent joint mission with
the Ministry of Agriculture, WFP and UNICEF. The mission carried an
assessment of crops and food for the 2001 B season (harvest in June).
Farmers' stocks of beans were relatively limited in most of the provinces,
FAO noted. Many of the farmers bought seeds or were assisted through
humanitarian aid. The FAO distributed 3,500 mt of bean seeds for this
season. The FAO said mineral fertilisers remained relatively inaccessible
to farmers. Pesticides too remained inaccessible, but "fortunately, there
were little significant diseases during the season".
Food crop production obtained for 2001 is close to the average total of
the pre-crisis period, the FAO said. It attributed this to an increase in
the planting of roots and tubers.  Following the climatic changes in the
last two years, farmers have heavily relied on roots and tuber crops such
as cassava and sweet potato which require less farming and are more
tolerant to drought conditions than beans, FAO said. "This increase in
production for 2001 is however misleading and will be hiding nutritional
imbalances with respect to energy supply," FAO noted. It said the roots
and tubers were "poor in nutrients" compared to cereals and legumes.

Original_Search_Title: "We Live in Nests Like Birds", Displaced Say
Headline: Burundi: "We Live in Nests Like Birds", Displaced Say
Body_Text:
Gihungwe Camp, Burundi — Gihungwe Camp, northwest Burundi- The road to Gihungwe
displaced people's camp in the northwest Burundi province of Bubanza is a
dangerous one. Rebel activity is rife in this area, the reason why so many
people fled to Gihungwe in the first place.
On either side of the road are fields of burnt grass. "These were the
rebels' hiding places from which they ambushed vehicles," a UN official
explained. The army has set the grass alight to prevent the rebels from
hiding and to open up the countryside.
This area is barely four kilometres from Burundi's border with the DRC
from where rebels of the Forces nationales pour la liberation (FNL) and
the Forces pour la Defense de la Democratie (FDD) are believed to have
their bases. They have engaged government forces in a civil war that has
gone on for eight years.
All the way, about every 10 to 20 metres are checkpoints manned by Burundi
soldiers. Life seems to go on somehow in this part of the country, but
"cautiously".
Gihungwe camp, established in 1998, is some 40 minutes by road from the
capital Bujumbura, down a rough track bordered by long grass on both
sides. The military are present everywhere. Visitors are enthusiastically
greeted by the displaced people who live here. Shabbily dressed, most of
them barefoot, men, women and children surge forward to meet the new
arrivals.
A tour of the camp reveals small huts built from straw, many of them
precariously close to cooking fires. In almost every open space, peeled
cassava tubers are neatly arranged on the ground to dry in the heat of the
scorching sun. "After the cassava dries up, it is ground and the flour is
cooked in boiling water to make 'ugali' [maizemeal] which we eat with
cassava leaves locally known as sombe," the leader of the camp, Mark
Mvano, told IRIN.
"We harvest maize and beans sometimes," he said. "This time we did not get
beans." They have farms some two kilometres from the camp and cultivate
their fields during the day, returning to the camp at night for security.
Relief agencies have often stepped in to distribute 15-day food rations.
The last distribution was carried out on 21 April to assist some 226
families, whose fields had been destroyed by floods. Earlier in February,
the population at this site received seeds and accompanying seed
protection rations, to ensure that the seeds for planting were not eaten.
"When we have food we eat, but of course it is never much. When even that
little amount is not there, we go hungry. What can one do?" Mvano asked.
The inhabitants of this camp, some 382 households with a total of 1,976
people mostly from the Hutu ethnic group, fled their homes because of
persistent rebel attacks and looting. "They could come and take away
everything," Mvano said. "It was as if we were just preparing and keeping
the things for them."
"Not even our self protection groups could help us because the rebels were
better armed. We fled our homes and sought refuge here where there are
soldiers," he added. "But this is no life. We live in nests like birds."
Basic commodities are not familiar possessions for people in this camp.
"We do not have soap, salt, cooking oil and we kind of don't think about
them," he said. "Some people sell part of their produce to get some money
to buy such items from the neighbouring Gatumba market." Sometimes during
the food distributions, relief agencies give non-food items like soap too.
"It is a life of abject poverty," Mvano added.
Gihungwe camp also has a serious water problem. "Water has been targeted
in the war, you see. Now we get water some two kilometres away," he
explained.
The soldiers use one of the two permanent block buildings in the middle of
the camp as their residence. The other is the school, but many school-age
children stay away from the classrooms because "their parents cannot
afford the fees", Mvano said. "Some of them cannot buy books, pens because
they lack money." The school has four teachers who come from Gihanga
commune.
Agencies working in the area concede that children's access to education
is limited, as many schools in Bubanza have closed during the past years
of crisis. They say the remaining schools are often too crowded to be able
to accept new pupils.
Mvano is concerned about indiscipline among children in the camp. "Because
of the way we live in the camp, our children have learnt very bad manners
and there is no way we as parents can stop it."
But one of the greatest enemies of the population in this camp is malaria.
"There are a lot mosquitoes around. So many people suffer from malaria all
the time and yet they have no money to seek treatment," Mvano said.
Although he could not give statistics on the number of malaria-related
deaths, he said the disease was a big problem. The nearest public health
centre from the camp is eight kilometres away.
The British-based NGO Children's Aid Direct (CAD) has trained "agents de
sante communitaire" [health workers] among the population on the site who
carry out simple treatments. According to Mvano, pregnant women mostly
have to deliver in the camp because they could not afford to get to the
health clinic 8 km away. "When there are complications people in the camp
often try to donate money to rush the victim to the health facility."
There is also an infestation of rats in the camp. "It is serious because
they have destroyed most of our blankets and the already old and scanty
beddings. We just don't know what to do about them," Mvano added.
About 300 metres from the grass huts, the construction of semi-permanent
houses with iron sheet roofs is underway for some 286 households. "It is
the reinstallation village," Mvano said. "These are people who have
decided to reside in this area and not go back to their original homes. We
are hopeful that the war will end one day. But it has totally disrupted
our lives."
The concept of reinstallation is taking root among displaced people in
Burundi. As the war continues with no end in sight, people are beginning
to accept wherever they are now as "home". With the help of international
NGOs, they are building permanent houses in which they will live with no
intention of going back to their original homes.
One such project concerns a 1993 caseload of Tutsis who fled to the Ragaza
I site - also in Bubanza province - which is a school building. They are
waiting to occupy houses in nearby Ragaza II, a reinstallation site.
Since the farming lands of much of the population in the Ragaza site, some
726 people, are too far away to tend to, the commune of Gihanga has made
limited space of land available. The half a hectare per family will be
used to cultivate maize and sweet potatoes, but livestock cannot be kept.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Production "Generally Better"
Headline: Rwanda: Crop Production "Generally Better"
Body_Text:
Preliminary impressions from a joint assessment team which visited parts
of Rwanda during its 2001 B season noted that crop production was generally
better than the corresponding season last year when the rains prematurely
ended by mid-May and crops failed.
An update last week from USAID's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS) said
the rains were "moderate and fairly distributed" in May, which allowed
sorghum - the main seasonal crop - to mature, and sweet potatoes, cassava
and bananas to further develop. However, the heavy rains of late April and
early May caused the maturing beans to rot in the fields, especially the
high altitude areas, FEWS noted.
The update says that food security has improved markedly in the areas
where it had been most worrying over the last 12 months. "In fact, the
provinces that had been most affected by last year's droughts - Kigali
Rurale, Kibungo and Umutara provinces - appear to have among the best yields
of beans, sorghum, sweet potatoes and cassava this season," FEWS said. It
added that the "unexpected, but welcome" result was due to the location of
these provinces in the low and mid-altitude zones where crops were less
damaged by the excess rains than in higher altitude provinces.
As a result of last year's recurrent drought, the cultivated area was
less than normal in Bugesera region. For the second consecutive month, no
new cases of foot-and-mouth disease were reported in Rwanda and the
quarantine measures that had been taken in the livestock sector will be
eased across the country, which will almost completely restore the
traditional livelihood systems in pastoralist areas of northeastern
Rwanda.
FEWS noted that over the past two weeks the market has responded to the
current and expected supply and demand conditions of food staples in
Rwanda. "All prices decreased in the 36 markets monitored with the arrival
of season B 2001 harvests," FEWS said.

Original_Search_Title: Sabiiti Talks of More
Headline: Uganda: Sabiiti Talks of More
Body_Text:
Denis Sabiiti, the parliamentary candidate for Rubanda West constituency in
Kabale district, has promised to intensify the growing of clonal coffee to
boost the people's income.
He also pledged to introduce credit schemes in the parishes.
Sabiiti, who addressed the people of Kashaasha, Namilyango and Butanda
parishes
in Bufundi sub-county last week, urged the residents to grow irish and sweet
potatoes, tea, coffee and peas for external markets.
He pledged to avail sorghum milling machines besides the maize mills he
donated
recently.
Sabiiti also promised to lobby the Government to avail funds to reconstruct
the
main Kashasha-Muko-Kabale-Bufundi marram road.
Other candidates running for the Rubanda West parliamentary seat are Dr. Ezra
Suruma and Henry Banyenzaki.

Original_Search_Title: Solar Eclipse: A Sight to Behold
Headline: Zambia: Solar Eclipse: A Sight to Behold
Body_Text:
Lusaka — For most people who saw it-the June 21 total solar eclipse- was a
mesmerising spectacle, a sight to behold.  It was a sight that left an
indelible mark on their lives. A view most had not envisaged and when it did
happen, it created a feeling of awe and wonder at this spectacular sight
beyond human making. It was indeed of God.  Except for a few widely
travelled tourists, the total solar eclipse experienced on Thursday was the
first for many in Zambia, and what an exhilarating moment it was-the first
total solar eclipse in the new millennium.
"Have you ever experienced the night at 15:00 hours," I heard someone
close to where I stood ask his daughter, his voice filled with
excitement. It was really night time during the day.
Having driven all the way from Ndola to Chisamba to view the eclipse,
this author couldn't help but notice the number of vehicles on the road.
The Kapiri-Kabwe road had never seen such an influx of traffic in a day- all
heading southwards. The last time I saw so many cars dashing down to Lusaka
was when the Zambian national soccer team was at its peak and they were
having an international game. Then, soccer fans from the Copperbelt would
begin their journey 'early bells' to watch their stars.
But that was a long time ago! This time however, there was no question
about the destination of the vehicles. Just as well the day was declared a
national holiday.  And for the first time, I had never seen so many people,
especially Zambians, take an interest in an event such as this one. School
children (at least those whose teachers were not on strike) had been told
about the total eclipse and how to and not view it.
It was interesting to hear a four-year-old warn me about looking at the
sun directly because I would go blind! At least most of the children in
urban areas, and I want to believe in some rural areas, had been schooled on
this natural phenomenon that was about to engulf Zambia.
Kapiri-Mposhi service station on the way to Lusaka was agog with a large
number of travellers. The whole place was milling with people and women of
different shades thronged the fee paying lavatory. It was an indication of
the anticipation that had gripped most people. A colleague observed that
although the path of the eclipse was somewhere from Zambezi through Lusaka,
the event had created a business boom for people along the line of rail as
scores of people heading to Lusaka and other areas were making stopovers for
drinks and other services.
In fact, this author observed that most traders selling pumpkins, sweet
potatoes and other merchandise along the road had upped their prices.  And
the police, bless them, were at their best. There were no requests for
triangles and the many questions associated with them.  Their job was to
simply wave the cars to pass, or they would have created traffic jams.  This
author asked a police officer at a road block on the number of vehicles that
had passed. Never before had he seen so many cars in a day head towards the
capital.
Just after Kabwe, this author noticed several people had 'set up camp'
wherever they found a lay-by, in readiness for the big event.  I wondered
why some people were making noise about some lodges not allowing them to
enter and view the eclipse from there, when all they had to do was park a
vehicle and wait for the moon to block the sun from the earth's
inhabitants.
Several tourists, and I mean both local and international, were dotted
along the road to Lusaka waiting patiently.  At Banani where this author
viewed the eclipse, the mood was euphoric and some people were actually
picnicking and roasting meat on braai stands.  There was total anticipation
as the moon inched slowly towards the sun.  Suddenly, the shadows started to
show as they grew longer and bigger.  The moon was eating into the sun
making it look a half moon, then like a banana, then a thin arched
line.
It was indeed getting dark. One would have though it was 18.00 hours.
The evening butterflies were beginning to come out and the birds were going
to roost. How confusing it must have been for the insects and birds.
Then slowly, but surely, it had happened! It was 'night time' for just
over three minutes. And there in full view for all to watch without the
eclipse glasses, was the sun completely covered by the moon. It was only the
sun's corona or halo that was visible. It was a spectacular sight! The stars
were quite visible and someone noted those could be the planets like venus
which are close to the sun.  And in a frenzy, motorists blew their car horns
in a chorus while other people screamed 'it has happened', as though to
convince some critics who may have doubted.
"It was a beautiful moment and we gave God the glory because that is
only possible with God," a friend, Wendy Njovu remarked.
"It was quite an intoxicating moment for me. That was not a man-made
event, but what we say icilengwa Lesa (God made)," Nathan Zulu, a Kitwe
resident who had travelled all the way to Chisamba said.
In other parts of Africa, Angola, Zimbabwe and Mozambique thousands also
viewed the eclipse. On the island of Madagascar also witnessed the moon
cover the sun.  The event was not just exciting for those who experienced
the total eclipse. Even those in areas of the partial eclipse talked of the
excitement they felt when suddenly the afternoon looked like evening.  Some
even talked of a drop in temperature while a caller to ZNBC's live coverage
of the eclipse claimed to have a slight headache.
Psychological? We'll never know but there were different observations
made.  Scientists of course, had their own explanation of the eclipse, but
for the Christians, this was indeed, the work of the Almighty God. It
reminds one of the song "I sing the mighty power of God" for indeed it was
the might of the Most High at work.
It was one of nature's wonders that will leave a lasting impression on
the minds of those who saw this once-in-a-lifetime spectacle.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Primed for Possible Drought
Headline: Ghana: Farmers Primed for Possible Drought
Body_Text:
The Ministry of Food and Agriculture (MOFA) in the Upper East Region is to begin an emergency advisory service programme to educate farmers on the type of crops to grow during this year's farming season.
The move is in anticipation of a drought especially in the Bolgatanga area where for the past two months there has not been sufficient rain to enable farmers to plant.
For those who took advantage of the first rain in mid-April, their crops are withering due to poor rainfall.
The Deputy Regional Director of MOFA, Mr. Roy Ayariga, who made this known to the GNA at Bolgatanga last week, said the strategies the Ministry has adopted include advising farmers to use early maturing crops developed by the Manga Agriculture Research Centre near Bawku and to grow leguminous crops such as groundnuts and cowpea should the present trend continue.
Mr. Ayariga said farmers would be supplied with seedlings to cultivate root tubers such as sweet potatoes and Frafra potatoes to replace the traditional early millet whose planting season is almost over.
Mr. Ayariga said although it is too early to predict whether the whole of the region would face food deficit this farming season, his outfit has instituted adequate measures to ensure a successful season.
"Farmers here practise intercropping with variation in the period of  growing, and therefore if the rains have failed them on the millet, there could be other alternatives".
The Regional Director of the Meteorological Services Department, Mr. Nathan Afunyah said it has been difficult to explain the prevailing rainfall trend. "On several occasions we had announced the weather forecast on radio only for things to take a different turn".
Mr. Afunyah, however, said the problem is restricted within the Bolgatanga Township.  "The formation of the clouds moves to the outskirts of Bolgatanga where these area get the downpour".
Rains continue to threaten within Bolgatanga and surrounding areas with the formation of heavy clouds.  But whenever it is about to rain, all the clouds disperse, leaving the weather bright and sunny.

Original_Search_Title: From Cape to Cuba
Headline: South Africa: From Cape to Cuba
Body_Text:
Kalk Bay, with its air of shabby English chic, is at first glance an un-
likely place to find a restaurant redolent with the Spanish-African sounds
and symbols of Cuba. From Christ to Che Guevara, the walls of the Cape to
Cuba restaurant are covered with genuine icons and artefacts that reflect
the cultural and political history of the largest and most beautiful island
in the Caribbean.
And yet the strip of land between Kalk Bay's Main Road and the harbour is
rich with the smells and sights of a fishing village that probably has much
in common with towns along the Cuban coastline. Fishing boats unload their
catches of sleek, silvery snoek in the harbour. Rows of tarpaulin-covered
bakkies groan under loads of fresh fish that find their way to the street
markets of Mitchells Plain and countless households on the Cape Flats. This
is a people's place, and the lined, sunburnt faces of the fishermen and
vendors contrast with the smooth, tanned faces of the tourists who trawl the
art galleries, antique shops and restaurants along Main Road, below the
genteel homes that cling to the steep mountainside. Shades of both Battista
and Castro -- local reminders of the eternal divide between those who have
and those who do not.
Bart van Vuuren has long had a love affair with Cuba, so when the goods shed
of the Kalk Bay station was offered for sale last year, he and his wife
Deona set about transforming the ruin into a
restaurant-cum-cocktail-and-cigar bar where Cuban furniture and decor are
all for sale. On a trip to Cuba last year he filled a container with
"non-national heritage furniture, icons, cigars, black beans, sandals and
T-shirts" -- and you can pick them up here, after the Cuban bread-and-fruit
mayonnaise starter, but before the rum tartlet.
Strangely, it's not at all disconcerting to sip a chicken-and-lime soup with
avocado (R14) under a tableau of two revolutionary martyrs, Christ and Che,
whose images adorn the roughly plastered walls. Smudged orange and washed
cobalt blue complement each other in the main restaurant, where carved
Spanish colonial furniture competes for space with mismatched wooden, wicker
and plastic chairs and rough wooden tables. For those whose bank balances
can take it, there's a magnificent Cuban colonial cabinet for R39 000.
What do you order when the black beans run out, as they had when we dined
there? Try the Aves de Cubano -- chicken breasts in a fragrant blend of curry
and spices, served with rice and vegetables that include sweet potato in
pastry tubes topped with fresh basil (R39). Or a Hemmingway (sic) salad of
fresh Cape salmon brushed with pesto (R34) that tastes of the sea outside
and exotic places beyond. Nothing prepared us for the chilli ice dessert,
which is a parable for the way to perdition with its blend of cream and
hellfire. To help one along the path of destruction, wines range from a
rather bland Capells Court Chardonnay 2000 (R39) to Meerlust Merlot (R150).
Guests are welcome to dance to the sensual music of Cesaria Evoras CafÃ©
Atlantico or Los Guaracheros de Orientes Grandes Exitos under a collection
of fabulous European chandeliers and ornate lamps.
Cape to Cuba has been fully booked every night since opening in December,
catering mainly to locals who flock from Clifton and Camps Bay for the kick
of the Cape to Cuba cocktail and any one of 19 varieties of Cuban cigars --
from a Romeo y Juliet to a Cohiba Corona. Guests laze on the Hemingway deck
under Papa's gaze from a photo on the wall and watch the last of the
graffiti-covered Metro trains whizz by -- colourful interruptions to the view
of the harbour and the expanse of False Bay, and the magical Hottentots
Holland mountains on the horizon.
Cape to Cuba: Restaurant, cocktail bar, cigars, Cuban furniture and
artefacts is in Main Road, Kalk Bay. Tel: (021) 788 1566; Fax: (021) 788
3695

Original_Search_Title: Japanese Crackdown Hurts Spares Market
Headline: Uganda: Japanese Crackdown Hurts Spares Market
Body_Text:
Prices for second hand vehicle and motorcycle spare parts are set to rise, due
to an increasing shortage of supply in the local market. This also explains
the
higher rate of thefts for vehicle accessories in the city centre.
Following police reports of second hand motorcycle and vehicle robberies in
Japan last year, the Japanese government imposed stricter restrictions on
issuing visas to Ugandan parts dealers. According to sources, Japanese police
uncovered a racket in which these stolen motorcycles and vehicles are later
sold to African dealers.
"Look! My shop is empty, there is nothing to sell, but I have spent $900 in
three trips I have made to Nairobi to crosscheck on whether the visa I applied
for in March is ready. Up to now it has not been issued yet they issued me
with this receipt confirming that I had satisfied the requirements," One of
the importers said at his empty shop in Ndeeba a Kampala suburb.
This was presumed to eliminate fraudsters, but many genuine importers have
found the new bureaucracy frustrating.Several dealers who Business Vision
talked to confessed they have run out of business, with their shops empty as
they impatiently wait for replies to their visa applications tendered to the
embassy some six months or longer.
Now sources say the robberies that had hit Japan have been transferred to
Uganda as scarcity of Japanese-made spare parts has created lucrative market
for the robbers.
About fifty importers who preferred anonymity for fear of jeopadising their
pending visa applications at the Japanese embassy in Nairobi told Business
Vision the delays in issuing Japanese visas have exposed their businesses to
uncertainty and caused huge losses.
He said that before the end of last year it took about a week for the
importers
to obtain a Japanese visa but today many traders who applied in January have
not received them.
Before September last year, Japanese Business visa application guidelines
required the importer to present one recent passport photograph, a valid
passport and a visa form duly completed, a letter explaining in detail the
nature of the business with a return air ticket as well as shipping bills,
invoices of previous transactions and detail sales records.
The new Embassy guidelines require that the applicants deposit original
certificates of incorporation and an original trading licenses of their
companies together with applications.
The Embassy also asks for telegraphic transfer forms and foreign exchange
receipts as proof of funds for travel, in addition to officially certified
bank
statements for the previous six months.
"We have met all these requirements amidst hardship, taken these certified
bank
statements to the Embassy, but they keep tossing us around and they even go
ahead to write to the banks for verification," the angry dealers told Business
Vision complained over the weekend.
Others bitterly added that to apply for a Japanese visa, one has to first
send
money to the supplier in Japan who has to act as a guarantor and whose
invitation and certified legal documents of his company must be received by
the
Nairobi embassy prior to issuance of the visa.
"I have deposited $22,000 at the embassy just as proof that that I have a
genuine transaction with my host," another motor cycle importer said.
All importers talked to said that before reports of massive motorbike
thefts in
Japan at the end of last year visa processing would take only one Week.
"Today despite a note pinned at the embassy reading that visas are processed
within three days, the actual time is indefinite," they noted adding that
besides, the officers at the embassy are so rude that they don't allow traders
to query any delays.
"They should tell us. Even if it takes four months, but when we know it is
four
months, we would plan accordingly," one of them proposed desperately.
The traders say they continuously reschedule their booking at the airlines,
which is so hectic and expensive.
"We would like to know whether the government of Japan has a direct policy
against Ugandans. They should tell us so that we find other things to do,"
angry traders quarreled.
Despite an alternative to order for the goods without physically going to
Japan, the importers say second hand items need the buyer to physically see
them to avoid substandard imports.
They argue that the actual motorcycle robbers are well established in Japan
and that some of them are married to Japanese women which warrant them to one
year's visas in the country.
"Besides the Ugandan genuine traders helped the Japanese security agencies in
compiling a register of the robbers and they have this list now," the traders
revealed.
There have been reports of increased robberies of motorcycles, Japanese-made
cars and vandalism of spare parts in Uganda.
Sources within the dealers told Business Vision that there is a bigger market
of spare parts following the restrictions On importers.
Police spokesman Eric Naigambi could not confirm whether there is an increase
in motorcycle and car robberies, saying that figures are still being compiled.
"What I know is that there is a direct link between the sale of second hand
vehicles and these robberies. This is obvious. Many people have found their
stolen vehicles and spares on the market," he said May 28
He added that many of the stolen vehicles are kept in high walls around
Kampala.
"One probably needs helicopters to trace these stolen vehicles," Naigambi
said. The Prices of second hand motorcycles have equally gone up by about 20
percent according to most dealers talked to with the Yamaha Badz commonly
used for bodaboda going at average of sh1.2m compared to about sh1m at the end
of last year.
When Business Vision contacted the visa section at the Japanese Embassy in
Nairobi on phone, the head of the visa section only identified as Mayikawa
said they issue visas everyday but was noncommittal on how long it takes to
get one.
"We issue visas everyday and you should come and see this for yourself," he
told Business Vision on Monday.
He however refused to explain why the guidelines had been changed by the end
of last year saying that it would be explained by the Japanese government.

Museveni Cautions
By John Eremu
PRESIDENT Yoweri Museveni has asked donors to help Uganda add value to its
traditional export crops, to make them more competitive on the international
market.
Museveni in a recent day-long talk with donors doubted whether non-traditional
export crops, like flowers would not be substituted by synthetic products in
future.
Ms. Dawn Liberi from USAID had presented a paper in which she said Uganda
could
diversify its exports by diversifying into non-traditional crops like flowers
and vanilla, which have ready markets in Europe.
But Museveni asked: "How durable is this flower market, because I don't
want to
tell people to grow flowers and tomorrow again tell them not to grow because
there is a synthetic flower."
Museveni said he was sure coffee and cotton-based textiles would be in demand
as long as the human race existed. "Why don't you support me in cotton,
because
all these people in the world will need clothes provided they have not
forgotten the lessons of Adam," the humourous Museveni said throwing the
audience into laughter.
Museveni said a tonne of lint cotton when transported to Mombasa costs $1,800
(sh3.2m), but would fetch up to $15,000 (sh27m) when processed into garments.
Likewise, he said, two-and-a-half kilogrammes of coffee beans fetch a mere $2
(sh3,600), but when processed into instant coffee costs as much as $70
(sh126,000)in London shops.
Museveni said he had secured a coffee market in China, but that the biggest
problem now is processing the coffee. He said the foods giant, Nestle has
turned down his requests to set up coffee processing plants here, but that
Uganda will now take the challenge.
"If the private sector can't do it, the government by teaming up with the
private sector will do it," Museveni said.
The President commended the donors for their continued support to Uganda and
assured them of his government's commitment to total transformation of the
socio-economic condition of the people. He said Uganda must industrialise if
transformation is to be achieved.
He praised the EU for allowing tariff free access for Ugandan products.

Welcome Offer From The Police
Business week in review by Mulinde Musoke
Paying a little extra for goods and services, tends to guarantee better
returns
on your money. Trying to save money often ends up with you losing much more.
Several individuals and institutions will have learnt this lesson, after
seeing
millions in cash being robbed, simply because they did not want to use the
guard services of the police. Some sceptics have suggested that perhaps all
these robberies are not as genuine as they appear and there is a racket to
defraud. Nonetheless, the offer by Police chief, Maj. Gen. Katumba Wamala to
provide free guard services should be commended. Anyone ignoring this new
service deserves to be robbed.
An odd 1000 people in the coming weeks, with shares in Uganda Clays Limited,
are going to experience the joys of being a shareholder. Perhaps the amounts
in question will not be substantial, but the feeling of being a recipient can
be satisfying. The ritual of being paid dividends places these people in the
high stakes world of corporate investment. The challenge for the rest of
us, is
to learn a little more financial sacrifice and patience by also investing in
the local stock exchange.
It was encouraging to read that German interests have plans to go into cassava
processing. Specifically to get inputs for the pharmaceutical industry. This
project is another example of how we can develop and build a strong
agro-industry. The foreign component in processing is low, but the forex
return
is high.

Caltex beefs up customer services
By Stephen Ilungole
CALTEX Oil Uganda on Tuesday, launched a customer service day aimed at
emphasising the character and personality of the Caltex brand.
Caltex general manager, James Chilongo said during the launch, the customer
service day, the first of its in the history of the oil industry in Uganda,
will be held on monthly basis in different filling stations throughout
their
over 70 filling stations spread allover the country.

in Kampala, saw Chilongo, together with other top managers, for a couple of

after he finished with his duties of a pump attendant.
Chilongo who also launched the customer hotline number, 041-348333 said
Caltex
oil customer service emphasis, does not only centre on the retail outlets
"but
the entire operations of Caltex Oil as a company. We now have a full

eager to attend to customers."

In love with vanilla
By Macrines Nyapendi
Growing vanilla has become a moneymaker for John Nviiri's, an elderly man and
resident of Mukono. He adds that "The sweet smelling aroma is pleasant to the
senses" and was a major reason why he went into producing the crop.
He got to know the value of vanilla when he visited Brussels for a trade fair
way back in 1962, when Uganda's vanilla stole the world attention. The spice
recorded the highest vanillin content and was purely organic.
Vanilla growing came to a standstill when Idi Amin Dada expelled the
Asians, in
1972.
Nviiri re-introduced the spice, in 1975,and with its 1962 fame, he knew it
would be marketable.
He said the late Dr. Samson Kisekka connected them to an international firm
called Questa International, which is based in Canada.
"We were later invited to brief the President (Museveni) about vanilla
farming,
and its standing in the international market," Nviiri said.
After this, USAID pledged to provide technical assistance to the farmer's
nationwide. Vanilla needs considerable attention, but it is also more paying
than other crops and spices.
"I voluntarily started teaching people how to grow the spice using a radio
programme called 'Vanilla Bugagga' on Radio Uganda", adds Nviiri.
However he complains that, "Whenever I go to the Ministry of Agriculture, and
ask for the literature of the spice it is unavailable."
But he has no plans to abandon the business just yet.
