Debt not beneficial, say MPs
--Uganda's Sixth Parliament, whose term expires on July 3, this year, has
approved over US$ 1.14b (about sh1.9 trillion), about 25% of the country's
debt
stock. Business Vision's Richard Mutumba reports.
With Uganda's debt stock already standing at over US $ 3.5 billion, the more
than US$ 1.14bn approved for borrowing by Parliament makes a significant
proportion of the country's indebtedness.
"Within half a decade we have borrowed such amounts of money, but we have
nothing much to show for it," Salaamu Musumba, MP Bugabula South and a
Parliamentary Commissioner said. "Instead we are now a donor, project-driven
country."
Musumba said although statistically the borrowed money makes good reading, it
should be born in mind that even the growth reflected in the GDP figures is of
borrowed money. She said it was highly questionable if such growth had led to
real development.
Like other members of Parliament, Musumba had requested for the summation of
all borrowing approved by the House in the course of the 6h Parliament.
"I am worried we did not keep stock of the money we were borrowing and how it
was being used." she said. "It was also evident that we had no mechanism of
keeping all this in check."
Before Parliament adjourned last week, State minister for finance and planning
Manzi Tumubweine, moved the motion seeking for the approval of $22m (about
sh39.2b) from the International Development Agency (IDA), for environment
management programmes; and $63.9m (about sh124b) from the International Fund
for Agricultural Development (IFAD) and the World Bank, for funding the
National Agricultural Advisory Services (NAADS).
Government also wanted to borrow $26m (48.6bn) from African Development Bank
(ADB), to finance the second education project, which covers the Universal
Primary Education (UPE). Tumubweine said the money would be used to construct
about 2500 classrooms, 348 teachers' houses, 978 latrines and buy 44,000
desks.
Another loan of $20.4m (about sh36b) from ADB to finance road maintenance
programmes in central Uganda, covering Gaba-Tank Hill, Kibuye-Makindye,
Nansana-Busunju, and Gayaza-Kalaji among roads, was also approved, in addition
to $40.1m (sh71.3bn) for Health Sector Strategic Plan (HSSP).
The money will be used to expand and rehabilitate mental health care
facilities
in Gulu, Soroti, Kabale, Hoima and Fort Portal regional Hospitals.
The Nordic Development Fund is also to lend government $9.2m (sh16bn) to
finance phase II of the road development programme.
Before approving this week's loans, Parliament had earlier approved 31 other
loans amounting to US $961.7m (about sh1.692trillion), for government projects
in various sectors of the economy.
The biggest loan contracted by Government and approved by Parliament, amounted
to $125m from the World Bank, for the Structural Adjustment Credit Project
(SAC
III), according to statistics compiled by the Parliamentary research unit.
Musumba said she was concerned about the Spanish loan worth US $ 24m, that
Parliament authorised in 1996, to finance the upgrading and supply of
equipment
to Kisoro and Kamuli hospitals.
"We were informed that if the House did not approve the loan on that day, we
would miss out on it. We passed the bill on condition that MPs, the districts
concerned and the Ministry of Health would scrutinise and renegotiate the
loan.
Everybody was left in a bitter taste. To date, we have used all channels at
our
disposal to find out how this money was used, but we cannot adequately account
for it," she says.
With such an experience, a number of MPs said they are reluctant to approve
the
borrowing of any other money, without accountability for previous loans.
The enactment of the Budget Bill, 2000, establishing the Parliamentary Budget
Office, may provide a mechanism for monitoring budget performance.
Parliament on December 19, 2000 passed the Budget law that seeks to strengthen
the powers of Parliament to monitor the enforcement and control of the
national
budget and expenditure estimates.
The law requires government at the time of presentation of the budget in every
financial year, to present to Parliament information relating to national
indebtedness.
About the time of presentation of the Budget in every financial year,
government shall cause to be presented to Parliament, information relating to
loan guarantees and grants made to individuals, companies or statutory
corporations and Parliament must be kept abreast of the performance of such.
Musumba decried the fact that for a significant amount of money borrowed in
the
course of the 6th Parliament, there was no proper consideration on its
necessity, environmental and other impact assessments.
"Can you imagine a food basket like Uganda borrowing colossal sums of money
for
children's supplements?" she asked.
Musumba cited the Northern Uganda Reconstruction Programme (NURP) for which
Parliament approved a loan. She said if it had not been for MTN Uganda, there
would hardly be any notable telephone system for the districts of Kitgum, Gulu
and Karamoja.
She also said it was doubtful the vegetable oil project is doing as well as it
should be. She, however, said some work was being done on the Owen Falls Dam
project and the Nakivubo Channel.
"Look at the Youth Scheme, would the youth be frustrated if the money was
properly targeted?" Musumba asked.
"I thought we were building social and physical infrastructure, upon which we
could bank for development. With all this borrowing, we have hardly built any
infrastructure," Mutumba said.
She said some loans were 'killing' self-determination.
Mutumba said Ugandans Ugandans are apparently, not looking at alternative ways
of surviving, instead borrow money and use it to buy cars and computers.
"We have missed the opportunity of cheap foreign money. I don't think this
goodwill will last forever. If the money had been put to good use, it would
have propelled this country far," she said.
"The biggest challenge for Parliament is to institute a mechanism of ensuring
that borrowed money is put into proper use. Hopefully, the 7th Parliament will
achieve that when the requirements of The Budget Act, 2000 are implemented,"
Musumba said.

Kabale targets pyrethrum
--FARMERS in Kabale have now discovered a viable cash crop that is belived to
boost farmers returns. Business Vision's Darious Magara reports about how,
Pyrethrum (echrysanthemum cinerariefolium), is the most viable crop that which
is fast replacing other cash crops in the area.
Traditional cash crops such as sorghum and Irish potatoes no longer yield much
as their markets are not stable and their production is low since the soils
has
lost fertility.
Kabale's unique and mountainous terrain but cool climate and good soils
together with fair amount of rainfall favours the growth of pyrethrum.
Pyrethrum, known in Kabale as Senturuma was first introduced in the area
thanks
to Agro Management (U) Limited .in 1991 and was the first plant of its kind in
Uganda.
It was mostly grown in Rukiga County but has now expanded to other areas in
the
district. It has also been introduced in Kasese and Kisoro districts, in South
Western Uganda.
Agro Management general manager Mr. Martin Oweka said the unique crop is not
only benefiting locals but will change the tide of Uganda's foreign exchange
stabilisation.
Oweka, in an interview with Business Vision last week, said "Pyrethrum
produced and processed in Uganda is the best with the highest quality in the
World today.
It's being used in branding with other products from Papua New Guinea and
Tasmania to come up with a medium quality which is finally sent to the world
market.
Oweka added, "About 1.5billion shillings will be earned in the year 2002 but
Uganda's pyrethrum exports supplied to the world market are still far below
the
required amount. If the government encourages the farmers to or increase their
production to full capacity then, Uganda would earn millions of dollars
annually from pyrethrum," he said.
He said the prices to the farmers are being revised regularly and they are
expected to rise in the near future.
The flowers are bought at sh2200 per kg from the farmers by Agro Management
officials who have a network of collecting centres in the district.
The chief agronomist Mr. Ronald Martin said farmers harvest 10kg of
pyrethrum from an acre.
"Pyrethrum was first grown in nursery beds for four months with seeds similar
to those of carrots. It's thereafter taken to the main garden and after
another
four months the first flowers can be seen," Martin said.
A farmer would from then harvest flowers every week. The mature flowers are
then dried and taken to the nearest collecting centre.
The flowers are later taken to the $3million processing plant that has
capacity
of processing 4 tonnes per day.
The factory is located on Crematorium road in Mwanjari about 2km from Kabale
town.
The company's agents at different centres advise farmers on how best they can
produce the crop and maintain its high quality.
They also distribute seeds to farmers.
Pyrethrum, is a plant with daisy-like flowers which contain six esters called
pytherins which are natural insecticides used for plant protection against
noxious insects.
The pyrethrum plant grows up to three feet high and can also be harvested by
children who would be required to know what a mature flower looks like.
The spacing of the crop is 2 feet by one foot and the plant population per
acre
should not exceed a certain limit.
Agro Management, is owned by the wife of Marilyn Boyd an Afro-American
millionaire from California USA.
An American company M.K.G of Minnesota in the US, buys all the pyrethrum
produced in Kabale.
Pyrethrum was first grown in Rukiga county but it has now spread to the whole
district.
But Agro-Management officials are still faced with challenges of changing a
misconception of locals that the cash crop reduces soil fertility and it's
poisonous.

Investors should make use of EAC
In January this year, the three East African leaders, President Yoweri
Museveni
of Uganda, Daniel arap Moi of Kenya and Benjamin Mkapa of Tanzania appended
their signatures to the East African Community (EAC) treaty in Arusha,
Tanzania.
The signing of the treaty signified the coming into force, a framework for the
formation of a common tax regime and total elimination of tariff and
non-tariff
barriers.
The conclusion of the treaty signing paved way for next level of negotiations
for a customs union, a common currency and a political federation. As
elaborate
as the treaty is, the major challenge is to translate document into
opportunities for the people in East Africa.
The establishment of a Customs Union will be an entry point in the process of
regional integration in East Africa, and will be achieved by the signing of a
negotiated Protocol.
With a population of 80 million, a single East African market could attract
billions of dollars, and could be even extended further if Rwanda and Burundi
are brought on board.
Uganda Investment Authority (UIA)'s strategy in attracting investments is
two-pronged. The investment promotion drive does not only sell Uganda as a
destination but also informs investors to take advantage of a bigger and
expanded market since goods will have free access within the
region.
When the community is fully functional, an investor may be located in Uganda
but the goods and services produced will move across the borders to Kenya and
Tanzania without payment of import duty to recipient countries.
For local investors UIA along with Uganda Manufacturers Association, Private
Sector Foundation and Chamber of Commerce are sensitising their members on the
challenges of being competitive as we open up the
borders.
This is to ensure that by the year 2004 when borders are opened, Ugandan
manufacturers are ready to face the challenge by producing quality goods and
pricing to compete with goods from within and outside the region.
Concerns of a huge trade imbalance between the three countries has been
addressed by proposals that will lead to favourable competition in the areas
of contention and underlying structures.

Uganda 3 0 1 2 1 7 1
Leading scorers:
6 - Etienne Bito'o (Gabon)
4 - Tasfaout (Algeria), Debbah (Liberia), Mubiru (Uganda), Petros
(Zimbabwe)
3 - Barro (B. Faso), Loko (Chad), Appiah (Ghana), Yoffou, Zoko (I.
Coast), Daye, Zizi (Liberia), Masli (Libya), Hary (Madagascar),
Goraseb (Namibia), Ikpeba (Nigeria), Mwaruwaru, Ndlovu (Zimbabwe).

Original_Search_Title: Researchers Attest to Safety of Genetically Modified Crops
Headline: Kenya: GM Crops Safe
Body_Text:
A new research has confirmed that genetically modified crops survive no better that their traditional counterparts.
The 10 - year study on the performance of modified crops conducted by the Imperial College in England allays fears that the genetically altered crops could stray from the farm fields and invade other natural inhabitants where they are not grown.
The research sampled four generically modified crops: maize, potato, oilseed rape and sugar beet. The researchers say they modified these four crops to equip them with the ability to resist or withstand insects and herbicides.
The trial seedlings were planted in 12 habitats in Britain, ranging from woodlands to Coastal areas. The study found out that neither the traditional nor the modified plants increased in numbers beyond their first plantings and the modified plants never lasted significantly longer that the traditional ones.
According to a Monsanto-Kenya publication, Kuza: "All the genetically engineered corn, oilseed, rape and sugar beet died out within four years. The modified sweet potato died too." says the publication.
The research also published in the authoritative scientific journal, NATURE says that the research was financed by a consortium of biotechnology multinationals including Monsanto Co. and Zeneca Ag products Inc, to find out: -
Whether the genetically engineered plants would crowd out natural species, cause health risk in humans and pass on traits such as herbicide resistance to weeds.
The research conducted by ecologist Michael J. Crawley also investigated the possibility of genetically engineered crops killing beneficial insects.
Crawley, the head of the team is quoted in the journal as saying "problem plants have attributes that are totally different from Crop plants. No matter what you do, an oilseed rape or wheat plant, it won't become a problem.
"This research, although concentrated more on the co-existence of genetically modified crops with the traditional plants, paying little attention to effects on human health, will be a milestone towards re-assuring countries skeptical of the safety of G.M crops that all is okay," says Dr. Hezron Kimani, an Agrobiologist.
"What is needed is further research on effects of G.M. to human health and African natural habitat. Again. The scientists modifying crops should work more on strengthening the survival of G.M.crops and more yields, because if the results from their study is anything to go by: "They survive no better than their traditional counterparts" adds Dr. Kimani.
Kenya is also trying G.M. crops. Last year, Kenya Agriculture Research Institute (KARI) collaborating with Monsanto launched the first ever genetically modified sweet Potato.
The transgenic sweet potato is said to have a protein coat that boasts its resistance to diseases and virus. If the trial goes on well, the virus resistant tubers are scheduled for distribution to farmers as soon as bio-safety is guaranteed.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 20 - Africa
Headline: Southern Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 20 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: (A) Resource situation for EMOPS and PRROs (B)
Southern Africa: (1) Angola, (2) Namibia (F) Horn of Africa: (1)
Ethiopia, (2) Eritrea, (3) Kenya) (C) Great Lakes (1) Tanzania, (2)
Burundi (D) West Africa Coastal: (1) Guinea, (2) Liberia
(A) Resource situation for EMOPS and PRROs
(a) During the first half of 2001, donors have continued to respond
favourably to substantial needs of emergency (EMOP) and protracted
relief and recovery operations (PRRO) of WFP. So far this year, USD
550 million have been contributed. Together with contributions called
forward in late 2000 and allocations from savings from 2000
contributions, these funds have been used to purchase, transport and
distribute some 1.4 million tons of food for the PRROs and EMOPs.
Nevertheless, a number of important operations are currently facing
serious shortfalls. Total relief shortfalls as of 13 May 2001
amounted to 2 million tons of food, worth USD 1.1 billion.
(B) Southern Africa: (1) Angola, (2) Namibia
(1) Angola
(a) During this week, the security situation remained unstable in
most provinces. Arrival of new IDPs was reported in the provinces of
Bie, Malange and Moxico. Night-time crime in Luanda has been on a
rise. Attacks in the province of Bengo continue, in particular in the
areas of Icau and Murima where WFP, FAO and FIDA have been
implementing a joint Food Security Project. 3,389 IDPs coming mainly
from Kambandua, Katabola, Kunhinga, Nharea and Andulo were registered
in Bie province between 7-11 May. Numerous attacks took place in
Uige.
(b) Fuel shortages in Catumbela are affecting the number of weekly
airlifts. Last week, less than 25 percent of the planned cargo were
airlifted from Catumbela and Benguela airports. WFP, UNESCO, FAO, and
the Ministry of Education visited Benguela and Baia Farta this week
with a view of implementing a pilot project for school feeding.
(c) The number of malnourished children in MSF-B TFCs in Kuito is now
reaching 600. Numbers in both SFCs and TFCs are increasing every week
due to the arrival of displaced families from Camacupa. In Bie
Province, 546 tons of WFP food were delivered to 43,726 beneficiaries
(mainly new IDPs) this week in Kuito.
(d) In Kwanza Norde province, 139 tons of food were delivered through
MINSA and World Vision International to 9,436 beneficiaries (mainly
IDPs) in the municipalities of Cazengo, Lucala and Golungo- Alto.
(e) In Luanda province, WFP, together with implementing partners,
distributed 21 tons of emergency 15-day rations to 3,000 IDPs from
Caxito. These IDPs were re-settled at Bairro Caop Velho, Funda in the
municipality of Cacuaco, where 4 hectares of land were made
available. 7,000 residents of Boa Vista area were reported to be
suffering from flood damages and MINARS was requested to draw up an
emergency action plan to assist them. In accordance with a joint
nutritional programme with MINSA, WFP assisted, this week 1,025 under
five children currently admitted in the hospital.
(f) The re-settlement of 25,000 IDPs is moving forward in Malange.
15,000 IDPs have been already re-settled in Lau after it was
de-mined. Re-settlement at the Quissol and Cambondo areas is
scheduled to begin soon. This week, WFP distributed to various
implementing partners, 198 tons of food which is expected to benefit
83,980 people in Malange.
(g) The nutritional situation in Moxico province is under control,
according to MSF-B, but the general population is highly vulnerable
and requires regular monitoring. MSF-B carried out a nutritional
survey among 3,882 IDPs and residents, between 7 and 11 May and
concluded that there is global malnutrition of 4 percent and a severe
malnutrition of 0.4 percent.
(h) The FAO/WFP Crop and Food Supply Assessment Mission began on 7
May with visits to Huambo, Huila and Malange this week. The Mission
intends to proceed to Lunda Sul, Moxico, Bie, Benguela, and Cunene
provinces and is expected to make a presentation of its preliminary
findings to the Government, donors and the humanitarian community on
25 May.
(i) Despite a new contribution of USD 3.24 million which will be used
for the PRRO, the Passenger Air Service and the purchase of non- food
Items, the PRRO for Angola continues to be significantly under-
funded.
(2) Namibia
(a) Following the confirmation of a new contribution of USD 1 million
for the Namibia EMOP, the food pipeline has been secured for the rest
of 2001. Procurement of CSB, Sugar and Oil will proceed shortly, as
these commodities are already lacking. Food rations can now return to
the normal level. The next distribution is scheduled for 4-5 June.
Supplementary Feeding Programmes will also be able to continue. Due
to the previous funding problems, a 71 percent ration had been
distributed for the month of May.
(C) Horn of Africa: (1) Ethiopia, (2) Eritrea, (3) Kenya
(1) Ethiopia
(a) General conditions have improved in the Somali Region, apart from
the pockets of serious needs among the drought displaced and people
in areas that have not received sufficient rain (Denan in Gode zone,
Danot and parts of Warder woreda in Warder zone and southern parts of
Afder and Liben zones). Good gu rain has been reported in most of the
region, though it is has been erratic and not evenly spread. Not much
rain was received in Gode woreda, but the river has flooded at the
usual locations that depend on flood recession agriculture. Rain has
recently been so heavy in Fik and Degehbour zones that road access
has been affected. Improved pasture and water supply, and an imminent
increase in milk supply are all promising signs for the region in
general.
(b) However, food aid reductions and problems with targeting,
combined with seasonal disease patterns and general lack of access to
health services, have led to deterioration of the situation among the
drought displaced population in various locations in Somali Region
(Fafen, Hartisheik and Kebrebeyah in Jijiga zone, Denan in Gode zone,
Gunegedo in Degehbour zone, Danot and nearby locations in Warder
zone, several locations in Fik zone and in north-eastern Afder zone).
Surveys in several of these locations show high levels of
malnutrition among children under five. The federal Disaster
Prevention and Preparedness Commission (DPPC) took action following
pressure from the regional DPP Bureau, WFP and MSF-B, and have
allocated more food to drought displaced and resident population in
Denan, Gode zone. Additional grain and some supplementary food have
also been allocated by DPPC for the drought displaced in Hartisheik,
Kebrebeyah and Fafen in Jijiga zone. A local NGO has reopened its
feeding centre in Fafen, and is opening a therapeutic feeding centre
in Hartisheik. A feeding centre has also been opened in Danot in
Warder zone.
(c) Belg rains have been good in many parts of the country. They
continued into mid-May in parts of the highlands, and have caused
flooding in some locations. The belg producing areas in the north-
eastern highlands include South Tigray, North and South Wello and
north Shewa. Extended belg rains, while good for the belg harvest,
may delay planting of meher crops in some areas. Pre-positioning of
food may also be affected.
(d) Rains still continue in the western parts of the country. These
are expected to be beneficial for planting long cycle crops and the
flowering of perennial crops mainly coffee. The southern parts of the
country including Borena zone, which are mainly dependent on the Belg
rains, have received good rains. The lowland areas of Bale have still
not received sufficient rain. Since these are among the areas that
suffered from consecutive drought and subsequent shortage of water
and pasture, they will remain areas of concern if the poor rainfall
situation persists.
(e) In a recent DPPC meeting with NGOs, concern was expressed about
the lack of confirmed pledges to cover distributions after June. For
various reasons, distributions during first part of year were not as
regular as expected.
(2) Eritrea
(a) ERREC reported distribution 1,480 tons of food to 86,059
beneficiaries. In addition, WFP provided ICRC with 684 tons of wheat
to be immediately distributed to 45,000 beneficiaries in the TSZ.
This caseload will be incorporated in the general feeding by ERREC in
June.
(b) There was no food distribution in the Northern Red Sea and Anseba
Regions due to lack of food commodities. Deliveries for May were also
delayed in Anseba and Northern Red Sea Region. It is expected that
there will be a full distribution in the next two weeks, to all
beneficiaries.
(c) Repatriation of IDPs has started. To date, about 16,000 IDPs have
successfully been resettled from camps in NRS, Gash Barka and Debub
Regions. All of them have received 30 days food ration from WFP prior
to departure.
(d) Return of Eritrean refugees from Sudan started, with the first
group of 934 refugees returning on 12 May. WFP has provided the
returnees with a two-month food ration on departure in Sudan, while
ERREC provided each returnee with 200 gr. of WFP biscuits upon
arrival in Tessenei.
(e) WFP conducted Post Distribution Monitoring (PDM) in Habero sub-
zones of the Anseba region where 27,830 beneficiaries are assisted.
The major findings indicated that WFP supplied food aid reached the
intended beneficiaries for whom food aid is the principal source of
livelihood. In addition, WFP food played a salient role in preserving
livestock assets and reduced the movement of population. WFP
beneficiaries were able to prepare the land on time.
(f) The Ministry of Education plans to start rehabilitation of
schools in the TSZ, beginning September. WFP is considering to
initiate school-feeding programme and possible FFW activities in
collaboration with the Ministry and UNICEF.
(g) Normal wet season is currently a month late in the highlands. WFP
monitors reported sighting dead animals. Nonetheless, farmers have
started to prepare the fields and pastoralists have moved their
livestock from the lowlands to the highlands, in search of better
pasture. In the war affected regions, there has been increased
farming activities during the past two weeks, mainly preparation of
fields.
(h) The general security situation in the TSZ and surrounding areas
remains calm. Mine incidents have become commonplace in areas
surrounding Shilalo and Senafe. This is of grave concern, in view of
the relocation of IDPs back to their areas of origin within the TSZ
and risks for WFP staff travelling through these areas to provide
assistance to the newly resettled population.
(3) Kenya
(a) The western half of Kenya received normal to above-normal
rainfall, while the eastern side received below-average rain during
April, according to data provided by the Kenya Meteorological
Department. Vegetation conditions have improved significantly in most
areas of the country.
(b) Key livestock production indicators are improving in several
areas of the western pastoral districts,, although the Arid Lands
Resource Management Project (ALRMP) reported that grazing is still
poor. Pastoralists are returning back to their normal wet-season
grazing areas in western Turkana, Marsabit and Samburu Districts.
(c) However, the food security prospects of pastoralists in the
eastern pastoral districts are uncertain. Livestock banditry has
constrained the recovery of pastoralists. In Turkana Districts,
farmers have moved from the relatively more resource endowed southern
parts into the central drier areas, due to the raids from southern
neighbouring districts.
(d) Over 3.5 million people in Kenya are being targeted under the
EMOP, under the general distribution and the Expanded School Feeding
Programme (ESFP). Around 72,000 people are expected to benefit from
WFP food through FFW schemes, which will operate in the six districts
where general distributions have been phased out (Kitui, Mwingi,
Makueni, Tharaka, Mbeere and Machakos Districts). There is currently
a challenge to resource non-food inputs for implementation of the FFW
activities.
(e) Of the estimated 547,952 tons of food required for the EMOP for
the period June 2000 to July 2001, around 78 percent have been
received from donors. Delays in the arrival of shipments have caused
problems since January. A pipeline break combined with internal
transport problems influenced the combination of the April and May
distributions into one. An incoming shipment in June and the
contribution from the Kenyan government will cover cereal
requirements for June and July.
(f) The current phase of the EMOP closes at the end of June, but
there are sill areas where food assistance is required, particularly
the northern pastoral districts. WFP is currently drafting a proposal
for a new phase of the EMOP, which will reflect the needs for
continued general distribution (in certain districts), emergency
school feeding and FFW activities during the period July to December.
(g) Recent UNICEF/NGO Nutrition Surveys showed that the overall
nutritional situation across drought-affected districts has generally
improved, mostly due to the emergency interventions during the last
ten months. However, current gains could easily be lost in the
absence of continued supplementary feeding for the children. The
Health and Nutrition co-ordination group is currently revising
beneficiary figures, reviewing early warning information and
discussing phase-out strategies.
(h) Tribal tensions in Marsabit caused restrictions of movement for
UN personnel. The Isiolo to Marsabit road was reported as
increasingly unsafe. Bandits attacked a Government vehicle in Garissa
District on 9 May and killed one person.
(D) Great Lakes (1) Tanzania, (2) Burundi
(1) Tanzania
(a) The security situation was calm in all camps. However, increased
crime rates were reported in Greater Lukole and Kitali Hills camps
after the holding of camp elections in March.
(b) During the last week of April and first week of May, WFP
distributed under various programmes a total of 3,526 tons of food in
Kigoma, Kasulu, Kibondo and Ngara districts: 523,734 refugees
(provisional UNHCR figures) received 80 percent of a two-week ration
under the general food distribution. A full ration was distributed to
5,481 vulnerable individuals in the four districts. In addition, WFP
continued to assist 18,291 malnourished cases through various TFCs
and SFCs, distributing a total of 66 tons of food.
(c) UNHCR and the Government agreed to extend Nduta camp in Kibondo
in order to accommodate approximately 3,120 refugees who arrived
recently. 959 new refugees from DR Congo, Burundi and Rwanda were
identified during the last week of April and the first week of May.
(d) The repatriation programme is on-going, with 124 returning
Rwandese refugees assisted. Since the beginning of the year, a total
of 1,268 Rwandese were repatriated under the programme.
(e) On 28 April a nutritional screening (based on MUAC) was conducted
in Lugufu camps among 7,638 children. The malnutrition rate in Lugufu
I, was 3.2 percent, which represents a significant decrease compared
to last December (9.2 percent). The malnutrition rate for Lugufu II,
where new arrivals are hosted, is 5.3 percent.
(2) Burundi
(a) Although security situation improved around Bujumbura, attacks
and armed robbery are still reported in many areas, including
Cankuzo, Makamba and Kayanza provinces. Confrontation between the
army and the rebels reduced in intensity in Bujumbura Rural. The
people from Nyabunyegeri are still displaced at Mubone site, Buterere
zone, Bujumbura Mairie. Incursions continued in the eastern provinces
(Ruyigi and Cankuzo) bordering with Tanzania. The security situation
near the Kibira forest in the North West recently deteriorated. An
attack on Matongo in Kayanza province caused the death of several
persons and destruction of the majority of houses. On 11 May, six
Memisa-Coped staff members were kidnapped in Kibago commune, Makamba
province and moved to Tanzania, before being released on 16 May. On
11 May, armed bandits attacked the MSF-F residence in Ngozi province
and looted the radio communication equipment.
(b) During the last week of April and the first week of May, WFP's
food economy teams assessed six communes in Muramvya and Mwaro
provinces where army and rebel forces clashed in early April. In
Nyabihanga and Kayokwe communes, Mwaro province, the belongings of
the civilian population were looted and the social infrastructures
destroyed. Harvests of beans and Irish potatoes are expected for the
end of May and June but meanwhile, some people live on immature sweet
potatoes and cassava/beans leaves.
(c) A total of 3,539 tons of WFP food were distributed to 373,965
people. Following displacements of populations in Butaganzwa commune,
Ruyigi province, in early April, caused by insecurity, WFP supplied 9
tons of food to 436 IDP households in Ruyigi town centre. 12,373
school children were assisted under the school feeding programme in
the most drought-affected communes, in Kirundo province. In Ngozi
province, a return package and food were given to 1,086 Burundian
refugees returning from Tanzania. In Bujumbura Mairie, WFP supplied
2,306 refugees from DR Congo with 39 tons of food, in collaboration
with GTZ. WFP also dispatched 173 tons of food to 45 projects
benefiting 13,801 vulnerable people. 818 tons of food were delivered
to nutritional NGOs running TFCs and SFCs in 15 provinces. The
nutritional situation was reported to be either stabilising or
improving in some provinces, as a result of food assistance, good
harvest and effective response to malaria epidemic.
(d) WFP met with FAO to discuss the principles of Seeds Protection
ration and the collaboration during the joint FAO/WFP Crop and Food
Supply Assessment mission planned to start on 21 May. The scope of
the Seeds Protection programme will be determined by the results of
that mission and the targeting that WFP will carry out in the most
vulnerable provinces.
(E) West Africa Coastal: (1) Guinea, (2) Liberia
(1) Guinea
(a) Although, the general security situation has stabilised in the
country, reports of fighting neighbouring Liberia are source of
concerns.
(b) As of 15 May, WFP has assisted 183,749 IDPs in collaboration with
ICRC and Premiere Urgence under its EMOP operation. During this week,
WFP assisted 3,374 IDPs with 45 tons of food. Premiere Urgence had to
delay the distribution in two camps in the Languette due to security
incidents. WFP has started the targeting of IDPs in Faranah, where
18,000 persons were assisted during the first phase of the operation.
(c) WFP, UNHCR and Donors undertook a joint assessment mission to
evaluate the current humanitarian situation and estimate the needs
for 2002. The mission visited the camp of Kountaya in Kissidougou.
The relocation of refugees from Forecariah restarted on 14 May, with
330 refugees moved to the Sembakounya camp near Dabola. Upon arrival,
refugees are provided with wet feeding for two days and then with a
30- day dry ration. The number of refugees in Sembakounya camp is now
close to 5,000. Some 250 refugees who arrived from various areas to
Mambia transit centre were repatriated to Sierra Leone on 15 May.
(d) During this week, WFP distributed under the PRRO 550 tons of food
to 33,218 refugees in the Kissidougou area. 852 refugees were
transferred from the Languette to Katkama. The total number of people
relocated since the beginning of May amounts to 5,283. Upon arrival,
all refugees receive WFP food assistance. The camp of Massakoundou
will be closed following the last transport on 15 May.
(e) With a current stock of the 12,301 tons of food in country, the
food pipeline is expected to be adequate until November 2001.
(2) Liberia
(a) Heavy fighting between Government and dissident forces in Lofa
County was reported on 10 May. In connection, the Government
restricted movement of diplomats, UN agencies and NGOs staff outside
Monrovia. Through the UNDP Resident Coordinator, WFP has informed the
Ministry of Foreign Affairs that these restrictions are affecting
food delivery to tens of thousands of people and expressed hopes that
the travel ban will be lifted.
(b) Due to the travel restrictions, WFP began to use commercial
trucks to deliver food assistance up-country. However, this is not
seen as a durable solution as the local commercial fleets remain
insufficient and are often unreliable.
(c) More than 6,000 IDPs who have been hosted in the town of Beyan,
in Lofa County, are being verified and relocated to Gbalatuah,
Bellefanai and Wainsue in Bong County. The exercise which started
this week, is jointly undertaken by the LRRRC and humanitarian
agencies. It is expected to last for a week. Exact figures will be
provided upon verification .
(d) WFP, in collaboration with LRRRC and LNRCS, is carrying a
verification exercises in Bong County. 2,524 IDPs were verified at
Gbalatuah and CARI and 3,643 additional IDPs arrived in Bong County.
Once verified and relocated, the IDPs are provided with food by WFP.
Note: All tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No 20)

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 19 - Africa
Headline: Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 19 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: (A) Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) (B)
Southern Africa: (1) Angola, (2) Namibia (C) West Africa: (1) Guinea,
(2) Sierra Leone (D)Chad
(A) Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)
(1) General
(a) On 8 May, WFP issued a press statement, warning that a
humanitarian crisis is surfacing in the northern Katanga province as
many displaced people arrive in towns in search of assistance, after
years of isolation due to civil conflict.
(b) WFP is quickly deploying more emergency staff to various parts of
the country to respond to the crisis. As aid workers are gaining
access to cut-off areas also in North and South Kivu, WFP is prepared
to find population in a similarly grave condition as those seen in
northern Katanga.
(c) WFP is constrained by a lack of logistics capacity such as
aircraft to overcome the widespread insecurity and bad road
conditions. Over the coming days, WFP would like to urgently deliver
some 650 tons of food into areas of northern Katanga, which can only
be reached by air. Barges are currently pre- positioning the needed
food stocks into Kalemie, from where it will be delivered.
(d) WFP plans to urgently launch an airlift of emergency supplies to
a number of isolated locations, where people are in urgent need of
food. WFP will be appealing to donors to fund the airlift, which will
be expanded as needs are identified. While Food needs have more than
doubled this year. WFP is currently facing a major funding problem
with only 30 percent of its operation resourced. USD 43 million is
still needed to feed 1.4 million people until the end of 2001.
(e) For the first time, WFP recently airlifted one ton of food to the
rebel- held town of Manono to be immediately distributed to 200
malnourished children in the feeding centre established by Nuova
Frontiera. However, this will barely last a week as more and more
malnourished people are streaming into the town. Milk provided by
UNICEF was also flown in from Goma. Roughly 23 percent of children
under five, of Manono's 25,000 population, are malnourished, 19
percent severely.
(f) A recent assessment mission to another N. Katanga town, Kiambi,
by WFP and its partner, Nuova Frontiera revealed alarming
malnutrition and mortality rates. WFP will try to reach Kiambi in the
coming days if security conditions permit.
(g) WFP's assistance to Northern Katanga dates back to January 2000,
with support to feeding centres run by Action Contre la Faim (ACF) in
towns like Moba, and food security programs with FHI (Food for the
Hungry). Continuous insecurity has prevented assistance from being
sent further inland. Through Nuova Frontiera, WFP is currently
feeding some 5,000 people in Kalemie, including 800?900 malnourished
children in Therapeutic and Supplementary feeding programs (TFC and
SFC). In Nyunzu, which opened up in March, WFP also supplies food to
700 malnourished children.
(h) The food situation for thousands more people in North and South
Kivu, such as Shabunda, Lulingu and Kasika remains precarious and
urgent assistance is needed. People are reported to be hiding in the
forests, from where some slowly emerge, malnourished and almost
naked.
(i) Following the murder of six ICRC staff in Ituri area on 27 April,
some 20 aid agencies working in eastern DRC have suspended
humanitarian activities from 30 April to 2 May.
(2) Goma and North Kivu
(a) Since the beginning of April, WFP delivered 338 tons of food
through different partners in favour of 35,072 beneficiaries in North
Kivu province. This included 6,557 malnourished children assisted in
different nutritional centres in Kirotshe, Masisi, Kayna, Goma and
Rutshuru areas. In addition, WFP assisted families of the
malnourished (26,935 persons) under a supplementary feeding
programme. Most of these families were also trained on gardening
activities and received vegetable young plants. A total of 480
in-patients admitted in public hospitals received a daily wet food
ration during their medical treatment. Finally, 1100 orphans and
street children have been enrolled in a Food For Training (FFT)
programme in Goma.
(b) WFP and World Vision International (WVI) visited Muheto (Masisi)
where 20,000 returnees were reported by the local authorities. Most
of them appeared to be vulnerable and in need of food and shelter.
WFP and WVI will provide them with food rations and emergency kits
respectively.
(c) More than 8,000 returnees have been registered in Rutshuru area
(North Kivu province) where security has improved. WFP and ECHO have
planned a joint assistance with food and non food-items (NFI) to
facilitate reinstallation of these returnees.
(d) WFP monitored several nutritional feeding programmes in Mweso
area (North Kivu province). Severe cases are transferred to Mweso
hospital where they continue to receive WFP assistance through MSF-H
during their stay in the hospital. MSF-H is planning to operate
therapeutic feeding programme integrated in the paediatric service in
Mweso hospital to avoid continuous movement between Kitshanga and
Mweso. Following a sensitisation campaign carried out by mobile
teams, admissions in the supplementary feeding centre have increased
by 41 percent in comparison to February. MSF-H is conducting
nutritional surveys in Mweso and Bishusha areas. The results will be
available soon and will eventually lead to opening of new nutritional
centres.
(e) Following a sensitisation conducted by mobile teams on the
lakeside in Kirotshe area, the number of new admissions in the WVI
TFC has increased by 54 percent in March. Pilot farms have been
established in various nutritional centres in Kirotshe. FAO
distributed 4.3 tons of vegetable seeds and 1,000 hoes to parents of
malnourished children in 10 supplementary feeding centres.
(3) Katanga
(a) WFP assisted a total of 26,606 persons in Lubumbashi. This
included 800 street children supported in collaboration with Human
Dignity in the World (HDW). This caseload is reported to be on the
rise as a result of a continuous deterioration of the economic
conditions. WFP in collaboration with Action by Churches Together
(ACT) is planning to assist 4,935 IDPs in Likasi as soon as food
commodities are supplied from Lubumbashi.
(b) A total of 232 tons of food was distributed to 30,411 persons,
mainly refugees, in Kisenge area under various programmes (general
and targeted Vulnerable Group Feeding (VGF) distributions, TFC and
SFC). The Maize Meal and pulses components of the VGF ration have
been reduced by half in April, as the beneficiaries were expected to
complement their food basket with products of harvest (sweet potatoes
and pulses).
(4) Kinshasa
(a) WFP in collaboration with Union Nationale des Femmes (UNAF)
launched its first School Feeding project on the 1 May. WFP is
planning to provide 7 tons of food for May and June, benefiting 1,000
school children in Kinshasa region.
(b) WFP continues to support nutritional programmes run by Save the
Children Fund (SCF) and Solidarites in Grand Nord (Katwa, Butembo and
Mutwanga area). The nutritional centre in Lume, situated at 10 km
from Mutwanga, registers a considerable number of IDPs children.
Mutwanga and Lume host IDPs fleeing attacks and looting in the
Rwenzori mountains. These IDPs have been permanently moving back and
forth from Mutwanga to Lume and do not have opportunity to cultivate.
(5) The WFP/UNHCR Joint food assessment mission (JFAM) was undertaken
from 23 April to 11 May. The team, including ECHO and USAID
representatives went to Kimpese and was due to visit Bas-Congo,
Bandundu and Katanga provinces. Delays in the issuing of travel
authorisation as well as delays on main roads significantly
constrained the movements of the team. The beneficiary figures,
currently under review include: - 11,500 IDPs, most recently arrived
to Bandundu (Kahemba), who are likely to continue to receive WFP food
assistance. - 23,500 refugees who arrived a long time ago in Kimpese
(Bas Congo province), who were expected to become self-sufficient
this year, but given their limited access to land WFP expects that a
significant number will need continuous food assistance. - 31,000
persons residing in Kisenge (Katanga province). This beneficiary
group has access to land and has developed a number of coping
mechanisms, so WFP is considering a possible termination of food
assistance.
(6) The new PRRO was due to start at the beginning of this year but
the first shipment of commodities is expected in mid-May. Although
WFP planned to distribute some 73,000 tons of food to 1,400,000
beneficiaries, only 15000 tons have been resourced to date. The
ongoing project for assistance to refugees was due to end on 30 April
but will be extended until 30 June.
(B) Southern Africa
(1) Angola
(a) The security situation remained unstable in most provinces during
this week, resulting in arrival of newly displaced persons in the
Provinces of Luanda, Bie, Moxico, and Lunda Sul. A major attack was
reported on 5 May in Caxito (Bengo Province) about 50 km north-east
of Luanda, when about 60 children were abducted from an orphanage run
by ADPP and supported by WFP. The incident claimed the lives of
several inhabitants, including four ADPP and COSV aid workers and
caused displacement of 10,000 persons into Luanda. Most of them were
afterwards reported to have returned to their communities. The
security situation in the Province remains tense, resulting in the
suspension of humanitarian activities. A camp managed by INTERSOS in
Huila province and supported by WFP was burnt down. INTERSOS has
temporally suspended its operations in the locality. More fighting
was reported in Bie, Uige and Luanda Sul provinces.
(b) The FAO/WFP Crop and Food assessment mission for 2001 has
started. Three teams composed of representatives from FAO, WFP,
USAID, MINADER, EC, CIDA, and SADC will be visiting ten Provinces for
the Assessment. Insecurity in and around Uige town has already
resulted in the postponement of field trips planned by the team.
(c) A WFP/FAO/FIDA mission met with central and local authorities, as
well as partners, to establish effective co-ordination and
collaboration mechanisms for smooth implementation of the second
phase of the Bengo food security project.
(d) WFP formally clarified the objective of its new PRRO to partner
agencies and government Counterparts in Benguela Province. WFP
advocated for better collaboration and co-ordination by all
humanitarian actors in the province to ensure more effective
humanitarian aid deliveries in the Province. The NGO community in
turn stressed the need for the provincial authorities to increase
their participation in social programmes. A FAO/WFP/UNESCO team
embarked on a feasibility assessment mission to Benguela Province,
for a pilot project on school feeding in the Province.
(e) During this week, 2,560 IDPs who recently arrived from Chinguar,
Kambandua, Nharea and Chitembo were registered in Kuito (Bie
Province). WFP provided food assistance to approximately 1,219 of
them, reported to be particularly vulnerable. The number of
malnourished people attending the supplementary and therapeutic
feeding centres in Kuito is reported to be on the rise as a result of
the increased number of newly-arrived IDPs already suffering from
malnutrition.
(f) WFP distributed, in collaboration with various implementing
partners a total of 340 tons of food to 15,575 beneficiaries residing
in Huambo.
(g) A joint WFP/OCHA/UNICEF/NRC/ACCORD/CARE-INT and JOHANNITER
mission assessed the condition of flood victims in Kwanhama and
Ombandja Municipalities of Cunene Province. Although access
constraints prevented the team to undertake a comprehensive
assessment, they observed damaged crops in some villages, destruction
of a number of houses and contaminated water resulting in an increase
of diarrhoea and malaria cases.
(h) A total of 182 newly displaced persons arrived this week in
Menongue (Kuando Kubango province). The IDPs are currently located in
a transit camp in "Bairro Pandera" where they will receive an
emergency food ration from WFP.
(i) WFP distributed a total of 29 tons of food to 1,835 beneficiaries
in Cazengo (Kuanza Norte province). The Provincial sub-group on
verification of IDPs visited Lucala to verify the number of new IDPs.
The team uncovered certain irregularities in the initial registration
and recommended a re-verification exercise and registration of only
IDPs located in camps.
(j) WFP delivered a total of 119 tons of food to its implementing
partner, for distribution to some 4,488 beneficiaries in Kuanza Sul
Province. During a meeting with WFP and SCF-USA, the Municipal
administration of Sumbe and MINARS approved a resettlement Programme
for about 120 IDP families. An area of land has been identified for
construction of houses and agricultural activities of displaced
families.
(k) The number of new enrolments in the nutritional centres for
children under five, supported by WFP in Luanda, is on the increase
as a result of the deteriorating sanitary condition provoked by
flooding in most part of the Province. The rate of diseases such as
acute diarrhoea and malaria among children is also reported to be on
an increase.
(l) WFP distributed approximately 118 tons of food to 7,939
beneficiaries in Lunda Sul Province. Following the inter-agency
verification of 3,095 IDPs arrived after 01 October 2000 in Saurimo
(Lunda Sul Province), new beneficiary cards were issued. The team
that carried this exercise was composed of WFP, MINARS, UTCAH/LWF and
MINADER.
(m) WFP delivered a total of 889 tons of food to its implementing
partners, for immediate distribution in Malange Province. Local
authorities and humanitarian agencies discussed the issue of land
allocation for resettlement of IDPs in the Province. The Governor
presented a plan of action for the first phase of the resettlement
programme, which will involve resettlement of 25,000 IDPs currently
located in the periphery of Malange, to identified areas in Lau,
Quissol and Combondo.
(n) A total of 126 newly arrived IDPs received a WFP emergency food
ration in Luena (Moxico Province). They were mostly coming from the
provinces of Bie, Malange, Lunda Sul and some municipalities in
Moxico.
(o) The results of a nutritional survey conducted in an IDP camp in
Luena (Uige Province) by MSF-B, indicate a sharp increase in severe
malnutrition. This is attributable to increased influx of IDPs into
Luena.
(p) WFP's new PRRO that commenced on 1 April remains seriously under
funded. Only 5.5 percent of the requirements have been met through
new contributions while the carryover stocks from the previous PRRO,
bring this figure up to 33 percent. Urgent additional contributions
are required.
(2) Namibia
(a) Due to funding shortages for the Namibia EMOP, a serious pipeline
break is being experienced for all commodities. Reduced rations are
being distributed to the refugees. Further reduction of the ration
may continue in the coming months, if donor pledges do not increase.
General distribution of CSB has stopped completely, to allow for its
continued use in the Supplementary Feeding Centres. UNHCR has
reported a continued increase in the number of refugees in the Osire
camp, which is currently put at 20,000.
(C) West Africa
(1) Guinea
(a) The general situation has stabilised and there is no reports of
insecurity in the forest region.
(b) As of 8 May, WFP assisted 180,375 IDPs in collaboration with ICRC
and Premiere Urgence. WFP has delivered 80 tons of food to ICRC in
order to assist approximately 7,500 persons in the Kissidougou region
found vulnerable through household surveys. WFP has started a
socio-economic survey in Faranah among 18,000 IDPs. OCPH is currently
undertaking a socio-economic survey in the region of Kankan and is
planning to organise a similar exercise in the region of Kindia next
week.
(c) As of the 8 May, 5,000 refugees have been transferred from the
camp of Kolomba in the "Parrot's beak" area to the transit camp of
Katkama. After an overnight stay, they are expected to be relocated
to the camps of Boreah and Telikoro/Albadaria.
(d) The planning discussions with the Government regarding transfer
of refugees from Forecariah region have been completed and the
operation is expected to start around14 May, following a
sensitisation campaign. Refugees willing to be transferred will
transit through the camp of Mambiya/Kindia. Some 150 Liberian
refugees are currently staying at the Liberian embassy waiting to be
transferred to the camp of Sembakounya/Dabola.
(e) Given the slow advancement of road works aimed at improving the
accessibility to the camps of Kountaya, Sembakounya and Boreah,
discussions are held with the Government to involve the army and
reinforce the GTZ team in order to complete the works before the
rainy season. In N'zerekore, the construction of the camp of Kola by
IRC, which is supposed to host 15,000 refugees from the border areas
of Lola and Yomou, is due to start soon and be completed within one
month. WFP will consequently open a new sub-office and a warehouse.
(f) With a current PRRO stock of 11,583 tons, WFP food pipeline is
expected to be adequate until July.
(2) Sierra Leone
(a) The security situation in the country is generally stable, but
reports of fighting between Revolutionary United Front (RUF) rebels
and Civil Defence Forces (CDF) in the eastern Kono district have been
confirmed by UNAMSIL peacekeepers. UNOCHA visited Koidu in Kono
district and reported presence of 1,500 returnees there.
(b) WFP and UNHCR undertook a joint food assessment in Sierra Leone.
The mission interviewed returning refugees and IDPs in several camps
around Bo and Kenema and visited various projects near Kenema. WFP
held a meeting with the Ministry of Health and Nutrition, UNICEF and
nutritional NGOs to discuss the new rations proposed in the PRRO for
2002. The committee reviewed all the rations on the basis of WHO
standards.
(c) WFP distributed in collaboration with International Medical Corps
(IMC) a total of 15 tons of food to returning refugees and IDPs
recently arrived from Guinea and Liberia in Daru. The influx of
returnees to Daru has decreased and most of the returnees to Kailahun
district are now settled in their host communities. Some 518 Liberian
refugees are reported to be staying in Daru. Movement of refugees
from Conakry to Freetown by boat has slowed down.
(d) During the last two weeks, WFP provided 45 tons of food to 557
returning refugees and IDPs that are being resettled in their areas
of origin, in Bo and Pujehun areas. The beneficiaries received a
two-month ration.
(e) Various WFP partners (Africare, CAD, CARITAS and ICRC) are
currently assessing and registering farmers who will be possibly
supported under Food For Agriculture (FFAg) Programmes in Makeni,
Magburaka and Lunsar (Northern Province). The exercise is expected to
be completed by the next week and will be followed by joint seeds and
food distributions.
(f) WFP distributed in collaboration with various partners a total of
416 tons of food to 10,498 persons involved in FFW projects (bridge
and road rehabilitation, water and sanitation activities and
agricultural projects). WFP monitored FFW projects for road
construction in Freetown, Bo and Kenema areas.
(g) WFP is currently distributing 57 tons of food to 9,949 children
enrolled in 38 schools in Mile 91, Port and Kenema.
(h) Current WFP food stocks will be insufficient to address food
needs in the country and continue the implementation of WFP
programmes. Sugar and CSB are already lacking while shortfalls of
oil, cereals and pulses are expected to occur respectively in July,
September and October. New contributions are urgently required.
(D) Chad
(a) Presidential elections are scheduled to take place on the 20 May
and the security situation is becoming tense. Movement of UN staff
has been restricted.
(b) During the first week of May, WFP distributed a total of 236 tons
of cereals to 28,000 beneficiaries in Biltine and Ouaddai provinces.
(c) The nutritional situation in the Kanem and the Ouaddai provinces
remains precarious. WFP Nutritionist started operating at the end of
April in Mao to support a local NGO. ACF is planning to resume
nutritional programmes in the Kanem area, starting with the
organisation of a nutritional survey in collaboration with WFP and
UNICEF. In the Ouaddai province, WFP in partnership with Africare
plans to implement a nutritional programme targeting 3,000 children.
(d) Activities implemented in other regions are yet to be defined on
the basis of findings of the assessments currently undertaken by
various NGOs, including WVI, MSF.
(e) WFP expects to face a food pipeline break this week. In-country
stock as of 6 May stood at 241 tons of cereals, while weekly
requirements amount to 1,125 tons. WFP is counting on arrivals of
1,556 tons of rice from Port Sudan, expected by 12 May. In addition,
700 tons of Maize meal borrowed from Bangui are due to reach
Mongo/Abeche by 20 May.
Note: All tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No 19)

Original_Search_Title: Varsity in Nutrition Awareness Scheme
Headline: Kenya: Varsity in Nutrition Awareness Scheme
Body_Text:
Egerton University has started community awareness programmes on nutrition.
The Vice Chancellor Prof Ezra Maritim says the move was inspired by the realisation that nutrition had not been accorded enough attention in people's lives.
However, he said there was a growing realisation that the country could not attain full economic growth if its peoples health status was poor. Hence the need to focus on nutrition as a critical contributor to development.
The University's department of Foods, Nutrition and Dietetics last week organised a nutrition awareness dayat the Nakuru Municipal Council Old Town hall. It was aimed at sensitising the public on the subject.
The chairperson of the Department Prof Prisca Tuitoek said similar programmes would be organised in the future.
She said such awareness days would be extended to the rural areas with the support of the University and well-wishers.
The lecturer said the event which was the third since the department was started at the campus four years ago had been made annual.
She said the objective was to raise awareness on the importance of proper nutrition for healthy living.
At the forum, speakers are invited to talk on topical health and nutritional issues affecting Kenyans.
Foods of all kinds are displayed and their values discussed.
Some of the foods at the exhibition include, the chapatis, made from Ndengu and wheat flour, cakes made from a blend of millet or Sorghum and wheat flour which enhances the fibre content of cakes.
Prof Tuitoek pointed out the importance of traditional foods, such millet, sorghum, sweet potatoes, cassava.
"These are drought resistant crops with good nutritional values which need to be grown and consumed to help reduce starvation," she said.
The lecturer said students were trained as professionals capable of solving problems related to food and nutrition in order to improve food security and nutritional status in the family, community and nationally.
Poverty, she added, had contributed to increased malnutrition in children and women especially in marginal areas.
The preference of westernised diets and lifestyles at the expense of traditional ones had led to increased food related ailments, Prof Tuitoek said.
She observed that campaigns on nutrition would strengthen primary health care, which emphasised on preventive rather than curative health care.
The degree programme includes a study of applications of scientific principles in understanding the nutritional value of foods, utilization, functions and inter-relationships of nutrients in health and disease.
It is also includes a study of chemical, physical and sensory properties of food, its quality and safety, and its social and psychological significance.
Professional opportunities for graduates in Food, Nutrition and Dietetics include the Ministry of Health and Agriculture, private and government hospitals and hospices, Ngos and community service oriented organisations.
The Rift Valley Provincial Medical Officer of Health Dr Kipruto Chesang commended the initiative by the University.
He said a majority of Kenyans were malnourished due to lack of information on food value.

Original_Search_Title: A National Disaster in the City of Thieves
Headline: Kenya: A National Disaster in the City of Thieves
Body_Text:
Those who live in what used to be "the city of many lights" but which
has lately turned into the city of many thieves - also called Nairobi - know
of two tribes of Kenyans. One tribe is called chokora, if you want to call
them by a bad name.
This is the tribe that once went to a place where the Son of the Soil -
or yours sincerely - has never been. Some two years ago, members of the
tribe were invited to the biggest house in the land, also called State
House, and had lunch with the main man there. I hear that as they had lunch
with the man who was born and brought up in Sacho. They wondered why he had
not kept cows and goats in State House yet the place is so big and green it
could become a cattle ranch.
I don't know in which language the main man spoke to the fellows who
sniff glue for breakfast, lunch, dinner and supper and escort it with a
slice of bread imported from a dustbin. I guess he took some lessons in
Sheng before he invited them to lunch. Thus he could have told them,
"Ma-beste, hii bash ni yenu. Kuna kuku porno na nyama choms na pia
machipo. Mangeni kama world inadedi." (In other words, "My friends, there is
plenty of grilled chicken, roast meat and chips. Eat as if it is the end of
the world.")
The other tribe consists of members of the female species and these days
they are called commercial sex workers. This tribe charges a fee to smile at
you and say they love you like roast chicken. I have seen them at night on a
road called Koinange Street in the city of many thieves. They wear skirts
the size of shrunk foolscaps and have the habit of shouting, "Darling!" to
any creature in trousers, especially if the creature is driving anything
resembling a car. Their normal greetings to a complete stranger are
delivered at the speed of lightning. It goes something like,
"Hi-I'm-Ivy. I-love-you-darling. And-I-drink-Tusker-baridi".
When I was growing up in the Slopes of Mount Kenya where I was born,
this tribe had very few members from the Slopes and they operated only in
the city. They were known by name and had certain peculiar habits other
skirt-wearers never did. One of them was to eat the African sausage, which
was supposed to be for men only. The African sausage is something made from
the intestines of a cow or a goat and is filled with blood and some meat
then roasted.
When I was growing up in the Slopes of Mount Kenya, it was called Mu-ten
or the ten-cent piece. It was called so because a piece cost ten cents. The
ten-cent piece was the size of the width of the blade of the butcher's
knife. When a real man ate a piece or two of Mu-ten, he escorted it with a
mug of soup in a cup called Mu-South, a sizeable tin cup. It was called so
because it was imported from South Africa. It was so sizeable that when you
drank from it, it covered most of the face.
One of the members of this second tribe who operated a Mu-ten joint then
was called Wanjiru Ki-Nylon. I have whispered something about her before and
if you have forgotten, she was called Ki-Nylon because when finally retired
from her profession, she was the owner of only one dress made of nylon
material. Nylon was also called wash-and-wear because it needed only a few
minutes to dry.
Wanjiru used to wash her only nylon dress at night and wear it in the
morning. Her trademark thus was that dress that had red dots. When she
retired and came back to the village, she was "kept", as they said, by a man
from Kavirondo. In the language of the Slopes, to be "kept" meant a woman
living with a man to whom she was not married, or rather, whose clan had not
delivered goats and beehives full of bees to the elders of her clan.
A person from Kavirondo was anyone who came from anywhere near Lake
Victoria. This particular Kavirondo was a man who had been trained at the
Kiganjo Police College in the science of arresting criminals. He had been
posted to the Slopes. It was in those days when the men who were trained in
Kiganjo could shoot so straight that when they pursued a most wanted
criminal, they just crippled their kneecaps. In those days, the most wanted
criminals were not armed like a whole army. All they owned was a homemade
club when they went about the business of reaping where they had not
sowed.
In those days, the word chokora was not known. Children were supposed to
know their fathers and their fathers knew them. A man was not allowed to sow
wild seeds all over and flee. Even Wanjiru Ki-Nylon could not allow herself
to get a child with a father who would be absent as soon as the mother's
belly started getting big.
Lately, the members of the two tribes have become so many that I won't
be surprised at the chokora supporting their own candidate as a councillor
come the next election. They could go ahead and ensure he actually becomes
the mayor of the city of many thieves. I also won't be surprised if the
women who wear shrunk foolscaps decide to support their candidate for the
mayorship.
That will indeed happen one day and so we shall have a mayor who retires
to his chamber to sniff glue after deliberations on what to do with garbage
in the city. That should not shock anyone in this country where maize
roasters are translated into councillors who then become "his worship the
mayor". It should not shock anyone in this country where former and very
junior men in blue have ended up in the honourable house and then become
whole ministers with flags on their cars. It will not shock me to have a
glue-sniffing character for a mayor since I have a feeling that some Members
of Parliament sniff and smoke illegal things of senior proportions.
That is the only way I can explain why they take warrior tactics to
Parliament and are given to saying some things that are not very honourable
in that honourable house.
I did not mean to talk about the chokora and the commercial sex worker
becoming mayors. I mean to ask what happens when the small chokora grows
beards. I also mean to ask what happens when the commercial sex worker gets
old and her voice is hoarse from telling total strangers, "Hi-I am-Ivy-I
love you darling-and-I-drink-Tusker-baridi" much too often.
I guess you will tell me that the chokora don't die when they reach the
age of getting beards. A fellow who has fed on half-baked sweet potato
peels, meat of unknown animals that even the vultures have refused to eat
and chips mixed with water from the mopping pail all his life - and survived
- cannot die just because he has become of age when a man gets a beard.
When a chokora reaches that age, he does not become a priest or go into
farming since he was born and brought up on the cold cement floors in the
city and has no idea whether maize is born or harvested. Those who know
will, of course, say that when the chokora grows a beard, he now has the
licence to do all what he has wished to do all his life.
If the man who lives in State House and who had invited the chokora for
lunch one day is reading, let me tell him to take a walk in the night and
without escort in the city and he will learn what senior chokora have wished
to do all their lives.
Let him take the walk at nine. Let him look as if he has just come from
selling a big herd of goats at Mogotio market and got senior
money. Suddenly, someone jumps onto his back like a lion onto a buffalo. A
pair of hands go for his throat and immediately cut off air supply to his
voice box.
In the meantime, other hands are working on his shoes and others through
his pockets at lightning speed. In a minute, the man who was born and
brought up in Sacho is on the floor, almost knocking on the door of
heaven. Or hell. He revives after three minutes with his throat feeling as
if it has swallowed a stone. For the next one week, he is not able to
talk. He also realises that quite a number of people walk without trousers
at night and it is not out of their will. He concludes this because, on
reviving, he discovers he does not have trousers - the senior chokora has
taken them.
The man who occupies the main seat in the country also finds out why
many people in Nairobi speak in whispers as if they are suffering from a
cold. He will discover that they speak so after an encounter with a senior
chokora trying to practice what he has wished to do all his life. The boss
of this country might then declare the chokora a national disaster and start
a fund to buy a farm for them somewhere where they can be taught that maize
is harvested and not born.
I would also be glad if the man who was born and brought up in Sacho
could find out why people drive with the windows of their cars closed even
when the heat inside makes it feel like an oven. There is only one way he
could find out: by driving an ordinary car alone in the city.
Let him put on a smile and he will discover how those kinds of smiles
are wiped from people's faces in an instant. He comes face to face with
something in the hand of a chokora who is aspiring for greater things in
life, like improving his tactics in the art of ngeta. The thing in the hand
of the chokora is what has gone through the digestive system of another
person. It has many names but for now, let us call it "the product of the
digestive system".
The chokora is holding it like a rugby ball and his hand is held high in
a manner likely to suggest that he does not need any persuasion to land it
on the smiling face of the driver. The other hand is outstretched and the
driver is supposed to get the obvious idea that the young fellow is saying,
"Your money or I colour you face with the products of the digestive system."
The young fellow might make another hint and say, "Mdosi chota ama
nifyatue."
It is obviously not good manners to drive in town with the products of
the digestive system of a person you don't even know all over your face, so
when that happens to the man born and brought up in Sacho, he wakes up one
day and decides that those boys (or men) need far more than one lunch at
State House once every five years.
For now, I think he imagines they retire to become bishops and the
commercial sex workers nuns. If the man who was born and brought up in Sacho
thinks otherwise, let him tell me through wis@mitsuminet.com .

Original_Search_Title: Mozambican Children Supplied With Vitamin A
Headline: Mozambique: Mozambican Children Supplied With Vitamin A
Body_Text:
Maputo, Mozambique — The campaign to administer
Vitamin A supplements to all Mozambican children under the age of
five years began on Monday across the country.
The central ceremonies of the campaign were held in Chibuto
district of the southern province of Gaza.
During the ceremonies chaired by Deputy Health Minister Aida
Libombo, sweet potatoes with orange pulp were distributed to the
local people.
A Health Ministry press release, received by the the
national news agency on Monday said that this measure aims at
promoting the consumption of food rich in vitamin A.
This programme was launched by the Health Ministry, in
cooperation with the National Agricultural Research Institute
and the Agriculture Ministry.
Vitamin A is to be administered to these children every six
months, during their normal check ups.
"To reduce child morbidity and mortality, one of the
strategies is the fight against deficiency in micro-nutrients and
other nutritional diseases", Aida Libombo told a press
conference.
Libombo said that the campaign of improved health care to
women and children will also include the administration of
iodine, in the form of capsules, to children under 14.
Iodine "will only be administered in the (western) Tete
province, where nutritional profile studies have shown that there
is a significant prevalence of iodine deficiency", said Libombo.
Iodine is essential for the good functioning of the brain
and the central nervous system. A shortage of iodine my also
cause the disfiguring disease known as goitre.
This is the third programme of the kind in the country, and
the last campaign, held in 2000, managed to cover 79 per cent of
the targeted children.
This year's campaign is expected to cover at least
90 percent of the target population, and is estimated to cost 500
million meticais (about 28,000 US dollars), mostly granted by
UNICEF.

Original_Search_Title: MK Veteran: The ANC Should Clear My Name
Headline: South Africa: MK Veteran: The ANC Should Clear My Name
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Johannes van Wyk clutches his crutch firmly for support each time he
rises from a chair as he goes about his household chores. Certified
disabled, he has lost the use of his left leg.
His cheekbone is also shattered. His scars bear testimony to his
three-year incarceration in African National Congress detention camps
in Zambia, Angola and Tanzania.
Although he received R4 530 as interim reparation from the Truth and
Reconciliation Commission in 1999 for that ordeal, Van Wyk is bitter
that he was never called to put his side of the story before the
commission.
At the Quatro detention camp he claims he was shot in the hip and
stomach in a bid by his torturers to extract a confession that he was
responsible for the assassination of anti-apartheid activist Dr
Fabian Ribeiro and his wife, Florence, in 1986.
Van Wyk cuts a battered figure, looking older than his 44 years. He
is unemployed; he lives on a R700 monthly pension as a former
Umkhonto weSizwe (MK) soldier, sharing a two-roomed mud house with
his Tanzanian wife and three children in a settlement outside
Kwaggafontein in Mpumalanaga.
Otherwise, his wife tends a vegetable garden, growing sweet potatoes
and pumpkins to provide food for the family.
An ANC and South African Communist Party member, Van Wyk says, "Now
that someone has confessed to the killing, the ANC should clear my
name."
Van Wyk is among the 9 771 members of MK and the Azanian People's
Liberation Army who did not join the South African National Defence
Force (SANDF).
These members will not benefit from the government's recently
announced R500- million pension plan for former MK veterans. The
pension plan " the details of which are yet to be released " will
benefit only those former guerrillas who entered into an employment
contract with the Department of Defence.
An angry Thandi Modise, chair of the parliamentary defence portfolio
committee, says she has asked the question: "What happens to those
who were injured, disabled and did not join the SANDF " She says Van
Wyk is fortunate to be in a position to receive at least R700 a
month. "There are others who receive nothing."
The defence department said the authority to redress the grievances
of those veterans who did not join the SANDF falls outside its
jurisdiction. The Mail & Guardian was advised to approach the MK
Veterans' Association (MKVA). The MKVA national chair Deacon Mathe
said the organisation runs projects which address the grievances of
all the MK veterans. He claimed they will be initiating projects
which will reach out to those who have not been covered, but was not
forthcoming with details.
A former Mamelodi resident, Van Wyk volunteered to work for MK in
Zambia in 1988 under the code name Bongi Zembe.
He says within three months of his arrival in Zambia he was accused
of being an apartheid government agent and sent first to the SRC
prison, and later Quatro in Angola, where he claims he was tortured.
"My Afrikaner surname caused suspicion," he adds, after he was
adopted by one of his aunts, who was married to a coloured man.
He claims he was released after a short stint at yet another prison
in Tanzania and made his way back to South Africa with his Tanzanian
wife Elizabeth in 1992.
"I don't want anything for myself " I just want to give some
opportunities to my children" " aged 18, seven and three years. He
indicates torn clothing piled on top of a heap of broken furniture,
and school shoes without laces placed in the corner. "That is their
wardrobe," he says.
Van Wyk says he has written several letters to President Thabo
Mbeki's office. In one of these letters he poignantly notes: "... I
still love the river [the ANC] that nearly took my life ..." All he
wants is someone to clear his name.

Original_Search_Title: Why the Green Belt Campaign Needs Me
Headline: Kenya: Why the Green Belt Campaign Needs Me
Body_Text:
I am going to join the Green Belt Movement. This has nothing to do
with that woman who has warrior blood in her system called Wangari, she
who thinks green, talks green, sweats green, walks and eats green.
I am talking about another green belt movement and it has nothing to
do with the twigs called miraa that grow in the slopes called Kangeta
and Maua in Meru. That is where a man is likely to slice your neck with
a panga for looking at his miraa in a manner likely to suggest that you
might take a few twigs.
I am going to join the kind of green movement that promotes the smoking
of certain leaves that the police and magistrates consider illegal. Some
people elsewhere, however, consider that smoke their staple food. The rolled
green leaves are sometimes called boza alias kaya, alias
Mary Jane, bhang, marijuana or cannabis sativa. I hear there is also another
variety called mushrooms in the land of George W. Bush.
Don't mark me wrong. I will not start smoking that stuff, which I hear,
gives a feeling called aire-aire. I will start growing it on that
piece of soil that I own in Ng'arua. In other words, instead of trying
to persuade that piece of semi-desert to produce maize and other miserable
grains, I will convince it to produce those green leaves that are smoked
for a high kick.
If any cop is reading this, he should not rush to arrest me. He should
keep away because cops are the ones who have given me the idea that growing
of the crop might be a good thing. They gave me that idea the other week
when they kept saying that one Kenyan may have made billions of shillings
by selling similar leaves in the US.
If you have forgotten, when this man brought in his billions, meaning
enough money to pay salaries for all the policemen, soldiers and watchmen
for more than a month in Kenya, his bank complained of being overloaded.
The bank complained that although it is used to hard work, the money was
too much for its store. So they called for some help from the police and
the men in blue started smelling bhang in the money.
When I read about that, I told myself, "Son of the Soil, another three
billion shillings is looking at you and wants to enter your pocket, what
are you waiting for?" After much meditation and thought, I decided to head
for Ng'arua and become a farmer of those green leaves.
When I was doing that serious meditation, I remembered that one day,
