many decades ago, my lungs had an encounter with that thing that some people
call "holy smoke". It was all on account of the stories that I had heard
about boza.
The stories said if you smoked the stuff, your body behaved as if it
had been fed on steroids and high voltage energisers.
It was said that if you smoked your share of boza, you could dig a whole
acre in two or three hours using a simple jembe. It was also said that
after digging, the same smoke made you eat a whole drum of food. In those
days, a character who dug an acre on his own was considered to be a tough
mohine and the village hero. Eating as much food as possible was considered
a virtue since there was a big enough supply of arrowroots, sweet potatoes
and other tubers.
I decided that the time had come for me to impress my mother Appep by
digging her two acres on my own and by reducing her population of tubers
by smoking the holy smoke. In those days, there was only one character
who used to smoke that stuff in the village. He was also the local cattle
rustler and the villagers called him Hitler, the son of Maceni.
I visited the son of Maceni with one shilling in my palm and soon he
was handing me a roll of boza after giving me instructions as if he was
a doctor handing out a prescription to a patient. He told me to eat enough
before smoking the joint. He advised against such light food as mashed
potatoes and recommended a full supply of cold arrowroots followed by a
huge calabash of fermented porridge.
Son of Maceni also advised me not to smoke the stuff in the open sun.
He advised that I do it under a shade and, of course, away from the eyes
and mouths that could whisper a thing or two to the chief or the people
called tribal police or today's Administration Police. Finally, he told
me that in case the smoke hit me too hard, I should drink some milk. In
other words, maumivu yakizidi yaone maziwa.
I followed the prescription up to the point of lighting up the
roll of boza under a shade and away from dangerous eyes. A few puffs later,
strange things started happening. For the first time, I saw my nose without
looking at myself in the mirror. I could not understand what was happening.
I told myself that either the smoke that had gone down my lungs had made
my nose bigger or my eyes had started seeing better. Before I could get
an answer to that puzzle, something told me that for the first time, my
left eye was looking at the right one. This is to say that my two eyes
were looking at each other. In fact, I thought I saw my left eye wink at
my right one.
Suddenly, I started seeing and feeling ants crawling all over
my body. I plucked off my shirt but of course there were no ants on me.
I don't know what I found funny in that but I started laughing as if I
had inhaled laughing gas. I guess the smoke I had inhaled was a relative
of laughing gas and that is why when a fly landed on my tummy, I thought
it was very funny and continued laughing as if I was being paid to do so.
After laughing myself hoarse, I decided to take a walk. Instead,
however, I felt myself walking on air. The boza in my head was actually
telling that my feet were on air and that I was the first man to do such
a thing. I guess that if there was water nearby, I could have attempted
to walk on it just to prove to the man who was born and brought up in Nazareth,
Jesus Himself, that he had done nothing major when he took a walk on Lake
Galilee.
It was while I was telling myself that I was walking on air that
my now very red eyes met four other eyes. The eyes belonged to two members
of the Tribal Police. In those days, they wore helmets all the time although
there were no riots in the slopes of Mount Kenya where I was born and brought
up. The helmets were tied with a leather string under the chin. The same
TPs as they were called, wore short pants and no shoes. Instead they wore
things called puttees around their legs. A puttee was a woolen cloth that
was wound around the leg.
I guess the TPs did not need more than that because their main duty
was to collect a tax called GPT (Graduated Personal Tax). I will tell you
about it one day but for now, just know that you had to carry a receipt
saying that you had paid Sh10 to the Government as your annual tax. If
you met a TP and you did not have that receipt, you were in real trouble.
The trouble included being locked up in the chief's cell until your relatives
sold some chickens and paid your taxes.
Immediately my eyes met those of the TPs, my dry mouth went to
work even before I had been asked anything. I told them, "I have not smoked
anything!" The perplexed four eyes looked at me and one of the TPs asked,
"Eti you have not smoked what?" I replied, "I have not smoked bhang at
all, at all!"
Instead of the TPs arresting me immediately, they laughed.
Then one of them said, "I think this Son of Appep is going insane. Just
look at him. He does not have a thing and then he starts telling us that
he has not smoked."
The other one looked at me and said, "Young man, go home to your mother.
At this late hour, someone might get excited and dispatch you to a mental
hospital." The two then walked on to do more serious business like arresting
those who had failed to pay taxes to her Majesty the Queen of England.
I headed for home but my intentions were not to have a chat with
my mother Appep on how I was feeling. I headed home to look for milk to
cure myself of the effects of the boza since I felt it had hit me too hard
and that is why I had dared to tell the TPs strange things.
Appep was not home when I arrived. However, her cow Ngunu was
in the compound. If you have forgotten about the cow, it is the animal
that used to produce one cup of milk. It produced that kind of milk after
Appep had said many prayers to the patron saint of cow owners asking him
to inspire Ngunu enough to produce that cup of milk.
When I looked at that cow when I arrived home, I saw milk. Then
something told me that I should not bother to milk Ngunu. I decided to
take the milk from the source. That is to say that I decided to kneel under
Ngunu and suck milk from her like a calf. What I had smoked told me that
I had come up with a senior thought and indeed, if I had walked on air,
I could also turn myself into a calf.
I went down on my knees and my mouth went to work. I had not sucked
more that three drops of milk when Ngunu turned her head to see what kind
of calf was sucking at her teats. I guess the cow was wiser than me and
could tell a real calf from a pretender. I was not looking but I guess
the cow gave me a nasty look and then decided that I was good material
for being turned into past tense.
Ngunu made that decision and that is why it raised its rear left
leg as I continued to suck milk from the source. The same leg landed on
my jaw with enough force to knock a calf down. I was that calf and the
kick threw me a good distance away. I saw stars fly across my eyes and
then what was in my head disappeared. I started seeing things clearly.
One of the clear things was that I had a broken jaw caused by a kick from
a cow. The other was that I could not recall why the cow had decided to
cause that damage to me. It took me a whole week to remember. In that one
week I could not eat or drink. My jaws were locked together by a bandage
that was tied over the head and passed under the chin by Sister Rosa at
the local dispensary.
I have never smoked those leaves since then and I don't intend to but
I will join the green movement as a farmer of that product. Anyone out
there willing to give advice on what I should do with the billions that
I will make? Advise me through  wis@mitsuminet.com

Original_Search_Title: 10 Years of RUF War: Progress Or Self-destruction?
Headline: Sierra Leone: 10 Years of RUF War: Progress Or Self-destruction?
Body_Text:
Ottawa, Canada — A country, like an individual, has a soul, has metaphysics, and has
something invisible that drives it-to what? March 23 marked 10 years of RUF
war in Sierra Leone. For progress or self-destruction, one may ask.
But sometimes, this does not happen in a straight line: the struggle for
progress, or self-destruction, comes in a painfully protracted fashion. The
reasons for such distractions toward progress may be because of either lack
of clear-cut vision or weak inner values and attitudes or history and
culture not informing goals. With such lack of balance the country swings
from one end of the developmental pendulum to another in a confused state of
animation.
In a much broader and deeper reflection, the 71, 620 square kilometers
area, shaped like human heart, that came to be called Sierra Leone by the
Portuguese explorer, Pedro da Cinta in 1462, or the mountains of what is now
Freetown peninsula, Lion Mountains. In some metaphysical twist, for the past
30 years the lion has been asleep, unconcerned by the stupidity, moral
decay, and senselessness wheeling around it. Then 10 years ago, the lion was
awakened, haven't taken the bullshit for too long. The result: a massive
scattershot and scatter brain of the entire Sierra Leonean system, reeling
from one senselessness to another, its spiritual bearing spinning in mid
air. The Revolutionary United Front, RUF, (pictured here) rolling out a
campaign, precisely from the eastern border area of Bomaru on March 23, 1991
to 'clean' the contaminated country, and in the interim, other 'cleaners'
such as the romantic West Side Boys, an offshoot of Johnny Paul Koroma's
junta Armed Forces Provisional Council, another presumed 'cleaner,' roaming
the country's outback.
But the RUF could not handle the forces of disorder it purported to
contain skillfully, and the result has been floodgate of actual evil,
confusion, arrogance, and general senselessness. And so instead of the civil
war and its on-again, off-again apparent slumber garnering Sierra Leoneans
for national unity and the ensuing reconstruction, the country is as much
more paralysed today than ever; its institutions weak, its capacity building
farfetched, its 13 ethnic groups much more disunited, and its psyche
terribly disordered. In a replay of the slave trade era, unknown to Sierra
Leoneans, even including its arrogantly self-centred elite, who really do
not understand the country, massive raids are the norms, arson is the daily
plasas (a vegetable stew made from either cassava or potato leaves),
lootings of diamonds are na God mark am, and mass movement of the civil
war's refugees, some 250,000, from one corridor to another a reminder of the
slave trade era, when during the later part of the 18th century the Freetown
port was one of the major slave trading operations along the West African
coast.
Ten and running, the country is not only intellectually divided, but
physically too. The RUF, enjoying the loot of diamonds, which the
heartlessly deceitful politicians have enjoyed 30 years ago, when Sierra
Leone became independent on 27 April 1961, are now much more entrapped in
the diamonds than any Chinese-type Cultural Revolution to rid the country of
stupidity, superstition, normlessness, moral decay, chronic confusion, and
disorder. The sweetness of the pap (a Sierra Leonean pudding) is in the
eating. Haven't eaten the 'diamond pap,' the RUF government, as they say in
the half of the area of the country under their control, is currently
playing chess game with peace, juggling diamonds in the air and using its
'magic' to charm the country. In the RUF's 10 years of senseless mayhem
everybody is diamond digger, wheeler, and dealer.
Two hundred and fourteen years ago, that's in 1787, Freetown and its
surrounding areas became a British colony. Today, thanks to the RUF, and
more appropriately, the Sierra Leonean rotten elite, the British are back in
some sort of re-colonization zeal: coming to teach these 'savages'
civilization. This is today the existential game in Sierra Leone, where its
police chief is British, where its army chief swings between a Nigerian to
Kenyan to British to Indian. Where the British, are managing its seaport
where its soldiers are being trained by the British, where its key advisers
are British, where its election matters are being sort out by the British,
where the British are the buffer against any eventual RUF second invasion of
the main prize, Freetown.
Foday Sankoh, the founder and former leader of the RUF, who is currently
in an undisclosed detention, reflects the awakened angry lion. And he is
variously seen as so. There may be 'General' Issa Sesey as the current
leader of the RUF but Sankoh remains the main spiritual vizier, oga, of the
RUF. The brutally well organized RUF reflects the dictum that deep down the
wailing and the mayhem of the country, Sierra Leone can emerge as a well
organized and productive society in the modern sense: its institutions
functioning normally, its capacity building assured, and its spiritual
affairs informed by its core cultural, ethical-moral regulations and
laws. If the RUF could organize such healthy campaigns, then the entire
Sierra Leone could, given the right elements and climate. But the RUF, come
to think of its evil deeds, have thrown overboard the moral mission it first
rolled out in the bush.
Either the RUF or the now defunct West Side Boys or the now dead Johnny
Paul Koroma's AFRC activities over the past 10 years is nothing new in the
Sierra Leonean scheme of things. One hundred and ninety-two years ago,
that's in is1808, when Sierra Leone was recognized as a British colony, when
ex-slaves from diverse ethnic groups and national backgrounds, developed an
English-speaking Creole culture unique in Africa, the natives of Sierra
Leone staged numerous rebellions against the British and the Creole elites,
resentment that focused on the tax system and on the privileges of the
Creole. Today, as refracted in the RUF and other marginal rebel groups, the
rebellion is largely between the 'country' and Freetown, between the rural
and the urban, between the fat cats in Freetown and the poor, slum rats in
not only the rural folks but also those in the urban areas.
The boiling of Sierra Leone is like the preparation of famed Sierra
Leonean pepper soup. The RUF, the SLPP, the APC, the NDP, Siaka Stevens, the
Margais, Valentine Strasser, the Maada Bio, the Johnny Paul Koroma, the dead
West Side Boys, the collapsed AFRC, the corruption, the chronic lies, the
disturbing deceit, the hatred, the psychic disorder, and the moral decay are
all ingredients boiling in the Sierra Leonean pot in the preparation of the
pepper soup. Later, much more later, the soup would be ready in the Sierra
Leonean development game.
In 1984 and 1987 Sierra Leonean students and public employees rioted
against the much hated Siaka Stevens' All People Congress, APC, government
1978 imposed one-party system. Out of this came the restoration of
multiparty system of the both Milton Magai and Albert Margai which had
existed before. Will the ten years of RUF campaigns metamorphosis, in the
years to come, into sound multiparty democracy, accountability, and rule of
law, freedom, equity, dignity, and fairness? I hope so in the pepper soup
preparation illustration, but it will not come easily.

Original_Search_Title: The Raging, Rising Toll On Our Highways
Headline: Nigeria: The Raging, Rising Toll On Our Highways
Body_Text:
Lagos — The 15 postgraduate students' death on Jebba - Mokwa highway on
December 7, 2000 epitomised that structure. They thus became 15 of
the obituary statistics out of a projected 8,000 in the red year
2000. And the tragedy continues unabated. Statistics is statistics.
Death is death. (See Nigeria's Table of Tragedy Scoreboard 1955-1998.
Regardless of our philosophically oriented feeling on the obituary
reports, and for some they run very deep, the overriding issue and
the most insidious evil in the overall evil of this said highway
crash is the spreading expectation that highway genocide will become
permanent and will continue to plague the nation without regard to
age, class, gender, creed, residence race... a philosophy of highway
accidents in Nigeria that's dangerously self-fulfilling.
With that epilogue, we come to the heart of what concerns us today
and I know of no better way to spell it out than to recall that
everyone now is eager to participate in the safety dialogue
especially after the recent sensational episodes at Ife-Road Toll
gate, consuming over 100 lives; the Ovia River tragedy on Benin-Ore
Road liquidating about 75 lives; the Carter Bridge Molue plunge
plucking 14 lives and of course the Jebba- Mokwa slaughter. But
unfortunately, there is a curious reluctance to face up and spearhead
the national agitation to reduce highway accidents. it is
paradoxical.
The cold blooded fact is that wailing, sighing and condolence will
not bring back dead people, neither would publishing the totals of
motoring deaths and injuries, nor public or gubernatorial
denunciations, jar the Nigerian motorists into a realisation of the
appalling risks of motoring on our highways. This is because we do
not translate the dry statistics into reality of blood agony. This is
the real antithesis of our highway annual traffic data.
The accident figures in university Ibadan tragedy for instance
exclude the pain, the horror of savage mutilation, the portrait of a
hysterical woman with her screaming mouth opening without a word; the
raw ends of bones protruding through flesh in compound fractures; the
frail and unanswered prayers of the dying students; the lady's corpse
still clutching her handbag on her laps as she has clutched it when
she felt the vehicle leaves the road; the trousers without legs, the
eyes on the back seat that popped out violently without stain, the
body that just lay lifeless without a scratch; the butchered pieces
of human flesh flying about like UFO; the brains scrapping out from
the skulls looking like mashed potatoes; the different shoes with no
feet in them the floor of the vehicle smeared with the crimson human
blood, faeces and urine forming a dry lake but slippery red; the
cracking of bones and joints that came apart like thick soft balloons
stretched too far, the two trousers and a blouse seemed entailed
under the wrought iron of the body of the vehicle, the blood leaping
out of mouths, ears, and noses, the head smashing into the
windscreen; the driver pinned against the steering shaft; and the
once shinning and imposing king of the road (the vehicle) becoming a
shabby paintless scrap. All in 10 seconds.
All these illustrated gruesome variable need to be brought closer
home to the Nigerian people who, over the years, have been accepting
fatalistically the inevitable prices to pay for the freedom of
romance with the automobile, so that we can truly appreciate the
latitudes, dimensions and magnitude of the highway trauma as a
national "life and death" problem.
That takes us back to the Ibadan tragedy scene where this pertinent
hypothesis could be tested.
They heaved. They sighed. They wailed. But the 15 postgrads would not
burge. "A stiff is a stiff, only the living heard you" said a Police
Sergeant at the morgue trying to get a breath of breathable air. "But
this is too much. Too much for anyone. Fifteen grads hacked down by a
technological monster under 5 minutes, wailed Sisi Debola.
I did not heave, I did not sigh, I did not wail. Not as a result of a
masticatory act to promote an image of professional insensitivity
toward the diseased (who were academicians like me) but for the
incomprehension of the homicide. I was just thinking, looking,
gaping. "Stop thinking Doc" says Sis Debola, "How do you stop
thinking," I murmured to myself but surmoned the face gesture to
correct her accident reconstruction timing of 5 minutes (as 10
seconds) before I opened up.
Sisi Debola, looked at that mashed skull of a student with his brains
nearly scrapped out so roughly one couldn't tell the frontal
cere-corticopontal tract from the sensory tract. And down below were
raw ends of bones protruding through flesh in compound fractures.
Look at the other guy with a complete separation of the brain matter
from the immediate organs below in such a way that the eye, the
internal ear, organs of balance, hearing and identification known by
the medicos as passingpoint would have resulted if he had been alive.
Take a close look at that lady. her body territorial integrity had
been abused. Look at the blind grad who had probably dived through
windows without superficial scratches and his guide-stick unbroken -
that's one of those examples where accident was only exercising its
prerogative of being erratic.
Sisi Debola cut in and said: "Doc, this was too much." What was much?
I snapped back, Remember each shattered man or woman who went to make
up the fatality statistics to be chalked up next year as 2000
statistics had to die personal death like them.
But then, death was death and raw death was raw death. Death that
does not allow one to say good-bye to the family or good night to his
roommate. Death that lets loose blood gurgle and choke the throat.
Death that screws out brains from skulls. That's death from
automobile.
But to me this death stepped beyond. "Beyond what Doc?" Beyond
reason, beyond rhythm, beyond comprehension. Reason has patterns.
Comprehension has sequences. All things have rhythm, But this Ibadan
case has none of the triple attributes.
And when something becomes very different, it becomes something new.
This post grads' death blew out of obituary proportions.
The 15 grads were just there motionless, lifeless with the taught of
their degree certificates which will lie deadly in their files
somewhere in the Academic office unseen, unclaimed, unused. And the
police would record it in the Accident Statistics Form: Number of
persons killed 16 (15+ 1 driver) Statistics is statistics. Death is
death.
As if I had been in a trance, the totality of the mindless highway
slaughters of the innocent on our highways struck me as never before
and I started thinking again. "What good is it to spend thousands of
naira to educate a youngster from Pre-UPE through the university and
beyond if only his or her life is to be snuffed out on our highways
unceremoniously before she or he has a chance to use his/her
knowledge and life."
I Postponed thinking and I started getting worried about the two
prominent cabinet Ministers (the Honourable Minister of Police
Affairs and Honourable Minister of Finance) within one year in quick
succession. Suddenly my mind went to the sad memory lane: the
accidents of Professor Achebe, the intellectual creator of Things
Fall Apart, Arrow of God, Man of the People, Edith Ihekweazu, the
first appointed female professor by the University of Nigeria Nsukka;
professor Chris Oparaocha, the vetenary Surgeon; Dr. Oluwole of
Medical Services of Unilag; Professor Fagbemi, the Unilag's first
professor of Informatics; Mary Ebigbo, the postgrad student. Others
include Segun Awolowo, Alhaji Adelabu Coloner Inenger; Mrs. Rasaki,
Mr. Fasakin And Emma Agese (the journalists); Chief Adedipe a
legislator of Ondo House of Assembly, Ambassador Isa Modibo from
Yola, Mr. Obateye, former Secretary of Old Western Region; Alhaji Ali
Akilu of the Northern Region Government, Commodore Wole Awe of former
DFRRI, Alhaji Sanni Bayero, (Galadima of Kano) Ambassador Sada
(former National Productivity; Michael Oghenede (Guardian Reporter);
Four President Shagari's children in one day in 1987; Alhaji
Mohammed, former Secretary to Government of Babangida, the Abacha
Escorts, Kunle Johnson (the son of late JMJ) first minister of
Sports, Mr. Godwin Osofie, the newspaper vendor in Benin. Some were
lucky because traffic death gave fine practical jokes on them. e.g.
Dr. Pat Utomi, Professor Achebe, the two cabinet Ministers, Dupe
Sadima in Texas, Adekunle Babalola and host of others. Motorcycle too
took its toll on A.G. Adejumo, a Nigeria Airways pilot on Badagry
road.
"Hold it Doc. Road Safety, I have heard enough of the doom roll call.
"Sisi Debola broke the trend. I quickly responded that she should
take cold comfort in the fact that the traffic accident's gloomy
picture is not a concern only in Nigeria. For instance, Charles Pete
Conrad, a veteran of four space flights died from a motorcycle
accident on July 8, 1999. (another traffic accident erratic
privilege? Others outside our shores killed in their prime include
Queen Adtrid of Belgium while on holiday in Switzerland; Pierre
Curie, a French Nobel Prize winner in Physics killed in Paris; James
Dean, world famous American film star, Isadora Duncan, the legendary
American dancer; Albert Camus, another French author and Nobel Prize
winner in Literature; Dr. Mohammed Tuleb Hachicha of Tunisia; Charles
Drew who invented the blood-bank system; Bessie Smith, empress of the
Blues, and ofcourse Princess Diana of Britain who died in Paris and
the most recent Mr. Tunji Ogunkanmi of Cornerstone Insurance who had
been erroneously labelled an armed robbery victim. The Ogunkanmi's
case eloquently illustrates the point that our knowledge of the
causes of traffic accidents in this country is fragmentary and
spurious.
And the raging epidemic continues unabated.
That's when the hospital door creaked open and the police sergeant
stepped gingerly outside looking to avoid the released body smells
with an overtone of sweet-tom-tom. It was the "eye witness story that
did it- the dead silence. It was one of the biggest stories of
highway suicides in Nigeria today. It was blacker than the
over-orchestrated armed robbery cases in Lagos in the past one month.
It was a scoop.
According to the story, they were singing and clapping. They were
happy. One was just dozing off. And suddenly there were screams and
awful tearing sounds "wham whoom," cracking bones and joints. The
butchered pieces of human flesh started flying about like UFO and in
a jiffy, the once shinning vehicle became a shabby paintless scrap.
It was incredible. 16 trunk bodies: 14 male and two female.
Ordinarily, it would have taken a good three-hour, job for a skilled
butcher to whack out the crevices seen in the wrecked vehicle and to
dismember the bodies as they were. But where were the butchers? Where
were the tools. The answers are shroud in secrecy in hi-tech safety
science.
The first doctor to arrive looked and made a blanket pronouncement of
death; 14 male; 2 female. And the 15 postgrads died, dead and
deadened. It was when the orthopaedic nurses tried to reassemble some
parts for identification (which they knew by sight would be
impossible) that I got effortlessly mad and suddenly discovered the
other beyond - reason element in this slaughter that I had already
stamped in my mind as beyond reason: MADNESS. Perishable madness.
Although we all know that man or woman is born under the sentence of
death but when 15 postgrads of a University in a developing country
like Nigeria with their brilliant promise only a quarter fulfilled
felled by an utterly incomprehensible tragedy through the use of
automobile, the loss is more than a great loss to the university as
postulated by Mr. Adebayo, the president of the Universities
Education Students Association (NUESA). It is a big loss to the
bereaved family. It is a loss suffered by the entire country, the
academic world, and all humanity.
That's one great reason today why it is necessary for the country to
take a more aggressive appraisal of the road accident scene. I am
sure that something fundamental within the subject matter demands
review, change, re-examination and redefinition. The magnitude and
alarming rate of fatality presently prevailing on our highways makes
it abundantly clear that something more than illiterate and
commercial driver, the age of the vehicle vis-a-vis poor maintenance,
the highway layout and the corrupt law enforcement agencies are the
epicentres of our traffic unsafety problems. the untimely death of
over 230,000 recorded persons and over 730,000 injured human beings
in over 920,000 accidents on our highways in the last 45 years
dictates a crying need to understand "WHY."
But that is statistics. Statistics, either mechanical or dry, is
statistics. And death, either mechanical or gruesome, is death. It is
therefore time to do away with double talk cosmeti and get down to
the substance of the specifics: saving lives. Statistics is
cosmetics. Death is substance.
The Question in our traffic scene is no longer when is it going to
happen but who's next? Your guess is as good as mine because as inter
alia stated, traffic accidents have no class barriers, geographical
boundaries or gender inequalities. Nobody is immune. It does not
respond to panic panaceas of fire brigade measures. it does not wait
for academic tautology or grandiloquent unfashionable theories. It
does not have patience for merger or no merger arguments.
It is a tough problem that does not believe in idle or
unsubstantiated experimentation but demand very touch solution. But
we can't get at any appreciable solution with our present level of
understanding and "Scissors and Paste" method. All the elements of a
highway safety matrix must be brought into balance. This is what the
social scientists call the "systems approach and the Engineers,
"systems engineering." There is need for an "umbrella" legislation
and administration in our highway safety beginning at the level of
the Presidency with the National Assembly providing good legal
foundations and adequate funding appropriated. The state's role must
become more prominent especially within its sub-political entities -
the local government, the private sector must function as the fourth
active partner in national highway safety effort.
And finally the Federal Government must make possible significant
innovative advances and extraordinary efforts in the delivery of
hi-tech highway safety services; federalistic approach; management
reforms, new funding system, traffic safety uniform standards, motor
vehicle safety standards, well-defined national goals, professional
manpower, and public acceptability.
So those who gathered at the funeral service for the 15 gone
academicians can begin to change the course of road safety in this
country. They should not only have gathered in desperation to bury
the departed students but also to start taking a realistic and
hard-headed look at road safety and the roles the university of
Ibadan must play within such a new framework. The emphasis should not
be upon negative but positive. From the educational stand point, why
can't the University start today the idea of establishing a Highway
Traffic Safety Centre? Michigan State University has one.
Missouri State University, south California University, Southern
Illinois University, University of North Carolina, Iowa University -
all have it.. Or in the alternative, spearhead the introduction of
traffic safety and driver education into the primary and secondary
schools in Nigeria. The UI Council, the Senate Academic Community,
and the Nations are ready to pay the price if such a project can help
bring an optimistic highway safety development not as astounding as
the 45 years of highway perishable madness behind us. Traffic safety
is an interdisciplinary subject and multi-jurisdictionary matter. The
subject can benefit from the University. Traffic safety need not be
all Abuja. The University of Ibadan can start The brainstorming today
because we don't have decades: we already had wasted decades. As
Mendelsohn aptly points out no one in his right mind wants to have an
accident. Safety itself needs no selling.

Original_Search_Title: Insight: History
Headline: Ghana: Insight: History
Body_Text:
Accra — The Long Trek: Malam Awudu Abdul Aziz like other contemporaries of his
had a good impression about the Gold Coast and so longed for the day
when he would set foot on this 'promised land' which to him flowed
with milk and honey.
In those days when non-farming engagements were scarce in Northern
Nigeria the Gold Coast on the other hand was blessed with jobs in
many of the emerging industries and farms. In 1947 Malam Awudu set
out in the company of two others for the Gold Coast to seek greener
pastures.
The good thing about the venture was that long before that time there
were in place thriving Zongo communities where new-arrivals were
taken care of until they were weaned to face life on their own. After
seeking the consent of his parents he joined two other persons for
the trip.
The kits for the journey consisted of a mat, a leather bag to contain
essentials like food, knife and others. There were also talismans and
other items to ward off evil on the long trek. If even vehicular
traffic was common in those days the financial situation of many in
those days could not support young men like Awudu and so the footpath
to the Gold Coast was the only option opened to him and his friends.
After all there was in existence the long established 'fataucin
Gbanja' route used by their forebears.
Consecutively the journey from Sokoto to the Kumasi, which was the
first major port of call of the trio, was in the neighbourhood of
over one thousand five hundred kilometers yet these men were able to
accomplish their mission in thirty-four days unscathed. In a blow by
blow presentation he recalls to the Accra Mail how they made the
trip.
TAKE-OFF From Sokoto we reached Jega and crossed River Sabara and
then advanced to Gidan Turu and then to Gidan Goje. We then moved on
to Gidan Tunda and from Kandi we entered Togo.
HAZARDS Although the journey was uneventful we nevertheless as we
moved through the bushes met hyenas and lions. After some time we
exhausted our stock of food so we started buying food at the various
stops on our way with the few coins we had on us. In those days the
Gold Coast and Nigeria had common currencies. We spent farthings,
halfpennies, pennies and shillings. Not all travelers made it to the
Gold Coast as some died and were buried.
The Sarkin Zango of a town where a traveler died was informed and he
arranged the burial of the deceased. His belongings were then
returned by a person who hailed from the same town with him.
ZANGON SALAGA With perseverance we reached Zangon Salaga, an ancient
settlement where our ancestors came to buy kola for the Hausa
markets. The town was referred to as a Zango for travelers who had to
reach other destinations had to take the much-needed rest at the
place. We rested for two days here before continuing with our
journey.
AKOSOMBO We set off again and reached a body of water at Akosombo
where a German pontoon ferried us across. We continued with the
journey until we reached Kumasi after some days.
BABA KOTIO In Kumasi we lodged at the house of a man called Baba
Kotio who ordered that we be served with tuon zaafi. The next day we
left for a town whose name I can't remember. In this town we got a
vehicle in which were other passengers clad in dark dresses. The
truck took us to a place where there was a group of people standing.
One of us Birbiso remembered the place as he had been to the Gold
Coast before. I smelled a rat as the driver made a stop. I warned the
driver that I would destroy his truck if he did not take us back.
CONTINUATION OF JOURNEY We were then conveyed to a town called Fosu
Prasu where we spent two days at the house of the 'Sarkin Fawa' or
chief butcher. From here we moved on to another town where we boarded
a truck for two pence and this took us to Accra.
KASOA AND WINNEBA We walked to Kasoa from where we continued the trip
to Winneba. The next town we went to in continuation of the journey
was Cape Coast where we were hosted by a Hausa man called Mamman.
Here we spent a year working as sand winners for 8 pence a day. With
one and halfpenny in those days one could buy a meal.
TAKORADI, TARKWA AND PRESTEA The next port of call was Takoradi where
as usual the Zongo offered us sanctuary from where we proceeded to
Tarkwa a journey of two days of walking, then to Bogoso and Prestea.
In the latter we spent 18 years working for a whiteman in a UAC store
for an income of seven pounds a month. The frontage of a shop served
as our sleeping place until we were able to hire a place for
ourselves for six pence a month.
STOP WORK! When independence was achieved for the Gold Coast the shop
ceased to operate as it was moved to Tarkwa which was a big town. In
Prestea I got a job as a night watchman. Word was sent to me by my
relation who was living in Samreboi that a job was waiting for me as
a security man. At Samreboi I took the job spending some five years
in the town.
ALIENS COMPLIANCE ORDER Enter the aliens compliance order and I
decided to return to Tarkwa. My employer refused to release me.
Finally though I managed to leave for Tarkwa where I landed a job
with a beer distribution company. This job saw me shuttling between
Bogosu, Essiama and Tarkwa.
PANBROS JOB From this occupation I moved to Kwashieman in Accra where
someone promised getting me a job with Panbros. I spent some four
years here but could not stand the many thefts going on at the place
and so decided to leave the job.
FAMILY LIFE In Prestea I married a Hausa lady and got my first son in
Tarkwa. This was followed by a girl who was born on the day Xmas
coincided with Sallah. I sent them to school but they were not
serious with their studies and so eventually dropped out.
COMPARISON BETWEEN The PAST AND PRESENT There are a lot of
differences between the olden days and now. In the past new arrivals
from Hausa land were fed for seven days by their hosts but today this
does not hold any longer. Today ordinary gari and sugar can cost one
thousand cedis.
HAUSA SETTLEMENTS There were many Zangos in Accra at the time of my
arrival in the town. Kwashieman for instance was a place where the
Hausas inhabited for a long time. A man who lived for about 115 years
in Kwashieman was called Billa. He asked to be buried at Kasoan Garba
on his death. Many of the early settlers at Kasoan Garba were
demobilised soldiers of the Gold Coast Regiment.
His relation called Sarki Tanko who is about 120 years now is still
living. Kasoa used to be dangerous in those days and only the
spiritually strong could dare tread the place even during the day.
The water body at Weija had a settlement called Makigani and Hausa
settlers peopled it. The colonial authorities moved these settlers to
a nearby hill where their descendants still live today.
OTHER SETTLEMENTS There were other Hausa settlements nearby like
Tungan Kada near Panbros which derived its name from the many
crocodiles at the place. The settlers used to hunt them. Others were
Oblogo and Malam named after one Malam Mashai whom I met when I
arrived in Accra. He also came to the Gold Coast on foot. H e was a
farmer cultivating sweet potatoes and henna.
CURRENT PLACE OF RESIDENCE I now live in a house owned by a woman who
has showed me so much love that I have adopted her as my daughter.
This part of the McCarthy Hill was uninhabited when I first came
there. There were some spirits here.
A white hen used to pass here with her chicks on Thursdays. It was a
jinn. There was also a female jinn but now all these have left the
place. They were disturbed by the blasting activities of the early
days on the hill.
AGE AND SECRET I am 86 years now and the only health problem I have
today is 'kookoo'(hemorrhoids). I have no friends and I do not
womanize. I do not engage in fights with people. I do not take
alcohol. The only drink I used to take was Portello. I take only
kola.
LINKS WITH HOME Ever since I set foot on the Gold Coast I have not
visited home again. Sometimes I have dreams of home though. There is
also no correspondence between me and the people at home.

Original_Search_Title: Vitamin 'A' Deficiency Can Lead Your Child To Death
Headline: Zambia: Vitamin 'A' Deficiency Can Lead Your Child To Death
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Have you ever thought of the serious consequences Vitamin A can cause
in the health of your child? How about having a blind child due to
Vitamin A deficiency? Perhaps, most people might not know that
Vitamin A supplementation is a very important component in the health
of children between the ages of six months and six years.
It is essential for the children to grow without major problems. With
enough Vitamin A, they are able to have a better chance of fighting
diseases such as diarrhoea, malnutrition, measles and other
infections.
The child's body needs enough of this important micro-nutrient to
grow healthy.
Take Brian, 4, as an example. His parents have been taking him to the
clinic to receive Vitamin A supplements since he was six months old.
He is very healthy and has very sharp eyes.
Now, imagine if his parents never took the responsibility of making
sure that he received his Vitamin A supplements.
Brain could have experienced a very painful life. A life of treating
measles, malnutrition, eye infections (Xerophtalmia) and many more.
His immune system could have reduced and made him vulnerable to many
attacks of diseases like diarrhoea.
In addition, his growth and development could have gone down due to
Vitamin A deficiency.
'It is painful to subject children to infections that can be
prevented simply and their severity by ensuring that they receive
their Vitamin A supplements every six months. Also their sight can be
protected from infections that could even lead to blindness,' Brain's
mothers Justina said.
Consequences Vitamin A deficiency is a major problem in Zambia. It
affects many children between the ages of six months and six years.
It reduces their immune system by making their bodies fail to fight
diseases.
Lack of Vitamin A can lead to serious consequences including death.
According to the National Food and Nutrition Commission (NFNC), other
consequences are complete blindness, night blindness, eye infection
(Xerophtalmia), higher risk of infections and poor growth and
development of the child.
To avoid these consequences, parents and guardians must ensure that
their children between six months and six years eat foods that are
rich in Vitamin A.
How to obtain Vitamin A However, NFNC says infants (zero to six
months) can obtain Vitamin A by strictly depending on breast milk
without getting any other food and water.
And for those children between six months and six years, parents and
guardians must give them enough foods that are rich in Vitamin A.
Foods such as meat, Kapenta, yellow fruits and dark green vegetables.
In addition, to help children's bodies absorb Vitamin A, parents
should prepare food with enough oils by adding groundnuts (intwilo)
to porridge, sweet potatoes and vegetables just to mention a few.
By making children eat the above-mentioned foods, their bodies will
be capable of fighting against many diseases.
Children must receive Vitamin A supplementation During Child health
week (February 26 to March 4), parents and guardians must ensure that
all children aged between six months and six years are taken to the
clinic to receive Vitamin A supplementation. This is very important
because it ensures that children have enough Vitamin A in their
bodies.
They must receive this vital micro-nutrient even if they take foods
that are rich in Vitamin A because it is difficult for children to
absorb enough from food. This will help them fight diseases.
The NFNC recommends that children between six months and six years
are given Vitamin A capsule every six months as a measure to help
fight Vitamin A deficiency. For lactating mothers, they should take
Vitamin A capsules within four weeks after birth.
Male participation Fathers and male relatives must participate in
child health week activities and take children to health centres to
receive Vitamin A supplementation.
If men are involved in these activities, then the chances of the
child getting their vitamin A supplements will increase.
Moreover, pressure on their spouses and female relatives to get all
other work and still rush the child to the hospital or clinic will
decrease, leading to a healthier and happier family.
QUIZ QUESTIONS 1. Why is Vitamin A important to the growth of the
child? Name two consequences of vitamin A deficiency.
2. Why is it important for men to participate in child health week
activities? Send your answers to the address below: To Your Health is
brought to you by the Central Board of Health. In addition, please
tune in to Your Health Matters on ZNBC's Radios One and Two and
Television.
If you need more information on this and any health topic, write to:
The Producer To Your Health Central Board of Health P O Box 32588
Ndeke House Lusaka HEALTH REFORMS NOW. ITS YOU AND ME FOR BETTER
HEALTH!

Original_Search_Title: Food & Drinks: Healthy Eating Even In Restaurants?
Headline: Nigeria: Food & Drinks: Healthy Eating Even In Restaurants?
Body_Text:
Lagos — It is not absurd that many people do not know how to choose what to
eat when they eat out.
You should choose only meals that are healthy for you. Restaurants
that serve foreign dishes now abound in Nigeria, making the question
of choice increasingly difficult. There are Italian, Chinese,
Lebanese, French, Mexican and Indian restaurants and fast food joints
fast out- pacing the local cafeteria.
The good news about Italian foods, for instance, is that the basics
are healthy. Italian foods include pasta, fresh vegetables, tomato-
based sauces, lean meats, poultry, seafood, and fresh bread. Calories
and fat slowly begin to mount when oil, butter, or cream is taken in
big portions. When you decide to dine at any Italian restaurant, some
of these healthy tips should guide you:
-Salad can be a good starter and filler. Ask for a salad with the
salad dressing or oil and vinegar on the side so that you can do away
with the excess. While olive oil is healthier than saturated fats
like butter, a tablespoon can still drizzle over 13 grams of fat on
your radicchio. Keep that number in mind when you're sopping fresh
Italian bread in deliciously flavoured olive oil on your table.
-If you love antipasto, go easy on the fatty cold cuts and cheeses;
order a vegetable antipasto. Watch the dressing.
-Classic pasta with marinara sauce can be a lean choice. Many
restaurants have fresh, shredded parmesan cheese. A sprinkle adds
only a smidgen of fat (one tablespoon) lus tons of sharp flavour.
-If you want a heavier entree such as fettuccini alfredo or lasagna,
then downsize. Forget about ordering it as an entree if the plate is
the size of a platter. See if you can get an appetizer-size portion.
By reducing the serving size, the fat and calories are also cut. You
can save additional calories and fat if you skip the dressing on your
salad and bypass the spread on the bread.
Here's another portion control trick: Ask for a half portion or have
only half of your entree served in the restaurant. Take the remainder
home for the next day's dinner. Think of this as dining out twice;
the bonus is that you only had to tip once (ha, ha).
For proper handling of take-home foods, ask the waiter to store your
leftovers in the refrigerator until you're ready to leave the
restaurant. Perishable food shouldn't stay at room temperature for
more than two hours. Re-heat food thoroughly to a temperature of 165
degrees F.
Although naked pasta is typically low in fat at about 200 calories
for a cooked cup, eating a large quantity of linguine could have you
bumping your daily calorie limit into the next day.
If you want something a little sweet at the end of the meal, order a
decaf cappuccino. Made with low-fat milk and topped with a sprinkling
of cocoa or cinnamon, it can replace the cannoli, or to make it
easier, share it with a friend.
However if tonight's dinner is a splurge night, just balance the
extra fat and calories over the next several days.
best bets in Chinese restaurants
The high increase in the number of Asian food lovers has created much
curiosity that a report was recently carried out to unravel exactly
what people got in Chinese food. After trekking across the country
and collecting 900 soups, appetisers, and entrees from 11 Chinese
restaurants, it was no surprise that the sodium content was hefty in
most items and the fat content high in deep-fried choices. But the
biggest surprise was that the average portion for the entrees tested
ranged from 15 to 26 ounces, which is enough food for a small Chinese
village. A surprise indeed! Here are some useful tips when eating
Chinese:
-Share among friends: When ordering, calculate at least two or three
people per entree. This will help alleviate the over-ordering
problem.
-Start with soup: Bypass more calorie-laden appetizers in favour of
soups, especially vegetable-based ones which are filling. Wonton soup
has less than 100 calories and only two grams of fat. Unfortunately,
at 641 mg per serving, the sodium is hefty. Most other Chinese
entrees also fare poorly when it comes to sodium. It is therefore
recommended that you keep your sodium intake under 2,400 mg daily or
balance it out the following day.
-Order steamed or stir-fried, not deep-fried portions: While sweet
and sour pork is a porker at over 20 grams of fat for a small, six-
ounce portion, the steamed fish had 11 grams of fat due, in part, to
its oil-based sauce. To bypass the fat, ask the chef to go easy with
the sauce, or better still, have the sauce on the side and only
drizzle a small amount on top. While you're at it, also ask the chef
to use a light touch with the oil when stir-frying.
-Go long on veggies: Few people can make vegetables taste as good as
the Chinese. Order a platter of steamed vegetables and mix them into
your entree. You may expand the entree to feed an army, and this
trick dilutes the fat and calories per serving. Make sure that the
steamed vegetables are free of sauce and seasoning that can spike the
sodium and fat content.
-Roll out the rice: You haven't lived it up, food-wise until you have
eaten steamed brown rice from a Chinese restaurant. It's absolutely
tasty and a low-fat filler. You won't miss the fattier fried rice
after you've tried it.
meatless fast foods
Some people now have to do without eating meat. Eating well while on
the run is difficult enough, but when you cut away meat, fish, or
poultry from the list of possibilities and add the desire to shed
unwanted pounds, it may seem like an Olympic challenge. But it's not
impossible.
Assuming that you eat dairy products, such as cheese, and legumes,
such as beans and chickpeas, I think these tips may help you scale
the hurdles:
-For a quick, no-fuss meal, head straight to a good supermarket or
deli salad bar. Typically, most are not only stuffed with tons of
low- calorie fresh vegetables, but also protein-rich items, such as
low-fat cottage cheese, chickpeas, three-bean salad, and possibly
too, chunks of tofu. Add some low-fat salad dressing and pita bread,
and you'll be delighted.
-Bean soups can also be part of a hearty lunch or dinner. Many take-
out delis, restaurants, and upscale supermarkets now serve takeaway-
soups. Order a hearty bowl and grab packed salad along with a whole
grain bagel, roll or bread. Pizza with vegetables and a salad is a
quick fix and can be found at most pizza parlours.
-Some fast-food restaurants offer baked potatoes. Order it plain
along with a side of steamed broccoli. Then head for the salad bar
and scoop low-fat cottage cheese to top your broccoli spud.
-Sometimes you may need to fortify your meals on wheels. Many folks
keep vegetarian burgers in their office freezers. When they are out
at noontime, they buy a salad 'on the run', and later at the office
zap the burger in the microwave and stick it in a pita. Try it, you'd
like it too.
seafood restaurants
To get he best low-cal options when ordering seafood requires that
when you dine at your local seafood restaurant, try to fish out the
best choices on the menu. Lean choices are endless.
-Most seafood is low in fat and calories: A three-ounce cooked
portion (about the size of a deck of cards) of either haddock, cod,
or halibut is less than 120 calories and no more than three grams of
fat. Shrimp, scallops, and clams are also great choices, with only
about 80 to 125 calories and one to two grams of fat. (Shrimp is
higher in dietary cholesterol, with a little over 165 milligrams in
three ounces.
The current recommendation is to keep daily dietary cholesterol under
300mg, on the average.) While salmon has a little more fat--nine
grams in three ounces cooked--the majority of it is the healthier,
unsaturated type. Ask the chef to go easy with the oil, butter, or
any other added fat when preparing your fish entree.
-Look for buzzwords: Words like baked, broiled, steamed, poached,
boiled, or grilled keep the calories and fat under wraps. Breaded
seafood such as fish and chips can soak up fat like a sponge. The
deep- fried Big Fish burger is a big catch at 720 calories and 43
grams of fat. Some of the added fat comes from the tartar sauce
served with the Big Fish. A tablespoon of tartar sauce can have 70
calories and eight grams of fat.
-Get a workout during your meal: A one-pound lobster in the shell has
less than 3.5 ounces of meat, about 85 calories, and less than a gram
of fat: the perfect entree to help you exercise perfect portion
control. And the best part is that you have to work at fishing out
all the meat, which slows down your eating and keeps your hands busy.
Bypass the dish of melted butter, which adds an extra 108 calories
and over 12 grams of fat per tablespoon. Instead, ask for an
additional empty plate and plenty of lemon slices. Squeeze the lemon
into the empty dish and then add a small amount of melted butter.
Presto: lemon sauce with a tinge of butter rather than the inverse.
-Don't go silly with the sauces: Creamy entrees, such as lobster
'newborn' (made with butter or margarine, egg yolks, and cream), can
tip the scale at 485 calories and over 25 grams of fat per cup. If a
creamy casserole or cheesy seafood dish is a favourite, order an
appetizer-size portion.
-Chow down only a cup of chowder: No one loves chowder better than
me. If you're a fan of this creamy delight, make it a cup, not a
bowl, to put a lid on the amount. Consider ordering a lean entree to
offset the calories. Tomato-based Manhattan clam chowder is typically
a leaner bet.
-Slide the bulk aside: Plain baked potatoes, garden salads, and
steamed vegetables will add bulk to your meal. Keep the spread and
salad dressing on the side and use it sparingly to help control the
calories.
Stay healthy.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's Face For Africa
Headline: Nigeria's Face For Africa
Body_Text:
The Nigerian leg of the year 2001 Face of Africa finals coming up
later in the year in Sun City, Cape Town, South Africa yields pretty
Adeola Adenugba, YINKA IJABIYI reports
The atmosphere at the Pro-flex Gym., venue of the second stage of the
preliminary screenings in the Lagos regional search for the Nigerian
finalist to the year 2001 Face of Africa finals (presented by M-Net
and Anglogold), was highly relaxed on the surface. The girls arrived
early and quickly formed alliances and friendships while they waited
for the screening to begin. Many had even began exchanging addresses
long before the commencement of the day's activities.
Soon, the screening team arrived and the day's task kicked off. The
girls were called in for the first level of screening which was the
height check.
Those who could not measure up to 5'8 were simply asked to go. In
some rare cases, a few who where too tall were disqualified.
Waistlines, bursts and shoulders caused others the chance to be super
models like Oluchi. There were those who got screened out because
their figure did not meet the required specification. Others just
didn't have the face, look, teeth or any of the other tiny details
that helped determine who becomes a super model and who has a chance
of becoming a model.
At the end of the highly tasking and tiring process, nine of the
girls were determined to have qualities that would enthrall the
fashion and beauty world.
The nine girls that emerged from the screening were expected to join
the group of girls that had earlier scaled the hurdles in the race
for the Face of Africa bumper four year prize in November last year.
Together, the two groups would join the quarter finalists from the
Abuja and Port-Harcourt preliminaries.
On Wednesday, 31 January 2001, the search finally came to an end. The
duo of Jan Malan and Ipetla Moatshe flew in from South Africa to
choose that one girl that had the most potential to be the Face of
Africa 2001. The first part of the three part screening was concluded
before a small crowd that comprised mostly journalists.
At the end of the first round, only seven of the twenty-seven girls
passed the test administered by Face of Africa originator and
outgoing producer, Jan Malan and in-coming producer, Ipetla Moatshe.
Unlike the other girls who had dropped out before this stage, there
were plenty of wet eyes, sour faces and bitter miens amongst the
twenty girls who got kicked out in the quarter-final. These girls
were escorted out of the screening hall to await the neatly packed
potato-chips and chicken lunch packs which was to be handed out to
them as their share of the elusive Face of Africa largesse.
Male journalists too had a taste of how the defeated girls must have
felt when they were escorted out of the screening room. They too were
politely but unceremoniously shepherded out of the room. The next
round of screening involved the girls appearing in swim suits. The
M-Net crew must have believed that allowing the "boys" see the sweet
sixteens clad in such sheer briefs, might cause the loss of a few
senses. After it became obvious that the nooks and crannies where
they might slip into to see the best of the girls was non-existent,
the 'boys' slunk off. The girls had their bikini parade without the
male journalists and at the end of it, Jan and Ipetla still could not
decide who would carry Nigeria's flag in South Africa in May. The
bikini parade was meant to help reveal any tell-tale ugly birth-marks
or scars that might further help to scale down the number .
Obviously, none had such misfortune. Finally, Jan and Ipetla had to
put it to vote. Voting was done by secret balloting. They both had to
choose one girl out of the lot and they had just one vote a piece.
Well, they both chose the same girl at the first ballot.
Nenye Ochuba (17), Fadimiyi Tola, Omoruwa Ebhojie, Ebisan Tonwe (15),
Agbani Darego (18) and Odisika Odogwu (18) finally had to bow to the
superior beauty (or is it Africaness?) of petite, leggy and
innocent-looking sixteen year-old Adeola Adenugba. Adeola thus would
be on the ramp in South Africa come May, for the finals.
Adeola lives in Lagos. She only recently completed her SSCE
examination at the Federal Government College, Ijanikin. She was
waiting at home for her university admission to fall through before
the M-NET win. Now, her life has just changed. Come May, she will
compete for the final crown of Face of Africa alongside twenty three
other belles from thirty African nations.
Adeola stands the chance of winning a lucrative four year modelling
contract worth a whooping two hundred thousand dollars (twenty
million naira) with Ford Models Inc. America's largest and most
prestigious modelling agency. This prize is unlike the one hundred
and fifty thousand dollars for a three year modelling contract with
Elite Models that Oluchi, Benvinda and Nombulelo, the three earlier
winners, respectively pocketed.
Though, the Ford Models Inc. prize is not that different from the
Elite prize. The new winner will simply get a chance to work for an
extra year under Ford Models.
Apart from the final winner's mouthwatering prize, the twenty-three
other finalists will not be going home empty-handed. Courtesy M-Net,
the finalists (like Adeola, if she doesn't become the fourth Face )
will get an opportunity to pursue careers in modelling in South
Africa.
M-NET now has a modelling agency to help manage the girls who made it
to the finals. This implies that, as long as they choose to (or as
long as modelling jobs can be found for them), the girls will live
the lives of princesses in South Africa. Or even America or Europe.
Like Ojy Eguwuenu and Funmilayo Alaiyemola, both Nigerian finalists
at last year's Face. Funmi continues to be a wave making model in
South Africa even now. Ojy has chosen to pick up a modelling contract
in New York. Like Funmi and Ojy, win or lose, the fun has only just
begun for Adeola.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers To Tackle Wildlife Menace
Headline: Kenya: Farmers To Tackle Wildlife Menace
Body_Text:
A fund has been set up to finance farmers' court cases for damage caused
by stray wildlife from private ranches in Laikipia District.
Speaking during the first meeting to raise money for the fund on Saturday,
Laikipia East Member of Parliament Mwangi Kiunjuri said the argument that
all wildlife belongs to the Government and not the ranchers on whose land
it lives has been used to deny villagers justice when they are attacked,
killed and their crops destroyed.
The meeting, at Tigithi, was the seventh organised by the MP in the
past month. Mr Kiunjuri has been meeting the people to map out strategies
to set up the fund and raise money for legal suits over the perennial wildlife
menace in the area. Other meetings have been held in Lamuria, Ngobit, Sirima,
Sweet Waters, Marura, and Charua.
At least 10,000 signatures will be collected to support the programme.
Village committees have been asked to collect five shillings from each
villager for the fund.
Some 13 people have been killed by wildlife in Laikipia since 1996,
but no compensation has been paid. Scores of others have been injured and
crops destroyed.
Recently, Mr Kiunjuri successfully tabled a motion in Parliament which
raised the minimum compensation for people killed by wildlife from Sh30,000
to Sh1 million.
Presently, all powers and controls over wildlife are vested in the Government
through the Kenya Wildlife Service.
Meanwhile, farmers in Kieni West, Nyeri District, have complained that
monkeys and other wildlife straying from a game lodge are destroying their
crops.
The farmers, who claimed that they had lost their harvest for years
to destruction by elephants and buffaloes, said the monkeys, warthogs and
wild pigs wandering from the neighbouring Aberdare Country Club's game
lodge had set on their crops.
They said fields of green maize, potatoes, cabbages and tomatoes had
been destroyed and that many farmers have to keep a 24-hour watch to try
to ward off the animals.
The club is a serving base for the Ark Game Lodge, situated in the Aberdare
Forest, and is owned by Lonrho Hotels.
The club's management said it was unaware of the problem and that the
matter had not been reported to them.
Although Mr Damian Davies, the general manager, said he could not rule
out invasion by the monkeys, he explained that the animals were the responsibility
of the Kenya Wildlife Service.
"The monkeys are very tricky and can jump over the electric fence, especially
during this dry season, as they look for food," he said.
He advised the farmers to officially report the matter to him so that
he can take it up with the KWS.

Original_Search_Title: Hippos Cause Food Crisis In Eastern Cameroon
Headline: Cameroon: Hippos Cause Food Crisis In Eastern Cameroon
Body_Text:
Yaounde — The riparian population of
Cameroon, along River Doume in the Mbang administrative
division of Eastern province, is currently facing a food crisis due
to the destruction of their crops by herds of hippopotamuses
which gathered on the farms for weeks.
The animals live on subsistence crops such as groundnuts,
sweet potatoes, melon, maize, cucumber and cassava leaves.
Residents of the area are experiencing an acute shortage of
cassava which is their staple food.
According to reports from the area, cassava and other tuber
crops such as onions that were on the farms, were destroyed by the
large herbivorous animals.
Meanwhile, the provincial authorities have visited the area to
assess the crop loss caused by the animals on about 20 acres. They
promised to find ways of preventing a recurrence of such an invasion.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Hit At Moi On Kirwa Order
Headline: Kenya: Leaders Hit At Moi On Kirwa Order
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A number of leaders yesterday defended MP Kipruto Kirwa against
President Moi's Saturday onslaught.
Mr. Kirwa, the Cherangany MP and a persistent critic of his party,
Kanu, and its top leadership, said he was unperturbed by the Head of
State's "pitiless" warning to leaders not to have any links with him.
"I am not bothered," he said on the telephone. He vowed not to
backtrack on his vow to decamp from Kanu, and stated that no
intimidation would sway him from the cause.
An ally, MP Jimmy Angwenyi, hoped the President had merely been
misquoted, while a Nakuru-based human rights lawyer, Mr. Mirugi
Kariuki, said it was now clear who the force behind the violence that
has dogged rallies convened by the MP is.
"Moi cannot choose friends for other people! His enemies are not
necessarily the enemies of his friends!" said Mr. Angwenyi, also a
Kanu dissenter.
He said President Moi should draw a line between dissent and enmity:
"He should regard everybody as a friend and not make public
statements disparaging elected leaders. There should be differences
in opinion, but, as a leader, he should be able to know that such a
situation does not add up to enmity," he said.
On his part, Mr. Kariuki said: "It is quite clear Moi was been behind
the disruption of Kirwa's harambees. The police and the provincial
administration had only been Moi's agents until yesterday [Saturday],
when he came out in the open. An elephant cannot hide under growing
sweet potato vines!
"The President's statement was unconstitutional. Kirwa, like anybody
else, had a constitutional right of assembly and association. It is
unfortunate that the Head of State can stoop so low as to put the law
aside so that he can demonstrate his hate for an individual. It is
now quite clear who has been fighting Kirwa!"
The Kanu secretary-general, Mr. Joseph Kamotho, supported the
President, saying: "If you associate with a rebel, you may as well be
mistaken for being one. The man should be shunned!"
At a harambee at Cherangany's Kapcheplanget Secondary School,
President Moi reportedly exhorted leaders to sever any links with Mr.
Kirwa, who has persistently criticised Kanu's top leadership.
"From today, I will keep my eyes open and if any leader who says he
is with me talks to him basi (that's it)!" said the President. He
directed Cabinet Ministers Nicholas Biwott, Julius Sunkuli, Henry
Kosgey, and Assistant Minister William Ruto to convene fund-raisers
in Mr. Kirwa's constituency.
But Mr. Kirwa made it clear to the President that he was not in
competition with him on fund-raisers: "I welcome anyone to hold an
harambee in my constituency, but I doubt they will get the crowd for
their functions."
He said he had already indicated he would not defend his seat at the
next General Election on a Kanu ticket.
On how he will be voting in the House following the party's de-
whipping of rebel MPs, he said he will support what is rational:
"Even that [Kanu] whip has was never applied to me before it was
withdrawn, I always voted on my own conscience."
Mr. Kirwa claimed a parastatal executive was being groomed by Kanu to
wrest the seat from him.
Later, he addressed the Press in Eldoret Town, where he hit at the
Government and the President over the ills facing farmers
countrywide. He warned that the Government risked inviting a farmers'
revolt if it implements President Moi's Jamhuri Day proposal to tax
idle land.

Original_Search_Title: Obure Denies GM Food Claim
Headline: Kenya: Obure Denies GM Food Claim
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The Government now says it is not against genetically-modified foods.
Agriculture Minister Chris Obure told reporters in his office on
Wednesday: "The Government has not changed its policy. In fact, we
are carrying out research on biotechnology and its use" .
Mr. Obure said research on GM foods by the Kenya Agricultural
Research Institute (KARI) in collaboration with multinational
companies was still on course.
On Tuesday, as he opened an agricultural workshop in Nairobi, the
minister warned farmers to be wary of genetically modified foods.
Mr. Obure said he had advised farmers not to abandon traditional
foods for modern ones.
The director of agriculture, Mr. Wilfred Mwangi, said the government
had begun collaborating with international research organisations to
ensure biotechnology was embraced.
In a press statement, Kinyua M'Mbijjewe of Monsanto Kenya, said the
organisation believed biotechnology was a safe and sustainable tool
for agricultural and economic development.
"We believe that biotechnology is one of the technologies that, if
accompanied with appropriate policies, can transform agriculture,"
said Mr. M'Mbijjewe.
He said evidence from countries like China, South Africa, the USA,
Canada and Argentina where the company marketed GM products showed
that farmers had realised increased yields, lower inputs and better
pest management.
On the relationship between GM foods and traditional crops, Mr.
M'Mbijjewe said biotechnology was being used to safeguard
conventional crops from pests and diseases.
"Crops like cassava, sweet potato and bananas have been improved to
protect them against viral and fungal diseases," he said.
The biotechnology company maintained it made a commitment in 1999 not
to commercialise the sterile seed technology popularly referred to as
"the terminator technology".
On the dangers posed by GM foods, the ministry of agriculture said
Kenya had a National Biosafety Committee in place mandated to
regulate the new products in the market.

Original_Search_Title: Antarctic Duo's Food Supplies Running Low
Headline: South Africa: Antarctic Duo's Food Supplies Running Low
Body_Text:
Antarctic adventurers Ann Bancroft and Liv Arnesen are within two
"good wind" days of the South Pole on their epic trans-continental
trek, but they're going to arrive at the emotional mid-way point
pretty hungry. The lack of wind that has plagued them from the
beginning of their journey at Blue One ice runway near the South
African side of the coast of Antarctica on November 14 has left them
short of dried meals.
On Sunday, they were just over 200km from the South Pole on day 62 of
their 100-plus-days, 3 800km trek that, if successful, will make them
the first women to cross the frozen continent on foot, using only
skis and sails attached to body harnesses.
Expedition headquarters in Minnesota in the United States reported
that American Bancroft, 45, and Norwegian Arnesen, 47, were running
out of meals because of their slower-than-planned progress.
On Sunday, they had only one freeze-dried dinner and seven breakfasts
left, although they still had enough "sweet" food containing lots of
calories to allow them to reach the pole safely.
"But the calories are mostly in the form of foods like chocolate and
granola bars and sports drinks, forcing them to modify their daily
diet," headquarters reported.
"They have enough 'sweet' calories to allow them to reach the South
Pole, even if the wind doesn't blow. But Liv reports they are craving
food that isn't sweet. They dream of cheese.
And wind."
The two polar adventurers will have their supplies topped up at the
pole for the rest of the 1 637km journey from the pole to McMurdo
Station on the New Zealand side of Antarctica, which they hope to
reach on or about February 15.
They had good winds on three days last week, and covered 106.6km on
Wednesday - their best so far - 89.4km on Thursday, and 49.8km on
Friday. But the wind died again on Saturday, allowing them to ski for
only 3.9km before stopping to rest and waiting for more wind.
"With two good days of wind, they can reach the pole. Without wind,
it could take a week," headquarters reported. To keep warm in
temperatures that can drop to minus 40ÂºC or lower, and to
maintain high energy levels needed for pulling their 113kg sleds and
coping with the physical demands of their sails, Bancroft and Arnesen
are eating and drinking foods high in nutrients, calories and fats.
The combination of extreme cold and up to 16 hours of skiing each day
requires each of them to eat (or drink, through sports drinks)
between 4 500 and 5 000 calories a day.
"An average-sized woman should eat 1 500 to 2 000 calories a day,
while the average sized man should eat 2 000 to 2 500," headquarters
said.
Their breakfasts consist of hot cereal, fortified with oil or butter;
hot drinks, including coffee and cocoa fortified with a high fat
cream; and a multi-vitamin high in calcium.
Bancroft and Arnesen do not eat lunch, but snack throughout the day
at hourly intervals, taking in chocolate bars, caramels, sports
drinks containing lots of calories and electrolytes, and assorted
nuts and dried fruit.
At night, while melting snow for water, they eat potato crisps (a
good source of fat, salt and calories) and then have a cup of soup.
This is followed by the freeze-dried evening meal, supplied by a
Norwegian company, that is fortified with fat.
Flavours include cod stew with sour cream, beef and potato stew, and
pasta bolognaise.
After dinner they will have a hot drink like mint tea or hot
chocolate, but nothing containing caffeine.
This is because caffeine is a diuretic (it makes you urinate a lot)
and decreases the blood flow to the extremities, increasing the
potential for frostbite.
To follow the two adventurers' progress, visit their website at
www.yourexpedition. com

Original_Search_Title: Regional Update
Headline: West Africa: WEST AFRICA: IRIN-WA Update 887
Body_Text:
WA: ECOWAS ambassadors concern over citizen's safety
Ambassadors of the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS)
have demanded that the Ivorian government protect all foreign
nationals from mob attacks amid allegations of foreign involvement in
Sunday's attempted coup, the official Fraternite-Matin reported on
Thursday.
The ambassadors, representing nine of the 15 West African states that
make up ECOWAS, conveyed their message on Wednesday to Foreign
Affairs Minister Abou Sangare. The delegation, while condemning the
coup, outlined reports of attacks on non-Ivorian residents. The
ambassadors said that non-Ivorian university students were chased out
of their dormitories, families thrown out of their homes and salesmen
forced to close shops in Abidjan.
The consulate of Burkina Faso, in the central business district of
the city, was attacked by a mob. The Burkinabe have been at the
centre of suspicion and allegations of supporting the overthrow of
the government. This is because a leading Ivorian political figure,
Alassan Ouattara, has been disqualified from running for political
office on the grounds that he once held Burkinabe nationality.
Sangare promised that government would take "every measure" to ensure
the security of foreigners and their property.
COTE D'IVOIRE: Religious leaders call for calm
Religious leaders have called for self-restraint and an end to the
harassment of foreigners, following suspicions of their involvement
in Sunday's failed coup, the official 'Fraternite-Matin' reported.
The leaders on Thursday called on Ivorians to avoid taking the law
into their own hands and await the outcome of the government's
investigation into the revolt.
NIGERIA: Another girl faces flogging for sex
A second teenage girl has been sentenced to flogging by an Islamic
court in northern Nigeria after being found guilty of engaging in
premarital sex, AFP reported on Thursday.
It said family members of Attine Tanko, 18, of Malumfashi town in
Katsina State, said she was convicted on 15 November 2000 and
sentenced to 100 strokes of cane, after she was discovered pregnant
while unmarried. The sentence is to be carried out after she has
given birth and weaned her baby over 18 months to two years.
Her boyfriend, Lawal Sada, received a similar sentence which was
carried out in public the same day.
In an earlier case in nearby Zamfara State, Bariya Ibrahim Magazu,
17, was sentenced to 180 strokes for premarital sex and falsely
accusing three men of being responsible for her pregnancy. Magazu
gave birth in December and is due to receive the punishment 40 days
later.
Zamfara state authorities have rebuffed protests by local and foreign
human rights groups. The state was the first in Nigeria's mainly
Muslim north to adopt full Islamic law, a step subsequently followed
by eight other states.
NIGERIA: Pfizer summoned over drug trial
Nigerian representatives of the US pharmaceutical company Pfizer have
been asked to appear before a committee investigating clinical trials
of its drug, Trovan, on patients with cerebro-spinal meningitis in
northern Nigeria in 1996, 'The Guardian' Lagos daily reported on
Friday.
Also to give evidence before the 10-member committee appointed by
Minister of Health Tim Menakaya are senior officials of the Aminu
Kano Specialist Hospital, Kano, where the trials took place.
Representatives of Kano State Ministry of Health, the National Agency
for Food and Drug Administration, and Medicines Sans Frontieres will
also face the committee.
The investigations follow a recent report in the 'Washington Post'
newspaper alleging that Pfizer carried out the trials at a time the
drug had not been approved for use by the US authorities, and without
the knowledge and consent of patients used as Guinea pigs.
The newspaper further alleged that use of the drug was not
discontinued when it was found ineffective, despite the availability
of approved alternatives. It said 11 children died while on Trovan,
while many others suffered different forms of disability.

"We want to establish the moral, legal and scientific basis for the
trial...If they are right in what they did, we commend them. If they
are wrong, we shall condemn them and probably seek legal remedies,"
'The Guardian' quoted Menakaya as saying.
CHAD: Government denies killing POW's
The government has denied a claim that it has killed 13 members of
the anti-government Mouvement pour la democratie et la justice au
Tchad (MDJT) who were taken prisoner in the recent fighting in
northern Chad.
The MDJT claims that the prisoners of war, including one of the
movement's leaders, Yaya Labadri, were executed earlier this week by
forces loyal to Chadian President Idriss Deby, AFP reported on
Thursday.
However, Minister of Defence Wielding Assi Assoue denied the charge
but conceded, in a radio broadcast, that Labadri died from his wounds
on 28 December, 2000. Assoue said the remaining 12 POW's were still
alive.
Government forces and the MDJT have been fighting in the northern
Tibesti region since 1998. The MDJT disapproves of Deby's regime and
has been demanding his resignation.
GHANA: Women demand that police chief goes
A non-partisan coalition of Ghanaian women, Sisters Keepers, has
demanded that President John Kufuor fire the police chief because of
his inability to end the serial killing of women in Accra, the Ghana
News Agency reported.
"We can no longer tolerate the anti-people and anti-women attitudes
repeatedly demonstrated by the head of the police," Elizabeth Akpalu,
the coordinator of the coalition, said.
She added that Inspector General of Police Peter Nanfuri had "not
deployed or organized to serve the public interest". So far, 30 women
have been killed since 1997. Most have been found naked with torn
clothes left nearby.
Nanfuri, under public pressure to quit, said in December last year
that resignation would be "cowardly, defeatist and a disgrace".
GHANA: National security adviser appointed
President John Kufuor has appointed Joshua Hamidu National security
adviser, state-owned radio announced on Thursday. Hamidu was a
retired army lieutenant-general and chief of defence staff. In
