addition, he served as Ghana's ambassador to Senegal.
CAMEROON: World Bank supports HIV/AIDS project
Cameroon has received US $50-million for an HIV/AIDS prevention
programme, the World Bank announced on Thursday. The loan would
strengthen the capacity of local communities to fight the spread of
the disease.
The money would support Cameroon's national strategy for the fight
against the disease, launched last year. It would allow the
communities to design their own programmes; implement education and
awareness programmes, and coordinate their efforts with national
organizations.
The project, which is part of the bank's Multi-Country HIV/AIDS
Programme in Africa, would receive an additional $10 from the
government and local donors. Cameroon's first AIDS case was reported
in 1986. As at September 2000, some 937,000 of Cameroon's 14.2
million people were infected with the HIV virus that leads to AIDS.
NIGER: Dam projects launched
Niger President Mamadou Tandja has launched a programme for the
construction of dams in every "sub-district" of the country to
improve water supply and agricultural activities, PANA reported on
Thursday.
The agency said the launch took place on Wednesday in the town of
Bankor, 120 km west of the capital, Niamey. The agency said the
project would cater for 4,563 people including 200 farmers who would
conveniently cultivate cassava, cowpeas, sweet potatoes and potatoes
the agency said.
Abidjan, 12 January 2001; 18:15 GMT

Original_Search_Title: President Tandja Initiates Dam Project
Headline: Niger: President Tandja Initiates Dam Project
Body_Text:
Niamey, Niger — President Mamadou Tandja of Niger,
has launched the construction of a mini-dam and water reservoir
facilities in a bid to improve the living condition of rural
the population, official sources said.
The project launched Wednesday in Bankor, 120 km west of
the capital, Niamey involves the construction of three dams per
year in each sub-district of the country.
The sources said the project is also aimed at alleviating
poverty by improving agricultural production through the
construction of water supply facilities, the development of
arable lands and promotion of the fisheries sector.
The Bankor project would cater for 4,563 people including
200 framers who would conveniently cultivate cassava, cowpeas,
sweet potatoes and potatoes.
The sources said the project would increase cereal
production to reach 228 tonnes each season.
Apart from agricultural production, the project would also
help reduce rural exodus as it would create jobs and off-season
farming activities including cattle herding and a variety of
other environmental activities.
Tandja said the initiative is in partial fulfilment of the
commitments he made to the people of Niger during his campaign
for president to alleviate suffering, hunger, malnutrition,
thirst, diseases and ignorance.
Meanwhile, latest official data indicate that the last
farming season in Niger recorded a deficit of some 163,000 tons
grains, thereby worsening the already precarious living
conditions of the people whose annual per capita income is about
60,000 CFA francs (1 US dollar = about 700 CFA francs).

Original_Search_Title: Districts Earn Sh4.8b Cotton Cash
Headline: Uganda: Districts Earn Sh4.8b Cotton Cash
Body_Text:
Kampala — Bushenyi, Kasese and Kabarole districts are set to earn
over sh.4.8bn from cotton this marketing season alone, state minister
for agriculture, Dr. Kibirige Ssebunya has announced.
Ssebunya revealed on Saturday at Karerera subcounty in Bushenyi
district.
Ssebunya who was accompanied by 15 MPs addressed farmers at the end
of a two- day cotton fields visit in Kasese and Bushenyi.
The minister said out of the 22,000 acres cotton farmers in the three
districts are expected to raise 1.2m kgs at sh400per kg. "You are
going to make real money and I must congratulate you the farmers of
Bushenyi and western Uganda in general for effectively contributing
to this country's ecomonic growth and development," Kibirige said.
"Beans, millet, Irish and sweet potatoes, maize, cotton coffee, tea
and everything is in western Uganda. What is not grown in western
Uganda?," Kibirige said to the amusement and excitement of the
farmers.
Cotton Development Organisation (CDO) board chairman, Levi Okodi said
cotton prices were at their lowest last year at sh230 a kilo.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security in The Sahelian Countries
Headline: West Africa: Irin Focus On Food Security in the Sahelian Countries //Yearender//
Body_Text:
This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations
The Sahel is known for its unpredictable weather patterns but in 2001 - particularly in the latter part of the year, it received well-spaced, consistent rainfall which helped bring crops to maturity and provided pasture for animals in most countries.
Food deficits are still likely in a few areas, but bumper cereal harvests are expected to cushion most Sahelian countries until the next harvests early in 2002. In some countries likely to suffer deficits, governments together with other partners and donors, have set up irrigation projects to provide water.
The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) said in a December report that bumper cereal crops harvested in the Sahel would "allow replenishment of farmers' and government stocks". Record harvests, it added, were estimated for Burkina Faso, The Gambia and Niger, while above average crops were estimated for Chad, Mali and Senegal.
Prospects were less favourable in Cape Verde and Guinea-Bissau when compared to last year. The same goes for Mauritania, where rain-fed crop production is likely to be 14 percent less than in 2000-2001 due to short dry spells and low yield from irrigated crops, according to the Famine Early Warning System Network (FEWS NET) November report.
Generally, national cereal output in Mauritania was expected to drop by about nine percent, the report said. A joint crop assessment mission to the country in November by FAO and the Permanent Interstate Committee For Drought Control in the Sahel (CILSS - French acronym) said the food supply situation would remain "tight" in several areas, notably in the Senegal river valley.
"Mauritania is a special case in any case - it never produces more than 35 percent of its cereal needs. Livestock seems more important," a WFP official in Dakar, Senegal, told IRIN in late December.
On the other hand, the forecast for Burkina Faso is no less than 2.6 million mt of cereals, according to that country's Ministry of Agriculture. "Such a harvest would make a great difference in terms of food security - availability as well as access - since last year the overall national cereal production hardly exceeded 1.8 million mt," FEWS NET said.
Farming is done on just 3.7 million ha of the 9.0 million ha of arable land in Burkina Faso. Less than a tenth of the country's nine billion cubic metres of water is used, various studies show. With the launch of a small-scale irrigation project in November, the government hopes to end cereal deficits in the country and targets an output of 4,000 mt of maize and 400 mt of niebe, a protein-rich bean.
In Mali, apart from localised pockets of drought which could affect crop yields, prospects for the 2001/2002 crop year are generally good. However, monitoring efforts will need to be stepped up to guard against pests such as grain-eating birds, "which are rapidly multiplying across a large part of the Niger River flood plain area," FEWS NET reported.
The overall food situation is "satisfactory" for Mali, FAO/CILSS said. It, however, noted that the reconstitution of the National Security Stock to its optimum level of 35,000 mt would require the purchase of 15,000 mt in 2001/02.
In July, a five-year irrigation project - Projet de developpement integral de l'aval de Manantali (PDIAM) - was launched in one of Mali's poorest regions to help the area attain food security. It involves the irrigation of 1,653 ha of land for rice farming on the River Senegal downstream of the Manantali Dam, PDIAM's Abdoulaye Dembele told IRIN. The planting, he noted, would start around April or May 2002.
In Niger, which had to appeal to donors for food aid after a disastrous 2000-01 harvest, rainfall was generally plentiful and well distributed, especially in July. Following good harvests, accumulated forage reserves would be sufficient to meet livestock needs until June 2002, FEWS NET said.
The Foreign Ministry had reported in April that about 40 percent of Niger's 10,094 villages had suffered food deficits of over 50 percent. The affected area had nearly 3.6 million inhabitants or 35 percent of the country's population. With funding from the European Union, 543 mt of fungicide-treated millet and sorghum seeds were distributed for planting to 713,643 people in villages with high food deficits.
After a food security assessment mission in Niger, FAO/CILSS reported in November that because of good harvests, farmers should be able to reconstitute their stocks. The government would also reconstitute the national security stock to its optimum level of 35,000 mt.
The food situation in Chad was described as "generally good" except in areas that were hit by floods that affected some 129,500 people. The worst affected areas were TandjilÃ©, Logone Occidental and Logone Oriental in the south, Baguirmi in the west and Ouaddai in the east. FEWS NET warned that conditions in the northern region of Kanem remained "precarious" following the influx of people expelled from Libya, and some 5,000 families displaced from the Tibesti mountains in the north, where armed rebels are operating.
In Cape Verde, the United States is to fund a food aid programme worth US $15.5 million over a five-year period, Thomas Gibb, country representative for the implementing NGO, ACDI/VOCA, told IRIN recently. Cape Verde, produces less than a third of its maize requirements, and imports 100 percent of other staples, such as rice, and oil. "Cape Verde is a food deficit country. It imports most of its food and often is reliant on food aid," Gibb explained. "It is a Sahelian country - very dry. And very little agriculture goes on."
In Senegal, WFP began a six-month emergency operation in October in the southern area of Casamance, home to a large number of internally displaced persons (IDPs), forced from their homes by a 19-year conflict between the state and an armed group fighting for self rule.
In Guinea-Bissau, the West African country in which WFP supports the highest percentage of the population, the agency's emergency operation faced a deficit of 2,400 mt for 2002.
Its emergency and development programmes target about 30 percent of the country's 1.1 million people, whose food security is threatened by two main factors: falling cashew nut prices and insecurity.

Original_Search_Title: Parastatals debt balloons to $60b
Headline: Zimbabwe: Parastatals debt balloons to $60b
Body_Text:
TOTAL parastatals debt, both domestic and foreign, is expected to be over $60 billion this year - a 33% increase from last year's $45 billion - a figure analysts say represents a direct threat to the fiscus as the debt bears government guarantees.
"The market expects the parastatal debt to continue rising under the current hostile economic environment and I estimate it to range between $60-$65 billion," said economic consultant John Robertson. Of the amount, over 60% is foreign.
Analysts said they were concerned that most loans raised by public enterprises were guaranteed by the state. Default results in creditors calling up the guarantees, putting pressure on the budget.
The analysts called for a speedy and transparent privatisation of beleaguered parastatals. This would not only stop the drain
on fiscal resources but enhance revenue generation through efficient economic activity and improved management as bureaucracy was replaced by keener and responsive market practices.
"Privatisation will also provide an effective means for redeeming parastatal debt," an economist said.
"Sale of government assets can also be used as a vehicle to mobilise foreign exchange."
Realising that the parastatals debt was exerting pressure on the budget, Finance minister Simba Makoni in his 2002 budget proposed to raise about $40 billion from the sale of assets.
"Of this amount, a minimum amount equivalent to US$150 million will be in foreign currency," Makoni said.
The country is currently facing a severe foreign currency shortage that has disrupted the normal functioning of the economy. The shortage has squeezed both importers and exporters.
Importers are forced to surrender to government 25% of their foreign currency receipts for payments of Noczim and Zesa debts leaving them with little for their own requirements.
The Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe has put in place regulations meant to crush the parallel market, forcing importers to queue for foreign currency from authorised dealers who have reported perennial shortages.
Market analysts said the privatisation of parastatals would also broaden the scope for foreign direct investment, crucial if the country were to capture the much-needed hard currency.
"For government to maximise privatisation proceeds, valuation of public assets on offer before disposal is very important.
"Investors' assessments of the overall credibility of the country's economic policies is also of critical importance to any divestiture programme," analysts said.
RBZ rejects bids for Treasury bills
Godfrey Marawanyika
THE Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe (RBZ) has rejected a number of bids for Treasury bills it has floated. Market analysts attribute the decline in offers to low yields offered by the bank.
From November 29 all the Treasury bills put on the market found no takers. Analysts predict that the central bank would eventually take up the offers.
On November 29 the RBZ floated Treasury bills worth $1,5 billion but found no takers.
A week later on December 6, another Treasury bill was floated but
found no buyers.
"The Reserve Bank rejected all the bids submitted for the $5 billion that it had sought to raise," said one analyst.
"Bids submitted for another $3 billion tender for the Treasury bills on Friday, December 7 were also rejected by the Reserve Bank," he said.
Analysts said the discount rate for the bids were all too high. As a result the government rejected all the bids.
"The average discount rate for the bids submitted was too high for government's comfort, hence the rejection," said one analyst.
Currently the discount rate for 90-day Treasury bills is 23,2% whilst the bidders want to have the rates between 25% and 40%.
Analysts however predict that if the government fails to raise money on the money market in pursuit of its low interest rates policy, it would be forced to resort to seignorage to finance the budget deficit.
As part of its debt restructuring the government has proposed that at least 30% becomes medium to long-term and not more than 70% short-term debt.
As at end of June 2001, the share of short-term debt declined to 72% compared to 94% at the end of last year.
The country experienced average daily shortages of $5,1 billion compared to daily average shortages of $1,4 billion the previous week.
"On December 5 Treasury bills amounting to $5,52 billion matured in a week in which all bids were rejected.
"Hence substantial liquidity was injected into the market," said Witness Chinyama, group economist with Kingdom Financial Holdings.
"We will wait and see whether the government will succeed in raising the $1 billion it intends to raise in 91-day Treasury bills next week." Another analyst said the Reserve Bank had no option but to take up the bids in the long-term.
"Government does not have the money it desperately needs to finance various activities like the presidential election," he said.
IMF to review Zim's debt and membership
Godfrey Marawanyika
THE International Monetary Fund board is expected to review Zimbabwe's debt and its participation as a member of the Bretton Woods institutions before the end of the year.
However, commentators said it was "very doubtful" the IMF would reverse its position on Zimbabwe considering the passing by the United States of the Zimbabwe Democracy and Economic Recovery Bill last week.
Economic consultant John Robertson said the probability of the country receiving any reprieve from the IMF was very slim considering that the Bill was passed last week.
"Americans sit on the IMF board so the probability of approving any form of aid is very minimal," Robertson said.
Zimbabwe effectively severed all ties with the IMF when it abandoned the institution's economic recovery programme.
The abandoning of the policy programme led to Zimbabwe being starved of international support since 1999 when donors suspended loans to the southern African country.
In August the IMF completed its consultation on Article IV under which it assesses on a regular basis member countries' economic status, the budget, monetary and exchange rate policies. It also meets different interest groups in the country.
The European Union has already indicated that it is not going to disburse its 300 million Euro worth of aid.
Dr Rogers Dhliwayo, a University of Zimbabwe economics lecturer, said the country would not benefit from the Washington-led economic recovery programmes.
"We are ineligible for any form of funding, that is what the Bill says. There will not be any funding for Zimbabwe," said Dhliwayo.
He said though still to be signed, the Zimbabwe Democracy Bill ruled against any form of funding from international donors and "it was highly unlikely any organisation would go against it".
By end of August, Harare's overdue obligations for Special Drawing Rights amounted to $42,3 billion (US$53 million), including US$24 million to the IMF's general department and another US$29 million to the Poverty Reduction and Growth facility.
Since September 1999, the suspension of aid by the IMF has resulted in a flight of investors, donors and loss of funding for Harare amounting to over $27 billion.
Starved of international support, Zimbabwe's economy has been in free-fall, with foreign exchange levels being critical and inflation reaching a new high of 97,9% in October.
Zimbabwe is now classified as a pariah state politically.
If Zimbabwe is suspended it would join the league of the Democratic Republic of Congo and Sudan who were suspended for failing to settle their arrears.
Zimbabwe's is indebted to the World Bank to the tune of US$41,9 million, the Africa Development Bank US$92,3 million and the European Investment Bank US$14,7 million.
CZI stresses need for orderly land reform
Godfrey Marawanyika
THE Confederation of Zimbabwe Industries (CZI), the country's industrial representative body, this week reiterated the need for an orderly land reform programme that should be carried out in a manner that is sustainable, restores business confidence and makes the country a worthy investment destination.
In their submissions to the Southern Africa Development Community (Sadc) inter-ministerial task force this week, the CZI said the Abuja agreement speeded up the process by which farms outside the resettlement area would be delisted.
"Our expectations were that farms in the agro-industrial sector would be delisted," CZI said.
"However, in some instances it has been most frustrating to note that there has been no change to the extent of negatively impacting on future viability. Timber plantations in particular continue to be occupied. Wood and wood processed products form a major component of the export market."
Despite claims by the government that during the land reform process coffee, tea, timber estates and church properties would not be designated, some of the country's prime estates have been acquired.
CZI said their interpretation of the Abuja agreement was that the agreement offered the government an opportunity to enforce the current constitution in carrying out land reforms.
"The Abuja Accord offers an opportunity for government to engage the international community as partners to guarantee resources for a successful land reform programme," CZI said.
"The Accord is also a chance for all parties to implement an orderly and sustainable land reform."
The industrial body said although the Ministry of Agriculture was allocated $11,7 billion to enhance agricultural development in support of ongoing agrarian reform, which includes $6,6 billion for the agricultural inputs scheme, it felt that this was insufficient.
"The allocation was part of a $28,8 billion capital expenditure allocation. We are of the view that the capital expenditure is inadequate. With the land redistribution programme being implemented there will be a great need for the development of infrastructure in areas where people will be resettled," CZI said.
The government's allocation of $11,7 billion comes against a backdrop of a projected 6,9% deceleration in growth in the agricultural sector.
CZI said due to the continued disruptions on the farms, the output for the upcoming year has potential to decline. The government therefore needs to demonstrate political will to see that the agricultural sector, which is linked to other sectors, remains viable.
Ariston Holdings records $393m profit
Forward Maisokwadzo
AGRO-processing giant Ariston Holdings has reported a 269% pre-tax profit to $393 million for the year ending September 30 compared with the previous period.
Earnings per share, after current and deferred taxation, was $1,52. Turnover for the period under review was up 92% to $1,52 billion.
The group produced a sterling performance under the current turbulent economic environment where production in the agricultural sector was disrupted because of government's pursuit of the controversial land reform programme.
According to the statement accompanying the group's results, tea production for the period under review increased to a record 4 542 tonnes, up 11% from last year.
The group exported about 19 million stems and said coffee volumes continued to grow, with the seasonal harvest amounting to 355 tonnes.
It said pome and fruit output at 1 788 tonnes was the highest in four years as a result of the orchard rehabilitation programme. The group attributed its impressive performance to an aggressive development programme such as the new Export Processing Zone tea factory which is nearing completion and is expected to become operational next year.
The thrust of the project was the development of small-scale out-growers in neighbouring communal areas. Its export drive also increased the group's earnings and the group aims to continue exploring its export opportunities.
Price increases feed high inflation
Barnabas Thondhlana
ANNUAL inflation has accelerated sharply from 86,3% in September to 97,9% in October 2001. This significant increase is underpinned by a surge in non-food prices, from 90,4% to 106%. Annual food prices have also risen from 81% to 84%.
Of major concern is the rapid drift to hyperinflation against the background of deepening recession and weakening domestic demand. Incessant inflation has, therefore, undermined the prospects for real growth.
Excessive growth in money supply - unrelated to underlying economic activity - has been the major cause of this high inflation. In addition, shortages of foreign currency and the large premiums on the parallel market have given further impetus to inflation. Current price structures reflect the effects of a widening parallel foreign exchange market - a phenomenon most discernible over the last couple of months.
For a start, the impact of inflation is most significant as it erodes export competitiveness of producers.
High inflation is a reflection of the high prices of raw materials and costs to produce, compared to regional and international trading partners.
According to Witness Chinyama, Kingdom Financial Holdings' group economist, there is need to devalue the dollar in line with the increasing rate of inflation.
"When inflation is rising, the exchange rate must move in tandem," Chinyama said. "Zimbabwe's inflation rate is rising but that in trading partner countries is not. This results in exports becoming expensive in the markets and losing demand."
This erodes industry's export competitiveness as the monetary authorities are adamant on devaluing the dollar. Studies have shown that the Zimbabwe dollar is overvalued by as much as 163% and should be at $150 to the greenback, nearly three times its pegged rate of 55.
The RBZ in August last year introduced a purchasing power parity concept where the local currency in a year's time would depreciate by the inflation differential. This failed to achieve the desired results because of a lack of confidence in industry and government's reluctance to stick to its regulations.
As industry is squeezed in regional and international markets, it turns to the local market which, however, is facing dwindling demand.
The high inflation rate has eroded the purchasing power of the population resulting in a decline in total demand and a slowdown in business. Companies have resorted to downsizing or closing operations altogether and unemployment has increased - a chain reaction.
Reducing inflation cannot be viewed in isolation. Ways and means that have the capacity to resolve inflation problems necessarily have to be such that they address the other ills of the economy.
This has to be a package and has to incorporate a number of scenarios.
There is need for a stable macro-economic environment facilitated from various sources. The relevant authorities have to deal with the parallel foreign currency market effectively as it is contributing to high inflation through high costs for companies with a foreign content on materials.
Inflation has also come from supply side bottlenecks. The fall in investment, machinery breakdowns and shortages of spare parts have constrained the economy's capacity to produce goods and services and has thus further fuelled inflation. In addition, entrenched expectations about inflation discretionary pricing and implicit price indexation have added to the inflation spiral.
Government must try to restore relations with multi-lateral organisations like the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. Few countries in the world have excess foreign currency. Their deficit is cushioned by assistance from the IMF and the World Bank.
For Zimbabwe, countries like Japan and China are still extending aid but it is not as pronounced as it was in the past. This has a direct impact on foreign direct investment.
The present interest rate environment must be pursued for a little longer. The cost of borrowing has to remain cushioned for companies but, unfortunately, on the other hand, we have negative real rates of return. The upside however is that if inflation is addressed the rates of return will also be addressed.
One of the major challenges facing the country now is how to bring down inflation to levels promotive of real economic growth. A successful disinflation programme would, however, have to encompass economy-wide measures that address both the demand and supply casual factors of inflation. Reduction of money supply growth and growing output remain critical anti-inflation components.
Dampening inflation expectations is another important pillar in the disinflation process. This, however, can only be achieved if economic agents take a steadfast and resolute but coherent course of action.
There is scope for reducing inflation and reversing economic decline in the medium to long term, however, contingent upon the implementation of an economy-wide stabilisation programme. Measures already put in place to stimulate economic growth and increase the supply of foreign exchange represent a significant step in the fight against inflation.
Radar warns of uncertain economic future
Barnabas Thondhlana
RADAR Holdings, one of the country's leading conglomerates, has warned of an uncertain future if the prevailing economic and political landscape does not drastically change.
Chairman Chris Schofield, in a statement to shareholders in Radar's annual report, said the group was busier than ever, but was "busy going out of business".
"It will require a sea of change in government's implemented policy, as against the endless gibberish we are currently subjected to, if the forecast contained in the opening paragraph is not to be borne out," Schofield said.
Group financial director Brett South said whilst the group tried to keep pace with inflation, the various volume declines experienced were of concern.
"It is pertinent to note that despite the influences of mix and volumes, no operating unit achieved a net price increase either equal to, or above, inflation level," South said.
Turnover for the group was $2,7 billion, compared to the $1,3 billion achieved last year. Income before interest was $437,5 million (2000: $108m). Net interest payable was $253,8 million (2000: $174m).
Radar Holdings is the holding company of a diversified portfolio which includes, Macdonald Bricks, Commercial and Industrial Holdings (CIH), Border Timbers, UBM and Radar Metal Industries, among others.
Only a total sceptic was entitled to the view that with no foreign direct investment, widespread retrenchments and collapsing services, government's approach to "job creation" was to let loose hordes of jobless/landless to careen over the countryside. Schofield said regardless of one's viewpoint, the reality was that "the programme" had cost the country countless billions and acquired the cachet of a "pariah state".
"Midst the on-going mayhem, group management set about doing what we said we would, concentrate on asset management and the expansion (or at worst retention) of our export markets. Some headway has been made in reducing the group's borrowings. Certainly the lower interest rates that applied in the last six months of our financial year made an appreciable, positive difference," he said.
"This position may well change in the not-too-distant future, though for all the wrong reasons. "The same commentators who are calling for a 'realistic' interest rate, ignore the very basis of supply and demand.
"As of now, with M3 simply rocketing, the country is awash with Zimbabwe dollars. The Victoria Falls may well be one of the Seven Wonders of the world, but with the deluge of printed notes flowing from Fidelity Printers, that concern may well be added to the list," he said.
"Cut the velocity of money supply and market forces will, of themselves, quickly adjust as borrowers are forced to cherchez la cash."
Schofield said a major negative difference impacting upon the group had been firstly the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe diktat that 25% of forex earned (the percentage has now been increased to 40%) be surrendered. Secondly, that the official, farcical rate of conversion be held at 55:1 to the US dollar. "The latter is obviously of greater import."
Giving an example of operations at Border Timbers, he said had the Zimbabwe dollar/US dollar traded in correlation with the Consumer Price Index, then total export proceeds would have amounted to $1 385 million as against the reported export earnings of $1 026 million - resulting in proceeds being $358 million lower than could have been achieved.
In two years, Border Timbers had been deprived of some $550 million in potential earnings.
"If one extrapolates those numbers to total national export proceeds, then it is not very difficult to determine why we are living on borrowed time in every sense of the word," he said.
CIH managing director Michael Rowland said in the same report that political uncertainty continued unabated with the ongoing detrimental impact affecting group operations and the country as a whole.
"The urgency to effectively implement full regional operational capability is even more pronounced to reduce the dependence on the withering local market and to simultaneously generate foreign currency," Rowland said. "The effect of central bank regulations in respect of exchange rates and the handling of export proceeds is self-destructive and, if not addressed in the short-term, will be the death knell of those locally-based operations that are unable to generate their own currency needs via export sales.
"Unless government face up to the reality of the problems facing the country and then actively and urgently address them in accordance with accepted international norms, the future of the group will be at best, mediocre."
Econet Wireless appoints international board
ECONET Wireless Ltd (EWL), the South African-based investment arm of the Econet Wireless Group, has appointed four international directors to its board.
The Zimbabwe Stock Exchange-listed Econet Wireless Holdings (EWH) is preparing to release its highly-anticipated shareholder circular which details the extent of EWL's international operations ahead of a group restructuring which will see EWH owning a significant stake in the international company.
The Econet Wireless Group now has operations across seven countries in its core business areas of fixed line telephony, mobile communications, satellite communications and Internet services.
Investor relations executive Kevin Kachidza said in a statement that four new members of the EWL board were Dr James Myers, a distinguished telecommunications expert and former vice-president of SBC Communications, one of the world's largest telecommunications companies; Dr John Temple, the founder of the Plessey Group; Dr Judith O'Neill, one of the world's leading telecommunications lawyers; and Peter Campbell, a prominent Scotland-based accountant.
Kachidza said three members of EWH Zimbabwe board had also been appointed to the international board: Professor Norman Nyazema, the chairman of Econet Wireless Holdings, Nigel Chanakira, the group chief executive officer of Kingdom Financial Holdings, and Tawanda Nyambirai, the chief executive of TN Financial Services.
In addition, Econet management would be represented on the board by Strive Masiyiwa, the group chief executive officer, and Finn Tolpinrud, a Norwegian engineer who heads the group's satellite communications business.
"We have chosen a board of some of the most respected practitioners in the industry in order to satisfy the high standards which are expected of us when the company ultimately lists on an international stock exchange," Kachidza said.
He said that the EWH shareholder circular relating to the
international company had now been completed and would be made available to shareholders as soon as relevant approvals had been secured.
Explaining the delay Kachidza said: "The shareholder circular contains over 50 pages of information and as such is as extensive and detailed as an information memorandum. While we would have preferred to release the circular sooner, we were obliged to prepare it to the very highest standards and every statement contained in the document had to be verified by international lawyers and auditors.
"Econet Wireless Ltd has businesses that are based in several countries so there was a considerable amount of work involved."
He said that the delay had not prejudiced the shareholders. "Quite to the contrary as in the interim we added new businesses to our portfolio that were not previously included." - Staff Writer.
Forex crunch set to deepen
Forward Maisokwadzo
THE foreign exchange market continues to decline on the back of high import demand by companies in a shortage market.
Analysts said import-dependent companies would continue to face difficulties in sustaining operations.
Importers have to queue for foreign currency allocation from authorised dealers who have reported perennial shortages.
According to Stanbic Bank's latest monthly economic report, total foreign currency inflows declined from the June-October levels of around US$470 million to under US$30 million last month.
Zimbabwe's stock of domestic debt currently stands at $190 million and instead of seeking to prevent this from increasing further, the authorities continue to borrow from the market.
The only way to finance this will be forcing savers to lend money to government at rates far below the inflation rate.
The huge inflows for the period June to October was attributed to the tobacco receipts which, however, failed to ease the country's foreign currency woes.
Analysts warned of a further decline in the market, a situation expected to worsen the performance of industry which of late has resorted to sourcing forex from the once-thriving parallel market before government imposed stringent controls to crash the errant market.
The analysts said although industry retreated in fear that total controls would hurt their business further, the official foreign exchange market could not meet the current high demand.
The foreign currency crisis was not only affecting industry but was also worsening the country's foreign arrears, estimated to end the year at around US$2,1 billion.
Realising the intensity of the current foreign exchange shortage, analysts forecast the crisis to continue unabated in the New Year, making 2002 another difficult year.
RBZ calls for transport revamp
THE Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe (RBZ) has called for a well-co-ordinated planning system to ensure that transport sector bottlenecks are removed, particularly in view of national priorities such as the efficient movement of agricultural inputs and fuel.
In its weekly commentary, the central bank said the transport and communication sector made a significant contribution to economic activity, through inter-linkages with other sectors. Timely distribution of goods and services, as well as the smooth movement of exports, was dependent on efficient transport services.
For a heavily import-dependent economy, domestic companies also depended on the transport sub-sector for timely delivery of raw materials, machinery and equipment as well as other essential imported inputs.
"The sector's contribution to gross domestic product (GDP) had risen steadily from 5,5% in 1990 to 7,9% in 2000, reflecting its potential to steer the economy towards sustainable growth," the RBZ said.
"A major boost for the sector has been from the deregulation of both the transport and telecommunications sub-sectors, as part of major macroeconomic reforms undertaken by government since 1991. This saw the entrance of new players in the provision of transport services particularly in urban areas."
The sector has, however, been in decline over the past three years, against the background of widening foreign exchange shortages. Growth fell from a peak of 23,4% in 1995 to a decline of 13% in 2000.
Present trends indicate a further decline of 3,5% in 2001.
"Foreign exchange shortages, particularly after 1997 adversely affected the availability of motor vehicles, spare parts and kits. The gravity of the foreign exchange problem has been underlined by low capacity utilisation at key players such as Willowvale Motor Industries, Dahmer, Quest Motors and others," the bank said.
"In addition, erratic fuel supplies have also hampered the smooth movement of exports and internal distribution of goods and services.
"Notwithstanding the current adversities, the transport sector has shown considerable resilience," the bank said. - Staff Writer.
IMM prepares for Marketer of the Year Award 2002
THE Institute of Marketing Management Zimbabwe has begun preparations for the second Marketer of the Year (Moty) award to be hosted in Harare next year.
The prestigious award, initiated by the IMM and first held last year, gives acknowledgement and recognition to companies judged to have achieved marketing excellence in Zimbabwe.
To this end, IMM Zimbabwe is encouraging organisations that can demonstrate successful marketing strategies to come forward and seek professional recognition by entering the Marketer of the Year Award 2002.
Last year's winner, Lynne Mortimer of NMB Bank, achieved recognition for the successful marketing campaign that helped the bank to become the largest merchant bank in Zimbabwe, quoted on both the Zimbabwe and London stock exchanges, and saw it launch its new but fast-growing retail banking services.
While many organisations may feel a strong temptation to despair about the current political and economic crisis, it is in fact during these times that the marketing function becomes more important than ever. In order to prosper - not just survive - organisations need to be proactive and re-examine their strategies on an on-going basis in order to meet the ever-changing environment.
This strategy will be judged according to the following major criteria.
l Marketing challenge: an understanding of the market for the product or service (including any market research or other forms of market analysis).
l Objectives and strategy: detailing the objectives and strategy used in the marketing campaign and defining strategic goals in a measurable, quantitative form.
l Implementation/action Plan/tactics: demonstrating the execution of the strategy - how the objectives were translated into action and what methods were employed.
l Results and evaluation: detailing the effectiveness of the campaign, including its impact on sales/profits and market share.
The IMM receives sponsorship from local companies towards the hosting of the Moty award event. In return, sponsors receive valuable exposure, as well as being associated with the most important and prestigious marketing event on the Zimbabwe calendar.
In the forthcoming award, sponsorship will not preclude organisations from catering the competition, as the mechanism in place - and demonstrated at the inaugural award for judging the entrants - are both confidential and transparent, being overseen by a firm of professional auditors.
It is intended that the call for entries will be issued towards the end of January in order to allow entrants time to include financial information for the period up to December 31 2001 in their entries. - Staff Writer
Tripartite agreement on violence collapses
Godfrey Marawanyika
A TRIPARTITE agreement between government, labour and employers on controlling violence and desisting from making inflamatory statements likely to lead to violence has collapsed.
The Zimbabwe of Congress of Trade Unions (ZCTU) and the Employers Confederation of Zimbabwe (Emcoz) had managed to convince the government to control widespread lawlessness in the country and avoid making inflammatory statements at a November 8 meeting of the three in Kadoma.
According to the declaration - Towards a Shared National Economic and Social Vision -- a copy of which is with the Zimbabwe Independent, as part of the solutions and the way forward, the parties agreed in principle that no selective application of the law should be applied to those who advocated for violence.
The agreement collapsed after government tried to sneak in an additional clause in which it wanted the three parties to admit they were also to blame for the current economic problems.
In the aborted agreement the social partners noted that of major importance to the country's risk factor was the lack of political tolerance, election-related violence and use of inflammatory language and political demonisation of the players.
"The government must apply the law on those who incite and perpetuate violence," reads the document.
"Government must urge all political party leaders to speak against violence and not to demonise each other."
During their assessment of the country's risk factor, labour and business lambasted the government over the absence of the rule of law and inconsistent policies.
Business and labour agreed to depoliticise the work place on condition government adopted the same stance. Government was also asked to ensure free and fair elections.
The social partners said Zimbabwe had become an international pariah state and the causes of the high risk factors included lack of political tolerance, lack of reciprocity to positive government policy initiative, failure by some government institutions to function effectively and mismatches between policy and action. The partners had also agreed that the government should ensure the application of the rule of law and depoliticise institutions of governance and work places. The selling of political party cards in government offices was to also stop.
The government was tasked with ensuring that civil servants did not wear party regalia or collect money for political parties at work places. Business and labour agreed they would abide by the same principles.
The Kadoma declaration is not different from one arrived at in January, the Declaration of Intent, which would have paved the way for the establishment of a social contract. Government also reneged on that deal.
"The government must commit itself to implementing its own policies and business must commit to helping government implement national and agreed policies and labour must support the implementation of national agreed policies," said the partners in the Declaration.
The partners said there was need for non-contradictory statements among the social partners, and that government must enunciate clear consistent policies and apply them uniformly. Business and labour were tasked to speak with one voice.
The partners agreed in principle that the impact of the country risk factor prolonged the current economic depression, led to capital flight and the drying up of credit lines.
No foreign takers for demerged Zimsun
Stanley James
NO foreign entities have expressed interest in taking equity in the demerged Zimbabwe Sun Ltd (Zimsun) because of the economic instability prevailing within the country.
Speculation has been rife that South Africa's leading hoteliers were keen to acquire a stake in Zimbabwe's leading hospitality concern, and that negotiations were at an advanced stage. Sources this week said Zimsun's management were in South Africa on a follow-up mission to seal the deal after a team of South African hoteliers had been in the country last week on a scouting mission.
Zimsun managing director John Smith this week said the macro-economic environment had made it difficult for foreign firms with any interest in the company.
"No foreign buyers have shown interest in taking a stake in our company; there are many reasons for such a stance and in fact of major concern has been the current adverse economic environment," Smith said.
He said Zimsun had shelved plans for a rights issue to raise over $1,2 billion the hotel group is loaded with as debt.
"To this extent it then becomes impossible for foreign parties to buy a stake," he said in a telephone interview from South Africa, where he said he is on holiday.
Delta Corporation Ltd, parent company of Zimsun is demerging its subsidiaries within the hotel, retail and service sectors of the economy in order to concentrate on its core beverage business.
Smith said local companies and industrial conglomerates had shown interest in getting part of the stake within the group but later withdrew after Zimsun advised on the suspension of the rights issue exercise.
Smith said the demerging programme had progressed well to the extent that operations had not been adversely impacted on.
The hospitality group director said shareholders of the entire company would in about two or three weeks hold an extraordinary meeting to review the demerging exercise as well as the position of the company when the entire exercise is completed.
"Shareholders of Zimsun shall meet before the end of this year to chart the way forward following the recent demerging policy," he said.
"It is through such a meeting that most of the critical issues regarding the shareholding structure will come under the spotlight."
He said management of the company had taken a leading role in as far as the entire future of the company was concerned.
The shareholding structure of Zimsun, said Smith, shall be in the hands of management which is poised to acquire the majority of the equity.
Tel*One restructures operations
Stanley James
TEL*ONE (Pvt) Ltd plans to restructure its operations to ensure it is responsive to changes in the economic environment.
The initiative comes in the light of the company's turnaround programme which resulted in the group posting a net profit of $348 million during the financial year ended June 30 2001 compared to a loss of $2 billion incurred during the same period last year.
In a report accompanying the group's unaudited financial results, Tel*One chairman Caxington Muzangaza said efforts to maintain consistency were being made.
"The structure of the company is being looked into to align it with the strategies and objectives of the company. The structure that is being developed will ensure that the company is responsive to changes in the economic environment," said Muzangaza.
According to the results, financing costs, made up of interest paid and exchange losses, were $572 million against $2,4 billion in the year 2000.
Expenses for the company during the period under review were affected by inflation which averaged 70%.
Turnover surged by 99% over the same period last year, but was below the target for the six months.
"The downward trend in the economy impacted negatively on telephone utilisation as there were a number of company closures and retrechments which affected the customer base," the chairman said.
"Some corporate and individual subscribers were implementing cost- control measures in order to survive the harsh economic environment; the cost of sales and administration went up by 28%."
The group's net working capital situation reflected that its current liabilities were $5 billion.
Muzangaza said the group was failing to pay its creditors because of the serious shortage of foreign currency.
He said management had set aside funds in a loan redemption fund to be used to pay off loan arrears once hard currency reserves were made available.
The company is heavily geared with a total loan of $12 billion, the bulk of which is foreign currency denominated.
Muzangaza warned that in light of such a situation, Tel*One risked incurring huge exchange losses on depreciation of the local currency.
Strategies were being worked out to ensure the company repositioned itself as a customer-focused entity that addressed customer expectations.
Tel*One is expected to perform below budget in both turnover and net income as a result of the negative economic fundamentals which have affected the demand for telephony services and the completion of certain projects to provide new products and value-added services.
A restructuring exercise aimed at scrutinising all processes to ensure only those that added value and improved on efficiency were maintained is already in progress.
Interest rates firm as RBZ increases rates
Forward Maisokwadzo
MONEY market interest rates are firming as the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe (RBZ) gradually increases rates to close the gap on run-away inflation.
The move by the central bank to increase rates comes on the back of several rejections of its bids by investors striving to keep their heads above water in the face of high inflation and the continued expansionary stance on the monetary front which is fostering highly negative real returns to savings.
The effective 91-day treasury bill rate rose from 19,34%, at the beginning of November, to 24,25% at end of the month.
"By maintaining low rates government is destroying the saving base of the whole economy," said economic consultant John Robertson.
He said the negative real interest rate policy was resulting in investors being reluctant to lend money to the embattled government whose appetite for funds was increasing as donors and international financiers have deserted the country due to differences in economic recovery policies.
Economist Witness Chinyama said government realised that low interest rates were unsustainable and was gradually increasing rates in line with its debt restructuring programme.
"Although government takes cognisance of the market's needs, it also has to keep in touch with its debt restructuring plan," said Chinyama.
Analysts said although rates were firming, ranging from between 27% to 30%, it was far below the inflation rate currently sitting at 97,9% according to the October figures. Money market investors expected the rates to be in tandem with inflation.
"If inflation goes up, rates should also go up as well for investors to get positive returns from their investments," said Robertson.
He said by cutting interest rates, government was making huge savings at the expense of investors like pension funds whose investments realised negative returns.
Notwithstanding the prevailing high inflation environment, government implemented targeted reduction in interests rates to provide relief to the productive and export sectors.
However, the impact of this measure, coupled with restructuring of domestic debt, saw money market rates fall to between 5 and 18% this year. Lending and deposit rates declined to ranges of 15-40%, and 0-15% respectively.
Analysts warned that although the policy provided relief for borrowing for production and investment, it had also financed unproductive consumption and speculative behaviour.
They said the low interest rate regime compromised the capacity
of insurance companies and pension funds to generate returns on investment that adequately compensated policy holders and pensioners for the loss of purchasing power.
Seed Co gains from high seed demand
PROSPECTS for a successful year for Seed Co are good as the main seed selling season draws to a close.
Seed Co group financial director Morgan Nzwere said as at the end of November, maize seed sales were 15% above budgeted figures.
"With the recent good rains that have fallen in most parts of the country, we are experiencing high demand for more seed," Nzwere said.
"This has resulted from very strong demand from the smallholder sector and is coupled with the current government inputs programme which should see us recording seed maize sales significantly above budgeted figures."
Nzwere said the regional market also looks promising.
Seed Co has always held the dominant position in the seed maize market. Since 1997, when the company embarked on a growth strategy of increasing its activities in the sub-Saharan Africa region it now exports to most regional countries.
"We are well on course to achieving our medium-term goal of having 50% of our total annual turnover coming from regional sales.We continue to pursue further areas of growth especially in east and north Africa," he said.
In the last financial year to February 2001, regional turnover increased by 74%, and contributed 36,6% to total annual turnover.
Apart from the good maize seed sales this season, Nzwere also said soyabean had generated a lot of interest among both small-scale and large-scale farmers, along with sorghum, groundnuts, beans and cowpeas.
"Despite the disruptions in the farming industry, good seed sales augur well for extensive planting of the maize crop, which, if adequate fertilization, management and rainfall are received, food security will improve significantly," he said. - Staff Writer.
Bank cheque use on the decline
Forward Maisokwadzo
THE use of bank cheques is set to decline on the back of high inflation that has turned the country into a cash economy, one of the country's leading commercial banks has said.
Stanbic Bank Zimbabwe said as competition in the banking sector intensifies, the attendant financial innovation culminates in revolutionary payment channels that are set to reduce the role of cheques in the economy.
"Adoption of improved settlement systems in the country's banking sector will also have an impact of relegating usage of cheques," the bank said.
The current hyper-inflationary environment has resulted in most individuals and corporates holding more currency to meet their day-to-day requirements.
However, the bank noted the perverse effect of inflation was that it
impoverished the masses and resulted in increases in fraudulent behaviour.
Sellers are demanding payment in cash for most transactions.
Stanbic said with regard to cheque transaction volumes, the low growth reflects reduced economic activity in virtually all key productive sectors, as well as the substitution effect of other alternative means of payment, such as, inter alia, cash, debit cards, credit cards and telephonic and automated teller machine (ATM) direct transfers.
In Zimbabwe, Metropolitan Bank was the pioneer of telephonic banking which was well received by the market. Trust Banking Corporation also recently introduced a user-friendly, state-of-the-art self service machine.
However, latest figures from the Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe showed that although the use of cheques was set to decline, the volume of transactions the banking system was handling through cheques has remained largely unchanged, growing by only 3% over the 12 months to November 2001.

Original_Search_Title: Where the Rain God Reigns
Headline: Uganda: Where the Rain God Reigns
Body_Text:
Kuno, the god brought drought upon Telekatajula village as punishment for stealing a piece of rock
AS cluster of white clouds ply beneath the evening sun, village children play and dart up and about under its dark shadows. They splash and spill the rainwater stored in a series of troughs curved into the rock pad at Telekatajula in Nago-ngera County, Tororo district.
A herdsman speaking the language of his cattle lashes at them for insisting on having a longer drink from the wells. These temporary rock water wells and streams are a lingering reminder of the now overly welcomed torrential rains. But why a herdsman will mercilessly lash at his cattle is beyond my understanding.
My remote presence sends tens of colourful lizards carelessly scurrying off into the bush. A forest of trees and overgrown vegetation encase the rock plain in a spiritual metaphor.
It is a sacred place where the removal of a rock spec is an abomination that will bring drought upon Katajula village and make a woman wander off to certain holy places and will cause untold mischief.
Such is the temple of the Japadhola god of the rain, Kuno.
A thud from one side of the rock plain faintly echoes through the empty space. Justine Nyakecho, a housewife, crashes cassava specs using a stone, against a bare rock plate. She collects the pieces using a broom and neatly spreads them out in the sun to dry.
Unlike the lizards and the children, she is not bothered by my presence. Through the cattle, I trundle and move up to her. She pounds one more time, looks up at me, grins politely. "in'tye nedi?" ("How are you doing?") she asks in Adhola. Apparently, my reason for being there was quite transparent to her. "Have you come to visit our god of the rain, Kuno?" She asks while smiling, trying to indulge me in a conversation.
"Many strange things have happened here," she says in a tone that speaks volumes about the reverence of Telekatajula in Katajula village where Kuno lives.
Fresh in her mind, Nyakecho recalls sadly the consequences of an arrogant village man who excavated part of the rock for his construction work. "While the neighbouring villages were blessed with heavy rains, we were cursed with a drought because of what he had done. We did not get groundnuts, sorghum, millet or any other crops in the first rainy season because the land was barren," she says.
Nyakecho says in an attempt to revert the situation, in the quiet of the night, the man stealthily returned the piece of rock with a sacrificial cock.
While he went about the appeasement rituals all by himself, a cock with a dark red comb, which the Adhola believe is the rainbow, descended upon his head and the world around him was thrown into a sudden crimson glow.
Nyakecho says the man was frightened, became confused and took haste. But since then, his home and world stayed the same. The rain god rejected his sacrifice and remorse.
Because of the growing community concern, he was sued to the clan god. The clan elders pitched up and part of the rock was returned together with a cock and goat, which were sacrificed.
"It was after the performance of the rituals that we began receiving a lot of rain like the other villages. We planted all the crops we wanted and every family granary is full," Nyakecho says with a sigh of relief.
As we paddled over the rock plain, she suddenly disembarks as I mount a rock moated by a stream of water from the early morning down pour. Instinctively, I stop and look back at her. She speaks while pointing at a spot where I am apparently hovering; "Women are not allowed to go over that place. That is where sacrifices are offered. Only men are allowed to go over it."
She said if a woman dares to walk over it, untold mischief befalls her. For the Adhola living in Katajula, the rainwater that collects around the area is considered to be spiritual and holy. It is communally collected and used for spiritual cleansing and various domestic purposes like bathing and cooking.
For a community whose livelihood has always depended on agriculture, the relationship with the god of the rain is a timeless personal bond. One whose jeopardy the Japadhola of Katajula have always guarded against and have unduly paid allegiance and faith.
In the past, rainmakers who had made it a custom to seasonally sabotage the rain were profusely fished out in a 'witch hunt' by the entire community. They were dragged to Telekatajula where the elders sat in trial and judgement of their guilt or innocence.
On a blazing hot day when the rock at Telekatajula was steaming and cracking in thirst, the guilty rainmakers were hauled, stripped naked and forced to sprawl on the rock. They twitched and groaned and begged and perhaps preferred the guillotine.
The idea behind was for the guilty rainmakers to speak words that appeased the rain god and served as an example to the other rainmakers. Their degree of honesty did not matter at that point. If the village was to be redeemed from the drought, justice had to prevail.
Since the community attributed drought to mischievous activities of rainmakers, they took revenge. They pitch camped at Telekatajula and performed rituals against the rainmakers.
Birds, goats and cows twitched as they were slaughtered for sacrifice. People frantically danced and sang songs of praise in worship of the rain god. Bloody chunks of meat were skewered onto stakes and roasted, and local beer was brewed in plenty. In a wild festive feast, beef was eaten and people drunk like fish. As a custom, none of the offering was to be carried away and no woman was to attend the rituals whatsoever
The response from Kuno was instant. During the heat of the festival, dark clouds came rushing in the sky, lightening struck, thunders groaned and exploded and the rains quenched the thirsty village and its barren gardens, marking yet another agricultural season and increased the Japadhola faith in Kuno.

Original_Search_Title: Beware of the Terminator Technology
Headline: Liberia: Beware of the Terminator Technology
Body_Text:
Do you remember how our parents in rural Liberia obtained rice and other seeds to plant in upcoming farming seasons? Basically, this is how they managed: At the end of each harvest, they would save some of the rice, beans and other seeds to be replanted in the next farming season. And if the present harvest was not enough to allow for any seed saving, they would buy or borrow seeds from those neighbors who had better harvests and saved seeds. This age-old traditional practice of saving farming seeds is not unique to Liberia and other African societies. In fact, according to some estimates, 1.4 billion rural farmers in the developing world depend on saved seeds. About 20% to 30% of all soybean farms in the US Midwest are planted with farmer-saved seeds. Most North American wheat farmers, including almost all of the farmers on the Canadian prairies, rely on farmer-saved seeds. Thus, this is a practice that has been a livelihood strategy for millions of farmers around the world.
Well, if the Mississippi-based seed company, Delta & Pine Land, and other large seed companies, and the US Department of Agriculture (USDA) had their way, then the more than 1.4 billion farmers who depend on this tradition of seed saving would not be able to do so in the near future. In March 1998, Delta & Pine Land and the US Department of Agriculture announced that they had received a US patent on a new biotechnology designed to prevent unauthorized seed saving by farmers. Dubbed as the “Terminator” by the Canadian-based international non-governmental organization, Rural Advancement Foundation International (RAFI), this technology enables a seed company to genetically modify any seeds of any species, including both transgenic and conventionally bred seeds, so that plants that grow from them are automatically sterile. In other words, seeds from such plants could only be consumed, since they could not germinate if a farmer replanted them in future farming seasons.
The primary goal of the developers of this technology is to force large numbers of farmers to continuously buy seeds and food from the commodity markets controlled by the seed companies. Thus, any successful global application of the technology, which prevents farmers from saving seeds, will generate new and increasing markets for these companies in both the North and South. Imagine that all of the farmers who now rely on seed saving were to buy from these companies each and every farming season! This would enormously boost their sales. Also, companies like Monsanto and Delta & Pine Land, which now hold licenses for commercializing Terminator seeds, are hoping that the technology will soon render redundant the job of enforcing their patent rights, especially in the developing world where most farmers depend on saved seeds, and where it has been impossible for them to police their rights. Even in developed countries, like Canada, it has been difficult for these companies to prevent farmers from using patented saved seeds, and they are hoping that the Terminator will solve their enforcement problems in such countries as well. For instance, in 1998 Monsanto sued Saskatchewan farmer Percy Schmeiser, in Canada, for replanting genetically modified seeds on which the company claimed patents. Well, thanks to the Terminator, Monsanto will not have to sue farmers like him, because they will not have the incentive in the first place to replant saved seeds since these seeds will not grow if replanted.
A global use of the Terminator technology will allow the commercial seed industry to enter entirely new sectors of the seed market, especially in seeds such as wheat, rice, cotton, soybeans, oats and sorghum. With the top ten seed companies controlling approximately 40% of the commercial seed market, coupled with the continuing decline in public sector breading, this new technology will accentuate farmers’ vulnerability in the market place and reinforce corporate control over the global seed industry. Since the introduction of the technology, it has been universally condemned as an “immoral technology” by various civil society groups, banned by international public agricultural research institutes, and censured by various United Nations agencies. Despite this widespread opposition, however, the US Department of Agriculture announced in August of this year that it had concluded negotiations to license the technology to Delta & Pine Land.
At present, farmers in Africa, Asia and other developing regions are generally allowed to save and replant rice seeds from previous harvests, because patents are not yet firmly enforced there. However, if the Terminator technology becomes globally used in these areas, then farmers will face increasing seed costs if they have to use the new improved seeds. This may substantially increase food insecurity in many areas of the developing world, including Liberia.
P.O. Box 450493
Atlanta, GA 31145
Website: www.theperspective.org
E-mail: editor@theperspective.org

Original_Search_Title: UNB Plans R46m Offer to Buy Back Its Shares
Headline: South Africa: UNB Plans R46m Offer to Buy Back Its Shares
Body_Text:
UNITED National Breweries (UNB), the former National Sorghum Breweries, announced on Friday plans to launch a R46m buy-back offer to its minority shareholders.
The offer at 100c a share will enable shareholders in the unlisted company to realise their investment. No dividend has been paid since 1995, when the company moved into the red.
Only at the financial year end of December 31 1999, did the company report it had moved into the black, with a profit of R504000. The upward trend in profits was repeated at the end of last year with R18,5m.
UNB president and chief operating officer, Rajan Ranganathan, said that on the basis of advice from the company's investment bankers, Nedcor, the directors had resolved to offer the buy-back to minorities, so providing the large base of small shareholders with an opportunity to realise their investment.
The company would repurchase a total of almost 46-million shares (65,61%) of the share capital from about 9000 shareholders, including the Premier group, Gensec, the Transvaal Municipal Pension Fund and Nedcor.
Ranganathan said that in view of the volatility in the financial markets and the probability that this would continue in the short to medium term a listing on the JSE Securities Exchange was not feasible.
He said that the steady loss of consumer rands to fast-growing industries such as cellphones and the lotto, and the attrition of the industry's consumer base through age and the HIV/AIDS pandemic, had made it exceptionally difficult for the industry to increase profitability significantly.
Ranganathan said Deloitte & Touche, acting as independent advisers to UNB's minority shareholders, had reviewed the terms of the acquisition and considered them fair and reasonable.
The implementation of the planned scheme is subject to court approval.

Original_Search_Title: Masindi Seed Project
Headline: Uganda: Masindi Seed Project
Body_Text:
When asked about their organisation's goal, workers at Masindi Seed Project answer simply -to avail quality seeds to the farming community in good time.
But there is another thing that keeps nagging them, the belief that the project would do better under privatisation.
One official said, "We feel this place could have done better as a private firm." Formerly, a project of the Bunyoro Growers Co-operative Union, Masindi Seed Project received a boost through a British loan in 1971.
This enabled the project to install seed processing machinery. The government took over the project in 1977. Over the years, it has benefited from various organisations like Food and Agricultural Organisation the European Economic Commission and African Development Bank, among others. The last aid package they received was in 1998 from ADB. The project is under the Ministry of Agriculture.
John Muyenzi, the project's administrator said the factory was lucky not have been ripped of its machinery and other assets in the 1979 and 1985 wars. Masindi seed project was recently valued at sh7b. This include it's land, estates and machinery.
Muyenzi said the factory is operating on its own revolving funds that was saved over the years. The project has been producing over 2000 metric tonnes of seeds every year since 1988.
Many of these find their way into Kenya, Rwanda and neighbouring countries. Some of the variety of seeds that the factory produces are: Maize-long I, long IV which matures in 100 days and is very popular with farmers. The long V which is open pollinated, beans, groundnuts, millet-pesse II, sorghum-sekedo, sunflower-sunfora, soya beans Nam I and Nam II which is grown in Kasese. Nam is an acronym for Namulonge and upland rice-abilony. The factory produces both high breed seeds and composite seeds.
There is a regional seed lab in Masindi which tests the seed moisture content, germination and health.
Muyenzi said the international standard for seed germination is 85% but that Masindi Project's Seed germination capacity is at 90%

Original_Search_Title: Mombasa Port Trade Picks Up
Headline: Kenya: Mombasa Port Trade Picks Up
Body_Text:
Large consignments of grain are expected at the port of Mombasa from this weekend, signalling an improvement in port activities as the year comes to an end.
Four vessels carrying more than 45,000 tonnes of grain are expected to dock at the port in the next two weeks. Mv Mok Nan, mv Noble Star, mv Jolly Rubino and mv An Nooru are carrying consignments of rice, wheat, yellow maize, soya and sorghum.
Some of the cargo is relief supplies destined to the neighbouring countries of Sudan and Somalia.
The UN has launched an appeal for its humanitarian activities in Afghanstan and other drought and war ravaged countries in different parts of the world.
In Kenya more than 4.4 million people have been affected by drought and according to statistic released by WFP in March it was having a shortfall of an estimated 249,000 tonnes of assistance worth US 117.65.

Original_Search_Title: Rain Spoils Soft-Drink Sales
Headline: South Africa: Rain Spoils Soft-Drink Sales
Body_Text:
Manufacturers of soft drinks and ice creams are even gloomier than the average rainsoaked resident of Gauteng and KwaZulu-Natal because they are looking at disappointing sales figures for key markets at the beginning of their peak selling seasons.
Alan Clark, MD of Amalgamated Beverage Industries, the biggest bottler of soft drinks in SA, said yesterday that this was the sixth week of almost continuously cloudy weather in Gauteng. Weather in KwaZulu-Natal had also been poor.
The group does not operate in Cape Town, where the weather has generally been hot in recent weeks.
Clark said that year-on-year sales of soft drinks were holding up and the group was maintaining the positive levels it reported at interim stage. In the six months to September it grew sales volumes 3%.
However, sales in the past few weeks could have been a lot better, Clark said. "As soon as there is a slightly sunny day we see sales picking up sharply."
Amalgamated's parent, SA Breweries, which dominates local beer sales and sells cider and alcoholic fruit beverages, was unable to comment yesterday.
But it said with its interim results that beer volumes had shown growth on a like-for-like basis since October 1 and were now ahead of the same period last year.
Sales of traditional beverages are not particularly seasonal "unless it rains very hard", Awethu MD Tony Ford said, in which case sales could fall 5%-10%. But sales had not been affected this year.
Awethu manufactures sorghum beer and mageu and its franchise outlets are mainly in Free State, Gauteng and Northern Province.
Mike Vesey, local MD of UKbased HP Bulmer, best known for its cider, said sales for the company were doing reasonably well, but this could partly be a result of the launch of new products such as Tattoo, a spirit cooler.
Sales of alcoholic beverages were certainly sensitive to the weather and Bulmer was hoping for a warm December.
Ice cream was another weathersensitive product. Marnus Swanepoel, a brand manager at Dairymaid-NestlÃ©, said good weather on Saturdays and Sundays was particularly important. The ice cream season usually started in November.
Swanepoel said he had noticed a decline in ice cream sales in November compared with the corresponding period last year but he did not give numbers.

Original_Search_Title: Father Hormisdas Nsengimana: Accused of Genocide; Sheltered By the Church
Headline: Rwanda: Father Hormisdas Nsengimana: Accused of Genocide; Sheltered By the Church
Body_Text:
London — A priest accused of genocide, Father Hormisdas Nsengimana, is serving
today in the Parish of Diang in the Diocese of Bertoua, Cameroon.
Individuals like Father Hormisdas are extremely difficult to accuse and
even more difficult to bring to justice. People prepared to testify against
them have to be able to stand up against the might of the Catholic Church,
still one of the most powerful institutions in the world. In speaking out
about the crimes of the clergy, survivors and witnesses of the genocide have
to relive the horror of their past amid the pressure of criticism from
voices imbued with moral authority. In a country like Rwanda, where most
people are Catholic, this pressure is even more acute.
African Rights has decided to publish the findings of its research into
the case against Father Hormisdas Nsengimana, despite persistent efforts on
the part of those close to the Church to discredit the organisation and its
sources. We are confident that the conviction in Belgium on 8 June of two
Rwandese nuns, Sister Gertrude Mukangango and Sister Julienne Kizito, whose
involvement in the 1994 genocide was detailed by African Rights in previous
reports, will have given pause for thought. But at present it seems that the
Church is still more likely to hinder than to help to those responsible for
bringing genocide suspects to trial, as is apparent in the case of Father
Athanase Seromba. Although the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda
(ICTR) managed to arrest another priest, Father Emmanuel Rukundo in
Switzerland, tribunal officials attempting to arrest Father Seromba in Italy
have so far been prevented by a wall of judicial bureaucracy, while his
parish authorities have spirited him into seclusion. It may be impossible to
disentangle the extent of the Church's influence in hampering his arrest,
but in a traditionally Catholic country, host to the Vatican, there can be
little doubt that its authority holds some sway. The Church should have used
its influence in a positive way to smooth the path of justice.
In this report, consistent accounts of Father Hormisdas' involvement in
the killings in the Parish of Nyanza in Butare, are given by genocide
survivors, witnesses and former members of Father Hormisdas' staff. There
are also chilling descriptions of the priest's cruelty from genocide
detainees who confirm that he was among the organisers of the
slaughter.
These may help to shine more light through the curtains the Church has
drawn over information linking certain of its clergy to the genocide.
Survivors and witnesses have given examples of Hormisdas' conduct,
during the genocide and before it. Their statements are careful records of
their own pain or of the suffering of other members of their community. It
would be extremely distasteful to try to silence their voices by trumpeting
the claim that the Catholic Church is a victim of a campaign sponsored by
the Rwandese Patriotic Front (RPF) and its allies, as some have done in
response to similar cases.  Tragically it is a fact that suffering and death
was inflicted upon Tutsis in Nyanza in 1994. Father Hormisdas was present
during the killings, and all the evidence suggests he was complicit in the
atrocities. The victims whose plight should be the focus of concern are the
dead, the maimed and the grieving people of Nyanza. For their sake it is
essential that Father Hormisdas be put on trial.
Teaching Hatred
Father Hormisdas was appointed the principal of Christ Roi, a respected
secondary school, in Nyanza, commune Nyabisindu in Butare in 1989. From the
time of his arrival there, both his sermons and his lessons were
divisive.
Many considered him an unlikely priest, because of his armed bodyguards
and his support for the ultra-extremist political faction, the Committee for
the Defence of the Republic (CDR). Tutsis were so wary of him that when the
violence against them began they did not seek sanctuary in the Parish of
Nyanza. This was unheard of at a time when all over the rest of the country
churches were crammed with displaced people. In the words of one witness:
