"The Christians of Nyanza were afraid of seeking refuge at the parish
because they knew Hormisdas would massacre them immediately." Father
Hormisdas had long been an ally of prominent Hutu extremists, including the
sous-prÃ©fet (governor), GaÃ«tan Kayitana, and the commander of the
gendarmerie, Captain Birikunzira. In April 1994, these three began to meet
very regularly and their purpose was soon apparent to the staff and people
living near Christ Roi. They blatantly exploited their positions in the
community to organise the genocide in Nyabisindu and to convince people to
participate. Their programme was launched with a meeting on 21 April,
convened by Fr. Hormisdas, to draw up a list of Tutsi victims. A man who
attended the meeting recalled Hormisdas' speech:
This evening we're quickly going to compile the list of Tutsis to
kill.
The leaders of the country are very angry with us because we still
haven't started the genocide, despite the fact that there are plenty of
Tutsis in Nyanza, especially educated people. I have to give the list that
we're going to compile to the soldiers so that they can start killing very
early in the morning I know that you, the Hutus of Nyanza, aren't used to
killing the Tutsis. Now you must kill. Things have changed.
The following morning the killings began.
A Leader Among the Killers
Christ Roi became a base for the planners of the genocide and the
militia.
The central role played by Fr. Hormisdas is apparent from the nature and
frequency of the meetings that took place there. Joseph Semuhunyege was in
charge of security at the college at night and kept a close eye on the
comings and goings.
After Habyarimana's death, clandestine meetings were held at Christ
Roi.
Hormisdas received a lot of people at his house. There were also
soldiers, natives of Gikongoro, who came to these meetings. These meetings
began at 8:00 p.m.; all the participants left at about 3:00 a.m.
Jean-Claude Higiro worked at the dairy under a man who is accused to
taking part in the genocide, Callixte Mirasano, a member of CDR.
Jean-Claude's father was also a CDR supporter and is imprisoned on charges
of genocide.
As Jean-Claude said: "no one could prevent me from moving around. I saw
everything." He is, therefore, well placed to comment upon the activities of
Fr. Hormisdas and others.
Birikunzira, Kayitana and Fr. Hormisdas organised and supervised the
genocide in Nyanza. They were always in the same car, inspecting the
roadblocks. I saw Kayitana and Birikunzira in Fr. Hormisdas' car. Fr.
Hormisdas was one of the people who set traps for the Tutsis.
Marthe Nyirahabiyambere, who sadly has died since her interview was
recorded, had the courage to put her own son behind bars for the crime of
genocide. Marthe, like Fr. Hormisdas, was a native of Gikongoro, and she
knew the priest's family. She was a Tutsi but had married a Hutu and moved
to Nyanza where she raised her children, seven girls and three boys. Her
daughters were all married to Tutsis and were killed with their families in
the genocide. But one of her sons, Pascal Mutabazi, became a
militiaman.
Pascal was, Marthe said, "the right-hand man of Fr. Hormisdas" in April
and May 1994.
I always forbade my son from participating in the massacres. But he
never listened to me. He considered me to be a traitor and a collaborator of
the Inkotanyi [RPF].
My son did whatever Fr. Hormisdas wanted him to do. He was his
right-hand man. I often saw them together at the home of ThÃ©rÃ¨se, a militia
woman who worked at Nyanza hospital. She also had a bar where the militiamen
met after they had been out killing. During the genocide, Hormisdas was no
longer a priest. He became a killer.
I saw Hormisdas at Gakenyeri with a lot of militiamen like Murindangabo,
an ex-FAR and a native of Bwakira commune in Kibuye, the brother of ThÃ©rÃ¨se;
Augustin Munyarugamba, a cellule responsable; Alphonse Nyiriminega, my
neighbour; and my son, Pascal. They had machetes and massues. As he was the
most educated, Hormisdas was their advisor.
It was Hormisdas who distributed the machetes to the prominent
gÃ©nocidaires. The machetes were stocked at Christ Roi. My son often went to
fetch them from there in order to hand them out to the militiamen in our
district.
Marthe's son came to search her house on several occasions to see if she
was hiding Tutsis. He warned her saying: "If I find a Tutsi here I'll kill
you straightaway. Hormisdas said we should not have pity on any Tutsi."
Around 4:00 a.m. one morning two women and their four children who were
fleeing the militia came to Marthe looking for somewhere to hide. She fed
them and gave them clothes. But before long Pascal returned. He followed
Hormisdas' orders and showed no mercy.
When my son saw them, he shouted and accused me of "hiding Inkotanyi."
He had a machete and a massue. I pleaded with him not to kill them. He beat
me seriously before he pounced on them, killing the women and two of the
children. He continued to maintain his relationship with Hormisdas. This
showed me that the priest was a friend of the gÃ©nocidaires.
According to several witnesses, Fr. Hormisdas was responsible for the
selection of an an elite team of militiamen known as "the dragon" who were
dispersed around Nyabisindu commune to lead the massacres.  Innocent
Habyarimana, a carpenter from Nyanza, commented:
It was the dragon which exterminated all the Tutsi of Nyabisindu
commune.
The dragon had all sorts of weapons. They used the Christ Roi car, the
car of the dairy in Nyabisindu and the vehicle that belonged to
Electrogaz.
After killing, these militiamen would meet at the home of either
Hormisdas or Simon Kalinda [a builder at Christ Roi] to eat and drink.
Janvier Habimana, who is under arrest charged with taking part in the
genocide in Nyabisindu, described the relations between Fr. Hormisdas and
the militiamen who included his own brother, Cyprien, a teacher.
The Tutsis of Nyanza were killed by soldiers and a group of gÃ©nocidaires
that Mirasano, Fr. Hormisdas and the sous-prÃ©fet GaÃ«tan had trained. This
group consisted of people who came from Ruhengeri and Gikongoro who worked
at the Nyabisindu dairy and at Christ Roi.  The head of this team was
Segema, an employee of the dairy. It was a very well-known team of
killers.
I often saw Fr. Hormisdas; GaÃ«tan; Birikunzira and Mirasano driving
together in the same car, around the roadblocks. This team of killers
followed the militiamen; we could see that they were on friendly terms.
This indicated that they were the ones who established the militia.
They were their employers.
The blue Toyota which belonged to Christ Roi was often seen transporting
militiamen on their killing sprees. Under Fr. Hormisdas' control, the
Church's resources were used to support the genocide.  Indeed, the school
buildings themselves were a home to killers.
Christ Roi: A College for the Genocide
A roadblock was established outside Christ Roi and was manned by a group
of extremist students from the college. These students had been displaced by
fighting between the government and the rebel Rwandese Patriotic Army (RPA)
in their home region of Byumba, and had come to Nyanza in 1992. Fr.
Hormisdas had quickly identified them as a receptive audience for his
views and he continued to influence them during the genocide.  Emma-Marie
Nyirazaninka survived the genocide because of the protection she received
from the headmaster of Nyanza primary school, Nyamulinda, who hid her in his
house. It was her ability to brew the sorghum beer known as ikigage that
endeared her to him and to the displaced students from Byumba who were
staying at the school, who told her of their exploits. She learned that they
were acting upon Fr. Hormisdas' orders.
Three of these students were terrible-Cyprien, Mudenge and Beyaka.  They
killed a lot of people. They were like wild animals. Every student had a
massue, but these three also had grenades and guns.  These three students
came in very tired, looking for me to give them sorghum beer. When they
started drinking, they laughed and said to me: "Despite everything that
Hormisdas told us about not having pity for a single Tutsi, we're going to
spare you." One day these students came in with a bucket containing
meat.
They told me: "We've just finished the mission that Hormisdas and the
employees of the dairy gave us yesterday during our meeting at Christ
Roi.
Really, there are no Tutsis left in Nyakabuye and Remera [in Nyanza].
We've combed these areas thoroughly."
Inside Christ Roi was another group of killers that were at the
forefront of the genocide in Nyanza. These were soldiers from the Ecole
SupÃ©rieure Militaire (ESM), who used the college as a barracks, sleeping in
the dormitories. Hutu employees of the college who remained on the premises
noted the links they established with Hormisdas. Callixte Kalisa, a cook at
Christ Roi, saw the soldiers both at the college and outside. He commented:
Hormisdas housed the soldiers who killed the Nyanza Tutsis at Christ
Roi.
He welcomed them to Christ Roi and they ravaged Nyanza, murdering the
Tutsis. They were officers. They blocked all the paths leading to the
college so that it was difficult to go there. These soldiers stayed at the
college during the entire period of the genocide, housed and fed by
Hormisdas. Sometimes I could hear them talking in the evenings or at
mealtimes. They were womanisers. Sometimes they would come to our
neighbourhood and ask us where they could find Tutsi women and girls.
Fear held most Tutsis back from looking to Christ Roi as a possible
shelter. But a few acquaintances of Hormisdas-old family friends, neighbours
and some students from Christ Roi-thought their ties with him might still
have influence and asked him for a hiding place at Christ Roi.
Some of those he denied refuge, in the expectation that they would be
killed, survived to speak of how he delivered their relatives, neighbours
and fellow-refugees to their deaths.
Amurani Mbabariye's family lived behind the college. He and his seven
relatives ran instinctively there on 25 April when they became too fearful
of remaining at home. They were startled to find Fr.  Hormisdas in the
company of an armed militiaman. Nevertheless, Amurani's brother pleaded with
the priest to give them refuge.  Hormisdas' reply was brief. He said:
"You're all Tutsis, I can't hide you." Amurani realised there was no
hope.
I became overwhelmed with fear. I told my family to leave as I saw that
we could be killed. I ran away immediately. After jumping back over the
wall, I heard gunshots. My mother had stayed at home because she was
Hutu. She went to see if it was her children who had died.  When she came
back, she was crying a lot because she had seen their bodies. My brothers,
sisters, nephew and niece had been killed at the college.
Amurani then hid in a forest near Christ Roi. But in May he was almost
discovered by a group that included Hormisdas.
A group of militiamen from Mugonzi came to clear the bush all around the
college. I was lying in the bush; they didn't see me but I saw
everyone.
When the militiamen arrived where I was, I could see Fr. Hormisdas,
FranÃ§ois Gashirabake, Simon Kalinda and the students from Byumba who had
stayed at the school. Hormisdas and FranÃ§ois weren't holding anything in
their hands as they weren't clearing the bush. I saw that they were
supervising the others. Whilst I was waiting to be killed, I saw the
militiamen running and shouting because they had found a young Tutsi boy
hiding in the bush next to me. I didn't know he was there. The boy was
killed. I saw them looking at his body. Luckily, they left before they
reached the place where I was hiding. I stayed in this bush until the end of
the genocide in Nyanza.
Bresile Mukandera, the headmistress of Nyanza's primary school, had come
to know Hormisdas well through teachers' meetings. She sought his help to
hide her eldest daughter, Marie-Claire, who was 14 at the time. When Bresile
reached Hormisdas' office, she saw many cars; she looked through the window
of his office and saw a meeting in progress. The participants included a
number of leading gÃ©nocidaires.
I was afraid to enter, so I sent a gardener in to tell Hormisdas that I
had a message for him. Hormisdas came out to see me. I said that I wanted
him to hide my daughter in his school; I explained that no-one could search
for her there because he was a priest. He shouted and said: "You think that
I, Hormisdas, can hide the child of an Inkotanyi like Munyawera? The Tutsis
killed our President. Because of that I cannot hide any Tutsi. I hope you
have understood. I want to go back to the meeting." I continued to plead,
telling him I was even prepared to pay for anything the child would eat
while at his house.  Hormisdas told me to look elsewhere. Why could he not
hide Tutsis?
A number of witnesses have accused Fr. Hormisdas of delivering Jean
Twagirayezu, the president of the district court in Nyanza, to his death in
early May. Many Nyanza residents mistook Jean for a Hutu, and he had
survived in various hideouts during the first weeks of the killings in
Nyanza. In search of a more secure sanctuary, he decided to ask Hormisdas
for help. Marie-ThÃ©rÃ¨se Mukankusi lived next door to Christ Roi. She was one
of the last people to see Jean Twagirayezu alive and described the events
leading up to his murder.
At the beginning of May, I saw Jean going into Christ Roi. That day I
was just next to the college and Jean passed me by. He asked me if Fr.
Hormisdas was there. I said "yes." He told me that he was being
threatened and that he wanted to ask Hormisdas for a place of refuge.  He
said: "I trust he will be able to take me in because he is a priest." I told
him to go and try.
Jean went into the school. It was about midday. In the evening, I saw
Hormisdas and Jean. But just before that I saw the militiamen Cyprien,
PhÃ©neas and Simon running out of the school. They were going towards the
church.
Jean and Hormisdas passed by just next to me. I heard Fr. Hormisdas
saying: "Go back to your home. Anyone who escapes today will live."  They
arrived at the church and Hormisdas then went back to his home.
Straightaway I heard gunshots. I was told that Jean had been killed.  I
realised that Hormisdas had delivered him to his death.
Complicity in the Murder of Priests and Nuns
From the time of his arrival at Christ Roi, Fr. Hormisdas had made
apparent his dislike of the Tutsi priests at the Parish of Nyanza. He had
repeatedly accused one of them, Fr. Justin Furaha, of supporting the RPF,
eventually forcing him to move to another parish at Save.  During the
genocide, Fr. Hormisdas showed how deep this hostility ran.
Because of the esteem in which he was held by militiamen, Fr.  Hormisdas
had the power to protect the priests in Nyana-Fr. Mathieu Ngirumpatse,
Fr. Innocent Nyangezi, Fr. Callixte Uwitonze and Fr. Jean-Bosco
Yirirwahandi.
But they were all killed. More than that, he is accused of arranging
their deaths and that of Fr. Furaha in Save.
When the priests realised that, as Tutsis, their lives were under
threat, the three younger men decided to leave the parish and hide in an
orphanage in the neighbouring commune of Ntyazo. But Fr. Mathieu was elderly
and ill, and hoped to find sanctuary at Christ Roi.  Workers at Christ Roi
and the parish gave corresponding reports of his decision and its
consequences. Fr.
Mathieu was seen arriving at the college; he left soon afterwards having
been "bluntly" been turned away by Fr. Hormisdas. Later that night, Fr.
Hormisdas and a group of militiamen armed with machetes and nailed clubs
left the college, heading for the parish. The following morning, news of
Fr. Mathieu's murder spread. His corpse was "strewn across the yard" at the
parish, but those who volunteered to bury him were deterred by
Fr. Hormisdas.
Joseph Semuhunyege was on duty as the night watchman at Christ Roi when
Fr. Mathieu arrived at the college.
When Fr. Mathieu realised that he was in danger, he fled to Christ Roi;
he thought that his colleague, Hormisdas, would protect him. He came in the
evening and I was at the college. I went to tell Hormisdas about it. He came
and told Mathieu that he should go back to the parish because the college
did not have more houses than the parish. He turned him away in a blunt
fashion; old Fr. Mathieu returned to the parish where he was killed the same
night.
Joseph's testimony leaves little doubt about Hormisdas' direct
complicity in the murder of Fr. Mathieu.
The group which killed him left the college at about 9:00 p.m. Fr.
Hormisdas was with them. It was a group of militiamen, including the
workers from Gikongoro recruited by Hormisdas. Two of the men had spent the
night in Hormisdas' own room. They were carrying machetes and massues.
Hormisdas left with the group, although he wasn't carrying a
machete.
DÃ©o Ntacyonzaba, who had been employed by the priests for 15 years,
spoke of how he found out the details of the incident and Fr.  Hormisdas'
role in it. DÃ©o had left the parish soon after the priests, preferring to be
at home because of the insecurity. After a week at home, he had received a
visit from a fellow-employee at the parish, John, who was a militiaman and
the brother of Simon Kalinda, a leader of Hormisdas' team known as the
"dragon." John told DÃ©o that Fr. Hormisdas wanted to see him "urgently." He
also told DÃ©o about Hormisdas' role in Fr. Mathieu's death and in the
murders of the other three priests who had hidden at the orphanage.
John said: "You know that Fr. Mathieu stayed at home on his own.
Hormisdas went to ask him the whereabouts of the other priests, supposedly
to protect them. Fr. Mathieu immediately told him they had gone to hide at
the orphanage. Afterwards Hormisdas came back to where we were assembled and
told us what Fr. Mathieu had said. Then Hormisdas sent the people who were
staying at his house to kill the priests in the orphanage. Fr. Mathieu was
also killed by someone despatched by Hormisdas. What was sad was that
Hormisdas would not let us bury Fr. Mathieu, telling us to let his body be
eaten by dogs."
Despite his distress at the news, DÃ©o went to see Hormisdas, along with
another parish worker, Gaspard.
When we arrived at the parish, Gaspard and I stayed in front of the
church. John went to fetch Hormisdas, who arrived with John, PhÃ©neas and
Simon Kalinda. The priest had a large sword. He greeted us and told us that
the priests-Innocent, Bosco and Mathieu-were dead and that Mgr. Gahamanyi
[the Bishop of Butare] had asked him to find the people who worked for the
priests so that they could look after the church's property.
Accompanied by Hormisdas and the two militiamen, DÃ©o and Gaspard entered
the priests' residential quarters.
When we arrived at the priests' stables, I saw crows eating a corpse.  I
took a good look and saw that it was the corpse of Fr. Mathieu.  Hormisdas
was laughing as he watched the crows eating the corpse.  There were only
bones left. His eyes and flesh had been eaten by the dogs and crows. We
wondered why Fr. Hormisdas did not bury a fellow priest.
Later, DÃ©o and Gaspard buried Fr. Mathieu, a decision that angered
Fr. Hormisdas.
Vianney, related to Simon Kalinda, came to tell me to escape because
Hormisdas and Simon had drawn up a plan to kill me, accusing me of having
buried Fr. Mathieu. Vianney told me that Hormisdas no longer trusted me,
saying that I was like other Nyanza Hutus with Tutsi mothers.
Both DÃ©o and Gaspard left the parish and hid until the interim
government was driven out of Nyanza.
The belief that Fr. Hormisdas was behind the murder of another priest,
Fr. Justin Furaha was voiced not only by people in Nyanza, but by priests
who were at the Parish of Save during the genocide. They point out that
Fr. Furaha was the only priest at the parish who was killed and that he was
not targeted by the local militia, who frequently allowed him through
roadblocks. One of the priests said a group of people came to Save to warn
them that Fr. Furaha was going to be killed by the militia from Nyanza.
Asked about this, Fr. Furaha explained that Fr. Hormisdas was his enemy
since he "kept on accusing him of being a member of the RPF."  Soon
afterwards, the gendarmes arrived to take Fr. Furaha away.
On Saturday 7 May, at 1:00 p.m., we were having lunch when three people
in civilian clothes turned up. They asked me to call Fr.  Furaha. I went to
call him. He came to say hello, then they went into his office. I asked
Furaha who they were. He replied that they were policemen from Nyanza who
had come to take him away. They put him in their car and they all left. I
then contacted Furaha's brother-in-law, FÃ©lix Semwaga, an important figure
among the killers, to see if he could save Furaha. He told me it was
impossible because his file had come from Nyanza. Furaha was taken to the
police station in Butare, then to Karubanda central prison.
Sister FÃ©licien, who died after the genocide, went to Butare town to
look for Fr. Martin Kabalira, the military chaplain in Butare. She was a
Benebikira nun. She wanted to ask him to look for some way to save
Fr. Furaha. Kabalira explained to the nun that it was impossible to save him
because Hormisdas had himself drawn up the file for his arrest.  This is
what she told us.
A few days after Furaha's arrest I received a letter from him. He wrote
that the person who had accused him was the one I knew to be his enemy in
Nyanza. He had been accused of holding meetings with the Inkotanyi, of
having purchased a large piece of land in Nyanza to build houses for the
Inkotanyi and of giving large amounts of money to the RPF. He had given the
letter to a prisoner who had been with him in Karubanda; this prisoner was
allowed to leave the prison and return there later. We gave this prisoner
money, food and clothes for Fr. Furaha. Later, another prisoner came to tell
us that Furaha was dead, along with some other priests who had been in
prison with him.
Furaha is the only priest who died in Save. He was killed by Hormisdas
because he had been his enemy for a long time.
A gendarme who saw Fr. Hormisdas frequently in their camp when he came
to visit their commander, Captain Birikunzira, said Fr.  Hormisdas was also
complicit in the deaths of two Benekibira nuns, Sr. FranÃ§oise and
Sr. Augustine.
Hormisdas had become a great gÃ©nocidaire. Some days after the genocide
began in Nyanza, Hormisdas came to our police camp, driving the college
car. I was there. It was between 8:00 a.m. and 9:00 a.m.  He told the
gendarmes who were there that they had forgotten to attack the Sisters'
convent. Hormisdas went then immediately went into the office of the
commander. The commander called some gendarmes, including his escorts,
Corporal Gahutu, a corporal who was a storekeeper and some others. He gave
them the order to leave with Hormisdas in order to attack the convent. Some
of them left on motorcycles and others in Hormisdas' car. They were
well-armed. It was Hormisdas who had these nuns killed. No one had dared
attack the convent.
A Benekibira nun noted his refusal to take part in the mass for their
burial.
We telephoned Fr. Hormisdas to get coffins. We also asked him to come
and say mass at the burial, saying that he should represent the Catholic
Church at the service since it was a funeral for nuns. He refused to give us
coffins.
Since Fr. Hormisdas had not come to join the service and help us pray, I
asked him to come and celebrate mass for the two nuns.  Hormisdas flatly
refused.
Hormisdas was well aware that our colleagues were dead but he never came
to visit us. He also knew that the militiamen had stolen our food but he
never came to bring us anything to eat. On the contrary, he went around with
the members of the militia who had attacked us, in particular the
soldiers.
Hormisdas' behaviour towards his fellow-priests astonished even some
other militiamen. They point out that while he conducted the burial ceremony
for a leading figure among the killers, he refused to either bury his fellow
priests and nuns. Jean-Claude Higiro asked "how could Hormisdas have pity
for other Christians when he did nothing to protect his colleagues?" While
Janvier Habimana pointed out that nothing prevented Hormisdas from hiding
the priests threatened with death.
Fr. Hormisdas is the first person who should explain, in a court of law,
how the priests of the Parish of Nyanza died. What prevented him from hiding
them? The people who killed the Tutsis were his collaborators; they could
not, therefore, touch anyone who was protected by Hormisdas.
A Call for Action
These are just some of the many allegations concerning Fr. Hormisdas
contained in African Rights' report. The task of establishing the identities
of the victims, the circumstances in which they died and the exact role
played by Fr. Hormisdas has only just begun. Angry that the planners of the
genocide reside abroad in comfort, prisoners in Rwanda are increasingly
willing to talk openly about how the genocide was orchestrated and how it
unfolded.
Further evidence about Hormisdas' conduct during the genocide will
eventually be exposed, whether the Catholic Church decides to assist in the
process of discovering it or not. But in the meantime the survivors of the
genocide in Nyanza find it difficult to bear the knowledge that their
persecutor has gone unpunished.
African Rights has long been supporting the demands of survivors in
Rwanda for the arrest and trial of the members of the clergy they hold
responsible for the deaths of their families and friends. We have identified
several of the accused clergy to the Church and to relevant judicial
authorities and have provided notice of their locations.
In an open letter to Pope John Paul II more than three years ago we
asked His Holiness to launch an inquiry into the role of the Catholic Church
in the 1994 genocide. We listed some of the allegations against Father
Hormisdas Nsengimana amongst others. No action has yet been taken. Those
responsible for Father Nsengimana's transfer first to Italy and then for his
appointment to Cameroon should take the time to read the anguished
statements of ordinary Rwandese people and reflect upon where their
responsibility lies. We are hopeful that the Catholic Church hierarchy will
see the exposure of the allegations against Father Hormisdas Nsengimana as
an opportunity for it to take a new approach to the issue of justice in
Rwanda.

Original_Search_Title: Scientists Control Killer Weed
Headline: Uganda: Scientists Control Killer Weed
Body_Text:
The weed attaches itself to the roots of these plants, and then sucks nutrients from it
Agricultural scientists have devised a method to tame striga, the most notorious weed affecting cereal crops.
Dr George Bigirwa, head of the Cereals Research Programme at Namulonge Agricultural Research Institute, said "elephant grass and desmodium, another pasture crop, can be used to control striga as well as a devastating pest called stem borer."
Striga causes millet sorghum and maize plants to shrink. The weed attaches itself to the roots of these plants then sucks nutrients from it. This reduces the yield of the crops by between 30% and 100%. In extreme infestation, the crops may not yield anything.
The weed is most common in Iganga, Tororo, Pallisa, Arua, Kumi, Kitgum and Adjumani districts.
"Striga is a parasitic weed which feeds directly from the plant. All the nutrients that should have been absorbed by the plant are eaten by striga. It is one of the biggest problems to Ugandan farmers," says Bigirwa.
The stem borer, locally known as Ndiwulira, digs tunnels into the stems and cobs of the maize plant. This causes the plants to collapse. "In some places like Kapchorwa, if you are going to grow maize you must apply pesticides to control the stem borer. The losses range from 30% to 70% of the expected yield," he says.
Bigirwa says if you plant two rows of elephant grass around a maize garden and desmodium between rows of maize, you terrorise striga and the borer at the same time.
The desmodium uncinatum (silver leaf), a leafy plant liked by cattle, produces a chemical known as voltales that repels the stem borer. It also releases certain chemicals that do not allow striga to suck nutrients from maize.
The elephant grass on the other hand attracts the borers away from maize. "If you have maize and elephant grass the borers would prefer to feed and lay eggs on the elephant grass. So in that case the elephant grass pulls the borers away from the maize," says Bigirwa.
The method is referred to as push-and-pull because desmodium pushes away the borer from the maize garden while the elephant grass pulls the pest out of the garden.
Apart from chasing the pests, desmodium is a good legume to feed live-stock because it enhances milk production in the cow. It also fixes nitrogen into the soil, reduces the risk of erosion by spreading on the soil, and suffocates striga as well as other weeds.
"Desmodium spreads and suppresses other weeds, the garden is weeded less times. You only need to weed two times instead of four times," he says.
Besides, farmers can sell the seeds of desmodium. A kilo of desmodium costs sh30,000 in Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: Windfalls Boost Tiger Brands
Headline: South Africa: Windfalls Boost Tiger Brands
Body_Text:
DURBAN Boosted by a surge in operating profit at its food brands division and an increase at Spar, Tiger Brands lifted headline earnings a share 13% to 612c in the year to September.
CEO Nick Dennis cautioned, however, that windfalls influenced performance.
Spar and the food brands division traded for an extra week compared with the previous year, and foreign exchange gains on exports were realised in the latter part of the year. These boosters added about 1% to the headline earnings increase.
In the longer term Dennis expected the food-brands category management programme to affect top-line growth positively.
Cost savings generated by the programme and cost reductions from initiatives mainly under way in supply-chain logistics, would initially be reinvested in market research, brand and customer management and research and development.
Dennis said he expected the benefits of restructuring to be reflected in the new year's results.
Turnover from continued operations rose 11% to R16,5bn, while operating income rose to R1,7bn (2000: R1,5bn) on a higher operating margin of 10,3% (9,9%).
Driven by favourable raw material input prices in maize meal and a turnaround in the profitability of the bakery division, the cereals and beverages category improved operating income 49%. Sorghum malt, rice and pasta also performed solidly.
The culinary business reported a 25% rise in operating income on the strength of improved efficiencies and higher export earnings. The confectionery category lifted operating income 20% and product innovation was a significant driver behind the 9% hike in turnover for Beacon.
The perishable category of fishing operations Sea Harvest and Oceana plus Dairybelle lifted operating income 28% on a 15% hike in sales.
Health-care brands improved sales and operating income by 10% during a period of reinvestment and positioning for growth.
However, the pharmaceutical business in this division is not expected to show meaningful performance improvements until 2003, which is expected to see the launch of a significant number of new products.
Tiger declared a 145c final dividend, bringing the total to 213c. This was 11% higher than the pro forma dividend declared last year ahead of unbundling agripoultry interests into Astral Foods.
In line with the aim to dispose of noncore interests, the group sold its stake in UK-based Lagap Pharmaceuticals, the 49,9% holding in Pets Products and the 30% stake in Jumbo Cash & Carry. It has sold its 50% holding in Dairymaid-NestlÃ©, subject to Competition Commission approval.
Dennis said these results were achieved while the group was involved in a change process aimed at forging profitable top-line growth through establishing a demand-driven synergised business. Looking ahead, he said food brands and health-care brands would raise their investments in market research, product innovation and spending on core brands. Dennis expects the group to achieve "a more modest rate of real growth" in the year ahead.

Original_Search_Title: Turkana Fighters Killed, Raid District
Headline: Uganda: Turkana Plunder Kotido
Body_Text:
Suspected Turkana warriors recently killed two elderly men and raided about 50 head of cattle from Kapedo, Kathile, Sidok and Loyoro sub-counties.
They also slashed gardens of sorghum for the Teuso (Ik) in Kalapata, Kotido district.
The out-going 3rd Division commander, Col. Geoffrey Taban, and MP for Dodoth county Ael Ark Lodou confirmed the incident.
"We may be forced to flush them out of Karamoja if they continue disturbing our people," Taban said.
Lodou said the Turkana used sticks to slash the crops and later drove their cattle to graze on the slashed crops.
He said the suspects belonged to kraals from Yaragete and Edapal, occupied by clansmen of Kenya assistant minister for labour, Ekwee Ethuro.
"The cows raided were driven towards Songot hills near the escarpment on the Uganda-Kenya border, Lodou said.

Original_Search_Title: Severe Flooding Kills And Displaces People in Southern Sudan
Headline: Sudan: Severe Flooding Kills And Displaces People in Southern Sudan
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Six people have died in the last few days from water-borne diseases
due to severe flooding in southern Sudan's Tonj County, a community
leader said.
The official said that the six died on diverse days and the figure
could rise if the flooding continues and the international community
does not intervene.
"Their (victims') health is not good and water-borne diseases are
spreading fast," said Lino Daffalla Dangau, the community
representative of Tonj.
Dangau said this in an appeal to the Catholic Bishop of Rumbek
Diocese, Caesar Mazzolari, under which Tonj falls. He has requested
the bishop to appeal to the international community to come to their
aid before the situation gets out of hand.
"Floodwaters now cover three-quarters of the Thony and Muok locations
in the area and have so far destroyed homes of 2,282 people," said
Dangau. He added that most of the mud-walled houses had collapsed.
Agricultural fields have also been destroyed, with the floods doing
away with the recently planted millet, sorghum and groundnut crops.
This has made the victims vulnerable to famine-related hardships,
said Dangau.
Bishop Mazzolari, who left Tonj last week, confirmed that the flood
situation was grave.
"A lot of people have been displaced and are in a desperate need for
food, shelter and medication," he said in Nairobi.
"We have managed to accommodate some children in our school-feeding
programme as we did in 1998, but additional support is required as a
matter of urgency, he added.
Tonj, said the bishop, remains isolated due to the floods that have
aggravated a bad situation occasioned by the aerial attack by the
Khartoum government on May 24, 2001.
Besides its indigenous people, Tonj in Bahr el Ghazal is hosting
thousands of Nuer people displaced from their homes in western Upper
Nile by oil companies and factional fighting.
Meanwhile, a new report on the impact of Sudan's oil business has
been released.
The document is titled "Report of An Investigation Into Oil
Development, Conflict and Displacement in Western Upper Nile, Sudan".
It is dated October 2001.
The report was commissioned by Canadian Auto Workers Union,
Steelworkers Humanity Fund, the Simons Foundation, United Church of
Canada, Division of World Outreach and World Vision Canada. The
investigation was funded by Canadian and British non-governmental
organisations.
It was conducted between April 8 and 27 by Georgette Gagnon, an
international human rights lawyer and member of the Canadian
government-sponsored Harker mission that visited Sudan in December
1999, and John Ryle, an African specialist and author of various
studies on Sudan.
The report documents and places into context an intensification of
armed attacks on civilians in key areas in Sudan's contested oil
region in Western Upper Nile during 2000 and 2001.
It states that the attacks were carried out by government forces and
local militias allied to the government and the rebel forces of, or
aligned to, the SPLA and the Sudan People's Democratic Front/ Defence
Forces (SPDF).
The information in the report is based on findings from a series of
visits in April 2001 in non-government-controlled areas and in
adjacent areas of Western Upper Nile and Bahr el Ghazal.
The investigators, the report says, conducted extensive interviews
with local inhabitants and displaced persons in the affected areas.
They also held discussions with field-based emergency humanitarian
workers, Northern Sudanese Arab traders, Nairobi-based diplomats,
Sudanese and non-Sudanese academics and researchers, and local
officials of the SPLA and the SPDF.
It notes that "although the Government of Sudan declined to issue
visas for the team, the investigators were able to speak to
individuals who had recent experiences of events in
government-controlled towns, including former government employees,
members of the government armed forces and employees of oil
companies.
Among other things, the report concludes that "indigenous, non-Arab
people living in rural oil-rich areas are regarded by Khartoum and
its armed forces as an obstacle to further oil development. They are
seen not as citizens but as a security risk, potential supporters of
rebel movements, to be forcibly moved off the land they inhabit to
facilitate oil developments."
"Inhabitants of rural areas are compelled by government military
action to flee their homes, to move to non-government controlled
areas further south or deeper in the swampland or into government
territory in the north.
Coming soon after another report by Christian Aid titled The Scorched
Earth, this report is a further indictment on the Khartoum government
and the multi-national corporations involved in the
multi-million-dollar oil trade.

Original_Search_Title: Sorghum Beer Losing Ground
Headline: South Africa: Sorghum Beer Losing Ground
Body_Text:
UNITED National Breweries, formerly National Sorghum Breweries, is concerned that SA's sorghum beer industry was declining in volume terms, despite the group reporting a profit of R18,9m for the year ended December 31, compared with R504000 previously.
United National Breweries president and chief operating officer Rajan Ranganathan said yesterday that although the company had managed to become profitable, the sorghum beer industry was still facing difficulties.
"The organised sorghum beer market is being sandwiched from both sides while on one side home brew is gaining market share, on the other end clear beer is taking over sorghum beer drinkers as sorghum beer is fast going out of fashion," he said.
Ranganathan said consumer spending patterns had changed over recent years with cellular phones, casinos and the lotto also contributing to the drop in numbers of sorghum beer drinkers. The HIV/ AIDS pandemic also contributed to the erosion of the current base of sorghum beer consumers.
He said that despite the problems facing the industry, the group's performance during the year under review was significantly better than in the previous year. Operating profit improved to R41,1m in 2000 from R27,6m.
The company hopes to maintain profitability by continuing to cut costs.
Last year the company acquired Traditional Breweries Investment (TBI) from SA Breweries (SAB) for R30m.
The total purchase consideration was discharged by the group by taking a loan from SAB, repayable over five years with interest at prime rate.
Ranganathan said even though the group's bankers approved the transaction and provided an additional R5m overdraft facility to fund restructuring costs, all payments had and would be funded fully out of operational cash flow.
The operations of TBI were integrated with the group's and restructured at a cost of R5,4m substantially less than the originally estimated R8m.
Headline earnings for the year was 30,6c a share compared with 0,3c a share for the previous year. No dividends were declared.
Ranganathan said the group's cash flow was positive and it expected to reduce its long-term liabilities, which stand at R88,4m, by R25m during the current year.
"The huge challenge at the moment for the group's executives is to realise returns for the shareholders who have not had any return on their investments for many years," he said.
Ranganathan said the situation would not be improved by listing the group on the JSE Securities Exchange SA as was originally intended. "This option is currently not possible as not only is the industry facing difficult times, the stock market is undergoing serious turmoil," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Emir Commends Nestle
Headline: Nigeria: Emir Commends Nestle
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Emir of Kano Alhaji Ado Bayero, CFR has commended Nestle Nigeria
for its immense contributions and long-term commitment to the
economic growth and development of Nigeria. He made this remark when
he received in audience, the managing director and chief executive of
Nestle Nigeria, Mr.
Jean-Louis Chaumeil who paid him a courtesy visit in his palace
recently.
Speaking during the occasion, the Nestle boss said that Neslte
Nigeria had been involved in commercial activity in Kano State since
1968. He said that Kano State has had the good fortune of producing
the number two distributor of Magi products in Nigeria and, indeed,
the world. Chaumeil said that the courtesy visit to the emir was part
of to the re-launch activities of Maggi Cube in Kano. He said that
Kano State has contributed tremendously to the phenomenal growth in
turnover for the product. Besides, the company also gets most of its
local raw materials such as sorghum and Soya beans from Kano.
The Nestle managing director said that the magnificent edifice known
as "Gidan Maggi" located at Abubakar Rimi's market in Kano is an
eloquent testimony of the wide acceptability of Maggi Cube among
Ksano consumers. In appreciation of this consumer loyalty, he said
that the company decided to accord Kano a pride of place by
re-launching it first in Kano before any other location in Nigeria.
The Nestle boss said that the company had powerful brands which
command strong market positions in Nigeria and around the world. He
said that those brands will continue to benefit from significant
investment in terms of both marketing and research and development.
He assured that Nestle Nigeria will continue to monitor consumers'
perception of its brand so as to ensure sustainable, capital
efficient and profitable long term growth of the company and
satisfaction of its consumers.

Original_Search_Title: Karamoja Faces Famine
Headline: Uganda: Karamoja Faces Famine
Body_Text:
Karamoja faces famine due to severe weather that has hit the area. The unreliable rains and the closure of the Katakwi-Soroti road have also led to a shortfall in food production.
The district Agricultural Officer Moroto, Acila Odongo, said, "We expect a very poor harvest which will not last even one month. The unreliable rainfall pattern has thwarted government efforts to boost production here."
Odongo said the Government had early this year distributed to locals 10 tonnes of beans, 6.5 tonnes of sorghum seeds, 145 bags of potato vines and 60 bags of cassava stems to boost the food production.
The LC5 boss, Margaret Teko, said the cattle rustlers have affected the district of Katakwi and Moroto's general economic performance.
Teko said, "Communication is now very costly for traders from Soroti skirting all the way to Mbale on their way to Moroto. This I fear will shoot up the prices of foodstuffs in Moroto which is mainly dependent on the neighbouring districts for food. And the market for the animals here has always been through Soroti."
Comboni Father Bruno Avonelli says the poverty among the populace will make the famine worse.
He told The New Vision that famine is a common occurrence in Karamoja region in every five years.

Original_Search_Title: Feedmiller Decries Terrorism - Wants Assistance for Farmers
Headline: Nigeria: Feedmiller Decries Terrorism - Wants Assistance for Farmers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Apparently alarmed at the rate of wanton destruction that accompanied
the terrorists' attack on the World Trade Centre in America and its
possible economic spill-over effect on the world, a leading poultry
feedmiller in Lagos, Mr. Yinka Adebogun has roundly condemned the
unwarranted attack on civilian targets.
The industrialist who described the attack as barbaric lamented that
the reported low financial transactions in the leading stock markets
of the world clearly attested to his belief that the situation, if
not properly handled could spell doom and worsen the world economy.
However, Mr. Adebogun, who is the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of
Boar Trading and Poultry Milling Company, producers of the popular
Boar feeds brand warns of a likely food scarcity, hunger, civil
crisis and influx of refugees into the country in event of a
break-out of full-blown war between America and Afghanistan.
He explained that such situation could bring mixed blessings as food
shortages, would spur farmers to produce more to feed the teeming
population, thereby bringing more profit and increased production to
the industry.
The CEO said the country could also enjoy boom in her oil earnings,
as according to him, a war situation would definitely create an
opportunity for increased energy consumption thus government would
have more money to allocate to the various sectors of the economy,
especially the agricultural sector of which animal feeds forms a
part. (A place where Boa feeds is very active).
The effect of a full scale war, he further explained, could also make
or mar the gains of the year, if one assesses the profit against the
cost. Evidently, he said, poultry farmers will be the worst hit "but
surely poultry farmers would survive".
Despite this analysis, the CEO maintained that war would not serve
any useful purpose and therefore asked the two warring parties to
sheath their swords and embrace peace. But according to him,
appropriate punishment should be meted to the culprits.
While lamenting the undue pressure which poultry farmers are made to
undergo during the year as a result of exhorbitant prices of basic
raw materials like maize, sorghum, soya and groundnut, he appealed to
the government to enact a favourable policy that will assist farmers
double their volume of business in next year's budget.
He advised poultry farmers not to declare poultry as an unprofitable
business because according to him, "this year will go down as a year
of reckoning".
It would be recalled that prices of Maize which forms the major part
of cost of feed, about (45-65%) has fluctuated between N13,000 -
N18,000 this year while those of eggs rose from N150.00 - N180.00 to
N230 -N260 per crate and chicken (live, processed or served at hotels
and fast food locations) rose marginally by (10 - 20%).
Mr. Adebogun, however, noted that in recent weeks, the price
situation particularly with regards to Maize is gradually returning
to normal as the price, according to him, currently hovers around
N20,000 and N24,000, per ton, while that of Soya Beans sells for
N30,000 to N32,000. He added that the development has increased the
tempo of activities in the business, while also encouraging the
return of run-away farmers who were temporarily put out of business
as a result of the high cost of farm inputs.

Original_Search_Title: Pastor And Son Shot At Refugees Inside Church, Claims Witness
Headline: Rwanda: Pastor And Son Shot At Refugees Inside Church, Claims Witness
Body_Text:
Arusha — Genocide suspects Elizaphan Ntakirutimana and his son Gerard shot at
ethnic Tutsi who sought refuge at the Murambi Adventist Church during
the 1994 genocide in Rwanda, a prosecution witness today told the
International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR).
Elizaphan was a pastor at the Mugonero Adventist complex while Gerard
worked as a doctor at the complex in 1994. They have denied charges
of genocide and crimes against humanity. The Ntakirutimanas are
accused of planning and participating in massacres of the Tutsi at
the Mugonero complex, the Murambi Adventist Church and in the hills
of Bisesero, all in Kibuye Prefecture, western Rwanda.
The witness -- identified only as "YY"-- told the court: "I would not
know who was shot by either of the two because I was watching from a
hiding place but I saw both of them shooting into the church."
YY is the eighth prosecution witness in the trial. Prosecution
attorney Charles Adeogun Phillips of the United Kingdom led YY in his
evidence in chief.
The witness agreed with previous witnesses who testified that the
roof of the Murambi church was removed on Elizaphan's orders. He said
Elizaphan ordered the roof removed so that the Tutsi refugees could
not use the church to shelter from the rain.
YY testified that he fled to Murambi after surviving an attack at the
Mugonero complex on 16 April 1994. He said an estimated 5000 people
died during the Mugonero attack. YY estimated that the number of
Tutsi refugees at Mugonero prior to the attack was "not less than
50,000."
According to YY, ethnic Hutu families who sought refuge in a chapel
at the Mugonero hospital, together with other Tutsi refugees, were
asked to go home. Hutu women with Tutsi husbands were also ordered to
leave their husbands and children and go home, YY claimed. It was
after this separation, according to YY, that the attack on the chapel
was launched.
"Children born of a Tutsi man were considered Tutsi. If a child
attempted to escape to follow the mother they were killed," YY
alleged.
After Murambi, YY stated, he subsequently fled towards Munira hill in
the Bisesero region, where he allegedly saw Gerard and other armed
men attacking refugees.
"I saw him at the bottom of the hill. We were trying to resist the
attackers but when the shots intensified we scattered and run in all
directions," YY said.
The witness explained that to resist the attack female refugees would
hand them stones to throw at the attackers. YY said Gerard shot one
of the women who were near him.
"I saw him [Gerard] shoot at us. The wife of Nzamweta fell," YY
added.
"People were falling like sorghum plants in a violent storm. The
attackers were using grenades and shots were coming from all
directions. Those who were shot died on the spot, others piled up on
top of each other. The [attackers] carried machetes, clubs, hoes and
spears. They finished off those who survived the shots and grenade
explosions," YY told the court.
YY explained that Gerard and other attackers were following the
refugees as they climbed the hills.
During cross-examination, Ramsey Clark of the United States, lead
counsel for Elizaphan, challenged YY's claim that more than 50,000
people sought refuge at the Mugonero church complex.
Gerard's attorney Edward Medvene, also of the United States,
questioned why YY did not name, in written statements to ICTR
investigators, the people killed by Gerard. Medvene also asked why YY
did not mention in his statement that Elizaphan and Gerard shot at
refugees inside the Murambi church.
YY maintained that he only responded to questions asked by the ICTR
investigators who took down his statement, adding that he was not
asked for the details he gave in court today.
The trial is held before Trial Chamber I of the ICTR comprising
Judges Erik Mose of Norway (presiding), Navanethem Pillay of South
Africa and Andresia Vaz of Senegal.

Original_Search_Title: 'I Hid Under Corpses to Avoid Death,' Genocide Survivor Tells Tribunal
Headline: Rwanda: 'I Hid Under Corpses to Avoid Death,' Genocide Survivor Tells Tribunal
Body_Text:
Arusha — A prosecution witness who survived the 1994 Rwanda genocide today
told the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) that he
hid under corpses to escape death during an attack by 'Interahamwe'
militiamen at the Gikomero parish, central Rwanda. The Interahamwe
was the youth wing of the then ruling Movement of the Republic for
National Development (MRND).
The witness identified only as -- "GEE" for his security - - claimed
that 400 ethnic Tutsi sought refuge at the parish prior to the
attack. GEE is the eighth witness to testify against Jean de Dieu
Kamuhanda, former Rwandan minister of higher education and scientific
research.
Kamuhanda, 48, is charged with nine counts of genocide, crimes
against humanity (murder and extermination), rape and violations of
the Geneva conventions. He has denied the charges.
GEE, who is Tutsi, alleged that the Interahamwe attacked the refugees
using machetes and other traditional weapons. The Tutsi sought refuge
at the parish following the death of Rwandan President Juvenal
Habyarimana in a plane crash on 6 April 1994. Violence erupted in
Rwanda after Habyarimana's death, claming more than 800,000 lives
between April and June 1994.
The militiamen arrived at the parish in three vehicles, GEE alleged.
The witness claimed that Kamuhanda, who also ordered the massacres,
led the Interahamwe during the attack.
"I lay among the dead to escape death," GEE told the court. "After
the Interahamwe completed the killings, they left. Soon thereafter, I
escaped and hid in a nearby sorghum field," GEE explained.
Led by prosecution attorney Ibukunolu Babajide of the United Kingdom,
the witness stated that he learned of Habyarimana's death through a
radio broadcast.
"The next day, Tutsi refugees sought shelter at my house. The
Interahamwe pursued them and killed them," he claimed.
The cross-examination of GEE was adjourned until tomorrow after Aicha
Conde of Guinea Conakry, lead counsel for Kamuhanda, told the court
that she was not feeling well.
During proceedings yesterday, another witness -- identified as only
"GAF" --- completed his testimony.
Grace Amakye, co-counsel for Kamuhanda who cross- examined GAF,
alleged that the witness concealed his identity as a former soldier
of the Rwanda Patriotic Front (RPF) in his written statement.
Amakye claimed that GAF was an RPF soldier from 1994 until last year.
In response, GAF said: "Beginning this year, I'm now a businessman."
Earlier, GAF testified that between 2500 and 3000 Tutsi sought refuge
at the parish. Most of them were killed at the parish, he said, but
others were killed while fleeing.
Twenty-one prosecution witnesses are expected to testify in the
trial, which opened on 3 September.
The trial is held before Trial Chamber II of the ICTR, comprising
Judges William Sekule of Tanzania (presiding), Winston Matanzima
Maqutu of Lesotho and Arlette Ramarason of Madagascar.

Original_Search_Title: Guinness Explores Use of Sorghum for Products
Headline: Ghana: Guinness Explores Use of Sorghum for Products
Body_Text:
A joint committee is to be set up soon to work out modalities for giving tax incentives to Guinness Ghana Limited to enable it to use an improved variety of locally grown sorghum - kapala - for its products.
The committee will comprise representatives from the Ministries of Finance and Trade and Industry, Internal Revenue Service, Customs, Excise and Preventive Service and Guinness.
Vice President Aliu Mahama, who announced this in Accra on
Thursday, said the incentives would be in line with World Trade Organisation (WTO) regulations.
He announced this when a six-member delegation from Guinness, led by Mr David Hampshire, Managing Director for Africa and Middle East, called on him at the State House.
The delegation had asked the government for incentives, including tax exemptions, to complement investments the company will make in the project.
Alhaji Mahama said the substitution of malt with sorghum was in line with the government's policy to encourage industries to use local raw materials to facilitate economic growth.
"The government is committed to assisting you to achieve this goal, but as far as taxes are concerned, the government can only give tax incentives that are consistent with WTO regulations," he said.
Alhaji Mahama said the substitution would save Ghana foreign exchange used to import malt and would also provide job opportunities.
Mr Hampshire said Guinness would invest about one million dollars to install a new plant and an additional 500,000 dollars to provide social amenities for communities that would cultivate the sorghum.
About 5,000 farmers in 50 communities are to cultivate kapala, developed by the Savannah Agricultural Research Institute at Nyanpkala, in an out-growers' scheme.
Mr Hampshire, who is on a two-day visit, expressed satisfaction with the performance of Guinness Ghana, saying the stability of the cedi in recent times was good for its operations.
He will attend the company's annual general meeting, visit the Stock Exchange and interact with a cross section of business people.
Mr Hampshire will also make donations to non-governmental organisations, including the Ghana Heart Foundation.
Guinness manufactures alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages.
The delegation included Mr David Devlin, Managing Director of Guinness Ghana.
GNA

Original_Search_Title: Pastoralists Dependent on Food Aid "As Never Before"
Headline: Kenya: Pastoralists Dependent on Food Aid "As Never Before"
Body_Text:
Persistent drought has forced many pastoralists in northern and eastern
Kenya to become increasingly dependent on emergency food relief, UNOCHA
reported on Wednesday in its monthly situation report for Kenya. Although
food security had improved in Kenya as a whole during August, drought
conditions persisted throughout most of the pastoral rangelands of
northern and eastern Kenya, especially in the extreme northwest of Turkana
District and in North Eastern Province, it said. A study by the Arid Lands
Resource Management Project (ARLMP) had shown the need for relief food in
Mandera District, northeastern Kenya, to be "widespread", and that many
herdsmen faced a 50 percent to 60 percent deficit of annual food needs,
UNOCHA added.
Food aid would be required at least up until May 2002, because livestock
were in very poor condition, and significant rainfall was needed during
the short rains in October and November to regenerate depleted pasture,
ALRMP said. Although animal health projects were already assisting
livestock owners in the affected areas, a more comprehensive programme of
de-stocking and supplementary feeding would be required if the
October-December short rains were insufficient, according to UNOCHA.
Pastoralist livestock production remained depressed in most districts,
with the exception of Samburu, Moyale, Marsabit and Ijara districts,
casting doubts on the long-term viability of pastoralism, according to
humanitarian sources.
However, UNOCHA reported that overall crop production in Kenya was "good",
particularly in the high-potential areas in Rift Valley, Western and
Nyanza provinces. Improved harvests together with relief food
distributions had resulted in low prices for maize, beans, millet and
sorghum, leading to an increase in the purchasing power of food-insecure
households, it said. In view of the good harvest in areas of Kenya, the
government has said it will stop the importation of cereals in order to
provide local farmers with a good price for maize. It has also urged
donors to provide cash to allow for the purchase of locally produced maize
for food relief programmes.

Original_Search_Title: Hunger Threatens Half a Million People
Headline: Somalia: Hunger Threatens Half a Million People
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The United Nations World Food Programme, WFP, has warned that more
than half a million Somalis face a serious food crisis following the
almost total failure of the rains in southern Somalia.
The worst affected areas are Gedo and the Bay-Bakool sorghum belt,
which borders southeastern Ethiopia and northeastern Kenya. This area
usually produces 70 to 75 percent of the annual sorghum crop.
However, this year's harvest is not expected to reach more than ten
percent of the average yield.
"The situation is extremely worrying," said Kevin Farrell, WFP's
Country Director for Somalia, who has just returned from some of the
worst affected villages.
"Somalia will need 40,000 metric tonnes of food assistance in the
coming months if we are to avoid a humanitarian tragedy."
The dry weather has also seriously affected the grazing and fodder
conditions for livestock. Many families have already begun to take
their animals elsewhere in search of better grazing, while others
have left the area seeking alternative means of survival.
Migration is normal in this region, but this year it has started far
too early. "We are appealing to donors to respond urgently," said
Farrell.
"We must be in a position to act now to prevent more people from
leaving their homes and losing what little they have. We want to
avoid a catastrophe in the months to come."
The WFP is deeply concerned that food is made available to those
worst affected by this drought, and has already issued an appeal for
20,000 metric tonnes of food assistance for Somalia.
"The effects of the crop failure in what is one of the most
productive areas of Somalia, the Bay-Bakool sorghum belt, could be
very far-reaching," said Farrell.
"It means that elsewhere, there will be less food available in the
markets and prices can be expected to increase significantly in the
coming months."
The poor harvest is hitting many areas where levels of malnutrition
have been relatively high for the last two years. Assessments carried
out in Bay-Bakool and Gedo, as well as information collected from
feeding centres, indicate that many poor families are at the end of
their resources.
Most of the poorer households have no stocks from previous years and
possibilities to find jobs or generate income are very limited.
Low cereal production and resultant food shortages will impoverish,
even further, families who already live on very little and whose
nutritional state is already fragile.
"I have seen myself that many of the underground food stores, which
should be full at this time of the year, are now empty," said
Farrell.
"When asked how they would cope during the next few months, many
people simply told me they hoped Allah (God) would help them."
Many pastoral households in the north of Somalia are also suffering
the effects of very poor rains. Grazing conditions are reportedly bad
for this time of the year, which can be expected to result in
significantly reduced milk production and a deterioration of the
livestock condition.
The ban on livestock from Somalia imposed in September 2000 by the
Gulf States - traditionally big markets for the livestock raised in
the northern region - has already badly hurt the economy of local
pastoral populations. u

Original_Search_Title: Music Durban
Headline: South Africa: Music Durban
Body_Text:
330, 330 Point Road. Having just celebrated its 13th birthday in  spectacular style, the hardest, fastest club in the country proves that it still  has what it takes to push the pleasure buttons of Durban's hardcore  party-freaks and seduce every hedonist in town on a Saturday night. Since it opened  in
1988, Durban's first and perennially foremost rave repository has  hosted the leading lights of the dance world, has been consistently rated as one  of the top 10 in the world, has opened a sister establishment in London and continues to play the hardest house on the biggest rigs for the most unrepentant members of the dance fraternity. Website:www.330.co.za.
Absa Stadium. Providing a sonorous dimension to the World Conference  against
Racism taking place in Durban is the United against Racism Concert onSaturday September 1. Featuring a breathtaking array of the finest  sounds to arise from African soil, the concert ranges from Khadja Nin's  Afro-soul ballads to Salif Keita's griot-inspired music and also stars Lingala  tenor
Kanda Bongo Man; Kapa Dech's full and generous voices, fusion beats,  fluid melodies and ardent vitality; and AlgerianÃ±born Hamid Baroudi's  musical innovations, which epitomise the cross-cultural, multi-lingual,  multigenre sound. South African artists include Miriam Makeba, Juluka, Jonas  Gwangwa,
Caiphus Semenya and Letta Mbulu. From the home crowd in KwaZulu-Natal  comes
Busi Mhlongo, Mandla Zikalala and Ladysmith White Mambazo. Contemporary kwaito sounds will be represented with short sets from Mdu and Joe  Nina.
Another interesting component of the event is Young Africa, a digital bonding of beats and urban culture from Senegal, South Africa, the
Democratic Republic of Confo and Angola, which has grown from the  Ateliers
Croises pan-African workshops programme. A high-calibre collection such  as this doesn't come along every day and with tickets priced at a modest  R20 you shouldn't miss out on this one. Tickets are only available in  advance from Ticketweb.
Backstage, Royal hotel, Smith Streeet. The feisty, flamboyant Durban  Divas Ã±
Pinkie Mtshali, Thandi Soni and Noluthando Madondo Ã± have this ritzy theatre/music venue under their spell with their slick revue Cultural
Crossover. Pushing the envelope of the conventional cabaret format, the three vibrant songbirds spice up the familiar repertoire of soulful  torch songs with an injection of well-known traditional music as sung in  shebeens around the country. Running until September 15 from Wednesday to  Sunday, the show features a multicultural mix of disco, popular, traditional and township numbers. The line-up for the show includes songs by divas such  as
Margaret Mcingane, Gloria Gaynor, Vicky Sampson, Sibongile Khumalo,  Aretha
Franklin and Whitney Houston. Foot-tapping numbers by the Durban Divas  will include the famous Click Song, I Will Survive, It's Raining Men,  African
Dream, Natural Woman, Greatest Love of All, I Know Him so Well and  Thula
Thula. For bookings contact the Royal hotel on Tel:304 0357.
Bargo, 15 Mitchell Crescent, Greyville racecourse. This suave, ritzy nightclub swings back into live-music mode next weekend featuring two  of the country's favourite bands. On Friday September 7 catch the angst-edged folk-pop of Henry Ate with Durban rockers Overtone in support, or bliss  out to the mellow acoustic trails Landscape Prayers weave in the air on  Sunday
September 9 from 4pm. E-mail:bandwagon@artslink.co.za. Tel: 083 463  8044.
Bat Deck, Bat Centre, Durban harbour. The breezy balcony on the  harbour's edge offers fine views, cool cocktails and chilled vibes each weekend  with the Friday-night Sundowner Sessions. On Friday August 31 catch unique  Durban musical phenomenon Home Cooking, the mbaqanga/calypso/folk/blues/ reggae/rock/pop quartet who "sound good enough to eat". Combining a  vast range of elements to create a singular fusion, Home Cooking sing songs  of praise about life in KwaZulu-Natal and its rich tapestry of sensual stimulation. The band, comprising Marius Botha, Pippa Dones, Vusi  Mkhize and
Yoyo Sikhosana, are as quintessentially Durban as a mutton bunny, bean-and-cheese roti, sorghum beer or pap and vleis, with almost each  of their songs a hymn to the lushness of the province. The show is from  5pm to
7pm and entrance is free, so relax on the scenic balcony and drink in  the serene view and melodies. Tel: 332 0451.
Bean Bag Bohemia, 18 Windermere Road. Stylish survivor of the cocktail crowd's fickle tastes, the suave speakeasy continues to lure Durban's decadenzia with ritzy design, old-world elegance, Bohemian flair, sophisticated charm, fine cuisine and a dollop of designer sleaze. For  some midweek fun, join the madding crowd on Salon Wednesdays when all  cocktails are R10 from 5.30pm to 6.30pm, live jazz piano is on hand and DJs play  a soulful selection of old-school cool. Indulge in the Bean Bag's latest  and greatest theme cuisine and drinks season, which features the famous  flagship of Belgian gastronomy, moules et frites. Kitchen wizard Brendan Newport  has whipped up a blizzard of variations on the classic mussels-and-chips  dish, which is to be washed down with a selection of fine Belgian beer. Tel:  309
6019.
Burn, 112 Umbilo Road. Durban's avatar of the alternative continues to showcase nascent rock talent, which on Saturday September 1 takes the  form of melodic, Britpop-influenced, indie-rock outfit Waverley, fronted by former Manchuri Resin frontman Dom Anselme. An ever-present attraction  are the R1,50 shooters, which are also in evidence on Wednesday nights when  the city's skaters, Goths, punks, metalheads, rapcore phreaks and veterans  of disorder collide with DJs spinning everything from heavy to pop. Tel:  201
0076 / 082 976 7895.
Cygnet Pre-Primary School, 28 Queen Elizabeth Drive in Westville.  Popular
Dixieland band Platform Jazz will present a concert here on Sunday  September
2 at 3pm. People are encouraged to bring their picnic baskets and  chairs.
Tickets are R25 and are available at the gate.Advanced bookings may be  made at the school. More information: Tel:266 1741.
Darkside/Lightside, corner of Clark and Umbilo roads. Fondly nicknamed
Sideburn, the next-door neighbour of alternative nightclub Burn  provides a gentler option to the mosh action of the latter. The club is  DJ-oriented and features DJ That Bahstid spinning the big beat, hip-hop and indie. DJ  Jamie cranks the Eighties faves on Wednesday and Saturday nights. Entrance is  R10, which also gets you into Burn, and shooters are R1,50 all night. Tel:  201
0076/082 964 2280.
Durban Art Gallery, Smith Street. Catch the second concert in the
KwaZulu-Natal Philharmonic Orchestra's innovative Sinfonia Series in  the elegant environs of the gallery on Thursday September 6 at 7.30pm. The  event features a chamber-orchestra component of 36 players in a sprightly programme including Handel's magnificent Concerto Grosso Opus 3 No 4,  Bach's
Keyboard Concerto No 1 in D Minor (with Philipp Maier as the piano  soloist) and Mozart's Linz Symphony, one of the loveliest of all the classical symphonies. Esteemed conductor Lykele Temmingh is at the podium. Book  at
Ticketweb.
Frequency, 249 Gale Street. Durban's best-kept secret on the  alternative dance scene is this great semi-industrial, multi-storey building, which features intense indoor dance action and a rooftop cocktail bar with  mellow vibes under the stars. The club is open on Friday and Saturday nights.  For a late-night jive under Southern skies this joint can't be beat. A new attraction and reason to frequent Frequency is the relocation here of  the much-loved Sunshine Sunday sessions from the Bat Centre. The mellow weekender kicks off at 6pm and features chilled vinyl vibrations  courtesy of some of Durban's top mellow-makers. Cover is R15. Tel: 082 927 5409/  082 795
6686.
Howard College Theatre, University of Natal, Durban. The 2001 edition  of the annual African Music and Dance Showcase hosted by the African Music  Project at the University of Natal, Durban, lifts the lid on a bubbling brew of authentic African music on Friday August 31 and Saturday September 1.  This year's event is themed A Celebration of African Women and features a  host of acclaimed and gifted women groups from Ghana, Zimbabwe, and South  Africa.
Well-known Zimbabwean artist Chiwoniso Maraire will perform on the  mbira and
Women of Hewale Sounds from Ghana will perform music using Atenteben  flutes,
Dagomba gourd calabashes as well as songs and dances of the Ashanti, Ga  and
Ewe of Ghana. Two South African grassroots women's groups from  Lusikisiki and Meadowlands will perform traditional songs and dances of Pondo and  Venda women in vibrant attire. The African Music and Dance Showcase will be rounded off by the African Music Project female ensemble Amaqhikiza,  who present a repertoire of Zulu women's music. An extra bonus for  aficionados of all things African comes in the form of a banquet of African cuisine  from eight African countries Ã± Angola, Botswana, the Democratic Republic  of
Congo, Ghana, Kenya, South Africa, Swaziland and Zimbabwe Ã± which  follows the performance on Saturday September 1. The shows are both at 7.30pm  and tickets for each are are R25 (R10 for students, R5 for children).  Taking part of the sumptuous African buffet at 9pm following Saturday's  performance means coughing up a mere R15 extra. E-mail:opondop@nu. ac.za. Tel: 260  1045.
Providing a welcome musical breather from academics on Monday September  3 is a free lunch-hour concert from 12.15pm with Dick Hathorn and Company.
Renowned soprano saxophonist Hathorn is joined by John Edwards and  Wesley
Gibbens for a performance original jazz compositions. Tel: 260 3353.
International Conference Centre, Walnut Street. In keeping with the  musical theme of the World Conference against Racism Ã± namely Echoes of Hope  Ã± world-renowned Durban-born jazz musician and composer Darius Brubeck  has assembled 15 musicians under the name Gathering Forces, who perform a three-segment suite ending with a new composition, Humanity Has No  Colour by
Darius and Catherine Brubeck. Produced by the latter under the auspices  of the Centre for Jazz and Popular Music, University of Natal, the  presentation takes place at the centre on September 7, the closing day of the  conference.
Gathering Forces aim to demonstrate the harmony and lyrical diversity  in the world by combining various styles of music, from elementary and  traditional melodies to complex jazz. Local maskanda will be trading sounds with  the
Western viola and Eastern sitar and while the musicians stay within  their own particular genre, the totality is an image of a better world where humanity has no barrier or colour. In addition to Brubeck, the line-up includes top local artists such as Sazi Dlamini, Brendan Jury, Joyce
Mogoloage, Shiyani Ngcobo and Vivek Ram. They will be joined by jazz  stars
Barney Rachabane and Lulu Gontsana, and charismatic actor and musician  Ellis
Pearson spreads his enchanting presence among the composed and  improvised elements of the musical message. Through its multicultural music,  appearance and dress, Gathering Forces aim to portray a heartfelt collaboration in spirit and result. Tel: 260 3385.
Jabula Inn Restaurant and Jazz Club, Oldwell Arcade, 384 Smith Street.  This fine downtown jazz dive and restaurant is one of the few places left in
Durban where you can partake of top-drawer, live jazz music in the  authentic environs of the city. Keeping it real on Friday August 21 and Saturday
September 1 will be legendary South African jazz trombonist and  director of the Amandla Cultural Group Jonas Gwangwa, who lays on serious musical wizardry with his powerful 10-piece band. He is accompanied by equally gifted jazz muso Barney Rachabane and his tight, potent quartet. Both evenings commence at 9pm and tickets are R75 at Ticketweb or R100 at  the door. Tel: 304 7547/ 301 3816/082 852 1200.
The Jam, St Michael's Sands hotel. With its distinctive atmosphere  pitched somewhere between Moby Dick and Mars Attacks!, the South Coast of
KwaZulu-Natal is a souped-up coelacanth sporting strange dorsals and seductive scales to be discovered by the intrepid traveller in search  of thrills off life's beaten track. Dishing up quality rock for the salty  South
Coast dogs every Sunday night is The Jam, which on Sunday September 2  hosts top South African rock band Sunways, currently touring the country in support of their first full-length album, The Moon Is a Spoon, before heading overseas for a tour of the United Kingdom. For some good old-fashioned, heartfelt melodic rock don't miss this aspiring young  band.
The evening kicks off at 7.30pm and tickets are R20 at the door.
E-mail:avante@ venturenet.co.za. Tel: 082 496 3545.
KwaZulu-Natal Philharmonic Orchestra. Classical-music fans are  well-advised to take advantage of the special offer on season tickets to the spring season of the orchestra's masterful World Symphony Series, which runs  from
September 20 to November 15. Featuring eight weekly evening concerts in  the
Durban City Hall at 7.30pm, subscribers enjoy the additional benefit of entrance to four extra non-subscription concerts in September, November  and
December. The KwaZulu-Natal Philharmonic Orchestra's guest roster is renowned for its showcasing of musical stars from the front rank of the international classical-musc scene as well as South Africa's finest musicians. For full details regarding subscription ticket prices and preferred seating, contact the info line on Tel: 369 9404 or e-mail kznposubs@iafrica.com. Book for non-subscription concerts at Ticketweb.
The Rainbow Restaurant and Jazz Club, 23 Stanfield Lane, Pinetown. This legendary jazz jol, opened by Ben Pretorius and Billy Mthembu in 1981, became KwaZulu-Natal's first tavern promoting and providing live black  music in a predominantly white area. Over the years the Rainbow built up a worldwide reputation as a refuge for black roots and jazz music during  the apartheid years, but of late the venue has been somewhat slack on the  music front. But new music coordinators Neil and Nicola Comfort open a new  chapter for the legendary club and plan to host quality live, local sounds on a regular basis. See Gig of the Week. E-mail:bandwagon@ artslink.co.za.  Tel:
702 9161/ 083 463 8044.
Rocbees, Stanger Street. King of the downtown Durban party scene,  Rocbees endears itself to career club cats by being open 24/7 and providing a constant flow of R&B, kwaito and hip-hop courtesy of DJs S'bu, Vivian,  Nqoko and Nkosi. Tel: 337 9527.
Tusk Inn, 103 Sarnia Road, Rossburgh. This rough, resilient old hotel  is as tough as the industrial area it resides in, yet remains a beacon of  light in the dark swathe of silent factories and dormant railway lines. The Tusk  lays on a fine slew of rock and pop on Friday August 31 with the live music provided by Dave Birch (ex-Squeal frontman), cajun/ funk/rock/goemba ensemble Scooters Union and rock band No More Mr Nice Guy. The gig  starts at around 8pm and entrance is R10. Saturday September 1 is something  special for the metal freaks with Karnige, Afflicted Truth and Dismortal  bringing on the nasty noise from 9pm. Entrance is R10. On Sunday September 2 catch  the
Dave Marais Jazzmen in action from 3pm with old-school rock from  Brooklyn and classic roots reggae courtesy of Winston Tosh Marley weighing in at around 9pm. Entrance is free. On Monday September 3 the Durban Roots  Music
Association features folk/jazz/blues legend Larry Amos. The Blues Train steams ahead on Wednesday September 6 with Will's Blues Band, Sippies  Blues
Band and The Strutters hanging out at heartbreak hotel. For the latter  two gigs entrance is R10 for the public and musos get in free of charge and receive a complimentary shooter. Get ready for the famous Hazil's  Revenge bash on Friday September 7 featuring Lush Puppy, Spoonfeedas,  Sutherland,
Montana Nymph, No More Mr Nice Guy and Scooters Union. Tel: 464 8435/  465
3339.
Upstage Jazz CafÃ©, The Playhouse, Smith Street. The world-renowned
Afro-Caribbean jazz band that somehow hails from The Netherlands,  Surinam, the Antilles and Africa and carries the unusual moniker Fra Fra Sound  are on a September tour of South Africa and hit Durban with a concert at this  fine jazz venue on Wednesday September 5. Together since the early Eighties,  this seemingly incongruous musical animal with a radically diverse musical heritage have established themselves as a unique force on the world  jazz stage. The Surinam phrase "fra fra" roughly translates as "balance" and emphasises the band's constant search for balance between a wide range  of musical and cultural influences from Africa, Europe and the Americas.
