4. FOOD SUPPLY AND DEMAND SITUATION
Access to food and prices
Lesotho is a net importer of maize, wheat, pulses, dairy products and
other food commodities. In a typical year, roughly half of the food
consumed in the country is imported. For maize, the main staple food,
imports represent 60-65 percent of national requirements. Other than
for wheat, virtually all imports come from the Republic of South
Africa (RSA). In accordance with the SACU agreement, Lesotho does not
impose duties on imports of agricultural commodities from RSA. Thus,
food prices in Lesotho are closely linked to those in RSA. Past
attempts at administering prices have resulted in increased
smuggling, leading informal market prices to move towards those in
RSA.
There are two large-scale milling companies in Lesotho, both of which
operate wheat and maize mills. They process and package maize meal
and maize by-products (animal feeds). The bulk of maize processed
comes from commercial imports. Less than 5 percent originates from
local production.
Once farmers' own grain is used up, they have to purchase maize
flower at the industrial mills. In practice, they do lack the
opportunity to buy imported whole grain and mill it cheaply at the
local hammer mill. When domestically produced whole grain is traded,
its price tends to be comparable to the mill gate price at harvest
time, but is likely to rise gradually during the season as the
scarcity and expectation of scarcity of maize grain, increases. This
may be particularly the case this year.
Small-scale hammer mills are estimated to number over 300 in the
country. They don't buy maize, but only process it for consumers. In
poor growing years, virtually no domestic maize is sold to the
large-scale mills. The majority of production is retained by farmers
or traded locally, and is hammer-milled into meal.
RSA's Free State is located near the border with Lesotho and is the
main supplier of maize to this country. The Free State produces more
maize than any other province of RSA and registers substantial
surpluses. Thus, Lesotho's relatively heavily populated lowlands and
foothills are the natural market for maize farmers of the Free State.
Under a free market in South Africa, maize is accessible to Lesotho
buyers from nearby Free State cooperatives and farmers at prices
which tend to compare favourably with those at most locations in the
RSA.1
For the period 1997/98 to 2000/01, the white maize average domestic
price paid by Lesotho's millers has fluctuated between M725/tonne
(1997/98) and M620/tonne (2000/01).
Cereal supply/demand balance, 2001/02
The forecast of the cereal supply/demand situation for the 2001/02
marketing year (April/March), summarized in Table 4 below, is based
on the following assumptions and Mission observations:

Government and millers' opening stocks figures (31/03/01) were
provided by the Ministry of Industry, Trade and Marketing.
* The mid-marketing year 2001/02 population is estimated at 2 192 400
and the per capita consumption at 125 kg for maize, 50 kg for wheat
and 10 kg for sorghum.
* "Other uses" cover essentially post harvest losses and seed use.
They are estimated at 6 percent for maize and sorghum and 5 percent

From a macroeconomic point of view, the direct convertibility of the
maloti into rands means that availability of foreign exchange is not
a major constraint to commercial imports. In the past, commercial
food imports appear to have been quite responsive to the large
fluctuations in the national production (see Figure 3).
From a household standpoint, most households should be able to cope,
mainly through sale of livestock, off-farm income activities and
remittances from relatives working in South Africa. Many households
have also harvested other crops than cereals. However, a number of
households who lost crops and have neither livestock nor off-farm
income will need food assistance until the next harvest. They will
also need seeds to plant in the next cropping season. This scenario
is realistic, provided that RSA's maize prices do not increase
significantly as a result of lower domestic production and higher
regional demand for maize. Otherwise, the number of households at
risk will increase.
5. FOOD SECURITY AND VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT
5.1 Background
Most households are facing a reduced harvest in the current year, for
some the reduction is not too great, but a few reported that crops
failed completely. However, it was encouraging that most households
expected to be able to cope without the need for external assistance.
Livestock generally looked in good condition.
5.2 Coping mechanism
Households in the districts of Mokhotlong, Thaba-Tseka, Qacha's Nek,
Mohale's Hoek and Quthing are hardest hit by the effects of drought
recurring on average about once every three years, and problems
resulting from soil erosion and poor farming practices. Food crops,
employment and remittances and livestock are the most important
aspects of family coping strategies.
In these marginal mountain districts, many households rely on the
WFP- assisted school feeding programme to help meet family food
needs. The Government and WFP are also in the process of finalizing
the programming of the other development food aid resources in
Lesotho, including Support to Disaster Preparedness and Mitigation
Initiatives in food-insecure mountainous districts, which will target
some groups affected by the current crisis. This activity has already
been approved under the umbrella of the WFP Country Programme.
Despite the fact that the number of Lesotho miners working in South
Africa has fallen from its peak (125 000 in 1989), the mission found
that remittances from workers in South Africa were still the most
frequently mentioned coping mechanism during interviews on the field
trips. A large number of householders questioned by the team
indicated that it was their main source of cash income and none of
these households were at risk because of the harvest failure. Two
factors help to explain the continuing importance of this cash
source. The number of non-mining job holders outside Lesotho is still
increasing according to the Central Bank of Lesotho so while the
number of miners working in South Africa has dropped rapidly, the
total number of workers outside Lesotho has partially compensated for
the loss of mining jobs. Secondly, the average earning for Basotho
miners has increased steadily in real terms since 1989. These
findings are very much in line with the Sechaba consultants 1999
household survey which recorded 56 159 households receiving income
from mine work and 27 188 households having income from other work in
South Africa. Altogether, an estimated 25 percent of all households
in Lesotho received income from South Africa.
The second most frequently mentioned main income source was the sale
of livestock and other agricultural produce and this provided the
main coping mechanism for over 20 percent of households. Furthermore,
roughly 20 percent relied on full-time work in Lesotho, running small
businesses for cash income, and almost 20 percent had a satisfactory,
though less reliable, source of cash from casual or part-time jobs.
Taken altogether, these four categories of main income sources meant
that at least 85 percent of households anticipated an adequate income
to buy in food. Almost all rural families buy significant amounts of
food in a normal year. This year, most will have to buy more, but
provided prices do not rise too much, they will get by.
5.3 Those most in need
This situation leaves about 15 percent of households which have lost
their crops entirely and have neither livestock nor off-farm income
with which to buy food from the market. Some may have just started
self employment enterprises and they are not sure how those will
prosper, others may have adult children away looking for work in
urban centres in Lesotho or South Africa, while others have married
children in employment but they are not sure if they will receive any
help from them. Some are entering the informal sector, brewing beer
or getting involved in petty trade. Targeting the really needy from
within this potentially vulnerable 15 percent is going to be crucial.
Several farmers reported only being able to plant quite small areas
because they had been sick and as these limited areas have only
produced poor harvests, their problems have intensified. There was no
evidence to link these farmers' illnesses with HIV, but given the
high estimated prevalence of HIV/AIDS infections in Lesotho (24
percent among those aged 15-49), it is reasonable to expect that low
plantings due to illness will become an even bigger problem in the
future. Pending the results of the recommended food needs survey, the
number of households in critical need is provisionally estimated at 5
000.
5.4 Lessons from the past and targeting the needy households
Target a smaller number of really needy households. During the early
to mid 1990s, there were several major drought relief operations in
Lesotho. In 1995- 96, over 600 000 people received drought relief and
perhaps another 250 000 benefited from free school meals or FFW
schemes. This means a third of the population received drought relief
and when you include the other food aid projects it could be that
almost half the population received some form of food assistance.
Evaluations of these projects suggested that the food aid played an
important role in stabilizing the food security of many households
but many families who were not really in need also received food aid.
That is why it should be aimed to provide a much smaller amount of
food aid this time and to redouble efforts to target this aid more
accurately.
Promote better understanding of who are to be targeted. Previous
drought relief programmes were criticized for not being sufficiently
transparent with the result that they were not well understood in
many villages. Given the large scale of previous operations, many
villages had most of their population receiving drought relief. The
few not receiving aid could be understandably aggrieved if they had
received nothing but had also lost crops due to drought. This time,
there seems to be an apparent consensus that only a limited number of
households need support. Better information about any relief
activities needs to be made available throughout communities (perhaps
including radio broadcasts and villages pitsos) so that everyone
understands that help must reach the most needy households who have
lost their crops.
In the past, such huge numbers have been involved, that detailed
screening of the most needy was impossible. It would have required
too much time. Working with Village Development Committees, NGOs
working locally and community based organisations as well as the
relevant government departments, it should be possible to identify a
limited number of households potentially at risk. These could then be
screened using a modified version of the 1995 vulnerable household
registration questionnaire (it will need to be updated for
inflation). Those not really in need can be screened out and the
remaining potentially at risk households could then be investigated
in more detail using agreed criteria.
The situation of children
In general, during field visits, children were observed to be healthy
and well fed. This was in part attributed to the school feeding
programme, which provides free school meals to children in the
mountain schools. As already reported, the mountain districts of
Mokhotlong and Thaba-Tseka were experiencing the poorest harvests, so
school feeding will be particularly important in these districts.
Every effort should be made to ensure that school feeding runs as
effectively as possible. Of course school feeding does not directly
benefit under 5s nor does it always benefit the poorest and most
needy children. But now that the first two years of primary schooling
are free, it should help most children in that age range. Also from
January 2002 the third year of primary school will be free, thus more
children will benefit. As schools are closed from 1 June through to
18 July, children will not be getting school meals during this period
but the overall effect of the school feeding programme is that it
provides a useful supplementary input into the total food pot of many
households in the mountains.
While it was reported that children generally seemed quite well, this
was a conclusion from casual observation rather from any reliable
data. As children under 5 are typically at risk from food shortages,
the situation needs to be watched carefully. In conjunction with the
Ministry of Health, UNICEF is planning to monitor the nutritional
status of under 5s at 30 sentinel stations distributed throughout
Lesotho. This is an invaluable initiative to help assess the evolving
situation
The Mission recommends that a rapid survey be undertaken in the four
affected districts (Mokhotlong, Thaba-Tseka, Mohale's Hoek and
Quthing) to identify, quantify and precisely target the at risk
households for food and seed assistance. It is recommended that the
survey be jointly undertaken by Government, WFP and FAO's Special
Relief Operations Service (TCOR).
6. LONG-TERM STRATEGY FOR SUSTAINABLE AGRICULTURAL DEVELOPMENT
The declining cereal yields in Lesotho are the result of a
combination of unfavourable climatic factors, worsened by poor crop
husbandry practices. In the short term, emergency measures need to be
taken, mainly the distribution of seeds and fertilizers to the
farmers who have lost their crops this season.
Crop yields are in general very low in Lesotho because most of the
cultivated soils have low levels of fertility and poor moisture
retention capacities. As soil fertility has declined, yield levels
have also decreased. In the mid 1970s average maize and sorghum
yields were in the order of 1 400kg/ha. Today the average is
600-700kg/ha. Crop production is heading towards a serious crisis and
could cease altogether if steps are not taken to reverse the decline
in soil fertility. As recommended in the Soil Fertility Initiative
Document, prepared for Lesotho by FAO (1999), what is needed is a
participatory comprehensive approach that takes advantage of
synergies of practices at field level, offering production, economic
and conservation benefits. This approach would emphasise building of
soil organic matter levels through proper use of inorganic
fertilizers, manure and ash, coupled with intercropping of improved
cereals and legumes, conservation farming and agro-forestry
practices. The overall benefits are the improvement of soil structure
and fertility, food security, cash incomes, dietary diversity and
protection of the environment. The improved soil structure and
fertility result in increased efficiency in plant nutrients uptake,
thus enhancing the profitability of crop production as well as
enabling crops to withstand drought.
This report is prepared on the responsibility of the FAO and WFP
Secretariats with information from official and unofficial sources.
Since conditions may change rapidly, please contact the undersigned
for further information if required.

Original_Search_Title: Cereal Deficit Drops Slightly
Headline: Namibia: Cereal Deficit Drops Slightly
Body_Text:
NAMIBIA will have to import 185 900 tons of cereal this year after
late rains hit crop production in the country.
The Namibia Early Warning and Food Information System (Newfis), in
its latest bulletin, forecasts domestic cereal supply at 116 700 tons
this year due to late rains in the commercial cereal-producing area
commonly known as the 'Maize Triangle' (Grootfontein, Otavi and
Tsumeb).
Namibia has a domestic cereal utilisation capacity of 302 600 tons.
In March the Newfis forecasted a cereal shortage of 208 500.
Several problems highlighted in the March report revolving around the
late onset of rainfall, short supply of improved varieties of seed,
tractor owners refusing to accept subsidy vouchers, among others, had
led to a reduction in the total cereal production this season."
"However, the negative effects of these problems were to a certain
extent reduced thanks to a longer rainy season, which lasted up to
the first week of May in most parts of the North Central regions,"
the latest report stated.
The bulletin said the average yields for millet and sorghum were
revised upwards from the March forecast, while those of maize either
remained unchanged or revised downwards substantially.
The revision was partly due to the fact that the late rains could not
revive most of the late-planted crops, and partly because of
widespread flooding of the fields in the flood plains of eastern
Caprivi.
The Government unit said the expected national coarse grain
production was tentatively calculated at 92 200 tons - comprising 19
000 tons of white maize and 73 200 tons of mahangu/sorghum.
This output is 68 per cent of last season's normal harvest of 135 500
tons.
In addition, the winter wheat crop to be harvested in
November/December was preliminary forecasted to be 5 000 tons.
Winter wheat is produced near the Hardap and Naute dams in the Hardap
and Karas regions, respectively, and also at Shadikongoro project in
the Kavango region.
The forecasted cereal production together with operating stocks give
a total availability of 116 700 tons for 2001-2002 marketing year,
comprising 66 800 tons of mahangu, 21 400 tons of white maize, 20 100
tons of wheat and 8 400 tons of sorghum.
Assuming a projected national population figure of 1,920 million and
an average per capita cereal consumption of 135 kg, the cereal import
requirement for this year, was calculated to be 185 900 tons.
The deficit will be the difference between the domestic cereal supply
of 116 700 tons and domestic utilisation of 302 600 tons.
The latest forecast is lower than the cereal deficit forecasted in
March and the shortfall comprises of 57 400 tons of wheat and 128 500
tons of white maize."
"Under normal circumstances, this shortfall is always covered by
commercial imports during the course of the marketing year," the
report said.
It said the commercial millers have reported planned imports of 42
300 tons of wheat (of which 8 300 tons were already delivered) and 54
000 tons of white maize (of which 4 500 tons were landed)."
"This leaves for the remainder of the marketing year, uncovered
requirements of 15 100 tons of wheat and 74,500 tons of white maize.
This shortfall could be covered by additional imports."

Original_Search_Title: ECHO Donates US$3.1m to WFP for Internally Displaced People and Returnees
Headline: Ethiopia: ECHO Donates US$3.1m to WFP for Internally Displaced People and Returnees
Body_Text:
The United Nations World Food Programme said it welcomed a US$3.1
million contribution from the European Community Humanitarian Office
(ECHO) to provide emergency food aid to over 300,000 internally
displaced people and returnees affected by the border conflict which
erupted in May 1998 between Ethiopia and Eritrea, according to a news
release from WFP.
WFP is using the cash contribution to purchase locally more than
7,500 metric tons of sorghum which will meet the cereal requirements
of displaced persons and returnees living in the northern region of
Tigray for a period of almost two months.
"This donation from ECHO is crucial to the continuation of WFP's
assistance to vulnerable people, mostly women, children and elderly
persons, uprooted by the Ethio-Eritrean border conflict. It will also
enable assistance to continue to be provided to Ethiopian returnees
from Eritrea who are based in Tigray Region," said Judith Lewis, WFP
Representative and Country Director in Ethiopia. "This contribution
will also provide a vital market to many Ethiopian farmers in surplus
producing areas of the country."
In confirming the pledge, the ECHO Representative, Nick Weatherill
emphasised his hope that with the end of the conflict and the
opportunity for many IDPS to return home, food aid of this nature
will soon no longer be needed, as communities recover their self
sufficient livelihoods.
In March 1999, WFP approved an emergency operation to feed 272,000
people displaced by the border conflict over a nine-month period. In
April 2000, WFP extended its emergency operation and the number of
people assisted rose to 287,500. In addition, provision is made to
assist some 35,000 returnees from Eritrea. Contributions to this
operation were also received from the United States, Canada, Ireland,
Luxembourg and Sweden.

Original_Search_Title: Alert On Crop Failure in South
Headline: Somalia: Alert On Crop Failure in South
Body_Text:
Crop failure in primary agricultural regions of southern Somalia is likely
to lead to serious food shortages over the next year, the USAID-funded
Famine Early Warning System Network (FEWS NET) warned in an alert released
on Tuesday. It called on the donor community and humanitarian agencies to
"intervene before conditions worsen and basic household assets are lost".
A FEWS-NET Rapid Crop Assessment carried out in June in the two most
important agricultural regions of Somalia, Bay and Lower Shabelle, found
that poor sorghum and maize crops indicated that southern Somalia may
suffer a 38 percent drop in average harvest levels. In a normal Gu season,
Bay and Lower Shabelle produce more than 60 percent of the staple sorghum
and maize crops consumed in Somalia, the report said.
"This is a serious alert, a looming crisis... There must be a sense of
responsibility for Somalia," Abdulkadir Shirwa, FEWS NET agriculturalist,
told IRIN. He said that although there had been two or three good seasons
last year, poor security in the south had meant there had been little
marketing of the grain. Scanty and poorly distributed seasonal Gu rains
this year had resulted in widespread failure in many rainfed areas of
southern Somalia. Shirwa said there was concern over the limited amount of
food aid positioned for Somalia by humanitarian agencies.

Original_Search_Title: Agric Show, Or a Military Display?
Headline: Lesotho: Agric Show, Or a Military Display?
Body_Text:
Maseru — This year's national agricultural show took a new surprise shift as
it became an agricultural-turned-military display of weaponery at Ha
Foso, in the district of Berea.
During this agricultural show where farmers, from throughout the
country, displayed their various agricultural products from animals
to food plants, the Lesotho Defence Force (LDF) showed their military
wares, an unusual feature as the occasion has exclusively been
organised for display of agricultural products and not a military
ware show.
The army displayed their military amoured vehicles, various arms
calibres, helicopters and their skills in resolving conflicts and
riots as well as entertaining the public with traditional dances.
They also featured on how to use those weapons.
Colonel Mohanoe Lefosa, who appreciated their being invited to show
their military ware, said the show was an opportunity for the army to
show transparency. He said the show was void of political
connotations. Members of the army teams such as netball, diving, and
taekwondo were introduced to the public.
Despite this year's shortage of rain, the show was dubbed the most
successful as farmers showed their agricultural products of a high
quality whereby farmers from the District of Maseru scooped the first
position with 314 points, ousting three-time winner Berea District
over the weekend.
The Chairman of the Central Agricultural Committee, Mphutlane
Kuleile, said the show should not only be agriculture-oriented but
should serve as the stage where the entire public display their
handicrafts and other handmade products.
"This was going to be far better, had it not been due to drought
which hit hard on farming industry earlier this year," he said,
adding that Basotho now have a chance to show their various products.
While appreciating a protection of their livestock against an
increasing theft by both police and the soldiers, Kuleile wondered
where street vendors selling roasted maize obtained the product. He
said the product was being sold throughout the year adding that those
who sell it should be interrogated.
The show also attracted the Bendrie Implement, a harvest Machinery
Plant from across the border. Bendrie displayed a wide range of
machinery some of which can plough on rocky soil. The firm
Supervisor, Jonasse Maloka, said some of the machines were used to
harvest maize, sorghum and sunflower, a product used to make cooking
oil.
Among the farmers were Seele Nkekele of Ha Koali in the Berea
district, who disclosed that he comes to the show every year. Nkekele
produces Maize, Beans, Peas, wheat and vegetables and he says most of
the time he is on top with his seeds. He said the show is important
as it gives one to learn from other farmers.
This year's show saw Bonang Tlali from Quthing smiling away with
Lesotho Small Stock Farmer of the year award. Tlali won a ram valued
at M8 000.00 as the first prize. The ram was from a Free State farmer
Andreas Piennar, as a donation. Tlali also won a floating trophy, and
M3000.00. He said it was the second time for him to take the first
prize as he won a M5 000.00 worth ram last year. "I am very happy to
have won this prize. It is easier to feed small stock," he said.
Morolong Nteleki of Semonkong won second prize. In milk cows the
winner is Villa Maria Mission-Quthing followed by one Mohlouoa.
Maseru farmers won most trophies and were followed by Berea and
Mafeteng respectively.
The sponsors of the show included Prime Minister Pakalitha Mosisili
with M10 000.00 for seeds and animals, Maseru Toyota with M3000.00
and a floating trophy, Frasers M6 000.00 M4 000 Flour Mills, M4 225
from Maluti Mountain Brewery, M100.00 from Malebo Transport and
Panaar with the seeds.
The Principal Chief of Maqhaka, 'Mamolapo Majara, urged for the
protection of the show's area by ensuring that no houses would
mushroom from the place. She commended the workers for displaying
their various products from the farming industry.
During the closure of the show, Minister of Agriculture,
Co-operatives and Marketing, Vova Bulane, said this year's show was
not only for agric but also for the army to show their military
weaponry.
"I do hope that next year it is going to be more than this year. And
I hope we have to invite all the public sections even out of the
country. This one is the most successful one. We are working on the
theft prevention and the public should put a hand in this.
He announced a decrease of ploughing prices per hectare. The prices
have gone down by half from M120 to M60 for a hectare.

Original_Search_Title: Alert on Crop Failure in South
Headline: Somalia: Alert on Crop Failure in South
Body_Text:
Crop failure in primary agricultural regions of southern Somalia is
likely to lead to serious food shortages over the next year, the
USAID-funded Famine Early Warning System Network (FEWS NET) warned in an
alert released on Tuesday. It called on the donor community and humanitarian
agencies to "intervene before conditions worsen and basic household assets
are lost".
A FEWS-NET Rapid Crop Assessment carried out in June in the two most
important agricultural regions of Somalia, Bay and Lower Shabelle, found
that poor sorghum and maize crops indicated that southern Somalia may suffer
a 38 percent drop in average harvest levels. In a normal Gu season, Bay and
Lower Shabelle produce more than 60 percent of the staple sorghum and maize
crops consumed in Somalia, the report said.
"This is a serious alert, a looming crisis... There must be a sense of
responsibility for Somalia," Abdulkadir Shirwa, FEWS NET agriculturalist,
told IRIN. He said that although there had been two or three good seasons
last year, poor security in the south had meant there had been little
marketing of the grain. Scanty and poorly distributed seasonal Gu rains this
year had resulted in widespread failure in many rainfed areas of southern
Somalia. There had also been heavy damage by crickets in Bay Region at the
beginning of the cropping season and an unusually long dry spell in May, the
alert said. "In rainfed crop regions such as Gedo, Bay, Bakol and Hiran, it
is too late to plant new crops, even if heavy rains arrive in June, because
these crops are unlikely to reach maturity," FEWS NET said. Shirwa said
there was concern over the limited amount of food aid positioned for Somalia
by humanitarian agencies. A humanitarian source told IRIN that agencies were
worried about moving food around in southern Somalia because of insecurity;
but that insecurity "had been worse" in previous periods of crisis.
Crop production is progressing "relatively well", however, in Middle
Shabelle, Lower Juba and Middle Juba regions, Wanle Weyne District (Lower
Shabelle Region) and, to a lesser extent, Tayegle District (Bakol) and parts
of Bur Hakaba District (Bay Region), FEWS NET said. It said "exceptionally
high fuel prices" and high tractor hire costs for land preparation had
hampered crop production in some parts of Lower Shabelle region, as well as
"land mines planted by militia loyal to Mogadishu warlords".

Original_Search_Title: Food Production Falls
Headline: Benin: Food Production Falls
Body_Text:
Benin's food output dropped 2.9 percent in 2001 after rising 12.49 percent
in the previous year, the Panafrican News Agency (PANA) reported on Monday,
citing details released by the technical director of the country's Board of
Food Security, Dossouhou Gaston.
Gaston blamed the development on a drop in rainfall nationwide.
While the nation recorded a maize surplus at harvest, sorghum, rice and
beans fell by 21,200 mt, 63,550 mt and 8,200 mt respectively. The deficit
has been largely offset through imports from neighbouring countries, PANA
reported.

Original_Search_Title: The Power Centre Without the Power
Headline: Nigeria: The Power Centre Without the Power
Body_Text:
The northwest zone as a power centre predated the formation of
Nigeria. Steeped in the nuances of power politics since the time of the
launching of Jihad by Uthman Dan Fodio in 1804, when it conquered and
occupied large tracts of northern Nigeria, the zone has been a leading power
block in the whole of north and by extension Nigeria. An indication of this
is that it is the only zone with seven states: Kaduna, Kano, Sokoto,
Zamfara, Jigawa, Kebbi and Katsina states, euphemistically known as Arewa
north.
Although as a power block, which has played in the cutting edge of power
politics since its foundation, it has on number of occasions lost power. The
first time power left it was during the period of British rule; the second
time was the six-month interregnum of Ironsi's regime in1966, for which it
quickly recovered after a few months in disarray, following the killings of
its leading lights like Ahmadu Bello and others. The third time the zone
will be outside the commanding heights of Nigeria's power structure was 1976
following the death of General Murtala Muhammed; Babangida, a middle-belter
from Niger State ruled Nigeria from 1985 to 1993; and now, in this 4th
Republic led by Olusegun Obasanjo, a man they had supported and hoped will
allow the status quo to remain.
When that did not seem to have happened, the zone's violent reaction in
the early months of this dispensation, threatened the very corporate
existence of Nigeria.
It started with the declaration of sharia in Zamfara by Governor Ahmad
Sani in early 2000, an example soon copied by other states in that region,
which now insisted that Sharia constituted a major part of its Islamic
culture; notwithstanding the position of leading legal scholars from the
south of Nigeria that it was an unconstitutional act.
The demand for the adoption of sharia in Kaduna state by Muslims and the
resistence by Christians sparked off the worst religious upheaval that
spread to other states of the region. Simultaneous uprisings took place in
Katsina, Kano and Sokoto states in sympathy with the Kaduna Muslims, but the
targets of these killings were southerners and Christians. The killings were
on such scale that it sparked revenge killings in some southern states like
Abia, Rivers and Akwa Ibom states. The slow intervention of the federal
government did not help matters; and it is thought in some quarters that
this disposition by the federal administration encouraged first, the breach
of the constitution, and the subsequent disturbances that took place. It is
not entirely certain why the president after initial bluster of resistence
to the adoption of sharia in those states subsequently preferred to take a
soft line.
In retrospect, what happened in the early months of year 2000, was not
entirely novel, because in July 1966, following the coup of Major Chukwuma
Nzeogwu, the Arewa north for the first time in a post independent Nigeria
lost power, which it recovered by the counter coup of General Murtala
Mohammed.
Leading politicians from that zone had tended to believe that ruling
Nigeria was its exclusive right, Alhaji Maitama Sule, Nigeria's former UN
representative(1979-1983), once said that the North had the divine right to
rule. The reason for this thinking is not far fetched. The handover of power
by the British to Nigeria was done in such a way that the Arewa north was
favoured. Archive materials reveal the reason the British had much fondness
for northern Nigeria. Professor Itse Sagay in a recent lecture, quoted Sir
James Robertson as saying "the differences in ordinary custom and behaviour
between the dignified, polite and aloof northerner and the uninhibited,
vociferous southerner, who nosily showed his disagreement in council and
parliament without good manner and restraint."
Thus it was understandable the prayer and hope the departing British
colonial officials viewed the possibilities of a Nigeria in control of the
north. Professor Sagay reveals that another British colonialist Lord Hacourt
reportedly said "we have released northern Nigeria from the leading strings
of the treasury. The promises and well conducted youths is now on an
allowance and on his own and is about to effect on alliance with a southern
lady. I have issued the special license and Sir Frederick Lugard will
perform the ceremony. May the Union be fruitful and the couple constant."
The well-conducted youth has obviously learnt well, because between
independence in 1960 to 1999, the Arewa north dominated political power,
whether it is military or civilian governments. However, the June 12
annulment was the beginning of its present predicament.
The zone had gambled on Bashir Tofa from Kano to win, when that did not
happen, preventing MKO Abiola was enacted. While Abacha was in power, the
injustice of the annulment of June 12 election haunted that regime. Moreso,
the junta's incarceration of the winner of that election, MKO Abiola did not
help in the resolution of the many questions the act raised. As it turned
out Abacha, suddenly died of unknown cause, and within weeks Abiola also
died. The nation was aghast, particularly the South West, which felt
cancelled out by the shocking death of Abiola. Nevertheless, the death of
Abiola did not completely reduce the moral guilt on those who perpetuated
the act. Abacha's death merely provided an escape valve to this redress
brazen act. The beneficiary of this 'concession' was General Obasanjo, who
ran against a fellow south westerner Chief Olu Falae. Thus for the first
time in the history of Nigeria, only two southerners, in this case from the
same zone vied for the highest office in the land.
But how have the states fared in the last two years of civil rule.
Zamfara State
Although, Sharia began in this state, it has more to offer the just the
Islamic code. Guasu, by every standard in a small town, just started to see
elements of modernity. There are still few cars on the street of Guasu, it
does have digital telephones and one decent hotel.
Otherwise, with over 95% her population earning its living from the
land. Sorghum, millet, corns and onions are the common crops that thrive in
this state. The greater quantity of these produce are transported to the
South and to neighbouring countries. Beyond the production of these primary
commodities, it is not clear what the Sani administration is doing to create
an intermediate cottage industries to process it, into some finished
products.
Such programme is not yet in place, that is understandable as the
government in Zamfara spent considerable amount of its time countering or
defending the establishment of Sharia in his state. Governor Ahmad Sani,
says in an interview last year that his greatest achievement was
"enlightening the people on Sharia, particularly non-Muslims, outside the
state, who were very critical of the introduction of Sharia." He said that
his government have in one year in office executed 100 projects. He did not
mention specifics of what there are, but one caught attention. The
segregation of men and women from sitting together in the same bus, and
banning of women from riding motorcycles.
On Sharia, Governor Ahmad Sani has demonstrated zest in implementing to
the latter, its law and the spirit. Alcohol is banned, prostitution is
prohibited; stealing receives the full weight of the Sharia
injunction. Mallam Jangedi; had one of his hands severed for stealing a
cow. Halimas, was caned 100 strokes for adultery. On and on, the
administration never relented in its Sharia pursuits. The resistant of
non-Muslims have been very ineffective, on account of the fact that they are
either non-indigenes and therefore constitute an insignificant
force. Although Zamfara is by every available index, a poor state, its share
of the federal revenue for January-November 2000, was N 5,127,552,609.61. In
May of 1999, it got N 125,392,421.35, in December of 2000-N
570,328,651.43. All their moneys that Zamfara, received had a considerable
content of profits from alcohol. On his part, Governor Ahmed Sani, had
argued that he would not oppose the removal of alcohol tax from the receipts
that he gets. When that happens, it would be interesting to see how he
copes.
For the two years he was in power, Governor Ahmad Sani has successful
thrust an obscure state into the limelight, questioning by his actions the
whole legal framework upon which this dispensation is built. He will in the
years to come remain an issue in the search for structure that Nigeria
really should have.
Kano State
The hot bed of Northern Nigerian radicalism typified by the politics of
Mallam Aminu Kano and later Abubakar Rimi. For the most part, the struggle
between Rimi and Kwankwaso has dominated headlines. The two after initial
romance fell out over control of the PDP party structure. Today, the
relationship is frosty as everyone is waiting for 2003 to prove who is the
actual power in Kano politics.
The other headline-catching event was the announcement of the
implementation of Sharia. For a state that is in many respects cosmopolitan,
adopting Sharia was viewed with grave concern. But here adroit
implementation by the administration has helped hold the peace and stave off
the anticipated general collapse of social harmony, typical of Kano. What
Sharia did however, was to drive away businesses, particularly leisure
business. Mostly affected is the Sabon gari, home to over 2 million
non-indigenes of the state. Business like pool betting, brothels, night
clubs, taverns, hotels and beer parlours were forced to shut down. Four
weeks ago, the deputy governor led a raid to arrest illegal operators of
brothels and right clubs. That is a clear demonstration of the determination
of the administration to enforce strict Sharia. With this source of revenue
gone, it is not certain, whether Kano has attracted fresh investment into
its economy.
But the federal revenue allocation of January-November 2000, shows Kano
state got N 108,472,647,064.17. In May, 1999, Kano get N 202, 173,543.49 and
in January of 2000, it got N571,684,932.29-surely, Kano state can use more
funds to execute her developmental needs.
One area though, which the government has paid attention to is in water
supply. The state is chronically lacking water, but some decent effort has
been made to take care of this. Some parts of the state now has regular
water supply. Yet the policy of the administration has not been even-
handed. Sabon gari do not get much as attention is it should get, and is
very often overlooked. The other area, for which the government deserves
accolade is on education. The free education policy to indigenes is backed
by payment of busery to the students for accepting to go to school. Although
an age old policy of many states in the north, the stumbling block is that
not many students are turning up.
Sokoto State
The land of the caliphate, that alone speaks volumes of the strategic
importance of Sokoto state in the power calculus of North West, and indeed
the whole of Nigeria. Time was when heads of state went to the airport to
receive the Sultan. A sign of deep reverence, not many traditional rulers,
get the same treatment.
Despite the recent humiliation of Sultan Dasuki, the veneration of this
symbol is by no means reduced. One time is clear, Sokoto has solid
infrastructure built over the years. It is well planned, and her people are
deeply into agriculture. But as the economy dipped, the revenue distributed
from the centre also dropped. That presents a special problem for which
Governor Attahiru Bafarawa, has pledge himself to work on.
He has put efforts in roads, but especially, he has paid some attention
to education. He says, he recognises that without education not much can be
done. Free education, supported with good teach aids and attractive salary
package. Again finding sufficient personnel to man the schools and getting
the parents to send their wards to school. To tackle this problem, the
governor issued executive order that parents resisting to send their
children to school would be penalised. Whether that alone will work remains
to be seen.
Kaduna State
Governor Makarfi has a reputation for been diplomatic and tactful. He
has certainly in the two years in office exerted every ounce of his
character to hold his state from disintegrating. After the religious riot,
war in some peoples views between the Christians and Muslims last over
sharia, at which there was massive destruction of properties and loss of
human lives. The governor has spent virtually every moment developing
different tactics to hold the fragile peace of the state together. He has
employed every stratagem in the book, dissimulation is one in
particular. Kaduna is a sharia state only in name, the administration has
not implemented the code as brazenly as Zamfara. The consequence of such
action is dire. However the rebuilding effort of the state has been quite
commendable.
Mid term verdict
The comments of Arewa consultative forum, the elite northern
socio-cultural, thumbing down the governors encapsulates the two years of
democracy in that zone. The chairman, Alhaji Liman Ciroma said
"increasingly, large quantity of volatile, oil related funds are flowing
into the coffers of state and local governments, the flow of such money
appears to have become overwhelming with unsavoury results, here and
there. The unfortunate consequences are becoming increasingly obvious with
very few exceptions, there is little evidence of prudence in spending by
public officers."
That clearly is the missing link.
Re-anointing a favourite heir
The calculation by the north west in backing Obasanjo was fairly
simple. He was a man who had shown himself to be pliable in his days as
military ruler. Between the time he was in power 1976 - 1979, General
Yar'Adua his deputy from Katsina was extremely influential, far beyond what
a deputy is entitled to. Of the seven states, Obasanjo received massive
support in three states of: Kaduna, Kano and Katisna, but had decent
showings in Sokoto, Kebbi, Jigawa and Zamfara states.
So supporting Obasanjo again was a demonstration of their trust in him,
with the tacit acknowledgment that with him in power nothing will
change. The Yoruba were fully aware of the grand designs of the north west,
but could do nothing about it. Chief Debo Akande puts the Yoruba reaction in
perspective," the Yoruba did not quite accept Obasanjo's imposition on
them. But having been voted in as president, the south west have realised
that this thing (presidency) will have to be rotated and that president
Obasanjo being a Yoruba man, it will not be a Yoruba turn for sometime
again, hence they have to support him." Out of contest for power, the north
west was determined not to allow a candidate it did not endorse to rule. But
adopting Obasanjo has proved in some sense at least by their estimation a
mistake.
The parting of ways
The president's decision to purge the army of many elements from the
region, came as a shock, because the army was very close to their artery of
power. Additionally the appointment of military high command was staffed by
north central a zone they are in contest with. Professor Sagay gives an
insight on the formula the northwest had used to hold onto power. He says:
"The control of the army and the manipulation of the census figures."
Chief Akande's analysis further the reason for the seem disenchantment
of the Arewa north with Obasanjo. "The consensus that produced Obasanjo is a
funny consensus, because it was a consensus of retired and serving members
of the army, who were anxious to protect themselves, and put somebody they
regarded is safe for their interest."
Secondly the consensus included also the northerners, who believed that
if they joined hands to produce him, he would do their biddings and they
will continue with business-as-usual. Money would be passed through middle
man, without tenders board, so contracts will be given out at any price they
bargained for."
But Chief Akande holds that two years hence, their expectation of
Obasanjo has back fired, somewhat, leaving them with a deep feeling of
disappointment. Chief Akande further avers "two years have shown, that
first, much as he has tried to balance the interest of Nigeria, both his
constituency which is the army and the northern interest are rather unhappy
with him, because he is not been doing their full bidding. He is not
prepared to give 70% of the posts to the northerners, he is not prepared to
make retired generals chairmen of government corporations to the extent that
70% is given to them. He retired some of the military officers of northern
origin, for which they are aggrieved. For the first time Obasanjo appointed
the secretary to the government from the south south for which the north
could not get over."
Sharia as a negotiating tool
Feeling exposed and helpless, Arewa north had to find a strategy to
bring it back to contention in the power game. It thought up sharia, the
Islamic legal code as the subsisting law in their area. In other words the
constitution ceases to be the grundurm - sharia will hence forth determine
how their states is governed.
When the proclamation started in Zamfara by Governor Ahmad Sani, nobody
was quite sure how to handle it. The legality or breach of it has since been
massively disputed, with no clear winner. Eminent jurist in the South like
Prof. Ben Nwabueze, secretary of Ohaneze, and member of Patriots, a group of
eminent Nigerians, had argued consistently that adopting the Sharia legal
code was illegal. He had for the most part built his position on the
constitution, citing various provisions to support his argument, Nwabueze
said the constitution forbids state religion. At the same time Governor
Ahmad Sani, on his part, never wavered in his belief that the constitution
allowed him the freedom to introduce Sharia. To support his argument, he
challenged anyone, who genuinely believed that he had breached the law to
take him to court. Nobody ever seriously did.
With the temperature heated up with denunciations and rebuttal, the
federal government vacillated, not knowing the appropriate policy response
to adopt. But President Obasanjo knew he was in a bind, his administration
had to address the question. Under pressure to take a position, the
government issued a statement, that any Nigerian could on their own sue the
Sharia states. Christian Association of Nigeria (CAN) heeded this call, but
only half heartedly, because shortly after wards, it withdrew from the
court, and no one is really sure why.
The president, aware that this was an emotion-laden issue with a great
deal of political content, however preferred a middle course that would not
alienate him any further from the zone, at the same time that will not
appear indecisive over what in many's view is a breach of the
constitution. The president in his public comment merely talked about the
evils of political Sharia that will not last. Although he once said, it was
unconstitutional, beyond that he merely resorted to slogans about the good
side of Sharia, that it is private as against political Sharia, which he
hoped will fizzle out. It did not because shortly afterwards the nation
watched in horror the religious riots in Kaduna, Kano and Sokoto
states.
The lack of leadership by the president on this matter has been perhaps
one of the major low points of his administration. What is clear is that the
president has allowed political consideration to outweigh other stronger
points of those who urged to take a firmer stand, perhaps one stands out the
president knows, when not to commit political hara-kiri.
Dividends of Sharia
For all the talk about crude oil being the mainstay of the nation's
economy, the north west has perhaps the richest deposit of solid minerals,
that have remained untapped. Minerals such as gold, Kaolin, iron-ore abound
in this region and can become a potential source of wealth. But the
constitution forbids the states from doing anything. Governor Bafarawa, of
Sokoto says there is nothing he could about those minerals, since it is
within the purview of the federal ministry of solid minerals. Meanwhile, as
the federal government fails to demonstrate seriousness illegal miners are
help themselves and making a fortune.
Katsina
ALHAJI Umaru Yar'Adua, apart from being the younger brother of late
General Shehu Musa Yar'Adua, whose political machine produced President
Obasanjo, early in this dispensation demonstrated signs of character . For
instance, he remains the only governor, indeed perhaps the only politician
of note to have publicly declared his assets. Governor Yar'Adua, caused to
be published in the press his net worth: assets and liabilities immediately
he was sworn into office.
Since then, he has remained steadfast in pursuit of a disciplined
financial management of the resources of his state. Katsina State, an arid
region, has very little resources that can generate wealth, although
precious stones are embedded, but exploiting this mineral is outside the
jurisdiction of the states, rather it is within the purview of the federal
government which has done nothing.
The governor's stern posture on financial matters, however earned him
the enmity of many contractors, who would otherwise prefer a spend thrift in
charge. But beyond the governor's attitude towards prudence, taking this
state from this basic level of existence to a more comfortable level, is
another matter which has proved to be a big challenge. Water is one area the
administration has tackled with mixed result. The government in year 2000
spent well over N1.5 billion on water projects that would supply 50,000
litres of water.
This quality of water supply, impressive as it may seem is a drop in the
ocean, a lot is needed. Perhaps finding the source of funding of this kind
of project is one major headache as the depth to be dugged before water is
struck is very deep, unlike in other areas where it need not take that much
effort and work.
The other major challenge is education. The governor early in the year
almost declared a state of emergency is his state over the appalling
standard of education in Katsina. He admitted that elsewhere in the country,
people are embracing education and moving forward, but in his state it is
the reverse. Apparently, to help him out of his quandary, the ETF intervened
in February with grants of N50 million to assist the government; details of
the assistance indicate that, the sum of N44.5 million was set aside to
rehabilitate the falling structures of primary and post primary schools. The
remaining N4.5 million was deployed to procure furniture for the
schools. The money aside, the biggest problem really, is getting parents to
accept to send their wards to schools; but even in the teeth of that
problem, the administration has not really shown some creativity in
policy. The other allied social problem which has defiled whatever remedy
that has been thrown at it is the plague of almajiris, street urchins, who
would rather beg than go to school or engage in any meaningful
work. Perhaps, the interest of an NGO, Katsina State initiative to help, may
lead to some reduction in the number of young people, who will rather idle
away their life.
Jigawa
Governor Samiu Turaki, is generally seen as a man who visits his state,
rather than actually stay to govern it. The state legislature, tired of his
constant junketing, akin to President Obasanjo's shuttle diplomacy, passed a
resolution early in the year asking the governor to stay back home in the
state, rather than continue his junketing. But the governor aware that the
motion was merely an advise rather than law spurned it.
A man with seeming high-minded vision, Alhaji Turaki says his regular
trips abroad will get investors to help the state grow. One of the things
that he hopes to achieve is to turn Jigawa into the Silicon Valley of
Nigeria. Not in itself a bad idea, but how he intends to execute his dream
is one tall order, especially in a state that is
educationally-backward. Mindful of that fact, he says schools enrollment
have increased twofold .But this claim is seen by residents of the state as
a fable. Jigawa state shares the common phobia in the zone against western
education.
Without real achievement, to show, the governor recently said that
paying the three months salary arrears of the four months owed to civil
servants was one of his greatest achievements.
That is not all. He also said his administration increase of workers
salary by 50% is another.
Kebbi
GOVERNOR Aliero is governing a state which by every standard should not
be under an Islamic law - the Sharia. This is because it is a state with
50-50 religious divide. Of the four emirate councils, none has a predominant
Muslim population to warrant the imposition of the shaira legal
system. These are - Arugungu emirate,(under Muslim control); Yauri emirate
(under Muslim control); (Zuru emirate (predominantly Christians); Gwandu
emirate (Muslim). The common view by Christians is that the governor has not
been even-handed in his policy of adopting sharia Islamic code, it merely
strengthened the fact of the age old suppression of Christians in that part
of the country.
However, by all intents, the implementation of sharia in Kebbi will not
take after Zamfara or Kano model, nevertheless, even the prospect of a
sharia with a human face cannot lift the feeling of marginalisation amongst
the Christians.
Although two years in office, the governor has not yet succeeded in
mobilising his people to embrace western education. A good number of young
people of school age are not within the school system. That is inspite of
the fact that the administration has done well in terms of renovating and
equipping the schools with decent furniture and good physical
structures.
One other area, which the state government has not handled well is in
the area of urban housing. Till date, the administration has to its credit,
a mere 400 housing units, full built-up on owner/occupier basis. For a state
with housing needs at a crisis point, this is a drop in the ocean. In Birin
Kebbi, rent for a 2 bedroom flat goes for between N5,000 - N6,000 with up
front payment of between two to three years. The 400 houses on the ground
are meant for civil servants and senior administration officials.
Yet, Kebbi State has 21 local government councils, while Bayelsa a state
of similar size and population has 8 local government councils.
The challenge before the administration is not just over social or
physical infrastructure, it has also barely coped with flood problems, which
struck late last year devastating 8 villages. The state government's plea to
the federal government for assistance has not completely removed the danger
of future re-occurrence of the flooding incident. Kebbi is one of the states
clamouring for the establishment of Hydro-energy commission by the federal
government.
Although today, Birin Kebbi is wearing a fresh, somewhat modern look
with good roads and street lights, however to install the lights did not go
without a fight by those who say it desecrates their women. Those who did
not take kindly to the street lights say they are against it because putting
street lights exposes their women. They claim that been a Muslim community
the only time their women could come out with been molested is at night,
therefore the street lights would make that impossible.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Says Food Shortages Widespread
Headline: East Africa: WFP Says Food Shortages Widespread
Body_Text:
Tens of thousands of Somalis face acute food shortages due to poor rains
and the prospect of a failed grain harvest, says the World Food Programme
(WFP). In the south-central Bay and Bakool regions, much of the vital
sorghum crop had already failed due to drought, and much of the remainder
had been damaged by insects, WFP said in its emergency report. "This is an
early warning of a very serious food situation," said Kevin Farrell, WFP
country representative in Somalia. Some poorer families are barely able to
afford one meal per day, with others migrating to urban centres in search
of work. Even if rains improved in the next few weeks, it was already too
late for most crops to recover, he added.
In the Somali region of Ethiopia, heavy rains had improved conditions for
the majority of the population, the UN food agency said. However, there
was still great concern over drought-displaced people in the region. In
Denan town (Gode Zone), the drought-displaced were suffering from very
high malnutrition rates, and now outnumbered the town's resident
population. There were also serious health concerns about the
drought-displaced population of Hartishek (Jijiga Zone), where poor
sanitation and hygiene had led to high morbidity rates, and high levels of
malnutrition among under-fives. WFP said it was currently providing
assistance to some one million people in the region.
In Eritrea, the drought-affected region of Anseba had still not received
any significant rainfall, and there was a serious risk that another
harvest would be lost, WFP said. The war-affected Gash Barka Region had
received increased rainfall, but crop planting was being hampered by
massive land erosion and the presence of land mines, WFP said. WFP added
that it had distributed emergency food to nearly half a million
war-affected people in Gash Barka and Debub regions, which together
produced 70 percent of Eritrea's food output before war broke out with
Ethiopia in 1998.

Original_Search_Title: Crop And Food Supply Assessment Mission
Headline: Zimbabwe: Crop And Food Supply Assessment Mission
Body_Text:
Rome — Mission Highlights * Cereal production for the current marketing year
(Apr-01 to Mar-02) is forecast at about 1.82 million tonnes, 24
percent down from last year, reflecting reduced plantings and a drop
in yields.
* The large scale commercial sector experienced a decline of about 30
percent in area planted under all crops during the main season
primarily due to the disruptions caused by land acquisition
activities. Maize was especially affected as it showed a decline of
54 percent in area planted. An additional reason for maize area
reduction was its reduced profitability relative to other crops.
* The reduction in maize area (8 percent) and production (12 percent)
in the communal sector is largely due to payment delays for the
previous year maize crop by the state-owned Grain Marketing Board
(GMB). High annual inflation (over 50 percent) and the rising costs
of inputs further encouraged a switch from maize to cash crops such
as soybeans, sunflower, groundnut and paprika.
* Cereal import requirements for marketing year 2001/02 are estimated
at 579 000 tonnes, of which 132 000 tonnes of wheat and rice is
anticipated to be covered by commercial imports, leaving a balance of
447 000 tonnes of maize which will need to be covered by a draw-down
on stocks or, preferably, through commercial imports. Bilateral
programme food aid to meet part of the import requirement may be
considered as an option.
* Emerging food insecurity problems in the country are primarily due
to diminishing purchasing power. The urban poor and many households
in food deficit southern and eastern areas of the country are
particularly vulnerable to food shortages and would need to be
targeted for assistance.
* The ongoing land redistribution programme poses a number of complex
problems. Appropriate measures need to be taken to minimise its
negative impact on agricultural productivity and food production.
1. OVERVIEW An FAO/WFP
Crop and Food Supply Assessment Mission visited Zimbabwe from 25
April to 11 May 2001 to estimate the production of the main season
cereal and pulse crops, forecast the 2001/02 winter season harvest,
and assess food import requirements. Three teams comprising of a
staff member from FAO/GIEWS, two international consultants and two
national consultants from FAO along with two staff members (one
international and one local) from WFP visited 12 selected districts
from all five agro-ecological zones in the country covering all eight
provinces. These teams were assisted by specialists from the Ministry
of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement (MLARR). USAID's Famine
Early Warning System (FEWS) Network joined one of the teams.
FOOD AND AGRICULTURE ORGANIZATION OF The UNITED NATIONS, ROME WORLD
FOOD PROGRAMME, ROME
The crop assessment teams obtained data relating to area and yield
collected by District Extension Agents (DEAs) of AGRITEX and by the
national Crop Forecasting Committee. Interviews were held with
farmers, traders, NGOs, project workers and government officials
during field visits. Crop inspections, spot check crop cuts, market
surveys and livestock condition observations were conducted en route.
In addition, the Mission teams were provided with remote sensed data
regarding rainfall estimates, vegetation indices and interim reports,
assessments and vulnerability maps. Thus, the teams were able to
fine- tune initial MLARR yield forecasts, taking into consideration a
wider information base and recent events. The food security
assessment members of the teams collected information on household
food security, vulnerability, coping mechanisms and social welfare
programmes of assistance for the districts visited. A standardised
questionnaire was used for semi-structured interviews at farm
household and district levels. The information obtained was checked
against crop and livestock production data and compared with the
previous year and an average of the 1990s.
Similar to the pattern of the previous year, the 2000/01 rainy season
was generally favourable for the northern provinces of the country
which normally produce nearly 70 percent of the national cereal
harvest. But, rains were late and below normal in the southern and
eastern areas of the country. There was a dry spell during January in
most parts of the country followed by widespread incessant rains in
February and March which resulted in localised flooding.
The area under grain crops in the 2000/01 main crop season decreased
by 15 percent compared to the previous year. The area planted to
maize in the large scale commercial farming sector was reduced by
more than half primarily due to land acquisition activities. The
smallholder sector including the resettled areas did not make up for
this reduction in maize plantings. Some of the decrease in total
maize area was compensated by an increase in the area under soybeans,
groundnuts, sunflower and paprika.
The Mission estimates a national cereal harvest of 1.57 million
tonnes (27 percent lower) in the 2000/01 main cropping season,
compared to 2.15 million tonnes in 1999/00. Maize production this
season is estimated at 1.47 million tonnes which accounts for the
largest share (over 90 percent) of the total grain production.
Livestock condition was generally good in the large scale commercial
sector while in the smallholder sector it ranged from fair to good.
However, animal deaths due to tick borne diseases were reported in
some communal areas. Pastures and water availability conditions were
generally good in most provinces of the country.
Based on the forecast production, the import requirement is estimated
at 579 000 tonnes. Given the substantial decline in gold production
and the tobacco harvest, and much of the expected foreign currency
earnings being pre-committed for fuel, other energy imports and the
international debt servicing, Government's ability to import maize is
extremely limited. Even if the wheat and rice deficit of 132 000
tonnes were to be met by commercial imports by private traders
primarily by raising foreign currency in the parallel market, there
remains a net deficit of about 447 000 tonnes of maize to be covered
by imports or by drawing down on stocks. Given the current economic
conditions and food insecurity in several parts of the country, a
major draw down of stocks may not be advisable. The country needs to
hold adequate cereal stocks to maintain stable and adequate supply
necessary to prevent any price escalations and to keep prices at
affordable levels for both rural and urban population.
Free food aid distribution is not appropriate. Bilateral programme
food aid may be considered as an option to help ensure an adequate
grain supply at affordable prices in the deficit areas, both rural
and urban.
2. SOCIO-ECONOMIC CONTEXT AND FOOD SECURITY
Zimbabwe is currently facing economic and agricultural decline. It is
becoming clear that this decline can have serious implications for
food security as prospects for recovery in agriculture and other
sectors of the economy in near future are not encouraging. The gross
national product is expected to decline by 5 percent in 2001. This
would amount to a decline in per capita income for a third year in a
row, with a cumulative decrease of 18 percent during 1999-2001. In
addition, the country is facing an acute foreign exchange crisis
limiting its ability to import fuel, energy and basic food grains to
meet the looming food deficit. Government has fixed the currency
exchange rate at Z$ 55 to 1 US dollar since October 2000, while the
parallel market rate in April 2001 was around Z$ 120. Consequently,
much of foreign currency finds its way into the parallel market (some
estimates indicate about 75 percent), exacerbating the problem of
hard currency shortages for the Government.
The total external debt of Zimbabwe is estimated at US$ 4.45 billion
resulting in debt servicing costs due as a percentage of exports for
2000 at the unsustainably high level of 69 percent. The projected
balance of payments for 2000 was a negative US$ 611 million with a
build-up in arrears to external creditors of over US$ 500 million.
The country had to suspend its debt payments to the IMF, World Bank
and other creditors; consequently, the debt service actually paid
during 2000 was 38 percent of the value of total exports. The overall
external trade position of the country during 1996-2000 period ranged
from a net import level of US$ 111 million in 1998 to a net export
level of US$ 379 million in 2000. However, with gold and tobacco
production down and receipts from the tourism industry plummeting,
the country's total export earnings are expected to decline
substantially during 2001.
The economy is also undermined by persistently high inflation. The
rate of inflation has been 32, 58 and 57 percent during 1998, 1999
and 2000 respectively. The latest data puts annual inflation for
March 2001 at 55.8 percent slightly lower than 57.7 percent during
the month before. The situation may worsen due to relaxed monetary
policies and a continued budgetary deficit (estimated at 23 percent
of GDP). The prices of maize are stabilising in this
harvest/post-harvest period. However, prices of wheat (a winter
season crop planting of which will begin soon) are rising fast. For
example, wheat prices at the commodity exchange (ZIMACE) increased
from Z$ 7 729 in March 2000 to Z$ 15 261 in March 2001 (98 percent
increase). The cost of the basic food basket has gone up by 57
percent (in February 2001 compared to the same period a year ago)
almost at the same rate as the official inflation rate. Food price
inflation has come down from the highest level of 87 percent per
annum in September 2000 to about 40 percent in March 2001 and has
remained generally lower than the non- food items inflation rate
since January 2000 (Figure 1).
Rapid inflation combined with high levels of unemployment is eroding
the purchasing power of households and worsening the problem of food
security in the country. The rate of unemployment is around 40 to 50
percent. A recent survey indicated that a total of 400 companies
(including 171 motor traders, 92 steel manufacturers, and 45 clothing
and textile companies) closed during 2000. Business closures and the
down- sizing of an additional 350 companies led to 10 000 workers
losing their jobs (Zimbabwe Chamber of Commerce).
Source: Central Statistical Office, Zimbabwe.
Out of 174 countries analysed by UNDP, Zimbabwe ranks 130th on the
Human Development Index (HDI) and 112th on the GDP per capita
(purchasing power parity basis) index. The HDI ranking is low due to
low life expectancy. Zimbabwe is one of the worst affected countries
by AIDS which has reached almost an epidemic proportion. Life
expectancy at birth in 1998 was 39.2 years while without the AIDS
scenario it would have been 64.9 years (US Bureau of the Census).
Life expectancy according to UNDP is 43.5 years.
GDP per capita in 1998 was US$ 471 but using the purchasing power
parity it was estimated at US$2 669 which is relatively high in
comparison with other less developed countries. Population below the
US$ 1 per day poverty line is 36 percent of the total (1989-94). A
Poverty Assessment Study (1995) by the Ministry of Public Service,
Labour and Social Welfare showed that 57 percent of the population
was below the food poverty line and an additional 17 percent was
below the total consumption poverty line.
High unemployment levels combined with high cost of living in recent
years have made the poverty situation worse. Particularly caught in
this double squeeze are the urban unemployed and the urban poor.
Other food insecure groups include
(a) farmers who were affected by the January dry spell and
February-March heavy rains causing water logging predominantly in the
southern parts of the country, and
(b) farm workers who have been out of work since the farm invasions
or land acquisitions for re-distribution. Their situation in
aggregate does not seem to be as desperate as previously thought
since many of the workers may still be partially employed and
partially paid by the land owners.
In rural areas where food insecurity is becoming a problem, the main
coping mechanism is remittances from men or children working in the
cities. If the urban unemployment increases due to factory shut
downs, this income stream could dry up taking away one of the
important coping mechanisms from the rural poor and food deficit
households.
2.2 Land reform in Zimbabwe Land distribution in Zimbabwe since
independence in 1980 has remained highly skewed. According to
Commercial Farmers Union data for 1995, the average size of large
scale commercial farms (4700 in total) was 3000 ha as opposed to less
than 30 ha for the communal and resettled area farms (862 000 in
total). However, large scale commercial farms being highly capital
intensive and dependent on relatively low wage labour force (on
average 40 workers per farm) contributed 80 percent of the national
agricultural output.
In order to re-distribute agricultural land, the Government has
implemented various land reform programmes. The land acquisition and
settler emplacement (Phase 1) programme from 1979/80 to 1997/98
acquired 3 498 444 ha and emplaced 71 000 new settlers. During the
new "Inception Phase" from October 1998 to June 2000, 85 more farms
covering area of 168264 ha were acquired for redistribution. The so-
called "Fast Track" programme was initiated in July 2000 and by April
2001 a total of 2 706 farms with 6 086 605 ha of land have been
designated for mandatory acquisition and re-distribution. Between
July 2000 and 9 February 2001, a total of 51 543 households were
settled on 2 083 301 ha of land. All of the large scale commercial
farms that have been designated even if not yet acquired, no longer
have access to credit from banks. Those farms that are not yet listed
for acquisition are uncertain about making preparations and the
necessary investments in inputs for the next season's crops. In many
cases long term investments have completely ceased. This will have a
negative impact on agricultural productivity and production in the
short term at least, and measures need to be taken to ensure recovery
in long-term agricultural growth.
2.3 Agriculture in the national economy Agriculture is one of the
most important sources of employment accounting for about 27 percent
of total employment during 1994-98 period. Agricultural exports are
very important to the national economy as they earn the major share
of the foreign exchange. In 2000 agricultural exports represented
about 42 percent of total exports (US$ 1708 million) of which tobacco
(26 percent) and cotton (6 percent) were the leading export earners.
Normally Zimbabwe exports its surplus maize but the country had to
import during the drought (or flood) years of 1992, 1993, and 1998
and again in 1999 (Figure 3). The country is generally deficit in
wheat with an average net import level of about 75 000 tonnes per
year during last six years (1994/95 to 2000/01).
3. FOOD PRODUCTION IN 2000/01
3.1 Rainfall Similar to the pattern of last year the 2000/01 rainy
season was generally favourable for the northern provinces of the
country which normally produce nearly 70 percent of the national
cereal harvest. However, it was not very favourable for crop
production in the southern areas of the country as it started late
and was below normal. Although there was a dry spell during January
in most parts of the country, widespread heavy rainfall in February
and March resulted in above normal rainfall for the season for most
parts of the country. The 2000/01 season was peculiar in that it
commenced over the central and northern areas first instead of as
normal in the south and western areas. The north and central parts of
the country received above normal rainfall up to December. The
southern districts suffered from persistent dry spells.
The rainy season effectively started from the third dekad of October
for most areas in Mashonaland East, Mashonaland West and Masvingo
Provinces. For Mashonaland Central and Matebeleland North, the season
started from the second dekad of November, whilst it started very
late for most parts of Matebeleland South. The month of November was
generally wet particularly over northern areas. In December,
substantial amounts of rainfall were received over large areas of
central, northern, western and eastern districts but the southern
districts of Gwanda, Masvingo, Bulawayo and Beitbridge received
below-normal rainfall. In January, the extreme north and extreme
south-west received above 70 mm of rainfall. However, the total
rainfall received during January in most parts of the country was
very low compared to their long-term average. In most southern and
eastern districts there was a prolonged dry spell during the whole
month of January followed by excessive and continuous rains during
February. These heavy rains in February resulted in nutrient
leaching, water logging and flooding in some areas in Muzarabani,
Guruwe, Kadoma, Chegutu, Masvingo, Tsholotsho and Gokwe Districts.
An abnormally slow movement of the Inter Tropical Convergence Zone
(ITCZ) northwards resulted in widespread heavy rainfall in most parts
of the country during March. Most areas received more than 100
percent of their average rainfall for the season except Beitbridge
and adjacent areas in the south and Southwest that received 60 to 80
percent of the mean. The distribution of rainfall was also abnormal
in this cropping season.
3.2 Inputs As in the past years, this year about 90 to 95 percent of
the maize seeds used by smallholder sector farmers were certified
hybrid seeds. Although the price of hybrid maize seeds increased
sharply (from 48 Z$/kg in the previous cropping season to 104 Z$/kg)
most small scale farmers in the northern provinces used hybrid maize
seeds. Only about 15 to 20 percent of the small farmers in the
southern and eastern provinces used open pollinated retained maize
seed. For sorghum and millet crops the seeds used were from local
varieties carried over from last year's harvest. In some areas the
seed source for sorghum and millets was reduced due to cyclone Eline
which occurred in early 2000. However, in all provinces cereal and
pulse seed availability in general was not a constraint.
National fertiliser use decreased by 10 percent from the planned 500
000 tonnes to 450 000 tonnes. This is mainly attributed to the
decrease in area planted by the commercial farming sector. The
pattern of use of Compound D (7N- 14P-7K) basal fertiliser and
Ammonium Nitrate (34.5 percent N) for top dressing, which are the two
main types used, vary significantly among provinces based on the
rainfall conditions. Fertiliser use in the northern provinces was
much higher because of better rainfall distribution compared to the
southern and eastern provinces where the conditions are generally
drier with erratic rainfall. In spite of a sharp increase in
fertiliser prices, most farmers in the northern provinces, even
smallholders, used normal amounts. Most small farmers in the southern
and eastern provinces did not use much fertiliser but relied on
cattle manure.
Use of credit by the smallholder sector for the purchase of inputs is
generally very limited. GMB provided seeds and fertilisers through
credit very late this year, particularly in the southern and eastern
provinces.
3.3 Pests and diseases The only migratory pest noted were quelea
birds on sorghum and pearl millet mostly in the south and south-
west, with areas adjoining national parks particularly affected.
Among the non-migratory pests and diseases of some significance this
year were stalk borer, grey leaf spot, cob rot and streak virus on
maize, aphids on finger millet, leaf rusts in soybeans and
groundnuts.
3.4 Area planted The total area under grain crops in the 2000/01 crop
season is estimated at 1 489 000 hectares. This represents a 15
percent decrease compared to 1999/00 (Table 1). The area planted to
maize, sorghum and pearl millet decreased by 14, 37 and 14 percent
respectively, but the area planted to finger millet increased by 49
percent. The 14 percent decrease in maize area is largely due to the
54 percent decrease in the commercial farming sector and 8 percent
reduction in small holder sector. The decline in maize area in the
