about the same time Olang'o was being killed over less than 2 cents, in
another part of Kenya, Ndakutha Machere had had his two fingers chopped off
before being killed by his three sons over a land dispute.
Kenya is a land full of wonders and is now becoming a very insecure
country. In fact the United Nations has downgraded Nairobi to the level of
Beirut due to its high crime rates. Indeed, Nairobi has been regularly
referred to in the Kenyan press as Nairobbery. In Nairobi, bank heists take
place in most brazen and dramatic fashions. The trigger-happy police have on
a number of occasions responded, when their cars had fuel, and the hot
pursuits, reminiscent of James Bond movies, that ensue have usually
culminated in some or all the gangsters losing their lives. However, it has
now come to the attention of the Kenyans that the Kenya Police never seem to
recover any money. In the most recent showdown between the police and
gangsters, the police gunned down four robbers but did not recover even a
cent of the at least $140,000 that had been robbed from a bank.
Now many Kenyans are asking some intriguing questions: Are those gunned
down real robbers? If they are, might they in turn be victims of police
robbery? Many Kenyans seem to have a feeling that the police deliberately
gun down the suspects so that they can help themselves on the loot. A clue
to where the stolen money disappears to was given in one of Nairobi's
sprawling slums, Kayole Estate, when Police officers were seen chasing
suspects who had robbed a pub, shooting the suspects, searching their
pockets and, in full view of onlookers, taking away the stolen
money. Policemen are also known to mug Nairobians staggering home after
happy hours in Nairobi's lively nightspots. If you go to report the mugging
at the nearest Police Station, the chap at the report desk will yawn, pick
his teeth and tell you to go back and bring your assailant(s) with you. It
is now common knowledge in Kenya that the police officers are either leasing
their weapons and uniform to violent gangsters, or they are themselves
engaged in terror orgies aimed at dispossessing the innocent public of their
valuables.
Kenya does not only have a police force renowned for it's unprofessional
and terrorist activities, but it also has courts that sadistically dispense
the harshest judgments. The Kenyan judiciary is one of the most corrupt
branches of government in the country that has seen a steady decline of
justice, spiraling crime and institutional corruption. Besides being openly
corrupt the magistrates are also very arrogant and merciless to the
poor. This was recently displayed in a magistrate's court in the central
Kenya town of Murang'a when a man was jailed for eight years for stealing a
Bible from a school he had broken into.
I recently came across an interesting study by the American National
Association for Continence (NAFC) that showed that Americans spend about an
hour in their bathrooms every day, which works out to two weeks a
year. About half of the people involved in the study said they spent their
time in the bathroom pondering serious issues, while a third admitted to
simply daydreaming, singing in the shower or even talking on the phone. I
have been tempted to ask NAFC to carry out such a study in Niger where the
government reportedly spent almost $60,000 on toilet rolls for just five
senior parliamentary officials last year. A toilet roll in dirt poor Niger
costs between 150 CFA francs (21 US cents) and 300 CFA francs (41 US cents),
meaning that each of the five officials used 40,000 rolls during the
year. Doesn't this give a completely new meaning to the clichÃ© that African
politicians are full of it?
In Paynesville, Liberia, a taxi driver, Papa George, was shot dead by a
senior immigration officer, Henri Cassell, who happens to be a
brother-in-law of president Taylor. Mr. Cassell killed Papa George because
Papa George's taxi over took Mr. Cassell's vehicle. Mr. Cassell, who is
currently serving his sentence, has assumed a new role: threatening his
fellow inmates form the Krahn tribe that if the current war in Lofa reaches
Monrovia, the Krahn inmates will be executed by the Liberian government. The
Krahn leaders were convicted of treason after another bizarre incident. In
September 1998, Liberian government forces, led by Chuckie Taylor (the son
of President Charles Taylor) raided Camp Johnson Road area in Monrovia to
allegedly evict Krahn people who they said were occupying private properties
illegally. The raid resulted into the slaughter of hundreds of Krahn women
and children. In a face saving endeavor, Liberian government charged the
Krahn leaders and subsequently convicted them of treason. The supreme court
of Liberia heard the ruling of the lower court and resolved to double the
Krahn inmates' ten years sentence, thereby making it twenty years.
In view of their historical past and conditions that they are in at the
beginning of a new millennium there has been a debate around the world on
whether or not Africans (or people of African origin) suffer from a massive
case of paranoia. There is not a doubt in Wallace Gichunge's mind that they
do. He says the widespread fear among black people that someone is out to
wipe them off the face of the earth has contributed to the suspicion about
the origin of Aids and a reluctance in some areas to get vaccinated, even
against polio. Now there is this rumor in Africa about a small magnet hidden
in the buckles of cheap belts that have suddenly flooded the market that is
said to be sapping the virility of the wearer and consequently leading to
sterility. Gichunge opines that the easiest way to scare the ordinary
African stiff, is by predicting doom on conjugal matters Â­ where virility on
the part of men and fecundity on that of women have a higher premium placed
on them than a couple of university degrees.
While still on the matters of fecundity the following story might sound
stranger than fiction but they actually happened. In Sio, Burkina Faso,
Modou DiÃ© took his younger brother, Hamidou, to the infirmary, some three
miles away after his stomach cramps had failed to ease. Later Modou was
thunderstruck not only after learning that his brother had been transferred
to the maternity ward but also that he had given birth to a baby boy. Now
called Sita, Hamidou DiÃ©, who had been raised as a boy and was privy to all
of the village's male initiation rites, has caused ripples in Sio with his
transformation into a young mother.
Apparently, she was born Sita but then her father realized she was a boy
and renamed her Hamidou despite her mother's certainty that she was a
girl. Central to this conundrum was the anatomical oddity of the baby having
been born with two sexual organs. But nature has always had its way and
could not be changed on a whim. On reaching puberty age, she started
menstruating but kept it a secret and underwent the initiation rites into
manhood. During this initiation in the forest, one of her friends discovered
the truth and started a relationship that resulted in a pregnancy. She
steadfastly stuck to her normal routine until the stomach pains became
unbearable and she had to seek medical help. Tragically, Sita's lover ran
away and her baby was found dead after two months. The villagers who had
considered it a curse are suspected to have caused the unnatural death of
Sita's baby.
Is it any wonder that after witnessing such amazing things some Africans
have developed second thoughts about conjugal matters? That is why I never
gasped for air when I recently read in an African color magazine about a
woman seeking a dream man with whom they could "start a loving and honest
relationship." However, the man, the 30-year old lady said, had to be
impotent. Indeed amazing things are happening in Africa!

Original_Search_Title: Drought Hits Arua
Headline: Uganda: Drought Hits Arua
Body_Text:
A Prolonged Dry Spell In Arua District Is Threatening To Affect This Season's
Crop Harvest.
According To The District Agriculture Officer Mrs. Caroline Arubaku, The Worst
Hit Counties Are Madi Okollo And Terego And The Crops Likely To Be Affected
Most Are Beans And Maize.
Speaking To Sunday Vision In An Interview, Arubaku Said Due To
Unpredictability
Of The Climate They Have Been Encouraging Local Farmers Not To Rely Heavily On
The Rains For Carrying Out Agricultural Activities, But Rather Explore
Alternatives Like Small Scale Irrigation In Areas Where There Are Rivers. She
Said They Have Also Been Advising Farmers To Grow Quick Yielding Varieties Of
Sorghum And Maize.
Meanwhile, Arua Deputy RDC Haruna Sebbi Has Appealed To World Food Programme
(WFP) And UNHCR To Initiate An Irrigation Scheme Along River Nile At Rhino
Camp
To Benefit Both The Refugees And Host Community.
Haruna Sebbi Said This Recently At A Debrief By A Three Member Team That Has
Been Evaluating The Progress Of WFP In The Area.
He Said It Is Important To Set Up An Irrigation Scheme Because Of Unreliable
Rainfall Patterns.

Original_Search_Title: ICRC Ends Aid to Huambo
Headline: Angola: ICRC Ends Aid to Huambo
Body_Text:
The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) is ending its
distribution of food and garden-vegetable seeds to almost 300,000 people in
villages and to internally displaced persons (IDPs) around the central
highland town of Huambo.
"Improved security conditions in the region mean that people can once again
work the fields and grow the crops they need to live," the ICRC's latest
news bulletin said. "An ICRC food survey carried out in March in the
villages around Huambo and Caala showed that the severe malnutrition rate
among the population had dropped to 2.2 percent. These remaining cases are
due more to endemic illnesses in the region such as malaria, parasitic
diseases and diarrhoea than to lack of food." The ICRC said that only
displaced people with no access to arable land will continue receiving food
aid. "Permanent village residents around Huambo will continue benefiting
from ICRC agricultural assistance," the bulletin noted.
The ICRC said that fierce fighting in the central highlands between
government troops and UNITA forces in late 1998 caused a food shortage
affecting civilians living in the Huambo area, and over 30 percent of the
population eventually suffered from severe malnutrition. "To meet the
resulting needs, the ICRC urgently began large-scale food distributions. A
considerable logistical effort made it possible to bring into the area and
distribute over 37,200 mt of food in all," it said.
In addition, an agricultural rehabilitation programme was launched involving
regular distribution of seed and implements, production of natural
fertilizer, diversification of local seed varieties, clearing of irrigation
canals, etc. Some 3,200 mt of seed (maize, sorghum and bean) have been
distributed to farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Debswana's Farm Model Could Feed Botswana
Headline: Botswana: Debswana's Farm Model Could Feed Botswana
Body_Text:
Gaborone — Dryland farming is possible in Botswana and can render this country
food sufficient, even during the most erratic and/or minimal
rainfall.
This sounds unbelievable, but Debswana has proved, through its Masedi
Farms project just how possible this is, and recently took a group of
reporters on a tour of the farms to see for themselves.
The farms are phenomenal. From a distance they look like gigantic red
cakes, with grand cream decorations, and vice versa, against a
backdrop of the black soil of Pandamatenga.
Some of these are purely red, or white. But as one nears the farms,
it becomes apparent that one's eye has only been befuddled: hundreds
of thousands of sorghum heads sprawl as far as the eye can see,
creating once again the impression of a mesa.
The sorghum is neatly planted in rows and maintains equal height. But
the atmosphere here is silent, without the usual quelea fluster and
mournful dove camaraderie. That is a wonder in itself.
The birds are every farmer's nightmare, but it looks like the farm
manager and farm hands here go to bed in peace of mind. Make no
mistake.
There are no scarecrows on these farms. Pests are almost non-
existent.
So promising is the crop on these farms that the expected yield for
this year is a staggering 3000 tonnes.
What is most astounding however, is the fact that this amazing yield
is not a result of irrigation. It is purely dryland farming - and a
fulfilment of Debswana's vision to provide a sustainable commercial
farming model for farmers in Botswana.
Debswana, the diamond partnership between the government of Botswana
and De Beers, established the farms in 1998, with an initial capital
injection of P10 million. The farms would produce cotton and other
suitable commercial crops.
The project was given eight years to prove its worth. The first two
years proved disastrous.
Debswana's Group Secretary Joe Matome, says crop yield was modest in
the first year, as the farms encountered teething problems from the
use of advanced farm equipment especially boom sprays.
Some of the machinery had been bought from Australia and South
Africa. There was also the problem of pests, which took advantage of
delays in applying insecticides, soil salinity in some parts of the
farms and low nutrient status in the soil.
There was similarly no luck with the 20 outgrowers that Masedi was
assisting as locust, animals and weeds destroyed most of their crop.
In the second year the company lost an estimated P1,5 million as a
result of floods. However there was an encouraging cotton and sorghum
harvest in farm areas not affected by the floods.
It was from the cotton of that season that Masedi pioneered the sale
of lint. For a second straight year the outgrowers performed
dismally, mainly because of lack of commitment.
As a result Masedi decided to reduce the number of outgrowers to 10
participants who showed some commitment.
Masedi Farms learnt from these first hiccups, and its determination
to reach its goal is seen in the splendid crop that followed.
This year it has sown 2400 hectares and has had a good crop in most
areas. It has also paid special attention to the soil infertility and
salinity problems, and insect management.
"Current estimates indicate that the company will achieve an
operating profit during this season for the first time. It is also
apparent that major agronomic problems have now been satisfactorily
dealt with," Matome said.
So promising is Masedi's harvest for this year that already
government has contracted it to supply its ministry of Local
Government with 2500 tonnes of Sorghum. In fact the delivery is
likely to be the only one from Botswana farms.
"This year we can proudly say our system of minimum tillage, use of
nutrients and fertilisers as well as hybrid seeds is working; it is
succeful," Matome said.
Masedi plants the high yeilding Eureka sorghum that was developed in
Australia and the Sudan White from North Africa's the Sudan.
This year's expected yield is three tons per hectare - about two and
a half times the national average.
Masedi's 10 outgrowers too are beginning to reap the fruit of their
determination. One such person is William Mothoiwa.
After ploughing twice and getting no yields, Mothoiwa did not give
up.
"I started ploughing on December 17 last year, but there was no rain.
I ploughed again but still there was nothing. But then Masedi came
along and offered to plough for us at a minimum charge. They also
sold us seeds at subsidised prices," he said.
Mothomiwa said he was encouraged by Masedi's help which was
invaluable.
Like the farms, he hopes to get an early and good harvest.
"I think I will be able to harvest more than double what I usually
harvest," he said.
Masedi does not only help outgrowers to have an improved crop and
harvest. Every year it trains two young people in farming.
The young people are sent to Australia for training after which they
are attached to Masedi until they are able manage their own farms.
Once the Farmers have finished their term, Masedi will help them
secure farms for themselves and continue to assist them until they
are established.
Debswana believes that the Masedi model is Botswana's best bet for
feeding her population.
It can also bring the country a significant income through the sale
of the produce, especially cotton, from which comes several products
such as lint and oil. Perhaps a tour of the farms by farmers from
other parts of the country could bring a revolution in the farming
industry in Botswana.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Operations Continue to Face "Serious" Food Shortage
Headline: Rwanda: WFP Operations Continue to Face "Serious" Food Shortage
Body_Text:
World Food Programme (WFP) operations in Rwanda have continued to be
hampered by a serious shortage of food commodities during April, according
to the WFP's monthly report from Kigali. The current pipeline problems
facing WFP-Rwanda have forced some nutrition centers to provide smaller
rations for therapeutic- and supplementary-feeding programmes. Staff at
these centres said they had insufficient cereals to supply
therapeutic-feeding patients, and were using porridge prepared for the
supplementary-feeding programme. Due to "a complete pipeline rupture", WFP
is substituting corn-soya blend (CSB) requirements with maize meal.
However, WFP is also facing an acute shortage of maize meal for nutrition
and refugee programmes.
A WFP assessment mission to the provinces of Kibungo, Kigali Rural
(Bugesera Region), and Umutara, the regions hardest hit by last year's
drought, found that with the exception of Bugesera, the harvest of January
had been relatively good, and that rains had been favourable to crop
development so far. In the most affected area of Kigali Rural, farmers
have planted early, and their May sorghum and bean harvests are expected
to be good if rains continue. Nevertheless, WFP found that a percentage of
households were so severely affected by the drought that, to cope with
hunger, they had had to sell assets such as goats and iron roofing sheets.
WFP warned that this population would remain highly vulnerable, and said
that most of Bugesera remained vulnerable to food insecurity as a result
of insufficient household food reserves following three years of
consecutive poor harvests. The mission also found that the rates of child
malnutrition in parts of Bugesera had not declined during the last six
months, noting that an epidemic of a virulent strain of malaria had
contributed to the static malnutrition situation. The mission recommended
that recovery programmes should focus on reducing food shortages in the
long run through interventions such as soil-erosion control, agricultural
intensification, and poverty alleviation
During the month of April, WFP-Rwanda distributed 1,094 mt of food aid to
86,634 beneficiaries.

Original_Search_Title: US Loan to Be Used for Food
Headline: Eritrea: US Loan to Be Used for Food
Body_Text:
The United Sates has agreed to provide a loan of US $10 million to the
Eritrean government, the pro-government Visafric news agency said on
Wednesday. The loan will be used to purchase approximately 81,000 mt
tonnes of wheat and sorghum, Visafric said. The agreement between the two
countries was signed by US Ambassador to Eritrea William D. Clarke and Dr
Woldai Fitur of the Eritrean government in Asmara, the Eritrean capital.
Eritrea is expected to repay the loan, at one percent interest, within 30
years.

Original_Search_Title: GMB to Raise $60 Million in Storage Fees
Headline: Zimbabwe: GMB to Raise $60 Million in Storage Fees
Body_Text:
Harare — The state-run Grain Marketing Board (GMB) will this year raise about
$60 million through the handling and storage of 140 000 metric tonnes
of agricultural commodities belonging to private companies and
individuals.
GMB chairman Canaan Dube said this week the parastatal had decided to
engage in commercial activities like the storage and handling of
privately-owned agricultural produce to utilise the vast resources at
its disposal and raise cash resources to finance its operations.
"The revenue generated last season through handling and storage work
amounted to $42 million, while this season we expect to generate $60
million for handling 140 000 metric tonnes of agricultural produce,"
he said.
"The board has widened the GMB's scope of activities, which are aimed
at the maximum utilisation of its vast resources. These activities
have seen the GMB increasing its revenue."
GMB, which enjoys a monopoly for all maize exports from Zimbabwe, is
saddled with a $10 billion debt, with the government owing the
parastatal about $3.4 billion.
Dube said the response to the GMB's storage and handling system from
the public, business and farming community had been very positive.
"The storage rates have been most reasonable and allow interested
parties to store their commodities in excellent condition, which is
guaranteed at reasonable rates, thereby enabling them to realise
better returns," said Dube.
The parastatal boasts of a number of modern storage facilities
throughout the country, most of which were underutilised until the
GMB decided to lease them out.
Agricultural commodities handled and stored by the GMB include white
and yellow maize, soya beans, rice, wheat, groundnuts and white
sorghum.
The GMB also fumigates crops for some commercial and communal farmers
around the country for a fee.
The GMB submitted a turnaround strategy aimed at restructuring the
company's operations to the government two months back. The
government has in the past taken over the parastatal's debt but has
not indicated that it would do the same this year.
Early this month, First Banking Corporation was awarded a tender to
raise $4.5 billion for the GMB through short-term tradable bills and
bonds.

Original_Search_Title: FAO Highlights Concern for Pastoralists
Headline: Sudan: FAO Highlights Concern for Pastoralists
Body_Text:
The overall food outlook for Sudan this year was highly precarious and the
purchasing power of pastoralists, in particular, had been seriously
eroded, the UN Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) reported on Monday.
In a special report from its Global Information and Early Warning System
(GIEWS), now accessible on the internet, the agency said the latest
estimate of the number of people in need of urgent food assistance,
because of drought or conflict, or both, stood at 2.97 million.
The situation was particularly acute for pastoralists, with the drought
having caused a substantial increase in the market supply of livestock,
depressing prices and thus household incomes. Lower cereal harvests in
1999 and 2000, and subsequent depletion of stocks, meant that prices
remained well above average through 2000 and 2001, the GIEWS report
stated. The terms of trade, or relative exchange value, of livestock
vis-a-vis grain had "deteriorated sharply", it said. For instance, in
March 2001 the quantity of sorghum (a staple food) that could be bought
through the local sale of a sheep was 300 percent down on March 2000. The
terms of trade for goats vis-a-vis sorghum had declined by more than 200
percent in the same period, it added.
In more general terms, with only a fraction of the appeal for
international food assistance pledged so far and the lean season ahead
(with the next harvest not due until October/November), the food security
situation was likely to worsen in the coming months, according to the FAO.
The populations most affected by the effects of last year's drought were
mainly located in greater Darfur and Kordofan, Bahr al-Ghazal, Bahr
al-Jabal, Eastern Equatoria, Jonglei, Red Sea and Butanah Province in
Al-Jazirah State. After anticipated commercial imports of about 1.2
million mt and food aid pledges amounting to 55,000 mt, there was still an
uncovered gap of 157,000 mt, the FAO reported. [for the full GIEWS report,
go to:
http://www.fao.org/WAICENT/faoinfo/economic/giews/english/alertes/sptoc.htm]
Nairobi, 16 May 2001

Original_Search_Title: Nsukka University Marches On At 40
Headline: Nigeria: Nsukka University Marches On At 40
Body_Text:
Lagos — At 40, University of Nigeria, Nsukka, is a celebration of monumental
success, a story of indigenous effort at self-discovery and a story
of Africa's capability for excellence.
The University of Nigeria Nsukka, UNN, recently celebrated its 40
years of existence. It was a celebration of monumental success. A
story of indigenous effort at self discovery. A story of Africa's
capability for excellence.
Series of activities held to mark the 40th anniversary of the
establishment of the university (1960-2000) provided opportunities
for the institution to take stock of its past and prepare on agenda
for the new millennium. From April 20 - 28, 2001, activities lined up
for the celebrations made the university environment in Nsukka look
like a bee-hive.
The activities include a press conference held in Lagos by the vice-
chancellor, Ginigeme Mbanefo, a professor, in conjunction with the
University of Nigeria Alumni Association, UNAA. There was an
Education Summit held at the University premises, April 25.
The highpoint of the anniversary ceremonies was a special convocation
for the conferment of degrees and certificates, held at Nsukka, April
28. The ceremony was proceeded by a convocation lecture, by Pascal G.
Dozie, Chairman of Diamond Bank; award of special honours and
fund-raising ceremonies, all held at Nike Lake Resort Hotel, Enugu.
In all these, UNAA, played key roles, under the able leadership of
Ezigbo Okoro, an architect.
UNAA also played a key role in providing veritable ground for the
self appraisals UNN undertook. One of the platforms UNAA provided for
the exercise was the education summit held April 25. Here, Fabian
Osuji, a professor of zoology, who is the pro-chancellor and UNN's
chairman of governing council spoke the mind of the university
authorities.
In an eight-page comments he presented on the occasion, Osuji
explained that the 40th anniversary celebrations of the university
provided the institution a unique opportunity to re-focus and
reposition itself into the 21st century and the third millennium
Given his background, as a product of, and participant in, the
Nigerian university system for over three decades, Osuji was amply
qualified to dissect the problems of UNN. He was part of the
administrative machinery in University of Ibadan and Imo State
University before he was appointed pro-chancellor for UNN.
He identified a number of things that made UNN unique. Among these is
that UNN started in October 1960, as an autonomous, indigenous
institution of higher learning, complete with a governing council,
senate convocation and congregation. With this status, it was able to
design its own curriculum, awarded its own certificates, degrees and
diplomas without reference to any outside university or institution.
Also, Osuji noted that UNN started with academic and professional
programmes some of which were previously considered alien to
university concept in Nigeria, as represented by the University
College at Ibadan, which up till 1962 awarded the degrees of the
University of London. He listed the courses, as architecture,
accountancy, land surveying, banking and finance, estate management,
food science and nutrition, home science, journalism, fine art and
even engineering. "These were regarded as belonging to the realms of
polytechnic education in the British type of education which Nigeria
inherited. The university of Nigeria started these courses from the
onset, regardless of mounting criticisms of, and cynicism about their
introduction," he said.
In spite of the pace setter roles UNN played in the past, Osuji
believes there is urgent need for reappraisal if the university must
remain in the forefront of the academia in the millennium. In his
words: "Whereas the UNN was unique and special in 1960 when compared
with Ibadan, the only other university type of institution at the
time, it is not so unique today when compared with the other 40 or
more universities, all of which offer the same programmes of study,
with the same large theoretical content and low practical contest."
Osuji pointed out also that UNN is now saddled with the extra burden
of competing with 23 other federal universities for funding. The
imperative of the situation, according to him, is for UNN to
re-discover itself and reclaim at least some of its special areas and
programme of distinction. He cited areas that could be explored by
UNN.
First, he talked about experimenting with the style of American Land
Grant Universities. He also talked about experimenting with
environmental studies, including landscape architecture or trying
hands on sciences and engineering programmes. The idea, according to
UNN's pro-chancellor, is to produce more practical and more
hands-on-scientists and engineers, ready for the 21st century.
He also suggested delving more into humanities and social sciences to
provide a more humane appreciation of Nigeria's indigenous arts and
social structure. In all these, Osuji said "emphasis must now shift
from number of graduates produced in how many disciplines to what
quality of graduates, what practical skills are imparted by the
various programme we offer." He said that is the challenge before UNN
at 40.
Mbanefo, the V-C agrees with Osuji. He made supportive submissions on
the way forward during the anniversary celebrations.
Agreeing that fundamental progress has been made since the past 40
years, Mbanefo gave details in a press briefing on April 18th in
Lagos.
Mbanefo also pointed out the level of progress recorded in academic
programmes. "From a modest beginning in 1960 with six faculties and
three institutes, the university has grown within 40 years to 14
faculties and nine institutes and centres, with a total of 102
academic departments and sub- departments," he explained.
There has also been tremendous increase in the population of staff
and students. The university is said to have started with 220
students. Today, it has about 26,000 regular students in various
disciplines. The staff strength at present stands roughly at 1,100
academic staff, 1,300 senior administrative staff, 1,000 technical
staff and 2,600 junior staff.
Many staff of the university have also been associated with
scientific break- through. Among others, a research team which worked
under the leadership of A.N.U. Obi, a professor, developed a potent
and safe cholera vaccine. In 1982, a team of scientists from the
university, led by N. Okafor, a professor in microbiology department
produced Dawa Beer with 100 percent sorghum. The breakthrough formed
the basis for the production of Femos beer.
Also, E.U. Odigbo, a professor led a team of agricultural engineers
to produce a number of farm machinery for the full mechanisation of
cassava, from planting to garrification. It is also to the credit of
UNN that the university's college of medicine became the first in the
country to perform an open-heart surgery in 1974. The college has
since been designated as centre of excellence for cardiothoracic
surgery by the federal government. But the university is faced with a
number of problems which has tended to mar much of the progress the
university would have recorded. Initially, there was the problem of
leadership. Mbanefo told Newswatch that when he assumed office, he
met a university community which was so divided into caucuses and
groups that many even refused to say good morning to each other for
years. Through a "peace and reconciliation committee" he set up soon
after, he said a number of the problems have been resolved.
He believes the university is also lucky to have a new crop of
leadership who will assist in unity and progress in the univesity. He
said these include Ogiame Atunwatse II, the Olu of Warri who is the
chancellor, Fabian Osuji who is the pro-chancellor and chairman of
governing council. Mbanefo is the vice- chancellor. He operates with
three deputy vice-chancellors. The DVC's who are all professors
include, C.C. Chukwu (administration), R.O. Ogbuji (academic) and
F.O. Okafor (Enugu campus). The registrar is Grace I. Adichie, O.I.
Ukonu, acting bursar, while Eruenike Ikegbune is the university
librarian.
The university is also facing problem of having a number of decaying
and abandoned infrastructures crying for rehabilitation. Some of them
are carry- overs from the civil war. "When war ended in 1970, the
university community was a ghost of its glorious early years. The
scars of that have remained to date," Mbanefo said.
He identified the problematic areas as severe deterioration of
existing facilities - academic and public buildings, student hostels
and staff houses. Also in the category are many stranded projects
that have remained unattended to in the past 15 years, serious
environmental degradation and inadequate spaces - for academic
activities, recreational facilities, inadequate office spaces,
inadequate hostel accommodation, etc.
There is no doubt that money is the solution expected. Mbanefo said
the university is shopping for about N5 billion to enable it complete
abandoned projects and keep itself ready and prepared for the
challenges of the new millennium. For a university that has been a
pace setter and source of pride to the country and encouragement to
young universities, a university truly committed to the restoration
of the dignity of man, as expected of it by its founding fathers,
especially, the late Nnamdi Azikiwe, Owelle of Onitsha and first
indegenous president of Nigeria, it is expected that governments at
local, state and federal levels and all lovers of academic excellence
would come to its rescue. It is indeed a clarion call on president
Olusegun Obasanjo who is the visitor to the University to come to
UNN's rescue.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Situation "Fast Approaching Critical" - Unicef
Headline: Sudan: Drought Situation "Fast Approaching Critical" - Unicef
Body_Text:
The situation in Sudan's drought belt is "fast approaching that of the
mid-80s and early-90s when water, food and pasture shortages in the
drought belt led to a great number of deaths of people and livestock,
large-scale [population] displacements and tribal conflicts," according to
a UNICEF donor update. The drought situation has already worsened
particularly in the drought belt areas of western, central and southern
Sudan, but April-September would be the worst period, UNICEF reported on 4
May. Most seasonal streams and hafir dams [surface water reservoirs] had
dried up, food supplies were running out and diseases were spreading due
to contamination by livestock of water sources, it said, adding that some
600,000 people were at risk of starvation. In Western Darfur State (among
the worst affected areas, along with Northern Darfur and Kordofan states),
the price of sorghum, a staple grain, was two to three times its normal
price at this time of year, it added. The government has now recognised
the existence of a crisis, but there is concern over the inadequacy of
information on drought requirements and on the authorities' plans to
respond, according to humanitarian sources.
The estimated food requirement to address the drought situation in Darfur
and Kordofan alone is almost 48,000 mt, but food stocks are running low
and the UN agencies' consolidated appeal for some US 194 million is just
21 percent funded so far, according to humanitarian sources. The duration
of the humanitarian emergency in Sudan and uncertainty about the exact
situation and needs of the country may be among the reasons for donors'
apparent reluctance to commit funding even in the face of a new crisis,
they added.
Meanwhile, armed conflict remained a key impediment to improving the
situation of women and children in Sudan, and children would continue to
be deprived until agreement was reached to end Sudan's main conflict -
between the government and the southern-based Sudan People's Liberation
Movement/Army (SPLM/A) - and the various tertiary conflicts, UNICEF
reported on 4 May. Vaccine-preventable diseases remained a threat, with
immunisation cover around 40 percent nationwide, but much lower in some
states, such as Western Darfur, where it stands at only 8 percent. "Until
such time as accord is reached to end the main conflict and
secondary/tertiary conflicts among the numerous warring groups, children
in Sudan will continue to be deprived of their basic rights to health,
education and other services," the UN children's agency added.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Inhabitants Trade Sweat for Food
Headline: Senegal: Rural Inhabitants Trade Sweat for Food
Body_Text:
Dakar, Senegal — Unlike the Iraqis who traded oil for food
and the Soviets in the early 90s had to trade personal jewellery,
including wedding rings, for apples, the inhabitants of a tiny
Senegalese village close to Ross Bethio, 300 km from Dakar, have
traded sweat for food.
Travelling along with the just-ended parliamentary campaign
convoy that took Senegal's President Abdoulaye Wade nation-wide, a
crowd of over 500 women and children struck my attention in the heart
of a clean, dry and arid settlement along the way.
Frantically disembarking the PANA press vehicle two journalists -
a reporter and a photographer - approached the crowd.
A slender and light-skinned man in his late forties suddenly
accosted the two men. Before the contact, the journalists had not seen
him as he had strategically concealed himself behind a tree.
He jetted directly into our direction from his observatory with a
long shepherd's rod in his left hand and enquired about our mission.
After telling him that we were journalists gathering information
that could lead to the further development of the inhabitants of the
village, he appeared very keen to help as he led us towards the women.
The women had gathered over a few dozen sacks of sorghum that was
being distributed by a slim and very fair-skinned village headwoman
with a pointed-nose in her late fifties.
She introduced herself only as Fatma in eloquent French and
welcomed us to take pictures and feel free to ask any questions.
Examining the bags of sorghum more closely, I could make out a
faded US flag imprinted on the bags. Thus my first question was: "Were
you given this food by the US embassy?"
"No, this is payment from the World Food Programme,"
Fatma responded.
" And I know your next question is, 'How did we get these bags of
sorghum,'" she said, taking words right out of my mouth.
"Well..." she started speaking and posed for a moment, as she
threw a wide gaze around us.
"You see, everyone of us in this village, I mean women, have over
the last two days been working very hard to clean our surroundings.
Today we are sharing the fruits of that labour," Fatma said.
She explained that a team of World Food Programme officials
during the course of the week had stopped by and promised to
compensate them with sorghum if they could undertake a clean-up
campaign of their village.
"We were very happy to do so, firstly because as you can see, the
land is very dry around here and it is difficult to grow crops.
"Secondly, it is to our advantage because we worked for ourselves
and for our health and, as such, I mobilised the women and children
over the last two days. So you can see we have very decent
surroundings now," Fatma said proudly.
She said that before the cleaning exercise, the village was
littered with animal dung, and non-biodegradable plastic bags that
were thrown around by careless passengers in passing vehicles.
"We have done everything to stop the passers-by from littering
our village but it has been in vain," she added.
As she spoke, my colleague took some shots of the group in their
new-look surroundings.
Several of them were busy loading their share of the sorghum on
donkey-drawn carts while others transported it on their heads and
across their shoulders to their respective homes, while others stood
with bare chests breastfeeding their kids.
"We need this food badly," another woman told the photographer.
"We are very poor here as you can see for yourself."
She said most of the households had very little to eat. She said
they would pound the sorghum and feed their infants with it. "They
like it very much and it is very healthy for adults, too," she added.
"We are very grateful to the World Food Programme, and we hope
you could pass our compliments to them and entice others to come to
our aid.
"But the most important thing we need here besides food is
potable water... We travel long distances here only to fetch stagnant
water from ponds infested with frogs and toads," she lamented.
If the World Food Programme is listening to these pleas, the
message is that the villagers are ready to embark on another food for
work project that would provide them with potable water within the
village.
Such a development would leave all the parties involved - the
toads and the villagers alike - have a happier separate existence.

Original_Search_Title: Armyworms Ruin Crops At Schemes
Headline: Kenya: Armyworms Ruin Crops At Schemes
Body_Text:
Armyworms have wiped out the entire sorghum and maize crops on West Pokot's Turkwell irrigation scheme.
The beasts have also attacked Katilu, Kainuk and Kapelibok irrigation schemes along River Turkwell.
Lodwar agricultural officer Simon Mutai said 625 hectares had been destroyed on the scheme.
However, the Agriculture Department has managed to stop the spread to other irrigation schemes.
Mr Mutai said that farmers on the affected schemes had received seeds to start replanting.
A number of organisations, including World Vision, the Christian Children's Fund and the Anglican Church, were helping the department to eradicate the pest by providing chemicals and seeds to farmers.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 18 - Africa
Headline: Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 18 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: (A) Southern Africa: (1) Angola, (2) Namibia
(E) Horn of Africa: (1) Ethiopia, (2) Eritrea, (3) Kenya (B) Niger
(C) Burundi
From Francesco Strippoli, Senior Humanitarian Adviser; available on
the Internet on the WFP Home Page (www.wfp.org), or by e-mail from
Zlatan.Milisic@wfp.org. For information on resources, donors are
requested to contact Valerie.Sequeira@wfp.org at WFP Rome, telephone
39 06 6513 2009. Media queries should be directed to
Trevor.Rowe@wfp.org, telephone 39 06 6513 2602. The address of WFP is
Via Cesare Giulio Viola 68, Parco dei Medici, 00148 Rome, Italy.
(A) Southern Africa: (1) Angola, (2) Namibia
(1) Angola
a) During this week, the security situation remained unstable in most
provinces. Arrivals of new IDPs were reported in the provinces of
Benguela, Bie, Huila, Kuanza Norte, Malange, Moxico and Lunda Sul.
Attacks, road ambushes or mine accidents were reported in the
provinces of Benguela, Bie, Kuando Kubango, Lunda Sul and Moxico. In
Huambo Province, police units angered by late payment of their
salaries caused fire shots and general disruption, resulting in the
death of one person.
b) No changes are reported on the status of the Cambambe II IDPs and
their re-settlement, in Bengo Province. The number of children at the
Nutritional Center has reportedly increased, probably due to the
temporary suspensions of humanitarian activities. Outbreaks of
measles, diarrhea and conjunctivitis have also been reported among
young IDPs.
c) The Provincial Government reports that it has assisted 3,306
families affected by floods in Dombe Grande, in Benguela Province,
distributing 63 tons of food (two weeks ration). Beneficiaries
include 1,851 IDPs at the Senje, Luacho, Muaningo and Seco camps. The
water level is expected to decrease in the next days. Numbers at the
Benguela TFC and the Cubal SFC both reported increases from last
week.
d) SFCs and TFCs are becoming overcrowded in Kuito, in Bie Province
due to the continuing arrival of children from Camacupa and Kuemba.
The majority of the parents accompanying their children are said to
be moderately to severely malnourished. WFP continues to investigate
the situation through its partners MSF-B and Caritas. WFP has already
taken action to provide additional support to vulnerable people in
the area through an expansion of its community kitchens with Caritas,
the supply of food for an MSF-run supplementary feeding centre for
malnourished children, and the distribution of monthly rations for
the families of the children in the SFC. A joint mission with OCHA
and several NGO partners is planned for the near future.
e) A joint mission visited 9 districts in Luabango municipality
(Luanda province) in order to assess the living conditions of people
who had been affected by the recent floods and found that 300 homes
had been destroyed. A second joint mission (made of WFP, OCHA,
UNICEF, NRC, OKIOS, ACCORD and MINARS) assessed the flood damage in
the municipality of Quipungo. Out of the 3 districts visited,
Massumba appears to be the hardest hit area with a total of 150
houses destroyed. The Mission recommended that non-food items be
supplied.
f) WFP and its implementing partners decided that an evaluation of
all social programs in Huila province would be carried out in May and
findings will be used to define the strategy for the next quarter.
NGOs were encouraged to present FFW projects as under the new PRRO
general rations will be decreased and self- sufficiency promoted.
g) The First UN food security assessment mission in over a year
(composed of WFP, OCHA, MINANDER and Trabalho Familha
Desenvolvimento) traveled to Caiundo, in Kuando Kubango Province.
5,000 IDPs arrived in Caiundo at the end of 1999 and early 2000. From
interviews with IDPs and residents, the mission estimated that maize,
sorghum and beans stocks would last for four to five months.
h) Government's mission to Cambambe/Dondo in Kwanza Norte Province
finds 1,574 people affected by the floods, which is well below the
21,815 people originally reported in a local administration document.
So far neither WFP nor any of its partners have received requests for
assistance from the government. The Provincial Sub-Group for
registration will travel to Dondo on 7 May after which more
information will be provided. World Vision successfully distributed
60 tons of food to 3,777 IDPs in Lucala.
i) Approximately 1,400 beneficiaries were assisted with FFW this week
in Kuanza Sul province. The present FFW projects in the province
include ? family and community farms and rehabilitation of roads and
general infrastructure. It is reported that the FFW projects are
moving along very well.
j) Registration of flood victims in Namibe was completed. On 2 May
distribution to all 31,956 flood victims in Namibe for this month was
completed.
k) Despite new contributions, the new PRRO is still funded only at
5.5 percent, with, carry over stocks and contributions from the
previous PRRO bringing it up to 33 percent. Major breaks in the
Pipeline are envisaged for the months of May (Pulses) and June
(Maize). WFP is currently purchasing locally some commodities to
temporarily cover parts of the shortfalls.
(2) Namibia
a) Serious Pipeline difficulties are being faced for all commodities
due a lack of funding for the Namibia EMOP. Reduced rations have been
given to IDPs over the past few months and will need to be further
reduced if Donor pledges do not increase. General distribution of CSB
has stopped completely to allow for it's continued use in the SFC. At
the same time numbers have increased at the Osire Camp with UNHCR
reporting that numbers at the camp have reached 20,000.
(B) Horn of Africa: (1) Ethiopia, (2) Eritrea, (3) Kenya
(1) Ethiopia
a) Significant progress with regard to the Temporary Security Zone
(TSZ) has been reported in the UN Security Council by the Under
Secretary-General for peacekeeping, Mr. Jean-Marie Guehenno. The TSZ
has marked the formal separation of forces of Ethiopia and Eritrea
and has allowed immediate restoration of Eritrean civil
administration and return of displaced persons to the buffer zone.
However, both parties sought adjustments to the southern boundary of
the TSZ.
b) The security situation in Addis Ababa, though apparently normal,
is still tense due to a combination of factors, including the recent
hijacking of a military aircraft to Sudan by military academy cadets,
the unknown status of the detained opposition leaders and the
dissatisfaction among the university students about resolution of
their demands, which led to violent protests two weeks ago.
c) Rains resumed for a short period in parts of Amhara region, which
have eased the fear that the dry spell in early April would affect
Belg crops. In Tigray, rains were reported to have stopped during the
week before last and very hot weather prevails in the region. It
seems that land preparation in Tigray for the upcoming main rainy
season is better than last year. However, there were no rains in
south Tigray where populations are partially dependent on Belg crops.
d) While substantial rains recently fell in south-eastern Somali
region, this Gu rain has not yet arrived in Segeg and Dihun of Fik
zone, where populations are extremely vulnerable. A recent nutrition
survey in the worst affected areas of Fik indicates worsening food
security conditions. There is very serious concern over the situation
in Denan, Gode zone, also awaiting rain, where nutritional status
among children under five is declining. DPPC has agreed to take
action to increase general food rations at this location, which had
been severely cut back over recent months. Other locations in Gode
zone have been receiving sufficient rations, though there on-going
complaints from administrators in various parts of Somali region
about decreased rations. Pasture availability still remains
sufficient in most of the region, and the current rain will ease the
seasonal problem of long distances between pasture and water.
e) A DPPC early warning department mission is currently underway to
check the situation of the drought displaced in Fafen, Jijiga zone
and near the refugee camps in Hartisheik, Kebrebeyah and Harshin. A
WFP monitor is accompanying the team. A nutritional survey among the
drought displaced in Fafen was completed by the Mother and Child
Development Organization (MCDO), in collaboration with DPPB and
UNICEF. The preliminary results are such that MCDO is reopening its
supplementary feeding centre using food from DPPB. Nutritional
surveys are also planned in the coming days in Hartisheik, where MCDO
will be opening a therapeutic feeding center.
f) In Amhara region, a disaster assessment in Gubalafto has been
completed and the mission has confirmed a food crisis in the area,
largely due to a significant reduction in the number of beneficiaries
as compared to numbers identified by the December needs assessment
team. Based on the team's findings and recommendations DPPC will now
be sending more food to this area.
g) The special operation for the Djibouti port was completed and
handed over on 18 April 2001.
(2) Eritrea
a) During the last two weeks of April, the Government partner in food
distributions (ERREC) reported distribution of 8,643 tons of
WFP-supplied food to 526,160 beneficiaries in Eritrea.
b) WFP conducted Post Distribution Monitoring (PDM) in Anseba and
Debub regions. Reports confirmed that food aid reached the intended
beneficiaries even though planned days covered by the rations were
less as a result of a redistribution to extended family members. The
PDM also indicated that children consume about 50 percent of the
family ration. WFP noted a lack of milling machines forcing women to
spend up to 30 percent of their time using traditional milling
methods. Food aid and kinship support appeared to be the principal
food sources. The team recommended that food aid should be provided
to the adult literacy programmes conducted in the area to improve
attendance and retain concentration levels of the students. There
were no signs of malnutrition; certainly an impact of WFP's
assistance to the region.
c) The dry season continued during the last 2 weeks with no
significant rainfall throughout the country, resulting in a rapid
deterioration of the condition of the livestock herds.
d) WFP stock as of 30th April 2001 amounted to 35,183 tons of food.
Following the confirmation of two new contributions, both the drought
and war EMOPs were 100% resourced, as of 24th April 2001.
e) New EMOP 10049 (Assistance to victims of drought) war approved in
April.
f) Following the formal announcement to the establishment of the TSZ
on the 18th of April, and although ERREC has not officially commenced
the repatriation of IDPs, the UN convened a meeting to prepare an
action plan. The importance of completing the repatriation process in
time for the cultivation season, while minimizing the exposure of the
IDPs to landmines has been emphasized. WFP has made enough provisions
for the feeding of the IDPs, whether they are inside or outside of
the TSZ. ICRC is still providing WFP supplied wheat to 45,000
beneficiaries inside the TSZ and will probably continue through May
2001.
g) The WFP/CARE Logistics PLUS project commenced on the 23rd April
2001. This project aims at assessing logistics capacities and systems
in Eritrea and based on the findings, design a training curriculum
for WFP and ERREC staff.
(3) Kenya
a) Reasonable levels of rainfall have been reported in most parts of
Kenya, but the food security of pastoralists remains precarious.
b) The Food Aid Estimates Sub Group (FAS) of the Kenya Food Security
Steering Group (KFSSG) advised that the April distribution should not
take place due to delays occasioned by slow completion of the March
distribution and disruptions in food transportation, caused by bad
weather and by transport company personnel striking over payment
issues. The May general distribution will go ahead.
c) Two new pledges were received during the reporting period from
Ireland and Switzerland, for the purchase of Corn Soya Blend. A
donation of 11,602 tons of maize was received from Kenya, which will
enable full cereal and pulses rations to be supplied to all districts
due to receive general distribution in May. Due to pipeline
shortages, there is currently no oil or blended food. Due to a delay
in the arrival of a 30,000 tons shipment, There will be probably a
shortfall in June. The possibility of locally purchasing cereal
supplies from the Government is currently being investigated.
d) A total of 2,408,040 people are being targeted for the May general
distribution, as in March. The EMOP continues to target 1,109,635
school children under the Expanded School Feeding Programme (ESFP).
The total number of beneficiaries is therefore 3,517,675. Under the
FFW scheme, it is estimated that around 72,000 people will be
indirect beneficiaries of WFP food, as the scheme will operate in 6
districts. Food for Work (FFW) project identification teams visited
four districts during the last two weeks (Kitui, Mbeere, Tharaka and
Makueni) in order to gain an understanding of areas of priority for
FFW activities. WFP is currently identifying NGOs in Machakos and
Kitui Districts to work as partners. WFP refined its FFW Guidelines,
in particular the work norms and the reporting system. Donors were
also informed of the progress of FFW activities, to encourage funding
of the project.
e) WFP-supplied supplementary rations are being distributed to
particulary vulnerable people simultaneously with general
distribution rations in the ten districts of Turkana, Mandera, Wajir,
Samburu, Marsabit, West Pokot, Garissa, Tana River, Isiolo and
Moyale. The pipeline situation being poor, CARE and World Vision have
been supplying additional food commodities in some districts.
f) Tribal tensions and banditry in Mandera and Tana River Districts
have been causing disturbances, including road mining and ambushes of
commercial vehicles transporting food. During the last 2 weeks of
April, the number of refugees from Bula Hawa has reportedly increased
in Mandera town. UNHCR is currently undertaking an assessment
mission. Kenyan troops recently sent to Mandera to reinforce the
regular garrison have reportedly been withdrawn in order to diffuse
tensions.
(C) Niger
a) WFP approved on the 2 May an Immediate Response Emergency
Operation for the canton of Damagaram Takaya in the region of Zinder
which is due to start next week. WFP is planning to distribute 430
tons of millet through school children and their family under the
responsibility of its sub Offices. The NGO and the Government
intervening in the area have assessed a total number of affected
populations at 30,675 in the department. The present Immediate
Response Emergency will concern 7,170 beneficiaries over a phase of
30 days. It should be later integrated and be part of a larger WFP
Emergency Operation. The commodities will be borrowed mainly from
in-country stocks and will be reimbursed when the first commodities
of the Immediate Response Emergency are delivered.
b) Further to a severe drought affecting the rainfall crops in the
region, cereal stocks were very low and prices have increased by 50
percent in March. Interventions implemented until now by the
Government have not succeeded in solving the problem and no other
NGO/bilateral donors are intervening in the canton. Reports from
Government and WFP indicate extremely insecure food situation likely
to cause additional exile of families to Nigeria and the cities of
Zinder, Maradi and Niamey. Abnormal moderate and severe malnutrition
have been reported in the region and minimal number of mothers have
given birth to stillborns. The response will contribute to provide
deprived school children and their families with the cereals to stay
in the area before the rainfall starts.
(D) Burundi
a) The security situation remained volatile in many provinces, with a
number of reports of confrontations in Gitega and Ruyigi provinces as
well as incursions in the South and the Eastern borders with
Tanzania. Main in-country roads were repeatedly closed to UN missions
due to insecurity, killings and looting in Cankuzo province. People
living in insecure zones have been regularly displaced. WFP lost a
national staff member in a road accident in Bujumbura town, and two
others were wounded.
b) WFP Implementing Partners, CARE and World Vision are currently
carrying out distributions in Bujumbura Rural, Muyinga and Karuzi
provinces. Populations displaced by hostilities from Gitega to Mwaro
province have been continuing to move and could not therefore receive
WFP food assistance.
c) During this week, WFP distributed 15-days rations to 260,970
people in Bujumbura Rural, Kirundo, Bubanza, Muyinga and Gitega
provinces, delivering a total of 2,131 tons of food. In addition, 27
primary schools in Kirundo province benefited from 92 tons of food
supplied for 20 days under the school feeding pilot project.
d) Food Economy Assessment Teams conducted surveys of emergency
assistance needs for victims of recent confrontations between rebels
and the army in 6 communes in Muramvya and Mwaro provinces. They were
however unable to access one commune in Muramvya province due to
insecurity. Results will be reported soon.
Note: All tonnage figures in this report refer to metric tons
(End WFP Emergency Report No 18)

Original_Search_Title: Border Committee to Settle Demarcation Issue
Headline: Sudan: Border Committee to Settle Demarcation Issue
Body_Text:
The Sudanese-Ethiopian border committee will present an agreement to a
joint ministerial meeting to be held in the Ethiopian capital, Addis
Ababa, between 30 April and 3 May. The recently established
Sudanese-Ethiopian committee had been commended for its "positive results"
by Sudanese Foreign Minister Dr Mustafa Uthman Isma'il, official Sudanese
television said on Wednesday. Isma'il held talks with the governor of
Al-Qadarif State, northeastern Sudan, Prof Al-Amin Daf'allah, who heads
the Sudanese side of the committee. The governor of Al-Qadarif said the
two sides had agreed on a number of issues, particularly border trade,
customs duty, and security.
Sudan and Ethiopia were eager to "develop and integrate" Al-Qadarif State
with Tigray State in northern Ethiopia, an official Sudanese source said.
The two regions were agriculturally productive, with Al-Qadarif,
considered to be the "bread basket" of Sudan, producing some 60 percent of
the nation's sorghum, the source said. The existing road from Al-Qadarif
to Mekele, the capital of Tigray, is being repaired and upgraded. Free
movement of people and goods has been encouraged. Plans for Ethiopia to
provide Al-Qadarif State with electric power were being considered, the
source said. The official said Tigray State would benefit through having
better access to the Red Sea, as the road to Al-Qadarif connected to Port
Sudan. "That makes Port Sudan closer to Mekele than the Eritrean port of
Massawa or the Somali port of Berbera," the official said. Tigray State
used the Eritrean port of Massawa until the Ethiopian-Eritrean border war
began in 1998.
The colonial border between northern Sudan and Ethiopia was "not
demarcated", and some areas, including plantations, had been disputed
between the two countries, the official explained. Good relations between
Ethiopia and Sudan had encouraged the two countries to settle the issue at
this time, with a joint committee headed by the governors of the two
states, the official said.

Original_Search_Title: N74m Spent On Fertiliser And Farm Inputs - Commissioner Kano
Headline: Nigeria: N74m Spent On Fertiliser And Farm Inputs - Commissioner Kano
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Kano State government has spent about N74.1 million to purchase
fertiliser and other farm inputs between 1999 and 2001, the state
Commissioner for Agriculture, Alhaji Yusuf Ado Kibiya, has said.
Yusuf told newsmen in Kano yesterday that 4,077.75 of the 4,800
tonnes, allocated to the state during the 1999 cropping season, were
actually received while 810 of the 15,907 tonnes allocated to the
state for the 1999/2000 dry season farming scheme, were also
received.
He said that N1.5 million was spent by the state government to revive
production and that it had purchased 30 tonnes of improved wheat
seeds which were distributed to participating farmers in the state.
Yusuf revealed that N2 million was spent on the purchase of
Agro-chemicals and improved seeds for its Bagauda multiplication
farms, explaining that selected crops, such as groundnut, sorghum,
millet and cowpea, were treated under the programme.

Original_Search_Title: Value of Crops Traded On Zimace to Remain Static
Headline: Zimbabwe: Value of Crops Traded On Zimace to Remain Static
Body_Text:
Harare — The value of commodities traded on the Zimbabwe Agricultural
Commodities Exchange (ZIMACE) in 2001 should remain static at last
year's $2.2 billion, in spite of a decline in output in the country's
dominant agricultural sector, a ZIMACE official said this week.
ZIMACE administrator Ian Goggin said although it was difficult to
forecast trading figures for this year because of uncertainty over
crop output in 2001, trading was likely to remain static at last
year's figures.
The commodities ex-change trades primarily in maize, wheat and soya
bean and also handles sunflower, groundnuts, sorghum and other
grains. Until this year, volumes traded had grown by an average 35
percent annually.
"The volume of commodities to be traded is linked to production
figures, but is extremely difficult to predict with any degree of
certainty. I however do believe that trade figures are likely to be
similar to those of last year," Goggin told the Financial Gazette.
Last year the bourse handled 250 000 tonnes of commodities valued at
$2.2 billion. Goggin said although there would be a reduction in
volumes to be traded this year, prices were likely to be high because
of crop shortages.
"Prices are generally dictated by the basis of supply and demand and
this has been reflected on ZIMACE by the upward movement in prices
for most commodities," he said.
A decline in output is expected for wheat and maize, production of
which has been disrupted by the invasion of commercial farms by
supporters of the ruling ZANU PF who have seized more than 1 500
farms across the country.
The 2001 wheat crop is expected to fall from 400 000 tonnes to 200
000 tonnes. The maize crop has also been affected by the dry spell
that swept the country late last year and by incessant rains in early
2001, which wiped out much of the crop.
Goggin however said soya bean production would either remain stagnant
at last season's 140 000 tonnes or surpass that figure and bring some
liquidity into the market.
"While soya bean production is likely to be the same or larger than
last year, the same cannot be said for maize and wheat which will be
well down," he said.
"A large portion of the soya bean crop is destined to be put into
ZIMACE silos and it is anticipated that this will bring further
liquidity to the market by involving financial institutions in
lending funds against commodities stored there."

Original_Search_Title: Massive Cereal Deficit Forecast for Namibia
Headline: Namibia: Massive Cereal Deficit Forecast for Namibia
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Namibia will have to import 208 500 tons of cereal this year after
late rains hit crop production in the country. The Namibia Early
Warning and Food Information System (NEWFIS), in its latest bulletin,
forecasts domestic cereal supply at a mere 90 700 tons this year due
to late rains in the commercial cereal-producing area commonly known
as the 'Maize Triangle' (Grootfontein, Otavi and Tsumeb).
Namibia has a domestic cereal utilisation capacity of 299 200 tons.
Cereal- producing areas received above normal rainfall in October,
but the subsequent three months were marked by below normal rainfall.
In February, the rainfall pattern changed for the better when the
areas recorded normal rain and the favourable conditions continued up
to the third half of March. NEWFIS said that as a result of the late
onset of rainfall, planting of crops in December was much below
average and in several places no planting took place at all."
"Only at the beginning of February 2001, with good rains, did
planting and replanting in few cases start in earnest and, with
continued favourable rainfall, continued through March," it said.
The Government unit said the expected national coarse grain
production was tentatively calculated at 64 800 tons - comprising 19
900 tons of white maize and 44 900 tons of mahangu/sorghum. In
addition, the winter wheat crop to be harvested in November/December
was preliminary forecasted to be 4 000 tons and brought the expected
total national cereal production to 68 800 tons which represented an
output of about 49 per cent of last season's production (140 000
tons).
It was also only 19 per cent higher than 1994/95 drought season's
cereal production of 57 800 tons. It said that as of May 1, the
operating stocks of the millers were forecasted at 10 000 tons of
wheat and 10 000 tons of white maize, while on-farm mahangu and
sorghum stocks were estimated to be 1 000 tons and 900 tons,
respectively.
The forecasted cereal production together with operating stocks give
a total availability of 90 700 tons for 2001/2002 marketing year,
comprising 40 500 tons of mahangu, 29 900 tons of white maize, 14 000
tons of wheat and 6 300 tons of sorghum. Assuming a projected
national population figure of 1,920 million and an average per capita
cereal consumption of 135 kg, the cereal import requirement for this
year, was calculated to be 208 500 tons.
The deficit will be the difference between the domestic cereal supply
of 90 700 tons and domestic utilisation of 299 200 tons."
"Under normal circumstances, this shortfall is always covered by
commercial imports during the course of the marketing year. However,
at this level this import gap is a cause for concern if the crop
situation does not improve during the remaining months of the
season," the early warning unit said.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya's Maize Deficit Stands At 3 Million Bags
Headline: Kenya's Maize Deficit Stands At 3 Million Bags
Body_Text:
The country faces a maize shortfall of nearly three million bags.
The deficit was reduced from eight million bags to three million following the rains in January, the national coordinator of the Arid Lands Resource Management Project, Mr Mahboub Maalim, said yesterday.
He, however, said the situation was not worrying though pastoral communities were in need. "Between now and September, we expect importation of up to one million bags of maize to cater for the deficit," he told the Nation.
Mr Maalim said Kenya requires 2.7 million bags a month, an average of 33 million bags a year.
He said the situation in some districts which had been included in the emergency relief bracket had improved with the onset of the unseasonal January rains
Mr Maalim said some districts that recieved the January unseasonal rains had been dropped out of the list of emergency operations.
"The government is now planning to face out relief operations in these districts and introduce other aspects of development," he said.
Mr Maalim said Northeastern, Eastern, Turkana and Tana River districts did not receive the January unseasonal rains and were still under state of emergency.
He appealed to Kenyans to promote traditional foods like Cassava, millet and sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Act Appeals for $2.4m As Crisis Worsens
Headline: Sudan: Act Appeals for $2.4m As Crisis Worsens
Body_Text:
Geneva — The unending civil war, reckless oil exploitations and a severe
drought is threatening the lives of hundreds of thousands of people
in Sudan, the Action By Churches Together ACT has said here.
In response to this crisis, ACT International has issued three
appeals for a total of US $ 2.4 million for relief work in southern
and western Sudan. ACT members plan to assist the affected people
with food, seeds and tools, shelter, mosquito nets, basic education
and health care.
The UN's World Food Program WFP estimates that some 600,000 people in
Sudan are in urgent need of food assistance while a total of 2.9
million people will need assistance to make it through 2001.
WFP recently warned of an impending crisis in Sudan if assistance is
not delivered in time before Sudan's next rainy season begins in
about a month from now.
Prolonged drought and war related displacement are at the root of the
problems for most of the more than 130,000 people targeted for relief
by ACT members working in Juba in Eastern Equatoria, Bentiu in Upper
Nile, Kadugli and Dilling in the Nuba Mountains, Damazin in Blue Nile
and Wau in Bahr El Ghazal.
Despite the recent harvest in Sudan, food prices on basic commodities
such as sorghum are soaring and have in some areas reached levels
three times higher than what would be expected this time of the year.
Such price hikes leave commercial food totally out of reach of the
poor and destitute.
A particular case is assistance to displaced in Bentiu in Upper Nile
and to people in the Nuba Mountains. Here fighting and raiding
motivated by the government's need to clear oil fields and
installations of the original population, has led to a particular
humanitarian crisis.
If the Sudanese government and its foreign commercial partners
continue their strategy of forced displacement from the areas around
the oil fields, a full scale famine is likely to erupt in parts of
Upper Nile and Bahr El Ghazal.
The ACT appeals all target populations living in areas controlled by
the government of Sudan. ACT said further appeals for people living
in rebel controlled parts of Sudan will be issued at a later stage. ?
SOURCE: ACT, Geneva - For further information please contact: ACT
Communications Officers Nils Carstensen (mobile/cell phone +41 79 358
3171) or ACT Press Officer Rainer Lang (mobile/cell phone + 41 79 681
1868)

Original_Search_Title: West Pokot Farmers to Receive Free Seeds
Headline: Kenya: West Pokot Farmers to Receive Free Seeds
Body_Text:
Farmers in famine-stricken West Pokot District will receive free cereals seed for this years's planting season.
The donation has been made by the government in conjunction with the World Food Programme and the National Council of Churches of Kenya.
The newly posted West Pokot District Commissioner, Mr N. Ole Lankas, said his he had already handed over 38 tonnes of maize and sorghum sees worth more than Sh4.5 million to be distributed to farmers.
"But more donations are coming from the NCCK, NGOs and other churches to benefit more growers," the DC said.
Speaking to the Nation by telephone, the DC said the district had registered poor crop harvests in the past three years due to drought.
"We want farmers to take advantage of the current rains and plant crops to stop over-reliance on relief supplies," he said.
Mr Lankas said everything had been put in place to ensure the seeds reached intended beneficiaries without any interference.
Kitale Anglican Church of Kenya Bishop Stephen Kewasis said in a separate statement the famine situation in West Pokot had worsened despite the onset of the rains.
Bishop Kewasis, who is the North Rift NCCK chairman, said the number of those relying on relief rations had risen from 152,000 and 189,000 in the past two months. The district has an estimated population of 310, 000 people. Speaking in Kapenguria, Bishop Kewasis said efforts were being made to assure the affected families of enough food supplies.
The government had donated 10, 000, 90-kg bags of maize to be distributed to the hardest families in the district this month, he said.
"Last month we distributed about 15,000 bags," he said.
Bishop Kewasis, however, said they were still waiting for lorry-loads of relief rations from WFP to supplement the food supplies. The food from the government, he said, was sourced directly from the Kapenguria National Cereals and Produce Board depot.
Bishop Kewasis said they were still consulting with Mr Ole Lankas on the distribution procedure of the 10,000 bags of maize.
The Bishop named Arlale, Kasei, Sigor, Kacheliba, Chepararia, Kongelai, Chesegon, and lower areas of Lelan and Kapenguria as the most famine hit places.
The district, he said, realised very minimal food production last year, as cultivation has never taken place in most of the areas for two years due to drought.
The food situation in the district is expected to improve towards the end of the year "if the rains continue."
The agricultural officer, Mr P.P. Tingaa, said the district faces a food deficit of 291,378 bags of maize and 114, 000 bags of beans.
Mr Tingaa said only 220, 000 bags of maize and beans were harvested last year and could not sustain locals up to August.

Original_Search_Title: Didiza Decides On Sab Head for Land Bank
Headline: South Africa: Didiza Decides On Sab Head for Land Bank
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — AGRICULTURE and Land Affairs Minister Thoko Didiza has surprised Land
Bank staffers by announcing she has decided to appoint SA Breweries
(SAB) director Monwabisi Fandeso as the bank's new MD.
Sources said a memorandum to this effect by bank chairman Sam
Mkhabela was circulated to all staff members yesterday.
It was unclear yesterday whether Fandeso had accepted the offer. He
could not be reached for comment.
Land Bank chairman Sam Mkhabela confirmed the cabinet had approved
the decision, but said Fandeso had been made only an informal offer.
"Discussions are still in progress. Once they are concluded an
announcement will be made in due course."
Fandeso was viewed as an unlikely candidate because he has neither a
formal banking background nor experience of the agricultural sector.
The frontrunners were seen as acting Land Bank MD Totsie Memela and
Standard Bank executive director Ayanda Mjekula.
Didiza informed Memela on Wednesday that her application was
unsuccessful.
Fandeso, in his early 40s, is currently the operations director of
SAB's northern division. Earlier he managed the company's Rosslyn
brewery, which is the largest in the southern hemisphere.
News reports characterise him as a stickler for detail, thoroughness
and efficiency, ascribing these qualities to his training as a
mechanical engineer.
Born in Port Elizabeth, Fandeso acquired his British Alevels at
Waterford- Kamhlaba School in Swaziland before studying at the
University of Natal and then the University of Wales in Cardiff.
He later studied business administration at the University of
Pennsylvania's Wharton School.
In addition to SAB, he has worked for De Beers in Namaqualand,
Johnson & Johnson in the US, and in Southern Life's strategic
planning division.
His career has not been without hiccups. He was president and CEO of
New Age Beverages, the joint venture with Pepsi Cola International
that foundered in the mid-1990s.
He was appointed MD of Vivo, National Sorghum Breweries' illfated
sortie into the clear beer market, but later resigned.
If Fandeso accepts the job he will be moving into a difficult working
environment. During the selection process Memela was the subject of
an anonymous letter campaign accusing her of nepotism and
mismanagement. Her predecessor, Helena Dolny, was ousted.

Original_Search_Title: War Vets Invade Research Station
Headline: Zimbabwe: War Vets Invade Research Station
Body_Text:
Bulawayo — War veterans have invaded Matobo Research Station near here,
threatening the smooth operations of the International Crop Research
Institute for Semi-Arid Tropics (ICRISAT), provincial governor
Stephen Nkomo said yesterday.
The veterans, said to be coming from in and around Matabeleland
South, moved into the station at the weekend and were yesterday still
keeping a vigil at the institute which boasts prime farming and
grazing land.
Nkomo, the governor and resident minister of Matabeleland South, said
the invasion was illegal and warned the settlers they risked the
wrath of the government if they remained on the property.
"At its ordinary meeting held on April 2 2001 at the Governor's
Office, the provincial land committee, which I chair as governor and
resident minister, resolved that it unreservedly condemns the
unlawful settlements on Matobo Station," he said in a statement given
to this newspaper.
Nkomo, the brother of the late vice president Joshua Nkomo, said the
Matobo Research Station was a strategic government project with
regional links that needed protection from trespassers.
"To this end, my committee will not entertain any illegal settlements
on the said property. Any illegal settlers at Matobo Research Station
are advised to leave with immediate effect. Failure to comply with
this directive will meet the wrath of the law."
The governor said the government was aware that housing stands were
being sold illegally at the station by the invaders and said the
culprits would be dealt with.
"I hope the leaders of Inqama (the veterans) will cooperate and avoid
unnecessary confrontation with the government," he said.
ICRISAT, a crop research institute specialising on small grains, was
established in 1984 and employs 12 international and local scientists
in the fields of crop genetics, resources enhancement, natural
resource management and economics.
Since its inception, it has developed 46 crop varieties as well as
amassed 75 000 gene extracts of stress-tolerant sorghum and millet.
Officials at the research station refused to discuss the invasion
yesterday, saying they feared reprisals from the invaders, some of
whom have closed rural council offices in and outside Matabeleland in
the past two months in the name of purging the offices of opposition
followers.

Original_Search_Title: Federal Government Moves To Stem High Food Prices
Headline: Nigeria: Federal Government Moves To Stem High Food Prices
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Government has ordered the release of 30,000 metric
