estimated revenue by R779 million. Not so sweet," said Manuel. These
measures will take affect from February 21, 2001.
He said that a recent study on smoking trends in South Africa between
1993 and 1999 revealed that aggregate cigarette consumption has
decreased by about 22 percent between 1993 and 1999 and smoking
prevalence among the adult population decreased by about 14 percent
over the period.
"However, the total number of smokers remained constant at
approximately 8 million," said Manuel.
The study found that the average cigarette consumption per smoker
declined by about 21 percent and smoking among young adults is below
the national average and falling.
He said that real price increases, partly due to increased excise
taxes, contributed to these decreases in prevalence and consumption.
"Real price increases appears to be effective at reducing smoking
prevalence among lower income and young people," said Manuel.
Manuel says that the social services - education, health and welfare
- take up about 58 percent of the consolidated national and
provincial non-interest allocations, and will grow steadily over the
Medium Term Expenditure Framework (MTEF) period.
"The largest single redistributive programme of government is the
system of social grants delivered by provincial welfare departments.
These include the old age, disability and child support grants and
provide support to more than three million South Africans every
month," Manuel said.
The maximum old age and disability grants will increase from R540 to
R570 a month in July this year, and the child support grant will
increase from R100 to R110 a month.
Compensation for injuries, disease or death in the course of
employment is currently governed by three Acts falling under the
responsibility of the Ministers of Labour, Minerals and Energy, and
Health respectively. Manuel proposes in his speech to replace these
with a single occupational health and safety statute, under the
Minister of Labour's charge.
The compensation funds are financed through assessed contributions by
employers and provide cover for medical and hospital costs,
compensation for disablement as a result of occupational injuries or
disease and survivor benefits in the event of death.
Health services - hospital construction and operation,
recapitalisation of the state's vaccine institutes and equipment
supplies is one of the areas Manuel proposed for Public Private
Partnerships (PPPs).
"The total value of the [38 potential] projects that have moved
beyond conceptual stage and are at draft feasibility stage or further
is about R9 billion in present value terms," said Manuel.
He also said that the department of health is currently in discussion
with the World Bank on support for its hospital rehabilitation
programme.
Meanwhile, community organisations led by the South African NGO
Coalition, Sangoco, held a protest outside Parliament this morning
ahead of the Budget presentation.
The group, including the South African Council of Churches and the
AIDS lobby group Treatment Action Campaign, are to submit a petition
to the National Treasury demanding greater emphasis in the Budget on
the poor, and less on debt reduction.
Sangoco spokesperson Mercia Andrews says the protesters, who marched
from Cape Town's Parade to Parliament at midday, will also demand
more say in the compilation of the Budget.

Original_Search_Title: K64bn Agro Loans Coming
Headline: Zambia: K64bn Agro Loans Coming
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Agricultural disbursement of the K64 billion announced in this year's
budget for loans and grants to peasant and small scale farmers will
start in June.
Agriculture Permanent Secretary Russel Mulele made the disclosure in
Mongu and Senanga in Western Province at the weekend when he
accompanied acting Finance Minister Syamukayumbu Syamujaye to explain
this year's budget to stakeholders in the province.
'By June/July the schemes should be working and inputs should start
flowing. Due to problems that Western Province has been facing, the
province will be given priority so that by July, all small scale
farmers and peasant ones should have inputs,' Mr. Mulele said.
He said Government would ensure only genuine peasant farmers who
would be able to listen to agricultural extension officers would be
eligible for the grants and loans.
At two meetings held in Mongu and Senanga, the permanent secretary
said the K32 billion would be a grant to households in desperate
situation to produce food and were very disadvantaged or vulnerable.
He said this money would give them some funds to buy inputs through
the Ministry of Community Development.
At least 200,000 people were expected to benefit from the money
expected to be used to purchase maize seed, sorghum, cassava, and
others.
The other K32 billion would be handled by the Ministry of Agriculture
and would be used for loans which should be paid back by the
beneficiaries.
'We will ensure only genuine peasant farmers who will be able to
listen to agricultural extension officers to grow enough for own
consumption and surplus for sale. These should have the ability for
paying back after the season,' he said.
Mr. Mulele announced that Government would investigate reports that
some animals being brought in from neighbouring countries were not
vaccinated yet found themselves branded together with those that had
been vaccinated against diseases like CBPP.
As a result, the spread of diseases could not be controlled.
Dr Syamujaye during question and answer session assured that poverty
reduction was Government's priority in this year's budget as most
resources had been directed towards improvement of infrastructure.
The minister whose sentiments were echoed by Finance Deputy Minister
Godfrey Simasiku, said most district councils were operating below
par and were always in salary arrears because they had no fresh ideas
on revenue collection after the Government empowerment programme of
selling houses to tenants.
He cited Kalomo district which had a reserve of K160 million and no
salary arrears just from funds raised from various levies including
levies from cattle, goats, maize and any other agricultural produce
that was leaving the district.
Mr. Simasiku cited Kabwe where a Japanese funded project has
collapsed after the Japanese had left and in Kalabo where the council
sold a grader at K10 million but no amount has so far been paid and
yet the buyer has collected the vehicle and was now demanding K20
million for what he claims were costs for rehabilitating the grader.

Original_Search_Title: Locusts Invade Farms
Headline: Zimbabwe: Locusts Invade Farms
Body_Text:
Chiredzi, Zimbabwe — Swarms of locusts
have invaded and destroyed crops in southern Zimbabwe
where drought had already drastically reduced yields, government
agriculture officials said on Thursday.
They said the locusts had covered 30 percent of
Chiredzi District, destroying more than 2 600 hectares
of cotton, maize and sorghum.
A government agronomist, Derick Zisengwe, said spray
teams had been dispatched to the area to control the
spread of the locusts, first noticed in small numbers
last week.
"The outbreak is confined to Sangwe communal lands and we
think we have done enough to contain the situation. We
have mobilised farmers to provide labour after we sent
in chemicals," he said.
Chiredzi district is part of Masvingo Province where
more than 200 000 families have registered for
government food assistance because of drought.
Agriculture officials say a persistent dry spell last
month had destroyed much of the crops in the southern
half of the country, which might force Zimbabwe to
import maize, the staple food.

Original_Search_Title: Seeds From Starving Ethiopia Give America Abundant Yields
Headline: Ethiopia: Seeds From Starving Ethiopia Give America Abundant Yields
Body_Text:
Dakar, Senegal — It may sound paradoxical, but as
informed sources at the Global Environment Facility (GEF) assert,
starving Ethiopia could well pass for the world's seed basket!
The very mention of Ethiopia readily evokes sad images of
raging battles and starving children - of a people bereft of the
bare necessities of food, clothing and tranquillity.
The least likely image is that of a nation whose farming
practices help provide food and jobs in places as far away as
Europe, Asia and North America. Yet this largely unknown profile
is a vital part of Ethiopia's complex reality.
Take the United States, for example. According to Girma
Hailu, a former US State Department Regional Environmental
Specialist for East Africa, germ plasma capable of resisting the
gene of the Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus (BYDV), is thought to have
been collected from the Ethiopian barley collection and
introduced into the genetic material of the California barley in
the 1960s.
Hailu, currently UNDP Assistant Resident Representative in
Ethiopia, adds: "This BYDV-resistant strain is saving California
state from a yearly expenditure of 160 million US dollars for
pest control. In simple words, the Ethiopian barley genetic
material contains elements of that particular pest-resistant
gene."
Ottawa-based Ethiopian scientist Awegechew Teshome says
Ethiopia's contribution may, in fact, be worth as much as 600
million dollars, with not only California, but all of the US as
well as Canada, Australia and many other countries benefiting
from Ethiopian barley.
He notes that, as one of the world's eight major centres of
agricultural biodiversity and of the wild relatives of cultivated
crops, Ethiopia has also shared the results of its farmers'
centuries-long wisdom regarding such crops as coffee and sorghum,
to Western agriculture.
Abebe Demesse, Acting Director of Ethiopia's Institute for
Biodiversity Conservation and Research (IBCR), identified Japan
and North Europe as other beneficiaries of Ethiopian planting
material garnered through "a number of collection expeditions."
The primary hero in the conservation of Ethiopia's genetic
diversity is the Ethiopian farmer.
The country still retains a large number of wild strains
of cereals and other plants, and also a number of "landraces," or
plant strains resulting from a process of selection carried out
by farmers over several centuries.
Ethiopia has thus been called a "living seed-basket" for
its almost bewildering variety of seeds and grains.
"Many of the plant species and genetic species in this
country are still untouched and still unspoiled," says UNDP's
Anne Ouma.
The traditional seed preservation practices of the
country's farmers aim to do more than ensure enough seeds for the
next crop.
Farmers have been concerned also with safety and security
issues and with preserving biodiversity - that is, ensuring that
certain plant species, for example, do not die out.
Not surprisingly, therefore, even during the drought and
the civil war of the 1980s, some areas of the country were
producing surplus grain.
Despite this pretty picture, all is not well. The tragedies
of the 1980s took their toll. In Ethiopia, as elsewhere in the
world, even the remotest parts of the country are not immune to
the belief that replacing landraces with modern strains of higher
yielding seed will offer protection from food shortages.
"Over the centuries," laments Hailu, "farmers were losing
farmer varieties due to changing circumstances such as recurrent
drought and famine."
A project jointly run by the Global Environment Facility
(GEF) and the UNDP in collaboration with IBCR, aims to help
reverse this trend.
With 2.5 million US dollars from GEF and UNDP and an
additional sum from the Ethiopian government, the project is
demonstrating that seeds and other planting materials resulting
from the genius of Ethiopian farmers can best be conserved by
these farmers on their own farm plots.
This is being achieved, in part, by training farmer
conservators to preserve planting materials and manage community
gene banks and by strengthening ties between farmer and
scientist. The project also links the farmers' work to national
and international gene banks.
The GEF, jointly implemented by the UNDP and UNEP, is an
independent multilateral financial mechanism that assists
developing countries to protect the global environment in four
areas: biodiversity, climate change, international waters, and
ozone layer depletion.
It currently funds more than 650 projects in 140
developing countries.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Loss For Flooded Province
Headline: Kenya: Crop Loss For Flooded Province
Body_Text:
Nairobi — As many as 60,000 hectares of food crops may have been destroyed in
the recent floods in Nyanza Province.
The Provincial Agricultural Officer, Mr. Zackayo Magara, yesterday
said he had ordered district agricultural officers to immediately
evaluate and compile detailed reports showing the extent of the
damage.
He said: "We have received an assessment report only from Nyando and
are waiting to hear from Karachuonyo and Kisumu to determine the
extent of the food crop destruction."
He said he had toured Karachuonyo during the week and established
that large amounts of maize and horticultural crops had been swept
away.
Mr. Magara said that in Nyando District, large tracts of rice paddy
in various stages of growth had been washed away.
The destroyed food crops included maize, beans, potatoes, cassava,
sorghum, tomatoes, sukuma wiki and other vegetables.
In an assessment tour with officials of the Red Cross Society of
Kenya, the Nation team established that Winam Division, Kisumu
District, had lost more than 15,000 hectares of food crops.
Kadibo Division lost almost all its crops.
The local chief, Mr. Stanley Omega, said although flood victims had
started returning to their homes, they needed food, medicine and
mosquito nets.
Medical personnel said they expected a sharp rise in cases of
malaria, typhoid, diarrhoea, measles and cholera.
At the10-bed Nyangande Health Centre, hundreds of flood victims were
waiting for medical attention, but there was no personnel to attend
to them as they were all said to have gone to attend a workshop on
Ebola at the Rabuor health Centre, near Kisumu.
In Miwani Division, it was difficult to estimate the damage because
some areas were still inaccessible.
The Red Cross team said the affected districts would need large
amounts of relief food supplies in the next six months until farmers
plant crops and they mature.
The regional officer, Ms Pamela Indiaka, said the residents also
needed to be protected from mosquitoes and required medicines and
safe water.
The residents claimed that although the Nyanza provincial
commissioner had announced that more than 3,000 bags of relief food
had been dispatched to the more than 10,000 displaced families, no
supplies had reached them.
"We are depending on wild roots and vegetation," said Mr. John Asiago
Awegi, a community leader.
Mr. Awegi, 64, blamed the recurrent floods on the National Irrigation
Board, saying that when it built its canals, it interfered with the
natural flow of the rivers Ombeyi, Orogono, Alwola, Miriu, Obange and
Orije.
He said the rivers reversed their course during heavy rains, causing
the rainwater to flood overflow onto the plains.
Mr. Omega said the Government had ignored recommendations that the
rivers' courses be redefined, claiming that there was no money for
the project.

Original_Search_Title: Drought-Stricken Cameroon Province Gets Relief
Headline: Cameroon: Drought-Stricken Cameroon Province Gets Relief
Body_Text:
Yaounde — President Paul Biya has
despatched some 4,000 tonnes of cereals to Cameroon's Far North
Province hit by food shortage following a severe drought.
The province has experienced poor harvests from
insufficient rains coupled with the devastating effects of
migratory birds.
Worst hit areas of the region include the Logone-Chari, and
Mayo-Sava Divisions.
Agriculture Minister Zacharie Perevet told pro-government
daily, Cameroon Tribune that of the 4,000 tonnes of cereals
despatched at the weekend, some 2,000 tonnes had already been
distributed to the needy population.
The Minister said although the relief would not be
sufficient to meet demand in the area, it was "a step in the
right direction."
In the wake of the drought, food prices in the province
have risen drastically, as high as 75 percent in the case of
cereals.
A jute bag of sorghum now sells at between 10,000 and
11,000 CFA francs as against between 6,000 and 7,000 CFA francs
in normal times. (750 CFA francs=1USD).

Original_Search_Title: Scientists Develop New, Safe Pests Control Methods
Headline: Kenya: Scientists Develop New, Safe Pests Control Methods
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Researchers working in Kenya say
they have developed a simple and bio-chemically safe method for
containing maize pests.
The "Push-Pull" system based on careful inter-cropping
method developed by the Nairobi International Centre of Insect
Physiology and Ecology (ICIPE), is said to dramatically reduce
losses in maize from stem-borers, termites and the parasitic weed
striga without the use of chemical pesticides.
According to ICIPE scientists, maize losses from stem-
borers can be as high as 40 percent in sub Saharan Africa, and
from Striga hermonthica, between 10 and 20 percent.
The Push-Pull "habitat management system," developed in
collaboration with the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute
(KARI), the Ministry of Agriculture and IACR-Rothamsted of UK,
ICIPE, uses two kinds of crops that are planted together with
maize.
The one that repels the borers (the push) and the other,
which attracts them (the pull), are inter-cropped with maize.
The scientists say the system has been tested on farms in
six districts of Kenya with participating farmers reporting maize
yield increases of 15 to 20 percent.
All of this, without using conventional hybrid seeds, and
without the need for expensive inputs such as synthetic
pesticides and fertilisers or genetically modified
seeds.
Two of the most useful trap crops that pull in the borers'
natural enemies are Napier grass (Pennisetum purpureum) and Sudan
grass (Sorghum vulgare sudanese), both important fodder plants,
planted in a border around the maize.
Two excellent borer-repelling crops, which are planted
between the rows of maize, are molasses grass (Melinis
inutifolia), which also repels ticks, and the leguminous
silverleaf (Desmodium uncinatum).
"Desmodium has proved to be a winner with a triple action,"
notes Zeyaur Khan, who led the ICIPE research.
"When inter-cropped with maize, it can suppress striga by a
factor of 40 compared to maize monocrop. Its Nitrogen-fixing
ability increases soil fertility, and it is an excellent forage,"
he adds.
The ICIPE project has also identified over 30 wild grasses
(Gramineae) that can serve as wild hosts of stem-borers, and
which might also prove useful as trap plants in the push-pull
tactic.
The underlying mechanism and chemistry of the attractive
odours given off by the trap plants is being investigated for
possible use in odour-baited traps for the borers.
Besides the Push-Pull system, ICIPE has been researching on
two other pest controls approaches.
According to Khan, the spotted stem-borer (Chilo
partellus), which was introduced into Africa in the early 20th
Century, is a foreign or 'exotic' species that has no effective
natural enemies
on the continent.
However, in 1993 ICIPE introduced a small parasitic wasp
called Cotesia flavipes from the borer's native home in Asia to
biologically control the pest.
The wasp kills the borers by searching out the larvae deep
inside the stem and laying its eggs in the pests. The wasp eggs
then hatch out and consume the borer from within.
In the four-year period after its release, the wasp density
remained low. But from 1997 onwards it has been noticeably
reducing the stem-borer populations in Kenya's coastal area and
some parts of Eastern Province, cutting the borer populations by
as much as half in Kwale and Kilifi districts.
But as scientists battle the stem-borer across the globe,
termites are becoming a serious threat to maize in many areas of
eastern and southern Africa, with growers in parts of Uganda and
at the Kenya reporting up to 100 percent losses in some fields.
Hence besides the stem-borer project, ICIPE scientists have
developed a fungal product to control termites within their
mounds and in crops.
Unlike the commercial chemical pesticides used to control
termites, this pthe "Push-Pull" system based on careful inter-
cropping roduct is harmless to humans and other animals
and can be prepared locally at a fraction of the cost.
Nguya Maniania, who isolated the Metarhizium fungal
strain and developed the product, reports that preliminary trials
in termite-infested fields in Uganda showed a 70 percent increase
in maize yields.
Moreover, the effect on the termite population lasts more
than one season.
Private partners are being sought to fund large-scale
production of the product, Maniania said.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Warns On GM Crops
Headline: Kenya: Minister Warns On GM Crops
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Farmers have been advised to be wary of
genetically-modified (GM)crops.
They should not be carried away by promised high yields from such
crops, Agriculture Minister Chris Obure said yesterday.
"Although modern biotechnological food production systems are
superior in many respects to the old ones, they are by no means
perfect," Mr. Obure said.
"We should use recent scientific techniques in farming wisely and
cautiously to ensure sustainability," he told participants of a
workshop at the National Museums of Kenya, Nairobi.
The two-day workshop, organised by the Museums and Stockholm
University, Sweden, is deliberating on the history and development of
food production systems in eastern Africa.
Mr. Obure said modern food production techniques, including the
controversy- ridden biotechnology, focused on profit maximisation.
He said some multinational corporations had pushed this objective too
far and are now producing seeds, dubbed terminator seeds, which
cannot be replanted.
The minister said the seeds pose huge cost implications for
cash-strapped farmers in developing countries because they have to
buy new seeds each season.
"Genetically modified seeds, which cannot be used for planting, are
now being sold to farmers in the developed world who, impressed by
high yields and improved incomes, have adopted them," Mr. Obure said.
He said these crops could also push to extinction traditional and
better- adapted crop varieties that may hold the key to food
security.
History had shown that technology-based agricultural production
systems from the West are never successful in the developing
countries, he observed.
He told farmers to evaluate traditional agricultural systems which
have proved sustainable.
"We have many things to learn from traditional farming systems, which
are more attuned to the ecosystem, if we have to achieve
sustainability in food production," the minister said.
The director-general of the Museums, Dr George Abungu, said
indigenous food crops like millet and sorghum could end Kenya's
recurrent food shortages because they can grow virtually anywhere in
the country.
"These crops are more nutritious and resistant to diseases. They do
well in poor soils and with low rainfall, giving bumper harvests,"
the Museums' boss said.
Dr Abungu said the government should promote and popularise
indigenous food crops to change negative attitudes towards them.

Original_Search_Title: Regional Update
Headline: East Africa: HORN OF AFRICA: IRIN-HOA Update
Body_Text:
SUDAN: "Gross violations" persist in Sudan
Both the government of Sudan and rebels groups have violated human
rights in the civil war, say the US-based Human Rights Watch in its
World Report 2001. The government has "stepped up its brutal
expulsion of southern villagers from the oil production areas and
trumpeted its resolve to use the oil income for more weapons," said
the report. Under the leadership of President Omar el-Beshir the
government had intensified its bombing of civilian targets, denied
relief food to needy civilians, and abused children's rights,
particularly through military and logistical support for the Ugandan
rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA), said Human Rights Watch. The
Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army (SPLM/A), the principal armed
movement based in the south, had "continued to loot food (including
relief provisions) from the population, sometimes with civilian
casualties, recruit child soldiers, and commit rape." Gross human
rights violations in Sudan came from the fact that "On both sides,
impunity was the rule." The government's human rights record was a
factor in the UN General Assembly vote in October that denied a
Security Council seat to Sudan, said the report. It said that efforts
to end the war had stalled on the issues of the relation of religion
to the state and self-determination. (For full Human Rights Watch
2001 report on Sudan see http://www.hrw.org/wr2k1/africa/sudan.html)
SUDAN: Church leader sends protest
The British Archbishop of Canterbury has protested to the government
about the destruction of the Episcopal Church Cathedral in Lui,
western Equatoria, southern Sudan. In a letter to the Sudanese
ambassador in London, he said the bombing of the church "highlights
the continued targeting of undoubted civilian centres by the
government of Sudan." It said Lui was a renowned centre of religious
life, education and health care and "has never been a military
centre, except during the period 1995-97 when it was a base for
government troops." "When such a centre is consistently targeted it
is hard to avoid the conclusion that the intentions to harm and
terrorise the civilian population," said the letter, dated 16
January, 2001. It requested that the letter be forwarded to President
Omar el-Bashir, and that he "clearly state that the armed forces will
not target civilian centres of population, but only legitimate
military targets, and then to abide transparently by this
commitment."
SUDAN: Security "key" to aid
The European Commission has approved euro 15 million (US $14.1
million) to maintain delivery of humanitarian assistance to Sudan. A
statement posted on 15 January by the European Community Humanitarian
Office (ECHO) said the assistance would aim to reduce morality rates
among the most vulnerable sections of the population and to promote
increased self-reliance in the war-affected society. It said that the
country was entering its eighteenth year of civil war.
"Administrative structures have all but collapsed in the South and
there are large numbers of internally displaced people (IDPs) in both
government and rebel-held areas." The aid would be directed through
ECHO and would focus on health, water/sanitation, food security,
non-food relief and logistical support both in government-controlled
and rebel-held areas. According to the statement, "the security
situation in Sudan remained highly volatile, jeopardising the safety
of relief workers." The ability to obtain access to affected
populations and security considerations would be key to the success
of future proposed interventions, ECHO said.
SUDAN: Beshir refuses to ratify women's rights
The government has refused to ratify an international treaty on
women's rights, saying it contradicts national traditions. The remark
made by President Omar el-Beshir, was published in the international
and local press on 14 January. The official news agency SUNA gave no
other details on Beshir's announcement regarding the Convention of
Eradication of all forms of Discrimination against Women (CEDAW).
Beshir's remarks came almost five months after UNICEF Deputy
Executive Director Karin Sham Poo urged him to sign the convention
which she said "seeks to remove harmful practices and maintain
equality between men and women." She has urged the government of
Sudan "to ratify CEDAW and make its commitment to its women not a
passive one but an obligation for the succeeding generations to
honour and uphold. The reports on Beshir's refusal were posted on the
UNICEF website.
SUDAN: Taxes removed on food imports
The Government of Sudan has exempted the imports of sorghum, maize,
and other cereals from customs duties, and other taxes on Monday. The
decision is linked to shortfalls in these stable commodities in some
parts of the country, the Associated Press (AP) said. Earlier this
month the government decided to lift a ban on importing sorghum and
millet. A statement from the Sudanese Ministry of Finance and
National Economy said the ministry would "shoulder all taxes and fees
imposed by seaports authorities or others on imports of sorghum and
animal fodder." Unofficial estimates in Sudan suggest a food shortage
of about 1.2 mt in western, southern, and south-central regions, AP
said. Humanitarian sources told IRIN that there was "great concern"
over the food situation in Sudan, and that food needs would rise
significantly this year.
SOMALIA: UN mission "fatal error"
Two Somali faction leaders expressed grave reservations over the
proposed UN mission to Somalia.
Mohamed Adan Qalinle of the Rahanwein Resistance Army (RRA) told
Agence France Presse in a telephone interview from Baidoa, southern
Somalia, that if the UN mission dealt with "a rival group as
Somalia's legitimate authority then we would not welcome the move."
Qalinle warned that "if the UN is siding with the Mogadishu based
non-representative administration, it is our responsibility to fight
injustice", and added that the UN was "making a fatal error and might
renew inter-clan feud in Somalia".
The RRA, which controls the Southern regions of Bay and Bakol, is one
of the factions opposed to the Transitional National Government (TNG)
in Mogadishu. Qalinle and Mogadishu faction leader Osman Ali Atto are
urging the UN to consult with faction leaders before the mission is
in place in Somalia, said AFP. "If the UN move is a political one
they first must seek the blessing of all warring sides", Atto told
AFP.
On Thursday the UN security council invited UN Secretary-General Kofi
Annan to propose a peace building mission for Somalia. 

SOMALIA: Islamic court attacked
Gunmen attacked one of Mogadishu's Islamic courts on Sunday night and
released 48 prisoners, including five convicted murders. More than 50
gunmen attacked the court located in south Mogadishu, near
Mogadishu's main stadium, freeing the prisoners and looting the court
premises, the Associated Press said. The attackers used three jeeps
mounted with guns to carry out the attack.
Business sources told IRIN that the attackers were angry about the
fact they were not being paid. "They were former members of the court
militia who were not added to the list of those being encamped and
paid by the interim government," a Mogadishu resident told IRIN.
No-one was hurt in the incident, said the source. It is the first
time the courts have been attacked, sources in Mogadishu said. The
Islamic courts have employed their own militia, and have their own
prisons, which are often just steel shipping containers with a few
holes pierced in them to allow prisoners to breath, AP said.
Meanwhile, sources in the president's office told IRIN that President
Abdiqassim Salad Hassan had appointed Sheikh Adan Mohamed Ibrahim, a
law professor, as the head of the new civilian Supreme Court on
Sunday.
ERITREA: US Commander meets president
The commander of the US Central Command in East and Central Africa,
General Tony Frank, arrived in Asmara on a two-day working visit to
Eritrea. He was received by Defence Minister General Sebhat Ephrem,
said official Eritrean radio on 16 January. The US general will hold
talks with President Isayas Aferwerki and other government officials,
the report said.
ERITREA-ETHIOPIA: Air route opened by UNMEE
The United Nations mission to Ethiopia and Eritrea (UNMEE) opened a
direct high altitude air route between the two countries. The route
was established by two aircraft which flew between the Ethiopian
capital, Addis Ababa, and the Eritrean capital, Asmara. According to
a statement released by UNMEE on 16 January, the route will be used -
along with helicopter and land routes already opened - to complete
deployment of 4,200 UN peacekeepers and to support their logistical
and medical needs.
A 25-km buffer zone still needs to be established between the two
countries forces, officially called the temporary security zone.
UNMEE has been mandated by the Security Council to monitor the buffer
zone, the cessation of hostilities, and the agreed repositioning of
troops from both parties.
Nairobi, 16 January 2001

Original_Search_Title: "Another NDC Victory Would Have Killed Me"
Headline: Ghana: "Another NDC Victory Would Have Killed Me"
Body_Text:
Accra — In the early 50s only a few people from the north, especially
Dagbon, were interested in taking up residence in the south. Those
who dared move out were regarded as venturing into a 'wilderness'. By
and large it was those who could afford to be indifferent to the
pleas of their people to return home who made the south their
permanent abodes.
Many who came down did so because they were involved in the selling
of an assortment of grains produced in the north which they brought
down to the southern markets. After selling these commodities they
bought manufactured goods for the home markets. Eventually some of
these traders took up permanent residence in this part of the
country.
The traders brought down to the south an array of items like maize,
sorghum, millet, guinea fowl and shea butter among others. All these
were in high demand in the south. The progress of this trade
encouraged the establishment of a lucrative business in transport
ownership. In those days commercial drivers were very respected
especially those involved in the long distance trips to and from the
south.
A few Islamic scholars also took up residence in the south where they
studied and established places of learning the Holy Quran. With the
passage of time people from the north Dagombas mostly, joined the
already established Zongo community whose people they shared a common
religion and culture with. In some instances there were
intermarriages between the 'immigrants' and the Hausa, Yorubas and
other ethnic groups who had long been residing in these areas with
Hausa remaining the predominant language.
In Accra the late Alhaji Tahiru of blessed memory was one of the
early settlers who lived here. There were also personalities like the
late Haruna Barazim and others.
When Alhaji Alhassan Abdulai Afatogma, the son of the late Islamic
scholar of Yendi Alhaji Abdulai Baba Gomda arrived to take up
residence in the capital he did not have much problems as his cousin,
the late Hajia Maata, wife of the late Alhaji Tahiru was around to
give a helping hand.
This was his second attempt at settling down in the south. His first
was in 1950 when he settled at Sekondi/Takoradi where he worked with
the Elder Dempster Lines and later with the Royal Airforce base where
he served British officers. He came to the south for the second time
because he had lost his beloved wife at home. Perhaps he took this
decision so he could get over the shock at the death of his wife in
Yendi.
As fate would have it Accra became his permanent home where he
married another woman in 1953 with whom he had many children. In the
course of his sojourn in Accra he became involved in the activities
of the CPP through the late Mr. J.H. Allassani through whom he came
into contact with other big names like Kweku Baako, S.D. Dombo,
Welbeck, the late Alhaji Yakubu Tali and others
When his hometown of Yendi was torn between joining the Mandated
Togoland or the then Gold Coast he joined those who were for the Gold
Coast. His late Uncle W.H. Husein was a strong figure in the 'Tidi
pirgi' or 'let's not divide' campaign.
When the colonial administration was encouraging people in the north
to send their wards to school it was his uncle the one-time District
Commissioner, Alhaji Abdul Majeed Husein who was given out to be
schooled. He however took advantage of the Teacher Kaleem inspired
mass education drive. This together with self-education equipped him
with the ability to read and write a little. His job as a pilgrims
agent to then West African Airways Corporation and later Ghana
Airways was made easy with this literary ability. He showed profound
interest in current affairs relying mostly on BBC for his news
update. Mention world leaders of yore like Kruschev, Broz Tito and
Winston Churchill and he would have something to say about them.
He recalled how in his youthful days he used to follow his father to
the Information Centre in Yendi where they followed the proceedings
of the Second World War with his idol Winston Churchill at the helm
of affairs on the allied side.
In Accra he was one of the first from the growing number of Dagombas
to enter the transport business. He bought his first lorry, which was
a Morris, when he was in his twenties for three hundred pounds. This
was followed by an Austin which went for seven hundred and fifty
pounds cash. In those days he said the journey to the north passed
through Senchi and Kete Krachi and it took about two or three days.
The journey took a break at Senchi where a ferry was available for
lorries. From this point there was another break some distance away
from Kete Krachi at a place called Banda. The last lap took them
finally to Yendi.
His love for politics was tested when on one occasion he abandoned a
mouth-watering deal to cart some goods worth seventy pounds for Accra
from Yendi. He did so because had he waited he would have missed the
voting which was billed for the next day in Accra.
Even though he was a die-hard Nkrumaist in those days, he had to jump
the CPP train when according to him the late president promulgated
the Preventive Detention Act (PDA) under which people were
arbitrarily arrested and detained. This did not go down well with
him. This move was temporary as eventually he found himself in love
with the CPP tradition once again when the Limann PNP came up.
From then on, he never hid his disdain for Rawlings, the man who
overthrew the PNP and this unflinching position was kept all the time
to December 28 2000 when Rawlings was finally removed through the
ballot box. It was very common for his friends to tease him with a
joke like 'Alhaji we hear you have joined the NDC'. This was usually
followed by a long tale of how the NDC administration was messing the
country up. No wonder when the NPP ousted the NDC in the last polls
many people wanted to see him and find out the mood in which he was.
Elated Alhaji Alfatogma said what happened on December 7 and 28 was
the work of God who has heard the cries of Ghanaians. He even said
had the results not gone the way they did, life would not have been
worth living anymore for him.
As a respected and popular man among members of his ethnic group in
Accra and indeed in the south, he felt disappointed when the results
of the first round of voting showed that his people up north had by
and large opted for the NDC. His only little consolation was that
Yendi where he hails from refused to toe the line of the other
constituencies in Dagbon where copious amounts of money of changed
hands.
His disappointment was shared by a majority of Dagombas in Accra and
Kumasi. When the decision to send a high powered delegation of his
people to the north to campaign for the run-off he was one of those
who went for the mission which can pass for an 'Operation Save Dagbon
from the intrigues of the NDC'.
When things got so bad in the country he resorted to prayers in line
with the dictates of Allah who he said directs in the scriptures
thus: 'When you are faced with a problem you cannot surmount, fight
it with your heart and prayers.'

Original_Search_Title: State Drops Taxes On Cereal Imports
Headline: Sudan: State Drops Taxes On Cereal Imports
Body_Text:
Khartoum, Sudan — Sudan has dropped taxes on
all cereals import, except wheat, until the beginning of
the new cropping season in October, a top government official
has announced.
The measure follows an earlier government decision to
import sorghum and maize to augment food deficiency in parts
of the country.
Finance Minister Mohammed Khair el Zubair said in a decree
Monday that his Ministry "would bear Customs duties, the Sea
Ports Corporation fees and any other taxes on the imports of
sorghum, maize and barley."
Sudan's cropping season starts in October and the new
measure would be in force until 30 September 2001.
Government said last week that it has embarked on a plan to
import sorghum and wheat to make up for cereal shortages in parts
of the country.
Kurdufan and Darfur regions in the West, northern Bahr el
Ghazal, Bahr el Jebel, East Equatoria, and Jongoli in the South,
and the Butana region in central Sudan, have been declared
emergency zones due to low rainfall in 2000.
UN agencies in December 2000 appealed for donors' assistance
to tackle Sudan's food crisis.
After a countrywide survey, a Joint Mission from the UN Food
and Agriculture Organisation and the World Food Programme said
the country needed 1.2 million tons of cereals in 2001.
The Mission said the short supply has pushed up sorghum
consumer prices.
"The price per 90-kilo bag of sorghum has jumped from 1,500
dinars to 3,500 dinars," the report said, adding that the price
was beyond the reach of poor consumers. (about 256 dinars=1USD).
Sorghum is a staple in rural Sudan, while wheat is the
favourite food for the country's urban dwellers.

Original_Search_Title: A Year Haunted By Norbert Zongo's Ghost
Headline: Burkina Faso: A Year Haunted By Norbert Zongo's Ghost
Body_Text:
Ouagadougou —  The year 2000 was marked in Burkina by the persistence of the socio-political crisis
following commemorative ceremonies of the assassination, on 13
December 1998, of the journalist Norbert Zongo. What became known
as
the university crisis were followed by a series accusations
levied
against Burkina Faso for trafficking in Angola and Sierra Leone.
The government concentrated mostly on the fight against
the HIV/AIDS pandemic and on the struggle to improve on the
living
conditions of the population. The following is an outline of the
main
events in the country for 2000.
January 2000
3 January : A planned demonstration by the Action Group of mass
democratic organisations and political parties to celebrate the
40th
anniversary of the first popular uprising which led to the
collapse of the first Burkinabe political regime, did not finally
take place.
The Mayor of Ouagadougou was opposed to the proposed routing of
the
demonstartion.
17 January : Militants of the national union of Faso students
(UNEF), armed with bladed weapons and sticks, attacked members
of the National Association of Burkina Students (ANEB), who were
organising picket lines to boycott the call for strike action
launched by UNEF. These two associations are the leading trade
union movements on the Ouagadougou university campus.
21 January : Celebration of the centenary of the establishment
in Kourittenga (Burkina Faso)of the first Catholic missionaries.
In  fact, it was on 22 January 1900, under the reign of
Naaba Yiri-Bi, the Samand Naaba Kaos Garga, that a delegation of
natives met with the very first missionaries: Fathers Menet,
Lacroix, Canac and Father Celestin.
February 2000
8 February : The former Burkinabe President, General Aboubacar
SangoulÃ© Lamizana (1966-1980) published his memoirs. "This book
urges people to practice work, abnegation, tolerance and
permanent dialogue, the only way to achieve a better future",
said El Hadj Lamizana.
9 February : A direct liaison agreement signed between
Burkina and the United States. The provisions of this "open-air"
agreement, which are provisionally implemented, will facilitate
air transport between the two countries.
The Yalgado OuÃ©draogo national hospital (CHN/YO) puts its
first scanner to use. The apparatus and the premises that house
it cost 731 million CFA francs.
22 February : Ad hoc committee in charge of national
reconciliation submits its report to Prime Minister KadrÃ© DÃ©sirÃ©
OuÃ©draogo.
23 February : Niger's President, Mamadou Tanja, pays a working
visit to Burkina Faso.
25 February : Launching of the asphalting works of the PÃ¢-Dano-
Dipologo;DiÃ©bougou-Gaoua-Kampti-Cote d'Ivoire border road. These
works are entrusted to the Oumarou KanazoÃ© firm for the sum of
21.511 billion CFAF, with a 3 year execution deadline.
March 2000
1st March  : The West African Conference on integrated water
resources management (COAGIRE) for a world partnership and a
global vision on water, convened its ministerial commitee meeting
in
Ouagadougou. It is meant to serve as a springboard for all
negotiations and policies in matters of water management.
5 March  : Justice Minister Boureima Badini, speaks on Burkina
national radio (RNB) about pending legal cases, and a letter
written by Burkinabe magistrates and published by the
international press denouncing administrative political
pressure in relation to the Norbert Zongo and David  OuÃ©draogo
cases.
6 March  : Mrs. Marguerite Sankara, nee Kinda, mother of former
President Thomas Sankara passed away. She is survived by her
husband, Joseph Sankara, nine children, twenty-five grand-
children and nine great grandsons.
A sub-regional meeting organised by the National Malaria
Research and Training Centre (CNRFP), within the framework of the
Multilateral Initiative on Malaria, is attended by twenty-five
health experts from some twenty countries and thirty research
institutions.
7 March  : Ouagadougou hosts a meeting of researchers from the
six States participating in the millet-sorghum initiative. The
initiative aims first to stimulate the growth of millet and
sorghum production while encouraging its evolution into permanent
production systems and the organisation of the market in the
medium and long terms.
8 March : The Kadiogo (Ouagadougou) women hold a rally-
demonstration focussed on peace for lasting development. They
expressed their support of efforts to eradicate poverty and
inequalities, considered as the major causes of violence on
women.
10 March : Publication of a report by UN experts on
human rights abuses and violation of sanctions against the
Angolan
rebel movement UNITA by certain countries, including Burkina.
The annual meeting of directors of Extended Immunisation
Programmes (EIPs) in the countries of the West African
epidemiological bloc, is held in Ouagadougou, with the aim of
contributing to the improvement of disease monitoring and control
in the countries of the region.
16 March : The feast of Tabaski is celebrated by Muslims in
Burkina in presence of several members of the government.  The
imam of the central mosque, Ibrahim Kouanda, called for God's
mercy on Burkina and its people.
17 March : UEMOA experts and national experts of the Regional
Support Programme for Integration (PARI) meet in Ouagadougou with
EU and UNIDO experts to speed up the integration process through
major projects such as the Customs Union, the Court of
Auditors and sectorial political reforms.
The Central Bank of West African States (BCEAO) launches a
historical book on the life of the UEMOA.
Prime Minister Kadre Desire Ouedraogo addresses parliament
on the state of the nation.  The drop in local rice sales is a
major concern.
18 March : The second meeting of rural women is held in Bobo
Dioulasso under the theme "the voice of rural women".  It is
attended by 176 delegates from Burkina, Benin, Chad, Cote
d'Ivoire, Guinea, Mali, Niger, Senegal and Togo.
A new insurance company called Colina Assurances opens in
Ouagadougou.  Colina S.A. Burkina has a capital of 500 million
cfa held by the parent company Conlina S.A., an Ivorian company
and Burkinabe investors.
The tenth edition of the National Week of Culture (SNC)
opens in Bobo-Dioulasso under the theme: "investing in culture
for the future".
27 March: The West African Economic and Monetary Union (UEMOA)
organises a campaign to inform and sensitise people on its
various organs, notably the UEMOA commission, the Court of
Justice, the  Court of Auditors, the Inter-parliamentary
Committee, the Regional Consular Chamber as well as major UEMOA
projects.
The Faso President, Blaise CompaorÃ©, presides over the
opening of the second conference of West African national
assembly presidents, in Ouagadougou. Parliamentarians came from
Benin, The Gambia, Ghana, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, Liberia, Mali,
Niger, Nigeria, Sierra Leone and Togo.
28 March : The statutory UEMOA Council of Ministers meeting was
held in Ouagadougou to examine community issues pertaining to the
Central Bank of West African States (BCEAO), the West African
Development Bank (BOAD) and the ECOWAS-UEMOA co-operation.
April 2000
2 April: The 10th National Cultural Week "Bobo 2000" ends.
3 April : The 4th Scientific Research and Technological
Innovation Forum (FRSIT)opens in Ouagadougou.
4 April : Senior Minister Ram Jean Baptiste OuÃ©draogo, chairs the
meeting of the inter-ministerial committee set up on the Faso
President's initiative to implement proposals contained in the
reports of the national reconciliation committee and the
committee of wise men.
6 April : A fire breaks out in the National Arts and Crafts
Centre (CNAA) of Ouagadougou and devastates the entire archives
of the centre. Over 1000 craftsmen were trained in this Centre
whose purpose is training as well as the promotion and
commercialisation of arts and crafts products.
7 April : The International Association of Biology Technicians
(ASSITEB) holds a training workshop to present and structure the
African trainers' network.  400 Technicians from 18 European and
African countries attend the meeting.
8 April : The group of mass democratic organisations and
political parties against impunity hold a meeting at the Union
Hall, after their march was broken up by security forces using
tear gas.
10 April : The Koudou palace of justice, 100 km west of
Ouagadougou is torched by demonstrators demanding the setting up
of
an independent judiciary in Burkina.
The national project for the development of agricultural
services is launched with the aim of contributing to poverty
alleviation.  The total cost of the four-year project (2000-2004)
is estimated at 25 billion CFA.
22 April : The Ouagadougou palace of justice is burgled.
Attorney-General Abdoulaye Barry reveals the disappareance of
documents related to cases pending, including the case of
the late Norbert Zongo.
25 April : The National Forum on Sports is attended by 250
participants who discuss how to boost sports in Burkina.
27 April : Kader Cisse, the minister of trade, industry and
crafts, announces a price hike on oil products.  The litre of
high grade petrol rises by 11 cfa, diesel by 5 cfa, and fuel
compound by 10 cfa.
28 April : BagrÃ©, located 220 km southeast of Ouagadougou, in the
Bouglou province, hosts the National Farmers' Day, celebrated to
enhance dialogue between the State, farmers' associations and
federations to achieve national cohesion among producers.
Burkinabe MPs sitting in an ordinary session question the
government on poverty alleviation strategies in Burkina.
May 2000
Tuesday 2 May : The National Assembly adopts the law relative to
the public financing of political parties and election campaigns
in Burkina.
Wednesday 3 May : Burkina celebrates International Press Freedom
Day under the theme "The role of the media in conflict and post-
conflict zones". This day was marked in our country by the
"refusal to forget".
University of Ouagadougou students started a three-day
strike, in response to the call of the National Union of Burkina
Students (ANEB) to support their fellow students of the Faculty
of Health Science (FSS) who were demanding the payment by the
government of allowances for internship in Yalgado hospital.
Thursday 4 May : The European Union grants 1.28 billion
CFA F to the Inter-State Committee for Drought Control in the
Sahel (CILSS), within the framework of drought control efforts.
Part of this amount will be used to complete the new head office
in Ouagadougou.
Members of Parliament question the government on the use of
chemical and carcinogenic products in the making of bread. The
MPs note that nothing has changed in the "criminal conduct" of
bakers who continue to poison the people".
7 May : A meeting of OAU Health Ministers, focussed on the theme
"AIDS Eradication in Africa, partnership and response of the
health sector", opened in Ouagadougou. The meeting was attended
by some hundred health specialists, financial partners,
associations, NGOs and persons living with the AIDS virus.
8 May : University of Ouagadougou students organise a
demonstration under the aegis of the National Association of
Burkinabe students (ANEB), to obtain better living and study
conditions.
9 May: President Blaise CompaorÃ©  receives, in Ouagadougou, a
delegation of the Action Group of mass democratic organisations
and political parties, led by its president Halidou
OuÃ©draogo. The encounter did not lead to a consensual end-of-
crisis proposal.
10 May : The Liberian Head of State, Charles Taylor, arrives in
Ouagadougou to inform Blaise CompaorÃ© about the conclusions of
the mini-summit held to diffuse the tension which recently
sparked
off in Sierra Leone. After the ECOWAS summit in Abuja (Nigeria),
President Taylor was appointed to serve as mediator with the head
of the United Revolutionary Front, Foday Sankoh.
11 May : President Compaore meets leaders of political parties
members of the moderate opposition, namely the Alliance for
Democracy and Federation/African Democratic Rally (ADF/RDA) and
Soumane Toure's the African Party for Independence (PAI).  The
meeting is part of a series of concertations initiated by the
president with political parties, in order to find a definite
solution to the crisis in Burkina.
15 May : Burkina places the International Family Day under the
theme "Family and AIDS control", to mark the seriousness of the
national AIDS situation.  In 1986, there were 10 cases of
HIV/AIDS in Burkina.  In 1999, the figure exceeded 13,000, with
an HIV infection rate of 7 to 8 per cent.
16 May : The national assembly adopts in a plenary session the
bill governing the composition, mandate, operation and procedure
applicable to the Court of Auditors.  The revision of the
constitution should confirm the break-up of the Supreme Court
into one institution and three autonomous jurisdictions,
including the Court of Auditors.
17 May : The 86th session of the management committee of the
Entente Council's Mutual Help and Loan Guarantee Fund is held in
Ouagadougou in the absence of the Ivorian delegation.  As at 31
December 1998, the Fund's capital was estimated at 16 billion cfa
and its operations in member states at 2.305 billion cfa, while
the loans guaranteed amounted to 33.256 billion cfa.
20 May : Political parties sharing the ideology of the late
Thomas Sankara set up in Ouagadougou the Pan African
Sanakarist Convention (CPS).  It is chaired by Ernest Nongma
Ouedraogo and supports the efforts of the group of mass
democratic organisations and political parties against impunity.
27 May : The Burkinabe U-17 national football team qualify for
the finals of the West African Football Union (WAFU) Cup after
beating Benin 2-1 in Ouagadougou.
29 May : Ouagadougou hosts regional workshop on the WTO rules
related to the multilateral trade system and the Uruguay Round.
The workshop is initiated by the West African Economic and
Monetary Union (UEMOA), in cooperation with USAID, and is
attended by some 40 public administration and private sector
experts from the eight UEMOA member countries.
31 May : Ouagadougou high schools are closed for 24 hours,
following a statement published by the minister of secondary
education, higher education and scientific research.
June 2000
2 June : The parliamentary group of the Party for Democracy and
Progress (PDP) criticises the silence of the national assembly,
as the representative of the whole people, on the unprecedented
crisis facing the country.
Dramane Nabaloum "Boum Boum", triple WBC African junior
lightweight champion, beats Ghanaian vice-champion Ben Odametey
on points in Ouagadougou.
4 June : Cardinal Paul Zoungrana, 83, first native bishop in the
Ouagadougou archdiocese, dies at Ouagadougou's Saint Camille
medical centre after an illness.
Burkina wins 20 gold medals at the second West African U-17
athletics championships, for Zone III of the Supreme Council for
Sports in Africa (SCSA).
5 June : The National Association of Burkina Students (ANEB)
starts a four-day strike to protest against the delay in
negotiations with government.
About 50 African and European legal experts hold a workshop
in Ouagadougou to exchange views and take stock of current
experiences in alternative penalties for minors.
15 June : Presidents Abdoulaye Wade of Senegal and Blaise
Compaore of Burkina chair the opening of an international
symposium in Ouagadougou, on "the vocation and role of the lawyer
in the emergence and strengthening of democracy".
19 June : A delegation from the ruling junta in Cote d'Ivoire
visit President Compaore as part of its sub-regional tour to
explain the situation in Cote d'Ivoire.
20 June : 113 African refugees in Burkina, on a hunger strike at
the Ouagadougou cathedral, commemorate the Day of the African
Refugee with prayers.
23 June : The Abidjan port community carries out commercial
promotion and sensitisation efforts on the use of the Ivorian
corridor by Burkinabe economic operators.  The commercial
offensive is marked by the official posting of a representative
of the Port of Abidjan in Ouagadougou.
26 June : The advisory technical committee of the Onchocerciasis
Control Programme in West Africa (OCP) starts its second annual
meeting in Ouagadougou.  OCP comprises the representatives of 19
member countries and partners and it will be completed in 2007.
27 June : Carribbean Cinema Days are launched in Ouagadougou with
the preview of a photo exhibition by three Burkinabe
photographers
at Cine Burkina.
30 June : To solve the academic crisis, professors at the
University of Ouagadougou set up a mediation committee to
reconcile the positions of the government and students.
July 2000
4 July :  Libyan leader Col. Muammar Kadhafi is the guest at a
political marathon for African unity in Burkina.
7 July : Mossi tribal leader Naaba Tigre of Tenkodogo, 230 km
east of Ouagadougou, is charge with failure to render assistance
following the death of Mahamoudou Kere at the royal palace.
18 July : Taiwan gives Burkina 45 tractors with ploughs and disk
harrows worth 425.8 million cfa to support agricultural
mechanisation.
Official installation of the National organising committee
for the 7th edition of the Ouagadougou International Crafts Show
(SIAO), to be held from 27 October to 5 November 2000.
August 2000
1 August  : The UK and US reiterate their accusations against
Burkina and Liberia for supporting Revolutionary United Front
(RUF) rebels at the second day of hearings by the Sanctions
Committee on Sierra Leone at the UN headquarters.
3 August : Senegal's Mamadou Moustapha Sall replaces Rambre
Moumouni Ouimiga of Burkina as head of the secretariat of the
African and Malagasy Council for Higher Education (CAMES)
5 August : The Pan African Sankarist Convention (CPS) agrees to
participate in the municipal elections of 24 September 2000,
following a vote by its national council.
6 August : The Bani dam breaks up in the Seno province,
destroying 36 houses and causing 520 disaster victims.
8 August : Eighth conference of ambassadors and general consuls
in Burkina, under the theme "diplomacy and new information and
communication technologies".
9 August : Burkina gets 46 billion cfa from the World Bank to
finance the second phase of its national land management
programme.
11 August : Burkina's attorney-general Abdoulaye Barry meets the
media, in prelude to the trial of the case of David Ouedraogo
(the late driver of President Compaore's brother), slated for 17
August.
18 August : Ruling on the David Ouedraogo case is given at 11
PM.  Warrant Officer Marcel Kafando and Sergeant Edmond Koama are
sentenced to 20 years each, and Private Yaro to 10 years.  The
court also sentenced the three to pay 200 million cfa in damages
to the late Ouedraogo's family, while Amidou Ilboudo will pay 50
million and Adama Tiendrebeogo two million.
Privates Marcel Kabre and Christophe Kombassere are acquitted.
21 August : Taiwan president Chen Shui-Bian arrives in Burkina
for a three-day official visit.  Presidents Compaore and Chen
visit the Bagre agricultural plain and launch the "rainfed rice
project" in Koudri, in the Gazourgou province.
24 August : As Taiwan president Chen Shui-Bian ends his visit,
Compaore says at a news briefing: "we will remain firm in our
political will to ensure that the Republic of China (Taiwan) is
admitted to the community of nations.
27 August : A UN committee of experts in charge of investigating
arms and diamond trafficking in Sierra Leone, arrives in
Ougadougou at the invitation of Burkinabe authorities.  The
committee is led by Chilean ambassador Juan Larain.
28 August : Ouagadougou hosts the 50th session of the WHO's
Regional Committee for Africa.
1 September : Burkinabe youth and sports minister Rene Emile
Kabore is elected chairman of the ECOWAS conference of youth of
sports ministers, and Simon Taro is re-elected for four years as
secretary-general, at the end of the seventh session of the
conference held in Ouagadougou.
9 September : Foreign minister Youssouf Ouedraogo rejects as
"unfounded" claims by Guinean authorities that Burkina is
involved in the conflict at the border between Guinea, Liberia
and Sierra Leone.
The Ougadougou labour court rejects the complaint filed by
the management of BICIA-B bank against its employees.
24 September: Municipal elections.
October 2000
3 October : The second bilateral meeting between Ghanaian and
Burkinabe customs officers opens to lay the basis of smooth
trade between the two countries.
6 October : The government of the Fourth Republic decides to
close the University of Ouagadougou, annul the academic year
1999-2000 and dissolve all faculties.
14 October : Women from the informal sector (fruits and vegetable
vendors in the Kadiogo province) stage a march to support the
reform of Ouagadougou University.
16 October : The three-day strike by local Air France workers.
They  demand that their requests tabled since 1993 be met.
19 October : Official launch of the immunisation campaign in
Dori, 265 km north of Ouagadougou, as part of the National
Immunisation Days (NIDs).
20 October : The Suka Foundation, led by first lady Chantal
Compaore, presents a cheque of 50 million cfa to support the
Solidarity Fund for AIDS Patients and Orphans.
26 October: President Blaise Compaore official launches the 7th
edition of the Ouagadougou International Crafts Show (SIAO).
November 2000
4 November : The 7th edition of SIAO ends, with Benin winning the
grand prix of the Faso President, worth 1.25 million cfa.
6 November : Prime Minister Kadre Desire Ouedraogo tables his
resignation and that of his cabinet to President Compaore, who
accepts it and names Paramanga Ernest Yonli as prime minister.
7 November : Liberian president Charles Taylor arrives in
Ouagadougou, for a visit "to exchange views" with his host on
regional problems.
8 November : The UNICEF office in Burkina hosts the 9th
concertation of West and Central Africa representatives to
determine UNICEF's priorities for the 21st century.
10 November : Official inauguration of the 19 members of the
National Organising Committee for the 17th Ouagadougou Pan
African Cinema and Television Festival (FESPACO).  The event will
be held form 24 February to 3 March 2001 under the theme "Cinema
and new technologies".
12 November : A presidential decree is published appointing a new
government, of 36 members and eight political parties
represented.
Some of these parties are members of the presidential coalition,
and
others are former members of the G-14 (radical opposition) and
the
Group against impunity.
15 November : The 14th cycling Tour of Burkina starts in Banfora.
17 November : Ouagadougou hosts the first joint general assembly
of the francophone and anglophone episcopate, attended by 150
West African bishops.
21 November : An international symposium 'Water and Health Ouaga
2000', under the theme "health and nutritional impact of water
projects in Africa" opens in Ouagadougou.
22 November : UEMOA statutory Council of Ministers meet to adopt
the draft budget of the union for the financial year 2001.
Diabetology Days open in Ouagadougou.
25 November : The chancellor of Ouagadougou University suggests
to the government to take measures in favour of professors and
students for the re-opening of the university.
December 2000
1 December : A European Union delegation expresses to President
Compaore the EU's worries following accusations of violation of
UN arms embargos against Sierra Leone and Unita by Burkina.
2 December : African mining and energy ministers meet in
Ouagadougou to discuss the mining strategy for Africa at the dawn
of the new millennium.
4 December : The Multilateral Investment Guarantee Agency (MIGA)
holds its 6th symposium in Ouagadougou.  The specialised World
Bank agency aims at attracting foreign investment in the mining
sector.
The sixth congress of the African Gynecology and Obstetrics
Society (SAGO) and the first congress of the Burkina
Gynecologists' Society (SOCOB) open in Ouagadougou.  About 30
francophone African countries are represented.
6 December : Flavien Nebie, a student, dies in Bousse following
demonstrations which led to clashes between security forces and
high school students preparing the commemoration of the
anniversary of Norbert Zongo's assassination.
7 December : Burkinabe security minister Djibril Bassolet informs
the representatives of the Group of associations and political
parties that all public demonstrations are suspended on 13
December, the anniversary of Norbert Zongo's assassination.
11 December : President Blaise Compaore addresses the nation on
radio and television on the 40th anniversary of Burkina's
independence.  The highlight of the speech is a call for social
peace and "social responsibility".
12 December : Virtually all secondary schools are closed in
Ouagadougou, after student demonstrations against the re-
organisation of the university and the death of a student in
Bousse.
13 December : Demonstrators and security forces clash in
Ouagadougou as the latter try to prevent any gathering to
commemmorate the second anniversary of Norbert Zongo's death.
14 December : The 12th conference of ministers of the Montreal
Protocol on ozone depleting substances decides in Ouagadougou to
build capacities for quick regeneration of the ozone layer.
18 December : Ouagadougou University opens under heavy
surveillance by security forces.  Students register in large
numbers.
17 December : The Burkinabe junior national football team are out
of the African Cup of Nations to be played in 2001 in Ethiopia,
following a draw in their return qualifier against Angola.  The
first leg match was won 2-0 by the Angolans.

Original_Search_Title: Sudan Steps To Redress Food Deficit
Headline: Sudan Steps To Redress Food Deficit
Body_Text:
Khartoum — Sudan has embarked on a plan to
redress the prevailing food shortage in some parts of the
country.
Due to low rainfall in 2000, the authorities have declared
Kurdufan and Darfur regions in the west, Northern Bahr el Ghazal,
Bahr el Jebel, East Equatoria, and Jongoli in the south and the
central Butana region as emergency zones.
The commissioner of humanitarian aid, Sulafeddin Saliah,
Sunday said in Khartoum his organisation has started purchasing
15,000 tons of sorghum for areas facing food shortage.
He added that an ad hoc committee assigned to address the
food shortage has also started the importation of 100,000 tons of
wheat and 100,000 tons of sorghum "which will be delivered in the
coming few days."
But Saliah has minimised reports of imminent famine in
Sudan. "The existence of a food gap should not mean we are facing
famine," he said. "The situation is under control and is being
closely watched at the highest government levels."
Meanwhile, the official has welcomed a UN offer to address
the water, drought and desertification problems in the two
regions as part of the world body's appeal to the donors for
the 2000.
"We appreciate the UN plan to tackle the water situation
and implement emergency plans to rehabilitate pastures," Saliah
told journalists Sunday after a meeting of the ad hoc committee
set up by the government set up to address the food deficit.
He said the agriculture ministry has directed its regional
departments to intensify sorghum cultivation during the winter
crop season in the country's irrigated schemes as one way of
reducing the food gap.
He said CARE International has volunteered to provide
earthmoving equipment and technicians to rehabilitate the Butana
water reservoirs.
The UN had declared in December that Sudan was facing a food
shortage and appealed to the donors to help.
This followed a countrywide survey by a joint mission from
the FAO and the WFP, which estimated the local cereal output at
3.6 million tons in the 2000 crop season.
Although the harvest was 14 percent than in 1999, the
output was less than the average of the previous five years by 18
percent.
The mission requested donors to provide 1.2 million tons of
cereals in 2001.
It said the low crop has caused the consumer prices of
sorghum to soar up. "The price per a 90-kg bag of sorghum has
jumped from 1,500 dinars to 3,500 dinars," the mission's report
said.
According to the report, low rainfall also had "a
devastating effect on the green cover that reduced available
fodder and forced herders to sell their livestock at low prices."
The mission has put the number of people affected by the
drought at 900,000, out of whom 600,000 are in bad need for food
aid during the coming five months.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Emergency Report No. 01 - Africa
Headline: Africa: WFP Emergency Report No. 01 - Africa
Body_Text:
Rome — This report includes: (A) Ethiopia (B) Angola (C) Sudan (D)
Burundi (E) West Africa Coastal a) Guinea 2) Sierra Leone 3) Liberia
From Francesco Strippoli, Senior Humanitarian Adviser. Available on
the Internet on the WFP Home Page (www.wfp.org), or by e-mail from
Zlatan.Milisic@wfp.org. For information on resources, donors are
requested to contact Valerie.Sequeira@wfp.org at WFP Rome, telephone
39 06 6513 2009. Media queries should be directed to
Trevor.Rowe@wfp.org, telephone 39 06 6513 2602. The address of WFP is
Via Cesare Giulio Viola 68, Parco dei Medici, 00148 Rome, Italy.
(A) Ethiopia
(a) A pledge of 27,200 tons has been received from US in support of
emergency operation (EMOP) 6080.01, which provides food assistance to
those internally displaced by the Ethiopia-Eritrea conflict. This
pledge has ensured that the operation is almost fully resourced to
the end of June 2001. No additional pledges have been received for
the Natural Disaster operation (EMOP 6218) during the reporting
period. The lack of pledges to cover global needs beyond February
2000 remains a critical concern. Cash pledges are urgently required
to enable local purchases from pocket areas of surplus production,
while pledges in kind will ensure that loans from the relatively high
stocks in the EFSR can be activated.
(b) Somali Region: Reports from WFP monitors indicate a mixed
situation in the region, in spite of good Deyr rains in many areas.
Water sources have been replenished by the Deyr rains in Degehbour,
Fik, Korahe, Gode and hard-hit Warder zone, though the usual dry
season problems will be faced in some areas when water sources dry
up. In parts of Degehbour, Fik and Korahe the rains were not
sufficient for the planted sorghum crop to reach maturity. Conditions
in parts of Afder and Liben, although improved from the height of the
drought, appear to be deteriorating again as little rain was
received. In Shinile, which does not receive Deyr rain, pasture
exists, but in the vicinity of both permanent and temporary
settlements, it has been exhausted. The absence of small livestock
means that dependence on food aid, especially among the drought
displaced, remains high.
(c) The livestock import ban imposed by Gulf States in September
continues to have a serious impact on the market for livestock in
Somali Region, with few animals now being sold. The price of relief
wheat in the markets, sold by beneficiaries to obtain other needed
commodities, remains low, as do the prices of maize and sorghum.
However, livestock prices remain high.
(d) Wolytia: Preliminary reports from the recent DPPC led
multi-agency Emergency Needs Assessment indicate that the situation
in Wolytia, SNNPR, is improving due primarily to an above average
meher harvest and food assistance received over the past few months.
(e) Between January and December 2000, over 622,000 tons was
transported by WFP to various hubs throughout Ethiopia. The peak
period was July and August, during which time over 230,000 tons was
dispatched. Some 89 percent of dispatches related to WFP commodities
while the balance of almost 70,000 tons or 11 percent related to
delivery of bi-lateral commodities.
(f) Of the 10,000 Ethiopian refugees in Sudan scheduled to be
repatriated, some 1,190 have returned to Ethiopia. A total of 4,500
are expected to be repatriated under the first phase, which should be
completed over the coming weeks. The remainder will be repatriated
under a second phase, which will be activated following a review of
phase one.
(B) Angola
(a) The overall contributions towards the WFP's protracted relief and
recovery operation (PRRO) in Angola remains at USD 120.3 million (58
percent of the total operational requirement). A request has been
made to ECHO for 2000 tons of maize to cover the February shortfall.
WFP is awaiting a response.
(b) Benguela: On 28 December the Benguela civilian airport was
subject to mortar fire in an apparent attempt to explode the fuel
depot. A civilian aircraft is reported as heavily damaged. On 16
December fourteen youths were killed and eighteen wounded when a
group of armed men entered and opened fired at a small discotheque in
Barrio Oito, between Cubal town and the Catholic Mission. In Cubal
the local authorities and NGOs are reporting a lack of rainfall
during the September to December 2000 planting season. Only 37.7 mm
of rain is reported, which could seriously affect the May 2001
harvest.
(c) Bie: Although the security situation was calm in the Kuito during
the third week of December, it still remains volatile in the
provinces. On 21 December, close to the IDP camp of Cambandua, a Soba
(traditional leader) was killed. The site is said to be heavily mined
and according to the local police people have been warned against
entering the area. Another site to relocate the IDPs has yet to be
located. The fourth week of December is reported to have been calmer
in the region. 2,887 IDPs were registered during the reporting
period. The Ministry of Social Assistence and Reintegration (MINARS)
has informed of the arrival of another 515 IDPs who will be
registered during the first week of January. 161 tons of dry fish
arrived in Kuito from Catumbela to cover the needed energy intake in
place of sugar and CSB. 86 tons have already been distributed.
(d) Huambo: In a coordination meeting held at MINARS on 20 December
WFP announced that both Chipipa and Joni II will be included in the
January plan. The NGO Okios will be responsible for food distribution
as well as run the soup kitchen in Chipipa. During the reporting
period beneficiaries of assistance were assessed and broken down into
the following three categories, (i) Vulnerable Groups: 19,621, (ii)
Refugees and IDPs: 468, (iii) Rehabilitation: 1,415. On 21 December
training for inter-agency monitoring teams for family rations was
carried out. Distribution of rations to families of children who have
left the feeding centres is reported as running smoothly.
(e) Kuando Kubango: A total of 303 new IDPs have been reported in the
second and third week of December. During the second week of December
189 new IDPs from the areas of Chinguanja and Licuve were reported.
On 24 December, the IDP camp in the village of Chiongo was attacked.
Six people were killed and 14 wounded. The camp is located 13 km
north of Menongue. The third week of December 114 new IDPs were
reported from Chinguanja and Cuchi area as arriving at the transit
centre in Menongue.
(f) Moxico: No security incidents were listed during this reporting
period. A total of 354 new IDPs have arrived in the Cawango IDP Camp.
299 from Luanda Sul, Dala municipality, 12 from Sauter, Malange, 31
from Moxico, Luacano municipality, and 12 returnees from Zambia,
originally from Moxico, Bundas, Luchazes and Moxico localities.
Separate meetings with MINARS and UTCHA were held with the Government
to discuss the need to open two new IDP camps in Cassongo and
Cawango. The Sangondo II camp was declared completely full by the NGO
in charge of the camp. It is reported that the results of the
meetings were positive and that the Government has plans to open the
new camps.
(g) Huila: A WFP/MINARS mission was carried out from the 18 - 21
December to update the number of yet to be re-located IDPs considered
to be vulnerable in the IDP camps of Palanca and Huila Unene in
Humpata municipality. The count confirmed 545 elderly, disabled and
orphans in those camps.
(h) Uige: According to MINARS/Negage 237 new IDPs from Bungo, Puri
and Milunga arrived in Negage during the reporting period. This
number is still awaiting verification.
(i) The provinces of Malange, Benguela, Huambo, Huila and Kuando
Kubango were visited in December for the Mid-Season Assessment, which
is being coordinated by the WFP led Vulnerability Assessment Group .
The assessment is taking place December 2000 - January 2001 and will
serve as the basis for a predicted change in overall strategy. This
change would include moving away from the current large scale food
distributions, to a three fold plan including: (i) Emergency response
via free distributions for newly arrived IDPs and returnees (ii)
Emergency/recovery via safety nets and (iii) Recovery/rehabilitation
via food for work. The results from the VA will be consolidated
during the third and fourth weeks of January. Evaluation will be done
at the municipal level to see to what extent the assumptions are
valid.
(C) Sudan
(a) More than 3.2 million Sudanese are facing serious food and water
shortages due to the combined effect of civil war and a widening
drought. WFP is urgently asking donors to contribute to its appeal
for USD 106 million in order to feed 2.5 million people in the
country. This includes 1.3 million women and 460,000 children. WFP
2001 appeal is targeted to purchase 117,000 tons of food. This is
almost double the number of people and tonnage that was needed in
2000.
(b) WFP is currently air-dropping food to people in the hardest hit
regions of East Equatoria and Jonglei, as part of its 13-plane
airbridge operation. The organisation expects that the regions of
Northern Darfur and Northern Kordofan will also be severely affected.
(D) Burundi
(a) Christmas Day and Eid-el-Fitr were celebrated without major
security incident in the capital Bujumbura. People also celebrated
the advent of the year 2001 without incident, although rumours of
possible attacks circulated in Bujumbura. Security forces were
deployed and increased security measures including identity control
were reinforced.
(b) During the past two weeks a serious ambush occurred at Muhweto
area, between Muramvya and Bujumbura Rural provinces. The ambush
