caught three vehicles including a lorry and two buses. One of those
buses was ensuring transport between Bujumbura and Kigali and was
managed by a Rwandan transport company. Witnesses said the bus was
shot at and stopped by assailants who demanded all the passengers to
step out. All but four passengers were executed, including a
voluntary worker from Britain and students from Canada and Europe.
Another ambush occurred in the southern province of Bururi where five
people were reported killed. Incursions of rebels continued to be
reported in Rutana province and fifteen people were reported killed
in the attack perpetrated in Bukemba commune.
(c) The Provincial Director of Agriculture and Livestock in Mwaro
province announced that bad weather could possibly affect the crops
in the province. Heavy rains mixed with hail in some areas provoked
erosion and floods in valleys hence spoiling plants in fields. He
also reported that fertilisers and pesticides were not sufficient in
the province.
(d) A joint monitoring mission between the Government of Burundi, WFP
and UNICEF was conducted in Muramvya and Mwaro provinces to monitor
food availability. The mission found that food availability might be
reduced due to bad precipitation. Concerning the nutritional
situation, the mission found global malnutrition rate of 11.6 percent
and 1.5 percent of severe malnutrition in Muramvya province. As for
Mwaro province, the global malnutrition rate was estimated at 9
percent whereas the severe malnutrition rate was at 2 percent. These
rates may, however, rise as food availability is not ensured in the
future due to bad weather.
(e) A total of 168,440 beneficiaries in Kirundo province, who are
victims of drought, were assisted by WFP during week 51. A total of
329 tons of food were distributed. In Bujumbura Rural 99 tons were
distributed by implementing partners to a total of 20,121
beneficiaries. Due to security precautions and holiday season most
partners did not carry out distribution through week 52.
(f) WFP authorised dispatch of 54 tons of food to 14 projects
benefiting 4,576 people. WFP staff monitored seven of these projects
assisting orphans, street children, chronically ill persons and
physically handicapped people. The staff found that some of those
projects were not regularly filling stock lists and provided
necessary information and advice in line with WFP requirements.
(E) West Africa Coastal
(1) Guinea
(a) ECOWAS decided on 28 December to send 1,676 peacekeepers to the
border region between Guinea and Sierra Leone, in order to halt the
spreading of insurgency and to get relief to tens of thousands of
endangered civilians. The troops will be deployed by mid-February.
(b) Hundreds of Sierra Leonean refugees, seeking passage home,
continue to stream daily in crowded transit centres of Conakry.
Nearly 2,400 refugees are presently at the centre. The
UNHCR-chartered ship made its fifth trip to Freetown, bringing the
total number of refugees who have been repatriated to 1,560.
Thousands of refugees are emerging daily from the bush, north of the
south-western town of Guekedou.
(c) The Government has given approval for 60,000 refugees north-west
of Kissidougou. Two other sites, north and west of Faranah, will
shelter up to 40,000 refugees.
(d) In the past week, WFP has carried out two assessment missions.
The first one took place in Kankan, where additional staff were hired
for registration and distribution to compensate for the lack of
implementing partners on the ground. It is estimated that 15,000
displaced are present in Kankan, most of them IDPs. The second
assessment took place in Faranah, where 18,500 people have been
displaced (11,000 IDPs and 7,500 refugees).
(2) Sierra Leone
(a) The head of the UN peacekeeping mission UNAMSIL met rebel leaders
to discuss the prospects for UN deployment in rebel controlled areas
under the current ceasefire. He travelled to the central town of
Magburaka with humanitarian officials to talk to the rebel leader.
(b) Sierra Leonean refugees displaced in south-east Guinea continue
to be repatriated to Freetown. Some will be able to go back as
returnees to their areas of origin, if safety allows. People who are
from rebel controlled territory will, however, come back as IDPs and
be reinstalled on temporary settlement in areas of relative safety.
WFP and the humanitarian community continue to increase contingency
measure to deal with the possible large influx of returning refugees.
(3) Liberia
(a) A UN panel recommended a total embargo on diamonds coming from
Liberia. The panel also recommended that the UN Security Council
consider an embargo on overseas missions by Liberian authorities and
diplomats.
(b) Lofa and Nimba counties remain volatile due to cross-border
incursions. WFP has emptied its warehouses in Nimba county and
relocated its staff. In other counties, food distribution has been
running smoothly.
(F) FYRo Macedonia
(a) The registration and verification exercise for Humanitarian
Assisted Persons in FYRo Macedonia, dated 30 November 2000, shows a
total of 9,060 refugees. 7,656 of these refugees were targeted with
food assistance by WFP during the months of November/December.
(b) During the reporting period the November/December double
distribution of food commodities to refugees accommodated with host
families was completed. WFP will continue with double distribution
for refugees living with host families, due to winter conditions.
(c) 11 tons of various food commodities were delivered to collective
centres and 42 tons of various food commodities to MRC branches
during the reporting period.
(End WFP Emergency Report No 01)

Original_Search_Title: Saving Somalia Without The Somalis
Headline: Somalia: Saving Somalia Without The Somalis
Body_Text:
London — For Somalis committed to peace and reconciliation in their country, the initial phases of "Operation Restore Hope" has had precisely the opposite effect.
If we turned back the clock a few weeks, we would see a Somalia lit up with many signs of hope. Throughout the country, ordinary Somalis were taking the initiative to bring the future of their country under control.
In the south, there were no such dramatic breakthroughs. However, a series of local agreements were making it possible for emergency relief to be delivered in a way unprecedented in the last year.
In the town of Baidoa, the heart of the famine zone, the success of local negotiations enabled food to be trucked from the airstrip to the town and local villages, with very low rates of looting. In the area around Baidoa, truckers were succeeding in bringing in food supplies, so that maize and sorghum were plentiful and cheap in the local markets.
Follow us on WhatsApp | LinkedIn for the latest headlines
There were some serious incidents, such as the looting of a warehouse and a relief convoy, but the situation was clearly improving. The region's death rate fell by 90% between July and November.
At a higher political level, there were signs of optimism too. For the first time in several years, elders and intellectuals from a variety of clans were meeting and beginning to agree that it was essential to isolate the warlords and to develop those structures of Somali society that have some accountability to ordinary people. Hawiye elders who had formerly supported Gen. Mohammed Farrah Aidid were increasingly of the view that their interests were better served by isolating him.
During October, Aidid's power was visibly eroding as the clan elders became more assertive and independent.
Another promising, albeit low-key initiative, was a series of meetings for intellectuals and elders sponsored by the European Community. This was the first casualty of the U.S. military deployment. As soon as President George Bush announced his intention of sending troops to Somalia, the situation became so unstable that the initiative had to be called off.
Although Somalia does not have a central government, important political, economic and social structures remain in place, however battered. The key personnel in the relief programs are Somalis. Without the expertise, political know-how and hard work of clan elders, doctors, nurses, relief officials, truck drivers and volunteers, international programs would have stood no chance of success.
But how many elders, professionals and humanitarian workers have been consulted about the U.S. plan? None. Those we have spoken to are appalled by the prospect of foreign troops arriving without consultation and without a well-thought-out program aimed at political reconciliation, disarmament and reconstruction. Such arrogance undermines credibility of local leadership and damages the recovery of Somalia's civic structures.
Other initiatives also foundered as the U.S. military moved in. The delicate web of negotiated agreements that had sustained the progress in Baidoa broke down. Aidid's militia, fleeing Mogadishu in advance of the U.S. Marines, went on a last-chance looting spree in Baidoa. And each politician and warlord is now vying to see what advantage can be gained from the U.S. military occupation. Previously negotiated agreements to establish workable relationships mean nothing.
The result was an orgy of violence in Baidoa - the killing of at least 70 people, the displacement of many thousands of civilians and the forced closure of the relief programs. Death rates escalated from about 40 to about 100 per day.
Among the most troubling episodes was the much acclaimed diplomatic "coup" in which Aidid and self-proclaimed Interim President Mohammed Ali Mahdi embraced each other. Careful observers of Somali politics noted that their peace accord was not the breakthrough that the media heralded, since many of its elements were already in place.
The announcement of a future national reconciliation conference was no more than the repetition of a former agreement. The tough questions that have blocked progress, such as how to decide who would attend were avoided.
These two warlords are detested by most Somalis as major war criminals. It is deeply worrying that the United States decided to deal so publicly with these thugs within days of arriving in Somalia. Both Aidid and Ali Mahdi crave international recognition. Up until now, they were denied that legitimacy, paving the way for more accountable and peace-loving elements of Somali society, notably the clan elders, to strive to marginalize them. In its eagerness to score diplomatic success for American television cameras, the United States has reinforced Aidid's murderous stranglehold on parts of the country.
U.S. occupation has raised the stakes of the Somali war game. With the military operations appearing to lead inexorably to a United Nations trusteeship, the warlords are vying for the patronage of that neo-colonial rule. And the United Nations, whose negligence and incompetence make it a prime culprit in the disaster of Somalia, is eager to avoid public scrutiny of its past actions.
An important element in the recent deterioration of the security situation was the forced resignation of Mohamed Sahnoun, the widely respected UN special envoy in Somalia. His brilliant strategy was to mobilize Somalis in helping to resolve the country's problems. He worked closely with clan elders, women's groups and intellectuals.
Unfortunately, his successor, Ismat Kittani, has not shown an interest in nurturing alternative structures to the warlords. When Kittani visited the northern territory of Somaliland recently, he behaved in the abrupt and undiplomatic manner that Somalis have come to expect of UN officials. Speaking to a committee of elders whose patient negotiation had brought to an end many months of bloody internecine strife, he arrogantly commanded them to produce a full agreement within two hours. The elders ordered Kittani out of their territory; they were right to do so.
On December 2, Rakiya Omaar, a lawyer from Somalia, was dismissed as director of Africa Watch for publicly disagreeing with unilateral U.S. military intervention in Somalia. Alex de Waal resigned as Africa Watch associate director after the group's parent body, Human Rights Watch, endorsed the deployment.
From AFRICA NEWS, December 21, 1992-January 3, 1993

Original_Search_Title: Globalisation Is Not New
Headline: Uganda: Globalisation Is Not New
Body_Text:
The question is, will the new phase of globalisation be less parasitic and more symbiotic or not?
ON 11 November, 2001, President Museveni addressed the UN General Assembly in New York. Below are excerpts of his speech
Globalisation
There is a lot of talk floating around regarding some catch words: New International Order, Globalisation, Global village etc. All these ebullient self-gratifying positions are, apparently, due to the end of the Cold War between Western countries and the former Soviet Union. Of course, the end of the dangerous and misguided rivalry between communism and capitalism is welcome and has indeed opened some new opportunities, if fully utilised by all concerned.
However, it must be pointed out unequivocally that many of the problems in the world today predate the Cold War; they predate the onset of communism in Russia in 1917. Slave trade started in the 1440s along the West African Coast. A shift from bleeding Africa through slavery to resident colonialism was ordained in Berlin in 1884. This was long before communism took over in Russia. Therefore, the Cold War could not have been responsible for these mistakes and for the complications they created, many of which we are still grappling with up to today, especially in Africa.
Therefore, the euphoria is not wholly justified. The song about globalisation, as I have pointed out repeatedly, is not a new tune at all. Since the 1440s, the Africans were globalised as slaves all over the world. My Christian name is Joel, a Jewish name. I am therefore already globalised! Many of my countrymen have got Arab names like Aziz, Musa because they are Moslems. We were globalised long ago. Unfortunately, though, that Globalisation Movement was parasitic, it was not symbiotic.
Therefore, the only new question we can legitimately and usefully ask is: Will the new phase of globalisation be less parasitic and more symbiotic or not? Will it be globalisation for everybody's mutual benefit, or the old story of parasitism on a global scale?
The parasitism in the world is not the sole responsibility of those who benefit from the inequality. Even the victims have always contributed to their marginalisation by their own wrong aims and methods before colonisation, during colonisation and, even after colonisation. African myopic chiefs were the main facilitators of the slave trade. Until very recently, the Europeans did not possess the technological means to subdue the African Continent as far as transport means (railway, weapons, or medicine (quinine) were concerned.
Without the fratricidal wars fomented by the African chiefs, neither slave trade nor colonialism would have been possible. We would have defeated the colonialists and forced them, right from the beginning, to co-operate with us for everybody's mutual benefit. Weakness on the side of the potential victim always tempts the aggressor.
Even today, however, the authorship of the inequality among peoples is still a joint responsibility of the victims (Africans, Arabs and other marginalised peoples) of the parasitic globalisation movement that is now 500 years old on the one hand, and the beneficiaries of this, hitherto, unequal and, in the past, evil movement on the other.
The beneficiaries of the hitherto parasitic globalisation movement have been the North Americans, the Europeans and the Japanese. On account of a variety of reasons, some of the formally colonised peoples (the Indians, the Indonesians, the Pakistanis, the South East Asians and Latin Americans) and the formerly semi-colonised peoples ( such as the Chinese) have made significant upward movements that are helping to slowly but surely, even out the balance of power in the world.
Some of the other peoples, however, on account of a number of endogenous and exogenous factors are still living as the wretched of the earth. Many of the Africans and some of the Arabs fall into this category of the still unredeemed of the earth. As I have said, the unredeemed are still so categorised partly on account of their own internal mistakes (sectarianism, xenophobia, unprincipled conflicts, strangulation of free enterprise, political balkanisation of their regions, strangulation of political freedom, etc) and, partly, on account of the still very unfavourable exogenous factors. The most unfavourable exogenous actor is lack of access to markets in North America, EU, Japan, China, India and Russia.
Trade
The song about aid is meaningless without access to markets. All protectionism, especially in the OECD countries, must end.
Subsidies to farmers of Europe must end if we are talking of a "global village" of symbiosis and not parasitism. I commend the American Government and the President, Mr. George Bush, for the AGOA (African Growth and Opportunity Act) initiative.
Although Uganda has not yet benefited from AGOA on account of our indigenous weaknesses accentuated by the blurred views of our multi-lateral institution partners, other African countries have started benefiting: Kenya, Madagascar, Lesotho and Nigeria.
This is good. The Americans are, at last, beginning to address their image as non-parasitic global villagers. The Europeans must catch up with the Americans in rubbing off this uncomplimentary label. Although they have talked about "everything but guns" going into Europe from Africa, they are still giving subsidies to artificial farmers in Europe. This distorts the trade in agricultural products.
As a consequence, out of US$ 1.2 trillion that is the value of the global trade in agricultural products, Africa gets only about US$20 billion (if you include the results of AGOA recently), which is about 2% of the total! At the same time, the OECD countries are spending US$361bn subsidising artificial farmers of those countries. Yet these are the countries that evangelise in the name of free trade! The preachers of free trade are practising incredibly expensive protectionism. What an unfortunate paradox. These double standards must end.
Africa has now removed some of the old impediments (endogenous factors) to private investment. The sanctity of private property is now almost a universal concept in Africa (nationalisation of private enterprises is no more); some of the African countries have got a consistently stable macro-economic framework (inflation in Uganda is now -0.3%); the African currencies are now convertible; a large part of Africa is very peaceful; infrastructure is reasonable; and democracy is widely practiced in Africa today.
We are even addressing the issue of excessive political balkanisation of the continent (with 53 states compared to three in North America) in a variety of ways, including economic blocks like SADC in Southern Africa, COMESA in East and Central Africa and ECOWAS in Western Africa. The world, therefore, needs to encourage these positive trends in Africa by opening up their markets on a quota-free, tarrif-tree basis. This will, ipso facto, force the multi-national investors to rush to Africa to invest there. They are already doing so on account of AGOA.
With more civilised aims and methods, balanced world development is possible and desirable for everybody, including the OECD citizens who are forced to eat inferior foods and are taxed to protect those poor quality foods against better foods from Africa. Whenever I travel abroad, I pack my own Ugandan foods (milk, millet-flour, fruits, legumes, chicken, honey and plantain bananas (matooke).
The other day my pineapple stocks ran out and my staff bought pineapples from a super market in UK, I just took one slice and terminated the whole exercise at once.
First of all, the pineapple is hard; it is less sweet, and had got an ammonia-like pungent taste. I had had the same experience in Washington. Why must the citizens of the world endure these deprivations on account of policies designed to serve narrow interest?
I will not eat pineapple again until I go back to Uganda.
Terrorism
The oppressors, the colonialists and those who sought to control the destiny of others, used barbaric methods: Genocide, forced labour, ethnocide, etc. It is amazing, therefore, that some of those who claim to be fighting for the liberation of the oppressed peoples also use barbaric means such as terrorism.
In the on-going debate about terrorism, I have not heard anybody bothering to define the difference between a freedom fighter and a terrorist. Mzee Nelson Mandela has been a freedom fighter until recently. Did you hear of him hijacking a plane; or planting a bomb in a restaurant? But Mr Mandela was a freedom fighter. Was he a terrorist? Not at all. The difference lies in the fact that, while a freedom fighter, sometimes, may be forced to use violence, he cannot use indiscriminate violence. I was forced to use violence against Idi Amin, but I never hijacked a plane or planted a bomb in a bar! In bars people just go to make merry; if you kill such people, what sort of revolutionary are you?
The one who uses indiscriminate violence is a terrorist, does not discriminate between combatants and non-combatants; between civilians and servicemen; between armed servicemen and unarmed servicemen.
Even if someone is a soldier, if he is off duty you should not attack him if you say you are a freedom fighter- this is against the laws of war. The terrorists fight a war without declaring one, that is why they hijack planes, plant bombs in populated centres, etc.
We normally get technical aid from other parts of the world: From Europe and other countries. I would like to give you technical aid on freedom fighting because we have done that very well. In Africa, since 1961, we fought wars of liberation in Mozambique, Angola, Guinea - Bissau (against the Portuguese), Zimbabwe, South Africa, Namibia (against minority whites), Uganda against Idi Amin, etc. We, however, never used terrorism -this is on record. We were always fighting combatant to combatant. Quite a number of times our comrades were using mass action-strikes, demonstrations, petitions, diplomatic struggle, etc. Not a single plane was hijacked by African freedom fighters although there were anti-colonial wars in all these countries. The actions by terrorists are misguided, criminal, and must be opposed by all as a matter of principle.
Africa was beginning to benefit from AGOA. Business has, however, now somewhat slumped in America. Therefore the terrorists, who claim to be fighting for the Palestinian cause, are objectively hurting the interests of Africa who have always been the allies of the Palestinians. This is counter-productive. Terrorists try to polarise the world on a wrong basis: Moslems vs Christians, etc. Exploitation does not know the boundary of race or religion.
Exploiters are found in all religions or races. The Arabs had to oppose Turkish imperialism. Yet both the Arabs and the Turks were Moslems. One of the 'monsters' of the last Century, Idi Amin, was a Moslem. We had to get rid of him to liberate everybody, including the Moslems. War has been going on in the Sudan for decades. Elements that have been claiming to act in the name of Islam have been the ones taking a wrong position in this conflict - a position of seeking hegemonism among the people of God.
I, therefore, this time, support the position of USA as we did in the Gulf-War in fighting and defeating these reactionaries profaning the name of freedom fighters. If necessary, all countries of the World opposed to terrorism should contribute troops and finish this job quickly. The coalition against terrorism should be regarded in the same way as the coalition against fascism in the 1930s and 1940s.
Nevertheless, the just aspirations of the Palestinian peoples and of other oppressed peoples like the people of Southern Sudan must be supported so that we get peaceful resolutions of these conflicts. I salute freedom and equality of all peoples of the World. Anybody with ambitions to dominate other human beings or exploit them has got illegitimate ambitions. We now have the chance to build a just World. Apart from ensuring the free will of all peoples, the most important instrument of emancipation is free trade, giving quota-free, tariff free access of African goods to the markets of the OECD countries and vice versa.
Africa is beginning to tame its conflicts. The conflict in Lesotho was resolved by an African mechanism. Recently, the Arusha Accord ended the conflict in Burundi. It is possible to resolve our age-old problems and, finally, become part of the "New World Order" and not just spectators of the process.

Original_Search_Title: Greetings To All of Those Who Feed Us
Headline: Ghana: Greetings To All of Those Who Feed Us
Body_Text:
Today is a holiday dedicated to our farmers, fishermen and all those who work in the agricultural sector.We cannot thank them enough.
Any country that is incapable of feeding itself cannot also talk of economic development.
Since we gained independence from Britain in 1957, we have proved totally incapable of satisfying our food needs from our own resources. Indeed, Ghana's markets are now swamped with food imports from all over the world. Some of these foods we have to source from other countries are foods we could easily produce ourselves. Let's take rice for example. Media reports have revealed that the country spends US$100 million annually to import rice, when Ghana should have been self-sufficient in rice production.
Even the basic staple of plantain has sometime had to come from our neighbour Cote d'Ivoire - a country with the same climatic characteristics as us. Curiously, even the plantains from the Cote d'Ivoire are much bigger than what we grow here.
We have become in some respects a dumping ground of sometimes-unwholesome food from abroad. Professor Akosa, the immediate past president of the Ghana Medical Association has been very concerned about our eating habits, especially when fatty turkey tails, imported - or rather discarded - from abroad became something of a delicacy.
Agriculture has suffered seriously since we gained independence and we must get our production, storage and distribution strategies right or we cannot get on to the next rung of the ladder of progress. Food must be available. Food must be cheap.
Once our food requirements are met, all else will fall in place.
We wish our food producers well. Ayeekoo, netuma, Akpe...

Original_Search_Title: Introduction of N5 Coin
Headline: Nigeria: Introduction of N5 Coin
Body_Text:
It seems to me that our economists and the various financial authorities in this country have spent time reading books and financial principles of many foreign lands like US, UK etc. that they forget the peculiarities of their homeland, Nigeria. They forgot our socio-cultural life and history as a nation. They forgot the ethnicity, religious, literacy problem and behavioural patterns of the people of Nigeria. If the people in authority know all these they would have even try to re-introduce N1 as a note as a way of giving purchasing power to N1 and more purchasing power to N5 rather than this ill-informed decision to introduce N5 as a coin. Recall that as soon as N1 was changed to coins that was when it lost its value and purchasing power. N1 is long forgotten as a denomination in our currency. May be some people in authority are to benefit immensely with the introduction of such coin because this is the antecedent of most policies in Nigeria (bye-bye to Chief Asiodu).
Otherwise, how can one explain that CBN authorities do not know or ignorance of the fact that introducing N5 as coin means phasing out the use of N5 and pushing up prices of goods of N5 to N10. Or does this not mean self-inflicted inflation just because of somebody's selfish interest and/or lack of the understanding of economics-of Nigeria and our local environment. We all know that nobody would like to start carrying the somewhat bulky coins about. The bus conductor will not like to collect it because of it bulkiness, hence if a 'drop' cost N5 somewhere the conductor will rather collect N10 note and so on. The groundnut seller, sobo seller, roosted plantain seller, the mallam's small 'supermarket' by the street corner all will reject N5 coin because of the bulkiness and rather collect N10 for those items that would have been sold for N5. The woman on the street who had budgeted N300 for a pot of soup for her family will have problems because items like pepper, maggi, salt, vegetable and other soup ingredients she would scaled for N5 per item may have to go up to N10 per item. This may push up her budget to N800 or even N1000 and the CBN would tell us they know what they doing? How many people can sincerely carry even N500 coin to make purchases or carry N500 coin for transport fares when going out.
Our bank notes are filthy rags and a shame to behold and carry about, but go to various bus stops at Ojota, Oshodi, Obalende, Mile 2, Orile to mention but a few you see genuine crisp new naira notes being advertised and sold at commission. You wonder where these notes come from because they are genuine; make no mistake they are the banks and CBN, yet the CBN feigned ignorance and did not think it wise to act appropriately. Of course they are involved in this illicit business.
G. A. N Nnabuchi, Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Digesting Uganda's Agriculture
Headline: Uganda: Digesting Uganda's Agriculture
Body_Text:
The chapter on soil and conservation has effective guidelines easily understood by farmers
Although agriculture is the backbone of the economy, it is embarrassing that it is very hard to come by informative sources on the subject.
Dr. J. D. Tothill made the first comprehensive account of agriculture in 1940 in the book Agriculture in Uganda, published by the Oxford University Press. This book was updated in 1970 by J. D. Jameson.
Now the good news. The National Agricultural Research Organisation (NARO) has released four books dealing with all aspects of agriculture. The books titled Agriculture in Uganda are in four volumes. They are all edited by Professor Joseph Mukiibi, the Director General of NARO.
"I was inspired by the need to update Jameson's 1970 book Agriculture in Uganda which had become completely outdated.
Agriculture in Uganda-Volume 1
This 466-page book gives general information about agriculture that provides a foundation for the reader to understand the other three volumes in the series. It gives background information on the physical environment, research, marketing and agricultural technology among other issues.
The book is ideal for students and teachers alike. It gives essential statistics on agricultural production and market trends within the country and the export avenues and potential. The chapter on soil and conservation is simple and has effective guidelines that are easily understood by farmers and extension officers.
With the widespread degradation of land throughout the country coupled with the ever reducing yields in the gardens, the details are invaluable.
The chapters on agricultural information services and agricultural extension and education need to be read side by side.
Whereas the information services are listed as the physical sources and custodians of agricultural information these appear more of use to the academic fraternity. But they are a good source of information to improve the up to date delivery of extension and education of the farming communities in the field.
Agriculture in Uganda-Volume 2
This volume concentrates on the crops sector discusses cereals, oil crops, roots and tubers, plantain and the traditional cash crops like cotton, coffee, cocoa, sugar cane and tobacco. It is an excellent source of information both for academic purposes as well as practical application.
Detailed instruction on how to plant crops, tend crops plus the common pests, diseases and their control is discussed.
The crop sector being the most important part of the agricultural production industry makes this 572 page-volume an essential inclusion in your reference library.
Agriculture in Uganda- Volume 3
This volume on Forestry in Uganda is 109 pages. Although forestry accounts for only 4% of the national agricultural GDP, it has tremendous potential for export substitution as well as economic wellbeing and environmental security.
Agriculture in Uganda Volume 4
This volume on livestock and fisheries is a section that makes Agriculture in Uganda a wholesome edition.
The 380-page volume discusses all sectors of animal production ranging from beef and dairy cattle production to poultry, pigs and bee keeping.
Fisheries and aquaculture in general is given prominence with presentation of production methods ideal to adopt for practical purposes.
Several research scientists with a wealth of experience have jointly written the four volumes. They are produced by the National Agricultural Research Organisation and the Technical Centre for Agriculture and Rural Cooperation(CTA).
ENDS

Original_Search_Title: Culture Shock
Headline: Ghana: Culture Shock
Body_Text:
As the time passes by, and only a month is left of my time here at the University of Ghana, I suddenly grow weary and sad about returning back to the states. Where else in this world besides Ghana can I purchase
toilet paper and plantain chips from a taxi window? The hissing sounds of Ghanaians trying to get my attention have gone from utter annoyance to sheer music to my ears.
The initial culture shock makes every little difference outrageously beautiful, and after living within the country of Ghana for more than three months with many lessons learned, I now feel prepared to handle any personal situation in any country I'm in with the patience and kindness that most Ghanaians would use. Thank you Ghana.
As I'm reminded still of American culture and its holidays on the calendar, I view the celebrations in a new manner. What is the essential meaning behind our holidays? Compared with Ghanaian cultural festivities, can Americans hold a candle? America's day of independence, the Fourth of July is the more justifiable of the holidays. Thanksgiving passed this past week, and as I dreamed of turkey, mashed potatoes, and pumpkin pie, and ate my palaver sauce instead. I realized what I was thankful for - my chance to live within Ghanaian culture for such a short period.
I depart in December, just in time to witness the overly consumer-oriented Christmas holiday. Most Ghanaian
Christians would agree that Christmas is essentially Jesus Christ's birthday, but most Americans would associate the day with evergreen trees and shiny presents. Quite a difference.
As I count down the days until my departure, I count the days until I'm living within the fast-paced, caffeine fuelled, money hungry country of America, and no longer laughing my days away with Ghanaians over a bowl of groundnut soup and fufu, or greeting Ghanaians whom I've never met before, just because we passed each other on the street. Not in New York City. Maybe I'm being a little cynical, and perhaps I don't have enough faith in my home country, but when one lives in such peace and happiness for such a long time here in Ghana, one has the right to complain.

Original_Search_Title: Neighbours, Kinsmen Celebrate Miss World
Headline: Nigeria: Neighbours, Kinsmen Celebrate Miss World
Body_Text:
It is no longer news that the historic feat of Agbani Darego at Sun City, South Africa late Friday has lifted Nigeria's and indeed Africa's image. What is interesting is the disbelief in which the crown carght people. A visit to Customs Barracks located on Simbiat Abiola Road, Ikeja, Lagos where the beauty queen was raised revealed lots of apprehension amongst her kinsmen who were still at a loss on the true situation.
From the entrance where about six young people clustered round a lady selling roasted plantain (boli) on the left side of the road through the un-manned gate there was no sign that a beauty queen was raised there. Inside the barracks towards the eastern wing some people were seen oblivious of the news making the waves across the globe. When their discussion was interrupted with the reporters mission their reaction, was that of disbelief as they repeatedly asked THISDAY reporter who was there to see things for himself if what they saw on television was for real.
A lady on the fat side, who we later got to realize is from Abonema, Rivers State as Darego threw caution to the winds in celebration thereby attracting attention from passers-by who became interested in the subject matter. And typical of Nigerians, encomium was freely poured on the lady of the moment. They were further elated when it was further confirmed by another lady who left almost immediately that the new Miss World was indeed raised there. The first salvo was from the youngster who claimed to have participated by voting on-line for the new beauty queen.
His anxiety was, however, doused by one Capt. D.D. Charles, a Kalabari, who voluntarily declared that the full meaning of the queen is Dagboarego literally meaning 'a torch bearer,' while her first name Agbani means 'destiny'. For him: "This is a victory that will be celebrated by all the good people of Kalabari." Another respondent, a custom officer who simply gave her name as Eni was too overwhelmed for comments.
It took her some minutes before she came to reality with the true situation, and she was all thanks to God for his goodness. According to her, "Agbani deserves what she has got because she is not only God fearing but a lady with good moral up-bringing."

have the chance to build a just World. Apart from ensuring the freewill of
all peoples, the most important instrument of emancipation is free trade,
giving quota-free, tariff free access of African goods to the markets of the
OECD Countries and vice versa.
Africa is beginning to tame its conflicts. The conflict in Lesotho was
resolved by an African mechanism. Recently, the Arusha Accord ended the
conflict in Burundi: It is possible to resolve our age-old problems and,
finally, become part of the "New World Orden" and not just be spectators of
the process.
I thank you

Original_Search_Title: For Omole, It's 50 Golden Cheers
Headline: Nigeria: For Omole, It's 50 Golden Cheers
Body_Text:
Yes, Sola Omole is 50. Or rather, his voice is 50. Still strong, vibrant and engaging, though bereft of its fabled 30 million nightly listeners on the powerful Nigerian Television Authority (NTA) Network news. The clarity, influence and power of the voice are, however, by no means diminished. No. The voice, and its owner, merely moved, providentially, to higher challenges and greener pastures. He now speaks for, and on behalf of, Chevron Nigeria Limited, a foremost oil and gas producing company with profound multinational impact, affluence and trans-border reach.
Today, his face betrays the battles of time with greying moustache as a symbolic trophy. His hairline has receded - and it is still receding, betraying a baldness that Nigerians identify with wealth but ascribed to stress by the more discerning precepts of science. He has also lost the battle against a protruding midsection, accepting the cross of a stubborn pouch. Finally, he has given up on the games boys play and, has instead, committed his ways (spelt life) to Christ. Today's torrents of "Hallelujah and amen" at celebrations marking his 50th natal year will be the unmistaken testimony of a new life.
Perhaps, only a few of those felicitating with him today would know that the celebrated broadcaster, turned public relations specialist, would have ended up as one of the thousands of Stamp Sellers in the employment of the Post and Telegraph (P&T) department, had he resigned himself to the first career opportunity available to him after his secondary school education. He may also have become a run of the mill salesman, selling photocopier machines if he had allowed the hardships of his earlier years of broadcasting to becloud his sense of mission.
In those inexplicable ironies of life, Omole's past has somewhat defined his future. We go back in time to November 14, 1951 when a housewife and a technician, living in the ancient city of Ibadan, had a baby boy, named Olusola. They offered him little in terms of material comfort but were generous in moral admonition and discipline. He was schooled in the Yoruba maxim that virtue is more precious than gold. The lifestyle of his mother, Felicia Idiegbia and father, Samuel Ajayi, struck a chord in the young Sola. He noticed that they lived simply in peaceful anonymity. The peace he cherished, the anonymity he disliked. He decided early in life to be popular without the travails of stardom - lack of peace. In his second year in secondary school at Fiditi Grammar School, Fiditi, (located in today's Oyo State), he informed his parents he would one day become a radio announcer, one of the most visible jobs available then. His father endorsed, with a caveat that he should go beyond the secondary school in his academic pursuit. At this stage, the colours that would adorn his life's canvas were emerging gradually - be a radio announcer, get good education and become an intensely private celebrity.
In 1977, when he was hunting for a degree, the choice of University of Ibadan was just natural. By 1980, when he was leaving, he had attained the highest peak of academic performance at the undergraduate level by making a first class in the Language Arts Department. It was the first ever and the only one since then to be awarded by the department. In his determined climb to professional prominence, the degree has been a worthy complement to his voice. Working hard was the flip side of life with a trading grandma who lived on the threshold of subsistence with her husband. After school, young Sola had to contribute to the welfare of the family by hawking plantain around the Abadina community. It was an unpretentious childhood that blended domestic poverty with the mental wealth of life in the ivory tower. In adulthood, Omole still remains a down-to-earth workaholic, performing any task, however menial it may appear, with dignity.
Marrying Biodun in 1980 was perhaps the most prudent decision of his past that has profoundly affected his today by giving him the peace of mind that he had seen and cherished in his parents. Like every successful broadcaster he had a retinue of awe-struck admirers. Biodun, he found totally different. Very unassuming, simple and divinely submissive, she fitted perfectly into his quest for privacy. She also lifted him spiritually by sustaining the Christian ethos drilled in him as a young boy by his mother, while also being a steadfast wife and dutiful mother to their three children - Odunayo, Sola Jr. and Temitope.
From his past was also a fortuitous meet with one John Adanini, his English Literature teacher at the Olivet Baptist High School, Oyo, where he studied for his Higher School Certificate (HSC). John later left his teaching job to work in the programmes division of the then Radio Nigeria (RN). If Radio was his passport to broadcasting, television was his visa to national acclaim and stardom. Though he loved his stay at the Radio House in Ikoyi, a daily regimen of trekking from CMS to Ikoyi forced him out of radio into salesmanship with Rank Xerox. He got the job on the recommendation of Mr Bode Alalade. At Xerox, his path crossed those of other celebrities like Oba Funsho Adeolu of the Village Headmaster television series and Segun Bucknor, the pop sensation of the 70s. After 10 months of selling photocopiers in Lagos and Kano, he knew he was like a minstrel in a cemetery. He left the job to study Communication Arts at the University of Ibadan and thereafter to Lagos for the mandatory National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) scheme. He was posted to teach English at a school on Apapa Road but for inexplicable reasons, the school rejected him.
His plight again took him to his old mentor - Bode Alalade, then Manager- Programmes and the late Martini Akande, then Manager News at the NTA. He not only served at NTA but also was offered a job at the end of the service as a senior editor/newscaster. His industry soon caught the attention of Engr. Vincent Maduka, then Director-General of NTA. For Maduka, a good newscaster must transcend the mellifluous and show depth by being able to shape the news.
"By 1985, I knew it was time to move on. I had come out with a first class from the University of Ibadan. I had attained a horizon in broadcasting. I had a lot of responsibilities - wife and children. I remember that I informed a lot of people that I wanted a new job, among them Chief Michael Omolayole and Dr Chris Kolade, hoping I would just get a job just like that."
It never happened like that. Rather, he credits divine intervention for his eventual move because someone called NTA to ask for him but he was not around. The typist took the name of the caller and his number and had the presence of mind to deliver the message. But Omole's attempts to contact the caller failed initially. At further attempts, the caller turned out to be a director of AGIP. He met the man who informed him that a colleague in Gulf Oil Company (GOCON), the precursor of Chevron Nigeria Limited, wanted somebody to handle media relations for the Company.
"I went to meet the gentleman at AGIP who said he had called on his friend in Gulf to inform him that he was looking for an engineer. But the man in Gulf also said he was looking for somebody for media relations. God then put my name in his mind at that particular time. He mentioned Sola Omole and Adeyinka (the man in Gulf) said get him (Omole) for me. That was how I got to Adeyinka at the Mandillas House) and was interviewed and got the job as Advisor- Public communications," he recollects.
From this job, he moved on to community relations. It was relatively an easy job then. Communal conflicts were almost non-existent and there was no CNN to turn issues in a remote Nigerian village into an international agenda. But as the terrain of Public Relations in the oil industry became more complex and volatile, Omole's career bloomed and blossomed. By 1991, he had become the Manager-Public Affairs, four years later, he was elevated to the post of General Manager-Public Affairs. In 1999, he was elected Fellow, Nigerian Institute of Public Affairs and, by this year, as if in commemoration of his 50th birthday, he was appointed General Manager-Government and Public Affairs.

Original_Search_Title: NDC is a Casualty of Its Own Politics of Scandal-Hunting
Headline: Ghana: NDC is a Casualty of Its Own Politics of Scandal-Hunting
Body_Text:
Until the opening of parliament this year with NDC and NPP switching majority and minority roles respectively, I was an ardent supporter of the NDC in absentia. I thought the team of politicians from the party were well educated and matured and could participate in sensible debate. I was wrong.
The NDC has been exposed, their cover blown just like the wind blows off the feathers of a chicken to expose its rump.
What turned me off is the puerile attitude of the party's parliamentarians, spearheaded by Alban Bagbin, Adjaho, Asiedu Nketsia, as well as the ramblings of some stalwarts like Spio-Garbrah and a few other notorious ones. The ex-president, Rawlings', favourite term can be visited on this group in the nation's legislative assembly and the party's scretariat. They have, gradually, altered the name of the party from National Democratic Congress to National Dzimakplatic Congress.
It started with the presentation of plantains to parliament when the ruling party had been in power for only three months. Even children know that it takes a year or longer for plantains to mature and yield fruits. If the party faithful wanted cheap plantains at that time, they should have planted them the previous year.
Then came the baseless Sahara, Kufuor private residence renovation, the Jake contract to himself allegations, all unsubstantiated, then the recent gift from a farmer to the president to relieve the state of forty-one
million cedis of debt "scandal." A friend joked that the opposition had the right to attack the gift as "immoral." He reasoned that it would place the donor at an advantage. Yeah, like the farmer would want the president to ditch his vice and replace him (the vice) with him (the farmer); he would talk the president into moving the capital to his farm; he would earn the
contract to supply special dried cocoa beans to the castle, all for a measly 41 million cedis!
Aside from this, Mills and Rawlings have been saying the nation has regretted voting the NPP to power and that they would like the NDC back. Yeah right! It's like the victim of a robbery beckoning the thief who's hurrying away "Hei, Bra Awi, you frogot to steal the VCR!
Come back and take it!"
I had been misled to support the party and now that I've heard what they did to the tax payers of the country, I wouldn't let them near any checkbook that belongs to the nation or the community in which I live. I have not decided what party to lean to, but meanwhile I'll lend my support to the rebuilding of Ghana.
I couldn't believe my ears when I heard on the news that Serlomey overpaid a car by $7,000. Seven grand!
And Kuntor and Rawlings. If it were a paperback detective novel, readers would have found it too stale and shallow. They wouldn't pay one cedi for it.
Last but not the least, I'll call on the leader of GDPP, Dan Lartey, to lend his support to bring things to normal rather than calling for early elections. If we did have early elections at all, he would not win, the CPP would not win, the PNC would not win two seats, and the NDC would lose big time this time.
If Dan Lartey insists on early elections, he should choose a date from December 1 to December 6, 2004.  That is when the people of Ghana will be prepared to retain or change government. Everyone should be heard in a democracy, and Dan Lartey would not be made an exception.
I would rather advice President Kufuor to use his four years to track down stolen state monies and the culprits, fight corruption and win, make the police service law-abiding and law-enforcing instead of law-breaking, and set in place a favourable atmosphere for investing in the country. That way the next President will have an easy ride through recognition. I'll love, dearly, any leader who does these things for us even if he does not build roads and bridges.
NDC, the more you witch-hunt for scandals by the NPP, the stronger it (NPP) gets and the more your supporters get turned off from you.  On the other hand, the more the NPP performs their "witch-hunt" on looting former policy makers of the NDC, the more points they score among the populace because the people suspected they had been hoodwinked and need to know more in order to recover the loot.
I, for one, burned my NDC membership card not long ago.

Original_Search_Title: How Not to Use the Child
Headline: Nigeria: How Not to Use the Child
Body_Text:
Have you seen those young boys who daily hang on the doors of reckless and fast moving Danfo and Molue buses on the ever-busy Lagos roads lately? What about those other boys and girls ages ranging between five to seventeen, who dangerously meander Lagos vehicles to display their wares of pure water, groundnut, gala, bread, plantain chips, chewing sticks, among others at traffic jams? Or may be you have noticed some other children who are used as fronts for alms begging for their "big bosses" who sit closeby across the Lagos metropolis?
These indeed, are some of our unfortunate future leaders of this great country "the giant of Africa" and the most populous black nation of the world richly endowed with wonderful natural resources.
To some of these children, life begins in the mornings under the bridges of Lagos, busy corners of Oshodi, Idumota, Mile 12, Mushin, Mile 2 and perhaps Ojuelegba and ends either in one of those packed and unserviceable cars and buses that now adorn many streets and bus terminals across the metropolis or by the road sides after a hard day's labour. The story is the same across the country.
These are Nigerian, children whose fortunate counterparts are pursuing good education in Atlantic Hall, Igbinedion Comprehensive School, Corona School, American International School, Ajayi Memorial, Heritage School, Kaduna, Caleb International School among others.
The average Nigerian child is one of the most bastardised children, the world over. Very early in life, the child is exposed to horrible experiences. 'beast of burden'. According to the 1998 International Labour Organisation (ILO) report, child labourers in Nigeria ranges between 20 and 30 percent of the entire children population. This menace of child labour has assumed a frightening dimension recently in that hundreds and thousands of Nigerian children, are daily being ferried across the country to neighbouring countries like Cameroon, Gabon and Equatorial Guinea for forced labour.
In our cities across the country, there are pathetic stories of children who were withdrawn from schools or who dropped out of schools to engage in some life threatening chores to make ends meet.
For Nkem Okafor, a 15-year old boy from Abia State, he cannot avoid going to the Mile 12 bridge daily to sell the goods given to him by his guardian to take advantage of the usual traffic jam on the Ketu- lkorodu expressway. Unless he is completely down medically he cannot afford to miss the daily ritual, otherwise he risks hunger and shelter for a good number of days. Nkem's case is not different from that of Miss Nike Yusuf, a 13 year old girl from Oyo State, who must be at the same spot as Nkem to sell 'pure water'. Nike who cannot volunteer any correct statement in English language, must not sell below the five bags average of pure water, daily unless accusation of negligence of duty await her before the "high court" of her madam, Alhaja Akinlade Any day Nike fails to meet up with her target, she had better not come home because food will be a stranger to her.
For Boleji Akeem, a fourteen-year boy who must hang at the door of a Danfo passengers bus as conductor daily, going back to school is better forgotten. The young boy said, he has been in the job for about two years now shortly after he ran away from his master who was to train him as a mechanic. Bolaji said one woman brought him from Iseyin to Lagos about three years ago only to hand him over to Baba Tope, the auto mechanic in Owutu mechanic village in the Ikorodu area of Lagos state. Stories like these abound all over the country and they are unfortunately on the increase daily.
A major source of worry in this child labour phenomenon is the seeming government's non-challant posture to the development. Occasionally, the Lagos, Kano, Anambra, Bauchi, Abia, Enugu and Imo states governments have had to make their usual ineffective 'fire-brigade' approach at tackling the problem of rounding up some of these children and taking them to remand homes for some assistance. These children usually find their way back into the waiting hands of their slave masters few days after they were picked up. To say that many Nigerians enjoy and greatly patronise the services of these less paid kid labourers is an understatement.
Unlike what obtains in advanced societies, Nigeria's legislations on the use of children for labour are fraught with a lot of inadequacies and contradictions.
Whereas the labour Act defines a child as any person under the age of twelve the criminal procedure act says a child is any person below fourteen years. Also the law of contract says that one cannot validly enter a contract until he/she is 21. Nevertheless, the constitutional law forbids any person under 18 years from voting or being voted for in general elections in the land inferring that they are children.
These seemingly legal controversies have not favoured the actual description and or definition of who a child really is in Nigeria. For instance, Section 59 (3) of the labour act allows the day-to-day basis of child labour so long as the children return each night to their place of residence. This provision is invariably a negation to the United Nations Convention on the Right of the Child signed by Nigeria in 1992. Legal experts have argued that even though the country signed the UN Convention on the Right of the Child long ago, it shall not become part of the local laws except it is duly passed by the National Assembly thereby making such treaty part of Nigerian law
As the legal music goes on, different dimensions of child labour now exist. Apart from hawking items on major roads and market centres, children are now made to engage in commercial sex work within and outside the country. The Italian connection readily comes to mind. This writer was fortunate to visit the Nigeria Immigration Service transit camp at Alagbon Close Ikoyi Lagos to catch a glimpse of some of the teenage "prostitutes" deported to Nigeria by the Italian government. These are children who are supposed to be in useful educational or vocational pursuits. Tell me, what is left in life for a 17-year old girl who have been exposed to big time prostitution in far away Italy and other major European cities. For some of them that are back home, where in the family or social strata or even trade will they be dedicatedly engaged in? Remember, we are talking about future leaders. These are girls who are ignorantly choosing to destroy their youthful years.
It has been revealed that a powerful syndicate usually trick these money conscious girls into the awful trade only to abandon them after serious damage have been done to them. Many of these girls today are HIV/AIDS carriers and the dangers they pose to society is in explicable considering their active sex life.
A very painful conclusion to draw from the above is that a great number of our youths are being wasted. Many have become hopeless and frustrated. These invariably will constitute great threats to the few ones we can now boast of undergoing good education. The question is how can these few educated ones cope with the large armies of frustrated youths tomorrow? Would our cities not be turned to slaughter table of more terrble dimensions than we are experiencing in some parts of the country today? What is the way out of this impending rash shock on the societal fibres?
I hereby recommend that governments must stop the normal fire brigade approach of picking up hawkers, street boys/girls and under age apprentices off the roads, who later find their ways back.
Rather government should engage the services of the various media apprati - a media mix approach to churn out effective enlightenment campaigns that will dissuade parents and guardians from forcing their children and wards into child labour. This is based on the premise that our economic policies are thoroughly implemented to have human face on the teeming masses. The television radio jingles and newspapers campaign messages must be embarked upon for mass mobilization on the need for all forms of child labour practices to be abandoned. Also the traditional rulers, opinion leaders and socio- cultural groupings across the federation, must all be sensitized to ensure that any child found doing jobs that are not supposed to be his/her responsibilities is shouted down, the parents brought before public court of ridicule and the child made to feel inferior in the society.
The government must ensure that the Universal Basic Education (UBE) programme is faithfully executed to cater for the children of the haves and the have nots.
Moreso, proper ratification of the UN convention of the Rights of the Child must be made through the National Assembly to make it a local legal instrument. Courts should be empowered to try parents and guardians and even children who fall foul of the law.
Non-governmental organisations (NGOs) must come in with children capacity building exercises like scholarship awards, seminars, symposia and provision of infrastructural facilities to assist governments' efforts.
Amos writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Street Children a Lost Generation
Headline: Sierra Leone: Street Children a Lost Generation
Body_Text:
The prevalence of street children in the capital, Freetown is not
anything anyone could fail to notice. It is a common sight to see
children between the ages of 9 and 12 hawking on the street, selling
ice water, yoghurt, plastic bags, sweets, chewing gum, biscuits,
peanuts, plantain chips, to name a few. By Labour standards it is
improper for these children to be selling goods at such a tender age
but if labour laws were to be enforced more strictly, many of them
would go hungry.
The latest method children have adopted to make quick money is by
assisting the disabled and blind adults to beg in the streets. The
disabled or blind people move along major roads where vehicular
traffic is often most tense.
Whenever there is traffic hold-up, say at PZ roundabout, the beggars
are led by these children to the vehicles where they beg money from
drivers and their passengers. But for most part of the day they beg
from one shop to the other. They will also beg passers-by who
normally snub them or give them a token.
This practice is significantly more pronounced in Freetown than in
other urban areas and more and more children are becoming involved in
this activity as a means of earning a living. Abu Conteh, 11 came to
Freetown from Mile 91 to seek a living and soon became involved in
the business. He is fed every morning by an adult beggar and sleeps
on the street, usually around PZ. He and the beggar make
approximately Le20,000 (twenty thousand Leones) per day two-third of
which went to Abu. He has now been doing this for the past three
years.
James Bull, 9, used to go to school but had to give up his education
to help his blind father beg on the streets.
Marie Ballu Mansaray from Bo is from a family of eight children and
the only way for her to make ends meet was to join in this business
of helping the disabled and the blind beg for money. She makes about
Le11,000 a day and says she is happy with this situation.
These are just a very few examples of hundreds of children caught up
in this unfortunate situation. Although many of them may be content
with the sums of money they are earning, yet they are Sierra Leone's
future generation and their great presence on the streets shows how
many of them are missing out from many educational facilities. Like
one educationist put it, without any strong educational foundation
these children are a lost generation. A huge chunk of this country's
future human resources is going astray, he added.
A high percentage of children in Sierra Leone fail to attend school
due to poverty. Despite government's declaration of free primary
education, many of these children abandon school to fend for
themselves due to lack of parental care. Most often these children's
parents are unemployed or have large and unplanned families.
If something is not done soon about this situation, more of Sierra
Leone's children will turn to the streets instead of enrolling in any
type of formal or informal education. The government and other
stakeholders such as Non-Governmental Organisations and individuals
must act now by providing alternatives in the form of education,
shelter and food for these children.
Parents who simply neglect and refuse to take responsibility for
their children have also contributed to this problem and must be
tracked down and be forced to face up to their responsibilities.
In many cases, street children are the products of teenage
pregnancies. In certain communities, peer pressure encourages girls
as young as 15 years to give birth and some of these young mothers
have up to three or four children from different men before they
attain the age of 20. There are over 3,000 of such children to be
found in Freetown alone.
Most of these children have nowhere to seek refuge especially those
from outside Freetown. They sleep mostly on the streets, in public
places such as the wreckage of the famous City Council building.
This writer is urging all stakeholders, NGOs, national and foreign
organisations and members of the civil society to pull together their
resources in an effort to minimize the presence of children on the
streets of Sierra Leone. It is the responsibility of Sierra Leoneans
in general to help in whatever way to salvage these children. They
are this country's future.

Original_Search_Title: Chances Are... You'll Enjoy The Volta
Headline: Ghana: Chances Are... You'll Enjoy The Volta
Body_Text:
The Volta Region of Ghana holds a lot of fascination and excitement which until you go there, you'll never know. PaJohn Bentsifi Dadson, just returned from there, and wants to go back.
When I realised I had to stay overnight at Ho, the capital of the Volta Region, I was not amused. I had always had this impression that I needed to 'psyche' myself up to make the trip there, more so, stay overnight. I visited Ho the first time several years ago when I was much younger. I had an unpleasant experience that time, so I was of the conviction that Ho was not one of the places I particularly wanted to visit. Like many Ghanaians, I had quite a hang-up about the people from this part of our country, and didn't think very much about their lot. Never mind that many of my closest friends come from here.  Moreover, with all the tourism talk, and not finding much about the Region to talk about, it was the last place I ever thought had anything exciting to offer.
So this Monday evening, as I sit in this VanefSTC bus speeding towards the town, I find my mind rushing through all kinds of unpleasant possibilities about where to stay and what to do. Luckily I would only be there over night. My meeting was almost informal, so we could have it tonight and then be on my way first thing in the morning with the VanefSTC bus which goes there from Accra only once a day at 3pm and leaves there back to Accra at 4am!
My uneasiness about doing this trip was compounded when the bus became ready for boarding one and quarter hours after the scheduled time of 3pm. Having travelled to Kumasi, Cape Coast, Takoradi and a few other places on their buses lately, and each time getting improved service, I was most disappointed and annoyed when this time round they were so late. More so when no one from the office at the Tudu branch where the Ho buses leave, came to apologise or even offer an explanation. Just as I was about to return my ticket for my money back, the bus came. I joined it only after I considered the urgency of the trip, and once we were on the way the bus was fast and drove rather very smoothly. Like clockwork, we were driving on the beautiful suspension bridge over the Volta Lake at Atimpoku within an hour, with approximately another hour and half to go.
Night was fast approaching as we careered on, my expectations getting wilder by the minute. By the time we got to the town, it was completely dark. As I no longer wear wrist watches these days, I couldn't really tell the time, but soon I could decipher it; as we halted at a red traffic light, I could hear the GTV news at 7 jingle being played in the distance. Looking out of the window, the town looked lively especially nearer the bus station. Aside the typical calls from mates looking for passengers, there was the usual loud music from a sound system over there, and this way, little kids, in tatters, playing, laughing with no care in the world. The night air was cool, with the distinctive smell of kelewele - hot spiced fried plantains - lingering along, and reminding me of how hungry I was by then.
It was a complete town that I saw, with every major governmental and corporate institution in place; the IRS, Ghana News Agency, Ghana Commercial Bank, Ghana Roads and Highways, Barclays Bank, and recently opened, Readwide Bookshop! We even drove past the stadium along Starlet '91 Street.
I was meeting my party at the Chances Hotel, and I was to stay overnight there tonight. Not having heard anything about this hotel previously, I went there nonchalant and unexpectant. As it was dark, I was just as unimpressed as we entered the unassuming grounds. The reception was just as simple, but clean and modern, and there sat Gladys, the first pleasantry since I arrived in Eweland, well, after the familiar smell of kelewele.
Gladys checked me in and before long I was ushered into room 27. There, my excitement rising, I appreciated that appearances really are deceptive! From the reception to my room, we passed a manicured lawn of perfect green grass decorated with white lamps, coconut trees, cosy benches with tables, and a sand bay with swings and things for kids to play with. Room 27 was in an unfinished block, which looked suspicious at night. But inside, the room was a breath of fresh air. Air-conditioned, it was floored with white tiles, and white walls, with a twin bed that looked king-size. The room was too spacious for a hotel room, and as soon as Alex, the porter left after settling my bag down, I began to twirl and jump and kick in an ecstatic manoeuvre that would have had you just stand and watch in utter amusement! By Jove, I was excited. This was nothing like I expected and I then began to relax, feeling I could like this place. Brightly lit, and just as spacious with hot shower, the private bathroom was right next to the room, I found myself on the floor, pushing-up before I entered the shower where I took a long most refreshing wash.
I met my associate for our meeting in the 80-seater City Gate restaurant. Doris, the waitress, was funny. Though they didn't have most of what I wanted in the menu, she wouldn't tell me what they had ready, and insisted I tell her what I wanted, just for her to say, "We don't have it." Eventually, I had a sumptuous plate of Pepper Steak with Rice, and it was delicious. Apparently the chef was French from Togo, and his repertoire on the menu was quite exciting as well, with intricate salads, stirfry's, and grilled fishes and other proteins.
A drink on the lawn with all the coconut palm surrounding us was nice. I felt completely relaxed. Nothing at the moment reminded me that I was in Eweland, a place that I had a hang-up about. I wished that the night wouldn't end. But soon I was back in my room. We were to return to Accra at dawn, setting out latest by 5. On M-Net was showing an interesting movie whose title I didn't see. I watched some of it and soon drifted into slumber. My wake-up call alarmed me. I managed to drag myself out of bed and took another hot shower, and joined my associates out at the car park, sorry to be leaving. The tranquillity that existed at this hotel, which is set on the outskirts of the town, was loud. In the darkness, I decided to take another last tour of the grounds before leaving. I noticed a 120-seater conference hall, an Internet cafÃ©, an art and craft shop, also stocked with vacation necessities like toothbrushes etc...
Emmanuel Chance, a builder by profession, has been building this illustrious and tasty hotel for the past ten years, literally putting up a chalet a year. Today, it has 16 self-contained chalets and another 52 rooms with twin beds by the end of the year. Plans are afoot for a swimming pool, and Mr Chance is also plotting an adventure trail up the nearby Kabakaba Mountain which actually engulfs the area, giving it a greenery which is quite rare. And as he tells me, the Volta has a whole lot more than just the Lake to offer including the Wli falls, caves and grottos in many parts, 'hikeable' mountains and monkey sanctuaries.
The drive back to Accra was equally stimulating, as I observed sunrise driving along the foot of lush Peki Hills, whose beyond, I understood, lay Kpeve, a town set along the Volta Lake, to my right, and to my left, a large expanse of land dotted with shrubs and trees. Having warmed up to the Volta, I could take in more of the Region's beauty, which I think is understated. In two and half-hours, we were driving into Accra on the Tema motorway, back into the traffic, wondering why I had not done the Volta Region long ago.

Original_Search_Title: Boat Owners Complain to World Bank
Headline: West Africa: Boat Owners Complain to World Bank
Body_Text:
The transport sector stakeholders meeting has ended at the Bank of
Sierra Leone Complex, Kingtom.
Addressing World Bank officials, the Assistant Secretary General of
the Seirra Leone United Boat Owners Association (SLUBOA), Mr. S.O.G.
Turay spoke about the newly created Safety and Security section,
which inspects boats for sea worthiness and machines for efficiency
before embarking on any trip at sea.
He said they have improvised safty gears for boat crew. These
measures, he said, have cut down on sea accidents, adding that their
fishing boats are believed to be delivering nearly 85% of the fish
consumed in the country. Mr. Turay however told the World Bank
officials the constraints their association is facing, including
landing sites which he described as deplorable.
He said that several boats have been lost because of poor ancorage
and expressed hope that the long awaited World Bank contruction
project on landing sites would start soon.
Dilating on piracy, Mr. Turay said that fifteen fishing boats at the
fishing ground at Plantain Island have so far been attacked and
dispossessed of their outboard engines, fishing gears, fuel and food,
leaving them at the mercy of cruel stroms. Several boatowners, Mr.
Turay noted, have suffered the same fate. He therefore called on the
World Bank officials for help inorder to halt the ugly practice.
The Assistant Secretary General noted that the cost of maritime
supplies is killing the artisanal boat industry, He said there is
only one shop in Freetown selling outborad machine at Le8 million
which is widely use here, adding that there are no facilities for
hire purchase for machines and nets. Safety gears, he said, are
nowhere to be bought in Sierra Leone. He said his association has not
yet recieved help from Government or any international NGO.
He pointed out the achievement the association has made by opening
sea link between Sierra Leone and the Republic of Guinea, creating
revenue for the State through customs collection from the goods
brought into country.

Original_Search_Title: African Market Holds House Party
Headline: Ghana: African Market Holds House Party
Body_Text:
The newly-commissioned African Market in Accra last week held a cultural briefing for ambassadors, media practitioners and stakeholders in the tourism industry.
The dÃ©cor of the three-storey market on the Abebresem Street opposite the Kikiriki Spicy Chicken in Osu has an African touch.
The floor is carpeted with traditional mats, the stairs are decorated with bamboo sticks and the ceiling is lined with colourful African batik. It has a bar that serves assorted African drinks including a locally brewed beer, pito and palmwine.
The Minister of Trade and Industry, Dr. Kofi Konadu Apraku was a special guest at the ceremony. Representatives of the traditional rulers of Osu who were at the event gave tourists an insight into the deep rooted customs of the Ga state.
After Dr. Apraku proposed the toast with a calabash of palm wine, it was all hearty for the invited guests who helped themselves with African dishes. The dishes on offer include Kenkey, Aprapransa, boiled plantain with Kontomere stew, Waakye, crab soup and a wide variety of vegetables.
Some tourists obviously delighted after enjoying the dishes, described them as very tasty and highly recommendable.
Also present at the function were the President of the Ghana Journalists Association, Mrs. Gifty Affenyi-Dadzie, the out-going Deputy British High Commissioner, Mr. Craig Murray and Dr. Ibn Chambas, Member of Parliament for Bimbila constituency.

Original_Search_Title: My Ghanaian Experience
Headline: Ghana: My Ghanaian Experience
Body_Text:
Accra — You're going to go where?" an incredulous friend asked me when I
proudly announced that I was planning to spend a month this summer in
Ghana, interning in a local newspaper.
Baffled and upset that my adventurous spirit was treated with
disapproval instead of admiration, I started to answer, but he didn't
give me the chance.
"Stay away from the water, don't drink any," he commanded (and what
am I supposed to do, I wondered, die of thirst?), and again did not
wait for a reaction before firing his next question.
"Are you sure they're not fighting in the streets there?"
Despite several similar reactions, the morning of June 25 found me at
the Accra International Airport, waiting for one of my American
contacts in Ghana to pick me up, surrounded by helpful, chatty
porters. My hunger and exhaustion did not diminish the feeling of
relief that the Ghanaian airport operated in an efficient,
professional way offered. I was finished with the visa-checking
process and collected my luggage in less than twenty minutes.
I was also relieved that I was just moments away from seeing a
familiar face; although for the most part I had been optimistic, the
fear of the unknown, fostered by all the warnings I had received,
sunk in during the last part of my flight.
After less than 24 hours in Ghana, having realised that the showers
worked, there was plenty of mineral water available, and the people
were peaceful and friendly, I was hopeful that my stay here would be
free of a severe cultural shock or negative experiences.
Two weeks and a day later, at the time I'm writing this, my overall
impression of Ghana and Ghanaians has not changed. But I'm no longer
in the newcomer's state of blissful ignorance, either, foolishly
making a judgement on the basis of my own sheltered surroundings.
My first 24 hours were spent in a western-style Accra hotel,
surrounded by well-dressed, well-behaved Ghanaians, eager to help the
newly arrived Americans. Fifteen days later, I can confidently say
that the vast majority of Ghanaians cannot afford to even cross the
door of such hotels.
My introduction to reality took place during the third day of my stay
in Ghana, when my group, Americans, all of us, except for our two
Ghanaian drivers, made an overnight trip up North to Tamale. On our
drive there and back, I had the opportunity to observe the living
conditions of the people along the highway, as well as in the city of
Tamale. Huts made of mud bricks, with doors and windows broken or
completely absent, and half-naked children running around are images
deeply imprinted in my mind.
Images that exist, as I later found out, in the capital city of
Accra, as well, just across the street, sometimes from western-style
luxurious public buildings, hotels or private residences. When I
asked my professor why those people live in those conditions, I was
told that they simply cannot afford anything better. That they can't
afford cement, to improve their houses and therefore their living
conditions. "And doesn't the government do anything?" was my next
question. I was told that the government is trying to the best of
their abilities, but this is a developing country
Little did I know, when I was having that conversation, that I would
get to experience first-hand some of the way those people, most
likely leading a below-poverty-level existence, devise to bring food
to the table.
Little did I know that I would get to realise first-hand that
innocence in children and self-respect in adults have in many cases
