been replaced by a sharp survival instinct. I witnessed that in small
children whose picture I tried to take and they demanded money in
exchange. I'm sure that, had they not grown up in poverty, those
children could find more fun things to do than hound foreigners for
money.
I'm also sure that the woman whom my American co-travellers found in
their room at 6 o'clock in the morning going through their suitcases
would rather be at home sleeping or taking care of her family, if she
wasn't in strong financial need, instead of being humiliated, caught
red-handed trying to steal from tourists.
Of course, after having read the newspapers and worked in one for two
weeks, I know that there is a multitude of development programmes
going on, foreign loans and so forth. I was also told by Ghanaian
friends that Accra looks much better now than, say, ten years ago.
But a question I can't answer, and neither can my Ghanaian friends,
is how those development programs are benefiting the common Ghanaian
citizen, the children that beg, or sell water bags or plantain chips
on the streets of Accra, in the middle of heavy traffic, carrying
their merchandise on their heads early in the morning, when they
should be sleeping, playing or going to school.
I don't want to sound overly critical; please, don't feel that I'm
judging or accusing you. I'm not judging the people who have to
satisfy basic needs and I'm not judging the government who's pressed
to fix the situation. Governments of developing countries have a very
heavy load on their hands and they're certainly not waiting for a
21-year-old American to come and instruct them on how to improve the
living conditions of their fellow citizens.
To cut a long story short, I think Ghana is a beautiful country. The
Ghanaians I have come to know well are proud, intelligent,
well-mannered and friendly people. It's also a country with a lot of
potential, because both the citizens and the new government realise
that there is much effort needed and much work to be done. And I
think that they are doing that work. But it's just that, next time I
come to Ghana, I would like to see more people enjoying the basic
privileges that I, a Westerner, came here taking for granted.

Original_Search_Title: Hounding IBB
Headline: Nigeria: Hounding IBB
Body_Text:
"NO one had so far offered any concrete evidence of corruption
against Ibrahim Babangida, former military ruler. I'm not afraid of
anybody in this world... if you can produce evidence... and I'm not
able to do anything about it within 24 hours, know that I am not the
son of Obasanjo."
The above statement published in Newswatch (On the Marbel page) of
June 12, 2000, was made by President Obasanjo, in reacting to calls
for probe of President Babangida, former head of state. But in spite
of the bill of health if you like, which President Obasanjo gave the
retired General, a section of the Nigerian press and those who do not
want IBB to be, keep harping on "treasury looting,"
"institutionalised corruption" etc. Which are allegations still being
levelled against him.
Is it not sheer shadow-chasing and sensationalism for rabid critics
of the General to keep clamouring for his probe at the Justice
Mustapha Akanbi anti-corruption commission, when there is in deed "no
concrete evidence of corruption" on his part as categorically stated
by President Obasanjo?
The truth is that journalism practice in these parts has been reduced
by a good number of its practitioners to something akin to black-mail
journalism. In deed, investigative journalism to a large extent, has
been thrown out of the window or dethroned to the detriment of a
healthy, responsible and reliable media practice.
A journalism expert, Akin Fatoyinbo, World Bank Regional
Communication Adviser, at a 5-day Workshop on Investigative
Journalism Organised for Senior Journalists in Nigeria. May 15th -
19th, 2000, at the IITA, Ibadan, had this to say about investigative
journalism: "Don't assume, always check everything (information)
including unconfirmed reports." Even the highly respected journalists
and former minister of information, Tony Momoh, also spoke in the
same vein at the workshop. His words: "... under the lilitary, you
(journalists) could write anything without substantiating it. But not
any more... "Felix Adenaike, former managing director of Sketch
Newspapers, still on the same occasion also warned journalists
against fabrication of stories and distortion of facts.
Yet, for a good number of Nigerian journalists in the print and
electronic media, the reverse of what these journalism gurus spoke
about and warned against, have often been the case. One is inclined
to think that IBB's legions of critics and kill joys are simply
scared-stiff of his remarkable wisdom and knack for out-witting,
out-smarting and out-shining his rivals - be it in politics, logic,
street-wisdom or power-play of any sort. The General's detractors are
already catching cold when he is yet to sneeze out what is already
being bandied about as his presidential ambition - come 2003 or 2007.
This group of persons are better advised not to get unnecessarily
hypertensive for there is in deed no cause for alarm.
Millions of Nigerians are living witnesses to the fact that, apart
from the 'June 12' imbroglio, IBB's tenure in the annals of military
rule in Nigeria was creditable in terms of the following:
* Provision of socio-economic infrastructures which were evenly
spread all over the country;
* Better funding of Universities and other federally-controlled
higher institutions of learning'
* Creating and adequate funding of new states;
* Relatively low cost of living;
* Less problematic power supply situation;
* Better treatment of and welfare for all categories of federal
judges and traditional rulers;
* A more liberalised economy which afforded a level-playing field and
more opportunities for more people to go into businesses hitherto
monopolised by a few;
* Sound political re-engineering which produced two ideologically
distinct parties (NRC and SDP) that could have endured - barring any
crisis and long after the military would have left the centre-stage
of political governance etc.
Such food items as garri (the common man's favourite staple),
plantain, egg, rice, beans, meat, fish etc. were easily affordable or
within reach of the teeming masses. Beavarages such as Peak Milk,
Bournvita and so on were also easily affordable in the IBB years.
Both the young and old had less stress trying to cope with the
demands of daily existence. But not any more.
Regrettably, what the teeming masses have to show for their being
Nigerians and residing in Nigeria today are excruciating poverty,
misery, alarming unemployment rate, high-level insecurity of jobs,
lives and property; stunted economic growth engendered by
mind-boggling profligacy in government and a rather selfish and
callous national assembly whose duty it is to ensure that the
executive arm of government implements policies and programmes aimed
at improving the lot of Nigerians.
During IBB's tenure as head of state, ministers, military governors
and other highly placed public officers as well as local government
chairmen and their spouses hardly glob-trotted. But what appears to
be the in-thing of fad today is the reverse of what obtained in IBB
years. Today, every Dick, Tom and Harry wants to be in London,
Sibera, New York, Moscow, Germany, Turkey and where have you: for one
spurrious reason or the other. At the end of the day, such frequent
junketing abroad by elected public officers has no direct bearing or
positive impact on the well-being of the common man on the street.
Millions of US dollars of the tax-payers' money are wasted just like
that and thus, the finances of the country are the worse for it.
Rather than use their pens on these elected public officers
persistently until they are able to curb their outrageous excesses,
IBB's detractors would want the entire world to believe that the
General ran the worst government this country ever witnessed.
Somebody gave evidence at the Oputa panel (sitting in Abuja)
recently, trying to show IBB's hands, Akilu's 'dagger' and Togun's
'bow and arrow' in Dele Giwa's murder.
All kinds of things are being said and done just to put the amiable
general and his aides on the spot. We wait and see how it all plays
out. One last word for IBB-bashers in the media: leave the shadow as
in unwarranted attacks on the general and chase the substance as in
condemning at every opportunity the outrageous excesses of our
elected public officials. In other words, poke your pens where they
are needed most to move the country forward and not backwards.
-Akpan is a member of National Solidarity Association

Original_Search_Title: Traders At War in Ketu
Headline: Nigeria: Traders At War in Ketu
Body_Text:
Lagos — The traders' associations in Ikosi Market, Ketu, Kosofe Local
Government are now at each others throat as the power tussle rocking
the market escalated last week.
About five market leaders were arrested and detained at the Ketu
police station at the weekend following the crisis.
P.M.News' findings revealed that the two groups at the centre of the
crisis are the Ikosi Market Association and Ikosi Market Perishable
Goods Dealers and Sellers Association.
Last week, in the thick of the crisis, the Ikosi Market Association
petitioned the Lagos state Commissioner of Police, alleging that the
Ikosi Market Perishable Goods Dealers and Sellers Association used
their plantain depot to harbour armed robbers as well as dissociating
itself from the entire Ikosi Market Association.
As a result, some leaders of the Ikosi Market Perishable Goods
Dealers and Sellers Association, including Prince A.A. Shonibare,
Chairman, Tunde Esun, Vice Chairman, Mohammed Alausa, Treasurer,
Segun Ifenaike and Tomide Akinsete were arrested and detained.
But in a swift reaction, the Ikosi Market Perishable Goods Dealers
and Sellers Association petitioned the Chairman, Lagos State Task
Force, in a letter dated 13 July, 2001, describing the allegation as
a "big lie." According to the group, "the main cause of the
allegation is the portion allocated to us by the Kosofe Local
Government Council as our depot which they (Ikosi Market Association)
wanted to take from us, but we resisted them." The perishable goods
dealers claimed that they spent over N650,000 to make the place
habitable and have since been paying N10,000 annually to the council.
They maintained that the inability of Ikosi Market Association to
take over the land from them has been responsible for the conflicts.
When P.M.News visited the market at the weekend, tension was noticed
as traders were suspicious of strangers to the market.
The Divisional police officer (DPO) of Ketu police station told
newsmen in his office that he was not aware of any robbery incident
in the market, describing the crisis in the market as a product of
power play.

Original_Search_Title: Kenkey And Plantain in Parliament
Headline: Ghana: Kenkey And Plantain in Parliament
Body_Text:
Accra — Members of Parliament were stunned last Tuesday when a member in his
contribution to the debate on the escalating food crises openly
displayed a ball of kenkey and a finger of plantain in the House.
The member, Hon. I.K.N. Ofori, MP for Sefwi Wiawso on catching the
eye of the speaker brought out the two food items to support the
position by the minority that the food situation is getting worse.
In his contribution he held up the kenkey and said that a ball of
kenkey sold at Â¢200 cedis in January this year but now incredibly
cost Â¢500 On the plantain he said, that size cost Â¢300 at the
beginning of the year but now goes for Â¢1000.
He said members of the minority are talking about practical realities
of the prevailing food situation in the country and that they were
not interested in politicizing it.
The Sefwi Wiawso MP noted that the minority only wants to draw
attention to the food situation so that government would move swiftly
to solve it.
Hon. Akua Dansua, MP for North Dayi, for her part attributed the
prevailing high cost of food to most intra-family quarrels with its
consequent breaking of marriages.
Whilst saying that women are the most affected by the food situation,
she explained that the constraint of having to spend more on food
causes stress on families and eventually lead to quarrels between
wives and husbands.
The MP for Odododiodio, Hon, Reginald Nii Bi Ayibonte noted that the
NPP government had been in power for only five months and wondered if
anybody in that position could perform wonders within such a short
period.
He said the motion was brought to the House out of mischief adding
that, if the nation can move forward it is important that members
adopt a bi-partisan approach to matters on agriculture. Hon. Norbert
Garko Awulley, MP for Builsa South said in his contribution that the
motion is to draw attention to the general food situation and
described the amendment motion as being brought in the spirit of
partisanship.
In his contribution, Hon. K. Sarfo, MP for Offinso South said the
answer to the food crises is the revamping of irrigation saying Ghana
has developed only 4% of its total land area meant for irrigation.
The Afram Plains, he said, abound in agriculture and called on the
government to establish an Afram plains Development Authority to
handle agricultural matters there.
He explained that with cutlasses and hoes there cannot be high food
security and called on the House not to blame the NPP government
since the food crises has been with Ghanaians for so many years. On
his part, Hon. Mohammed Seidu Issah Abah, MP for Chereponi noted that
the government could have sought assistance from friendly nations as
it did for fuel.
The Asikuma-Odoben-Brakwa MP said the movers of the nation failed to
identify the factors that led to the rising cost of goods and
services, blaming the situation on the NDC for a poor foundation.
Hon. S. P. Adamu, MP for Bibiani noted in his contributions that
economic activities have slowed down which have brought down the rate
of productivity adding that workers are being paid for no work done.
The Minister for Energy and MP for Afigya-Sekyere West, Hon. Albert
Kan Dapaah stated that rising cost of food during this time of the
year is not surprising and added that the problem could be solved
either by swallowing the bitter pill or adopting a populist approach.
The Minority Leader and MP for Nadowli North, Hon. Alban Bagbin said
in his contribution that the irresponsible increase in the fuel
prices cannot be stomached by Ghanaians saying, the situation is such
that workers cannot afford one good meal a day.
He said from the arguments of the Majority and the amendment motion
one could deduce that the Majority is insensitive to the cry of
Ghanaians.

Original_Search_Title: COCOBOD Denies Fixing Prices of Cocoa And Coffee
Headline: Ghana: COCOBOD Denies Fixing Prices of Cocoa And Coffee
Body_Text:
The Ghana Cocoa Board (COCOBOD) said on Wednesday that it is no longer in charge of the pricing and purchasing of coffee since the sector was liberalised about 10 years ago to allow private sector participation.
The acting Chief Executive of the Board, Mr S.K. Appah in written answers to a GNA questionnaire said farmers had since that time negotiated their own prices with the Licensed Buying Companies (LBCs) using the prevailing world coffee prices.
"When world market prices are high, farmers are able to negotiate equally high producer prices. However, when world prices fall, farmers are unwilling to accept the lower producer prices," he explained.
Currently, the LBCs offer coffee farmers a producer price of between 60,000 and 65,000 cedis per 64-kilogram bag of unshelled coffee.
Some Coffee farmers recently expressed concern about government's failure to ensure the purchase of coffee produced last year, threatening to cut down their trees to cultivate plantain and cocoyam.
They said government's interest was only in the production of cocoa, and cited the mass spraying of cocoa farms against the black pod and other diseases and increase in the producer price of cocoa as enough demonstration of government bias.Mr Appah, however, maintained that the Board represents the interest of both farmers and buyers at the international forums and continues to carry out research, supply inputs and offer expert advice to coffee farmers.
"Management staff and officers also interact frequently with both coffee buyers and farmers," he stressed.
Mr Apah said the policy of COCOBOD is to continue regulating the sector and supporting farmers and buyers through the provision of guidelines for participation in the coffee sector, provision of planting materials at reasonable prices and supply of inputs.
Other areas include advice to farmers and buyers to improve upon produce quality for export, development of planting materials and alternative cost effective planting methods as well as other agronomic practices such as monitoring of the performance of LBCs and sanctioning those who do not adhere to the regulations.

Original_Search_Title: Reps to Stop Importation of Luxury Items
Headline: Nigeria: Reps to Stop Importation of Luxury Items
Body_Text:
Abuja — Worried by the sinking fortunes of the naira, the House of
Representatives has begun moves to ensure a ban on the importation of
"luxury items" into Nigeria.
Briefing journalists on the country's curious "whole hearted
embracement of the World Trade Organisation (WTO) agreement and the
need to articulate a "better position" for Nigeria in the forthcoming
November WTO Conference in Qatar, the Chairman of the House Committee
on Industries, Alhaji Hamisu Shira said a bill was in the works to
ensure that local industries do not suffer the consequences of a
one-sided trade.
The luxury items slated for ban include electric cables, tissue
paper, bottled water, biscuits, fruit juice, edible oil, textiles,
furniture, paints, matches, tooth picks, ceramics, tyres, batteries
and fresh fruits. Displaying samples of the items to the press, Shira
noted that "we are convinced that the dwindling fortunes of the naira
is clearly traceable to the unchecked appetite for these goods
arising from placing undue demand for scarce foreign exchange by both
the serious investors and importers of these superfluous items that
our people don't really need."
Reminded that a blanket ban seems contrary to the WTO provisions,
Shira noted that "what we are against is the importation of those
items that we can produce here... For God's sake why should we be
importing tissue paper which I can show you (raising up a
locally-produced one) is inferior to the one we produce here, bottled
water and even fresh fruits and tooth pick. This is ridiculous. You
see why Mr. President had cause to publicly denounce the other day
that ordinary fried plantain is now being imported into this
country..."
At a recent Senate public hearing, Industries Minister Chief Kola
Jamodu complained that the Federal Government is helpless with regard
to stemming the menace of dumping, because Nigeria is still a
signatory to the WTO agreement.
But this, according to Shira, is unacceptable as "clauses in the
agreement clearly spell-out what needs to be done, should a member
country want to review its position within its ambit."
Article 2 of the Agreements on Safeguards in the results of the
Uruguay Round of Multilateral Trade Negotiations stated that: "A
member may apply a safeguard measure to a product. If that product is
being imported into its territory in such increased quantities,
absolute or relative to domestic production and under such conditions
as to cause or threaten to cause injury to the domestic industry that
produces like or directly competitive products." Stressing that
efforts being put in place to meet the yearnings of Nigerians by
local industrialists would come to naught so long as basic
infrastructural facilities are lacking, Shira further disclosed
yesterday that moves are now being finalised to send a delegation to
the pre-conference forum in Switzerland.
"What this means is that government should as a matter of urgency now
come up with its agenda for the conference," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Directs CBN to Penalise Erring Banks Over Sliding Naira
Headline: Nigeria: Govt Directs CBN to Penalise Erring Banks Over Sliding Naira
Body_Text:
Owerri — In furtherance of measures to redeem the naira, banks and other
financial institutions that through their activities aid the dip in
the currency will henceforth be liable to severe sanctions.
Already, the Federal Government has directed the Central Bank of
Nigeria to enforce such penalties.
Disclosing the government's fresh move at the weekend in his keynote
address at the 32nd meeting of the National Council on Information
held at the Concorde Hotel, Owerri, Professor Jerry Gana, the
Minister of Information and National Orientation said:
"In extreme cases, the sanction may include the withdrawal of the
license of offending banks.
"However, cognisance should also be taken of other fundamentals which
impinge negatively on the nation's foreign exchange reform, as well
as the entire economic sector. The present low level of our national
productivity which grew by only 3.8 per cent last year needs to
increase substantially to match our high expectations of economic
prosperity and progress"
He reiterated President Olusegun Obasanjo's advise that Nigerians
tame their appetite for frivolous imported goods to reduce the heavy
demand of foreign exchange on the economy and move it to a
sustainable level of growth.
Gana, who arrived Owerri last Thursday and paid a courtesy call on
the governor of Imo State, Chief Achike Udenwa on Friday decried a
situation where tooth picks, vegetables, fried plantain and over 80
brands of toothpaste are imported into Nigeria.
"It is time we all work resolutely for a productive economy, able to
meet the collective aspirations of all Nigerians, now and in the
future," he said.
On combating high food prices, Gana told members of the council that
the government had taken decisive steps towards reversing the
disturbing trend.
He added that in the interim, the government had directed the release
of 30,000 tonnes of assorted grains worth N1 billion from its eight
reserved grains silos across the country to the states and local
governments for sale to the public.
He assured that the government from the next planting season will put
in place policies to encourage farmers by subsidising agricultural
inputs.
He noted: "It is now time to engage in large-scale mechanised farming
to feed our rising population and even have appreciable surplus for
exports towards diversifying our national revenue base.
"The banks in this regard are being encouraged to support the
nation's critical agricultural and industrial sectors through long
term, investable loans."
On the liberalisation of the oil sector, Gana said that a 34-member
committee set up by the president to review petroleum products supply
and distribution which was inaugurated on August 14, 2000 had
submitted its reports.
According to him, the committee recommended the breaking of the
monopoly by the NNPC, and encouraging the participation of other
relevant agencies so as to make petroleum products available at
competitive prices.
He added that this would promote availability of petroleum products
on demand by consumers in line with the government's planned massive
capital investment in the public transportation system.
Some other recommendations include opening up of the industrial
sector to generate massive employment and increase in acquisition of
technological skills in petroleum industries.
It was also recommended that the rural areas should be opened up to
infrastructural development and generates huge revenues for the three
tiers of government through taxes and job creation.

Original_Search_Title: Rainstorm Destroys N500m Property in C'River
Headline: Nigeria: Rainstorm Destroys N500m Property in C'River
Body_Text:
Calabar — PROPERTY estimated at about N500 million, including the presidential
lodge and temporary governor's office, were destroyed by rainstorm
which hit Calabar, capital of Cross River State, at the weekend.
Other property ruined by the storm were the sculpture the Cross River
State map at the Hope Waddel by Club Road roundabout, leading to the
governor's office while the University of Calabar Teaching Hospital
was shattered by the wind.
NEPA cables and poles, several roof tops and trees were pulled down
leaving the entire Calabar and its environs in darkness.
Most streets like Uwanse, Goldie, Nelson Mandela, Mayne Avenue
Atimbo, Hope Waddel Road and others, were strewn with damaged NEPA
low tension cables.
Okon Ibok, a woman whose rooftop at Edim Otop was completely blow
off, told The Guardian that she has lost everything she had to the
rain.
At the presidential lodge, a wing of the recently renovated roof top
was blown off and property inside the rooms soaked by the rain which
started about 12.30 a.m. on Friday.
Similarly, the makeshift Governor's Office was affected as part of
the roof was destroyed by the rain.
Food crops like plantain were not spared by the rainstorm, even as no
life was lost which also destroyed NEPA facilities.
Meanwhile, the state government while sympathising with victims of
the rainstorm, has called for submission of genuine claims for
adequate compensation.
Power supply has however, been restored to some parts of the state
capital. Few other areas like Goldie Anantigh were nonetheless, still
in darkness.

Original_Search_Title: Coffee Going Waste at Juaso
Headline: Ghana: Coffee Going Waste at Juaso
Body_Text:
Coffee farmers at Juaso in the Asante Akyem South District have expressed concern about the government's failure to ensure the
purchase of coffee produced last year.
A spokesman for the farmers, Mr Stephen Kwaku Adu told the Ghana News Agency (GNA) that since what they harvested last year the crop has not been bought, and they find it frustrating to harvest this year's crop.
"In the past, the government encouraged farmers to go into coffee farming by offering us high yielding seedlings. Coffee farmers in the district bought the idea, spent a lot of money to expand their farms to increase the yield."
Mr Adu said the government appears to be only interested in the production of cocoa and not coffee, which incidentally, attracts a higher price on the international market.
"For example in this year's budget statement, the government decided to undertake mass spraying of cocoa farms against the black pod and other diseases and increased the producer price of the crop but nothing was said about coffee," he said.
Mr Adu said in 1992, COCOBOD purchased un-husked coffee at 35,000 cedis per bag of 60 kilos and later raised it to 40,000 cedis.
When the purchasing was privatised, CASHPRO, a private buying company paid 55,000 cedis for the same quantity and later increased it to 70,000 cedis but two years ago the price fell to 60,000 cedis.
"Another private company, which initiated a move to purchase our produce decided to do so without using weighing scales, which many of us disagreed."
He said the company told them the price had fallen two years ago on international market but this time round, nobody has told them anything.
Mr Adu said coffee farmers have entered into another harvesting season and there seemed to be no hope to market their produce.
"Some of us are contemplating whether we should cut down our trees to enable us to produce plantain and cocoyam instead".
Mr Adu, therefore, appealed to the government to institute a mechanism to purchase and offer a producer price for the crop just as it does for cocoa.
The government should also assist them to purchase machines to husk their coffee. Under the present arrangement it is those who buy the coffee that send it to Abuakwa in Ashanti for husking.

Original_Search_Title: Beans From Burkina Faso
Headline: Nigeria: Beans From Burkina Faso
Body_Text:
Lagos — The pursuit of agriculture is first and foremost for the purpose of
food security in any country that wants to hold its own in the comity
of nations.
The spiralling price of foodstuff in the market is indicative of a
food crisis waiting to snowball in the country. Such perennial
agricultural staple items as garri and beans, which are ordinarily
regarded as the last hope of the common man have suddenly been priced
out of the reach of many.
Government's response by way of the proposed importation of beans
from Burkina Faso to force down the price of the commodity is clearly
off the mark and points to the absence of a clear-cut agricultural
policy on the part of the Obasanjo administration.
The decision to import beans which is the fallout of President
Olusegun Obasanjo's visit to Burkina Faso has been variously
justified, surprisingly, by the two ministers in charge of the
Agriculture and Rural Development Ministry. While the Minister,
Alhaji Adamu Bello believes the importation will promote trade and
economic relationship in African nations, the Minister of State,
Chief Chris Agbobu reasoned that since the price of beans in Burkina
Faso is 50 percent cheaper than what obtains in the Nigerian market,
importation will force down the price of beans.
It is unfortunate that both Ministers whose brief is to ensure that
farmers produce bountifully for the benefits of the populace do not
see anything wrong in importing a staple like beans which Nigeria has
the capacity to grow in abundance and export to neighbouring
countries. To import beans from Burkina Faso when there is no drought
or crop failure in the country is disgraceful. It is just as
ridiculous as importing plantain chips or Onions at a time the
Federal Government is blaming the fall in the naira at the foreign
exchange market on indiscriminate importation of goods.
The imminent food crisis is symptomatic of more fundamental problems
in the agricultural sector, which cannot be tackled by mere
importation of food.
Will the Federal Government also import garri to stem the rising
price of the popular staple? A recent World Bank/ International
Finance Corporation (IFC) private sector assessment clearly showed
that Nigeria can out-compete most African countries in the export of
agricultural products. All the government needs to do is to galvanise
our largely agrarian populace with the right policies. Beans is a
perennial crop. Any serious government desirous of boosting beans
production can do so within nine months by assisting the farmers in
the beans-growing belt of the country to increase their output.
It is paradoxical that President Obasanjo who himself is a farmer has
not done much to boost food production after two years in office.
Government must discard its half-hearted approach to subsidy in the
agricultural sector. All countries that are self-sufficient in food
production have subsidised agriculture at one time or the other. The
Obasanjo administration will do well to significantly intervene in
the provision of agricultural inputs to enable our farmers produce
more while at the same time improving the storage facilities
nation-wide to minimise waste during harvest. It is only when there
is bountiful harvest that the strategic grains reserve policy of the
government can be given more verve.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo to Battle Defects in Nigeria's Foreign Exchange
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo to Battle Defects in Nigeria's Foreign Exchange
Body_Text:
Washington, US — President Olusegun Obasanjo has assured
the Nigerian community in Washington of his government's resolve to
deal with criminal activities and frivolities in the import business
that have lately had a run on the local currency, the Naira.
Explaining Thursday to his compatriots why the Naira had declined
in the past few weeks, the President said the recent upsurge in demand
for foreign exchange was fuelled by people engaged in round tripping -
the practice of buying hard currency from the inter-bank foreign
exchange market at a lower rate and selling in the street market at
higher rate.
Obasanjo said those who decide to go to the street market or parallel
market to buy foreign exchange are those who have things to hide, such
as having to import things that they intend to under-declare or
misdeclare.
He cited a recent case where the contents of five imported
containers claimed to be second-hand clothes by the importer were
found to be brand new cars when checked.
With the Customs now working harder upon his prodding, the
president said, those who engage in such practices would be dealt
with.
For those engaged in frivolous importation, the president said,
government would deal with their situation by imposing tariffs on
their imports, since it cannot ban such items outright under the rules
of the World Trade Organisation.
Noting Nigerians were importing even fried plantain, the
president said those engaged in such frivolous importation were not
only draining the country's foreign exchange but also taking its jobs
away by causing local industries to close shop.
The president attributed the recent depreciation of the Naira to
frivolous importation, round tripping in the foreign exchange market
and the depreciation of the Euro and the Japanese Yen against the
dollar.
But he assured that the country, with a foreign reserve of 10.5
billion US dollars, was in a position to satisfy the legitimate demand
for foreign exchange.

Original_Search_Title: Jungle Men in Uniform
Headline: Nigeria: Jungle Men in Uniform
Body_Text:
Lagos — Civil Liberties Organisation releases some details of extra- judicial
killings by Lagos police command. It a controversial issue everyone
talks about but government does nothing about.
Vitus Obi, a 32-year old casual worker at Stu-dio Press Nigeria plc,
woke up from sleep December 4, 2000 a healthy man. He dressed up and
made for his place of work. He was picked up by men of the Special
Anti-Robbery Squad, SARS, of the Lagos State police command from his
place of work. Ten days later, Obi was a dead man. He died as a
result of injuries he sustained following severe torture by his
captors.
Before he left SARS Ikeja, where he was detained, Obi had suffered
paralysis. Obi's sin was that Benjamin, his younger brother had
stolen the sum of N500,000 from Shoetide Nigeria Limited, Apapa. The
boy was nowhere to be found, so Obi was arrested in his place.
Beatrice Illoh, a mother of nine children went to the place where she
sells roast plantain at Alhaji Marsha Road, Surulere, December 23 to
do her normal business. But she never returned. She was hit by a
police bullet in controversial circumstances. While police claims she
was killed in a crossfire between it and armed robbers, eye witnesses
alleged that the police opened fire on a group of youths at a
carnival. A stray bullet hit Illoh.
Peter, the aggrieved widower said his wife was not killed in any
crossfire but by a policeman who jumped out of a moving taxi and shot
a group of youths on carnival.
Such killings have pitched the Civil Liberties Organisation, CLO,
against the police in Lagos State. CLO says the police is carrying
out its duties as if the country is still in a military regime. At
two different press conferences, the human rights group accused the
police of reminding Nigerians of the dark days of Abacha's regime.
"The very reason why we must condemn these acts of extra-judicial
killings is because it reminds us of the Abacha years when men in
uniform killed and tortured innocent citizens with impunity, often
times in connivance with the high and mighty in society," said Abdul
Oroh, executive director, CLO.
The CLO also cited the case of Kalu Samuel Iroh, alias, Kiro, a 35-
year old businessman at Tejuosho Market, Yaba, Lagos, murdered in
cold blood at the Area C command headquarters, Surulere, Lagos, March
18. Kiro was arrested along with four others by six policemen for
alleged impersonation. They were taken to the Area C command
headquarters where he was mercilessly beaten and battered to death.
According to CLO, Kiro and the others arrested with him, were
returning from Adeniran Ogunsanya Street, Surulere in a blue Peugeot
504 Salon car belonging to his friend, Frank Otonko of 20 Mobile
police force, Ikeja, Lagos. They were intercepted around Makoko
police station by six policemen in a police jeep. The policemen
demanded their identities which they supplied with particulars of the
car. The policemen were said to have neglected the mobile policeman's
identity card, handcuffed them and took them back to the barracks
police station.
While at the station, Kiro was said to have demanded to know why he
was arrested and protested the way they were being manhandled by the
police. The leader of the police team, according to CLO, vowed to
deal with Kiro "since he thought he was a strong man."
His widow, Ngozi, was one of those arrested. She said her husband's
head was broken leading to severe loss of blood. "His knee, ankle
bones and ribs were shattered, and the beating continued until he
became unconscious," she said.
She added that Kiro was refused bail for medical treatment by the
police. She said they pleaded with the policemen to either get a
doctor to attend to him in the cell or allow them to get one, but the
investigating police officer, IPO (Andrew) refused and after much
persistent pleadings from the relatives, he demanded a bribe of
N60,000.00 before he could release him on bail. Since they couldn't
raise the money demanded, Ude Samuel, a relative, pleaded with the
IPO to detain him in the cell while Kiro be taken to hospital for
treatment. The IPO refused. Pleas from Kiro's wife to see her husband
were also turned down by the IPO.
According to Ude: "When the police were aware that Kiro had died in
custody, they started playing us around." He said the cell inmates
told him that Kiro had died and was already taken to the mortuary.
Otonko confirmed that he visited the station to ascertain what
happened, but the IPO merely pointed at Kiro's corpse lying in a pool
of his own blood at a corner near the cell. On further prodding, the
IPO told him that "Kalu was an impersonator, he was a stubborn man
who stabbed himself to death with broken louvres at the station."
In the case of Obi, he was arrested with his mother, Justina and
Adindu, his cousin who was released after "N14,000.00 bribe" had been
given. Justina said that while in the cell, she overheard her son
crying in anguish from an inner chamber of the station where he was
being tortured. Vitus was brought out paralysed after the torture and
was placed on his mother's laps. Justina told Newswatch that when she
spoke to her son and there was no response, she decided to inquire
from the IPO what happened to him, but the IPO, she said, claimed
that Vitus had been sick all night and that he had spent his personal
money treating him.
The IPO allegedly asked the mother to bring N7,000 to take him on
bail so that they could take him to a hospital for treatment. When
Happiness, Vitus's sister eventually got the money, she took it to
the IPO who insisted that only a man could sign the bail bond. That
means that since Happiness is a woman, she cannot sign a bail bond.
Happiness said she ran to look for a man to sign the bail bond and in
the course came across one Peter Okpara, (also a policeman at the
station) who gave her a note to the IPO, which he refused to give
attention to.
Vitus was taken to a private hospital, Lifelink Medical Centre at Idi
Mangoro, Agege, Lagos, where he died. The doctor discovered that
Vitus' hip bone and spinal cord were broken while there was also a
deep wound beside his anus, which was decaying internally. His back
was covered with blisters and bruises.
"The doctor also confirmed that he was permanently paralysed and that
his heart was palpitating," CLO said.
The CLO is not only accusing the police of intimidating the family
into running out of their 25, Talabi, Agege residence but also
stalling the post mortem processes. Obi was hurriedly buried after
his body started decaying.
CLO also mentioned the cases of Samuel Izah Oshimili who was shot
dead at Ikeja and Ayuba Hamza Parakoyi, who was shot dead at the
National Stadium, January 13 during a football match between Nigeria
and Zambia during a scramble for Gulder T-shirts.
The human rights group insists that government should set up an
investigation panel into the killings to punish the culprits and to
work towards purging the Nigeria police of paranoid elements who
present the police as enemies of the people rather than what they
claim to be-friends of the people.
Mike Okiro, Lagos State police commissioner told Newswatch that the
police is not engaging in extra-judicial killings. He attributed the
increase in police killings to the frame of mind of certain policemen
whom he described as suffering from the hangover of military regimes.
He said that he has ordered full scale investigation into the case of
Vitus Obi, while Martin Maduagwu, the policeman that killed Illoh has
been charged to Court 6 Ebute-Metta, Lagos. He said that he was still
studying the case of Iroh "whose detail is still sketchy."
Besides, Okiro said he is organising lectures for his men to keep
them in tune with the demands of the democratic dispensation. "Some
of these boys have not witnessed democracy before. They need to be
taught about what is required," he said. He said that the issue of
stray bullet would not be totally out-ruled. "There is nothing we can
do about it especially when they are engaged with robbers. The only
thing people can do is to run away from crime or violent scenes,"
Okiro said.
Additional report by Geoffrey Ekenna.

Original_Search_Title: Resource Control: Court Verdict Not Final - Obasanjo
Headline: Nigeria: Resource Control: Court Verdict Not Final - Obasanjo
Body_Text:
Lagos — A political solution to the ongoing face-off between the Federal
Government and the states over the control of on-shore/offshore
mineral resources currently before the Supreme Court could still be
resolved politically even after the court verdict, President Olusegun
Obasanjo said in Abuja yesterday.
Answering questions from a three-man panel of journalists on 'media
chat', a network service programme of the Nigerian Television
Authority (NTA) last night, Obasanjo said after the apex court must
have made pronouncements on the case, based on the justices
understanding of the relevant constitutional provisions, whatever
political discussions needed to be done thereafter, could still
proceed.
"After the court must have issued its declaration, if you want to
talk politics, politics is something you talk all the time. I am not
averse to talking politics," he explained.
His explanation tallied with that of Vice President Atiku Abubakar
who said recently in Calabar that a political settlement to the
crisis was not ruled out by the Federal Government.
Speaking for the first time on last week's retirement of the three
service chiefs, Obasanjo said it was in line with military tradition.
He explained that any head of service had his specified tenure or
tour of duty at the end of which he leaves, adding that appointments
into such positions were political. He said one of the service chiefs
had been scheduled to proceed on formal retirement by June.
"Normally, there is a term or a tour of service for the head of
service ranging from two to three to four years. Because you do not
want a situation where other members of that service have to be
waiting for dead man's shoe.
That, in itself, is destabilising.
"Again, normally the retirement of officers is based on age and
length of service or whichever comes first. Then, of course, normally
too, appointment of officers beyond certain ranks is regarded as
political. This is because there are a number of parameters that have
to be considered," he said, adding that the retention of officers
followed the pattern.
Obasanjo said in retiring the two others, he looked at what were the
next steps to take in the service.
"As I said to them when I talked to them, they have done a reasonably
good job at this first stage of transition, but the next stage
requires something slightly different from what they have been used
to and it is necessary that they understand it and they understood it
and that is the way it is.
"In this country, we should realise that like the service chiefs
themselves rightly understood, as we move into new democratic or the
entrenchment of democracy, there will be things that we have to do
slightly more differently and we will go on nurturing all the
institutions that will uphold democracy, that will stand to underpin
democracy," Obasanjo said.
Dismissing speculations that the retirements might not be unconnected
with recent pronouncements by the former Chief of Army Staff,
Lt.-General Victor Malu, regarding his opposition to the presence of
foreign military personnel in Nigeria and his defence of his service
under the Abacha regime, Obasanjo said: "Well, I don't know the
recent pronouncement by General Malu. So if I don't know the recent
pronouncement, I won't know what was connected or not connected."
Speaking on the rash of new political associations and calls for
registration of new political parties, Obasanjo said:" we should not
be unduly worried about these associations. He also said the duty of
registering new political parties rested with the Independent
National Electoral Comm-ission (INEC).
On the dwindling value of the naira against the United States'
dollar, Obasanjo gave assurance that government would take steps to
save the national currency and the economy as a whole.
He, however, regretted that banks were putting more pressure on the
foreign exchange market through speculative buying and accused them
of engaging in round tripping. He said instead of loaning money to
manufacturers they are issuing loans for trading.
He also accused Nigerians of buying frivolous things in the name of
importation. He lamented that people even imported fried plantain.
Obasanjo said some of the industries now operated at about 50 per
cent of their installed capacity more than what it was at the
beginning of the life of his administration. He, however, admitted
that this varied from industry to industry as the textile industry
was still operating at a very low ebb.
Obasanjo, though exp-ressed concern at increasing food prices, said
it also meant more money in the pockets of farmers. He gave the
assurance that government would ensure the availability of stable
food.

Original_Search_Title: Farmer To Drak Cedecom To Court . . . Over Destruction of Farm
Headline: Ghana: Farmer To Drak Cedecom To Court . . . Over Destruction of Farm
Body_Text:
Accra — A 43-Year-old farmer of Antwikwaa, resident in Cape Coast, has
threatened to drag the Central Regional Development Commission
(CEDEC0M) and the Ghana Heritage Conservation Trust (GHCT), to court
for destroying her farm.
The farmer, Madam Marie Issah, is claiming a compound interest on a
compensation of Â¢3,244,367.25 promised her since 1992, but which had
since not been paid to her. Narrating her pathetic story to the
Chronicle, Madam Marie Issah, stated that some time in November 1992,
the CEDECOM, which was undertaking an expansion project at the Kakum
National Park, destroyed her 0.61 acres of cocoyam, 450 acres of
plantain, 244 cocoa trees and another 0.61 acres of cassava on her
farm.
According to her, some time ago, her brother came to inform her that
her farm at Antwikwaa had been given to CEDECOM for a certain
project. Based upon that information, she said, she visited the farm
to see things for her-self only to find out that considerable damage
had been caused to her farm and the crops on it.
She further stated that on reaching her farm, she met two officials
of CEDECOM, Messrs K. Abrey and Ntiamoah Mensah, in charge of the
project who assured her of an adequate compensation.
According to her, she was provided with some surveyors from the Lands
Commission, as well as the Forestry Department, to conduct surveys on
the farm and to submit estimates for payment. Madam Marie Issah told
the Chronicle that she later realised that she had no option but to
accept the proposals put up by Mr. Ntiamoah because her farm had
already been destroyed. She intimated that since 1993, she had been
made to shuttle between the two offices for the payment of the
promised compensation without success, until some time in March, this
year when she approached one Mintah of CEDECOM, who referred her to
the offices of the Ghana Heritage Conservation Trust to see one Mr.
Akobah. Madam Marie said when she got the Trust, the head, whose name
she gave as Mr. Tawiah Akya, walked her out of the office saying, "we
have nothing to pay you as compensation," She stated that a part from
wasting money on transport to and from Antwikwaa and Cape Coast over
the past eight years, the face value of the compensation promised her
in 1992 has so eroded that unless she is paid an interest on the
amount, she will resist any attempt to force it on her.
"I have, therefore, decided to drag the two institutions to court to
explain why I should not be paid compound interest on the money they
had withheld for over eight years now," she told the Chronicle.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana Has No Excuse for Not Feeding Herself - Quashigah
Headline: Ghana Has No Excuse for Not Feeding Herself - Quashigah
Body_Text:
Accra — The Minister of Food and Agriculture, Major Courage Quashigah (rtd)
has said Ghana has no excuse for its inability to feed herself and
called for strategies to increase food production.
Major Quashigah, who was opening a three-day strategic planning
workshop at Cape Coast, expressed concern over the high cost of food
imports and charged the participants to come out with meaningful
strategies to "put food on the table of every Ghanaian."
The workshop was meant to evolve plans for the production of adequate
food. It focused on the development of an integrated strategic plan
to achieve of production targets year by year.
Major Quashigah pointed out that any nation endowed with natural and
human resources such as Ghana "offends God for not taking advantage
of the vast arable lands to grow what they can eat and what they can
not eat for export."
The Minister stressed the need for financial interventions in the
agricultural sector by the Agricultural Development Bank (ADB) and
other banks.
Over 40 participants, including the Minister of Fisheries, Mr.
Ishmael Ashittey and top scientists, who head various units at the
ministry, attended the workshop, the first of its kind.
The participants suggested that production of rice, maize, cassava
and plantain, among other staples should be increased by not less
than 30 per cent by the end of the year.

Original_Search_Title: Robbers in Police Uniform
Headline: Nigeria: Robbers in Police Uniform
Body_Text:
Here is how the people's protectors have turned the enemies of the
people. As the three policemen were led into the dock, they looked woebegone
like cows that sighted the butcher's knife. Not even the afternoon heat of
the Lokoja, the Kogi State capital's courtroom prevented passers-by from
flocking in to witness the spectacle of law enforcement agents who were
themselves at the receiving end of justice.
That Monday 2 April, Benjamin Oyakhire, Jimoh Michael and Gerson Saba,
the three police constables who were standing trial, could not face the
spectators straight. Many of them had, in the past, been victims of
extortion, blackmail and brutality meted out by these morose-looking
gumshoes. Nervously, one of the accused persons was busy drawing imaginary
lines on the wooden panel of the dock, another was scratching his head while
the third was picking his nose.  They were absent-minded when the lawyers
trenchantly argued out all the wherefores and hereinunders of the case.
But the policemen were jerked awake into the world of reality when
Justice Umaru Ali Eri, the state Chief Judge, passed his verdict. "I see the
three accused persons clearly as people who have no regard whatsoever for
the sanctity of human life," Justice Eri began. A hush fell on the audience
as the policemen craned their necks to listen to their fate.  The judge
allowed his words to sink in before he cleared his throat to proceed: "From
my careful observations of them throughout the trial, their apparent sign of
remorse is, to me, deceitful.  Undoubtedly, the accused persons are unfit
for decent societies which we must cherish."
Justice Eri is, indeed, an expert at putting criminals on
tenterhooks. After giving the policemen the worst dressing-down of their
lives, he delayed what everyone, including the accused, expected till the
end of his judgement. But when he finally issued the clincher, it hit the
criminals with the devastating effect of a stiletto: "The overall effect of
their sentence," Justice Eri said, "is that all the three accused persons
stand sentenced to death and each shall be hanged by the neck until he
dies." Not a few among the audience held their own necks in vicarious
suffering or mockery or both as the condemned men were led out by their
fellow policemen.  The judge rose languidly , leaving the criminals to face
the hangman's noose.
"Court..!" For the three cops, Monday, 2 April was a day of
reckoning. The sentence passed on them was to be a deterrent to other
policemen who still behave like what a writer describes as Louis Freeh's
Doppelganger, "a would-be scourge of evil, who ended up collaborating with
the demon he was trying to exorcise." In Nigeria though, policemen have been
found to be more than collaborators but actual perpetrators of armed
robbery, extra-judicial killings, brutality and perversion of
justice. Nemesis caught up with Constables Oyakhire (NPF 174214), Michael
(185850) and Saba (201977) on 17 February 2001 when they absconded from
their duty posts in Okene and mounted an illegal roadblock at
Okengwen-Obehira junction, on the highway leading to Akure, the Ondo State
capital. The cops stopped an Osun State Toyota Liteace bus with registration
number XB 104 SGB which carried seven passengers. They were travelling to
Katsina to buy rams for the coming Id-El-Kabir festival.
One of the security agents sighted a bag containing N1.4 million. On
reflex, they snatched the money, diverted the vehicle to a primary school
where they set it (the bus) on fire, including the passengers. Saka Jimoh
and Saleh Badmus, two of the vehicle occupants who escaped, reported the
matter to the police at Okene. Saka's survival had a touch of
drama. Although he is a livestock dealer, his average literacy and love of
movies helped him out.
The captives were being taken to Lokoja by the police to "explain to our
bosses how you came about this money," when they met a team of mobile
policemen in a patrol van. The captors quickly shouted "escort!" so that
their colleagues would not stop them. Saka received knocks on the head when
he dared to question why they were being driven to Lokoja and not
Okene. Eventually at the school where they stopped, the three policemen
divided the seven passengers among themselves for torture.
"It was at this juncture that I realised the policemen had a mission to
accomplish. Instantly, I summoned courage and said to myself, God, Jimoh
Saka you must not die," he narrated his ordeal to TheNEWS. The policeman who
was assigned to torture Saka was drunk and, "could barely muster enough
strength to deal with me." With the agility of a fox, Saka pushed off the
uniformed geek and ran, zig-zag, into the bush with the staccato sound of
gun shots ringing after him. He found his way to the police station. In an
act of self-purification which, to be fair to the police authorities, is now
a priority preoccupation, the criminals were promptly arrested by security
agents who had been alerted by the survivor. TheNEWS gathered that during
interrogations, one of the condemned men told investigators that they
committed the offence because they wanted the huge sum to become
theirs. Oyakhire, a notorious casanova, confessed that he gave his own share
of N230,000 to his girl friend. While Michael buried his own loot of
N209,000 at the mobile police barracks on Lokoja-Abuja highway, Saba kept
his N200,000 with a cop colleague, attached to the Government House,
Lokoja. Mr. Wilfred Ehikamator, the Kogi State Commissioner of Police, said
the condemned cops' acts "are shameful and embarrassing to the police
force." The Kogi judgement was an indirect replay of the epic 1986 verdict
on Assistant Superintendent of Police, George Iyamu, who was sentenced to
death by firing squad. He connived with Lawrence Anini, Monday Osunbor and
their gang in robbing and terrorising residents of Benin City and its
environs. Another case which may be of epic proportion is, according to
documents in possession of TheNEWS, brewing in Akwa Ibom State. It concerns
a Superintendent of Police, Ikpe Johnson Udoaka. In a petition which a
concerned security agent wrote to the Inspector General of Police,
Mr. Musiliu Smith, Udoaka was alleged to belong "to a syndicate that
specialises in stealing people's motor vehicles, defacing them and later
(selling them) to either their accomplices or to unsuspecting members of the
public ." The petitioner revealed to the IG that in August 2000, "through a
concerted intelligence network, the syndicate was smashed but Supol...Udoaka
was quick to play his usual role (of offering) a bribe of N145,000 to the
investigators to kill the case." But the police, bent on redeeming their
image, refused the bait and registered the bribe money as exhibit, details
of which were contained in the Police Investigation Report, reference number
1330/A15/CIB/Vol. 2/37 of 14 August 2000.  The saga started on 7 June last
year when Assistant Commissioner of Police, Egbe M. Preye, led a team of
detectives to Ifiayong Usuk village in Uruan Local Government Area of Akwa
Ibom State where a Nigerian Navy Peugeot 504 Pick-up van was parked. But all
naval inscriptions had been skilfully removed from the vehicle, parked on
the compound of Warrant Officer (W.O) II Okon Ekpeyong. His son, Jude whom
the police picked up, confessed that the vehicle was brought to their
compound by one Jameson Akpan, a Chief Petty Officer with the Nigerian Navy,
Lagos. The sleuths narrowly missed Akpan. Determined to nab the naval
personnel, the police detectives searched the village with a
toothcomb. Based on a tip-off by his mother's friend, Akpan drove off in a
car, but was pursued by those who were after him. Akpan abandoned his
vehicle and took to his heels, but was,on 23 June 2000, arrested. Both Jude
Okon Ekpeyong and Akpan sang to the police. The detectives found out that on
5 June 2000, Chief Petty Officer Akpan unlawfully removed a Navy blue
Peugeot 504 Pick-up van from the premises of the Nigerian Navy, Ojo, Lagos
and brought it to Ifiayong Usuk village. On 6 June 2000, one Mr. Asuquo Ani,
a publisher on 14, NEPA Line, Uyo came to inspect and express interest in
the automobile. One revelation led to another. Akpan allegedly removed
another 30-seater coaster bus from the Navy in 1999. The colour was changed
to milky white from the navy blue and kept on the premises of Rev. Emmanuel
Samuel Nduesa of the Presbyterian Church, Ikot Ekpene. The man of God,
suspicious of all these moves, refused to buy the bus for his church's
use. But the vehicle was removed again by one Mr. Uyoh and two others. The
police, however, arrested the duo in Kaduna from where they released the
vehicle to Anny Asikpo, acting on behalf of Akpan. Asikpo thereafter sold
the bus to one Alhaji for N700,000. As one chain of events led to the other,
DSP Udoaka, the uniformed kingpin of the Uyo syndicate, allegedly offered
money to the investigators who, to the glory of the police, turned it
down. Worse still, Udoaka, "instigated a civil case against the detectives
who smashed the syndicate and it is pending in High Court 9, Uyo
(MU/MISC. 212/2000)." The same Udoaka, according to the petition, dragged
the Akwa Ibom Police Command to the Eket High Court. "One of his
concubines," the petitioner writes, "Miss Mary C.  Etukudo, stole the sum of
N50,000 belonging to Eket Students' Association and it is being investigated
by the Police. But Supol Udoaka quickly manufactured another petition to
take over the case and went as far as keeping the girl in his office. The
next day, he instituted a court action against the CP Akwa Ibom State and
the Investigating Police Officer, Sgt. Imoh David and Christopher Friday
Inyang." Meanwhile, the police in Akwa Ibom released Petty Officer Akpan on
bond to Lt. S.A. Akinwande of the Nigerian Navy for "departmental
proceedings." On Monday, 26 March, some policemen from Alagolo Police Post
in Lagos kidnapped an accident victim from the Emergency Unit of the Ikeja
General Hospital. The patient was taken to a bank in Apapa where he was
forced to cash a cheque of N430,000 which the policemen shared. Two
motorists, according to P.M.News, were engaged in a quarrel over traffic
disagreement at Iyana-Ipaja.  They were knocked down by a commercial
vehicle. The victims were rushed to the hospital by some sympathisers. When
the policemen from Alagolo got wind of the incident, they towed the two
vehicles down to their station. Apart from refusing to release the Nissan
vehicle to the family of the deceased, the policemen converted it to their
personal use. In the process, they discovered a cheque of N430,000 written
in the name of the accident victim, Mr. Ogundele. With the instinct of an
alley cat, the policemen swiftly rushed to the hospital, abducted Ogundele,
dragged him down to the bank and asked him to sign and clear the cheque on
"orders from above." The cops, while not giving Ogundele any chance of
survival, allegedly collected and shared the money. A friend of Ogundele who
called at the hospital was informed of the incident. He dashed to meet the
cops. When he was told at Alagolo Police Station that they knew nothing
about the case, Ogundele's friend petitioned the Lagos State Police Command
which arrested the suspects. They have been singing ever since. On 7 July
1999, Sergeant (Sgt.) Odugbesan Adekunle collected a letter from his duty
post on the Railway Unit of the Nigeria Police Force (NPF), Lagos. The
letter was addressed to Assistant Superintendent of Police, ASP
A.O. Babawale, commander of the Federal Highway Patrol (FHP), Zone 5, Benin
City. The letter stated : "The above named NCO attached to the office was
detailed to escort a Mitsubishi vehicle with Chassis No. 22707940 788 from
Iddo to Onitsha. The vehicle was impounded and parked at Ekhiadolor Police
Station by your team.  Grateful (sir) release the said vehicle to the NCO."
The letter was signed by one D.A.U. Oyebade, for the Railway Police
Commissioner.  This letter served to confirm that Sgt. Adekunle was on
official duty on the 6 July 1999-a duty that required he be physically
present with the driver and vehicle. The Sergeant was not new to this
business, the bosses did not think otherwise either and so, he was allowed
the use of his SMG rifle with 15 rounds of live ammunition for the
assignment. The journey had barely gone three hours on the Lagos-Benin
Highway, when the policeman and the driver ran into a blockage of the
Federal Highway Patrol, FHP near the Benin tollgate.  This was one point
where esprit de corps would have paved a safe passage for any vehicle. But
for Sgt. Adekunle, it did not work. One thing led to another and the
Sergeant was "arrested" along with the driver of the vehicle he was
escorting and taken to Ekhiadolor Police Post on the highway. But TheNEWS
learnt that the driver of the vehicle based on the value of the goods he was
moving to Onitsha was asked to part with N5,000 cash by the FHP man. He
pleaded he had only N500. The men were not interested. The action of the man
did not go down well with ASP Babawale who was infuriated at the audacity of
Sgt. Adekunle .The ASP locked them up at Ekhiadolor. Bewildered Sgt.
Adekunle later rushed to Benin Zonal Headquarters of the NPF Zone 5 in
search of ASP Babawale, but he did not find him. He was perplexed to hear
that the vehicle he was escorting was stolen, according to ASP Babawale. The
owner of the vehicle showed up and allegedly settled the Highway Patrol
team. Adekunle was called all names, branded a fool and threatened with
detention. However, by 12 July 1999, the vehicle landed safely at Onitsha
still with Sgt.. Adekunle as escort. By 13 July 1999 when he returned to his
post in Lagos, a signal had arrived from Benin asking him to show up there
to face some charges. This magazine learnt that in Benin, this policeman was
placed on orderly room trial on two counts of "discreditable and
discreditable conducts." On the first count, he was said to have violated
paragraph E (3) of the 1st schedule of the Police regulation 355 of
1968. His conduct was said to be; "prejudicial to discipline unbecoming of a
member of the force by being out of control," and corking his rifle
threatening to fire a police officer. The policeman denied this charge. On
count two, Sgt. Adekunle allegedly ran foul of paragraph E (3) of the 1st
schedule of the same regulation by forging a letter from his Commissioner of
Police (CP) purportedly to have been written by the CP. Again, he
denied. The trial opened on 12 August 1999 in Benin with DSP Johnson Oni
presiding. The exercise revealed that Mr. A. Ocheke officially applied for a
police escort and got approval from the Railway Police Command on July
1999. The trial found that Sgt. Adekunle was on the escort of the vehicle
unofficially and actually insulted his superiors in the course of the
illegal conduct. Sgt. Adekunle was also discovered by the panel to have
threatened his superiors by corking his rifle. He was therefore, found
guilty of the charges and recommended for demotion to the rank of Corporal
(CPL) from sergeant. He reportedly begged for leniency but he was turned
down. Although he attended the trial and received a fair hearing, the
policeman said that he had appealed against the judgement but was yet to get
any response from the authorities. This appeal, TheNEWS learnt, was sent to
the office of the Inspector General of Police. Provost Marshal J. Olisa
Rhodes in Benin told this magazine that the NPF Zone 5 was not aware of the
appeal. He wondered why the media should be interested in a purely internal
affair of the police. At the Ekhiadolor Police Post, neither ASP Babawale
nor the DPO could be reached for their comments. Adekunle said he would not
rest until justice is done. He said there was a clear case of injustice in
the orderly room trial. The grounds of appeal, TheNEWS learnt, appear to
favour the Sergeant, because the letter from the CP approved his escort of
the vehicle. The CP confirmed same in another letter. Meanwhile, for
shouting out too loud, Adekunle's home was razed by a mysterious fire after
he dropped his appeal for a retrial.  He claimed that goods and effects
worth over N1 million were lost in the inferno. In December, 2000, about 115
policemen were said to have been arrested for alleged extortion from
motorists nationwide at roadblocks. So also is the case of a driver who was
beaten up by a team of policemen at their checkpoint over N20 on Saturday 10
February 2001. The driver incurred the wrath of the team when he refused to
offer them N20 to ease the traffic jam. So also is the case of a police
corporal at Eiyenkorin, a suburb of Ilorin when he killed a commercial
driver, his sergeant colleague and himself over N150 bribe. Eyewitness
account said the corporal engaged in an argument with the driver at the
roadblock over his refusal to increase the N150 bribe he demanded. In the
process, the policeman opened fire on the driver. The cop who was angered by
the driver's refusal also shot to death his sergeant who rebuked him for his
action. The three corpses were later taken to the University of Ilorin
Teaching Hospital. Simon Adewole will never forget his experience with
policemen from the Barracks Police Station, Surulere, Lagos on Sunday, 1
April 2001. Simon, a businessman, had gone to pick up some money from his
business partner. As he mounted an okada to return home, a police van drew
up to his side. Their search yielded nothing incriminating. A sum of N20,000
was, however, found on him.  This was followed by barrage of questions about
what he does for a living, who has the money and where he lives. Not
satisfied, the cops took the man in their vehicle to his house. Simon was
lucky to meet his co-tenants and brothers at home. After turning his room
upside down with "searches," they demanded that Simon follow them to the
police station. He did, but not without a handful of his tenants
following. Many of them, suspicious of the police's moves, had decided to
pursue the matter to a logical conclusion. At the station, the police, still
in custody of Simon's N20,000, advised him to cooperate. They told him he
must part with some of the money or else he would be thrown behind
bars. Simon remembered one of them barking at him: "You better co-operate or
now now, you go get case for neck."  The negotiation began with the
policemen asking for half of the money. Meanwhile, Simon's group of
concerned friends was kept outside the police station from where they
watched the proceedings with fear.  The police, after much begging, took
N4,000 from Simon's money and gave him the remaining N16,000. Fuming, Simon
narrated his ordeal to neighbours, threatening to take up the case. But the
consensus among Simon's acquaintances was that he should not pursue the case
or else he wound be digging his own grave. Yinka Adepegba is a furniture
maker in Sango, Ibadan. He had come to Lagos to pick up some materials for
his job. He faced Mushin, Lagos in the company of a friend for the
materials. But, luck ran against him when his friend's car in which he was
driving was stopped by a group of policemen who discovered N65,000 in the
car boot. Yinka pleaded endlessly with the police that the money was meant
for his business in Ibadan. They insisted they must be given part of the
money. His bold friend proved that the police violated their fundamental
human rights; he proceeded to give them some lecture on what their original
duties ought to be.  Somehow petrified about this bold and grammar man, the
policemen softened and asked for "cola money." Yinka's friend insisted they
were not going to part with anything. After spending about three hours on
the same spot, Yinka himself had to persuade his friend to allow him meet
some of the demands of the policemen. He gave them N500 which they flatly
refused. After much plea, they collected N1,000 and let them off the
hook. There are indications that some policemen in Edo State are also
involved in criminal activities to the detriment of the force's
image. TheNEWS learnt that whereas the arms and ammunition seized from shot
robbers are kept in an armoury where they can be retrieved, more often than
not, the same weapons find their way back into the hands of bandits. We
gathered that the development is the result of a symbiotic relationship
between some policemen and notorious brigands. Attempts to get the stock of
arms and ammunition and the makes from the authorities in Benin were
futile. The Police Public Relations Officer, Mr. Alean Eznia, ASP, said only
his Commissioner of Police could speak on the issue. The CP could not be
reached for his comments either. Sources at the command also stated that the
police had perfected a system of using the same arms and ammunition to
explain the shooting of robbery suspects, whenever there are incidents. A
top official of the force in Benin City, however, denied this. The officer,
pleading anonymity, declared there was "no way he could release the weapon
seized from robbers again to robbers." The police command in Edo "is highly
disciplined and would not compromise its integrity at any cost," the source
further said. The Edo State Police Command said it had not had any case of
its men being involved in helping to resell stolen cars, although it is
working on allegations of some car dealers operating with such
syndicates. The command maintained that unless investigations are completed,
it might not be in a position to present the true picture of things. Other
areas where the police have become the Frankenstein monster of the society
are extra-judicial killings. Last January, Ayuba Amsat Parakoyi was gunned
down by a trigger-happy cop at the National Stadium, Surulere, Lagos. Ayuba,
an indigene of Saki in Oyo State, went to watch the Nations Cup qualifier
between the Super Eagles of Nigeria and Chipolopolo of Zambia. Ayuba, a
soccer freak, made it to the stadium but never saw one minute of the
march. His life was cut short by a mobile policeman over a T-shirt which
both of them had struggled to possess. A policeman shot him. Abayomi Oyebola
was a dock worker and an undergraduate of the Lagos State University,
Ojo. Abayomi was having his bath on the Apapa Ports, Lagos premises when a
stray bullet, allegedly fired by policemen, pierced through his
head. Abayomi fell and died. Abayomi's sad story brings to mind the killing
on Wednesday 17 January 2001 in Anambra of Dr. Ignatius Igwu, a civil
servant in Enugu State. He was allegedly killed in the police custody. But
the State Police Public Relations Officer, Mr. Yemi Ajayi, denied that Igwu
was killed in the police cell. Igwu was, as the PPRO argued, killed while
attempting to escape from the custody of the police.  Almost three weeks
after Igwu's death, Mr. Tajudeen Kazeem Ademola became another victim of
police extra-judicial killings. Ademola, a plumber in Dolphin Estate, Ikoyi,
was on Monday 5 February brutally murdered in police cell at Ikoyi. Reports
had it that he was first beaten comatose by a group of policemen in the
station for allegedly refusing to sign a written document implicating him of
taking part in robbery operations. TheNEWS gathered that his captors tied
him with a rope and began to lash heavily at him with a cudgel. In great
agony, Ademola appealed to his tormentors to please ease the rope on him.
But the policemen refused, leaving Ademola dead. On Friday 9 March 2001, two
students of the University of Lagos were gunned down by gun-totting
policemen at Ilaje, Bariga Station. The latest victim of police killings is
Mr. Kalu Samuel Iroh, a businessman at Tejuosho market, Yaba, Lagos. On
Sunday 18 March, the 35-year-old native of Nkporo, in Ohafia Local
Government Area of Abia State, left his No.  6, Alhaja Abass Street, Ogudu,
Lagos around 3 p.m. He, along with four others, were arrested as they were
returning from Adeniran Ogunsanya Street, Surulere in a blue Peugeot 504
salon car, with Registration No. AA 947 SKY belonging to his friend, Frank
Kerry Otonko of 20 Mobile Police Force, Ikeja, Lagos. They were heading for
Yaba when six policemen in a jeep intercepted Kalu's vehicle. The policemen
demanded their identities and particulars of the car. While Kalu showed the
policemen his identity card, he told them that the car belonged to his
friend, a mobile policeman. Not quite satisfied with his explanation, the
policemen searched the car, discovered the mobile policeman's identity card,
and whisked the group away to Barracks Police Station, Area 'C' Command
Headquarters, Surulere, Lagos in handcuffs. At the station, Kalu asked his
captors the reason for their arrest but, he was, instead, beaten with baton
and metal objects. The head of the captors, Sgt. Andrew, swore that he would
deal with Kalu for having the effrontery to ask a question. As a result of
his beating, Kalu sustained much injuries, severe loss of blood, shattered
knee, ankle and ribs. He became unconscious. Kalu's detained colleagues and
relatives pleaded that the policemen should release him on bail for medical
treatment, but the policemen refused.  The Investigating Police Officer,
Andrew, was alleged to have demanded a bribe of N60,000 before he could
release him on bail.  Before the deal could be fixed, Kalu died. With the
tragedy, the DPO was alleged to have asked Kalu's brother to get a private
doctor to treat his detainee in the cell. Anxious to see that his brother
did not die in police cell, Mr. Ude dashed out to get a doctor for his
brother. When Ude came back, neither the DPO nor his brother could be
found. Kalu's cell mates told Ude that Kalu had died and had been taken to
the mortuary. Shocked, Ude went to see the IPO who quickly directed him to
the DPO who said that Kalu had been taken to Lagos General Hospital . After
intensive search in the hospital, Ude discovered his brother's body in the
morgue. When TheNEWS sought to hear the police side of the story, the
Commander of Area C Command, Assistant Commissioner of Police (ACP),
M.E. Akpoyibo said that he was not around during the period. "I went for
senior officers' course." The police officer who is also a lawyer claimed
Akpoyibo commented further that investigation was on-going on the matter and
that it would be wrong for him to make any statement when the investigation
had not been concluded. According to the senior police officer, when
investigation is concluded, the commissioner would be briefed, and the
people saddled with giving out information about police affairs would tell
the general public the true situation of things. However, the police officer
admonished members of the public to always admit when members of Nigeria's
police do anything good.  "You see, people always see the negative side of
the police, it is unfair, they must also admit when police are doing good
things." The killing of the traders has already led to protests in Yaba
area. On Wednesday, 21 March, the Igbo traders union in conjunction with the
tailors association at Ojuelegba demonstrated at the premises of Nigeria
Television Authority (NTA), Tejuosho. Among the inscriptions on their
placards were "Police Stop Killing us," "Enough is Enough" etc. Recently,
Yaba area of Lagos was literally turned to a battlefield by the students of
University of Lagos (UNILAG) and members of Nigeria Police, when the
students protested the killing of their colleagues. At a press conference
called by Civil Liberties Organisation (CLO) last Tuesday on Kalu's brutal
killing, the human rights body said that despite several complaints by its
organisation and other concerned groups over serious brutal killings of
innocent Nigerians by the police, the culture of police brutality still
persists. "It is a sad reality that the same law enforcement agents
entrusted with the protection and safety of citizens have proved to be the
greatest threats to the lives and security of the citizens," Mr. Okechukwu
Nwanguma, head, Law Enforcement Monitoring Project of the organisation told
reporters at the crowded press conference room of the organisation. Nwanguma
said further that beyond condemning the excesses of law enforcement
agencies, government had done nothing practical to stem it. He argued that
the basic "existence of any government is the welfare and security of the
citizens." Although CLO and the Committee for the Defence of Human Rights
(CDHR) is yet to complete collecting data of victims of police brutality in
Nigeria for last year, CDHR's annual report showed that 55 Nigerians were
mowed down by overzealous policemen in 1999. In its own compilation in the
last two months, the CLO said no fewer than seven innocent persons were
wrongly killed by policemen in Lagos alone. Another unconfirmed report in
the year 2000 showed that Lagos police alone killed 387 citizens who were
dubbed robbery suspects. Not even women are spared in those killings. Many
