of them, the young and promising, were gunned down last year. An annual
report of attacks on women in Nigeria by Project Alert on Violence Against
Women, a non-governmental organisation, spearheading protection of women
rights showed that between December 1999 and November 2000, six innocent
ladies were sent to their untimely graves by policemen. On Saturday, 23
December 2000, Mrs. Margaret Ilo, 41, a mother of nine, was killed when a
policeman's bullet hit her in the head at her stall on Alhaja Masha Road in
Surulere, Lagos where she was selling roasted plantain, groundnuts and
coconuts. In a press briefing after the incident, the police said Ilo's
death was due to a crossfire between a police officer and a gang of
unidentified armed robbers. The husband denied that his wife was shot in a
crossfire between the policemen and robbers. "There was no shoot-out between
robbers and policemen around the area on the said day." Mrs. Ilo's sad end
brings to mind the killing on Friday, 5 May 2000 in Lagos of Miss Odunola
Hannah Ali, a 25-year-old Building Technology graduate of the Yaba College
of Technology. She was allegedly felled by bullet from a security agent in
the convoy of the Deputy Governor of Lagos State, Mrs. Kofoworola
Bucknor-Akerele. Odunola was waiting for a cab at the bus stop to take her
back to her office from an official assignment when her life was snuffed
out. Three weeks after Odunola's death, Mrs. Juliana Obiajulu became another
victim of police reckless killings. She was shot dead in her room by
policemen who were said to be chasing a prostitute in Makoko, Yaba area of
Lagos. According to the report, policemen from Area C invaded Makoko in Yaba
pretending to be raiding the red-light district for prostitutes. In the
process, they allegedly began to shoot indiscriminately particularly as some
of the prostitutes began to flee. Children are not left out either.  Last
June, four primary school children, aged between 11 and 14, were shot dead
at the site of a leaking pipeline in Eleme, Rivers State.  They were killed,
according to Eleme community, by mobile policemen.  After the incident,
police spokesman, Mr. Nnana Ama, said the command was not aware of the
killings. Yet, available information showed that the four children whose
bodies were found in shallow graves 24 hours after the gruesome murder, had
bullet wounds all over their bodies.  On 15 March , two-year-old Mojisola
Adeleye was hit by a police stray bullet which affected her two eyes and the
bullet is still lodged in her skull. It happened at 53, Ayoola Lawal Street,
Ilaje-Bariga, Lagos. Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu has approved the sum of
N250,000 for her treatment.
t the Justice David Ahuora Judicial commission of inquiry into the
mobile police killings in Mbasombo and Mbalim, in Benue State last month,
the confession of mobile police corporal Ogbonaya Okorie, sent many jaws
hanging slack. Ogbonaya, notoriously called "Achiozie" (warrior in Igbo),
described how he killed a pregnant woman on 10 December last year. "I asked
the woman to position her hands in front of her face as if she was making
supplications to God. I shot her and my bullets pierced her hand and brain,
gboaam!". On the third day after the incident, two students of the Benue
State University Ehono Itolo and Robert Aleh were shot dead by the police at
the "B" division police station, Makurdi. The police claimed that the
students got caught up in a cross-fire. February 2001 was a bloody month for
the families of Okenini and Enabulele, both of Jalipa and Oliha communities
in Delta and Edo states. They lost two of their loved ones through police
brutality. The quest for justice forced Osayemwenre Enabulele to petition
the Presidency. Mr. Enabulele said the duo were shot and killed by one
Inspector John Okhue in concert with Mr. Samson Obaseki, Head of the Edo
Vigilante Group. The group is yet to have the legal basis for its operations
. Notwithstanding that, TheNEWS was informed that the death of Nosa and
Osagie was connected with their activities as land speculators. The Edo
State Legislature had passed a motion condemning the antics of these
speculators in recent times. The lawmakers said the speculators only extort
money from well-meaning Nigerians eager to erect buildings on legitimately
acquired plots of land. They urged the police to help rid the City of Benin
of the miscreants. However, Mr. Enabulele said, he heads a group of youngmen
who engage in road and most maintenance and collection of levies (fees) from
site developments on behalf of the Oliha community. At the State Information
Ministry, Benin and the Corporate Affairs Commission (CAC) the group's
identity could not be traced. The youth group makes so much money that they
recently attracted interest from the police, some of whom felt the youths
ought to make returns to them. When a police officer, Inspector John Okhue
allegedly visited the offices of the group asking for his share on 23
February 2001, Mr. Enabulele said, the group called his bluff because his
demands were getting out of control. TheNEWS learnt that the youths had in
the past paid between N5,000 and N10,000 but because he was "broke" on the
fateful day, he offered a mere N2,000 which the Inspector rejected. The lot
fell on Musa who shouted at the Inspector and called him names. The angry
police officer was to later leave the premises, only to return the following
day with Samson Obaseki. According to Enabulele, it was Obaseki who shot
Musa in the neck and killed him. The Nigeria Police Force has, many times,
also allegedly perverted justice. Banjo and Lanre Adekanle are now cooling
off their heads at Ikoyi Prisons, courtesy of the magic hands of the men of
Nigeria Police that thwart cases. Banjo, the older Adekanle, who is an auto
dealer bought a house from one Miss Toyin Bademosi some time last year. As
he was about to renovate the house, he discovered that someone else had
taken it. He reported the matter to the police, which did nothing to stop
the intruder. But the real trouble started on 20 January when Banjo and his
younger brother went to the premises of the disputed building only to see
about eight people working there. An argument ensued and Banjo called the
police with his mobile phone. One of the intruders was apprehended and
detained at Alagbon (Annex). The Adekanle brothers were at the station to
know how far the case had gone, when they were arrested right inside the
station. In a twist of fate, the accuser suddenly became the accused. Today,
a case of murder is hanging on their necks. Yet, the police could not take
them to court for prosecution.  A reliable source told this magazine that a
top female police officer has a personal interest in the matter. Not even
Miss Bademosi, who flew in from London to give testimony that she sold the
house to Banjo could get them freedom. The intruder is now renovating the
house while Banjo is languishing in Ikoyi Prisons. It was gathered that the
spurious murder case was a ploy to scare him from his property. Loremikan,
project co-ordinator of CDHR said investigations by his organisation showed
that they (evil police officers) were either drunk or under the influence of
dangerous drugs like cocaine or Indian hemp. He also attributed it to
over-zealousness and lack of proper training on the part of the
police. "Once these people (police) carry guns, they see themselves as
having the licence to do anything," he said. In his own submission,
Mr. Nwanguma attributed it to long period of military rule which has made
the police to inculcate the military mentality. "They have imbibed this
syndrome and this makes them prone to senseless killings of innocent
Nigerians," he told this magazine. Gani Adams, head of a faction of the
Oodua People's Congress, OPC, in an interview with TheNEWS said that police
are on the pay roll of armed robbers. According to the OPC chief, police
kill innocent people in lieu of suspects. He cited one incident in Gbongan,
Osun State where one Rufus Obagade, who was persuaded by the traditional
ruler of the town to assist as a vigilante, was framed up by the police,
because his activities disturbed them from getting returns from
criminals. Odumegwu Ojukwu, the Eze Igbo Gburugburu, told TheNEWS recently:
"I abhor the rampant situation in Nigeria where, any time you get the police
on duty, they tell you it is 'accidental discharge.' The time has come to
make sure that there are no accidents, because those people killed are
Nigerian citizens who (the police) are supposed to protect." He advised the
Federal Government to rein in its coercive agents and certainly, check the
level of command that can unleash death on the citizenry.  He added: "The
most effective police in the world are (those) that invest themselves in the
community they are defending. But when you send a Sokoto policeman to
maintain order in Nnewi, you are asking for trouble." At the recent
inauguration ceremony of the Nigerian Bar Association and the presentation
of the Human Rights Action Plan for 2001-2005, Chief Bola Ige, the Justice
Minister, was so angry with the corrupt section of the Nigeria Police that
he rained curses on the force: "This illegal and illegitimate act, this N20
or N30 you extort from innocent people, your arbitrary arrest and detention,
will never be useful to you. You will spend the money on hospital bills. You
will use the money to bury your children. God will put holes in your pockets
and drain this illegally acquired money. God will strike them with thunder!"
But Chief Ige has since denied ever pouring vituperations on the Nigerian
cops. All these notwithstanding, the police authorities are not blindly
defending their officers and men. In an interview with the British
Broadcasting Corporation, BBC, the Inspector General of Police, Musiliu
Smith admitted that, they "still have some bad eggs who are not good
ambassadors of the force." He added that any time "we see such elements, we
have been very prompt in taking good care of them."  True, the speed at
which the Kogi police robbers were arrested and tried attests to this
assertion. In the words of Mr. Haz Iwendi, the "Police Public Relations
Officer, PPRO, in Abuja, "Police authorities are very pleased with the
judgement in which three erstwhile police officers were sentenced to death
for the killing of some traders. The facts of the case were too obvious for
them to appeal and the police want to congratulate the judicial arm for
using this case to set a good record in criminal justice system. Justice
delayed is justice denied..."
Additional reports by: Lara Owoeye-Wise, Onyema Omenuwa, Tope Ogunjinmi,
Bamidele Adebayo, Bukky Omotola, Victor Osehobo, Richard Elesho, Abimbola
Ogunnaike, Sunday Orinyan, Gbenga Arifayan and Emma Ojo.

Original_Search_Title: Agriculture Minister Visits Plantain Island
Headline: Sierra Leone: Agriculture Minister Visits Plantain Island
Body_Text:
Freetown — The Minister of Agriculture, Forestry and Marine Resources has told the people of Plantain Island that the Chinese government has provided US$1million worth of fishing equipment for artisanal fishermen.
Mr. Okere Adams made this revelation during the distribution of fishing boats with outboard engines and nets to both Shenge and Plantain Island in the Kagboro chiefdom. The Food and Agricultural Organisation, in collaboration with the Ministry of Agriculture donated the equipment.
Mr. Adams said previously, only people residing in big towns benefited from such assistance, and reaffirmed the government's avowed determination to improve the standard of living of the people.
He told the people that recently, government banned channel fishing, warning that defaulters would be brought to book.
The Coordinator of the FAO's Emergency Assistance Project in Sierra Leone, Zain Muzami, said his organisation appreciated the role played by women in the community, and thanked the Director of Fisheries for successfully implementing the project. He assured the Plantain Island community that he would encourage other non-governmental orgainsations to assist them.
The director of Fisheries, A. B. C. Jones, encouraged the people to report to the Ministry any destruction of fishing gears by fishing trawlers.
The Regent Chief of Kargboro Chiefdom, A. A. Lamin, told the minister that Plantain Island, which has a population of about 4000 people, has no medical personnel, and appealed to the government to act quickly to avert a "humanitarian catastrophe".
Earlier, the Permanent Secretary in the Marine Resources Ministry, Ms. Simi-Lola Wright, informed the women that government had always been thinking of them, adding that her ministry believed in women's participation.

Original_Search_Title: FAO Donates Fishing Gear to Shenge & Plantain Island
Headline: Sierra Leone: FAO Donates Fishing Gear to Shenge & Plantain Island
Body_Text:
Freetown — The Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) in collaboration with the
Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Marine Resources has
distributed fishing boats with outboard engines and nets to both
Shenge and Plantain Island and in the Kargboro chiefdom, Moyamba
district.
Presenting the fishing gears at an impressive ceremony in Plantain
Island, the Minister of Agriculture, Okere Adams said previously only
people residing in big towns benefited from such assistance and
reaffirmed the government's commitment to transform the living
condition of the people in the interior.
He disclosed that the Chinese government has provided one million
dollars worth of fishing equipment for artisanal fishermen which he
said are due soon and advised them to licence their boats, adding
that the provision of smoked house, fishing nets and boats would
improve their living status.
Hon. Okere Adams informed the Fishermen that channel fishing has been
banned, stressing that defaulters would be prosecuted and informed
them about government's intention to extend the micro-credit
facilities to the people of Kargboro chiefdom and promised to inform
the Health Minister about the lack of health official on the Island.
The Coordinator, FAO's Emergency Assistance Project in Sierra Leone,
Zain Muzami thanked the Director of Fisheries, ABC Jones and staff
for successfully implementing the project.
He said FAO appreciates the role women play in the community and
thanked the Minister of Agriculture for ensuring the success of the
project while assuring the Plantain community that he would encourage
other NGOs to visit the Island.
The Director of Fisheries, Mr. A. B. C. Jones encouraged them to
report to the ministry cases of destruction of fishing gears by
fishing trawlers.
Giving an overview of FAO's Emergency Assistance Project, the
Coordinator, Alpha Bangura said the second phase of the programme
would involve similar presentation to the northern province.
He said since the inception of the project ten permanent structures,
house for trying fish have been built countrywide, and thanked FAO's
zain Muzami for soliciting assistance for the poor.
The Regent Chief of Kargboro chiefdom, A.A. Lamin, informed the
minister that Plantain Island with the population of 4000 inhabitants
has no dispenser or nurse and added that most to the fishermen's
fishing gears have been destroyed by trawlers leaving them in abject
poverty.
Earlier, the Permanent Secretary in the Marine Resources Ministry,
Miss Simi- Lola Wright who chaired the ceremony informed the women
that government has always been thinking of them, adding that her
ministry believes in women participation and advised them to send
their girl child to school.
Similar donations and statement were made at Shenge Community hall at
the end of the ceremony.
The smoke house and fishing gears were officially handed over to the
chairlady of the Plantain Fish Women's Association, madam Mamailia
Bangura who gave the vote of thanks.

Original_Search_Title: 'Prostitution War Should Now Focus On Men'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Prostitution War Should Now Focus On Men'
Body_Text:
Lagos — Rather than continuous emphasis on the role of women in the prevalent
societal ill known as prostitution, the focus must shift from the
girls or ladies to their male counterparts if the war against the
vice would ever be won in the country.
A philanthropist and evangelist, Rev. (Dr) Mrs. Rose K. Ogbe, made
this assertion while exchanging views with the press on the
programmes and activities of "The Help Ministries International"
(T.H.M.), which she runs in Benin City, the Edo State Capital.
Founded 10 years ago, the organisation was the first from Nigeria to
establish a special centre in Italy in 1998 to rehabilitate ladies
who had travelled to Europe to engage in sex trade. Mrs Ogbe the
founder of the organisation stated that the battle to "rescue these
girls and women from selling their bodies for money" would remain a
futile until serious attention shifted to "the men who actually keep
these unfortunate - I think we must regard them with love - girls in
business."
She argued: "We all know that supply would be nil if there is no
demand. So, the next area God has specifically directed me in T.H.M.
is to embark on anti- prostitution campaign that would focus squarely
on the men."
Condemning such subtle encouragement of prostitution by men in
positions of authority in the country as witnessed in the so-called
"Protocol girls," which Ogbe termed "glorified prostitution involving
even some married women, she argued that prostitution must be
redefined to include "engaging in sex for money or status."
Challenging men in government and other influential positions to
undertake self-examination, Ogbe said: "Let it start from 'Mr. A',
yes, myself! kick out all the girl friends and then we'll laugh about
that. The men are talking and complaining about prostitutes, yet they
have women housed and located here and there on their payrolls, they
are housing, accommodating, feeding, clothing, sponsoring and giving
all sorts of status to their concubines... that is prostitution and
its all sex for money or status."
She said she has withdrawn from the Church of God Mission after she
graduated from its Bible Institute in 1989, stating that "God has
told me directly to establish a special ministry designed entirely
towards helping the poor and needy.
"At the Faith Arena in Benin-City, God told me that the gap between
the rich and the poor in the church was getting wider, and wider,
that I should help to bridge that gap through The Help Ministry."
Through personal sacrifice, soliciting for items of clothing
materials, the establishment of large scale farming in Cassava,
Plantain and other crops, Mrs. Ogbe has been able to touch the lives
of poor folks in villages in Edo and other states in Nigeria, Togo,
Ghana, Cote d' Ivoire, Zaire, South Africa, Italy, United Kingdom and
the United States of America. Looking back and feeling somewhat
fulfilled today, she said T.H.M. has decided to hold a thanksgiving.

Original_Search_Title: Murder Over Egunje
Headline: Nigeria: Murder Over Egunje
Body_Text:
Lagos — Sunday, 18 March 2001 was particularly an uneventful day for the
family of Kalu Samuel Iroh, who reside at 6 Alhaja Abass Street,
Ogudu, Ojota, Lagos.
On that fateful day, Kalu, his wife, Gloria and their two kids,
Samuel (three years) and Michael (9-months) had returned from the
church, feeling fulfilled. In order to enliven the atmosphere in the
home, Kalu asked his wife to proceed to the kitchen and prepare a
meal for the family. He reminded her not to forget to put vegetable
in the stew she was to cook.
But alas, that was his last instruction to his wife. No sooner had he
given this instruction than he decided to visit a friend in Adeniran
Ogunsanya Street, Surulere. He decided to use the car of his friend
(a mobile policeman) parked at his residence. But this decision
proved to be his major undoing as he was arrested along with four
others by policemen along Makoko road, Yaba after a hot chase.
They were accused of unauthorised use of the siren and detained at
the Makoko Police Station. The five were subsequently transferred to
the Barracks Police Station, Surulere where Kalu was allegedly beaten
and battered to death by policemen attached to the station.
Kalu's death marked another instance of police brutality and
extra-judicial killing of innocent Nigerians. Last January, some
traders from Akure, Ondo State were going to Katsina to buy rams for
the sallah celebration. They were accosted by three mobile policemen
at Okene road, Kogi State, who upon discovering the huge amount of
money on them, (reports claimed the traders were carrying N1.5
million), killed them. They did not stop at that. They set the
vehicle the traders were travelling in on fire to cover up their
dastardly deed. However, luck ran against them as one of the
occupants of the bus managed to escape. It was the escapee who
alerted police authorities of the evil deed of these satanic agents
who were promptly arrested and charged to court. On Monday this week,
a court in Kogi found the policemen guilty and ordered their
execution by hanging.
Recently, at Surulere, a woman, Mrs. Iroh, roasting plantain by the
roadside was killed by the bullets from the gun of a policeman. When
asked why the offending policeman had not been brought to book, the
Lagos Police Commissioner, Mr. Mike Okiro said the police have
discovered that the woman was a victim of police crossfire, that the
policeman who fired the shot did not intend to fire her, but at a
crowd that was threatening the peace of the area.
In February, leader and founder of the Oodua Peoples Congress (OPC),
Dr. Fredrick Fasehun had cause to bring the corpse of one of his
members killed by the police for no reason to the offices of P.M.News
on Acme Road.
These and many others are instances of police brutality against
innocent Nigerians which has made non-governmental organisations like
the Civil Liberties Organisation (CLO) to ask whether police are paid
to kill and maim Nigerians at the slightest opportunity. Apart from
drawing public attention to excesses of the law enforcement agents,
some of these NGOs have taken it upon themselves to be at the
forefront of seeking redress for victims of human rights violations.
It is in accordance with this mandate that CLO decided to bring to
the fore once again the brutal killing of Mr. Kalu Samuel Iroh,
popularly referred to as Kiro by his friends and business associates.
Until his untimely, painful and tragic death in the hands of brutal
policemen, Kalu was a businessman at Tejuosho Market, Yaba, where he
made and sold household accessories such as bedsheets, window blinds
and curtains.
Narrating the incident that led to Kalu's death, CLO's Head of Law
Enforcement Monitoring Project, Barrister Okechukwu Nwanguma, said
the victim was arrested by six policemen from Barracks Police Station
Area 'C' Command headquarters, Lagos on Sunday 18 March, 2001 at
about 8 p.m. According to Mr. Nwanguma, Kalu and four others were
arrested at Makoko Police Station, Yaba. Those arrested with Kalu
were Mr. Azuma Kalu Ibeh, his wife, Mrs. Ngozi Kalu, Mr. Kanu Akpanya
Imo and Miss Nnenna Mang.
They were all handcuffed and without being told their offence or
reasons for their arrest, were taken to the Area C Command
Headquarters where Kiro was mercilessly beaten and battered to death.
Nwanguma said one of the policemen who arrested the suspects claimed
they were arrested for impersonation.
Metrolink investigations revealed that Kalu and the others arrested
with him were returning from Adeniran Ogunsanya Street, Surulere in a
blue Peugeot 504 saloon car with registration number AA 947 SKY
belonging to his friend, Frank Kerry Otonko of 20 Mobile Police
Force, Ikeja. They were heading towards Yaba when they were
intercepted around Makoko Police Station by the six policemen in a
police jeep. The policemen demanded their identities which they
supplied. Further, the policemen demanded for the particulars of the
car and Kalu explained to them that the car belonged to his friend, a
mobile policeman and upon searching the car, the policemen discovered
the mobile policeman's identity card. They handcuffed Kalu and others
and took them to Barracks Police Station.
When they got to the station, Nwanguma told Metrolink, Kalu demanded
the reason for their arrest and protested the way they were being
manhandled by the policemen.
"He was pummelled, kicked and beaten with baton and metal objects," .
The leader of the police team (name withheld) was reported as saying
that he would deal with Kiro (Kalu) since he thought he was a strong
man.
"The six policemen were at once battering Kalu, ignoring pleas to
stop by those around. One of the policemen pulled his pistol and
threatened to shoot Mr. Azunna (one of those arrested) for protesting
the indecent manner the policemen were treating his wife, Ngozi,
Kiro's (Kalu) head was broken, leading to severe loss of blood, his
knee and ankle bones and ribs were shattered and the beating
continued until he became unconscious."
"In that unconscious state, Kalu was carried into the cell," the
lawyer activist stated.
It was gathered that Kalu's colleagues pleaded with the police to
release him on bail for medical treatment, but the policemen refused.
The investigating police officer was said to have demanded for the
sum of N60,000 before Kalu could be released.
Unable to raise the money, Kalu's elder brother, Mr. Ude I. Samuel
and another relative pleaded with the IPO to detain them in the cell
while Kalu be taken to the hospital for treatment. Yet, the IPO
refused until the man died.
Taking the story from here, Mr. Samuel said the DPO, aware that Kalu
was dead, refused to let him see his brother until some of the cell
mates of the deceased later confirmed to Samuel that Kalu had died
and deposited at the Ikeja mortuary. Kalu died Monday, a day after he
was arrested.
"The last time I saw him was on Monday, I saw him breathing heavily.
He was gasping for breath. I told them to let me take him to the
hospital but they refused. On Tuesday, I went to the station with a
lawyer, but the DPO was dribbling us. Rather than tell us that Kalu
was dead, he was deceiving us. It was Kalu's cell mates who told us
that Kalu had died and taken to Ikeja mortuary. On getting to Ikeja
mortuary, we were not allowed to see the body," the deceased's elder
brother stated.
It was further gathered that the others arrested persons were
released on the second day after paying various sums of money to the
police.
Late Kalu's friend, Mr. Otonko in whose car the deceased was
arrested, reportedly visited the station to inform the police that
the deceased was actually his friend and that he (Otonko) gave him
the car for use. But the policemen at the Barracks Station refused to
release the deceased.
If indeed Kalu impersonated, did that justify his being killed by the
police? Is impersonation a capital offence, warranting death? And is
it the duty of the police to determine the guilt of a suspect, impose
punishment and execute same?
The law of the land is very clear on the processes to follow in
meting out punishment to offenders. The police is certainly not
charged under the constitution of the land to impose punishment on
culprits. It is the duty of the courts to do this.
Now that Kalu is dead, who takes care of his wife, Gloria and
children? An encounter with her revealed a completely perplexed woman
shattered by the death of her husband. She said since the death of
her man, she has found it difficult to focus on anything. She said
she has been wondering how she will take care of the children and the
aged parents of her husband.
"I am not doing anything. I am a housewife. I am completely dependent
on my husband. Now that my husband is dead, I am going to take care
of the children. Kalu's parents are still alive. Who will take care
of them now?" the bereaved widow asked .
Even though Kalu is dead, his colleagues at Tejuosho market have
vowed to do everything within their powers to bring his killers to
book. Said Prince Christopher Ohaburuiko, President, Tejuosho Market
Union: " We are calling on government to bring the perpetrators of
this killing to book. They must be punished, we are also seeking
compensation for the family of the bereaved."
In his own comment on the death of Kalu, Chairman, Igbo Community,
Ogudu, Theophilus Ezeanya, said incessant harassment of Igbo at Ogudu
is becoming unbearable. He condemned the killing of Kalu, describing
it as wasteful. He called on the government to act swiftly to bring
those who killed Kalu to book. People's anger against the police is
rising and unless something urgent is done to stem the ugly trend,
the people might be forced to take law into their hands.
The committee of friends of late Kalu also called on the
Inspector-General of Police to bring his killers to book. "We
strongly suspect that Kalu was murdered by the police. Those
responsible for the murder should be punished," spokesman of the
group, Mr. Onyembi Eugene Uzochukwu, demanded.
National coordinator, Eastern People Solidarity Alliance, Eze Onwuma,
also called on the Inspector-General of Police to set up a
high-powered inquiry into the circumstances leading to the death of
Abia born Kalu and bring the perpetrators to book. "We want this
death investigated because this is not the first time the people
would be killing innocent members of the public under the guise of
maintaining law and order. We want compensation for the family of the
deceased and a halt to the incessant killings," Onwuma stated.
Meanwhile, Metrolink gathered that police authorities have commenced
investigation into the matter. Lagos Police Command spokesman, Victor
Chilaka was said to have told officials of CLO that the state command
will soon make its findings on the matter public.

Original_Search_Title: Cocoa Production for 2000/2001 Estimated At 90,000 Tons
Headline: Cameroon: Cocoa Production for 2000/2001 Estimated At 90,000 Tons
Body_Text:
Douala, Cameroon — Cameroon's Cocoa production
for the 2000/2001 season could be around 90,000 tons,
according to industry sources.
Sources at the Union of Cocoa and Coffee Processors
and Buyers (SUACC) of Mungo and the Cocoa and Coffee Inter-
professional Council (CICC), told the press that the figure
represents a shortfall of some 10,000 tons compared to the
previous season.
Exporters in the Division of Mungo, one of the major
cocoa producing regions in Cameroon, blamed the situation on
the collapse of the cocoa prices in the world market.
They complained that the development has forced many
cocoa farmers in the Mungo Division to destroy several
hectares of cocoa plantations to pave way for food crops such
as tomato, vegetables and plantain.
The situation has been further compounded by the high
costs of farming inputs.

Original_Search_Title: Land Of Our Death: What Is The Rate Of Insha Allah To The Cedi?
Headline: Ghana: Land Of Our Death: What Is The Rate Of Insha Allah To The Cedi?
Body_Text:
Accra — It is the good book that talks about duplication of activities in
heaven and on earth, and that whatever is bound in heaven is also
bound on earth. And vice versa. I have in recent times noticed a
similar thing happening in our dear country.
You know what, ever since it was announced that Ghana, our beloved
country, had joined the HIPC group, some individuals have also
announced that they had joined some local HIPC clubs, so that their
former debts shall be written off. After all if Britain and Canada
and the other G-7 countries have started announcing debt cancellation
packages for Ghana ever since she joined the august HIPC club, why
can't same be done at the micro level?
And that is why this five-star brokeman became righteously angry when
one of his regular 'donors' sent her daughter to collect money from
him one afternoon. 'Who is your mother?' 'Auntie Amina' 'And she
says...?' 'I should collect the pito money from you,' 'Does she
listen to news?' 'News? 'Go and tell her I've joined HIPC, all my
former debts should be written off.'
When a nation goes HIPC money and money-related issues occupy the
thoughts of her citizens and feature prominently in their
conversations. Though I had gone to the comm-centre to make
photocopies it was the telephone conversation going on behind me that
caught my attention. 'They're on it - the foreman is strict; they
will finish soon.'
'In two months?' 'Less than that - but we'll need more cash.' 'What
is the rate now, one Isha Allah to the cedi?' 'I'll have to find
out.' I thought of Malam: Ghanaians are going to perpetuate the
memory of his misfortune by creating a new expression out of that
unfortunate episode. The dollar is now called Insha Allah among a
section of Ghanaians now. Studies in Linguistics reveal a variety of
ways in which language communities increase their repertoire of
lexical items.
One of these is the assignment of new meanings to existing words or
expressions as a result of a particular event. Malam Isa, whose
tenure at the Ministry of Youth and Sports passed away like a walking
shadow, was fond of the Arabic expression Insha Allah, if Allah
permits. His problem with the $46,000 has made Ghanaians now refer to
the dollar as Insha Allah. Sometimes it is the names of the
characters involved in certain significant events that are turned
into expressions to describe such happenings or others like them.
When Captain Boycott's troops abandoned him in battle, little did
anyone suspect that his name would one day become an English word,
boycott. During my student days at the then Advanced Teacher Training
College, Winneba, I did my teaching practice at Asamankese Secondary
School (ASASCO). One day I fixed an extra class after lunch, I waited
long after the lunch period, but the students were not showing up.
When the first batch started trickling into the class I asked why
they had kept so long in coming. "Sir, today they served Owusu
Fordwuor in the dining hall, so we attempted all the questions."
My enquiries revealed that Owusu Fordwuor was the students' jargon
for fried plantain and beans, a.k.a 'red red'. And its etimology?
When the then Commissioner for Education, Mr. Owusu Fordwuor, first
visited the school and decided to pay a courtesy call on the students
in their dining hall, they were eating red red. From that day, red
red became Owusu Fordwuor at ASASCO. The University of Ghana is only
language community which exhibits this phenomenon of creating words
from events and even proper names. Legonites refer to semester and
other examinations as mfodwo.
My enquiries reveal that Mr. Mfodwo used to be a Registrar in Legon
and he often signed releases on examinations usually displayed on
notice boards in the various halls and departments. The frequent
appearance of his name on examination releases led to the
substitution that has persisted till today. Therefore, when the
drummers from Vandal Lily pour down the hill singing Mfodwo ee,
yensuro Mfodwo ee, yensuro Let all know that they are referring to
their semester examinations.
Another Legon expression that best illustrates this mode of adding to
the lexicon of a language as well as other characteristics of
language is narrowing. When a male student receives a female visitor
and he asks his room mate to go and spend time out so that he can
give to the lady whatever it is that she has come to him for, it is
said that such a fortunate (?) student has narrowed his room mate.
During our time, a percher narrowed his host for several hours in
Commonwealth Hall. He was ponded severely for abusing the hospitality
of his host.
But other students argued that if the host wants to remain a five-
star chewer does that prevent his guest from saying hello to the
Daughters of Eve? Now, from where came the expression 'to narrow'? As
I have stated earlier, this expression illustrates some aspects of
the nature of language, especially its inclination to change with
time. The word was coined out of the name of Chief Anthony Enahoro,
one of the Nigerian politicians who were struggling for political
power in the days leading to their independence.
The struggle was between Enahoro, Balewa, and Awolowo. When
eventually Sir Abubakar Tafawa Balewa won politcal power and became
Prime Minister of Nigeria, Enahoro ran away to Ghana, leaving Balewa
to enjoy his 'bride', political power. It thus happened that on Legon
campus in later years whenever a male student had a female visitor
and kicked his room mate out, it was said that such a guy had been
enahoro- ed. Language changes with time. That is why today enahoro-ed
has become narrowed.

Original_Search_Title: I Wonder As I Wander
Headline: Ghana: I Wonder As I Wander
Body_Text:
Acrra — Sing a Song You Can Hum
For about a decade, roughly between 1987 and 1997, no diplomat left
the Castle after a visit with Chairman/President Rawlings without
praising the economic success and wisdom of the P/NDC. It was the
routine recitation after every ambassador or high commissioner
presented his or her credentials or bade farewell to the
Chairman/President after a mission.
Delegations of all types and "investors" of all shades seemed to have
rehearsed the lines to sing the praises of the "economic wizardry" of
Rawlings' men and women. Why not?
The Golden Tulips and others had sprang up where the Continentals had
slumped to the ground. There were corn flakes and every imaginable
product dumped on the streets that now swarmed with grocery shops
misnomered supermarkets and arcades. Where local banks withered there
grew forex bureaux.
The foreign banks expanded, got refurbished, improved their services,
and published hefty profits. Streets got wider in Accra, Kumasi,
Takoradi, Tamale and elsewhere. Trunk roads got dressed with asphalt.
Some, of course, received bitumen today and returned to potholes
tomorrow. And there were many, many more signs of progress.
It was all there for the eyes to see. Accra got larger. New houses
sprang up: every "burgher" bought a plot to build - if he wasn't
swindled. Cars abounded - "home second-hand" for all and custom-made
Jaguars for "men of integrity".
Gaunt actors sold Uncle Ben's perfumed rice on TV. The glare,
however, did not include the fact that soon we were importing
plantains from Cote d'Ivoire. We were importing toothpicks.
So, where did all the progress go? How come it all evaporated so
quickly? How come now it is the diplomats and high commissioners who
lead the refrain of the dirges over the collapse of the Ghana
"success story"? Why are yesterday's advisers clambering over each
other to come to "rescue" the result of their advice?
It makes you dizzy, doesn't it?
The praise singers are now the judges? That is not fair to Rawlings.
Except that he and Kwesi Botchwey and P.V. Obeng and so on all
believed the hyperbole. But now they themselves will not want to hear
a replay of their own music. It disturbs their sleep. That's what
hurts: not to be able to sleep so many years after the act.
I was therefore a bit uncomfortable with the tone, sense of
achievement and excessive confidence by which Dr. Apraku, Dr. Nduom,
and (slightly less so) Mr. Baah Wiredu defended their budget and the
HIPC on a TV programme the evening after the budget reading.
Defend they must defend their own government's programme. Nor must
they paint such a pessimistic picture as to make us lose hope and
faith. But they must also tell us the costs of the choices they have
made. They must tell us what we lose by the choices they have made.
We must be told the difficulties that will certainly result from the
decisions they have made to pick the HIPC. We all know they had a lot
of misgivings; they said so.
Right now, the general public sentiment seems to appreciate the
difficulties of the economic choices the NPP has to make. They must
seize the opportunity to be bold and open about the real difficulties
and losses to be expected.
To sound all too flowery so soon will not help us, and it will not
help you. Sing a song you can hum with nostalgia when alone tomorrow.

Original_Search_Title: NBA, NLC And Wage Increase
Headline: Nigeria: NBA, NLC And Wage Increase
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Nigeria Bar Association in its recent communique, advised
Nigerian workers to refrain from further agitation for wage increase.
This position of the NBA places her in predictable opposition or,
more explicitly put, confrontation with the Nigeria Labour Congress.
Further to the public ire that will inevitably attend upon the
perspective of the NBA, it is most expedient to do a dialectical
appraisal of the political economy of wage increase beyond the extant
benefits of populist agitation.
The NBA need to know that the Nigerian worker on a monthly wage of
seven thousand five hundred naira, works for a wage rate of sixty-two
dollars per month at the present Naira/Dollar exchange rate. Freed of
the handicaps replete with loss of memory, we are able to appreciate
that just in 1985 the dollar exchanged to the Naira at sixty-six kobo
for one dollar. At that rate of Naira/Dollar exchange, the Nigerian
worker's earning was less than fifty-five Naira per month.
The NBA leadership would do better with the consciousness that the
Nigerian worker, with the present wage earning, is
economically-deprived, dispossessed and dehumanized. He does not
compare with what his counterparts in other parts of the world earn.
The Nigerian worker on his present wage of seven thousand five
hundred Naira per month, earns about two dollars per day. With the
official eight working hours per day, he earns 0.25 dollars per hour.
For a better appraisal of the phenomenon, let the NBA compare this
with a salary range of 13.46 dollars per hour for the American
worker. At the present exchange rate of one hundred and twenty-five
Naira to a Dollar, this translate to one thousand six hundred and
eighty-two Naira, fifty Kobo and therefore thirteen thousand four
hundred and sixty Naira for eight working hours per day. In Britain,
the worker earns 13.77 dollars per hour. This translates to one
thousand seven hundred and twenty one Naira twenty-five kobo and
therefore, thirteen thousand seven hundred and seventy Naira, for
eight working hours per day. The Canadian worker earns 16.03 Dollars
perhour. This translates to two thousand and three Naira seventy-five
Kobo and consequently, sixteen thousand and thirty Naira for eight
working hours per day. For the Japanese Worker it is 23.66 Dollars
per hour. This amounts to two thousand nine hundred and fifty seven
Naira fifty kobo and therefore, twenty three thousand six hundred and
sixty Naira, for eight working hours per day. The Hungarian worker
earns 1.70 Dollars per hour. This translates to two hundred and
twelve Naira, fifty kobo and therefore one thousand seven hundred
Naira for eight working hours per day. It is 2.90 Dollars per hour
for the polish worker. This amounts to three hundred and sixty-two
Naira, fifty kobo and therefore, two thousand nine hundred Naira for
eight working hours per day.
Thus, in Naira equivalent, it is a monthly minimum wage of two
hundred and sixty-nine thousand, two hundred Naira for the American
worker, two hundred and seventy-five thousand four hundred Naira for
the worker in the United Kingdom. It is three hundred and twenty
thousand six hundred Naira for the Canadian worker and four hundred
and seventy three thousand two hundred Naira for the Japanese worker.
Yet, workers in these countries still agitate for wage increase
without bad blood from the government and civil society.
This difference in wage rates is accountable for the massive brain
drain syndrome that has placed our best materials in the hands of the
advanced capitalist world. On the other hand, the high level of
unemployment and the slave wage system frustrate our youths in
particular and the citizenry in general and facilitate their
indulgence in advance fees fraud, drug trafficking, prostitution,
robbery, bribery and corruption, etc.
Taken from another perspective, our leaders must appreciate the
interrelationship between poverty alleviation, industrialization and
wages. Investors are better attracted to invest in an economy with a
high propensity to consume. No investor would plough his resources
into an economic environment where people cannot buy what he
produces. When we enhance the real income of the worker through
increase in wages and anti-inflation policies, we increase his
propensity to consume and by that initiative, enhance increased
production and consequently large-scale production and further
investment.
With an enhanced environment for further investment and large-scale
production, the economy facilitates employment of more workers in the
formal sector and the informal sector such as distribution and ware
housing. This is the primary functional scope for increased wealth
creation, redistribution of wealth and expansion of the scope of
economic enhancement. The present wage system confines the Nigeria
worker to "Palo" (chewing stick) alternative rather than tooth-brush
and tooth- paste. It forbids our people access to milk and tea for
breakfast and therefore constrain the full capacity utilization of
the beverage industry. It confines our people to "boole" (roasted
plantain, eaten with groundnut) and strangulates the fast food
industry. It confines our citizens to second hand clothing (Okrika or
Tokunboh) and negate the functionality of our textile industries. It
frustrates such large- scale investment as the production of
automobiles since we can only afford second hand vehicles.
The NBA might do a lot better with the information that over three
hundred and fifty thousand workers in the textile industry have lost
their jobs, as our people on the present slave wage cannot afford new
clothing. Thus, we survive on second hand textiles from Europe and
America to the economic benefit of the imperialist countries. Our
present wage system enhances our economic dependence on the advanced
capitalist world and our perseverance as a merchantilist capitalist
economy.
With the present wage system, the worker's propensity to save is non-
existent. To that effect, it limits small-scale investment capacity
for retired personnel and energetic youths. The NBA need to borrow
enormously from the Keynesian submission that increased resources in
the hands of the people enhances increased consumption. Increased
consumption enhances increased production. Increased production with
its consequent industrial expansion fosters increased employment
opportunities and propels an economy towards full employment. Full
employment or a low degree of unemployment and underemployment
eliminate poverty. The solution adopted to reorder the Wall Street
crash and the depression of the 1930s finds expression in this
perspective. It enhanced full employment for Hitler's Germany as at
1939.
Thus, the demand for increased wages by Nigerian workers is not a
self- fulfilling demand that offends their patriotism. Rather it
reflects a clarion call encased in organized patriotism. It is,
sensitizing the nation to a basic imperative that is indispensable if
we hope for any success at shoring up our depressed economy. It is
integral to removing our over six million unemployed graduate youths
from the streets of this country.
The NBA needs to find out why the Nigeria Police is essentially
handicapped in crime prevention. The implicit thoroughness of the OPC
and the Bakassi Boys in flushing out armed robbers even when they are
less equipped in relation to the Nigeria Police live sour grapes in
the mouth of patriotic Nigerians. When you give a policeman who earns
N7,500 a gun for the purpose of protecting public interest, guarantee
peace, law and order, you create a police force that use the same
guns to extort twenty Naira from every road user that transverse his
check point. You consciously or unconsciously put in place a police
force that connive with criminals and aid them in their endeavour as
long as they can afford the necessary returns.
Deriving from our present wage system, our borders will forever
remain porous as your customs and immigration officers have domestic
debts in the home front awaiting them each day. Our police must
continue to commit permissible armed robbery by extorting at gun
point. Our military men must continue to make up for the family's
economic shortfalls through across border operations that you can
call smuggling if you like; and for their activities, our people must
continue to pay more for fuel. Our civil servants must continue to
delay files for the purpose of "egunje".
The present wage of the Nigerian worker fosters the elevation of
personal interest above national interest. It makes nonsense of the
term security as it makes the security mechanism of the nation
inapplicable. The defect in our wage system is largely accountable
for the present insecurity of lives and properties.
The present effort of government at wooing foreign investors can only
thrive if the nation's economy indicates a commendable level of
consumption of industrial goods and services. This is strictly tied
to the real income of the people. With our present wage system,
government's efforts at attracting structurally meaningful
large-scale investors from the global arena remain an illusion. To
that effect, our poverty alleviation project remains at best, a
cosmetic palliative.
We require an economic environment that will encourage our few
large-scale industrialists to invest in Nigeria, as it will foreign
investors from the global arena. To escape the lingering economic and
social crisis that attend upon dependent capitalist nations as is
being witnessed in our present economic depression, and savor the
benefits of an industrialized nation, the Nigerian worker needs
increased pay.
I hope the present NBA leadership will keep faith with history by
rising above the settlement syndrome and desirability for government
patronage and uphold truth, honesty and integrity in its submissions.
The NBA is essentially a mass based organ with the primary agenda of
elevating the majority of Nigerian lawyers from "charge and bail"
activities to a more professional legalism and consequently, a
befitting economic status. To this effect, it needs an economically
empowered populace to get patronage for its rank-and-file lawyers.
The NBA leadership must be consciously informed and guided by the
historic legacies for which the organization has been known over the
years. It remains a mass based organization that should be
patriotically dedicated to the defence of nationhood. It must
continue to conform to the protection of the rights of all within the
ambit of natural law.
- Anyiam-Osigwe writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: We Should Be Sensitive to Our Environment
Headline: Ghana: We Should Be Sensitive to Our Environment
Body_Text:
Accra — Environmental degradation is becoming more serious in Ghana today and
it is high time this bad practice is brought to a halt. What have
become common now are bush-fire and indiscriminate felling of trees
by illegal timber contractors and chain-saw operators.
These unhealthy practices have had very serious repercussions on the
echo system culminating in the depletion of our forest reserves,
incessant drought and heavy economic loss to the already poverty-
stricken farmers in the forest and Savannah zones.
Apart from the natural resources that we are losing through bush-
fire and deforestation, we also have acute unhygienic problems in the
cities, towns and villages. Contrary to what used to be the practice
in our villages with respect to good sanitation and environmental
hygiene, the practice has been abandoned over the years.
WASTE MATERIALS
These days, one only sees choked gutters, indiscriminate littering of
refuse and waste materials all over our streets and compounds. Hardly
would one see inhabitants of a community weeding or sweeping their
environment and desilting gutters. Today, communal labour is a thing
of the past.
What is more serious and heartbreaking is that you see food vendors
displaying their food alongside filthy gutters, exposing them to
flies. People, particularly travellers, carelessly throw polythene
bags, plastic containers, orange and banana peels from moving
vehicles onto our streets with no sense of guilt.
Unfortunately, sanitary inspectors who used to ensure that people
keep their surroundings clean, maintain good health and bring to book
those who violate sanitary laws are no more. Thus, today, our towns
and villages have become the breeding grounds for mosquitoes and
flies, causing outbreak of malaria, cholera and other contagious
diseases in our communities.
It has also been observed that undeveloped plots and uncompleted
houses both in towns and villages have become refuse dumps and public
places of convenience where people ease themselves and also urinate.
The rate of environmental degradation both in towns and villages,
calls for concern. I think it is high time we became sensitive to our
environment and co-operate with Metropolitan, Municipal and District
Health and Waste Management authorities to keep our cities, towns and
villages clean. Through this, we can combat the malaria pandemic that
has plagued our country for some time now.
Also, as a country that seeks to promote tourism, we should endeavour
to keep our beaches and environment clean; free from mosquitoes and
human excreta. If we preserve our natural resources as well as the
infrastructures put in place we would be able to attract more
tourists into the country and increase our foreign exchange earnings.
In my view, a number of policy measures as well as pro-active
legislations should be enacted to forestall further destruction to
our hitherto "Garden of Eden". There should be laws prohibiting such
bad practices as littering, spitting and urinating in public. People
who use gutters, refuse dumps and riverbanks as places of convenience
must also be brought to book.
AWARENESS CAMPAIGN Vigorous environmental awareness campaign should
be mounted to sensitize the public about the need to maintain and
preserve their environment. This, I believe, should start right from
the nursery to the institutions of higher learning. Possibly,
Environmental Science should be re-introduced as part of the
syllabus.
The media has a very crucial role to play in environmental education.
Apart from using TV adverts in this direction, the various FM
stations should incorporate environmental awareness campaign in their
daily programme outlines to help achieve more fruitful results.
There used to be this GTV programme, Watch These Things, which
exposed people who throw rubbish from moving vehicles; ease or
urinate anywhere; and other such bad practices that people exhibit on
our environment. This programme really made people more cautious of
their actions. I think it was a very good programme that should
re-surface on our screens to help curb the massive sanitation
problems we are experiencing these days.
PLACES OF CONVENIENCE Besides, more public places of convenience
should be sited at vantage points in our cities, towns and villages
including schools, lorry parks and filling stations to cater for the
increasing population.
At the community level, Assemblymen and Health care workers should
step up efforts to educate the public on environmental awareness
through public lectures and routine sanitary inspection.
Food vendors and traders in general should be encouraged to keep the
places they sell clean and if possible sanctioned for violating
environmental protection laws.
Buses and 'trotro' drivers should have small dustbins in their
vehicles for passengers to put in waste materials, and this must be
emptied regularly.
There should be laws governing undeveloped plots and uncompleted
houses with regard to sanitation and hygiene and if any, they must be
enforced vigorously.
Above all, stringent laws must be enacted and enforced to halt the
diabolic activities by hunters, smokers and even some farmers who for
their selfish interests deliberately set fire to the bush for meat,
and end up burning cocoa and plantain farms.
Also, laws prohibiting 'galamsey' mining, with serious environmental
repercussions, and chain-saw operators from felling trees
indiscriminately, must be vigorously enforced to save the environment
and our fast depleting forest reserves.
It is said 'prevention is better than cure'. Let us have a nation
with healthy people to be able to realise and fulfill the set goals
of the NPP government by contributing in no small way to the positive
change we voted for.

Original_Search_Title: Riots At the Funeral
Headline: Kenya: Riots At the Funeral
Body_Text:
The story so far: Tessa, wife of Justin Quayle, a middle ranking
official at the British High Commission in Nairobi, has been found near
Lake Turkana with her throat cut. Dr Arnold Bluhm, her Belgian African
friend and fellow activist, is missing. Before her death, Tessa had passed
on documents to Sandy Woodrow, Head of Chancery at the High Commission,
implicating drug multinationals in the testing of dangerous drugs on poor,
unsuspecting Kenyans. Tessa herself had witnessed the slow death of a young
mother, Wanza, in the hospital ward where she delivered her stillborn child.
Justin Quayle buried his murdered wife in a beautiful African cemetery
called Langata under a jacaranda tree between stillborn son Garth and a
five-old Kikuyu boy who was watched over by a plaster-cast kneeling angel
with a shield declaring he had joined the saints. Behind her lay Horatio
John Williams of Dorset, with God, and at her feet Miranda K. Soper, loved
for ever. But Garth and the little African boy, who was called Gitau Karanja,
were her closest companions, and Tessa lay shoulder to shoulder with them,
which was what Justin had wanted...
Throughout the ceremony Justin stood apart from everyone, Tessa's grave
to his left and Garth's to his right, and a full two paces forward of Woodrow
and [his wife] Gloria, who until then had hovered protectively to either
side of him, in part to give him comfort, in part to shield him from the
attentions of the press which, ever mindful of its duty to the public,
was relentless in its determination to obtain pictures and copy concerning
the cuckolded British diplomat and would-be father whose butchered white
wife - thus the bolder UK tabloids - had borne a baby by her African lover
and now lay beside it in a corner of a foreign field - to quote no less
than three of them on the same day - that was forever England.
Beside the Woodrows and well clear of them stood Ghita Pearson [Tessa's
friend, a junior employee at the High Commission] in a sari, head forward
and hands joined before her in the ageless attitude of mourning, and beside
Ghita stood the deathly pale [High Commissioner] Porter Coleridge and his
wife Veronica...
Langata graveyard stands on a lush plateau of tall grass and red mud
and flowering ornamental trees, both sad and joyful, a couple of miles
from the town centre and just a short step from Kibera, one of Nairobi's
larger slums, a vast brown smear of smoking tin houses overhung with a
pall of sickly African dust, crammed into the Nairobi river valley without
a hand's width between them.
The population of Kibera is half a million and rising, and the valley
is rich in deposits of sewage, plastic bags, colourful strands of old clothing,
banana and orange peel, corn cobs, and anything else the city cares to
dump in it. Across the road from the graveyard are the dapper offices of
the Kenya Wildlife Service and the entrance to the Nairobi Game Park, and
somewhere behind them the ramshackle hutments of Wilson airport, Kenya's
oldest.
To both of the Woodrows and many of Tessa's mourners there was something
ominous as well as heroic in Justin's solitude as the moment of interment
approached. He seemed to be taking leave not just of Tessa but of his career,
of Nairobi, of his stillborn son, and of his entire life till now. His
perilous proximity to the grave's edge appeared to signal this. There was
the inescapable suggestion that a good deal of the Justin they knew, and
perhaps all of it, was going with her to the hereafter. Only one living
person seemed to merit his attention, Woodrow noticed, and that was not
the priest, it was not the sentinel figure of Ghita Pearson, it was not
the reticent and white-faced Porter Coleridge his Head of Mission, nor
the journalists who jockeyed with each other for a better shot, a better
view, nor the long-jawed English wives locked in empathetic grief for their
departed sister whose fate could so easily have been their own, nor the
dozen overweight Kenyan policemen who tugged at their leather belts.
It was Kioko. It was the boy who had been sitting on the floor of Tessa's
ward in the Uhuru Hospital, watching his sister [Wanza] die; who had walked
10 hours from his village to be with her at the end, and had walked 10
more to be with Tessa today. Justin and Kioko saw each other at the same
time and, having done so, held each other's gaze in a complicitous exchange.
Kioko was the youngest person present, Woodrow noticed...
White gateposts marked the graveyard's entrance as Tessa's cortege arrived.
Giant cacti, red mud tracks and docile sellers of bananas, plantain and
ice-creams lined the path to her grave. The priest was black and old and
grizzled. Woodrow had a recollection of shaking his hand at one of Tessa's
parties. But the priest's love of Tessa was effusive, and his belief in
the after life so fervent, and the din of road and air traffic so persistent
- not to mention the proximity of other funerals and the blare of spiritual
music from mourners' lorries and the competing orators with bullhorns who
harangued the rings of friends and family picnicking on the grass around
their loved ones' coffins - that it was not surprising that only a few
of the holy man's winged words reached the ear of his audience. And Justin,
if he heard them at all, showed no sign of having done so.
Dapper as ever in the dark double-breasted suit he had mustered for
the occasion, he kept his gaze fixed on the boy Kioko who, like Justin,
had sought out his own bit of space apart from everyone, and appeared to
have hanged himself in it, for his spindly feet scarcely touched the ground
and his arms swung raggedly at his side and his long crooked head was craned
in a posture of permanent enquiry.
Tessa's final journey had not been a smooth one, but neither Woodrow
nor Gloria would have wished it to be. Each tacitly found it fitting that
her last act should contain the element of unpredictability that had characterised
her life. The Woodrow household [where Justin had been staying since Tessa's
death] had risen early although there was nothing to rise early for, except
that in the middle of the night Gloria realised she had no dark hat.
A crack-of-dawn phone call established that Elena had two, but they
were both a bit 1920s and aviator-like, did Gloria mind? An official Mercedes
was despatched from her Greek husband's residence, conveying a black hat
in a Harrod's plastic carrier. Gloria returned it, preferring a black lace
headscarf of her mother's: she would wear it like a mantilla. After all,
Tessa was half Italian, she explained.
"Spanish, darling," Elena replied. "Nonsense," Gloria riposted. "Her
mother was a Tuscan contessa, it said so in the Telegraph." "The mantilla,
darling," Elena patiently corrected her. "Mantillas are Spanish, not Italian,
I'm afraid." "Well, her mother was bloody well Italian," Gloria snapped
- only to ring again five minutes later, blaming her temper on the stress.
By then the Woodrow boys had been bundled off to school and Woodrow
himself had left for the High Commission and Justin was hovering in the
dining room wearing his suit and tie and wanting flowers. Not flowers from
Gloria's garden, but his own. He wanted the yellow scenting freesias he
grew for her all year round, he said, and always had waiting for her in
the living room when she came back from her field trips.
He wanted two dozen of them at the least for Tessa's coffin. Gloria's
deliberations on how best to obtain these were interrupted by a confused
call from a Nairobi newspaper purporting to announce that Bluhm's corpse
had been found in a dried-up river bed 50 miles east of Lake Turkana, and
had anyone anything to say about it? Gloria bawled "No comment" into the
receiver and slammed it down.
But she was shaken, and in two minds whether or not to share the news
with Justin now, or wait till the funeral was over. She was therefore greatly
relieved to receive a call from Mildren not five minutes later saying that
Woodrow was in a meeting but rumours about Bluhm's corpse were drivel:
the body, for which a tribe of Somali bandits was demanding $10,000, was
at least 100 years old, and more like 1,000, and was it possible for him
to have a tiny word with Justin? ... With a show of discretion she removed
herself to the kitchen in order to listen at the hatch - only to find a
grief-stricken Mustafa [Justin's house servant], who had arrived unbidden
at the back door with a basketful of yellow freesias which on his own initiative
he had picked from Justin's garden.
Armed with this excuse, Gloria marched into the dining room, hoping
at least to catch the end of Justin's conversation, but he was ringing
off as she entered. Suddenly, without more time passing, everything was
late. Gloria had finished dressing but hadn't touched her face, nobody
had eaten a thing and it was past lunchtime, Woodrow was waiting outside
in the Volkswagen, Justin was standing in the hall clutching his freesias
- now bound into a posy - Juma was waving a plate of cheese sandwishes
at everyone and Gloria was trying to decide whether to tie the mantilla
under her chin or drape it over her shoulders like her mother...
Entering St Andrew's Church, Woodrow took stock of the congregation.
In a single sweep, he spotted the pallid Coleridges and behind them Donohue
and his weird wife Maud, looking like an ex-Gaiety girl fallen on hard
times, and next to them Mildren alias Mildred and an anorexic blonde who
was held to be sharing his flat. The Heavy Mob from the Muthaiga Club -
Tessa's phrase - had formed a military square. Across the aisle he picked
out a contingent from the World Food Programme and another that consisted
entirely of African women, some in hats, others in jeans, but all with
the determined glower of combat that was the hallmark of Tessa's radical
friends.
Behind them stood a cluster of lost, Gallic-looking, vaguely arrogant
young men and women, the women with their heads covered, the men in open
necks and designer stubble. Woodrow, after some puzzlement, concluded that
they were fellow members of Bluhm's Belgian organisation. Must be wondering
whether they're going to be back here next week for Arnold, he thought
brutally.
The Quayles' illegal servants were ranged alongside them: Mustafa the
houseboy, Esmeralda from South Sudan and the one-armed Ugandan, name unknown.
And in the front row, towering over her furtive little Greek husband, stood
the upholstered, carrot-haired figure of Darling Elena herself, Woodrow's
bete noire, decked out in her grandmother's funereal jet jewellery. "Now,
darling, should I wear the jet or is it over the top?" she had needed to
know of Gloria at eight this morning.
Not without mischief, Gloria had counselled boldness. "On other people,
frankly El, it might be a tad too much. But with your colouring, darling
- go for it..." His gaze returned to Tessa's coffin lying in state before
the altar, Justin's yellow freesias safely aboard. Tears filled his eyes,
to be sharply returned to where they came from. The organ was playing the
Nunc Dimittis and Gloria, wordperfect, was singing lustily along.
House evensong at her boarding school, Woodrow was thinking. Or mine.
He hated both establishments equally. Sandy and Gloria, born unfree. The
difference is, I know it and she doesn't. Lord now lettest Thou Thy servant
depart in peace. Sometimes I really wish I could. Depart and never come
back. But where would the peace be? His eye again rested on the coffin.
I loved you. So much easier to say, now it's in the past tense. I loved
you.
I was the control freak who couldn't control himself, you were good
enough to tell me. Well, now look what's happened to you. And look why
it's happened to you... Needing to distract himself, Woodrow embarked on
an energetic study of the church windows. Male saints, all white, no Bluhms.
Tessa would go ballistic. Memorial window commemorating one pretty white
boy in a sailor suit symbolically surrounded by adoring jungle animals.
A good hyena smells blood 10 kilometres away. Tears again threatening,
Woodrow forced his attentions on dear old St Andrew himself, a dead ringer
for Macpherson the ghillie that time we drove the boys to Loch Awe to fish
the salmon. The fierce Scottish eye, the rusty Scottish beard.
What must they make of us? he marvelled, transferring his misty gaze
to the black faces in the congregation. What did we imagine we were doing
here, back in those days, plugging our white British God and our white
Scottish saint while we used the country as an adventure playground for
derelict upper-class swingers? ... Woodrow swung sharply round. Gloria
beside him did the same and so, from the other side of the aisle, did the
Coleridges. A scream from outside the church had been followed by the smash
of something big and glassy breaking.
Through the open doorway, Woodrow saw the forecourt gates being dragged
shut by two frightened vergers in black suits as helmeted police formed
a cordon along the railings, brandishing metal-tipped riot sticks in both
hands, like baseball players limbering for a strike. In the street where
the students had gathered, a tree was burning and a couple of cars lay
belly-up beneath it, their occupants too terrified to clamber out.
To roars of encouragement from the crowd, a glistening black limousine,
a Volvo like Woodrow's, was rising shakily from the ground, borne aloft
by a swarm of young men and women. It rose, it lurched, it flipped, first
to its side, then onto its back, before falling with a huge bang, dead
beside its fellows. The police charged. Whatever they had been waiting
for till then, it had happened.
One second they were lounging, the next they were hacking themselves
a red path through the fleeing rabble, only pausing to rain more blows
on those they had brought down. An armoured van drew up, half a dozen bleeding
bodies were tossed into it. "University's an absolute tinderbox, old chap,"
Donohue [the intelligence officer at the High Commission] had advised when
Woodrow had consulted him on risk. "Grants have stopped dead, staff aren't
being paid, places going to the rich and stupid, dormitories and classrooms
packed out, loos all blocked, doors all pinched, fire risk rampant and
they're cooking over charcoal in the corridors.
They've no power, and no electric light to study by, and no books to
study in. The poorest students are taking to the streets because the government
is privatising the higher education system without consulting anyone and
education is strictly for the rich, plus the exam results are rigged and
the government is trying to force students to get their education abroad.
And yesterday the police killed a couple of students, which for some
reason their friends refuse to take lightly. Any more questions?" The church
gates opened, the organ struck up again. God's business could resume. In
the cemetery the heat was aggressive and personal. The grizzled old priest
had ceased speaking but the clamour had not subsided and the sun beat through
it like a flail.
To one side of Woodrow, a ghetto-blaster was playing a rock version
of Hail Mary at full throttle to a group of black nuns in grey habits.
To the other, a football squad of blazers was gathered round a coconut
shy of empty beer cans while a soloist sang goodbye to a team-mate. And
Wilson airport must have been holding some kind of air day, because brightly
painted small planes were zooming overhead at 20-second intervals. The
old priest lowered his prayer book.
The bearers stepped to the coffin. Each grasped an end of webbing. Justin,
still alone, seemed to sway. Woodrow started forward to support him but
Gloria restrained him with a gloved claw. "He wants her to himself, idiot,"
she hissed through her tears. The press showed no such tact.
This was the shot they had come for: black bearers lower murdered white
woman into African soil, watched by husband she deceived. A pock-faced
man with a crew-cut and cameras bouncing on his belly offered Justin a
trowel laden with earth, hoping for a shot of the widower pouring it on
the coffin. Justin brushed it aside. As he did so, his gaze fell on two
ragged men who were trundling a wooden wheelbarrow with a flat tyre to
the grave's edge.
Wet cement was slopping over its gunwales. "What are you doing, please?"
he demanded of them, so sharply that every face turned to him. "Will somebody
kindly find out from these gentlemen what they are intending to do with
their cement? Sandy, I need an interpreter, please." Ignoring Gloria, Woodrow
the general's son strode quickly to Justin's side. Wiry Sheila from Tim
Donohue's department spoke to the men, then to Justin. "They say they do
it for all rich people, Justin," Sheila said. "'Do what exactly? I don't
understand you. Please explain." "The cement. It's to keep out intruders.
Robbers. Rich people are buried in wedding rings and nice clothes. Wazungu
are a favourite target. They say the cement's an insurance policy." "Who
instructed them to do this?" "No one. It's five thousand shillings." "They're
to go, please. Kindly tell them that, will you, Sheila? I do not wish their
services and I shall pay them no money. They're to take their barrow and
leave."
But then, perhaps not trusting her to impart his message with sufficient
vigour, Justin marched over to them and, placing himself between their
barrow and the grave's edge, struck out an arm, Moses-like, pointing over
the heads of the mourners. "Go, please," he ordered. "Leave at once. Thank
you." The mourners parted to make a path along the line his outstretched
arm commanded.
The men with their barrow scuttled down it. Justin watched them out
of sight. In the vibrating heat the men seemed to ride straight into the
blank sky. Justin turned his body round, stiffly like a toy soldier, until
he was addressing the press pack. "I would like you all to go, please,"
he said in the silence that had formed inside the din. "You have been very
kind. Thank you. Goodbye." Quietly, and to the amazement of the rest, the
journalists stowed their cameras and their notebooks and, with mumbles
like "See you, Justin," quit the field. Justin returned to his place of
solitude at Tessa's head.
As he did so, a group of African women trooped forward and arranged
themselves in a horseshoe round the foot of the grave. Each wore the same
uniform: a blue-flowered frilly dress and headscarf of the same material.
Separately, they might have looked lost, but as a group they looked united.
They began singing, at first softly. Nobody conducted them, there were
no instruments to sing to, most of the choir were weeping but they didn't
let their tears affect their voices. They sang in hamony, in English and
kiSwahili alternately, gathering power in the repetition: Kwa heri, Mama
Tessa ... Little Mama, goodbye ... Woodrow tried to catch the other words.
Kwa heri, Tessa ... Tessa, our friend, goodbye ...
You came to us, Mama Tessa, Little Mama, you gave us your heart ...
Kwa heri, Tessa, goodbye. "Where the hell did they spring from?" he asked
Gloria out of the corner of his mouth. "Down the hill," Gloria muttered,
nodding her head towards Kibera slum. The singing swelled as the coffin
was lowered into the ground. Justin watched it descend, then winced as
it struck bottom, then winced again as the first shovel-load of earth clattered
onto the lid and a second crashed into the freesias, dirtying the petals.
A frightful howl went up, as short as the shriek of a rusty hinge when
a door is flung back, but long enough for Woodrow to watch Ghita Pearson
collapse to her knees in slow motion, then roll onto one shapely hip as
she buried her face in her hands; then, just as improbably, rise again
on the arm of Veronica Coleridge and resume her mourner's pose. Did Justin
call out something to Kioko? Or did Kioko act of his own accord? Light
as a shadow, he had moved to Justin's side and, in an unashamed gesture
of affection, grasped his hand. Through a fresh flood of tears, Gloria
saw their linked hands fidget till they found a mutually comfortable grip.
Thus joined, the bereaved husband and bereaved brother watched Tessa's
coffin disappear beneath the soil.

Original_Search_Title: AGC Bibiani Engages 200 Youth In Agric
Headline: Ghana: AGC Bibiani Engages 200 Youth In Agric
Body_Text:
Accra — Ashanti Goldfields Bibiani Ltd. has created jobs for over 200 youths
at Bibiani with an investment of Â¢120 million in farming projects.
The company owns 32 acres of pawpaw, 18 acres of plantain and
vegetable fruits.
Mr. Paul Ahedor, the company's farm manager, disclosed these when we
took newsmen round the farms at Ahwiaso and Bibiani. He suggested to
other companies to engage the youth in farming activities as a means
of creating jobs and prevent rural-urban drift.
Speaking in an interview later, the personnel manager of the company,
Augustus Ato Brucem, noted that the aim of the company was to
encourage and assist the youth to engage in pawpaw farming, which is
profitable in international market.
He added that, the company has started exporting non-traditional
export products for foreign exchange.
"The main problem facing agriculture in the country is marketing," he
said and appealed to the government to address this problem.
Earlier, Mr. Thomas Nyarko-Danquah, who is administrator for the
company, hinted that the company's gesture to engage in farming
activities was to ensure cordial relationship between the people in
the area and the company.

Original_Search_Title: Rat-hunters Set Forest Reserve Ablaze
Headline: Ghana: Rat-hunters Set Forest Reserve Ablaze
Body_Text:
Accra — Hungry Hunters pursuing rats on the night of Sunday, February 18, set
the 147 sq-km Southern Scarp Forest Reserve, which protects the Kwahu
Mountains, on fire, putting Forestry and Fire Service personnel on
overtime duties that lasted till after midnight.
When they eventually put the fire under control, 15 hectares of the
forest reserve had been considerably burnt down.
According to the Kwahu South District Manager of Forests, Joseph
Osei, though the 15ha looked only a small portion of the reserve, the
undergrowth, valuable trees such as "odum," "wawa" and "amire" burnt
by the fire were quite valuable.
The forest is also home of thousands of deer, grass cutters,
antelope, monkeys, squirrels and the like and some of them could have
been killed, he said.
But the most important damage caused by the fire was the destruction
of the vegetation bordering the Nkawkaw-Atibie road.
As the vegetation is destroyed the slopes of the hill are exposed to
erosion and landslides. That particular section of the road had
recently experienced two serious rockfalls, which blocked the road
and cut off Kwawu from the rest of the country for days.
Narrating the ordeal he and his staff went through, the Forest
Manager said he returned from a journey last Sunday around 8.30 p.m.
to find the Southern Scarp on fire.
When he called the Fire Service Station at Nkawkaw, he was told the
fire tender there had long broken down and been towed to Koforidua
for repairs.
"When I turned to Aduamoa, the story was the same - their tender had
also broken down. So I had to rely on two firemen's guidelines to
