make a fire belt around the blazing portion."
Later investigations had confirmed that the burning started from
furnaces carelessly left behind by rat-hunters, Mr. Osei said,
expressing regret that as at last Tuesday the culprits had not been
found.
"It was evident that they did the hunting very late, hence the
burning also started in the night."
Other items destroyed by the fire included plantains, cassava and
pineapples, in what is described as "Admitted farms" within the
reserve.
The Southern Scarp Reserve was founded in 1927.
Before then, people had made farms on the scarp, so the colonial
government recognised those farms as bonafide property of their
owners, and they have maintained the farms to date. These are the
"Admitted farms."
According to Mr. Osei, occasional fire outbreaks in the reserve had
repeatedly burnt done the vegetation near Atibie. As a result that
portion was planted with "gmelira aborea."
"Though these trees are used for the production of paper, we planted
them here primarily to protect the hill and yet last Sunday someone
came to burn them. We can only hope that they will survive and grow
during the rainy season," he said.
Mr. Osei expressed serious concern over the delay in prosecuting
those who burn the bush irresponsibly or engage in illegal timber
felling.
He attributed much of the devastation of the environment to the delay
and appealed to the authorities to see to remedy the situation.

Original_Search_Title: Diary Of A Killing Force
Headline: Nigeria: Diary Of A Killing Force
Body_Text:
Lagos — Mr. Ifeanyi Chinyere is lucky to be alive. By now, his parents would
have been receiving condolence visits. But fate decreed that he would live
to see another day. On new year eve, the 23-year-old, like many Christians
all over the world, went to his church, Holy Cross Cathedral on Mission
Road, Benin City, Edo State to thank God for sparing his life in the
outgoing year and to pray for abundant blessings in the new year.
Ironically, it was at this church that his life was almost snuffed out
by a stray bullet. The shot was fired right inside the "House of Prayer."
The pellet hit him between the eyebrow and the eyelid, hurling him down. On
his bed at the Central Hospital on Sapele Road in the state capital where he
was recuperating, Ifeanyi gave an account of how he was shot. At about 10
p.m on that fateful day, Ifeanyi, in company of his best friend, had gone to
the church which was just some 20 minutes away. The new year service was
already on and they promptly joined the faithful, while outside the church,
other youths were busy with firecrackers which went off every other second,
lighting up the skies and disturbing the peace of the night.
Apparently worried by this development, the church authorities called in
the police who came quickly from the nearby New Benin Police
Station. Immediately the police van screeched to a halt in front of the
church premises, the policemen swung into action, arrested the youths who
were subsequently locked up in a room within the church premises. Inside the
church, Ifeanyi was on his knees, offering prayers to God. That almost
became his last prayer.
Then within a twinkle of an eye, he felt a sharp pain in his eyes and
shouted. Instantly, blood started oozing from his eyes. He later discovered
that it was a shot fired by one of the policemen who was called to restore
order at the church premises. Surrounded by the worshippers, the policeman
who pulled the trigger was on his knees begging and saying he didn't set out
to kill him but that he only fired to disperse some youths who were
disturbing the peace of the church. Ifeanyi was alive to listen to the
sermon of the policeman.
But it was not so for Ayuba Amsat Parakoyi, the 23-year-old soccer fan
whose job was to discharge bunches of plantains from trucks.  Ayuba, an
indigene of Shaki in Oyo State on Saturday, 13 January 2001, went to watch a
Nations Cup qualifier between Super Eagles of Nigeria and Chipolopolo of
Zambia at the National Stadium, Surulere, Lagos. Ayuba who had hardly missed
an important match made it to the stadium but never saw one minute of the
match. He was shot and killed by a mobile policeman at the stadium over a
T-shirt which both of them had struggled over, only for Ayuba to have an
upper hand. But Ayuba's luck became his greatest undoing as he was shot in
the forehead, at close range.
Stunned fans later shook off their lethargy and reacted swiftly by
pelting the policeman with missiles. Other policemen who came to the scene
of the incident rescued the policeman from the jaws of death as they fired
tear gas canisters to disperse the irate mob. Expectedly, the following day,
the Lagos State Commissioner of Police, Mr. Mike Okiro denied that the
policeman intentionally killed Ayuba. Okiro bandied two theories to support
his argument. First, the police chief said Ayuba had actually tried to
disarm the policeman before he was shot. Second, he said he was making away
with a carton of Gulder that the Nigerian Breweries Plc (which bought the
rights to the match) brought to the stadium to spice up the match. But the
family of the deceased debunked all the claims that Ayuba was shot while
trying to cart away a carton of Gulder.
Spokesman of the family, Comrade Tajudeen Adelowo Tijani said Ayuba was
actually shot after he outwitted the policeman to grab a Gulder T-shirt
thrown into the air by officials of the sponsoring company, Nigerian
Breweries Plc. "The policeman thought he (Ayuba), would leave the T-shirt
for him because he was in uniform but when he refused to let it go, he
instantly shot him," Tijani revealed.  Relations and friends corroborated
Tijani's claim. Six days after Ayuba's demise, they stormed the stadium in
three commuter buses with a coffin and placards proclaiming that the
deceased was not a robber as claimed by the police. Some of the placards
read; "Ayuba was not a robber, but a strong follower of football," "Police
killed Ayuba because of Gulder T-shirt" and "Justice must be done to the
killer of Ayuba" among others. Ayuba was buried in an emotion-laden ceremony
at the Matori cemetery in Lagos.
Since the brutal killing of Ayuba, the police had come under heavy
criticisms that many observers thought would put a stop to the senseless
killings of innocent Nigerians. But it seems the trigger-happy syndrome is
endemic. As Nigerians were about to put behind them the brutal killing of
Ayuba, the angels of death struck again cutting short the life of another
young Nigerian, Abayomi Oyebola, a dock worker and undergraduate of the
Lagos State University, Ojo. Abayomi and Ali Salami, a fellow dock worker
were within the Apapa Ports, Lagos premises having their bath.
Abayomi had pulled off his clothes and had only his shorts on. He was
only waiting for bath soap from Salami but that was not to be. Unknown to
him, he wouldn't even be able to clean up for the journey to eternity. As
Salami was dipping his hand into his bucket to give him a soap, a gun shot
was heard. Abayomi screamed, and by the time his partner turned to see
what's amiss, what confronted him was Abayomi's lifeless body in a pool of
his blood. A stray bullet had pierced through the young man's
head. Frightened, Salami shouted for help which attracted other fellow
dockworkers into the bathroom. As dockworkers were trying to see whether
Abayomi was still alive, Salami ran out to trace the source of the gun
shot. He saw a gun-wielding policeman in front of him.
Salami confronted him: "Oga, you don kill my friend o!" Having noticed
that other dock workers were trooping out of the scene of the tragedy, the
policeman cocked his gun and threatened to shoot them. The dock workers took
to their heels. Seeing this, the officer also dialogued with his
legs. TheNEWS gathered that another officer was later overheard asking him
why he committed the act. The police officer replied that he was pursuing a
wharf rat. Wharf rat is the name given to the thieves who operate within the
port premises.
The workers did not accept this theory. The brutal killing infuriated
them and they combed the nooks and crannies of the port for the
policeman. Unable to locate the policeman, they carried Abayomi 's corpse,
wrapped it in a white cloth and placed it on a plank and carried it round
the port premises chanting war songs.  Chants of "How many people police go
kill rent the air." These incidence of police killing of innocent citizens
are reported across the country. The police have become harbingers of
death. If it is not accidental discharge, it is extra judicial killings. The
terror unleashed on innocent citizens by these trigger-happy policemen seems
endless.
Although two human rights bodies, Civil Liberties Organisation (CLO) and
the Committee for the Defence of Human Rights (CDHR) are yet to complete
collating data of victims of police brutality in Nigeria last year, CDHR's
annual report showed that 55 Nigerians were mowed down by overzealous
policemen in 1999. In its own compilation in the last two months, the CLO
said no fewer than seven innocent persons were wrongly killed by policemen
in Lagos alone. Not even women are spared in these killings. Many of them,
very young and promising, were gunned down last year. An annual report of
attacks on women in Nigeria made available to TheNEWS by Project Alert on
Violence Against Women, a non-governmental organisation spearheading
protection of women's rights showed that between December 1999 and November
2000, six innocent women were killed by the police.
Recently, Lagos was agog with the pathetic story of how Mrs. Margaret
Ilo, 41, a mother of nine was killed when a policeman's bullet hit her in
the head at her stall on Alhaji Masha Road in Surulere, Lagos where she was
selling roasted plantain, groundnuts and coconuts. In a press briefing after
the incident, the police said Mrs. Ilo's death was due to a cross-fire
between a police officer and a gang of unidentified armed robbers. The
husband of the deceased, Mr. Peter Ilo who claimed they had been married for
21 years, denied that his wife was shot in a cross-fire between the
policemen and robbers. "There was no shoot-out between robbers and policemen
around the area on the said day Saturday, 23 December 2000.
The evidence of my wife being killed by bullets of Mr. Martins, the
policeman, who was chasing and firing at the youths, who were using
fireworks during the street carnival around the point she was selling, is
overwhelming." Mrs. Ilo's sad story brings to mind the killing on Friday, 5
May 2000 in Lagos of Miss Odunola Hannah Ali, a 25-year-old Building
Technology graduate of the Yaba College of Technology. She was allegedly
killed by bullet from a security agent's gun in the convoy of the Deputy
Governor of Lagos State, Mrs.  Kofo Bucknor-Akerele. Odunola was waiting for
a cab at the bus stop to take her back to her office from an official
assignment when her life was snuffed out. Her death was widely publicised in
the media.
Three weeks after Odunola was felled by police bullets, Mrs. Juliana
Obiajulu became another victim of police reckless killings. She was shot
dead in her room by policemen who were said to be chasing a prostitute in
Makoko, Yaba area of Lagos State. According to the report, on that fateful
day, policemen from Area C invaded Makoko in Yaba pretending to be raiding
the red-light districts for prostitutes. In the process, they allegedly
began to shoot indiscriminately particularly as some of the prostitutes
began to flee on sighting the police. Juliana was hit by a bullet in the
stomach. She slumped, bled profusely and died few minutes later.  Deaths
from stray bullets are not new. About mid 1980, Dele Udoh, a promising
foreign-based Nigerian athlete heeded a national call to partake in an event
in Lagos. A policeman gunned him down at Yaba before the events for which he
was invited began.
Since then, it has become a trademark of the police to shed the blood of
innocent Nigerians in hazy circumstances. The question on everybody's lips
is: Why do policemen regale in killing those they are supposed to protect?
Mr. Shina Loremikan, project coordinator of the CDHR said investigations by
his organisation showed that they (indicted police officers) were either
drunk or under the influence of dangerous drugs like cocaine or Indian
hemp. He also attributed it to over-zealousness and lack of proper training
on the part of the police. "Once these people (police) carry guns, they see
themselves as having the licence to do anything," he told TheNEWS . The CLO
Police Research Project Officer, Mr. Okechukwu Nwanguma echoed Loremikan's
view. Perhaps more worrisome is why relations of these innocent victims
prefer to keep quiet rather than dragging these murderous security
operatives to court? According to Loremikan, the process of seeking legal
redress could be very problematic. "You need to know the name of the
policeman who shot the victims," he said, adding that "it is just not enough
to say just one policeman." He also said access to justice is another
problem. "To get justice in Nigeria is very costly. To get the services of
lawyers to file papers is a costly venture."
In his own submission, Nwanguma attributed it to prolonged cases of
litigation in Nigeria. "In Nigeria, it takes years before a case is done
with coupled with the fact that the deed has been done and the dead cannot
be brought back to life." "Even if they win the judgement, it is registered
in their mind that the judgement cannot bring the victims back to life." How
then do we stop the killings? Nwanguma said the Nigeria Police Force needs
total reform. " The Police should review their curriculum to include
training in human rights laws, not just criminal laws and how to pull the
trigger." Additional reports by Richard Elesho.

Original_Search_Title: Economic Development: Ghana Should Pursue Economics Of Commonsense
Headline: Ghana: Economic Development: Ghana Should Pursue Economics Of Commonsense
Body_Text:
Accra — With the advent of the 31st December revolution, many novelty efforts
were sprung upon the nation Ghana. With the novelty came the loss of
our virginity in a lot areas of human endeavour.
This submission will not evaluate every aspect of human life and
efforts made to "revolutionalise" our society but the focus will be
solely on the economy and its immediate ambit.
Immediately after the coup of 31st December 1981, no one could
actually tell what the revolution was meant for, so far as our
economic development was concerned, despite the rhetorics, slogans,
revolutionary songs, etc. The revolution did not provide any economic
blue print with a clear cut path that could state what parameters
were required and for what, what conditions were needed to achieve
short, medium and long term targets etc.
The result was that the revolutionaries embarked on all kinds of
actions some bordering on plain absurdity, For example, our borders
were closed for a long time, crippling trade between our West African
neighbours; they seized all goods they could find anywhere in the
country - shops, warehouses, storage points etc., and sold them at
very low prices without giving the proceeds to the owners of the
goods; they imposed a rigid curfew, the compliance of which affected
all levels of productions including industrial productions, because
the revolutionaries would not accept night work even in our factories
initially; there was a Citizen Vetting Committee composed of naive
and envious men who through plain hatred for the rich and industrious
in society, pulled down the "wage payers" and the wealthy in the
society and their businesses.
The combined effect of all these and other similar actions grounded
the nation's domestic industrial production, other economic
activities and trade with dire long-term consequences. In their
frenzy it did not occur to them to pay attention to income generation
for the country or better put, creation of wealth, whether in the
short or long term whilst indigenous companies of successful
Ghanaians were confiscated with careless abandon. All these short
sighted and unprudent actions brought the country to its knees
economically in the first year of revolution.
The situation became so bad that there was a sharp change of
direction from the socialist orientation of the revolution to quickly
consider the idea of dealing with the two Breton-Wood Institutions of
International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank.
The long and short was that the Economic Recovery Programme which was
started in 1983 later resulted in Structural Adjustment Programme.
The pursuit of these programmes brought about: massive foreign
capital into the country; terrible retrenchment of workers, neglect
of State Owned Enterprises and their later divestiture; removal of
subsides from education, agriculture and the like; globe-trotting in
search of foreign investment, etc. After the 19 long years, they have
come to accept wholly the private sector as the engine of growth
after they had destroyed local confidence in private enterprise.
God and daily bread The wisdom of the ages should teach us, as a
people, that the economic activities (daily bread) of any people,
have a lot of input from the Lord God Almighty - divine input The
earlier we Ghanaians realise and accept this fact of life and history
the better. It is not a weakness. This is not dependency, it is
common sense, at least it is better than borrowing from human
institutions or human governments. We must be ashamed, as a people,
that since independence we do not have national day(s) of fasting and
prayer to ask for God's blessings and guidance through Christ upon
our national endeavours or a National Thanksgiving day(s). Do not
tell me this is religious bigotry.
The fact that United States of America has such days and we go to
them cup in hand to borrow, beg for help and even go to benefit from
their educational institutions must cause us to think seriously on
this issue. Economic pursuit Some aspect of the P/NDC economic
policies and efforts accordingly to this writer has been too much of
a textbook prescriptions; the evidence of the inexperience of the
revolutionary administrators and unclear intent of their foreign
advisors, otherwise we should have known that over-liberalisation
will weaken our production base and destroy our currency which could
lead to the closure of our local industries thereby causing
unemployment.
Secondly, over emphasis on the building of physical infrastructure at
the expense of human resource as a factor of economic development and
other key sectors like education, health, job creation, agriculture
etc could never ever lead us to economic prosperity and any
meaningful human progress. We should have also known that if
foreigners invest heavily in your country, they will repatriate
portions of their profit which will put pressure on your currency.
After the long experiment their last found attractive phrase of
"private sector as the engine of growth" for which reason the PNDC
sold State Owned Enterprises (SOEs) whether viable or not kicks
against common sense.
This is so because;
(1) The Government cannot be in control of accelerated job creation,
if it has to wait for outsiders to come and invest in your country,
whilst you look on with unemployed youth staring at you on the
streets.
(2) Since every investor has his/her area of interest, certain very
unattractive yet essential sectors will suffer if Government dose not
get involved.
(3 ) Our circumstances as a people cannot allow local private
entrepreneurs to be in serious reckoning in heavy industries like
VALCO or similar ventures now, because the capital is just not
available in private hands or in the banks.
The economic mess in which we find ourselves must never happen again
and we can only ensure that if and only if we can develop our
resources including our enterpreneural resources.
This requires some serious participation of government or the public
in establishment of firms to begin with, and later float shares to
Ghanaians both home and abroad. We must also go into Joint Ventures -
Government/Private Sector. What is important is to privatise the
management.
Therefore, it is important that Government should and must restrain
the sale and recover some of the SOEs.
The solution to SOEs,
(a) Is to privatise the management. (b) Give the management
guidelines as regards targets allowing them full responsibility for
employment and disengagement of staff.
(c) Remove any protections, open them to reasonable competition.
(d) Ensure that reputed private accounting firms audit their
accounts.
These could go a long way to bring sanity and sound management
practice into these state owned companies. We can also sell shares
and allow Ghanaian shareholders to hold responsible management
positions. It is unacceptable that in the past we had a careful
policy of import substitution by which we produced sugar, tomato
paste, corned beef canned fish, textiles, garments food processing
etc., but today, we are importing everything. I mean everything. The
Private Press - the solution to Corruption As a nation, let us come
to terms with one of our worst shortcomings, which is corruption.
This one failing has destroyed all economic efforts since
independence and we need to accept and tackle it head-on.
If our new Government seriously wants to tackle this problem of
corruption and other malfeasance of government functionaries then
there must be a substantial allocation in the national budget to the
private press-both electronic and print media to cover some aspect of
their cost with the aim of playing their watchdog role, especially
those involved in investigative journalism Only God knows how much it
has cost, "The Ghanaian Chronicle" to investigate the GNPC for which
no action has been taken so far. The press must be allowed to operate
in an environment free of unacceptable laws at the same time,
protecting the privacy of individual but that law cannot be the
criminal libel law.
Government must act when the press blow, the whistle. We shall
achieve so much if we can minimise corruption as compared to how much
we could ever allocate to the private press.
Reduce the Import Bill No country worth its salt has liberalised so
irresponsibly as Ghana has done and other African countries would
like to. From the USA, Japan, and the EC countries trade is
regulated. China must be the shining example of any third world
country, for their domestic effort at economic sufficiency ------
"Domestication".
One of our most pathetic and miserable assets today is the Cedi. It
is actually worthless against all the major media of exchange. The
textbook economics prescribed by IMF and World Bank and executed by
the (P) NDC has rendered our national currency so low and this is to
our eternal shame for our lack of collective pride, creativity and
foresight, therefore, something drastic must be done about it. We,
led by Mr. J. A. Kufour's government, must accept this singular
challenge and deal with the miserable Cedi not with any cosmetic
approach, but with common sense, and sacrifice.
Any attempt at this will be a good start at the restoration of our
national pride. Our bane is that the (P)NDC's unacceptable
importation of everything should and must be a thing of the past. Our
import Bill must be reduced without any further delay. Immediately we
must institute a programme ostensibly to reduce our over dependence
on imports within reasonably short period e.g.,
(a) Within six months or so we must stop the importation of any
chicken parts into this country.
The Government must meet with Poultry Farmers Association and discuss
this possibility and inspire them, ask for and remove all production
bottlenecks and look for West African markets should a glut result.
(b) By December 2002 we must produce 60% of the rice we consume in
Ghana. From Afife to the plains of the North rice must spring out
(c) Stop immediately the importation of plantain and cassava.
(d) Look for other items that we could easily produce in the short
term and stop their importation e.g., revive our sugar factories by
the end of 2001 December.
In the medium term, let us start bringing to life some of our
factories creatively. We must show ingenuity in garment production
and let us invade the U.S.A. and the West Africa market with our
"Batik" and "Adjoa Yankey" etc. The Government must know that we
shall sink as a nation if by December 2002 we are still importing
rice, sugar, chicken parts in the same quantities as today etc.
The New Ghanaian If Ghana must go forward, then a "new" Ghanaian is
needed. This does not mean that all of us should die for new ones to
be born, but it calls for a change of attitude and total resolve to
see Ghana assume its position in the comity of nations, for this
writer believes it was not for nothing that we produced the Aggreys,
J. B. Danquahs, Kwame Nkrumahs, Kofi Annans etc. We need:
(a) A new Ghanaian who will take hard work and sacrifice for granted.
(b) A new Ghanaian who will eschew greed; embrace modesty and
simplicity. Accept that without simplicity we cannot be thrifty and
without thrift we cannot save and without savings our poverty shall
forever remain.
(c) A new Ghanaian who shall truly love Ghana for what Ghana is and
shall pledge head, heart, and hand to her without moderation
(d) A new Ghanaian who is willing to meet his/her tax obligations.
(e) A new Ghanaian that will respect and value time, and is prepared
to throw out of his/her vocabulary the metaphor of "African Time".
(f) In summary a new Ghanaian who believes and gives practical
meaning to the old biblical truth that "RIGHTEOUSNESS EXALTS A
NATION." NEVER AGAIN SHOULD A TRAGEDY BE VISITED UPON US SUCH AS The
(P)NDC May the Lord God Almighty gather all progressive forces
together, remove our divisions and help us build a new economy for
Ghana.

Original_Search_Title: We're Doing Well
Headline: Nigeria: We're Doing Well
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Governor Bola Ahmed Tinubu of Lagos State will soon be two years in
office. Up till mid last year, the perception was that his government
was not doing well. But that perception is fast changing, so much so
that a former governor of the state, Alhaji Lateef Jakande, who once
criticised Tinubu, withdrew his hard punch last week when he
described Tinubu as an "active governor". What is responsible for
this turn-around ? Only the man on the spot can give a credible
answer. And so recently, TheNEWS editors engaged the governor in a
question and answer, no-holds-barred session. Excerpts.
In about four months, it will be two years that you were elected
to govern this state, how has the journey been so far?
It has has been very rough, but satisfying. It has been very rough
because we met a difficult terrain, we met a system that could not be
relied upon. We met deep indebtedness, very unorganised operational
system, lack of a reliable accounting system and porous financial
situation. All the jobs that we did, preparatory to the assumption of
office were extremely helpful. We had a blueprint, we had a plan. But
it is one thing to have a policy, implementation of that policy is
the most difficult aspect if you don't have on the ground a very good
system to actualise it. But we thank God for what we have been able
to do in the last 18 months or so.
When you came in, the perception was that the shoe that your
predecessor, Brigadier-General Buba Marwa, left would be too big for
you to wear. What do you say to that?
I was not looking at that shoe, but I was looking at myself, how I
can fulfil myself and my mission. That shoe was not big, rather it
was extremely too small for me.
It was difficult at the beginning for me to outshine what I will
describe as a military populist propaganda, well entrenched on the
ground, but whose agenda was very cosmetic. Before the military
populism, the state had suffered denials and deprivations of all
sorts under other military rulers. Then euphoria greeted the populist
soldier who out performed those before him. The euphoria of that
military populism was still there when I came in. It was inherited.
But you look at what we have been able to get done within 18 months
of being in office. Over 300 roads tarred, two new major roads
constructed.
Show me where that is matched. The evidence and the records are
there. The highest financial position the state ever achieved
throughout the years of that military populism was N14 billion. The
record is there. In my first year in office, I took that budget to
N40 billion. I had a bigger challenge. The budget was a quantum leap.
I met skyscrapers of refuse on the ground. I met several kilometers
long of refuse. Dump sites at Olusosun and other locations had no
tarred access roads, not to talk of the surroundings. How do you
improve efficiency, how do you improve waste collection and disposal,
all year round, come rain, come sunshine? We first tackled the dump
sites. All of them now have very effective and superior road networks
today, all achieved within the first 12 months in office.
Because we did not employ so much of propaganda, a lot of our efforts
were not appreciated by our people. But I feel if you have a serious
investigation and do a comparative analysis of what our predecessor
achieved and what we achieved, ours is better. You look at the
education sector, was it touched by the previous administration? If
it was touched, it was cosmetic. All the shanties under which our
children learnt, and which Alhaji Lateef Jakande administration left
them behind were not improved upon. Jakande built them 20 years ago
to occupy land in order to collapse the three shifts or evening
schools with the hope that they would now continue to build them into
a habitable and good learning environment later on.
Ever since Jakande left office, successive military governments
(except Group Captain Gbolahan Mudashiru's), did not touch any of
those schools. He alone built solid structures that are historic. But
look at the successive governments. They ignored all those primary
schools. They did not touch any one of them for almost 20 years. How
many of them supplied benches and desks to schools? Show me the low
income housing that was embarked upon by the military governments.
What then could be the reason for the general perception that you
were not doing so well?
First of all, it was the propaganda of some political enemies, an
internal faction of the Alliance for Democracy, AD singing a
different song. And again, we were too busy. We had too much to
achieve that we had no time to announce them to the public. The
people at the grassroots, whose areas and lives we touched know that
we have done something.
Having put over 25 rural water projects together, having put over 25
rural mobile clinics into service, having improved the ambulance
system, the programme of treating the children free, our social
programmes, that we have just embarked upon, you don't see them as
you see bricks and mortar. Then there is the payment of the
examination fees. Our administration has done that.
We put people-centred programmes together. You cannot eradicate
poverty by not putting people first. When we put people first, those
social programmes are not visible as bricks and mortar.
So, immediately you announce such a policy and you don't make noise
about it, and you are busy doing all the things to make you achieve
more, people would not know.
Are you ready to announce and make noise now?
Yes, we will have to. We were baffled by all those uninformed
criticisms and we were touched that ah ah, maybe people really think
that we were doing nothing. We are determined to show our people that
we have done a lot.
There is this argument that you could not do much because you
spent much of the time on political wrangling.
No. The time for that is different from the time to do the work.
Let us take the challenge of taking the budget from N14 billion to
N40 billion in the first one year. That was quite a phenomenal
accomplishment. But what is going to follow? Is the volume of
transaction not going to increase? Is the volume of supervision not
going to increase? How are you going to pronounce that budget and not
prepare to target generating that revenue for the implementation of
that budget, particularly when you have a unitary, lopsided federal
system. That is one area of challenge. You have to be creative,
innovative and effective in your supervision and in the
implementation of those goals. That is one area.
Two, you have sudden labour unrest that you took in a pragmatic
manner. Today, if you look at what is happening around the country,
after all the hue and cry on this so-called increase in wages, what
is it ending up to now? I pointed out that the end result will be
retrenchment of workers. What is happening around the country now?
Tell me.
Has the Federal Government been able to implement that salary fully
and efficiently as it pronounced? It is good to improve the
purchasing power of the employees, the workers, but you do it in a
manner that would not be injurious to the workers themselves.
And if you say you are running a people- centred government, you have
to look at the intricacies of the required method to have a balance.
We did not come here to serve the rich who can take care of
themselves. And they should be able to take care of the poor equally.
This is because if you neglect the poor, you would pay the price
somewhere along the line. Can you imagine that it is a driver, a
carpenter or a taxi driver's child that will drop out when you charge
school fees?
I met several people that asked me to help them during my campaign on
their WAEC fees, that the children had been unable to take the
examinations. I met a woman, who said 'I have two of them, I am
afraid the third one will soon join them. They are unable to take the
examination, because I sell bread.'
Can you imagine if you have two or three secondary school children
and you are asked to pay N6,000 as a bread seller who supports a
carpenter husband, how will you pay? What is their total income?
So, if you don't pay for the children of the poor to be able to take
examinations and make progress, then, there will be trouble in
future. By the time I approved the first N100 million towards that
(West African Senior Secondary School Certificate) examination, I had
forgotten that the practical examination for science students is a
separate expenditure. When they brought the bill that some of them
were required to pay for the practicals and the apparatus, I almost
refused to pay. One day, as I was going home, I saw a woman, sitting
by the roadside roasting corn and plantain with a kid running around.
I asked myself, "if you refuse to pay for the science practicals, are
you saying the child of this poor woman should not be given the
opportunity to become a pharmacist, an engineer or a doctor? I said
no. I turned round, went back to the office and signed the file.
But those are the things that you would not see physically. If I put
those funds in a low income housing or used it for something else
that has physical structures, you will see the applause. You can ask
the Commissioner for Works how many ( in the first year and at what
cost) roads were we able to cover? If they had done a good job, prior
to our coming in, then the roads would not be in such deplorable
condition.
Which of your programmes would you say satisfies you the most in
Lagos State?
The revolution created in the electricity sector. Things will
never be the same again in that industry. I don't care who claims the
credit, where or when. Without the wake-up call, without that
innovation and creativity to have the international contract to bring
in Enron, an international company of that stature will not want to
have anything to do with this country. We brought that project, we
reminded the Federal Government that it was part of our
people-centred programme. I pursued Mr. President (Olusegun Obasanjo)
to South Africa that, one thing he must do is to decentralise the
control, the monopoly on electricity by NEPA. Privatise it, bring in
new companies; that he should bring emergency relief, the support
structure even if people are arguing that they must first repair or
buy new things. I said bring the emergency support to electricity,
generation, supply and distribution first.
There are reports that Enron finds it difficult to obtain finance
and that the company brought in obsolete equipment from the
Philippines. Is that why it has not commenced operation?
No, the equipment were being used in the various areas that
required emergency. They are assets.
You have to give credit where it is due. Enron's equipment are not
obsolete. Two, we must not forget that there is this general
misunderstanding that Enron was a contractor to the Lagos State
Government. No, Enron is an investor. The people will only pay for
the service Enron delivers. It is not that Lagos State bought the
equipment. We did not even invest any capital.
It is Enron's money. Anyway, there is no power generating plant in
Nigeria that is not three times as old as their (Enron's) barges. By
international standard of those equipment, they are new. People don't
even understand, Enron is even at risk. If it has an agreement and it
does not generate, you don't pay.
People forget the idea of foreign direct investment in capital
projects. They forget the concept of build, operate, hold and
transfer. They forget the benefit of just bringing investment into
the economy. The danger of continuing to be in contractual
procurement mode is non-innovative, fraught with fraud and it takes
too much capacity and opportunity away from both sides.
What we did with Enron is to invest nothing, but goodwill. They
brought their capital. It's like you want the revenue, you want the
profit. We create the environment and the opportunity for you. Come
and invest with the support of the Corporate Council on Africa.
Enron is one of the largest electricity generating companies in the
world and it does not need to secure funding from any sector. Is it
not because of the same Enron that India had to change its laws in
order to have Enron intervene in its electricity generation?
I have a copy of Fortune 500, the magazine that wrote on this
authoritatively. The same Enron is the largest electricity trader in
America. It does not depend on the World Bank or anybody, it has its
own funds for the project. And the equipment are there for you to
see.
Then AES has reportedly taken over Enron's share in the project...
No, that is just a trading option. Now, you could see the
integrity. In asset base, Enron is number one. In electricity
generation, AES is number one. Check the records. Now, for AES to
want to partner Enron, in order to have inroad to that investment is
a double accomplishment, that is to have the calibre of those
corporations invest in Nigeria.
At the level of risk that we have in Nigeria today, how many foreign
companies are ready to invest here. Now, we are talking of $147
million investment in Nigeria. How many companies are ready to do
that, save the oil companies that are already in the business. The
other companies you could find are those that buy our GSM licence
today.
Foreign direct investment is needed for us to really put the benefit
and dividends of democracy in the hands of our people. We cannot
operate in this closed circuit system. You have to open up and create
the enabling environment so that foreign direct investment could flow
into this country. Without all these, we will continue to have this
depreciation and devaluation in our currency. You would continue to
have problems in the job creation aspect. You would continue to
depend on foreign aids and grants in order to alleviate poverty.
Worse still, your credit worthiness is zero. You are still begging
for debt cancellation.
When exactly will Lagosians start benefitting from this new power
project?
They are already benefitting. This is because the challenge we
have given to NEPA to put its house in order, despite efforts to
discredit Enron, by rushing to do things, is good for the public.
Isn't it?
And within the next two months, Enron will release the 90 megawatts
into the system and it will continue to release as soon as it
finishes. By April/May, you will have a total of 270 megawatts. If
you add this to about 800 megawatts that is due to Lagos from the
national grid, electricity supply will improve. Lagosians are already
benefitting. Improved performance is what we want and that is a good
challenge.
How is Lagos State sure that NEPA will use Enron's generated power
for Lagos?
It will be used for Lagos. It is a promise in the contract. Those
who are technically competent will tell you. They cannot be taken
back. The level at which they do it in Ikorodu and the technicalities
involved are better explained by an expert. The arrangement is such
that NEPA captures, and reverts it to Lagos. The power coming down to
Lagos has to be stepped down to a level. At that level, Enron's
megawatts will enter. Obviously, you cannot step up to go to the
national grid. It is like you are taking water into a town with a
three metre wide pipe. For you to use the water, you will make it
come down to a metre pipe. Then water cannot go back three metres
because the pressure of three metres is more than a metre.
We are taking power at 133KV level, ours is 33 or 11 KV. It is
technically impossible for what we in Lagos are given (through Enron)
to return to the national grid. What is possible (which you and I can
defend) is that NEPA can deliberately reduce power supply to Lagos
from the grid.
Is there any way to monitor NEPA so that it does not do that?
Yes, there is a way. We must all be alert to that. There will be a
captive 270 megawatts below which Lagosians cannot have. What is
added to it from the national grid will be an addition. And this
cannot go back to the national grid.
Let us go into how you achieved the budget feat. You took the
budget from N14 billion to N40 billion. How did you fund the budget
without borrowing ?
We did not borrow, we just put a lot of things in place to block
the holes. There was a significant amount of leakage. In fairness to
the Federal Government, we benefited from the increase in the
statutory allocation to states. We also improved our revenue
collection system, not generation alone.
All these are protected against various leakages. We also improved
the efficiency and accountability from the banks. It is one thing to
have revenue into your system and how many days does it take for that
credit to be given by your bank. We have modernised the system to the
extent that, today, with Citibank connection, we should be able to
trace all our invoices on-line, like what the banks are doing to
themselves.
I give the Commissioner for Finance, Olawale Edun and Commissioner
for Budget, Olayemi Cardozo, kudos for being able to accomplish that.
We have to select those banks which can modernise and be on the swift
system to give us the credit to reduce borrowing. We improved our
collection of revenues and blocked the holes and have achieved close
to 70 or 75 per cent.
Again, in the various programmes, priority programmes are given on a
term payment basis. In construction, payments are done, based on the
certificate that is evaluated and deemed ready for payment. You can
award a contract of N400 million or N1 billion. You don't need the
entire N1 billion the same day. Progress payment is one thing and
then you will be able to perform as well as face the social
programmes that require cash outlay.
The combination is good. We have a very effective Ministry of Works
team under Engineer Raufu Aregbesola who has been working effectively
and efficiently. They work day and night. Perhaps that is one of the
reasons many people may not even know that we have achieved more than
any other government, especially on road works. We have about 14 to
17 gangs working at night with generators. It is a feat that has
never been accomplished in the state before.
So, where you have a lot of traffic, where the road work will disturb
the business and the traffic flow, we do it at night. People just
wake up and find out that the road is done. May be we should make
more noise about that and show the public.
People have expressed happiness about the Oregun road, are you
planning to transform all Lagos roads to that standard?
That is our dream. Good roads will enhance the value both of the
property and the street businesses. We want to be bold and courageous
about it and put a long-term programme in place.
That is what we are doing to other roads. We are facing challenges.
Soon, you will see a couple of roads on Victoria Island, that would
go in that direction. We are upgrading Adeola Odeku, Victoria Island;
Awolowo Road, Ikoyi, Iyana Itire Road, up to Yaba. All will be up
graded to the standard of Kudirat Abiola (formerly Oregun) road with
walk-ways, street light. In fact, Kudirat Abiola Road will link the
Lagos-Ibadan expressway at the landfill site.
Is your government doing anything about the Awolowo Way Ikeja-Iju
Ishaga road?
A road will link Mende to Opebi and the project will move straight
to Allen over the marshy land. We have not yet decided what will
happen to Allen whether it will have a fly-over. A traffic study is
going on in Allen.
If the standard of the Kudirat Road is extended to other roads,
your government will get more accolades...
You are right. If we want to behave like the previous government,
we could. But that is not the goal of this government. Our goal is to
look at the fundamental economic problem of traffic that is not
self-serving but more valuable to the future growth of the city and
the state as a whole.
No matter what short term programme and the benefit that you would
enjoy, if you can create a commercial economy, low, middle and high
income people, it is better. Because roads bring high development to
rural areas, you take it to Ikotun, Igando where growth and
development have been suppressed and denied over the years.
Successive governments created an unmanageable traffic into the city
centre. Whereas if you do a good job there, open up those roads, you
have given people the opportunity to circumvent various bottlenecks,
develop economically and stop migration into urban centres to seek
businesses.
We are doing Iju road. It is being rehabilitated. It is going through
a serious face lift and expansion. The cattle road that leads to the
lairage is being rehabilitated. We want to discourage heavy trucks
from going through the main Pen Cinema road. The other road, by the
rail line will be done in the second phase. People have to be
patient.
There is a large population of people who work in Lagos but live
in the suburbs of Ogun State. There is a community in Ogun that want
to drag the Lagos State government to court over the Lagos water
project. That is along Ajuwon, Akute, Ojodu where a bridge has been
destroyed by the state water corporation. What are you doing about
this?
Is that the one behind the Adiyan water project?
The one behind Gbolahan Mudashiru's farm.
Oh, that is Ogun State. We are doing something. But we are only
doing it on goodwill, brother-sister relation. We paid for it, don't
forget. For the sake of the water, project you have the Ogun State
shopping complex, next to the Sheraton Hotel and many more as a kind
of exchange. The Ogun State local government concerned should have
done that. But if they appeal, we may join hands.
Is it not a case of environmental damage?
You have to prove that.
Some residents in those areas work and pay their taxes in Lagos.
Their tenement rates are not paid to Lagos. But more importantly,
before this administration, the stretch of roads just before the
waterworks Ajuwon (after the Grail Land) was bad. Nobody could deny
that. We rehabilitated it with N22 million. We did it from the Lagos
end to the Ogun end.
Lagos also did the road that links Ojodu to Akute. Lagos State has
awarded the contract for a link road from Alakuko to Iju for about
N76 million. The project is on going. We have tarred Ijaiye to Iju
Station again. Costain is doing it.
Let us discuss the state of insecurity in Lagos State. What is
your government doing about armed robbery, for example?
We are doing a lot, we are supporting the Nigeria Police, the
neighbourhood watch. We are re-equipping them, buying vehicles,
coordinating with the federal agencies, supporting the Rapid Response
Squad and the Special Anti-Robbery Squad to be more effective and
efficient.
We have brought in the joint police-military patrol. The crime rate
is going down. You cannot eliminate crime, poverty and unemployment
in the country. But you can reduce them considerably.
You have to create more jobs. You have to create opportunities for
people, thereby creating the right environment for foreign direct
investment that will have impact on our economy. We have to give
security to investors in order for them to invest here and help our
growth and jump-start the economy.
Have you dropped your regional police advocacy?
I will never drop it. I believe so much in it and other governors
of the South do. You cannot effectively combat crime with the type of
police structure that we have. People have to stay away from the
politicisation of this idea. If you don't have a community-based
police, if you don't have a state-owned police, you cannot
effectively combat crime. Police should forget this old barracks
system approach. Those people who should live in the barracks should
be the violence-combating police. They have to be ready at the beck
and call, such as the mobile police. But those who are supposed to
fight crime should live within the community. They would know the
areas where the criminals live. They know people in their
neighbourhood and must be able to react to or prevent crime. It is
not to look for armed robbers after the damage had been done. They
are to redevelop the minds of the people, to interact and have access
to information. Criminal tendencies will be identified easily. Then
the command structure will be different. In fact, it is
unconstitutional to have one federal police.
Then there is the Lagos abattoir crisis. A section is opposed to
government's policy of no business after 6 p.m. What is the way out
of the crisis there?
Other markets in Lagos close at 6 p.m. So, there is nothing
discriminatory there. We have a huge investment of Lagos State there,
about N600 million, which is going into decay.
You have the environmental hazard, people living in there. Different
transactions go on there, prostitution, drugs etc. Crime wave is high
there, environmental damage and inconveniences to other people who
are not trading in cattle or are not in meat business are complaining
and writing to government. If we refuse to take action, then we are
not fit to be in office. We are not reacting to anybody's emotion. We
have the exclusive prerogative to ensure a high level of hygiene in
that environment. Human beings cohabiting with animals in animal
houses! That is unhygienic.
There is another abattoir by the bridge along the Lagos-Ibadan
expressway. They wash waste into the water and take water from there
to prepare meat. A lot of beef from that place is brought into Lagos.
We are going to pursue that and we will ban it. We are going to
enforce the law.
Lagos State has suddenly lost its first position as the richest
state to Delta.
We have not. Even though, we don't enjoy oil derivation, we are
not jealous. We support them, even if that will result in the control
of resources. They have the God-given right to their resources. They
should enjoy them. But Lagos State will have to fight for itself. It
is in the position of leadership, to be assertive in the pursuit of
the principles of federalism. Ports, for example, are controlled and
managed by the local governments all over the world. What is the
Federal Government doing there? It takes all the port levies and the
port charges, gives us nothing and leaves the trouble here- the
damage to the environment, our economy, infrastructure, the problem
of safety of our people is left with us. The Federal Government carts
away the money. We will not allow that to continue. It is our
God-given water. It could have been land or sand dunes!
strong> Apapa Local Government Council is laying claim now to wharf
landng fees.
Yes, they are entitled to it, even the entire state. We have to
pursue our rights. If the Federal Government does not yield, we will
go to court.
Lagos State has a deposit of bitumen? Is it part of the resource
control issue?
Yes, we are going to pursue that. I won't disclose what is going
to happen in the next couple of weeks.
Is it not a Federal Government project? Is Lagos able to handle
its own differently?
We are looking at that, that is why we are not talking about it.
Otherwise we may have to include it as part of the Federal Government
project, depending upon the agreement. But the question is they still
classify bitumen as mineral resources or part of the federation
account and we have to work to achieve self-control over it.
Why is Lagos State, which is regarded as having oil not
benefitting from derivation?
Majority of the oil blocks are still in the off-shore dichotomy.
We believe that offshore items should be included in derivation and
that is the only time Lagos State will enjoy the benefit of what is
taken from its territorial waters.
Still on resource control which the southern governors are
clamouring for: Is it feasible?
Yes, it is feasible. That is what the federal constitution is all
about. You contribute to the centre. You control your resources, even
if you have to contribute in form of multiple taxation.
You know the danger of instability. After 40 years of independence,
yet we don't have a stable democracy. That is the danger of
overbearing authority and unconstitutional financial power at the
centre.
That is why people are not competing to develop their areas. That is
why the federal is so powerful to deny states their resources. This
is part of the human rights abuses of the Federal Government. It is
unconstitutional.
Some governors have indicated interest in re-election, a case of
jumping the gun, so to say, are you interested in a second term?
I will make up my mind at the right time. I am still serving the
people for the confidence they reposed in me during the last
election.
I will continue to dedicate myself to serving the people. I don't
have to dance around for re-election. By the end of the year, most of
the programmes we are implementing will have come to full
fruition.And I am hopeful that the people will appreciate everything
we have done and compensate me with a second term.
If you look at the level of infrastructural decay and the neglect in
education and what we have been able to accomplish, how many lives we
have been able to touch, the schools (primary and secondary) that we
have rehabilitated, how many schools we have been able to equip
(laboratory equipment, benches, desks, supplied to those schools,
rehabilitation and equipment supplied to several hospitals. If you go
through the enhancement of the doctors' allowances to the building of
12 modern courts equipped with modern facilities, it will come to a
stage that the people will know at what level we are operating, which
governor has accomplished as much in the history of Lagos State and
at the national level.
Some of the governors who are seeking re-election have been
realigning here and there with the National Integration Group, NIG
and the Progressive Action Movement, PAM. Which group do you belong
to?
I am in the Alliance for Democracy, AD.
Some of them were in Jos recently, were you invited?
I was invited, but I was very busy. If not, I would have gone to
socialise, and interact with my mates.
The issue here is that if membership...
Once you are 50, you are no longer qualified. I will be 49 in
March, and one year before the election, I will be over 50. What
happens?
What is the latest on the government-labour conflict on the
minimum wage in the state?
We have paid the salaries. We have given the workers the basic
level that is federal and maximum. We have given all the junior
workers and we are paying the new wages. We have almost completely
resolved all problems. No matter what level of agitation there is, as
leaders, we must not stampede them into the unemployment market. We
must not reverse the progress and development of the state in various
sectors and sub-sectors of the economy, the future of their children
and the social welfare programme of the government.
While you also have to enhance the standard of living and the
purchasing power of the workers, you have to try as much as possible
to educate them to be able to stay in employment. The area of
friction now between government and workers is what level of arrears
should be paid and how much. We will resolve these problems within
the shortest possible time.
We even paid the teachers (primary school) on behalf of the Federal
Government in the last four months. People don't know that we are not
responsible for their salaries and those of the retired teachers. We
don't just want to embark on propaganda that the Federal Government
is not taking its responsibilities seriously.
May be we have to start talking about that. We have spent several
billions of naira on federal roads, but we are yet to be reimbursed.
The neglect of federal infrastructure in Lagos State is terrible. Go
to Obalende. I had to move in before Christmas to repair Apongbon,
because the traffic was moving at a snail speed to the inconvenience
of the people. People were just suffering. No amount of signpost that
I put there, that this is a federal road, would convince the people.
The same thing with Ojuelegba, Idi Oro, etc. These are monumental
problems. The Federal Government created these problems. Go to
Germany and find out. When the German government moved out of Bonn,
it paid a huge sum of over $10 billion. And the German Federal
Government has been paying $2 billion annually for the maintenance of
Bonn.
Lagos has been discriminated against. Billions of naira are being
mopped up from this state as education tax fund. How much are we
getting back. In the last 18 months, we have received, only N10
million from the Federal Government.
What of the Value Added Tax?
Yes, even on VAT. VAT is a violation of the constitution, so are
the education tax fund, petroleum taxes. They are consumption taxes,
which belong 100 per cent to the states. The states generated all
these and the Federal Government carries everything.
What will you do to stop this "stealing"?
I will go to court. It is an abuse of our rights. Political abuse
is even worse than human abuse, because indirectly it is an abuse of
humanity, when you deny them their resources to develop.
Buildings are being marked for enumeration. People see it as a way
for taxation. What is happening to MOT which people see as another
revenue generating means?
We are pursuing it. We will implement it, we are going through the
process of implementation.
MOT is in the public interest. It is not essentially a revenue
generating thing. But you have to pay for the service for you to have
a trafficator and good brake system. You can remember how many
vehicles have plunged into the lagoon recently. You cannot constitute
danger to the public because you don' t want to pay N1,000. In fact,
you journalists must help. You travel around the world, can you drive
without a sticker of road-worthiness in America or Britain? You go
there, you comply, but back home, you don't want to comply. You want
to continue to maintain the same backwardness in the society.
Leadership is not just the emotive aspect of popularity. It is the
courage to deliver what is good for the society, even if the society
does not see it. That is one of the qualities of a good leader.
We are going to enforce the MOT. The enumeration of those properties
is necessary. How can you plan for water, roads, markets, if you
don't have the enumeration of the properties. How can you be
efficient and fair in your taxation plans, drainage plans, education
plans, if you don't enumerate those properties?
A few people obey the law in this country. Even what you call the
tenement rate, only a few pay. And the rest of the society suffer.
How can you be equitable, how can you make everybody pay if you don't
have a proper enumeration? How can you get the statistics that you
need for the growth, development and administration of the state? How
can you even plan the traffic through that area if you don't have
efficient and reliable data?
As one of the leaders of AD, do you think AD has a future? Will
you still be able to control the South-West in the next election?
Yes, we will control the South-West and win more states. We will
expand. Believe it, AD is here to stay. I may not be able to tell you
precisely the possibility of alignment or realignment, anything is
possible in politics, it is dynamic.
AD crisis apart, there is this general perception that since
South-West governors have not performed up to expectation, the
People's Democratic Party may snatch one or two states in 2003.
That is propaganda! We are performing. We are performing. The
PDP-controlled government is embarking on the Universal Basic
Education, UBE which has heavy lead-feet. Since when? Since
inception. We all went to Sokoto to launch and give them support.
The free education programme of AD alone is people-centred and the
Federal Government has not been able to embark upon it. How will the
cocoa, groundnut sellers vote for the PDP that cannot provide free
education? The government at the centre is 18 months in office and
the UBE programme has not taken off. Show me, therefore, how they can
win in the states. Has the PDP government provided water for people?
Can we say the AD is still the same as the one we used to know?
Yes! AD members are the true democrats. How short are the people's memory. Look at the characters. AD is still a party with the people's
programme.  Show me any other governor who has performed better than what we have
in Lagos State, despite the complexity of our problems. Count the
number of our roads, the traffic, population, number of hospitals,
number of workers, refuse generated, water level (you have to
maintain those drainages). Rainfall in Kano is a celebration, it
sinks into the ground and dries up. But in Lagos, it creates problems
- flood, etc. How many states are exposed to the complexity,
diversity and urbanness of Lagos State? Tell me a farmer who can
single-handedly eat his food without the support of Lagosians! So
also the cattle dealers. Without Lagos, do you think they can sell?
Lagos is mini Nigeria. The complexity of governance here is more than
we have at the federal level.

Original_Search_Title: The Intervention Force: Why African Presidents Steal
Headline: Africa: The Intervention Force: Why African Presidents Steal
Body_Text:
Accra — The intervention force such as what the Economic Community of
West African States (ECOWAS) employed in Liberia and Sierra Leone in
the '90s is still not a bad idea. But no one is waiting to book
ECOWAS' Economic Community Monitoring Group (ECOMOG) for the next
conflict in the West African neighbourhood. The reason for the
hesitancy is that whatever is wrong with Africa generally, and West
Africa particularly, is pretty much reflected in the make-up of our
political and military units.
What fuels the continuing problems for Africa is that most African
militaries are raised like free-range livestock - they mostly
scrounge for themselves. But the fact that African armies are headed
by generals with chests full of metals belies their lack of both
structure and discipline. And so any response to conflict outside
each garrison's own station is an opportunity for robbing civilians
to feed their own brood. Yet, we cannot decry the armies' conduct
without acknowledging their responsibility to their families who
depend on them for, at least, part of their survival.
That is the general situation, so let's plug in the relevant
examples. Before I went to Sierra Leone and then Liberia as a United
Liberation Movement for Democracy in Liberia (ULIMO) official in
1993, I had already heard a warning about Nigerian servicemen crating
cars in Liberia and shipping them back home to Nigeria. That warning
was delivered in November 1990 in Washington, D.C., by Ms. Ellen
Johnson-Sirleaf. It was meant to underscore what appeared to be the
transplantation of Nigeria's criminal elements into Liberia through
the Nigerian military. I had dismissed Ms. Johnson-Sirleaf's charge
without denying its validity, and that's because for me, the only thing worth protecting in Liberia, at the time, was
life.
Less obvious at the time was the ploughing of the Liberian
countryside by Nigerian soldiers in search of plantains and other
staple for shipment back to Nigeria. Soon there were scheduled
departures from Liberian ports by Nigerian vessels with goodies bound
for home. Even then, our gratitude to the intervention force still
exceeded our need to complain about some of the indignities we
suffered at the hands of our saviours. So the disassembling of
Liberia for shipment abroad continued, and the number of complainers
multiplied.
One of the broadcast towers for a religious group in Liberia was
meticulously taken apart, crated, and sent to Nigeria. The dish that
had crested the tower in Liberia reappeared atop the structure
somewhere in Nigeria, with the calling sign of the tower's last
transmission in Liberia still intact - ELWA. It was the American
missionaries complaining this time.
Some citizens of Burkina Faso didn't need an excuse for going to
Liberia, and so they felt no need to apologise. They went to Liberia
to loot, and did. They carried away power plants, railroad tires, and
other industrial equipment. As soon as a conduit for transferring
diamonds from Sierra Leone through Liberia to the world market was
established, the South Africans came in. But through it all, the
biggest concern was not the rogues who came to rob, but the angels
who took to stealing - the ECOMOG.
There were moments of pride, however. At the Sierra Leonean
international airport, I watched two Nigerian airmen in their 20s
climb into the cockpits of their Alpha Jets, and moments later they
unzipped the African sky on their way to the Liberian warfront.
There were racial, regional, and continental prides all mixed up
inside me as tears welled in my eyes. The pilots would fly back to
their hotel rooms or commodious staff quarters in Sierra Leone after
their bombing runs. But the foot soldiers in Liberia had their noses
to the ground in search of survival. Whatever discipline they had
would break down against the uncertainties of their mission and the
need for survival. And when they came in contact with civilians, it
was easy to tell who the predators were. The ones with the guns got
what food was available.
So the question is not about African intents toward other Africans.
The intent can always be adjudged as being good, but it cannot stand
much longer in the face of pressing needs which are often satisfied
by rationalising criminal conducts. The corruption bred this way is
often already present when the African soldier or civil servant takes
his next assignment. But this corruption, which was at first
need-based, forms part of the character when an affected African
migrates to bigger responsibilities. This is why most African
presidents steal. This provides a neat perspective for viewing the
conduct of African soldiers who, from what we have seen, have a need
to steal.
Tarty Tey is a Liberian citizen exiled in the United States since
1984. He was trained in the United States as a journalist prior to
being perceived as threat to the military government of Liberian Sgt.
Samuel Doe and now President Taylor, both of Liberia, through his
writing. He works in Washington, D.C.
