Original_Search_Title: MP Maps Out Priority Projects
Headline: Ghana: MP Maps Out Priority Projects
Body_Text:
Accra — The Member of Parliament for Atiwa in the Eastern Region, Dr.
Charles Brempong-Yeboah, has outlined the provision of quality
education, job opportunities and road infrastructure as well as the
preservation of the environment as his priority projects for the next
four years. Dr.
Brempong-Yeboah made the disclosure at a meeting held with the 26
assembly members and opinion leaders in his constituency at the
Anyinam Mensco Motel last Saturday.
Before the meeting, the MP had toured the whole constituency to
acquaint himself with the people and their problems, beside
contracting a private consultant to appraise job opportunities in the
area. On education, the MP promised to seek government assistance for
a school at Sekyere and other second- cycle schools currently being
run privately in order that their facilities and tuition would be
improved.
Touching on the Kwabeng Abomusu Highway and several feeder roads
which are in disrepair, he announced that he had already met
authorities in the Roads and Transport Department. He explained that
their renovation could delay for some two years as they were yet to
be budgetted for.
Concerning a one kilometre road from the main road to a clinic at
Anyinam, however, the MP assured that it would soon be repaired as
plans are far advanced to execute the project.
Dr. Brempong-Yeboah noted with regret that "most of the streams I
knew to be flowing all-year-round have suddenly dried up. Excessive
logging and unsustainable agricultural practices are chiefly
responsible." Other threats to environmental safety, he noted, were
caused by people defecating in the outskirts of the towns in the
constituency.
This had come about because septic tanks for latrines at Anyinam,
Akropong and other towns had been choked and rendered unserviceable.
Aided by the private consultant called Kwaku Asante, the MP explained
to the assembly members that snail culture, honey production, the
growing of mushrooms and multiplication of plantain suckers could
create jobs and incomes in the Atiwa constituency.
The MP who holds a doctorate degree in Agriculture hinted that "by
this Easter, we shall start running training programmes, mainly five
different courses, for the youth in the constituency." By 2003, the
MP hoped, "contacts we have established would have matured for
investors abroad to start opening business centres here."
Meanwhile, he announced a proposal to put Atiwa on the website to
attract the attention of more potential investors and Atiwa people
working abroad to go to the aid of the constituency. On the creation
of markets for foodstuffs grown in the area, he suggested the
building of a centre at Anyinam similar to that at Takyiman in the
Brong Ahafo Region. An integral part of the market would be a big
parking lot for haulage vehicles whose drivers could be taxed to
raise revenue.
The Eastern Regional Organiser of the New Patriotic Party (NPP), Mr.
Alex Agobo, in a contribution assured that the new government would
prefer all district assemblies remain independent of political
parties. He, however, urged assembly members to follow with keen
interest all projects being executed in their electoral areas, to be
able to report delays and shoddy works to the appropriate quarters.
All the assembly members catalogued the needs of their constituents
and urged the MP to tackle them as early as possible. Among them, a
senior secondary school at Anyinam which once had Forms One through
Three has lost all the classes remaining only Form One and which
requires immediate salvation. By consensus, the meeting determined
that the surest way for the speedy development of Atiwa is the
creation of a separate district for it, detached from the East Akyem
District.

Original_Search_Title: Ghana in Retrospect
Headline: Ghana in Retrospect
Body_Text:
Accra — It's been almost four months since I arrived here in Ghana.
For the first month, I couldn't believe I was actually here.
Everything was a new adventure, from going to class to travelling to
places like Cape Coast and the Volta Region.
But gradually I became used to the differences in my everyday life. I
quickly learned how to bargain with taxi drivers, and I adjusted to
the different diet of mostly rice, plantain, meat and occasionally
fufu. Thankfully the cold showers were not so cold anymore and the
homesickness wasn't as strong as before.
But besides the everyday lifestyle changes, I make a very important
observation. Being on campus and writing for The Accra Mail, I had
the opportunity to talk with students about their country. Being here
in an election year let me see how students were keeping up with the
politics on and off campus. Through conversation with fellow
classmates, I heard and saw the yearning for a change in Ghana,
starting with a change in governance. This observation was one of the
most important because it showed me the unity that exists among the
young people here. This is something that the Black youth of America
need to see and learn from. This will enable more of us to come
together and change our communities for the better.
I feel it is my duty to relay this observation to friends and family
back home, in Washington, DC, to first start a discussion and then
start taking action.
It is my wish that the youth of Ghana continue to fight peacefully
together through voting and education. These young women and men have
the power to change the country politically, economically and
socially for the better.
As for my stay, I have learned immensely about myself and Ghanaian
culture. I will walk away from this country a more open and wise
person. This, I think, is the importance of exchange programs. I am
glad I chose Ghana.
Lastly, I thank The Accra Mail for giving me the opportunity to write
for this esteemed paper. And to the staff, thank you for being so
welcoming to me during my stay.

Original_Search_Title: Upper West Faces Looming Food Crisis
Headline: Ghana: Upper West Faces Looming Food Crisis
Body_Text:
Illegal exportation of foodstuff from the Upper West Region to Burkina Faso is causing price hikes and pushing the region to the verge of scarcity.
The situation has generated public outcry and people from all walks of life are calling for government's intervention.
Investigations conducted by the Ghana News Agency showed that about eight cargo truck-fulls of maize and yams leave Wa every market day for markets at Fielmua, a Ghanaian border town and Leo in Burkina Faso.
As a result, prices of the two commodities, which are normally low at this time of the year, are rising astronomically.
Maize, which sold at 100,000 cedis two weeks ago, now sells at 140,000 per maxi bag while three large tubers of yam, which cost 5,000 cedis, can now be purchased at 10,000 cedis.
Mr Iddrisu Mahama, an educationist described the situation as unprecedented and called on the government to intervene to prevent famine during the lean season.
He suggested the formation of a task force to team up with the GPRTU to bring the situation under control.
Mallam Mahamudu Iddrisu, Chairman of the Wa Cargo Truck drivers branch of the GPRTU admitted that their members were involved in conveying food across the borders but said the union lacked the power to stop them.
"They are our members but we do not control their movement. We can only stop them if we are given power to do so."
Alhaji Seidu Fuseini, a member of the GPRTU said smuggling of foodstuff would compound the problem of scarcity following the abrupt end of the rainy season, which had caused many cereal farms to wither.

Original_Search_Title: Government Releases Additional Grains - Minister
Headline: Nigeria: Government Releases Additional Grains - Minister
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development has
released more than 443,966 bags of grains from the strategic grains
reserve for distribution to the public.
This, the minister, Adamu Bello, said was to cushion the effect of
rising prices of food.
Speaking at a news conference to mark the World Food Day in Abuja
yesterday, Bello stated that all the states of the federation had
paid for their allocated grains
He also said that all the 774 local governments in the country and
Federal Government agencies, benefited from the grains release.
The minister announced that the ministry would buy off from farmers,
all excess grains produced during this year's harvest, adding that
this was with a view to encouraging them to boost production and earn
more income.
Mallam Bello emphasised that to achieve self-sufficiency in food
production, the ministry had continued to uphold its mandate by
initiating and coordinating agricultural programmes including food
and industrial crops production.
He said that under the programme, the minitry had embarked on the
provision of improved seeds and seedlings to farmers, backed up by a
virile extension service, training, publicity and research.
"The industrial crops production programme will promote the
cultivation of cocoa, rubber, gum-arabic, oil palm, cashew, cotton,
groundnut and soyabeans," the minister said.
Other crops approved under the programme this year included beniseed,
wheat, sorghum, cowpea, ginger, maize, kenaf, sun-flower and millet
among others, Bello added.
The minister further said that to consolidate the gains of the nation
as a leading cassava producer in the world with an average annual
output of 3,410 million tonnes, the ministry had similarly initiated
the Roots and Tuber Expansion Programme (RTEP).
The RTEP programme seeks to enhance hunger and poverty reduction
through increased production of roots and tubers such as cassava,
yams, sweet pototoes, Irish potatoes and cocoyams, Bello explained.

Original_Search_Title: Gemade's Re-Election Battles
Headline: Nigeria: Gemade's Re-Election Battles: the Odds, the Strategies As the Leadership of the Ruling Peoples Democratic Party (pdp) Gets Set for Its National Convention in Early November, Wale Olaleye Unveils the Plot to Unseat the Incumbent National Chairman, Chief Barnabas Gemade And His Strategies for Survival
Body_Text:
The next national convention of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP)
promises to be an interesting one not just for members of the party
alone but also for the generality of Nigerians who would be watching
the event closely as the outcome could decide where the pendulum
swings in the next general election between the party on one hand and
the other parties. It could also signify which direction Nigeria
would move after 2003. As the convention moves closer the shape of
what to expect is getting clearer and an epic battle for the national
Chairmanship is looking increasingly likely between the incumbent
Chief Barnabas Gemade and second Republic Minister of Communication,
Audu Ogbe
The convention which some members have dubbed the real convention
being the first to be conducted by an elected leadership of the party
could also be a test for Nigeria's democracy as it could showcase
Nigeria'scivilian government ability or lack of it to conduct a
general election successfully. This 2001 convention would be a
decider of many fates and a finisher of many aspirations. But beyond
the surface of the normal voting, characteristic of a convention,
stares many a surprise. From the seat of the National Chairman to the
least elected position in the party, awaits the biggest stun.The stun
is simply entrenched in some meticulously thought out plans to cut
short the dreams of many elected party office holders by not
returning them to office. This would be in apparent contrast with
what obtained at the November 1999 convention that brought the Gemade
executive to power. Even though tensions were high as candidates
scouted for offices at all cost, little was known about what were at
stake then. After all, new executives were just elected to chase
aside the appointed protem officers. Suffice to say that normal
political manouvering of wanting some candidates at the expense
others equally played out
The Plot Against Gemade
The arrow head of the plot to get Gemade out of the exalted
chairmanship post is believed to be no other man than Mr. Fix It
,himself. The man who perhaps, aside from President Olusegun Obasanjo
is the only one who can get things done both within the government
and the PDP. He is the Minister of Works and Housing, Chief Tony
Anenih. THISDAY investigation revealed that the entire game plan had
begun at the meeting allegedly called at the instance of Anenih. At
the meeting, it was further learnt, were the Vice President, some
members of the National Executive Committee (NEC) including some
governors of PDP controlled states. Anenih, sources said had called
the meeting to unveil to them, the purported decision from the top
that the Chairman, Chief Barnabas Gemade had not done enough since he
was elected national chairman two years ago to merit a second term in
office. In justifying this position, Anenih was also said to have
told them that the caucus that decided the fate of Gemade had also
resolved to field as the party's new boss, Audu Ogbe, second republic
Minister of Communication. Surprisingly Anenih did not have it as
envisaged as he encountered stiff opposition to the plot from most
members of the NEC and some of the state governors who were present.
And behold, the meeting ended in deadlock.
Allegations Against Gemade
Ironically, Anenih's apparent failure at getting these people behind
this projct was said to be largely due to the unconvincing reasons he
adduced for the plot. His allegations against Gemade was said to have
been dismissed as lacking substance and of no political weight. He
was quoted to have told the gathering that Gemade was becoming unduly
powerful and independent minded. He was further quoted to have
alleged that the PDP chairman had courted too much, the state
governors and was therefore becoming an institution. Beside these,
however he was said to have said nothing damning against the Chairman
that could possibly have been tenable to the meeting,hence those
present were not swayed by his arguments.
Gemade's Adamance
Apparently unpertubed, Gemade was said to have staked his neck that
come rain, come shine he would seek re-election. Even while Ogbe was
tipped as the annointed candidate, he was said to have insisted at
the meeting that he would test his popularity and electability before
the rank and file of the party at the convenion. At this stage a
source at the meeting said Gemade got more of the sympathy of some
governors and members of the NEC present They thereafter, THISDAY
gathered, kicked against the resolve to push aside Gemade basically
because of what it described as too much interference from the
government, specifically the Presidency. They further argued that
some of the problems plaguing the party, even at some state levels,
was because of the vested interest and interference the government
was having in the affairs of the party. They picked quarrel with the
Samuel Ogbemudia led-convention committee for releasing the
convention time table without having gone through the ratification of
the NEC. Such development, they described as insurbodination and
would not let go. And this perhaps, may have been responsible for the
slight alteration in the party's convention time-tableby the NEC at
its meeting in Abuja last Thursday .That meeting was said to have
been polarized by the chairmanship election as some of the state
governors who have been routing for Gemade, had under pressure,
supposedly from the Presidencysuccumbed o backing Ogbe .At the
meeting no fewer than 12 out of the 21 PDP governors may have changed
over to Ogbe. Their reason being that with Ogbe increasingly becoming
the favoured candidate of the Presidency, it would be politically
suicidal for them to oppose him knowing fully well that the
Presidency holds the knife and the yam so to speak in Nigeria.
Moreover majority if not all of them are interested in a second term
in office and the support of the Presidency would be very crucial to
their aspiration. Another development that could have swayed their
support for Ogbe was that PDP National Secretary, Okweisilezie Nwodo
may have finally accepted not to recontest but take up the offer of
Nigeria's High Commisioner to Zimbabwe believed to have been
offeredby Obasanjo. InNwodo's place Chief Vincent Ogbulafor is being
tipped and highly favoured. But these notwithstanding Gemade, THISDAY
gathered,was still insisting on giving Ogbe a good fight,even in
spite of the offer of a ministerial appointment made to him, which he
may eventually be persuaded to accept.
The Strength and Support of Gemade
In as much as it could not be ruled out that Gemade may have risen to
power on the strength of the Presidency support, there is also the
tendency that the incumbency factor which he may have wielded while
in office remain a useful tool to him as his faithfulls moves round
the party members to garner support for him. These loyalists have
begun serious campaign to thwart all the plans against his
re-election. This was sequel to the decisions reached at the end of
their caucus meeting. A competent party source who also attended the
meeting gave myriad reasons why the party boss would be re-elected.
He cited amongst several other reasons, the fact that Gemade had
brought the party to a lime light and that through him, a lot of
personalities have begun to join the party. He was instrumental in
luring two prominent Senators and a member of the House of
Representatives from the rival Alliance for Democracy party (AD),
into the PDP. Similarly he influenced prominent politician, Pa
Anthony Enahoro into teaming up with the party as well as getting one
time Foreign Affair Minister Chief Tom Ikimi from the rival All
Peoples Party (APP) to come on board.Also his not belonging to any of
the groupswithin the pdp has enabled him to cut across members from
all the sides building bridges . The source maintained that any
attempt to frustrate Gemade's re-election could in turn spell doom
for the party. The source which cited continuity as another major
factor for the man to be re-elected, reasoned that the task of
repositioning the party which Gemade had started would only
materialise if a stretch of sufficient time is allowed for such
planning and execution of innovations that his era had brought into
the party politics.
The Presidency As License
Even as the inter-play of interest continues to take its toll on the
major players, the only joker and guarantor of position that is being
used is the consent of the Presidency. And this has been used
severally used in the past even without its knowledge and consent for
selfish and personal reasons. Now, the name had become prominent in
this chairmanship struggle. And the way the anti-Gemade group had
been using the name may have confirmed the belief that the Presidency
was practically sponsoring some of the issues that are now
contentious in the party. But this was ironically being done as if
the Chairman did not know or have a one-on-one relationship with the
President. Gemade who seemed not perturbed by the trends had
therefore resolved to jettison whatever threat and put to test, his
credibility to win election, the source further hinted.
...And the Probable Consequences
Certainly, all the interplay of interest and muscle flexing could not
come and take its leave without leaving behind marks, either
positively or negatively.That the PDP is about the only party with
some seeming credibility is more of a mere fallacy now. The three
political parties are no better than one another. The difference
could be that the PDP at all times had tried to manage its crisis
such that it did not degenerate into a lousy affair, though this has
failed often times. But with the convention approaching faster and
the events still trailing, the out-come could be a surprising
misfortune that could spin around any likely success. If the party
does not put its house together and design further plans towards next
election, the November convention could surprisingly vitiate whatever
dreams it may have been nurturing of winning the next round of
elections. All other parties that are today seen as sleeping may
after all be working on their own success story to push the PDP out
of power.

Original_Search_Title: Good Food
Headline: Ghana: Good Food
Body_Text:
I discovered Caribbean cooking years ago when I befriended a lovely lady from the Caribbean. It was amazing that a lot of their dishes have ingredients similar to those of Ghanaian dishes. Caribbean food is exciting and vibrant. Just like some of our very own dishes here in Ghana. Luscious fruits, exotic vegetables and deliciously spicy flavours all creating a cuisine that is not only rich in variety but packed with delightful surprises.
I can tell you here and now that some of these dishes are so tantalising, your palate won't know what hit it and life will never be the same. Not with recipes like chicken in coconuts, pineapple spare ribs to plantains baked in cheese sauce and candied sweet potatoes. Oh, and how about crab gumbo? So along with me for the next week or two on an exotic visit to the Caribbean. Their foods, even though many carbohydrate based, are healthy too because a lot of the foods are complex carbohydrates.
In the Caribbean, plantain, both green and ripe is used in every unusual exotic dishes like stuffed rolled plantain, plantains baked in cheese sauce and plantain balls. And it's also called foo foo (same sound, different spelling), but they use it as a garnish for soups and stews. I bet you couldn't imagine eating foofoo with stew. But it's done in the Caribbean. This week I have two classic recipes for you from the Caribbean that you can try to your heart's delight. And don't forget to have your rum handy. I guess you could say the Ashanti is to fufu as the Caribbean is to rum. The recipes are beef and okra stew or what is popularly known as Beef Gumbo. Over here okro is usually chopped up for okro and garden eggs stew or "fetre dechi" or okro soup with kokonte or tuon zaafi. In this recipe okro takes on a life of its own; and then the dessert (I can never resist one) is rum and banana mousse.
Beef and Okra stew (Beef Gumbo)
Ingredients
450g/1lb stewing beef cut into 2.5cm/1in cubes
salt and freshly ground black pepper
Â½ teaspoon ground shrimps
5 tablespoons oil
22g/8oz okro, trimmed
1 onion, finely chopped
2 garlic cloves, crushed
4 tomatoes, skinned and chopped
8floz coconut milk
some fresh red pepper crushed to add some hotness
Method
Wipe the meat and dry on absorbent paper. Season with Â½ teaspoon salt and peppers. In a pan fry the meat until browned. Remove with a slotted spoon. Add okro to the hot oil and fry until brown. Remove that too with a slotted spoon. Next fry the onion, garlic, shrimps and pepper for 5mins. Add tomatoes and simmer for 5mins, stirring constantly. Return the meat and okro to the pan, pour in the coconut milk and 8floz water. Season with salt and black pepper and bring to a boil. Lower the heat, cover the casserole and simmer for 1-2 hours or until the meat is tender and the stew reduced and thick. Transfer to a warmed serving dish, garnish with sliced tomatoes if wished and coriander sprigs. Serve immediately. Eat with rice or yam.
Rum and Banana mousse
Bananas and rum are two ingredients, which feature, strongly in Caribbean cooking. And the two combine deliciously in this rich, alcoholic dessert.
Ingredients
2 tbsp. powdered gelatine
175ml/6floz rum
3 ripe bananas,vanilla essence
5 eggs, separated,pinch of salt
75g/3oz caster sugar,
450ml/Â¾ pint double cream,
1/8 teaspoon grated nutmeg
Method
Sprinkle the gelatine over 4 tablespoons of the rum in a small ramekin dish. Leave to soak for 5mins, then put the ramekin into a small saucepan of gently simmering water and heat gently for 2-3 minutes until the gelatine has completely dissolved, stirring occasionally. Remove from the heat and set aside to cool. Put bananas, the remaining rum and vanilla essence into a food processor or blender and process for 1min or until they form a smooth puree. Set the banana mixture aside. Using an electric mixer or beater, whisk the egg whites and a pinch of salt until stiff. Gradually add half the sugar, whisking constantly. Whisk the remaining sugar with the egg yolks until light and fluffy. Beat in 300ml/Â½ pint of the cream and continue beating for 5mins. Add the banana mixture to the cream mixture then fold in the egg whites and the gelatine. Pour the mousse into a decorative glass bowl. Cover with clingfilm and refrigerate for 2-3 hours or until set. Whisk the remaining cream in a bowl until firm. Remove the mousse from the refrigerator and pipe the cream on top in rosettes, sprinkle over the nutmeg and serve immediately.
Restaurant of the week
I paid a visit to the Novotel Hotel in the heart of the city. They have a spacious restaurant sectioned with lovely latticed wooden dividers giving each diner(s) that feeling of privacy yet spacious too. They serve buffet type lunches and each day's lunch has a Ghanaian speciality. For a French oriented hotel, this is very commendable. Novotel has a quiet ambience about it, a certain serenity that engulfs you when you are there. This has been achieved with a combination of the lovely dÃ©cor and a conscious effort to maintain serenity. You'll like the food served at the Novotel and their melt-in-the-mouth desserts. All in all, your experience will be well worth it.
When you enter this hotel, from the front desk to the waiters and waitresses in the restaurant, they make you feel at home with their smiles and willingness to be of great help to you. Another plus to the management of this eminent hotel. The Food and Beverage Manager with his staff will give you such a rousing welcome; so I say to him and his staff, good show. I had a delicious time and I will certainly be paying another visit soon.
The prices? Bearable for a first class hotel.
Top five restaurants
Princess Garden,Makaira
Novotel,Austina's CafÃ©
Taverna Tropicana
Pricewise
Princess Garden - bearable
Makaira -very painful
Novotel - bearable
Austina's CafÃ© - bearable
Taverna Tropicana - bearable
Top four supermarkets

Sotrec

Original_Search_Title: Nothing To Celebrate
Headline: Nigeria: Nothing To Celebrate
Body_Text:
If President Olusegun Obasanjo does not change his itinerary, he will
most probably miss activities marking the second anniversary of his
administration. He is expected to arrive the Asian country of
Indonesia on Monday, 28 May for a three day official visit to attend
the meeting of the G-15 group of developing countries. The president
may well consider the option of missing celebrating May 29 in
Nigeria. As it stands, Indonesia or any other country may prove a
more conducive place for him on that day. For many Nigerians, the
verdict on Obasanjo and his co-travellers in power is that of
resounding failure. Two years down the line, the much touted
dividends of democracy have not only failed to materialise for
millions of Nigerians, the living condition of the people have become
worse than it was under the military. Save for those in the corridors
of power, the two years of the current civilian rule has been full of
sorrow, tears and blood.
President Obasanjo knew what Nigerians wanted to hear on the day he
was sworn in. That day, after he took the mantle of leadership from
General Abdulsalami Abubakar, Obasanjo told Nigerians that "I am
determined with your full cooperation to make significant changes
within a year of my administration." One area where the people
expected the president to make a significant change is in the area of
power generation. Obasanjo himself knew this. He took a step towards
it by sacking the former helmsmen at the National Electric Power
Authority (NEPA). He made Chief Bola Ige (SAN), his minister of power
and steel and the former promised to turn stone into bread. An
initial promise of December 2000 was given by the administration for
Nigeria to achieve an uninterrupted supply of electricity. That
target has been shifted several times to accommodate NEPA's
inefficiency and the massive corruption in the organisation.
The 24-month-old administration of President Obasanjo has produced
five major NEPA system failure, an average of one in every four
months. With three hydro power stations and five thermal stations,
NEPA currently has an installed generating capacity of 6,000 mega
watts (MW) of electricity, what it gives the country now at best
hovers between 2,100 and 2,300MW, a far cry from the nation's minimum
capacity of 3,600. Again, the president set a target of 4000 MW by
the end of this year for NEPA but barely scratches the surface.
National President of the Institute of Directors (IOD), Mr. Emmanuel
Ijewere explained recently that 'even the 4000 megawatts would not
meet the demand." This is because Lagos State alone requires 4000MW.
All the state gets now is a miserable 800 megawatts. Speaking on why
NEPA has continually failed the nation, the incumbent minister for
power and steel, Dr. Segun Agagu puts the blame on capacity loss
suffered by the system due to poor maintenance and overhaul
schedules, prolonged neglect of equipment, seasonal fluctuations in
water levels at the major power stations and general indiscipline and
corruption by some NEPA officials. The last point is definitely the
most apt of them all. Even Obasanjo knows that himself and he has
been facing the challenge of putting the right man in charge of the
diseased NEPA.
Last year, he sacked Alhaji Bello Suliaman as the Managing Director
of NEPA, and placed the authority directly under the presidency. He
also appointed Engr. Joseph Makoju as the new M.D but all that has
failed to turn the power house fortunes around. Naturally, the
government lays most of the problems with NEPA at the door steps of
vandals, saying little about what happens to the millions that is
sunk into the organisation every year.
On 31 March 2001, the Federal Government read the riot act to
independent petroleum marketers and smugglers involved in the alleged
diversion of petroleum products to stop the act or face the full
weight of the law. The warning went on to say that marketers caught
in the process of fuel diversion shall be charged for economic
sabotage. Those who fail to deliver fuel lifted within three days
shall have their licences withdrawn and prosecuted accordingly. That
has, however, failed to ease the pains of fuel scarcity. With the
Obasanjo administration bent on deregulating the downstream petroleum
sector, the country has been thrown into the excruciating grip of
fuel scarcity. Last June, President Obasanjo frittered away so much
good will from the citizens when his government jacked the price of
petrol from N20 to N30. It took a well coordinated civil disobedience
spearheaded by the Nigeria Labour Congress (NLC), to get the
government to rescind its decision. Even then, a compromise was
reached and to save the Federal Government from total humiliation,
the price was pegged at the current official rate of N22. But the
government was not done yet.
It soon emerged with a campaign of appropriate pricing championed by
information minister, Prof. Jerry Gana and Philip Asiodu, the
president adviser on economic matters. To curb smuggling and increase
efficiency, the government is toying with the idea of further
increasing the price of petroleum products which will invariably
further emasculate the citizens. President Obasanjo gave an insight
into what his government has in stock for Nigeria when he said the
price of petrol will not exceed N40 when the oil sector is
deregulated. Deregulation, the government argues, will produce a
permanent solution to the scarcity of fuel and make it unattractive
to smugglers. Herein lies the cause of the present fuel scarcity.
Expectedly, the unavailability of fuel has affected all sectors of
life. The most noticeable is the price of foodstuff and the cost of
transportation.
The most glaring evidence of the failure of the Obasanjo government
is in the food sector. Almost all the staple food stuff common to the
common Nigerian has been priced out of their reach. Gari which used
to be the last resort of the impoverished masses has become gold
dust. With more people falling out of the elite class into the lower
rungs of existence, the demand on gari and other items like beans and
yam has increased geometrically. The result is the out pricing of the
products from the table of most Nigerians. A bag of gari which sold
for N1,000 on 29 May 1999, now attracts over N3,000 as Obasanjo gets
ready to celebrate his second year in office. Beans which was N4,600
per bay at his inauguration now cost about N7,100. No wonder a lowly
country like Burkina Faso, whose land mass is not big enough to
produce food crop for Nigeria could offer to export beans here. Apart
from the neglect of the agricultural sector by Obasanjo, who combines
his tenancy in Aso Rock with farming in Otta, the collapse of the
energy sector in Nigeria has made food production difficult. The
country now depend on importation of such staples from other
countries.
While Nigerians groan under ever rising high cost of living, official
corruption continues unabated despite the government's
anti-corruption law. An opinion poll conducted by a national daily
fingered corruption, unemployment and bad leadership as the worst
problems bedevilling the country's development. Little wonder that
Transparency International, ranked Nigeria as the most corrupt
country in the world. The position is an unenviable leap from the
27th slot that the country occupied in 1999 when this democratic
experience started. On his part, Dr. Pat Utomi, a business expert
blamed weak institutions, poor management and reward system as the
reason for the high incidence of corruption in Nigeria.
One other area where the president promised to work on during his
inauguration is that of unemployment. According to statistics
released by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN), over 60
per cent of Nigerians are jobless while factories run at less than 30
per cent capacity. While Jerry Gana says that the government has so
far spent N125 million on job creation, his counterpart in the
ministry of women affairs and youths development, Hajia Aisha Ismail,
revealed that over 60 million Nigerians are unemployed. That
represents more than half of the 1999 government census of the total
population of Nigerian. According to the National Manpower Board,
about 5.66 million graduates were unemployed in 2000. The figure is
expected to hit 10 million by 2002. A smoke screen scheme, Poverty
Alleviation Programme (PAP), was launched last year by the government
to provide self employment N10 billion was budgeted for the scheme in
2000 and at the end, it failed woefully for all the administration
did with the money was to use it to pacify its party followers.
Hand in hand with the problems of unemployment is the increase in
violent crimes. No class of Nigerian is insulated from the growing
menace of armed robbers. From the National Chairman of the People's
Democratic Party (PDP), Chief Barnabas Gemade to popular actress, Liz
Benson, Nigerians daily live in fear of armed robbers. One month
after the celebration of one year of democracy, dare devil robbers
stormed the Otta farm of President Obasanjo, shot and killed a driver
before finally driving away with a Toyota Jeep that belongs to the
President's African Leadership Forum. Increasingly, men in police
uniforms are arrested for robbery. Recently, three policemen were
sentenced to death for robbing and killing Katsina State bound
traders.
Not content with destroying its present, the country is also steadily
destroying its future. Although, the Federal Government has launched
the Universal Basic Education (UBE), the quality of students produced
in the country is appalling. Presently, the Universities have been
closed for the umpteenth time because lecturers are on strike. When
the schools manage to open, the infrastructure in them are not only
grossly inadequate, they are not properly used by the teachers who
themselves were not properly taught. As the count down to 29 May 2001
draws near, most Nigerians have no plans to celebrate. Like it
happened last year, only those in power see something to celebrate.
For the masses, they have a little praise for the government.
Stripped of their security details, what the ruling class will get
from the citizens come May 29 are deafening jeers.
.Additional reports by: Adesina Abiodun, Jide Olatundun, Okwu
Timothy, Dickson Offre and Olusegun Ajiboye.

Original_Search_Title: Why Agriculture Deserves More Attention
Headline: Nigeria: Why Agriculture Deserves More Attention
Body_Text:
There is not much to write home about economic policy of the Obasanjo
administration because not much has happened. I have been very
critical that the economy is still sluggish. I should have thought
that we would have paid greater attention to the development of
agriculture and agro-allied industry. There are a few examples that
one can easily call to mind. You know in this country, we can grow
all the sugar we need and save ourselves from importation of sugar.
Some years back, we started the paper industry and newsprint plant in
Akwa Ibom was established. We can revive them very quickly. In
Iwopin, another paper plant was developed to produce culture paper
which did not see the light of the day up till now. In Jebba, another
one was developing.
It was also done in craft and industrial paper. Then, we have the
sugar factory in Baccita and all these can be revamped. The
plantations-palm plantation, rubber plantation, cocoa plantation and
varieties of tree plantations can be encouraged. There is, of course,
the normal production of staples like yam, cassava, maize, rice,
beans, guinea-corn etc. If we can put things in motion and produce
these things in large quantities we will get more people employed.
And the contribution of agriculture to the Gross Domestic Product
(GDP) is very high. I think it is still higher than that of petroleum
products. And no country can in the real sense develop without
developing agriculture. We have to be able to feed ourselves and we
are meant to do this. Until and unless we pay attention to thisÃ±
there are, of course, other areas such as the petro-chemical industry
and the oil and gasÃ± we are not likely to make much progress. The
refineries should be able to work too.
There are a lot of by-products we can get from the petro-chemicals;
instead of importing the raw materials that we use in our plastic
industry, we can produce the raw materials here. The fertilizer
companies can be expanded. All these are issues that the government
ought to pay attention to. If we come to the cement industry, why
should a bag of cement cost more than N1,000 when deposits of
limestone are in Nigeria and evenly spread everywhere. We have
millions of tons of depositsÃ± we have them in Sokoto, in Kogi, around
Lokoja area, Nkalagu, Ewekoro, Shagamu, Ukpela and there are lot of
other areas. So, we can produce the cement we require. The government
can improve infrastructures and expose the industry for high
production. The commodity can be exported and if we cannot export, at
least, we are self-sufficient and we would keep the price low.These
are a few examples.
I supported Obasanjo and I supported his election, but the issue is
that his hands are tied. There are lot of things that he cannot on
his own do, under the present circumstances. Let me give a few
examples. Here, in our country, we never see any policy that endures
for long. We keep on changing, you lay foundation, and the
expectation is that people who come after you should build upon that
foundation. But, in the Nigerian situation, we keep on changing and
keep on uprooting the foundation.
Somebody was telling me recently that the Indonesian minister for
finance had been in that position for 15 years or so. We need this
kind of continuation, where is the continuation in Nigeria? We keep
on changing everything overnight. And then we want it to go roundÃ±
that everybody must have a chance whether they are capable of doing
it or not. President Obasanjo, to that extent, is limited. Take the
issue of external borrowing. He gave state governments such a free
hand to borrow money offshore and many of them borrowed such money to
build hotels and that kind of thing. Which is quite unnecessary. Look
at the time of Abacha; they started improving the railways but what
has happened to it today? In the time of Abacha again, they invited
the Petroleum Trust Fund (PTF) and we got roads improved. I know for
certainty that there is no part of Nigeria the PTF did not have some
impact. I can recall Mile 2 in Lagos, Apapa, Ikorodu Road and various
areas. In water supply, there was also improvement, like the impact
in 1004, Ikoyi and Ijora. The PTF got involved in those places, but
as soon as this government took over power, nothing did they do but
to criticise PTF, for what? Why not examine what that organ had done
and build on it? You see, these are some of the problems that we are
facing. We have to be patient, but then we always want things done
overnight. And that is why we talk of dividend of democracy what
dividend do you want? If we are peaceful and development is going on
smoothly, quietly is that not enough? So, this talk of dividend of
democracy is not existing. I don't believe in the concept of dividend
of democracy. I think we should rather have a peaceful and orderly
development. Can you imagine how we are going to conduct the next
elections. Is it going to be easy from the way things are moving?
This is the problem people ought to address their minds to rather
than expect something from nothing. If you don't work for democracy,
why do you expect dividend from it? Are we working for democracy in
this country, the way people are doing things as they like? Look at a
country like China; look at how it has developed, even Southeast Asia
in recent years. Do you know that at the time of independence, we
were far ahead of Malaysia and Indonesia, but they are now
approaching First World status. Why are we still blaming ourselves,
struggling over the development of basic infrastructures? We build
roads, we don't maintain them well. Which town in Nigeria has
adequate water supply scheme and why should it be so? And if people
don't get good water, how can they be healthy and productive? But, we
keep on floating higher ideas and doing things that we shouldn't be
doing.
I have not travelled to the council areas. But, from the general
information everybody seems to have, we could have done better than
we have either at the council level, state or federal levels. Maybe
our attention has been diverted in dealing with political problems,
which are valid. I mean our leaders have that problem to deal with.
And they cannot just set aside their political claims from different
communities on political events. So, from that point of view, one
must be very understanding of the problem the authority faces. But,
having said so, with these large amounts of money that accrue to
states from the federation accountsÃ± I believe there are states down
south that get billions of naira monthly and I don't know how much of
that money has gone into the development of infrastructures,
expansion of agricultural projects and industry. But from all
accounts, there does not seem to be much praise for wisdom and
prudence in utilizing the resources at their disposal.
I have seen the amount of good work governor Makarfi (of Kaduna
State) has achieved. The simple one is the roads. Everybody talks
about it; you know there are areas here in Kaduna about which a lot
of people had given up any hope concerning road repairs, but they
have now got those roads constructed. So, the general improvement on
roads is acknowledged by everybody. I think he is also doing well in
education and water supply. These are of great importance to the
people. I don't know what other governors are doing, certainly Alhaji
Makarfi should be given credit here. He also brought peace, because
the creation of administrative organs at all levels certainly tended
to bring everybody on board. And generally speaking the people in
Kaduna are happier today.
If we can put things in motion and produce these things in large
quantities we will get more people employed. And the contribution of
agriculture to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is very high. I think
it is still higher than that of petroleum products. And no country
can in the real sense develop without developing agriculture. We have
to be able to feed ourselves and we are meant to do this. Until and
unless we pay attention to thisÃ± there are, of course, other areas
such as the petro-chemical industry and the oil and gasÃ± we are not
likely to make much progress. The refineries should be able to work
too.
There are a lot of by-products we can get from the petro-chemicals;
instead of importing the raw materials that we use in our plastic
industry, we can produce the raw materials here. The fertilizer
companies can be expanded. All these are issues that the government
ought to pay attention to. If we come to the cement industry, why
should a bag of cement cost more than N1,000 when deposits of
limestone are in Nigeria and evenly spread everywhere. We have
millions of tons of depositsÃ± we have them in Sokoto, in Kogi, around
Lokoja area, Nkalagu, Ewekoro, Shagamu, Ukpela and there are lot of
other areas. So, we can produce the cement we require. The government
can improve infrastructures and expose the industry for high
production.

Original_Search_Title: Deregulation Will Not Stop Oil Smugglers
Headline: Nigeria: Deregulation Will Not Stop Oil Smugglers
Body_Text:
The most contentious issue in Nigeria today is unarguably the question of deregulation of the oil sector which has been generating heated debates and arguments from its protagonists and antagonists. Expectedly, organised labour is in the forefront, the masses of Nigeria queuing up behind it in opposition to the vexatious matter. What this contribution is about is to state that the argument for and against deregulation is a combination of facts, fallacies and emotions from both sides of the divide. My contribution therefore, is to state the issues involved in oil pricing so that the debate may have some focus.
Nigeria operated free market price system in oil between 1960 up to about 1973. Also, in 1997, after General Sani Abacha, then Head of State joined the World Trade Organisation, he deregulated. As in the previous free price era of 1960-1973, the 1997 deregulation did not achieve the expected result. Parity prices of imported petroleum products made importation unprofitable and the marketers stopped importation. A current and still existing experience is the deregulation of (LPG) Liquefied Petroleum Gas, that is, the domestic cooking gas. The price of gas has reached the roof at about N1,800 per 12.5kg cylinder. Yet, it is not available in the market for the few that could afford it.
Have we asked why the two experiences in deregulation and the free play of market forces failed? We should even ask why the deregulated (LPG) domestic cooking gas supply now is failing to flood Nigerian market with domestic cooking gas. Perhaps, part of the answer lies in the fact that the handling facilities at the Nigerian ports are not designed to handle massive importation of such petroleum products. The importers need the off-loading, transportation and storage facilities that are not available and which cannot be provided by the simple deregulation cry, otherwise, they would have been provided in 1960s and 1970s and would have been provided in 1997-1998 and, for the domestic cooking gas, such equipment should have been provided since deregulation in 1997. There are therefore, bottlenecks that deregulation and market prices have failed to remove. There is no certainty that they would be solved by the announced programmed of deregulation.
In Nigeria, we are endowed by God with oil resources. The economic principle of the law of comparative advantage dictates that Nigeria should produce, consume and export the excess of her need in exchange for goods and services that she is less endowed by God to produce. The argument that Nigeria should sell petroleum products in Nigeria at the price in Europe or even other non-oil endowed Africa countries, ignores the economic principle of the law of comparative advantage.
The case of the deregulations is that:
Petroleum products are being smuggled out through our porous borders, and if the price is raised to the price at the border countries, then incentive to smuggle will cease;
Petroleum products that are in short supply will become sufficient and perhaps more than needed in Nigeria. This will lead to competitive prices and possibly lower prices after the initial high prices; and,
That the profit margin shall be enhanced and that will invite investors to the sector to the overall benefit of the economy.
The case is persuasive, but to my mind, fallacious. Nigeria employs all sorts of security personnel to guard her borders. These include the customs officers, the immigration officers, the navy, the police, the air force and other services. They are paid from taxes paid by Nigerians. The government seems to be admitting that these employees are not doing their work and still should keep such jobs and continue to be paid from the taxes being paid by Nigerians. Government is also saying Nigerians should pay border prices so as to prevent smuggling. Nigerians are therefore, being invited to undertake double expenditure - one to keep ineffective security officers who cannot protect our borders - and the other, pay for that failure in high prices of petroleum products that are being smuggled. There must be a way to eliminate one of these two expenditures. Nigerians should not be invited to pay the two bills. The factor that is responsible for smuggling of oil products is not price differential between Nigeria and her neighbours. The craze for U.S. dollars at any price is the reason that encourages smuggling of oil and any other exportable products. Experience from the cocoa trade is a classical evidence that price increase has no role in smuggling. After the abolition of the marketing boards, the commodity trade in Nigeria was totally deregulated in 1986. Cocoa was bought from the farmers at the Naira price that was higher than world price in cocoa at the then exchange rate of the Naira to the U.S. dollar or British Pound Sterling. The foreigners who were determined to repatriate their investment in Nigerian bought cocoa at the price they could not sell it in export market. They were desperate to repatriates their investment. Capital flight then and at this uncertain period of the Naira exchange rates was responsible and will continue to facilitate the smuggling of petroleum products. Nigerians of substance that want to keep their wealth in foreign currency will not stop smuggling oil no matter the deregulation and high prices that will follow. We will only succeed in raising the poverty level and hardship of Nigerians. The second argument that petroleum products will be made abundantly available by deregulation is not supported by the experiences of 1960-1973 and 1997 when petroleum products were deregulated nor is it supported by our current experience in domestic cooking gas which should not sell for more than N600 per 12.5kg cylinder but which, if you are lucky to get, you will pay N1,800 or more.
The third plank of the deregulations is higher profit margin. That was not the experience in the two periods when free market price operated.
Before we examine the case of subsidy, we need to define what subsidy is. In economic principle, a situation of subsidy exists when consumers are assisted by the government to pay less than the market price for the product they are consuming. Example is payment for medicine by the government for poor citizens, payment for food items that the populace cannot afford but are required for their subsistence. Other form of subsidy is the payment to producers of some commodities by the government not to produce at all or to augment their income when the price of their product is less than break-even point. In economic principle, a producer of a commodity will go on until his cost of production equals his marginal price. There is, therefore, no subsidy where the barrel of crude petroleum delivered to our Nigerian refineries covers the cost of production of that drum. At the moment, the cost of producing a barrel of crude oil is less than $9. The argument for subsidy will not arise until the cost of production is less than the delivery price to our refinery.
The point of the deregulations is that opportunity cost. The opportunity cost of exporting a barrel of crude oil is $30. On the basis of opportunity cost, therefore, the deregulations insist that $20.5 is the subsidy per barrel on the crude oil refined for consumption in Nigeria. In my view, this definition of subsidy ignores the economic principle earlier stated. A producer exports the excess of his need - a farmer will feed first from his farm maize or yam, before selling the balance. This is not a theoretical economic principle. It accords with human behaviour and experience. A farmer will not first export his yam to Europe and re-import it for his own consumption at the price that will now include freight, import duty, port surcharge and other government's payment including handling and transportation cost. This is not in my view, deregulation and it should not be.
The general comment on the topic is that petroleum products are so crucial and central to almost all other domestic economic and social activities. If deregulation is to raise the price in the uncertain hope that prices will adjust to its own competitive level at a point, that point may be the point at which Keynes says, "We will all be dead".
The Nigeria government must look at the earing capacity of Nigerians before plunging into this deregulation menace. The earing of a Nigerian best paid worker at N2 million a year amounts to less than U.S. $17,000 at today's Niara exchange rate.
An average worker in Europe earns some U.S. $25 - 30, 000. An unemployed European earns more than the best paid Nigerian from unemployment benefits. An European has easy means of transportation.
He does not need to own a car there are buses and city trains and other means that both the rich and the poor patronise.
By comparison, these facilities are not available to Nigerians. Many of the workers leave their house at 5a.m. in the morning to get to work at 8a.m. Many will not get back home having closed at work at 5p.m. until 9 or 10p.m.
Hee spends about 40% of his home pay n transportation. My driver told me of spending 200 naira on a bowl of gari that he used to by for 50-70 Naira year ago. We must contemplate and consider the transportation and food items that must be bought by the average Nigerian worker if deregulation means paying 40 Naira per litre for oil. We do not need to repeat some of the valid argument about the impact of prices increase on all economic activities. The costs of inputs of most of them will jump by more than hike in the price of oil. This is a reality we cannot ignore.
In conclusion, the rating for and against deregulation of the oil sector which is expected to raise prices of the pump price is valid if our consideration is the question of opportunity cost. The argument is not valid if it is view in term of production cost and the law of comparative advantage - considering the price of $9.5 at which a barrel is delivered to Nigerian refineries be use the cost o production and refining is less than $9.5 in Nigeria. The Trade Union and indeed all other Nigerians will adversely affected as an increase will raise their poverty level which is already bad. Industries and employment will be adversely affected. We must do nothing that will make the situation of Nigeria and our industries worse than what they are as both are at an unbearable level of hardship.
Chief Kuye, (OFR) wrote in from Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Eastern File: Welcome, NPP Government (11)
Headline: Ghana: Eastern File: Welcome, NPP Government (11)
Body_Text:
Accra — EDUCATION
The prestigious schools cited in the President's address such as
Achimota, Prempeh College and the like are not exactly here in the
region. Even the closest in endowment - such as Pope John, St.  Peter's and
Aburi Girls schools - have started showing signs of serious hardships in the
results of their certificate examinations.
With the probable exception of these schools which can actually be
counted on the fingers of one hand, the rest are rather poorly endowed. In
all, 73 senior secondary schools, five technical institutions, six teacher
training colleges, two nursing institutes and a poly technic offer education
here.
The worst of the worse schools are over 40, a situation which makes most
parents and wards clamour for the better schools while ten others run empty
classrooms and dormitories.
An establishment of one quite good secondary school in each of the 15
districts, the improvement of facilities in all the 925 junior secondary and
1,890 primary schools will go a long way to improve the education delivery
in Eastern Region which the authorities have declared as not being quite
encouraging.
ROADS AND WATER
In a recent assessment, the Roads and Highways sector classified 577.3km
of roads in the region as good; 541.4km as fair and 804.4km as poor. It
admitted that several roads remained to be paved or rehabilitated.
While it expected that a systematic plan will be followed to continue
from where the old administration left off, the further opening-up of the
Afram Plains, Birim North, Manya and the immediate rehabilitation of
Nkawkaw, Nsawam, Suhum, Asamankese and Aburi town roads must be given
specific mention. One idea sold to the NDC government by this column was the
bridging of the Afram River between Tease and Adawso.
Though the NDC could not adopt it, it is resold to the new
administration as, sincerely speaking, it is believed that to attain
President Kufuor's vision of "Growing what we eat; eating what we can, and
canning what we cannot", we need to among other things, open up the Afram
Plains. And one sure way of doing that is to bridge the river.
For improved health and industrialization, water supply for the capital,
Koforidua and such urban areas as Nkawkaw, Somanya, Odumase, Begoro and the
Akuapem towns should immediately be improved.
JOB CREATION
For sometime now, the exploitation of bauxite near Kyebi has remained on
the drawing board and needs to be seriously pursued. Food crops, cocoa and
coffee production needs to be modernised to attract the energetic idling
youth. Cashew whose farms cover only 600ha, exotic mangoes (62ha), exotic
pawpaw (32.6ha) and pineapple (240ha) are areas still to be explored and
introduced to farmers. The vegetation, soils and weather conditions also
suit others such as black pepper, bananas, nut meg, sweet potatoes and
sunflower.
There are, of course, many more that the government can do to help
answer the demands of the people which cannot be captured in this piece. It
is only hoped that the NPP manifesto, the National Development Planning
effort and the yearly budgets will adequately cater for the immediate
concerns and speedy development of the Eastern Region. In making such a
demand one is conscious of the needs of other regions and would readily add
that they too should be given their fair share of the national cake.
LOCAL GOVERNANCE
Nobody today can deny the enormous responsibilities devolved on the
District Chief Executives in the process of urban, town and rural
development by the Decentralization process commendably started by the
erstwhile (P)NDC government. But we must hasten to add that the way to make
the process work was not how the NDC turned the positions of (DCEs)
government appointees and virtually, the whole assemblies into jobs for the
boys.
I believe the phenomenon was not obvious only in the Eastern Region but
throughout Ghana. Most of those mentioned above and even regional ministers
saw their duties more as working to entrench the NDC in power than serving
the whole society. Skewed tender boards, inflated contracts, over-invoicing,
under-invoicing, shoddy contract works and other fraudulent operations that
stemmed from the system are all now history.
It is in this vein that the recent wave of party functionaries flooding
the homes and offices of NPP executives, MPs, ministers and leaders, in
general, are particularly disturbing. Right away, the President and Local
Government Minister should get advertised the modalities and qualifications
for applying for the post, and ensure that NPP functionaries and
opportunists go looking for jobs they qualify for. I know that some
apologists in the party will damn me asking "Did the NDC not do the same"?
Well, really they did. But one of the quotations I made my daily verses
when I was a youth was this advice I picked from Dr. Kwame Safo- Adu. He
says "The way out is not the way back". Take heed.
GOOD WISHES
In conclusion, the Eastern Region is strategically well-placed to be a
partner with the new government in its vow to embark on agricultural
transformation and establishment of agro-based industries. As we welcome the
Kufuor administration, we wish them what we wish ourselves - Peace, Progress
and Prosperity.

Original_Search_Title: Bumper Harvest Boosts Food Security
Headline: Uganda: Bumper Harvest Boosts Food Security
Body_Text:
Kampala — THERE was a bumper harvest of food crops during the last season,
guaranteeing food security in the country this year, an agricultural
expert has said.
Mrs. Annunciata Hakuza Nkeza, an expert in charge of early warning
and food security functions in the Ministry of Agriculture, Animal
Industry and Fisheries, recently said a good harvest is also expected
in the second season.
Nkeza, who speaking at a seminar to analyse the implications of the
September - December 2001 seasonal weather forecast at the Prime
Minister's office, advised farmers to use even the current rains to
plant.
But principal meteorologist, A.W. Majugu, warned that random planting
at this stage of the season might be dangerous since the crop-growing
period is now halved. He said farmers must carefully select quick
maturing crops.
The seminar, moderated by Assistant Commissioner for Meteorology,
Aliphaz Bazira, attracted participants from the Prime Minister's
office, Ministry of Agriculture, Animal Industry and Fisheries, Water
resource department, FAO and others.
Nkeza said although forecasts are not very timely, they have helped
the agriculture ministry guide farmers.
"We have not yet completed compiling the data on the harvest, but the
indications we have are that crops performed well in the whole
country. There was a bumper harvest of maize in Busoga, Hoima and
Masindi, and sorghum in Kisoro and Kabale," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortage Hits Fragile Population
Headline: Somalia: Food Shortage Hits Fragile Population
Body_Text:
The shortfall of food availability in southern Somalia is likely to have a
serious impact on a population already experiencing high levels of
malnutrition, and the numbers of malnourished arriving at some health
facilities are already on the rise. The World Food Programme (WFP) has
warned that half a million Somalis in the south face hunger, and has
urgently appealed for 40,000 mt to cope with a looming crisis following
widespread rain failure.
In its August Nutrition Update, the USAID-funded and FAO-implemented Food
Security Analysis Unit (FSAU) said poorer households throughout Bay,
Bakool and Gedo regions in southern Somalia were already experiencing
problems with food access, while casual employment opportunities in the
agricultural sector had diminished, and recent escalations in tensions had
caused displacement of already fragile communities. The report noted that
critical periods of stress in Somalia all too often coincided with poor
humanitarian access. "Insecurity in southern Somalia currently poses
enormous challenges for organisations attempting to monitor the situation,
identify populations in need of assistance, and deliver that assistance
effectively."
In Baidoa, FSAU said that movement of people from the rural areas in
search of alternative sources of livelihood started in March 2001,
following poor germination of the first planting of crops. There had been
a general failure of crops in the southern sorghum belt. Food prices had
increased and milk had become expensive in Baidoa town, said FSAU. The
Baidoa Mother and Health Clinic records indicate high malnutrition - over
47 percent - among the attendants since January 2001. The report warned
that "there is a high readmission in the feeding programme as a result of
reduced ability to provide food of adequate quality and quantity in the
household".

Original_Search_Title: Government Sends SOS Against Famine
Headline: Niger: Government Sends SOS Against Famine
Body_Text:
Niamey, Niger — The government of Niger has sent out and
urgent appeal for emergency aid of some 60,000 tonnes of cereals for
May-August to avert the wave of famine in the country.
Prime Minister Hama Amadou Thursday told donors and development
partners that all the indicators of humanitarian disaster due to food
shortages were manifest nationwide since March.
He revealed that farmer supplies in the 12 districts have
completely run out while cereal prices have skyrocketed.
He said the bag of millet, Niger's staple food, has now reached
20,000 CFA francs as compared to 12,000 francs in January.
Amadou complained of the poor nutritional state of most
vulnerable groups (children, women and the elderly), depopulation
sometimes leading to the movement of entire families from the North to
the South and overpopulation in the suburbs of major urban centres by
rural dwellers.
He blamed insufficient rains and crop failures for the famine.
This situation was said to have led to a cereal deficit of about
163,360 tonnes affecting 4,155 of the country's 10,094 agricultural
villages hosting about 3,584,558 persons or 35 percent of Niger's
entire population.
To cope with the situation, the government has embarked on
irrigated farming and the sale of cereals at reasonable prices.
The off-season farming programme, with an estimated cost of 1
billion 600 million francs, started last November with the development
of 54,400 hectares distributed in 1998 sites for 220,000 head farmers.
But Amadou pointed out that just 59 percent of the forecast was
realised due to poor rainfall.
He, moreover, said that the operation to sell cereals at
reasonable prices was also hampered by the insufficiency of the offer
on the sub-regional market.
Only 9550 tonnes could be acquired during the two-implementation
phases. Partners supplied 4,500 tonnes of these while the State
provided 5,050 tonnes.
"Despite government efforts, the food situation has become
increasingly disturbing, and even alarming", the Prime Minister
pointed out.
Cereal prices, particularly those of millet, have sharply risen in
the past few days.
In February, Nigeria donated 1000 bags of rice, 500 bags of niebe
(local beans), 500 bags of maize, 500 bags of millet and 500 bags of
sorghum to famine stricken populations in Niger.

Original_Search_Title: Massive Crop Failure Anticipated
Headline: Namibia: Massive Crop Failure Anticipated
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Massive crop failure and a drastic reduction in maize and sorghum
yields is expected to hit communal farming areas in the four 'O'
regions in the North, as well as the Caprivi and Kavango regions, due
to late and erratic rainfall."
The prospects for crop growing activities in most crop producing
regions looks bleak this season due to the late onset of rainfall,"
says a recent crop forecast by the Namibia Early Warning and Food
Information System, compiled by the Ministry of Agriculture.
"Planting activities have been relatively delayed in the North
Central regions as well as Kavango and parts of Caprivi... as a
result of erratic and extremely low rainfall," says the Agriculture
report.
Despite the uncertainty and the anticipated crop failure recent
downpours have raised hopes among subsistence farmers who are still
ploughing and planting their their communal fields in the Kavango,
Oshikoto, Oshana, Ohangwena and Omusati regions.
Veikko Himalua, the acting Deputy Director for North Central -
covering the Oshikoto, Oshana, Ohangwena and Omusati regions - said
yesterday that the situation in the four northern regions was slowly
unfolding into an impending catastrophe.
"The situation is critical ... as you know at this time of the year
crops should be at the flowering stage... but unfortunately people
are still ploughing and planting. We are worried that we are going to
have a zero harvest," Himalua said.
He said the four 'O' regions received negligible rainfall at the
onset of the rainy season.
Some farmers, despite planting drought-resistant sorghum and mahangu
crops, were now pinning their hopes on the current rains continuing
to the end of April.
Peter Horn, the Deputy Director for Agriculture for the North-East
Division, covering the Kavango and Caprivi regions, said despite
recent downpours he expected a decrease in this year's harvest
compared to last year's yield. "Another problem that we have
experienced throughout the Kavango and the Caprivi (is) we get the
impression that less has been planted than in the past... and most
was planted late," said Horn."
"We will be lucky if we get what we got last year," he said.
Sources at Katima Mulilo said a large percentage of the current crops
in the region, consisting mostly of maize, had wilted as a result of
erratic rainfall.
At Rundu many communal farmers did not plough their fields because of
lack of rains while others "dry-planted" their fields.
Before the present nationwide downpours some of the areas in the
Kavango only received one millimetre of rain which was too little to
sustain even hardy, drought-resistant crops.
The more than 100 commercial farmers in the fertile Maize Triangle
are also expecting a reduced crop yield as only 5 000 of the 15 000
hectares of arable land has been planted due to late rains.
Jan Engelbrecht, Chairman of the Agronomic Producers' Association,
which groups together 150 commercial farmers in the Maize Triangle,
said the low hectarage planted would result in a reduced yield of the
maize crop produced this year.
Members of the Agronomic Producers' Association last year accounted
for over 50 per cent of the country's total maize production.
The crop and food assessment report says the state of grazing in the
country ranged from "fair to poor" while the condition of livestock
followed the pattern exhibited by pastures in most cases. "Pasture
condition is poor mostly in the North Central regions in the wake of
very low and late onset of rainfall," it notes.

Original_Search_Title: Output Estimated to Be Satisfactory
Headline: Burundi: Output Estimated to Be Satisfactory
Body_Text:
The output of the 2001 season crops is estimated to be satisfactory,
FAO said in its Africa August report. A locally organised FAO/WFP/UNICEF
assessment, estimated food production at 85,000 tons of cereals, 68,000 tons
of pulses, 483,000 tons of roots and tubers and 465,000 tons of bananas and
plantains.
This, according to the team, is 15 percent, 4 percent and 1 percent
respectively higher than in the 2000 A season. The mission, however,
observed that the outputs remained below the pre-crisis (1988-1993) average
levels due to insecurity and disruption of agricultural production since
1994. It said the area planted increased significantly this season
reflecting a relatively better security situation in western parts,
particularly in the provinces of Bubanza and Cibitoke, and the closure of
regroupment camps in Bujumbura Rural province, which allowed farmers to
return to their fields.
The other factor which contributed to higher plantings this season was
the timely seed distributions by the government and international agencies,
mainly in Kirundo and Muyinga provinces, the worst affected by drought
during the 2000 A season. FAO noted that the food and nutrition situation of
vulnerable groups remains precarious.
It said the situation was particularly difficult for some 324,000
internally displaced people and for drought affected persons in the
provinces of Karuzi, Gitega, Kayanza and Muyinga. "A recent nutrition survey
in seven provinces of the country indicated a 10 percent rate of acute
global malnutrition. Similarly, it quoted a recent report by the medical NGO
MSF, as indicating that the number of malnourished children admitted to its
therapeutic feeding centres in Karuzi province had doubled in January
2001. Emergency food aid continues to be necessary for the vulnerable
sections of the population, it added.

Original_Search_Title: Robbers in Police Uniform
Headline: Nigeria: Robbers in Police Uniform
Body_Text:
Here is how the people's protectors have turned the enemies of the
people.
As the three policemen were led into the dock, they looked woebegone
like cows that sighted the butcher's knife. Not even the afternoon
heat of the Lokoja, the Kogi State capital's courtroom prevented
passers-by from flocking in to witness the spectacle of law
enforcement agents who were themselves at the receiving end of
justice. That Monday 2 April, Benjamin Oyakhire, Jimoh Michael and
Gerson Saba, the three police constables who were standing trial,
could not face the spectators straight. Many of them had, in the
past, been victims of extortion, blackmail and brutality meted out by
these morose-looking gumshoes.
Nervously, one of the accused persons was busy drawing imaginary
lines on the wooden panel of the dock, another was scratching his
head while the third was picking his nose. They were absent-minded
when the lawyers trenchantly argued out all the wherefores and
hereinunders of the case. But the policemen were jerked awake into
the world of reality when Justice Umaru Ali Eri, the state Chief
Judge, passed his verdict.
"I see the three accused persons clearly as people who have no regard
whatsoever for the sanctity of human life," Justice Eri began. A hush
fell on the audience as the policemen craned their necks to listen to
their fate. The judge allowed his words to sink in before he cleared
his throat to proceed: "From my careful observations of them
throughout the trial, their apparent sign of remorse is, to me,
deceitful. Undoubtedly, the accused persons are unfit for decent
societies which we must cherish."
Justice Eri is, indeed, an expert at putting criminals on
tenterhooks. After giving the policemen the worst dressing-down of
their lives, he delayed what everyone, including the accused,
expected till the end of his judgement. But when he finally issued
the clincher, it hit the criminals with the devastating effect of a
stiletto: "The overall effect of their sentence," Justice Eri said,
"is that all the three accused persons stand sentenced to death and
each shall be hanged by the neck until he dies." Not a few among the
audience held their own necks in vicarious suffering or mockery or
both as the condemned men were led out by their fellow policemen. The
judge rose languidly , leaving the criminals to face the hangman's
noose. "Court..!"
For the three cops, Monday, 2 April was a day of reckoning. The
sentence passed on them was to be a deterrent to other policemen who
still behave like what a writer describes as Louis Freeh's
Doppelganger, "a would-be scourge of evil, who ended up collaborating
with the demon he was trying to exorcise." In Nigeria though,
policemen have been found to be more than collaborators but actual
perpetrators of armed robbery, extra-judicial killings, brutality and
perversion of justice.
Nemesis caught up with Constables Oyakhire (NPF 174214), Michael
(185850) and Saba (201977) on 17 February 2001 when they absconded
from their duty posts in Okene and mounted an illegal roadblock at
Okengwen-Obehira junction, on the highway leading to Akure, the Ondo
State capital. The cops stopped an Osun State Toyota Liteace bus with
registration number XB 104 SGB which carried seven passengers. They
were travelling to Katsina to buy rams for the coming Id-El-Kabir
festival. One of the security agents sighted a bag containing N1.4
million. On reflex, they snatched the money, diverted the vehicle to
a primary school where they set it (the bus) on fire, including the
passengers. Saka Jimoh and Saleh Badmus, two of the vehicle occupants
who escaped, reported the matter to the police at Okene.
Saka's survival had a touch of drama. Although he is a livestock
dealer, his average literacy and love of movies helped him out. The
captives were being taken to Lokoja by the police to "explain to our
bosses how you came about this money," when they met a team of mobile
policemen in a patrol van. The captors quickly shouted "escort!" so
that their colleagues would not stop them. Saka received knocks on
the head when he dared to question why they were being driven to
Lokoja and not Okene.
Eventually at the school where they stopped, the three policemen
divided the seven passengers among themselves for torture. "It was at
this juncture that I realised the policemen had a mission to
accomplish. Instantly, I summoned courage and said to myself, God,
Jimoh Saka you must not die," he narrated his ordeal to TheNEWS. The
policeman who was assigned to torture Saka was drunk and, "could
barely muster enough strength to deal with me." With the agility of a
fox, Saka pushed off the uniformed geek and ran, zig-zag, into the
bush with the staccato sound of gun shots ringing after him. He found
his way to the police station.
In an act of self-purification which, to be fair to the police
authorities, is now a priority preoccupation, the criminals were
promptly arrested by security agents who had been alerted by the
survivor.
TheNEWS gathered that during interrogations, one of the condemned men
told investigators that they committed the offence because they
wanted the huge sum to become theirs. Oyakhire, a notorious casanova,
confessed that he gave his own share of N230,000 to his girl friend.
While Michael buried his own loot of N209,000 at the mobile police
barracks on Lokoja-Abuja highway, Saba kept his N200,000 with a cop
colleague, attached to the Government House, Lokoja.
Mr. Wilfred Ehikamator, the Kogi State Commissioner of Police, said
the condemned cops' acts "are shameful and embarrassing to the police
force." The Kogi judgement was an indirect replay of the epic 1986
verdict on Assistant Superintendent of Police, George Iyamu, who was
sentenced to death by firing squad. He connived with Lawrence Anini,
Monday Osunbor and their gang in robbing and terrorising residents of
Benin City and its environs.
Another case which may be of epic proportion is, according to
documents in possession of TheNEWS, brewing in Akwa Ibom State. It
concerns a Superintendent of Police, Ikpe Johnson Udoaka. In a
petition which a concerned security agent wrote to the Inspector
General of Police, Mr. Musiliu Smith, Udoaka was alleged to belong
"to a syndicate that specialises in stealing people's motor vehicles,
defacing them and later (selling them) to either their accomplices or
to unsuspecting members of the public ." The petitioner revealed to
the IG that in August 2000, "through a concerted intelligence
