network, the syndicate was smashed but Supol...Udoaka was quick to
play his usual role (of offering) a bribe of N145,000 to the
investigators to kill the case." But the police, bent on redeeming
their image, refused the bait and registered the bribe money as
exhibit, details of which were contained in the Police Investigation
Report, reference number 1330/A15/CIB/Vol. 2/37 of 14 August 2000.
The saga started on 7 June last year when Assistant Commissioner of
Police, Egbe M. Preye, led a team of detectives to Ifiayong Usuk
village in Uruan Local Government Area of Akwa Ibom State where a
Nigerian Navy Peugeot 504 Pick-up van was parked. But all naval
inscriptions had been skilfully removed from the vehicle, parked on
the compound of Warrant Officer (W.O) II Okon Ekpeyong. His son, Jude
whom the police picked up, confessed that the vehicle was brought to
their compound by one Jameson Akpan, a Chief Petty Officer with the
Nigerian Navy, Lagos. The sleuths narrowly missed Akpan.
Determined to nab the naval personnel, the police detectives searched
the village with a toothcomb. Based on a tip-off by his mother's
friend, Akpan drove off in a car, but was pursued by those who were
after him. Akpan abandoned his vehicle and took to his heels, but
was,on 23 June 2000, arrested. Both Jude Okon Ekpeyong and Akpan sang
to the police.
The detectives found out that on 5 June 2000, Chief Petty Officer
Akpan unlawfully removed a Navy blue Peugeot 504 Pick-up van from the
premises of the Nigerian Navy, Ojo, Lagos and brought it to Ifiayong
Usuk village. On 6 June 2000, one Mr. Asuquo Ani, a publisher on 14,
NEPA Line, Uyo came to inspect and express interest in the
automobile. One revelation led to another. Akpan allegedly removed
another 30-seater coaster bus from the Navy in 1999. The colour was
changed to milky white from the navy blue and kept on the premises of
Rev. Emmanuel Samuel Nduesa of the Presbyterian Church, Ikot Ekpene.
The man of God, suspicious of all these moves, refused to buy the bus
for his church's use.
But the vehicle was removed again by one Mr. Uyoh and two others. The
police, however, arrested the duo in Kaduna from where they released
the vehicle to Anny Asikpo, acting on behalf of Akpan. Asikpo
thereafter sold the bus to one Alhaji for N700,000.
As one chain of events led to the other, DSP Udoaka, the uniformed
kingpin of the Uyo syndicate, allegedly offered money to the
investigators who, to the glory of the police, turned it down. Worse
still, Udoaka, "instigated a civil case against the detectives who
smashed the syndicate and it is pending in High Court 9, Uyo
(MU/MISC. 212/2000)."
The same Udoaka, according to the petition, dragged the Akwa Ibom
Police Command to the Eket High Court. "One of his concubines," the
petitioner writes, "Miss Mary C. Etukudo, stole the sum of N50,000
belonging to Eket Students' Association and it is being investigated
by the Police. But Supol Udoaka quickly manufactured another petition
to take over the case and went as far as keeping the girl in his
office. The next day, he instituted a court action against the CP
Akwa Ibom State and the Investigating Police Officer, Sgt. Imoh David
and Christopher Friday Inyang."
Meanwhile, the police in Akwa Ibom released Petty Officer Akpan on
bond to Lt. S.A. Akinwande of the Nigerian Navy for "departmental
proceedings."
On Monday, 26 March, some policemen from Alagolo Police Post in Lagos
kidnapped an accident victim from the Emergency Unit of the Ikeja
General Hospital. The patient was taken to a bank in Apapa where he
was forced to cash a cheque of N430,000 which the policemen shared.
Two motorists, according to P.M.News, were engaged in a quarrel over
traffic disagreement at Iyana-Ipaja. They were knocked down by a
commercial vehicle. The victims were rushed to the hospital by some
sympathisers. When the policemen from Alagolo got wind of the
incident, they towed the two vehicles down to their station. Apart
from refusing to release the Nissan vehicle to the family of the
deceased, the policemen converted it to their personal use. In the
process, they discovered a cheque of N430,000 written in the name of
the accident victim, Mr. Ogundele.
With the instinct of an alley cat, the policemen swiftly rushed to
the hospital, abducted Ogundele, dragged him down to the bank and
asked him to sign and clear the cheque on "orders from above." The
cops, while not giving Ogundele any chance of survival, allegedly
collected and shared the money. A friend of Ogundele who called at
the hospital was informed of the incident. He dashed to meet the
cops. When he was told at Alagolo Police Station that they knew
nothing about the case, Ogundele's friend petitioned the Lagos State
Police Command which arrested the suspects. They have been singing
ever since.
On 7 July 1999, Sergeant (Sgt.) Odugbesan Adekunle collected a letter
from his duty post on the Railway Unit of the Nigeria Police Force
(NPF), Lagos. The letter was addressed to Assistant Superintendent of
Police, ASP A.O. Babawale, commander of the Federal Highway Patrol
(FHP), Zone 5, Benin City.
The letter stated : "The above named NCO attached to the office was
detailed to escort a Mitsubishi vehicle with Chassis No. 22707940 788
from Iddo to Onitsha. The vehicle was impounded and parked at
Ekhiadolor Police Station by your team. Grateful (sir) release the
said vehicle to the NCO." The letter was signed by one D.A.U.
Oyebade, for the Railway Police Commissioner.
This letter served to confirm that Sgt. Adekunle was on official duty
on the 6 July 1999-a duty that required he be physically present with
the driver and vehicle. The Sergeant was not new to this business,
the bosses did not think otherwise either and so, he was allowed the
use of his SMG rifle with 15 rounds of live ammunition for the
assignment.
The journey had barely gone three hours on the Lagos-Benin Highway,
when the policeman and the driver ran into a blockage of the Federal
Highway Patrol, FHP near the Benin tollgate. This was one point where
esprit de corps would have paved a safe passage for any vehicle. But
for Sgt. Adekunle, it did not work. One thing led to another and the
Sergeant was "arrested" along with the driver of the vehicle he was
escorting and taken to Ekhiadolor Police Post on the highway.
But TheNEWS learnt that the driver of the vehicle based on the value
of the goods he was moving to Onitsha was asked to part with N5,000
cash by the FHP man. He pleaded he had only N500. The men were not
interested.
The action of the man did not go down well with ASP Babawale who was
infuriated at the audacity of Sgt. Adekunle .The ASP locked them up
at Ekhiadolor.
Bewildered Sgt. Adekunle later rushed to Benin Zonal Headquarters of
the NPF Zone 5 in search of ASP Babawale, but he did not find him. He
was perplexed to hear that the vehicle he was escorting was stolen,
according to ASP Babawale.
The owner of the vehicle showed up and allegedly settled the Highway
Patrol team. Adekunle was called all names, branded a fool and
threatened with detention. However, by 12 July 1999, the vehicle
landed safely at Onitsha still with Sgt.. Adekunle as escort. By 13
July 1999 when he returned to his post in Lagos, a signal had arrived
from Benin asking him to show up there to face some charges.
This magazine learnt that in Benin, this policeman was placed on
orderly room trial on two counts of "discreditable and discreditable
conducts." On the first count, he was said to have violated paragraph
E (3) of the 1st schedule of the Police regulation 355 of 1968. His
conduct was said to be; "prejudicial to discipline unbecoming of a
member of the force by being out of control," and corking his rifle
threatening to fire a police officer. The policeman denied this
charge.
On count two, Sgt. Adekunle allegedly ran foul of paragraph E (3) of
the 1st schedule of the same regulation by forging a letter from his
Commissioner of Police (CP) purportedly to have been written by the
CP. Again, he denied.
The trial opened on 12 August 1999 in Benin with DSP Johnson Oni
presiding. The exercise revealed that Mr. A. Ocheke officially
applied for a police escort and got approval from the Railway Police
Command on July 1999. The trial found that Sgt. Adekunle was on the
escort of the vehicle unofficially and actually insulted his
superiors in the course of the illegal conduct.
Sgt. Adekunle was also discovered by the panel to have threatened his
superiors by corking his rifle. He was therefore, found guilty of the
charges and recommended for demotion to the rank of Corporal (CPL)
from sergeant.
He reportedly begged for leniency but he was turned down. Although he
attended the trial and received a fair hearing, the policeman said
that he had appealed against the judgement but was yet to get any
response from the authorities. This appeal, TheNEWS learnt, was sent
to the office of the Inspector General of Police.
Provost Marshal J. Olisa Rhodes in Benin told this magazine that the
NPF Zone 5 was not aware of the appeal. He wondered why the media
should be interested in a purely internal affair of the police. At
the Ekhiadolor Police Post, neither ASP Babawale nor the DPO could be
reached for their comments.
Adekunle said he would not rest until justice is done. He said there
was a clear case of injustice in the orderly room trial. The grounds
of appeal, TheNEWS learnt, appear to favour the Sergeant, because the
letter from the CP approved his escort of the vehicle. The CP
confirmed same in another letter.
Meanwhile, for shouting out too loud, Adekunle's home was razed by a
mysterious fire after he dropped his appeal for a retrial. He claimed
that goods and effects worth over N1 million were lost in the
inferno.
In December, 2000, about 115 policemen were said to have been
arrested for alleged extortion from motorists nationwide at
roadblocks. So also is the case of a driver who was beaten up by a
team of policemen at their checkpoint over N20 on Saturday 10
February 2001. The driver incurred the wrath of the team when he
refused to offer them N20 to ease the traffic jam. So also is the
case of a police corporal at Eiyenkorin, a suburb of Ilorin when he
killed a commercial driver, his sergeant colleague and himself over
N150 bribe. Eyewitness account said the corporal engaged in an
argument with the driver at the roadblock over his refusal to
increase the N150 bribe he demanded. In the process, the policeman
opened fire on the driver. The cop who was angered by the driver's
refusal also shot to death his sergeant who rebuked him for his
action. The three corpses were later taken to the University of
Ilorin Teaching Hospital.
Simon Adewole will never forget his experience with policemen from
the Barracks Police Station, Surulere, Lagos on Sunday, 1 April 2001.
Simon, a businessman, had gone to pick up some money from his
business partner. As he mounted an okada to return home, a police van
drew up to his side. Their search yielded nothing incriminating. A
sum of N20,000 was, however, found on him. This was followed by
barrage of questions about what he does for a living, who has the
money and where he lives. Not satisfied, the cops took the man in
their vehicle to his house. Simon was lucky to meet his co-tenants
and brothers at home. After turning his room upside down with
"searches," they demanded that Simon follow them to the police
station. He did, but not without a handful of his tenants following.
Many of them, suspicious of the police's moves, had decided to pursue
the matter to a logical conclusion.
At the station, the police, still in custody of Simon's N20,000,
advised him to cooperate. They told him he must part with some of the
money or else he would be thrown behind bars. Simon remembered one of
them barking at him: "You better co-operate or now now, you go get
case for neck." The negotiation began with the policemen asking for
half of the money. Meanwhile, Simon's group of concerned friends was
kept outside the police station from where they watched the
proceedings with fear. The police, after much begging, took N4,000
from Simon's money and gave him the remaining N16,000.
Fuming, Simon narrated his ordeal to neighbours, threatening to take
up the case. But the consensus among Simon's acquaintances was that
he should not pursue the case or else he wound be digging his own
grave.
Yinka Adepegba is a furniture maker in Sango, Ibadan. He had come to
Lagos to pick up some materials for his job. He faced Mushin, Lagos
in the company of a friend for the materials. But, luck ran against
him when his friend's car in which he was driving was stopped by a
group of policemen who discovered N65,000 in the car boot. Yinka
pleaded endlessly with the police that the money was meant for his
business in Ibadan. They insisted they must be given part of the
money. His bold friend proved that the police violated their
fundamental human rights; he proceeded to give them some lecture on
what their original duties ought to be. Somehow petrified about this
bold and grammar man, the policemen softened and asked for "cola
money." Yinka's friend insisted they were not going to part with
anything. After spending about three hours on the same spot, Yinka
himself had to persuade his friend to allow him meet some of the
demands of the policemen. He gave them N500 which they flatly
refused. After much plea, they collected N1,000 and let them off the
hook.
There are indications that some policemen in Edo State are also
involved in criminal activities to the detriment of the force's
image. TheNEWS learnt that whereas the arms and ammunition seized
from shot robbers are kept in an armoury where they can be retrieved,
more often than not, the same weapons find their way back into the
hands of bandits. We gathered that the development is the result of a
symbiotic relationship between some policemen and notorious brigands.
Attempts to get the stock of arms and ammunition and the makes from
the authorities in Benin were futile. The Police Public Relations
Officer, Mr. Alean Eznia, ASP, said only his Commissioner of Police
could speak on the issue. The CP could not be reached for his
comments either.
Sources at the command also stated that the police had perfected a
system of using the same arms and ammunition to explain the shooting
of robbery suspects, whenever there are incidents.
A top official of the force in Benin City, however, denied this. The
officer, pleading anonymity, declared there was "no way he could
release the weapon seized from robbers again to robbers."
The police command in Edo "is highly disciplined and would not
compromise its integrity at any cost," the source further said. The
Edo State Police Command said it had not had any case of its men
being involved in helping to resell stolen cars, although it is
working on allegations of some car dealers operating with such
syndicates. The command maintained that unless investigations are
completed, it might not be in a position to present the true picture
of things.
Other areas where the police have become the Frankenstein monster of
the society are extra-judicial killings. Last January, Ayuba Amsat
Parakoyi was gunned down by a trigger-happy cop at the National
Stadium, Surulere, Lagos. Ayuba, an indigene of Saki in Oyo State,
went to watch the Nations Cup qualifier between the Super Eagles of
Nigeria and Chipolopolo of Zambia. Ayuba, a soccer freak, made it to
the stadium but never saw one minute of the march. His life was cut
short by a mobile policeman over a T-shirt which both of them had
struggled to possess.
A policeman shot him. Abayomi Oyebola was a dock worker and an
undergraduate of the Lagos State University, Ojo. Abayomi was having
his bath on the Apapa Ports, Lagos premises when a stray bullet,
allegedly fired by policemen, pierced through his head. Abayomi fell
and died. Abayomi's sad story brings to mind the killing on Wednesday
17 January 2001 in Anambra of Dr. Ignatius Igwu, a civil servant in
Enugu State. He was allegedly killed in the police custody. But the
State Police Public Relations Officer, Mr. Yemi Ajayi, denied that
Igwu was killed in the police cell. Igwu was, as the PPRO argued,
killed while attempting to escape from the custody of the police.
Almost three weeks after Igwu's death, Mr. Tajudeen Kazeem Ademola
became another victim of police extra-judicial killings. Ademola, a
plumber in Dolphin Estate, Ikoyi, was on Monday 5 February brutally
murdered in police cell at Ikoyi. Reports had it that he was first
beaten comatose by a group of policemen in the station for allegedly
refusing to sign a written document implicating him of taking part in
robbery operations. TheNEWS gathered that his captors tied him with a
rope and began to lash heavily at him with a cudgel. In great agony,
Ademola appealed to his tormentors to please ease the rope on him.
But the policemen refused, leaving Ademola dead. On Friday 9 March
2001, two students of the University of Lagos were gunned down by
gun-totting policemen at Ilaje, Bariga Station.
The latest victim of police killings is Mr. Kalu Samuel Iroh, a
businessman at Tejuosho market, Yaba, Lagos. On Sunday 18 March, the
35-year-old native of Nkporo, in Ohafia Local Government Area of Abia
State, left his No. 6, Alhaja Abass Street, Ogudu, Lagos around 3
p.m. He, along with four others, were arrested as they were returning
from Adeniran Ogunsanya Street, Surulere in a blue Peugeot 504 salon
car, with Registration No. AA 947 SKY belonging to his friend, Frank
Kerry Otonko of 20 Mobile Police Force, Ikeja, Lagos. They were
heading for Yaba when six policemen in a jeep intercepted Kalu's
vehicle. The policemen demanded their identities and particulars of
the car. While Kalu showed the policemen his identity card, he told
them that the car belonged to his friend, a mobile policeman. Not
quite satisfied with his explanation, the policemen searched the car,
discovered the mobile policeman's identity card, and whisked the
group away to Barracks Police Station, Area 'C' Command Headquarters,
Surulere, Lagos in handcuffs.
At the station, Kalu asked his captors the reason for their arrest
but, he was, instead, beaten with baton and metal objects. The head
of the captors, Sgt. Andrew, swore that he would deal with Kalu for
having the effrontery to ask a question. As a result of his beating,
Kalu sustained much injuries, severe loss of blood, shattered knee,
ankle and ribs. He became unconscious. Kalu's detained colleagues and
relatives pleaded that the policemen should release him on bail for
medical treatment, but the policemen refused. The Investigating
Police Officer, Andrew, was alleged to have demanded a bribe of
N60,000 before he could release him on bail. Before the deal could be
fixed, Kalu died.
With the tragedy, the DPO was alleged to have asked Kalu's brother to
get a private doctor to treat his detainee in the cell. Anxious to
see that his brother did not die in police cell, Mr. Ude dashed out
to get a doctor for his brother. When Ude came back, neither the DPO
nor his brother could be found. Kalu's cell mates told Ude that Kalu
had died and had been taken to the mortuary. Shocked, Ude went to see
the IPO who quickly directed him to the DPO who said that Kalu had
been taken to Lagos General Hospital . After intensive search in the
hospital, Ude discovered his brother's body in the morgue.
When TheNEWS sought to hear the police side of the story, the
Commander of Area C Command, Assistant Commissioner of Police (ACP),
M.E. Akpoyibo said that he was not around during the period. "I went
for senior officers' course." The police officer who is also a lawyer
claimed Akpoyibo commented further that investigation was on-going on
the matter and that it would be wrong for him to make any statement
when the investigation had not been concluded. According to the
senior police officer, when investigation is concluded, the
commissioner would be briefed, and the people saddled with giving out
information about police affairs would tell the general public the
true situation of things. However, the police officer admonished
members of the public to always admit when members of Nigeria's
police do anything good. "You see, people always see the negative
side of the police, it is unfair, they must also admit when police
are doing good things."
The killing of the traders has already led to protests in Yaba area.
On Wednesday, 21 March, the Igbo traders union in conjunction with
the tailors association at Ojuelegba demonstrated at the premises of
Nigeria Television Authority (NTA), Tejuosho. Among the inscriptions
on their placards were "Police Stop Killing us," "Enough is Enough"
etc. Recently, Yaba area of Lagos was literally turned to a
battlefield by the students of University of Lagos (UNILAG) and
members of Nigeria Police, when the students protested the killing of
their colleagues.
At a press conference called by Civil Liberties Organisation (CLO)
last Tuesday on Kalu's brutal killing, the human rights body said
that despite several complaints by its organisation and other
concerned groups over serious brutal killings of innocent Nigerians
by the police, the culture of police brutality still persists. "It is
a sad reality that the same law enforcement agents entrusted with the
protection and safety of citizens have proved to be the greatest
threats to the lives and security of the citizens," Mr. Okechukwu
Nwanguma, head, Law Enforcement Monitoring Project of the
organisation told reporters at the crowded press conference room of
the organisation.
Nwanguma said further that beyond condemning the excesses of law
enforcement agencies, government had done nothing practical to stem
it. He argued that the basic "existence of any government is the
welfare and security of the citizens."
Although CLO and the Committee for the Defence of Human Rights (CDHR)
is yet to complete collecting data of victims of police brutality in
Nigeria for last year, CDHR's annual report showed that 55 Nigerians
were mowed down by overzealous policemen in 1999. In its own
compilation in the last two months, the CLO said no fewer than seven
innocent persons were wrongly killed by policemen in Lagos alone.
Another unconfirmed report in the year 2000 showed that Lagos police
alone killed 387 citizens who were dubbed robbery suspects.
Not even women are spared in those killings. Many of them, the young
and promising, were gunned down last year. An annual report of
attacks on women in Nigeria by Project Alert on Violence Against
Women, a non-governmental organisation, spearheading protection of
women rights showed that between December 1999 and November 2000, six
innocent ladies were sent to their untimely graves by policemen.
On Saturday, 23 December 2000, Mrs. Margaret Ilo, 41, a mother of
nine, was killed when a policeman's bullet hit her in the head at her
stall on Alhaja Masha Road in Surulere, Lagos where she was selling
roasted plantain, groundnuts and coconuts. In a press briefing after
the incident, the police said Ilo's death was due to a crossfire
between a police officer and a gang of unidentified armed robbers.
The husband denied that his wife was shot in a crossfire between the
policemen and robbers. "There was no shoot-out between robbers and
policemen around the area on the said day."
Mrs. Ilo's sad end brings to mind the killing on Friday, 5 May 2000
in Lagos of Miss Odunola Hannah Ali, a 25-year-old Building
Technology graduate of the Yaba College of Technology. She was
allegedly felled by bullet from a security agent in the convoy of the
Deputy Governor of Lagos State, Mrs. Kofoworola Bucknor-Akerele.
Odunola was waiting for a cab at the bus stop to take her back to her
office from an official assignment when her life was snuffed out.
Three weeks after Odunola's death, Mrs. Juliana Obiajulu became
another victim of police reckless killings. She was shot dead in her
room by policemen who were said to be chasing a prostitute in Makoko,
Yaba area of Lagos. According to the report, policemen from Area C
invaded Makoko in Yaba pretending to be raiding the red-light
district for prostitutes. In the process, they allegedly began to
shoot indiscriminately particularly as some of the prostitutes began
to flee.
Children are not left out either. Last June, four primary school
children, aged between 11 and 14, were shot dead at the site of a
leaking pipeline in Eleme, Rivers State. They were killed, according
to Eleme community, by mobile policemen. After the incident, police
spokesman, Mr. Nnana Ama, said the command was not aware of the
killings. Yet, available information showed that the four children
whose bodies were found in shallow graves 24 hours after the gruesome
murder, had bullet wounds all over their bodies. On 15 March ,
two-year-old Mojisola Adeleye was hit by a police stray bullet which
affected her two eyes and the bullet is still lodged in her skull. It
happened at 53, Ayoola Lawal Street, Ilaje-Bariga, Lagos. Governor
Bola Ahmed Tinubu has approved the sum of N250,000 for her treatment.
t the Justice David Ahuora Judicial commission of inquiry into the
mobile police killings in Mbasombo and Mbalim, in Benue State last
month, the confession of mobile police corporal Ogbonaya Okorie, sent
many jaws hanging slack. Ogbonaya, notoriously called "Achiozie"
(warrior in Igbo), described how he killed a pregnant woman on 10
December last year. "I asked the woman to position her hands in front
of her face as if she was making supplications to God. I shot her and
my bullets pierced her hand and brain, gboaam!".
On the third day after the incident, two students of the Benue State
University Ehono Itolo and Robert Aleh were shot dead by the police
at the "B" division police station, Makurdi. The police claimed that
the students got caught up in a cross-fire.
February 2001 was a bloody month for the families of Okenini and
Enabulele, both of Jalipa and Oliha communities in Delta and Edo
states. They lost two of their loved ones through police brutality.
The quest for justice forced Osayemwenre Enabulele to petition the
Presidency. Mr. Enabulele said the duo were shot and killed by one
Inspector John Okhue in concert with Mr. Samson Obaseki, Head of the
Edo Vigilante Group. The group is yet to have the legal basis for its
operations .
Notwithstanding that, TheNEWS was informed that the death of Nosa and
Osagie was connected with their activities as land speculators. The
Edo State Legislature had passed a motion condemning the antics of
these speculators in recent times. The lawmakers said the speculators
only extort money from well-meaning Nigerians eager to erect
buildings on legitimately acquired plots of land. They urged the
police to help rid the City of Benin of the miscreants.
However, Mr. Enabulele said, he heads a group of youngmen who engage
in road and most maintenance and collection of levies (fees) from
site developments on behalf of the Oliha community. At the State
Information Ministry, Benin and the Corporate Affairs Commission
(CAC) the group's identity could not be traced. The youth group makes
so much money that they recently attracted interest from the police,
some of whom felt the youths ought to make returns to them.
When a police officer, Inspector John Okhue allegedly visited the
offices of the group asking for his share on 23 February 2001, Mr.
Enabulele said, the group called his bluff because his demands were
getting out of control. TheNEWS learnt that the youths had in the
past paid between N5,000 and N10,000 but because he was "broke" on
the fateful day, he offered a mere N2,000 which the Inspector
rejected.
The lot fell on Musa who shouted at the Inspector and called him
names. The angry police officer was to later leave the premises, only
to return the following day with Samson Obaseki. According to
Enabulele, it was Obaseki who shot Musa in the neck and killed him.
The Nigeria Police Force has, many times, also allegedly perverted
justice. Banjo and Lanre Adekanle are now cooling off their heads at
Ikoyi Prisons, courtesy of the magic hands of the men of Nigeria
Police that thwart cases. Banjo, the older Adekanle, who is an auto
dealer bought a house from one Miss Toyin Bademosi some time last
year. As he was about to renovate the house, he discovered that
someone else had taken it. He reported the matter to the police,
which did nothing to stop the intruder.
But the real trouble started on 20 January when Banjo and his younger
brother went to the premises of the disputed building only to see
about eight people working there. An argument ensued and Banjo called
the police with his mobile phone. One of the intruders was
apprehended and detained at Alagbon (Annex).
The Adekanle brothers were at the station to know how far the case
had gone, when they were arrested right inside the station. In a
twist of fate, the accuser suddenly became the accused. Today, a case
of murder is hanging on their necks. Yet, the police could not take
them to court for prosecution. A reliable source told this magazine
that a top female police officer has a personal interest in the
matter.
Not even Miss Bademosi, who flew in from London to give testimony
that she sold the house to Banjo could get them freedom. The intruder
is now renovating the house while Banjo is languishing in Ikoyi
Prisons. It was gathered that the spurious murder case was a ploy to
scare him from his property.
Loremikan, project co-ordinator of CDHR said investigations by his
organisation showed that they (evil police officers) were either
drunk or under the influence of dangerous drugs like cocaine or
Indian hemp. He also attributed it to over-zealousness and lack of
proper training on the part of the police. "Once these people
(police) carry guns, they see themselves as having the licence to do
anything," he said.
In his own submission, Mr. Nwanguma attributed it to long period of
military rule which has made the police to inculcate the military
mentality. "They have imbibed this syndrome and this makes them prone
to senseless killings of innocent Nigerians," he told this magazine.
Gani Adams, head of a faction of the Oodua People's Congress, OPC, in
an interview with TheNEWS said that police are on the pay roll of
armed robbers. According to the OPC chief, police kill innocent
people in lieu of suspects. He cited one incident in Gbongan, Osun
State where one Rufus Obagade, who was persuaded by the traditional
ruler of the town to assist as a vigilante, was framed up by the
police, because his activities disturbed them from getting returns
from criminals.
Odumegwu Ojukwu, the Eze Igbo Gburugburu, told TheNEWS recently: "I
abhor the rampant situation in Nigeria where, any time you get the
police on duty, they tell you it is 'accidental discharge.' The time
has come to make sure that there are no accidents, because those
people killed are Nigerian citizens who (the police) are supposed to
protect." He advised the Federal Government to rein in its coercive
agents and certainly, check the level of command that can unleash
death on the citizenry. He added: "The most effective police in the
world are (those) that invest themselves in the community they are
defending. But when you send a Sokoto policeman to maintain order in
Nnewi, you are asking for trouble."
At the recent inauguration ceremony of the Nigerian Bar Association
and the presentation of the Human Rights Action Plan for 2001-2005,
Chief Bola Ige, the Justice Minister, was so angry with the corrupt
section of the Nigeria Police that he rained curses on the force:
"This illegal and illegitimate act, this N20 or N30 you extort from
innocent people, your arbitrary arrest and detention, will never be
useful to you. You will spend the money on hospital bills. You will
use the money to bury your children. God will put holes in your
pockets and drain this illegally acquired money. God will strike them
with thunder!" But Chief Ige has since denied ever pouring
vituperations on the Nigerian cops.
All these notwithstanding, the police authorities are not blindly
defending their officers and men. In an interview with the British
Broadcasting Corporation, BBC, the Inspector General of Police,
Musiliu Smith admitted that, they "still have some bad eggs who are
not good ambassadors of the force." He added that any time "we see
such elements, we have been very prompt in taking good care of them."
True, the speed at which the Kogi police robbers were arrested and
tried attests to this assertion. In the words of Mr. Haz Iwendi, the
"Police Public Relations Officer, PPRO, in Abuja, "Police authorities
are very pleased with the judgement in which three erstwhile police
officers were sentenced to death for the killing of some traders. The
facts of the case were too obvious for them to appeal and the police
want to congratulate the judicial arm for using this case to set a
good record in criminal justice system. Justice delayed is justice
denied..."
Additional reports by: Lara Owoeye-Wise, Onyema Omenuwa, Tope
Ogunjinmi, Bamidele Adebayo, Bukky Omotola, Victor Osehobo, Richard
Elesho, Abimbola Ogunnaike, Sunday Orinyan, Gbenga Arifayan and Emma
Ojo.
